english_head: and not the entire conduct of life that for each of us would make living most worth while? Well, do I deny it? said Thrasymachus. You seem to, said I, or else to care nothing for us and so feel no concern whether we are going to live worse or better lives in our ignorance of what you affirm that you know. Nay, my good fellow, do your best to make the matter clear to us also:

greek_head: κακῶς σοι κείσεται ὅτι ἂν ἡμᾶς τοσούσδε ὄντας εὐεργετήσῃς ἐγὼ γὰρ δή σοι λέγω τό γ' ἐμόν ὅτι
greek_tokens: kakos su keimai ostis an egw tososde eimi euergetew egw gar dh su legos o ge emos ostis ou peiqw oude oimh adikia dikaiosunh kerdaleos eimi oude ean eaw tis autos kai mh diakwluw prassw o boulomai alla agaqos eimi men adikos dunamai de adikew h o lanqanw h o diamaxomai omws egw ge ou peiqw ws eimi o dikaiosunh kerdaleos outos oun
english_head: it will be no bad investment for you—any benefit that you bestow on such company as this. For I tell you for my part that I am not convinced, neither do I think that injustice is more profitable than justice, not even if one gives it free scope and does not hinder it of its will. But, suppose, sir, a man to be unjust and to be able to act unjustly either because he is not detected or can maintain it by violence, all the same he does not convince me that it is more profitable than justice.

greek_head: καὶ ἕτερος ἴσως τις ἡμῶν πέπονθεν οὐ μόνος ἐγώ πεῖσον οὖν ὦ μακάριε ἱκανῶς ἡμᾶς ὅτι οὐκ ὀρθῶς βουλευόμεθα
greek_tokens: kai eteros isos tis egw pasxw ou monos egw peiqw oun eimi makarios ikanos egw ostis ou orqos bouleuw dikaiosunh adikia peri pleiwn poiew kai pws fhmi sos peiqw ei gar os nundi elegos mh peiqw tis su eti poiew h eis o yuxh ferw entiqhmi o logos ma diw eimi de egw mh su ge alla proteros men o an eipon emmenw outos h ean metatiqhmi faneros metatiqhmi kai egw mh ecapath nun de oraw eimi qrasumaxos eti
english_head: Now it may be that there is someone else among us who feels in this way and that I am not the only one. Persuade us, then, my dear fellow, convince us satisfactorily that we are ill advised in preferring justice to injustice. And how am I to persuade you? he said. If you are not convinced by what I just now was saying, what more can I do for you? Shall I take the argument and ram it into your head? Heaven forbid! I said, don’t do that. But in the first place when you have said a thing stand by it, or if you shift your ground change openly and don’t try to deceive us.

greek_head: γὰρ τὰ ἔμπροσθεν ἐπισκεψώμεθα ὅτι τὸν ὡς ἀληθῶς ἰατρὸν τὸ πρῶτον ὁριζόμενος τὸν ὡς ἀληθῶς ποιμένα οὐκέτι ᾤου
greek_tokens: gar o emprosqen episkeptomai ostis o ws alhqhs iatros o proteros orizw o ws alhqhs poimhn ouketi oiomai dew usteron akribhs fulassw alla piainw oiomai autos o probaton kaqa osos poimhn eimi ou pros o o probaton beltistos blepw alla wsper daitumwn tis kai mellw estiaw pros o euwxia h au pros o
english_head: But, as it is, you see, Thrasymachus—let us return to the previous examples—you see that while you began by taking the physician in the true sense of the word, you did not think fit afterwards to be consistent and maintain with precision the notion of the true shepherd, but you apparently think that he herds his sheep in his quality of shepherd not with regard to what is best for the sheep but as if he were a banqueter about to be feasted with regard to the good cheer or again with a view to the sale of them

greek_head: ἀποδόσθαι ὥσπερ χρηματιστὴν ἀλλ' οὐ ποιμένα τῇ δὲ ποιμενικῇ οὐ δήπου ἄλλου του μέλει ἢ ἐφ' ᾧ τέτακται ὅπως
greek_tokens: apodidwmi wsper xrhmatisths alla ou poimhn o de poimenikos ou dhpou allos tis melos h epi os tassw opws outos o beltistos ekporizw epei o ge eautou wste eimi beltistos ikanos dhpou ekporizw ews ge an mhdeis endew o poimenikos eimi outws de oiomai egw nundi anagkaion eimi egw omologew pas arxh kaqa osos arxh mhdeis allos o beltistos skopaw h ekeinos o
english_head: as if he were a money-maker and not a shepherd. But the art of the shepherd surely is concerned with nothing else than how to provide what is best for that over which is set, since its own affairs, its own best estate, are entirely sufficiently provided for so long as it in nowise fails of being the shepherd’s art. And in like manner I supposed that we just now were constrained to acknowledge that every form of rule in so far as it is rule considers what is best for nothing else than that which is governed and cared for by it,

greek_head: ἀρχομένῳ τε καὶ θεραπευομένῳ ἔν τε πολιτικῇ καὶ ἰδιωτικῇ ἀρχῇ σὺ δὲ τοὺς ἄρχοντας ἐν ταῖς πόλεσιν τοὺς ὡς ἀληθῶς
greek_tokens: arxw te kai qerapeuw en te politikos kai idiwtikos arxh su de o arxw en o polis o ws alhqhs arxw ekwn oiomai arxw ma diw ou fhmi alla eu oida tis de eimi de egw eimi qrasumaxos o allos arxh ou ennoew ostis oudeis eqelw arxw ekwn alla misqos aitew ws ou autos wfeleia eimi ek o arxw alla
english_head: alike in political and private rule. Why, do you think that the rulers and holders of office in our cities—the true rulers—willingly hold office and rule? I don’t think, he said, I know right well they do. But what of other forms of rule, Thrasymachus? Do you not perceive that no one chooses of his own will to hold the office of rule, but they demand pay, which implies that not to them will benefit accrue from their holding office but to those whom they rule?

greek_head: τοῖς ἀρχομένοις ἐπεὶ τοσόνδε εἰπέ οὐχὶ ἑκάστην μέντοι φαμὲν ἑκάστοτε τῶν τεχνῶν τούτῳ ἑτέραν εἶναι τῷ ἑτέραν
greek_tokens: o arxw epei tososde eipon ou ekastos mentoi fhmi ekastote o texnh outos eteros eimi o eteros o dunamis exw kai eimi makarios mh para doca apokrinw ina tis kai perainw alla outos fhmi eteros oukoun kai wfeleia ekastos outos idios tis egw parexw alla ou koinos oios iatrikos men ugieia kubernhtikos de swthria en o plew kai o allos outws panu ge
english_head: For tell me this: we ordinarily say, do we not, that each of the arts is different from others because its power or function is different? And, my dear fellow, in order that we may reach some result, don’t answer counter to your real belief. Well, yes, he said, that is what renders it different. And does not each art also yield us benefit that is peculiar to itself and not general, as for example medicine health, the pilot’s art safety at sea, and the other arts similarly? Assuredly. And does not the wage-earner’s art yield wage? For that is its function.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν καὶ μισθωτικὴ μισθόν αὕτη γὰρ αὐτῆς ἡ δύναμις ἢ τὴν ἰατρικὴν σὺ καὶ τὴν κυβερνητικὴν τὴν αὐτὴν καλεῖς
greek_tokens: oukoun kai misqwtikos misqos outos gar autos o dunamis h o iatrikos su kai o kubernhtikos o autos kalew h ean boulomai akribhs diorizw wsper upotiqhmi oudeis tis mallon ean tis kubernaw ugihs gignomai dia o sumferw autos plew en o qalassa eneka outos kalew mallon autos iatrikos ou dhta fhmi oude ge oimh o misqwtikos ean ugiainw tis misqarnhs ou dhta tis de o iatrikos misqarnhtikos ean iaomai tis misqarnew
english_head: Would you identify medicine and the pilot’s art? Or if you please to discriminate precisely as you proposed, none the more if a pilot regains his health because a sea voyage is good for him, no whit the more, I say, for this reason do you call his art medicine, do you? Of course not, he said. Neither, I take it, do you call wage-earning medicine if a man earning wages is in health. Surely not.

greek_head: Οὐκ ἔφη Οὐκοῦν τήν γε ὠφελίαν ἑκάστης τῆς τέχνης ἰδίαν ὡμολογήσαμεν
greek_tokens: ou fhmi oukoun o ge wfeleia ekastos o texnh idios omologew eimi eimi fhmi ostis ara wfeleia koinh wfelew pas e dhmiourgos dhlos ostis koinh tis o autos prosxraomai apo ekeinos wfelew eoika fhmi fhmi de ge o misqos arnumai wfelew o dhmiourgos apo o prosxraomai o misqwtikos texnh gignomai autos sumfhmi mogis
english_head: But what of this? Do you call medicine wage-earning, if a man when giving treatment earns wages? No, he said. And did we not agree that the benefit derived from each art is peculiar to it? So be it, he said. Any common or general benefit that all craftsmen receive, then, they obviously derive from their common use of some further identical thing. It seems so, he said. And we say that the benefit of earning wages accrues to the craftsmen from their further exercise of the wage-earning art. He assented reluctantly. Then the benefit,

greek_head: Οὐκ ἄρα ἀπὸ τῆς αὑτοῦ τέχνης ἑκάστῳ αὕτη ἡ ὠφελία ἐστίν ἡ τοῦ μισθοῦ λῆψις ἀλλ' εἰ δεῖ ἀκριβῶς σκοπεῖσθαι
greek_tokens: ou ara apo o eautou texnh ekastos outos o wfeleia eimi o o misqos lhyis alla ei deomai akribhs skopaw o men iatrikos ugieia poiew o de misqarnhtikos misqos kai o men oikodomikos oikia o de misqarnhtikos autos epomai misqos kai o allos paomai outws o eautou ekastos ergnumi ergazomai kai wfelew ekeinos epi os tassw ean de mh misqos autos prosgignomai esqw ostis wfelew o dhmiourgos apo o texnh ou fainw fhmi
english_head: the receiving of wages does not accrue to each from his own art. But if we are to consider it precisely medicine produces health but the fee-earning art the pay, and architecture a house but the fee-earning art accompanying it the fee, and so with all the others, each performs its own task and benefits that over which it is set, but unless pay is added to it is there any benefit which the craftsman receives from the craft? Apparently not, he said. Does he then bestow no benefit either

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν οὐδ' ὠφελεῖ τότε ὅταν προῖκα ἐργάζηται Οἶμαι ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: ara oun oude wfelew tote otan proic ergazomai oimh egw oukoun eimi qrasumaxos outos hdh dhlos ostis oudeis texnh oude arxh o eautou wfelimos paraskeuazw alla osper pala legw o o arxw kai paraskeuazw kai epitassw o ekeinos sumferw hsswn eimi skopaw alla ou o o kreisswn dia dh outos egw eimi filos qrasumaxos kai arti elegos mhdeis eqelw ekwn arxw kai o allotrios kakos metaxeirizomai anorqow
english_head: when he works for nothing? I’ll say he does. Then, Thrasymachus, is not this immediately apparent, that no art or office provides what is beneficial for itself—but as we said long ago it provides and enjoins what is beneficial to its subject, considering the advantage of that, the weaker, and not the advantage the stronger? That was why, friend Thrasymachus, I was just now saying that no one of his own will chooses to hold rule and office and take other people’s troubles in hand to straighten them out, but everybody expects pay for that,

greek_head: ἀλλὰ μισθὸν αἰτεῖν ὅτι ὁ μέλλων καλῶς τῇ τέχνῃ πράξειν οὐδέποτε αὑτῷ τὸ βέλτιστον πράττει οὐδ' ἐπιτάττει
greek_tokens: alla misqos aitew ostis o mellw kalos o texnh prassw oudepote eautou o beltistos prassw oude epitassw kata o texnh epitassw alla o arxw os dh eneka ws eoika misqos dew uparxw o mellw eqelw arxw h argurion h timh h zhmia ean mh arxw pws outos legw eimi swkraths fhmi o glauc o men gar duo misqos gignwskw o de zhmia ostis legw kai ws en misqos meros erw ou sunihmi o o beltistos ara misqos fhmi ou sunihmi dia os
english_head: because he who is to exercise the art rightly never does what is best for himself or enjoins it when he gives commands according to the art, but what is best for the subject. That is the reason, it seems, why pay must be provided for those who are to consent to rule, either in form of money or honor or a penalty if they refuse. What do you mean by that, Socrates? said Glaucon. The two wages I recognize, but the penalty you speak of and described as a form of wage I don’t understand. Then, said I, you don’t understand the wages of the best men

greek_head: ἄρχουσιν οἱ ἐπιεικέστατοι ὅταν ἐθέλωσιν ἄρχειν ἢ οὐκ οἶσθα ὅτι τὸ φιλότιμόν τε καὶ φιλάργυρον εἶναι ὄνειδος
greek_tokens: arxw e epieikhs otan eqelw arxw h ou oida ostis o filotimos te kai filarguros eimi oneidos legw te kai eimi egw fhmi dia outos toinun eimi de egw oute xrhma eneka eqelw arxw e agaqos oute timaw oute gar faneros prassw o arxh eneka misqos misqwtos boulomai kalew oute laqrh autos ek o arxh lambanw klepths oude au timaw eneka ou gar eimi filotimos deomai dh
english_head: for the sake of which the finest spirits hold office and rule when they consent to do so. Don’t you know that to be covetous of honor and covetous of money is said to be and is a reproach? I do, he said. Well, then, said I, that is why the good are not willing to rule either for the sake of money or of honor. They do not wish to collect pay openly for their service of rule and be styled hirelings nor to take it by stealth from their office and be called thieves, nor yet for the sake of honor,

greek_head: αὐτοῖς ἀνάγκην προσεῖναι καὶ ζημίαν εἰ μέλλουσιν ἐθέλειν ἄρχειν ὅθεν κινδυνεύει τὸ ἑκόντα ἐπὶ τὸ ἄρχειν ἰέναι
greek_tokens: autos anagkh proseimi kai zhmia ei mellw eqelw arxw oqen kinduneuw o ekwn epi o arxw eimi alla mh anagkh perimenw aisxros nomizw o de zhmia megas o upo ponhros arxw ean mh autos eqelw arxw os deidw egw fainw arxw otan arxw e epieikhs kai tote erxomai epi o arxw ou ws epi agaqos tis eimi oude ws eupaqew en autos alla
english_head: for they are not covetous of honor. So there must be imposed some compulsion and penalty to constrain them to rule if they are to consent to hold office. That is perhaps why to seek office oneself and not await compulsion is thought disgraceful. But the chief penalty is to be governed by someone worse if a man will not himself hold office and rule. It is from fear of this, as it appears to me, that the better sort hold office when they do, and then they go to it not in the expectation of enjoyment nor as to a good thing, but as to a necessary evil and because they are unable to turn it over to better men than themselves

greek_head: ὡς ἐπ' ἀναγκαῖον καὶ οὐκ ἔχοντες ἑαυτῶν βελτίοσιν ἐπιτρέψαι οὐδὲ ὁμοίοις ἐπεὶ κινδυνεύει πόλις ἀνδρῶν ἀγαθῶν
greek_tokens: ws epi anagkaion kai ou exw eautou beltiwn epitrepw oude omoios epei kinduneuw polis androw agaqos ei gignomai perimaxhtos an eimi o mh arxw wsper nuni o arxw kai entauqa an katafanhs gignomai ostis o eimi alhqinos arxw ou fuw o eautou sumferw skopaw alla o o arxw wste pas an o gignwskw o wfelew mallon airew upo allos h allos wfelew pragma exw outos men oun egw oudamh sugxwrew
english_head: or to their like. For we may venture to say that, if there should be a city of good men only, immunity from office-holding would be as eagerly contended for as office is now, and there it would be made plain that in very truth the true ruler does not naturally seek his own advantage but that of the ruled; so that every man of understanding would rather choose to be benefited by another than to be bothered with benefiting him. This point then I

greek_head: Θρασυμάχῳ ὡς τὸ δίκαιόν ἐστιν τὸ τοῦ κρείττονος συμφέρον ἀλλὰ τοῦτο μὲν δὴ καὶ εἰς αὖθις σκεψόμεθα πολὺ δέ μοι
greek_tokens: qrasumaxos ws o dikaios eimi o o kreisswn sumferw alla outos men dh kai eis auqis skeptomai polus de egw dokew megas eimi o nun legw qrasumaxos o o adikos bios faskw eimi kreisswn h o o dikaios su oun poteros eimi de egw eimi glauc airew kai poteros alhqhs dokew su legw o o dikaios egw lusitelhs bios eimi
english_head: by no means concede to Thrasymachus, that justice is the advantage of the superior. But that we will reserve for another occasion. A far weightier matter seems to me Thrasymachus’s present statement, his assertion that the life of the unjust man is better than that of the just. Which now do you choose, Glaucon? said I, and which seems to you to be the truer statement? That the life of the just man is more profitable, I say, he replied.

greek_head: Ἤκουσας ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὅσα ἄρτι Θρασύμαχος ἀγαθὰ διῆλθεν τῷ τοῦ ἀδίκου
greek_tokens: akouw eimi de egw osos arti qrasumaxos agaqos dierxomai o o adikos akouw fhmi alla ou peiqw boulomai oun autos peiqw an dunamai ph eceuriskw ws ou alhqhs legw pws gar ou boulomai eimi de os an men toinun eimi de egw antikatateinw legw autos logos para logos osos au agaqos exis o dikaios eimi kai auqis outos kai allos egw ariqmew dehsis
english_head: Did you hear, said I, all the goods that Thrasymachus just now enumerated for the life of the unjust man? I heard, he said, but I am not convinced. Do you wish us then to try to persuade him, supposing we can find a way, that what he says is not true? Of course I wish it, he said. If then we oppose him in a set speech enumerating in turn the advantages of being just and he replies and we rejoin, we shall have to count up and measure the goods listed in the respective speeches

greek_head: τἀγαθὰ καὶ μετρεῖν ὅσα ἑκάτεροι ἐν ἑκατέρῳ λέγομεν καὶ ἤδη δικαστῶν τινων τῶν διακρινούντων δεησόμεθα ἂν δὲ
greek_tokens: agaqos kai metrew osos ekateros en ekateros legw kai hdh dikasths tis o diakrinw dew an de wsper arti anomologeomai pros allhlwn skopaw ama autos te dikasths kai rhtwr eimi panu men oun fhmi opoteros oun su eimi de egw areskw outws fhmi eimi dh eimi de egw eimi qrasumaxos apokrinw egw ek arxh o teleios adikia teleios eimi dikaiosunh lusitelhs fhmi eimi
english_head: and we shall forthwith be in need of judges to decide between us. But if, as in the preceding discussion, we come to terms with one another as to what we admit in the inquiry, we shall be ourselves both judges and pleaders. Quite so, he said. Which method do you like best? said I. This one, he said. Come then, Thrasymachus, I said, go back to the beginning and answer us. You affirm that perfect and complete injustice is more profitable than justice that is complete.

greek_head: Πάνυ μὲν οὖν καὶ φημί ἔφη καὶ δι' ἅ εἴρηκα Φέρε δή τὸ τοιόνδε περὶ αὐτῶν πῶς λέγεις τὸ μέν που
greek_tokens: panu men oun kai fhmi fhmi kai dia o erw ferw dh o toiosde peri autos pws legw o men pou areth autos kalew o de kakia pws gar ou oukoun o men dikaiosunh areth o de adikia kakia eoika ge fhmi eimi hdus epei ge kai legos adikia men lusitelew dikaiosunh de ou alla tis meis enantion eimi de os eimi o dikaiosunh kakia ou alla panu gennaios euhqeia
english_head: I affirm it, he said, and have told you my reasons. Tell me then how you would express yourself on this point about them. You call one of them, I presume, a virtue and the other a vice? Of course. Justice the virtue and injustice the vice? It is likely, you innocent, when I say that injustice pays and justice doesn’t pay. But what then, pray? The opposite, he replied. What! justice vice? No, but a most noble simplicity or goodness of heart. Then do you call injustice badness of heart?

greek_head: Τὴν ἀδικίαν ἄρα κακοήθειαν καλεῖς Οὔκ ἀλλ' εὐβουλίαν ἔφη
greek_tokens: o adikia ara kakohqeia kalew ou alla euboulia fhmi eimi kai fronimos su eimi qrasumaxos dokew eimi kai agaqos e adikos e ge teleios fhmi oios te adikew polis te kai eqnos dunamai anqrwpos ufh eautou poiew su de oiomai egw isos o o ballantion apotemnw legw lusitelew men oun eimi de os kai o toioutos ean lanqanw eimi de ou acios logos alla o nundi elegos
english_head: No, but goodness of judgement. Do you also, Thrasymachus, regard the unjust as intelligent and good? Yes, if they are capable of complete injustice, he said, and are able to subject to themselves cities and tribes of men. But you probably suppose that I mean those who take purses. There is profit to be sure even in that sort of thing, he said, if it goes undetected. But such things are not worth taking into the account,

greek_head: Τοῦτο μέν ἔφην οὐκ ἀγνοῶ ὃ βούλει λέγειν ἀλλὰ τόδε ἐθαύμασα εἰ ἐν ἀρετῆς καὶ σοφίας τιθεῖς μέρει τὴν ἀδικίαν
greek_tokens: outos men fhmi ou agnoew o boulomai legw alla ode qaumazw ei en aretaw kai sofia tiqhmi meros o adikia o de dikaiosunh en o enantios alla panu outws tiqhmi outos eimi de egw hdh stereos eimi etairos kai ouketi radios exw ostis tis eipon ei gar lusitelew men o adikia tiqhmi kakia mentoi h aisxros autos omologew eimi wsper allos tis exw an tis legw kata o nomizw legw nun de dhlos ei ostis fhmi autos kai kalos kai isxuros eimi kai allos autos pas prosqew
english_head: but only what I just described. I am not unaware of your meaning in that, I said; but this is what surprised me, that you should range injustice under the head of virtue and wisdom, and justice in the opposite class. Well, I do so class them, he said. That, said I, is a stiffer proposition, my friend, and if you are going as far as that it is hard to know what to answer. For if your position were that injustice is profitable yet you conceded it to be vicious and disgraceful as some other disputants do, there would be a chance for an argument on conventional principles. But, as it is, you obviously are going to affirm that it is honorable and strong and you will attach to it all the other qualities

greek_head: ἃ ἡμεῖς τῷ δικαίῳ προσετίθεμεν ἐπειδή γε καὶ ἐν ἀρετῇ αὐτὸ καὶ σοφίᾳ ἐτόλμησας θεῖναι
greek_tokens: o egw o dikaios prostiqhmi epei ge kai en aretaw autos kai sofia tolmaw qeinw alhqhs fhmi manteuomai alla ou mentoi eimi de egw apoknhteon ge o logos epecerxomai skopaw ews an su upolambanw legw o dianoeomai egw gar dokew su eimi qrasumaxos atexnos nun ou skwptw alla o dokew peri o alhqeia legw tis de su fhmi outos diaferw eite egw dokew eite mh alla ou o logos elegxw
english_head: that we were assigning to the just, since you don’t shrink from putting it in the category of virtue and wisdom. You are a most veritable prophet, he replied. Well, said I, I mustn’t flinch from following out the logic of the inquiry, so long as I conceive you to be saying what you think. For now, Thrasymachus, I absolutely believe that you are not mocking us but telling us your real opinions about the truth. What difference does it make to you, he said, whether I believe it or not? Why don’t you test the argument?

greek_head: Οὐδέν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἀλλὰ τόδε μοι πειρῶ ἔτι πρὸς τούτοις ἀποκρίνασθαι ὁ δίκαιος τοῦ δικαίου δοκεῖ τί σοι ἂν
greek_tokens: oudeis eimi de egw alla ode egw peiraw eti pros outos apokrinw o dikaios o dikaios dokew tis su an eqelw pleos exw oudamos fhmi ou gar an eimi asteios wsper nun kai euhqhs tis de o dikaios pracis oude o dikaios fhmi o de adikos poteros aciow an pleonektew kai hgeomai dikaios eimi h ou an hgeomai hgeomai an eimi de os kai aciow alla ou an dunamai alla ou outos eimi de egw erwtaw alla ei o men
english_head: No difference, said I, but here is something I want you to tell me in addition to what you have said. Do you think the just man would want to overreach or exceed another just man? By no means, he said; otherwise he would not be the delightful simpleton that he is. And would he exceed or overreach or go beyond the just action? Not that either, he replied. But how would he treat the unjust man—would he deem it proper and just to outdo, overreach, or go beyond him or would he not? He would, he said, but he wouldn’t be able to. That is not my question, I said,

greek_head: δικαίου μὴ ἀξιοῖ πλέον ἔχειν μηδὲ βούλεται ὁ δίκαιος τοῦ δὲ ἀδίκου
greek_tokens: dikaios mh aciow pleos exw mhde boulomai o dikaios o de adikos alla outws fhmi exis tis de dh o adikos ara aciow o dikaios pleonektew kai o dikaios pracis pws gar ou fhmi os ge pas pleos exw aciow oukoun kai adikos ge anqrwpos te kai pracis o adikos pleonektew kai amillaomai ws apas pleistos autos lambanw eimi outos wde dh legw fhmi o dikaios o men omoios ou pleonektew o de anomoios o de adikos o te omoios kai
english_head: but whether it is not the fact that the just man does not claim and wish to outdo the just man but only the unjust? That is the case, he replied. How about the unjust then? Does he claim to overreach and outdo the just man and the just action? Of course, he said, since he claims to overreach and get the better of everything. Then the unjust man will overreach and outdo also both the unjust man and the unjust action, and all his endeavor will be to get the most in everything for himself. That is so. Let us put it in this way, I said; the just man does not seek to take advantage of his like but of his unlike, but the unjust man

greek_head: τοῦ ἀνομοίου Ἄριστα ἔφη εἴρηκας
greek_tokens: o anomoios ariston fhmi erw eimi de ge fhmi fronimos te kai agaqos o adikos o de dikaios oudeteros kai outos fhmi eu oukoun eimi de egw kai eoika o fronimos kai o agaqos o adikos o de dikaios ou eoika pws gar ou mellw fhmi o toioutos eimi kai eoika o toioutos o de mh eoika kalos toioutos ara eimi ekateros autos os eoika alla tis mellw fhmi eien eimi qrasumaxos mousikos de tis legw eteros de
english_head: of both. Admirably put, he said. But the unjust man is intelligent and good and the just man neither. That, too, is right, he said. Is it not also true, I said, that the unjust man is like the intelligent and good and the just man is not? Of course, he said, being such he will be like to such and the other not. Excellent. Then each is such as that to which he is like. What else do you suppose? he said. Very well, Thrasymachus,

greek_head: ἄμουσον Ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: amousos egw poteros fronimos kai poteros afrwn o men mousikos dhpou fronimos o de amousos afrwn oukoun kai o fronimos agaqos o de afrwn kakos nai tis de iatrikos ou outws outws dokew an oun tis su eimi aristos mousikos anhr armozw lura eqelw mousikos anhr en o epitasis kai anesis o xordh pleonektew h aciow pleos exw ou egw tis de amousos anagkh fhmi
english_head: but do you recognize that one man is a musician and another unmusical? I do. Which is the intelligent and which the unintelligent? The musician, I presume, is the intelligent and the unmusical the unintelligent. And is he not good in the things in which he is intelligent and bad in the things in which he is unintelligent? Yes. And the same of the physician? The same. Do you think then, my friend, that any musician in the tuning of a lyre would want to overreach another musician in the tightening and relaxing of the strings or would claim and think fit to exceed or outdo him? I do not. But would the the unmusical man? Of necessity, he said. And how about the medical man?

greek_head: Τί δὲ ἰατρικός ἐν τῇ ἐδωδῇ ἢ πόσει ἐθέλειν ἄν τι ἰατρικοῦ πλεονεκτεῖν ἢ ἀνδρὸς ἢ πράγματος
greek_tokens: tis de iatrikos en o edwdh h posis eqelw an tis iatrikos pleonektew h anhr h pragma ou dhta mh iatrikos de nai peri pas dh oraw episthmh te kai anepisthmosunh ei tis su dokew episthmos ostis plew an eqelw airew h osos allos episthmos h prassw h legw kai ou tautos o omoios eautou eis o autos pracis alla isos fhmi anagkh outos ge outws exw tis de o anepisthmwn ou omoios men episthmwn
english_head: In prescribing food and drink would he want to outdo the medical man or the medical procedure? Surely not. But he would the unmedical man? Yes. Consider then with regard to all forms of knowledge and ignorance whether you think that anyone who knows would choose to do or say other or more than what another who knows would do or say, and not rather exactly what his like would do in the same action. Why, perhaps it must be so, he said, in such cases. But what of the ignorant man—of him who does not know? Would he not overreach or outdo equally

greek_head: πλεονεκτήσειεν ἄν ὁμοίως δὲ ἀνεπιστήμονος Ἴσως
greek_tokens: pleonektew an omoios de anepisthmwn isos o de episthmos sofos fhmi o de sofos agaqos fhmi o ara agaqos te kai sofos o men omoios ou eqelw pleonektew o de anomoios te kai enantios eoika fhmi o de kakos te kai amaqhs o te omoios kai o enantios fainw oukoun eimi qrasumaxos eimi de egw o adikos egw o anomoios te kai omoios pleonektew h ou outws legw egw fhmi
english_head: the knower and the ignorant? It may be. But the one who knows is wise? I’ll say so. And the wise is good? I’ll say so. Then he who is good and wise will not wish to overreach his like but his unlike and opposite. It seems so, he said. But the bad man and the ignoramus will overreach both like and unlike? So it appears. And does not our unjust man, Thrasymachus, overreach both unlike and like? Did you not say that? I did, he replied.

greek_head: Ὁ δέ γε δίκαιος τοῦ μὲν ὁμοίου οὐ πλεονεκτήσει τοῦ δὲ ἀνομοίου
greek_tokens: o de ge dikaios o men omoios ou pleonektew o de anomoios nai eoika ara eimi de egw o men dikaios o sofos kai agaqos o de adikos o kakazw kai amaqhs kinduneuw alla meis omologew os ge omoios ekateros eaw toioutos kai ekateros eimi omologew gar o men ara dikaios egw anafainw eimi agaqos te kai sofos o de adikos amaqhs te kai kakos o dh qrasumaxos omologew men pas outos ou
english_head: But the just man will not overreach his like but only his unlike? Yes. Then the just man is like the wise and good, and the unjust is like the bad and the ignoramus. It seems likely. But furthermore we agreed that such is each as that to which he is like. Yes, we did. Then the just man has turned out on our hands to be good and wise and the unjust man bad and ignorant. Thrasymachus made all these admissions

greek_head: ὡς ἐγὼ νῦν ῥᾳδίως λέγω ἀλλ' ἑλκόμενος καὶ μόγις μετὰ ἱδρῶτος θαυμαστοῦ ὅσου ἅτε καὶ θέρους ὄντος τότε καὶ
greek_tokens: ws egw nun radios legos alla elkw kai mogis meta idrws qaumasths osos ate kai qeros eimi tote kai eidon egw proteros de oupw qrasumaxos eruqriaw epei de oun diomologew o dikaiosunh areth eimi kai sofia o de adikia kakia te kai amaqia eien eimi de egw outos men egw outws kew fhmi de dh kai isxuros eimi o adikia h ou mimnhskw eimi qrasumaxos mimnhskw fhmi alla egw oude o nun legw areskw kai exw peri autos legw ei oun legw eu oida ostis
english_head: not as I now lightly narrate them, but with much baulking and reluctance and prodigious sweating, it being summer, and it was then I beheld what I had never seen before—Thrasymachus blushing. But when we did reach our conclusion that justice is virtue and wisdom and injustice vice and ignorance, Good, said I, let this be taken as established. But we were also affirming that injustice is a strong and potent thing. Don’t you remember, Thrasymachus? I remember, he said; but I don’t agree with what you are now saying either and I have an answer to it,

greek_head: δημηγορεῖν ἄν με φαίης ἢ οὖν ἔα με εἰπεῖν ὅσα βούλομαι ἤ εἰ βούλει ἐρωτᾶν ἐρώτα ἐγὼ δέ σοι ὥσπερ ταῖς γραυσὶν
greek_tokens: dhmhgorew an egw fhmi h oun ea egw eipon osos boulomai h ei boulomai erwtaw erwtaw egw de su wsper o graus o o muqos legw eien eraomai kai kataneuw kai ananeuw mhdamos eimi de egw para ge o sautou doca wste sos fhmi areskw epei ou eaw legw kaitoi tis allos boulomai oudeis ma dios eimi de egw alla eiper outos poihsis poiew egw de erwtaw erwtaw dh outos toinun erwtaw osper arti ina kai echs diaskeptomai
english_head: but if I were to attempt to state it, I know very well that you would say that I was delivering a harangue. Either then allow me to speak at such length as I desire, or, if you prefer to ask questions, go on questioning and I, as we do for old wives telling their tales, will say Very good and will nod assent and dissent. No, no, said I, not counter to your own belief. Yes, to please you, he said, since you don’t allow me freedom of speech. And yet what more do you want? Nothing, indeed, said I; but if this is what you propose to do, do it and I will ask the questions. Ask on, then. This, then, is the question I ask, the same as before, so that our inquiry may proceed in sequence.

greek_head: τὸν λόγον ὁποῖόν τι τυγχάνει ὂν δικαιοσύνη πρὸς ἀδικίαν ἐλέχθη γάρ που ὅτι καὶ δυνατώτερον καὶ ἰσχυρότερον
greek_tokens: o logos opoios tis tugxanw eimi dikaiosunh pros adikia legw gar pou ostis kai dunatos kai isxuros eaw adikia dikaiosunh nun de ge fhmi eiper sofia te kai areth eimi dikaiosunh radios oimh fainw kai isxuros adikia epei eimi amaqia o adikia oudeis an eti outos agnoew alla ou tis outws aplos eimi qrasumaxos egw epiqumew alla ode ph skeptomai
english_head: What is the nature of injustice as compared with justice? For the statement made, I believe, was that injustice is a more potent and stronger thing than justice. But now, I said, if justice is wisdom and virtue, it will easily, I take it, be shown to be also a stronger thing than injustice, since injustice is ignorance—no one could now fail to recognize that—but what I want is not quite so simple as that. I wish, Thrasymachus, to consider it in some such fashion as this. A city, you would say, may be unjust and

greek_head: πόλιν φαίης ἂν ἄδικον εἶναι καὶ ἄλλας πόλεις ἐπιχειρεῖν δουλοῦσθαι ἀδίκως καὶ καταδεδουλῶσθαι πολλὰς δὲ καὶ
greek_tokens: polis fhmi an adikos eimi kai allos polis epixeirew doulow adikos kai katadoulow polus de kai ufh eautou exw doulow pws gar ou fhmi kai outos ge o aristos malista poihsis kai teleios eimi adikos manqanw fhmi ostis sos outos eimi o logos alla ode peri autos skopaw poteros o kreisswn gignomai polis polis aneu dikaiosunh o dunamis outos ecis h anagkh autos meta dikaiosunh
english_head: try to enslave other cities unjustly, have them enslaved and hold many of them in subjection. Certainly, he said; and this is what the best state will chiefly do, the state whose injustice is most complete. I understand, I said, that this was your view. But the point that I am considering is this, whether the city that thus shows itself superior to another will have this power without justice or whether she must of necessity combine it with justice.

greek_head: Εἰ μέν ἔφη ὡς σὺ ἄρτι ἔλεγες ἔχει ἡ δικαιοσύνη σοφία μετὰ δικαιοσύνης εἰ δ' ὡς ἐγὼ ἔλεγον μετὰ
greek_tokens: ei men fhmi ws su arti legw exis o dikaiosunh sofia meta dikaiosunh ei de ws egw elegos meta adikia panu agamai eimi de egw eimi qrasumaxos ostis ou epineuw monos kai ananeuw alla kai apokrinw panu kalos sos gar fhmi xarizomai eu ge su poa alla dh kai ode egw xarizomai kai legos dokew an h polis h stratopedon h lhisths h klepths h allos tis eqnos osos koinh epi tis erxomai adikos prassw an tis dunamai ei adikew allhlwn
english_head: If, he replied, what you were just now saying holds good, that justice is wisdom, with justice; if it is as I said, with injustice. Admirable, Thrasymachus, I said; you not only nod assent and dissent, but give excellent answers. I am trying to please you, he replied. Very kind of you. But please me in one thing more and tell me this: do you think that a city, an army, or bandits, or thieves, or any other group that attempted any action in common, could accomplish anything if they wronged one another?

greek_head: Οὐ δῆτα ἦ δ' ὅς Τί δ' εἰ μὴ ἀδικοῖεν οὐ μᾶλλον
greek_tokens: ou dhta eimi de os tis de ei mh adikew ou mallon panu ge isthmi gar pou eimi qrasumaxos o ge adikia kai misos kai maxh en allhlwn paraxew o de dikaiosunh omonoia kai filios eimi gar eimi eimi de os ina su mh diaferw alla eu ge su poa eimi aristos ode de egw legos ara ei outos ergnumi adikia misos empoiew opou an enihmi ou kai en eleuqeros te kai doulos eggignomai misew poihsis allhlwn kai stasiazw kai adunatos eimi koinh meta
english_head: Certainly not, said he. But if they didn’t, wouldn’t they be more likely to? Assuredly. For factions, Thrasymachus, are the outcome of injustice, and hatreds and internecine conflicts, but justice brings oneness of mind and love. Is it not so? So be it, he replied, not to differ from you. That is good of you, my friend; but tell me this: if it is the business of injustice to engender hatred wherever it is found, will it not, when it springs up either among freemen or slaves, cause them to hate and be at strife with one another, and make them incapable

greek_head: ἀλλήλων πράττειν Πάνυ γε
greek_tokens: allhlwn prassw panu ge tis de an en duo eggignomai ou diaferw kai misew kai exqros eimi allhlwn te kai o dikaios eimi fhmi ean de dh eimi qaumasios en eis eggignomai adikia mhs mh apollumi o eautou dunamis h oudeis hsswn ecis mhdeis hsswn exw fhmi oukoun toiosde tis fainw exw o dunamis oios os an eggignomai eite polis tis eite genos eite stratopedon eite
english_head: of effective action in common? By all means. Suppose, then, it springs up between two, will they not be at outs with and hate each other and be enemies both to one another and to the just? They will, he said. And then will you tell me that if injustice arises in one it will lose its force and function or will it none the less keep it? Have it that it keeps it, he said. And is it not apparent that its force is such that wherever it is found in city, family, camp, or in anything else

greek_head: ἄλλῳ ὁτῳοῦν πρῶτον μὲν ἀδύνατον αὐτὸ ποιεῖν πράττειν μεθ' αὑτοῦ διὰ τὸ στασιάζειν καὶ διαφέρεσθαι ἔτι δ' ἐχθρὸν εἶναι
greek_tokens: allos ostis proteros men adunatos autos poiew prassw meqo eautou dia o stasiazw kai diaferw eti de exqros eimi eautou te kai o enantios pas kai o dikaios ou outws panu ge kai en eis dh oimh eneimi tautos outos poihsis o fuw ergazomai proteros men adunatos autos prassw poihsis stasiazw kai ou omonoew autos eautou epeita exqros kai eautou kai o dikaios eimi gar nai dikaios de ge eimi eimi filos kai e qeos eimi fhmi
english_head: it first renders the thing incapable of cooperation with itself owing to faction and difference, and secondly an enemy to itself and to its opposite in every case, the just? Isn’t that so? By all means. Then in the individual too, I presume, its presence will operate all these effects which it is its nature to produce. It will in the first place make him incapable of accomplishing anything because of inner faction and lack of self-agreement, and then an enemy to himself and to the just. Is it not so? Yes. But, my friend,

greek_head: Καὶ θεοῖς ἄρα ἐχθρὸς ἔσται ὁ ἄδικος ὦ Θρασύμαχε ὁ δὲ δίκαιος φίλος
greek_tokens: kai qeaw ara exqros eimi o adikos eimi qrasumaxos o de dikaios filos euwxew o logos fhmi qarsos ou gar egw su enantioomai ina mh ode apexqomai eimi dh eimi de egw kai o loipas egw o estiasis apoplhrow apokrinw wsper kai nun ostis men gar kai sofos kai ameinwn kai dunatos prassw e dikaios fainw e de adikos oude prassw meta allhlwn oios
english_head: the gods too are just. Have it that they are, he said. So to the gods also, it seems, the unjust man will be hateful, but the just man dear. Revel in your discourse, he said, without fear, for I shall not oppose you, so as not to offend your partisans here. Fill up the measure of my feast, then, and complete it for me, I said, by continuing to answer as you have been doing. Now that the just appear to be wiser and better and more capable of action and the unjust incapable of any common action,

greek_head: τε ἀλλὰ δὴ καὶ οὕς φαμεν ἐρρωμένως πώποτέ τι μετ' ἀλλήλων κοινῇ πρᾶξαι ἀδίκους ὄντας τοῦτο οὐ παντάπασιν
greek_tokens: te alla dh kai os fhmi errwmenos pwpote tis meta allhlwn koinh prassw adikos eimi outos ou pantapasi alhqhs legw ou gar an apexw allhlwn komidh eimi adikos alla dhlos ostis eneimi tis autos dikaiosunh o autos poiew mhtoi kai allhlwn ge kai epi os aeidw ama adikew dia os prassw o prassw ormaw de epi o adikos adikia hmimoxqhros eimi epei e ge pamponhros kai teleios adikos teleios eimi kai prassw adunatos outos
english_head: and that if we ever say that any men who are unjust have vigorously combined to put something over, our statement is not altogether true, for they would not have kept their hands from one another if they had been thoroughly unjust, but it is obvious that there was in them some justice which prevented them from wronging at the same time one another too as well as those whom they attacked; and by dint of this they accomplished whatever they did and set out to do injustice only half corrupted by injustice, since utter rascals completely unjust

greek_head: μὲν οὖν ὅτι οὕτως ἔχει μανθάνω ἀλλ' οὐχ ὡς σὺ τὸ πρῶτον ἐτίθεσο εἰ δὲ καὶ ἄμεινον ζῶσιν οἱ δίκαιοι τῶν ἀδίκων καὶ
greek_tokens: men oun ostis outws exis manqanw alla ou ws su o proteros tiqhmi ei de kai ameinwn zaw e dikaios o adikos kai eudaimwn eimi osper o usteron protiqhmi skeptomai skepteos fainw men oun kai nun os ge egw dokew ek os erw omws de eti beltiwn skepteos ou gar peri o epitugxanw o logos alla peri o ostis tropos xrh zaw skopaw dh fhmi skopaw eimi de egw kai egw legos dokew tis su eimi ippos ergnumi
english_head: are completely incapable of effective action—all this I understand to be the truth, and not what you originally laid down. But whether it is also true that the just have a better life than the unjust and are happier, which is the question we afterwards proposed for examination, is what we now have to consider. It appears even now that they are, I think, from what has already been said. But all the same we must examine it more carefully. For it is no ordinary matter that we are discussing, but the right conduct of life. Proceed with your inquiry, he said. I proceed, said I. Tell me then—would you say

greek_head: Ἔμοιγε Ἆρ' οὖν τοῦτο ἂν θείης καὶ ἵππου καὶ ἄλλου ὁτουοῦν
greek_tokens: egw ara oun outos an qeia kai ippos kai allos ostis ergnumi o an h monos ekeinos poiew tis h ariston ou manqanw fhmi alla wde esqw ostis an allos eidon h ofqalmos ou dhta tis de aekwn allos h ous oudamos oukoun dikaios an outos outos fhmi ergon eimi panu ge
english_head: that a horse has a specific work or function? I would. Would you be willing to define the work of a horse or of anything else to be that which one can do only with it or best with it? I don’t understand, he replied. Well, take it this way: is there anything else with which you can see except the eyes? Certainly not. Again, could you hear with anything but ears? By no means. Would you not rightly say that these are the functions of these (organs)? By all means. Once more,

greek_head: Τί δέ μαχαίρᾳ ἂν ἀμπέλου κλῆμα ἀποτέμοις καὶ σμίλῃ καὶ ἄλλοις πολλοῖς
greek_tokens: tis de maxaira an ampelos klhma apotemnw kai smilh kai allos polus pws gar ou alla oudeis ge an oimh outws kalos ws drepanon o epi outos ergazomai alhqhs ara oun ou outos outos ergnumi tiqhmi tiqhmi men oun nun dh oimh ameinwn an manqanw o arti erwtaw punqanomai ei ou outos ekastos eaw ergnumi o an h monos tis h kallista o allos apergazomai alla fhmi manqanw te kai egw dokew outos ekastos
english_head: you could use a dirk to trim vine branches and a knife and many other instruments. Certainly. But nothing so well, I take it, as a pruning-knife fashioned for this purpose. That is true. Must we not then assume this to be the work or function of that? We must. You will now, then, I fancy, better apprehend the meaning of my question when I asked whether that is not the work of a thing which it only or it better than anything else can perform. Well, he said, I do understand, and agree

greek_head: πράγματος ἔργον εἶναι Εἶεν ἦν δ' ἐγώ οὐκοῦν καὶ ἀρετὴ δοκεῖ σοι εἶναι
greek_tokens: pragma ergnumi eimi eien eimi de egw oukoun kai areth dokew su eimi ekastos os kai ergnumi tis prostassw eimi de epi o autos palin ofqalmos fhmi eimi tis ergnumi eimi ara oun kai areth ofqalmos eimi kai areth tis de aatos eimi tis ergnumi nai oukoun kai areth kai areth tis de pas peri o allos ou outws outws exw dh ara an pote omma o eautou ergnumi kalos
english_head: that the work of anything is that. Very good, said I. Do you not also think that there is a specific virtue or excellence of everything for which a specific work or function is appointed? Let us return to the same examples. The eyes we say have a function? They have. Is there also a virtue of the eyes? There is. And was there not a function of the ears? Yes. And so also a virtue? Also a virtue. And what of all other things? Is the case not the same? The same. Take note now. Could the eyes possibly fulfil their function well

greek_head: ἀπεργάσαιντο μὴ ἔχοντα τὴν αὑτῶν οἰκείαν ἀρετήν ἀλλ' ἀντὶ τῆς ἀρετῆς κακίαν
greek_tokens: apergazomai mh exw o eautou oikeios areth alla anti o aretaw kakia kai pws an fhmi tufloths gar isos legw anti o oyis ostis eimi de egw autos o areth ou gar pw outos erwtaw alla ei o oikeios men aretaw o eautou ergnumi eu ergazomai o ergazomai kakia de kakos alhqhs fhmi outos ge legw oukoun kai ous steromai o eautou aretaw kakos o eautou ergnumi apergazomai panu ge
english_head: if they lacked their own proper excellence and had in its stead the defect? How could they? he said; for I presume you meant blindness instead of vision. Whatever, said I, the excellence may be. For I have not yet come to that question, but am only asking whether whatever operates will not do its own work well by its own virtue and badly by its own defect. That much, he said, you may affirm to be true. Then the ears, too, if deprived of their own virtue will do their work ill? Assuredly. And do we then apply

greek_head: Τίθεμεν οὖν καὶ τἆλλα πάντα εἰς τὸν αὐτὸν λόγον Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ
greek_tokens: tiqhmi oun kai allos pas eis o autos logos egw dokew eimi dh meta outos ode skeptomai yuxazw eimi tis ergnumi o allos o eimi oude an eis prassw oios o toiosde o epimeleomai kai arxw kai bouleuw kai o toioutos pas esqw ostis allos h yuxw dikaios an autos apodew kai fhmi idios ekeinos eimi oudeis allos tis de au o zaw ou yuxazw fhmi ergnumi eimi malista ge fhmi oukoun kai areth fhmi tis yuxazw eimi fhmi
english_head: the same principle to all things? I think so. Then next consider this. The soul, has it a work which you couldn’t accomplish with anything else in the world, as for example, management, rule, deliberation, and the like, is there anything else than soul to which you could rightly assign these and say that they were its peculiar work? Nothing else. And again life? Shall we say that too is the function of the soul? Most certainly, he said. And do we not also say that there is an excellence virtue of the soul?

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ποτε ὦ Θρασύμαχε ψυχὴ τὰ αὑτῆς ἔργα εὖ ἀπεργάσεται στερομένη τῆς οἰκείας ἀρετῆς ἢ ἀδύνατον
greek_tokens: ara oun pote eimi qrasumaxos yuxh o eautou ergon eu apergazomai steromai o oikeios aretaw h adunatos adunatos anagkh ara kakazw yuxw kakos arxw kai epimeleomai o de agaw pas outos eu prassw anagkh oukoun areth ge sugxwrew yuxazw eimi dikaiosunh kakia de adikia sugxwrew gar o men ara dikaios yuxh kai o dikaios anhr eu biow kakos de o adikos fainw fhmi kata o sos logos
english_head: We do. Will the soul ever accomplish its own work well if deprived of its own virtue, or is this impossible? It is impossible. Of necessity, then, a bad soul will govern and manage things badly while the good soul will in all these things do well. Of necessity. And did we not agree that the excellence or virtue of soul is justice and its defect injustice? Yes, we did. The just soul and the just man then will live well and the unjust ill? So it appears, he said, by your reasoning.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν ὅ γε εὖ ζῶν μακάριός τε καὶ εὐδαίμων ὁ δὲ μὴ τἀναντία
greek_tokens: alla meis o ge eu zaw makarios te kai eudaimwn o de mh enantios pws gar ou o men dikaios ara eudaimwn o de adikos aqlios eimi fhmi alla meis aqlios ge eimi ou lusitelew eudaimwn de pws gar ou oudepote ara eimi makarios qrasumaxos lusitelhs adikia dikaiosunh outos dh su fhmi eimi swkraths estiaw en o bendideia upo sew ge eimi de egw eimi qrasumaxos epei egw praos gignomai kai xalepainw epauw ou mentoi kalos ge
english_head: But furthermore, he who lives well is blessed and happy, and he who does not the contrary. Of course. Then the just is happy and the unjust miserable. So be it, he said. But it surely does not pay to be miserable, but to be happy. Of course not. Never, then, most worshipful Thrasymachus, can injustice be more profitable than justice. Let this complete your entertainment, Socrates, at the festival of Bendis. A feast furnished by you, Thrasymachus, I said, now that you have become gentle with me and are no longer angry. I have not dined well, however—

greek_head: εἱστίαμαι δι' ἐμαυτὸν ἀλλ' οὐ διὰ σέ ἀλλ' ὥσπερ οἱ λίχνοι τοῦ ἀεὶ παραφερομένου ἀπογεύονται ἁρπάζοντες πρὶν
greek_tokens: estiaw dia emautou alla ou dia sos alla wsper e lixnos o aei paraferw apogeuw arpazw prin o proteros metrios apolauw kai egw egw dokazw outws prin o o proteros skopaw euriskw o dikaios ostis pote eimi afihmi ekeinos ormaw epi o skeptomai peri autos eite kakia eimi kai amaqia eite sofia kai areth kai empitnw au usteron logos ostis lusitelhs o adikia o dikaiosunh ou apexw o mh ou epi outos erxomai apo ekeinos wste egw nuni gignomai ek o dialogos mhdeis
english_head: by my own fault, not yours. But just as gluttons snatch at every dish that is handed along and taste it before they have properly enjoyed the preceding, so I, methinks, before finding the first object of our inquiry—what justice is—let go of that and set out to consider something about it, namely whether it is vice and ignorance or wisdom and virtue; and again, when later the view was sprung upon us that injustice is more profitable than justice I could not refrain from turning to that from the other topic. So that for me

greek_head: εἰδέναι ὁπότε γὰρ τὸ δίκαιον μὴ οἶδα ὅ ἐστιν σχολῇ εἴσομαι εἴτε ἀρετή τις οὖσα τυγχάνει εἴτε καὶ οὔ καὶ πότερον ὁ ἔχων
greek_tokens: oida opote gar o dikaios mh oida o eimi sxolazw eimi eite areth tis eimi tugxanw eite kai ou kai poteros o exw autos ou eudaimwn eimi h eudaimwn
english_head: the present outcome of the discussion is that I know nothing. For if I don’t know what the just is, I shall hardly know whether it is a virtue or not, and whether its possessor is or is not happy.

greek_head: Ἐγὼ μὲν οὖν ταῦτα εἰπὼν ᾤμην λόγου ἀπηλλάχθαι τὸ δ' ἦν ἄρα ὡς ἔοικε προοίμιον ὁ γὰρ Γλαύκων ἀεί τε δὴ
greek_tokens: egw men oun outos eipon oiomai logos apallassw o de eimi ara ws eoika prooimion o gar glauc aei te dh andreios eimi tugxanw pros apas kai dh kai tote o qrasumaxos o aporrhsis ou apodexomai alla fhmi eimi swkraths poteros egw boulomai dokew peiqw h ws
english_head: When I had said this I supposed that I was done with the subject, but it all turned out to be only a prelude. For Glaucon, who is always an intrepid enterprising spirit in everything, would not on this occasion acquiesce in Thrasymachus’s abandonment of his case, but said, Socrates, is it your desire to seem to have persuaded us

greek_head: ἀληθῶς πεῖσαι ὅτι παντὶ τρόπῳ ἄμεινόν ἐστιν δίκαιον εἶναι ἢ ἄδικον
greek_tokens: alhqhs peiqw ostis pas tropos ameinwn eimi dikaios eimi h adikos ws alhqhs eipon egw an airew ei epi egw eaw ou toinun fhmi poiew o boulomai legos gar egw ara su dokew toiosde tis eimi agaqos o dexomai an exw ou o apobainw efihmi alla autos eautou eneka aspazomai oios o xairw kai o hdona osos ablabhs kai mhdeis eis o epeita xronos dia outos gignomai allos h xairw exw egw eimi de egw dokew tis eimi toioutos
english_head: or really to persuade us that it is without exception better to be just than unjust? Really, I said, if the choice rested with me. Well, then, you are not doing what you wish. For tell me: do you agree that there is a kind of good which we would choose to possess, not from desire for its after effects, but welcoming it for its own sake? As, for example, joy and such pleasures are harmless and nothing results from them afterwards save to have and to hold the enjoyment.

greek_head: Τί δέ ὃ αὐτό τε αὑτοῦ χάριν ἀγαπῶμεν καὶ τῶν ἀπ' αὐτοῦ γιγνομένων οἷον αὖ τὸ φρονεῖν καὶ τὸ ὁρᾶν καὶ τὸ
greek_tokens: tis de o autos te eautou xaris agapaw kai o apo autos gignomai oios au o fronew kai o oraw kai o ugiainw o gar toioutos pou dia amfoteros aspazomai nai eipon tritos de oraw tis fhmi eidos agaqos en os o gumnazw kai o kamnw iatreuw kai iatreusis te kai o allos xrhmatismos outos gar epiponos fhmi an wfelew de egw kai autos men eautou eneka ou an dexomai exw
english_head: I recognise that kind, said I. And again a kind that we love both for its own sake and for its consequences, such as understanding, sight, and health? For these presume we welcome for both reasons. Yes, I said. And can you discern a third form of good under which falls exercise and being healed when sick and the art of healing and the making of money generally? For of them we would say that they are laborious and painful yet beneficial, and for their own sake

greek_head: τῶν δὲ μισθῶν τε χάριν καὶ τῶν ἄλλων ὅσα γίγνεται ἀπ' αὐτῶν
greek_tokens: o de misqos te xaris kai o allos osos gignomai apo autos eimi gar oun fhmi kai outos tritos alla tis dh en poios fhmi outos o dikaiosunh tiqhmi
english_head: we would not accept them, but only for the rewards and other benefits that accrue from them. Why yes, I said, I must admit this third class also. But what of it? In which of these classes do you place justice? he said.

greek_head: Ἐγὼ μὲν οἶμαι ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἐν τῷ καλλίστῳ ὃ καὶ δι' αὑτὸ καὶ διὰ τὰ γιγνόμενα ἀπ' αὐτοῦ ἀγαπητέον τῷ μέλλοντι
greek_tokens: egw men oimh eimi de egw en o kalos o kai dia eautou kai dia o gignomai apo autos agaphteos o mellw makarios eimi ou toinun dokew fhmi o polus alla o epiponos eidos o misqos su eneka kai eudokimhsis dia doca epithdeuteos autos de dia eautou feukteos ws eimi xalepos oida eimi de egw ostis dokew outws kai pala upo qrasumaxos ws toioutos eimi yegw adikia de epainew alla egw tis ws eoika dusmaqhs
english_head: In my opinion, I said, it belongs in the fairest class, that which a man who is to be happy must love both for its own sake and for the results. Yet the multitude, he said, do not think so, but that it belongs to the toilsome class of things that must be practised for the sake of rewards and repute due to opinion but that in itself is to be shunned as an affliction. I am aware, said I, that that is the general opinion and Thrasymachus has for some time been disparaging it as such and praising injustice. But I, it seems, am somewhat slow to learn. Come now,

greek_head: Ἴθι δή ἔφη ἄκουσον καὶ ἐμοῦ ἐάν σοι ἔτι ταὐτὰ δοκῇ Θρασύμαχος γάρ μοι φαίνεται πρῳαίτερον τοῦ δέοντος ὑπὸ
greek_tokens: eimi dh fhmi akouw kai egw ean su eti tautos dokazw qrasumaxos gar egw fainw prwi o deon upo sew wsper ofis khlew egw de oupw kata new o apodeicis gignomai peri ekateros epiqumew gar akew tis su eimi ekateros kai tis exis dunamis autos kaqa eautou eneimi en o yuxw o de misqos kai o gignomai apo autos eaw xairw outwsi oun poiew ean kai sos dokazw epananeoomai
english_head: he said, hear what I too have to say and see if you agree with me. For Thrasymachus seems to me to have given up to you too soon, as if he were a serpent that you had charmed, but I am not yet satisfied with the proof that has been offered about justice and injustice. For what I desire is to hear what each of them is and what potency and effect it has in and of itself dwelling in the soul, but to dismiss their rewards and consequences. This, then, is what I propose to do, with your concurrence. I will renew

greek_head: τὸν Θρασυμάχου λόγον καὶ πρῶτον μὲν ἐρῶ δικαιοσύνην οἷον εἶναί φασιν καὶ ὅθεν γεγονέναι δεύτερον δὲ ὅτι
greek_tokens: o qrasumaxos logos kai proteros men eraomai dikaiosunh oios eimi fhmi kai oqen gignomai deuteros de ostis pas autos e epithdeuw akwn epithdeuw ws anagkaion alla ou ws agaqos tritos de ostis eoikotws autos draw polus gar ameinwn ara o o adikos h o o dikaios bios ws legw epei egw eimi swkraths ou tis dokew outws aforaw mentoi diaqrulew o ous akouw qrasumaxos kai murw allos o de uper o dikaiosunh logos ws
english_head: the argument of Thrasymachus and will first state what men say is the nature and origin of justice; secondly, that all who practise it do so reluctantly, regarding it as something necessary and not as a good; and thirdly, that they have plausible grounds for thus acting, since forsooth the life of the unjust man is far better than that of the just man—as they say; though I, Socrates, don’t believe it. Yet I am disconcerted when my ears are dinned by the arguments of Thrasymachus and innumerable others. But the case for justice,

greek_head: ἄμεινον ἀδικίας οὐδενός πω ἀκήκοα ὡς βούλομαι βούλομαι δὲ αὐτὸ καθ' αὑτὸ ἐγκωμιαζόμενον ἀκοῦσαι μάλιστα δ'
greek_tokens: ameinwn adikia oudeis pw akouw ws boulomai boulomai de autos kaqa eautou egkwmiazw akew malista de oimh an sew punqanomai dio katateinw eraomai o adikos bios epainew eipon de endeiknumi su os tropos au boulomai kai sew akouw adikia men yegw dikaiosunh de epainew alla oraw ei su boulomai o legos pas malista eimi de egw peri gar tis an mallon pollakis tis new exw xairw legos kai akouw
english_head: to prove that it is better than injustice, I have never yet heard stated by any as I desire to hear it. What I desire is to hear an encomium on justice in and by itself. And I think I am most likely to get that from you. For which reason I will lay myself out in praise of the life of injustice, and in so speaking will give you an example of the manner in which I desire to hear from you in turn the dispraise of injustice and the praise of justice. Consider whether my proposal pleases you. Nothing could please me more, said I;

greek_head: Κάλλιστα ἔφη λέγεις καὶ ὃ πρῶτον ἔφην ἐρεῖν περὶ τούτου ἄκουε τί ὄν τε καὶ ὅθεν γέγονε δικαιοσύνη
greek_tokens: kallista fhmi legw kai o proteros fhmi erew peri outos akouw tis eimi te kai oqen gignomai dikaiosunh fuw gar dh fhmi o men adikew agaqos o de adikew kakos pleiwn de kakazw uperballw o adikew h agaqos o adikew wste epeidan allhlwn adikew te kai adikew kai amfoteros geuw o mh dunamai o
english_head: for on what subject would a man of sense rather delight to hold and hear discourse again and again? That is excellent, he said; and now listen to what I said would be the first topic—the nature and origin of justice. By nature, they say, to commit injustice is a good and to suffer it is an evil, but that the excess of evil in being wronged is greater than the excess of good in doing wrong. So that when men do wrong and are wronged by one another and taste of both, those who lack the power

greek_head: μὲν ἐκφεύγειν τὸ δὲ αἱρεῖν δοκεῖ λυσιτελεῖν συνθέσθαι ἀλλήλοις μήτ' ἀδικεῖν μήτ' ἀδικεῖσθαι καὶ ἐντεῦθεν δὴ
greek_tokens: men ekfeugw o de airew dokew lusitelew suntiqhmi allhlwn mhte adikew mhte adikew kai enteuqen dh arxw nomos tiqhmi kai sunqhkh eautou kai onomazw o upo o nomos epitagma nomimos te kai dikaios kai eimi dh outos genesis te kai ousia dikaiosunh metacu eimi o men ariston eimi ean adikew mh didwmi dikh o de kakos ean adikew timwrew adunatos eimi o de dikaios en mesos eimi outos amfoteros agapaw ou ws
english_head: to avoid the one and take the other determine that it is for their profit to make a compact with one another neither to commit nor to suffer injustice; and that this is the beginning of legislation and covenants between men, and that they name the commandment of the law the lawful and the just, and that this is the genesis and essential nature of justice—a compromise between the best, which is to do wrong with impunity, and the worst, which is to be wronged and be impotent to get one’s revenge. Justice, they tell us, being mid-way between the two, is accepted and approved,

greek_head: ἀγαθόν ἀλλ' ὡς ἀρρωστίᾳ τοῦ ἀδικεῖν τιμώμενον ἐπεὶ τὸν δυνάμενον αὐτὸ ποιεῖν καὶ ὡς ἀληθῶς ἄνδρα οὐδ' ἂν ἑνί ποτε
greek_tokens: agaqos alla ws arrwstia o adikew timaw epei o dunamai autos poiew kai ws alhqhs anhr oude an eis pote suntiqhmi o mhte adikew mhte adikew mainomai gar an o men oun dh fusis dikaiosunh eimi swkraths outos te kai toioutos kai ek os fuw toioutos ws o logos ws de kai e epithdeuw adunamia o adikew akwn autos epithdeuw malista an aisqanomai ei toiosde poiew
english_head: not as a real good, but as a thing honored in the lack of vigor to do injustice, since anyone who had the power to do it and was in reality a man would never make a compact with anybody either to wrong nor to be wronged; for he would be mad. The nature, then, of justice is this and such as this, Socrates, and such are the conditions in which it originates, according to the theory. But as for the second point, that those who practise it do so unwillingly and from want of power to commit injustice—we shall be most likely to apprehend that if we entertain some such supposition as this in thought:

greek_head: τῇ διανοίᾳ δόντες ἐξουσίαν ἑκατέρῳ ποιεῖν ὅτι ἂν βούληται τῷ τε δικαίῳ καὶ τῷ ἀδίκῳ εἶτ' ἐπακολουθήσαιμεν
greek_tokens: o dianoia didwmi ecousia ekateros poiew ostis an boulomai o te dikaios kai o adikos eimi epakolouqew qeaomai poi o epiqumia ekateros agnumi epi autofwros oun lambanw an o dikaios o adikos eis tautos eimi dia o pleonecia o pas fusis diwkw fuw ws agaqos nomos de bia paragw epi o o isos timh eaw de an o ecousia os legos toiosde malista ei autos gignomai oios
english_head: if we grant to each, the just and the unjust, licence and power to do whatever he pleases, and then accompany them in imagination and see whither his desire will conduct each. We should then catch the just man in the very act of resorting to the same conduct as the unjust man because of the self-advantage which every creature by its nature pursues as a good, while by the convention of law it is forcibly diverted to paying honor to equality. The licence that I mean would be most nearly such as would result from supposing them to have the power

greek_head: ποτέ φασιν δύναμιν τῷ Γύγου τοῦ Λυδοῦ προγόνῳ γενέσθαι εἶναι μὲν γὰρ αὐτὸν ποιμένα θητεύοντα παρὰ τῷ τότε Λυδίας
greek_tokens: pote fhmi dunamis o gughs o ludos progonos gignomai eimi men gar autos poimhn qhteuw para o tote ludios arxw ombros de polus gignomai kai seismos arassw tis o gaia kai gignomai xasma kata o topos h enihmi eidon de kai qaumazw kaqaptw kai eidon allos te dh o muqologew qaumasths kai ippos xalkeos koilos quris exw kaqa os egkuptw eidon eneimi nekros ws fainw megas h kata anqrwpos outos de allos men oudeis peri de
english_head: which men say once came to the ancestor of Gyges the Lydian. They relate that he was a shepherd in the service of the ruler at that time of Lydia, and that after a great deluge of rain and an earthquake the ground opened and a chasm appeared in the place where he was pasturing; and they say that he saw and wondered and went down into the chasm; and the story goes that he beheld other marvels there and a hollow bronze horse with little doors, and that he peeped in and saw a corpse within, as it seemed, of more than mortal stature,

greek_head: τῇ χειρὶ χρυσοῦν δακτύλιον ὄντα περιελόμενον ἐκβῆναι συλλόγου δὲ γενομένου τοῖς ποιμέσιν εἰωθότος ἵν' ἐξαγγέλλοιεν
greek_tokens: o xeir xruseos daktulion eimi periairew ekbainw sullogos de gignomai o poimhn eqw ina ecaggellw kata mainomai o basileus o peri o poimnion afikneomai kai ekeinos exw o daktulion kaqhmai oun meta o allos tugxanw o sfendonh o daktulion periagw pros eautou eis o eidomai o xeir outos de gignomai
english_head: and that there was nothing else but a gold ring on its hand, which he took off and went forth. And when the shepherds held their customary assembly to make their monthly report to the king about the flocks, he also attended wearing the ring. So as he sat there it chanced that he turned the collet of the ring towards himself, towards the inner part of his hand, and when this took place they say that he became invisible

greek_head: ἀφανῆ αὐτὸν γενέσθαι τοῖς παρακαθημένοις καὶ διαλέγεσθαι ὡς περὶ οἰχομένου καὶ τὸν θαυμάζειν τε καὶ πάλιν ἐπιψηλαφῶντα
greek_tokens: afanhs autos gignomai o parakaqhmai kai dialegw ws peri oixomai kai o qaumazw te kai palin epiyhlafaw o daktulion strefw ecw o sfendonh kai strefw faneros gignomai kai outos ennoew apopeiraomai o daktulion ei outos exw o dunamis kai autos outws sumbainw strefw men eidomai o sfendonh adhlos gignomai ecw de dhlos aisqanomai de euqus diaprassw o aggelos gignomai o para o basileus erxomai
english_head: to those who sat by him and they spoke of him as absent and that he was amazed, and again fumbling with the ring turned the collet outwards and so became visible. On noting this he experimented with the ring to see if it possessed this virtue, and he found the result to be that when he turned the collet inwards he became invisible, and when outwards visible; and becoming aware of this, he immediately managed things so that he became one of the messengers

greek_head: δὲ καὶ τὴν γυναῖκα αὐτοῦ μοιχεύσαντα μετ' ἐκείνης ἐπιθέμενον τῷ βασιλεῖ ἀποκτεῖναι καὶ τὴν ἀρχὴν οὕτω κατασχεῖν
greek_tokens: de kai o gunh autos moixeuw meta ekeinos epitiqhmi o basileus apokteinw kai o arxh outws katasxaw ei oun duo toioutos daktulion genoisqhn kai o men o dikaios periqew o de o adikos oudeis an gignomai ws dokew outws adamantinos os an menw en o dikaiosunh kai tolmaw apexw o allotrios kai mh aptw ecesti autos kai ek o agora adehs ostis boulomai lambanw
english_head: who went up to the king, and on coming there he seduced the king’s wife and with her aid set upon the king and slew him and possessed his kingdom. If now there should be two such rings, and the just man should put on one and the unjust the other, no one could be found, it would seem, of such adamantine temper as to persevere in justice and endure to refrain his hands from the possessions of others and not touch them, though he might with impunity take what he wished even from the marketplace,

greek_head: καὶ εἰσιόντι εἰς τὰς οἰκίας συγγίγνεσθαι ὅτῳ βούλοιτο καὶ ἀποκτεινύναι καὶ ἐκ δεσμῶν λύειν οὕστινας βούλοιτο καὶ
greek_tokens: kai eiseimi eis o oikia suggignomai ostis boulomai kai apokteinw kai ek desmh luw ostis boulomai kai allos prassw en o anqrwpos isoqeos eimi outws de draw oudeis an diaforos o eteros poiew alla epi tautos an eimi amfoteros kaitoi megas outos tekmhrion an fhmi tis ostis oudeis ekwn dikaios alla anagkazw ws ou agaqos idios eimi epei opou ge an oiomai ekastos oios te eimi adikew adikew lusitelew gar dh oiomai pas anhr polus
english_head: and enter into houses and lie with whom he pleased, and slay and loose from bonds whomsoever he would, and in all other things conduct himself among mankind as the equal of a god. And in so acting he would do no differently from the other man, but both would pursue the same course. And yet this is a great proof, one might argue, that no one is just of his own will but only from constraint, in the belief that justice is not his personal good, inasmuch as every man, when he supposes himself to have the power to do wrong, does wrong.

greek_head: μᾶλλον ἰδίᾳ τὴν ἀδικίαν τῆς δικαιοσύνης ἀληθῆ οἰόμενος ὡς φήσει ὁ περὶ τοῦ τοιούτου λόγου λέγων ἐπεὶ εἴ τις
greek_tokens: mallon idios o adikia o dikaiosunh alhqhs oiomai ws fhmi o peri o toioutos logos legos epei ei tis toioutos ecousia epilambanw mhdeis pote eqelw adikew mhde aptw o allotrios aqlios men an dokew eimi o aisqanomai kai anohtos epainew de an autos allhlwn enantion ecapataw allhlwn dia o o adikew fobos outos men oun dh outws
english_head: For that there is far more profit for him personally in injustice than in justice is what every man believes, and believes truly, as the proponent of this theory will maintain. For if anyone who had got such a licence within his grasp should refuse to do any wrong or lay his hands on others’ possessions, he would be regarded as most pitiable and a great fool by all who took note of it, though they would praise him before one another’s faces, deceiving one another because of their fear of suffering injustice. So much for this point.

greek_head: Τὴν δὲ κρίσιν αὐτὴν τοῦ βίου πέρι ὧν λέγομεν ἐὰν διαστησώμεθα τόν τε δικαιότατον καὶ τὸν ἀδικώτατον οἷοί τ'
greek_tokens: o de krisis autos o bios peri os legw ean diisthmi o te dikaios kai o adikos oios su eimi krinw orqos ei de mh ou tis oun dh o diastasis ode mhdeis afairew mhte o adikos apo o adikia mhte o dikaios apo o dikaiosunh alla teleios ekateros eis o eautou epithdeuma tiqhmi proteros men oun o adikos wsper e deinos dhmiourgos poiew oios kubernhths akros h iatros o te adunatos en o texnh kai o dunatos diaisqanomai
english_head: But to come now to the decision between our two kinds of life, if we separate the most completely just and the most completely unjust man, we shall be able to decide rightly, but if not, not. How, then, is this separation to be made? Thus: we must subtract nothing of his injustice from the unjust man or of his justice from the just, but assume the perfection of each in his own mode of conduct. In the first place, the unjust man must act as clever craftsmen do: a first-rate pilot or physician, for example, feels the difference between impossibilities and possibilities in his art

greek_head: καὶ τοῖς μὲν ἐπιχειρεῖ τὰ δὲ ἐᾷ ἔτι δὲ ἐὰν ἄρα πῃ σφαλῇ ἱκανὸς ἐπανορθοῦσθαι οὕτω καὶ ὁ ἄδικος ἐπιχειρῶν
greek_tokens: kai o men epixeirew o de eaw eti de ean ara ph sfallw ikanos epanorqow outws kai o adikos epixeirew orqos o adikhma lanqanw ei mellw sfodra adikos eimi o aliskomai de faulos hghteon esxatos gar adikia dokew dikaios eimi mh eimi doteos oun o teleios adikos o teleios adikia kai ou afaireteos alla eateos o megas adikew o megas doca eautou
english_head: and attempts the one and lets the others go; and then, too, if he does happen to trip, he is equal to correcting his error. Similarly, the unjust man who attempts injustice rightly must be supposed to escape detection if he is to be altogether unjust, and we must regard the man who is caught as a bungler. For the height of injustice is to seem just without being so. To the perfectly unjust man, then, we must assign perfect injustice and withhold nothing of it, but we must allow him, while committing the greatest wrongs, to have secured for himself the greatest reputation for justice;

english_head: and if he does happen to trip, we must concede to him the power to correct his mistakes by his ability to speak persuasively if any of his misdeeds come to light, and when force is needed, to employ force by reason of his manly spirit and vigor and his provision of friends and money; and when we have set up an unjust man of this character, our theory must set the just man at his side—a simple and noble man, who, in the phrase of Aeschylus, does not wish to seem but be good. Then we must deprive him of the seeming. For if he is going to be thought just

english_head: he will have honors and gifts because of that esteem. We cannot be sure in that case whether he is just for justice’s sake or for the sake of the gifts and the honors. So we must strip him bare of everything but justice and make his state the opposite of his imagined counterpart. Though doing no wrong he must have the repute of the greatest injustice, so that he may be put to the test as regards justice through not softening because of ill repute and the consequences thereof. But let him hold on his course unchangeable even unto death,

english_head: seeming all his life to be unjust though being just, that so, both men attaining to the limit, the one of injustice, the other of justice, we may pass judgement which of the two is the happier. Bless me, my dear Glaucon, said I, how strenuously you polish off each of your two men for the competition for the prize as if it were a statue. To the best of my ability, he replied, and if such is the nature of the two, it becomes an easy matter, I fancy, to unfold the tale of the sort of life that awaits each.

english_head: We must tell it, then; and even if my language is somewhat rude and brutal, you must not suppose, Socrates, that it is I who speak thus, but those who commend injustice above justice. What they will say is this: that such being his disposition the just man will have to endure the lash, the rack, chains,

For it is literally true, they will say, that the unjust man, as pursuing what clings closely to reality, to truth, and not regulating his life by opinion, desires not to seem but to be unjust, Exploiting the deep furrows of his wit

The consequences of my being just are, unless I likewise seem so, not assets, they say, but liabilities, labor and total loss; but if I am unjust and have procured myself a reputation for justice a godlike life is promised. english_head: we shall reply. But all the same if we expect to be happy, we must pursue the path to which the footprints of our arguments point. For with a view to lying hid we will organize societies and political clubs, and there are teachers of cajolery who impart the arts of the popular assembly and the court-room. So that, partly by persuasion, partly by force, we shall contrive to overreach with impunity. But against the gods, it may be said, neither secrecy nor force can avail.
