english_head: neither need we concern ourselves with eluding their observation. If they do exist and pay heed, we know and hear of them only from such discourses and from the poets who have described their pedigrees. But these same authorities tell us that the gods are capable of being persuaded and swerved from their course by sacrifice and soothing vows and dedications. We must believe them in both or neither. And if we are to believe them, the thing to do is to commit injustice and offer sacrifice

greek_head: ἀπὸ τῶν ἀδικημάτων δίκαιοι μὲν γὰρ ὄντες ἀζήμιοι μόνον ὑπὸ θεῶν ἐσόμεθα τὰ δ' ἐξ ἀδικίας κέρδη ἀπωσόμεθα ἄδικοι
greek_tokens: apo o adikhma dikaios men gar eimi azhmios monos upo qea eimi o de ek adikia kerdos apwqew adikos de kerdainw te kai lissomai uperbainw kai amartanw peiqw autos azhmios apallassw alla gar en adhs dikh didwmi os an enqade adikew h autos h pais pais alla eimi filos fhmi logizomai o teleth au megas dunamai kai e lusios qeos ws o
english_head: from fruits of our wrongdoing. For if we are just, we shall, it is true, be unscathed by the gods, but we shall be putting away from us the profits of injustice; but if we are unjust, we shall win those profits, and, by the importunity of our prayers, when we transgress and sin, we shall persuade them and escape scot-free. Yes, it will be objected, but we shall be brought to judgement in the world below for our unjust deeds here, we or our children’s children. Nay, my dear sir, our calculating friend will say, here again the rites for the dead have much efficacy, and the absolving divinities,

greek_head: μέγισται πόλεις λέγουσι καὶ οἱ θεῶν παῖδες ποιηταὶ καὶ προφῆται τῶν θεῶν γενόμενοι οἳ ταῦτα οὕτως ἔχειν μηνύουσιν
greek_tokens: megas polis legw kai e qea pais poihths kai profaw o qea gignomai e outos outws exw mhnuw kata tis oun eti logos dikaiosunh an pro megas adikia airew an os ean meta eusxhmosunh kibdhlos ktaomai kai para qeaw kai para anqrwpos prassw kata new zaw te kai teleutaw ws o o polus te kai akron legw logos ek dh pas o erw
english_head: as the greatest cities declare, and the sons of gods, who became the poets and prophets of the gods, and who reveal that this is the truth. On what further ground, then, could we prefer justice to supreme injustice? If we combine this with a counterfeit decorum, we shall prosper to our heart’s desire, with gods and men in life and death, as the words of the multitude and of men of the highest authority declare. In consequence, then, of all that has been said, what possibility is there, Socrates, that any man

greek_head: τίς μηχανή ὦ Σώκρατες δικαιοσύνην τιμᾶν ἐθέλειν ᾧ τις δύναμις ὑπάρχει ψυχῆς ἢ χρημάτων ἢ σώματος ἢ
greek_tokens: tis mhxanh eimi swkraths dikaiosunh timaw eqelw os tis dunamis uparxw yuxazw h xrhma h swma h genos alla mh gelaw epainew akouw ws dh su ei tis exis yeudhs men apofainw o erw ikanos de gignwskw ostis ariston dikaiosunh polus pou suggnwmh exis kai ou orgizw o adikos alla oida ostis plhn ei tis qeia fusis dusxerainw o adikew h episthmh lambanw
english_head: who has the power of any resources of mind, money, body, or family should consent to honor justice and not rather laugh when he hears her praised? In sooth, if anyone is able to show the falsity of these arguments, and has come to know with sufficient assurance that justice is best, he feels much indulgence for the unjust, and is not angry with them, but is aware that except a man by inborn divinity of his nature disdains injustice, or, having won to knowledge, refrains from it,

greek_head: ἀπέχεται αὐτοῦ τῶν γε ἄλλων οὐδεὶς ἑκὼν δίκαιος ἀλλ' ὑπὸ ἀνανδρίας ἢ γήρως ἤ τινος ἄλλης ἀσθενείας ψέγει τὸ
greek_tokens: apexw autos o ge allos oudeis ekwn dikaios alla upo anandria h ghras h tis allos asqeneia yegw o adikew adunatew autos draw ws de dhlos o gar proteros o toioutos eis dunamis erxomai proteros adikew kaqa osos an oios su eimi kai outos apas oudeis allos aitios h ekeinos oqen apas o logos outos ormaw kai ode kai egw pros sos eimi swkraths eipon ostis eimi qaumasios pas
english_head: no one else is willingly just, but that it is from lack of manly spirit or from old age or some other weakness that men dispraise injustice, lacking the power to practise it. The fact is patent. For no sooner does such one come into the power than he works injustice to the extent of his ability. And the sole cause of all this is the fact that was the starting-point of this entire plea of my friend here and of myself to you, Socrates, pointing out how strange it is that of all you

greek_head: ὑμῶν ὅσοι ἐπαινέται φατὲ δικαιοσύνης εἶναι ἀπὸ τῶν ἐξ ἀρχῆς ἡρώων ἀρξάμενοι ὅσων λόγοι λελειμμένοι μέχρι τῶν
greek_tokens: su osos epaineths fatos dikaiosunh eimi apo o ek arxh hrws arxw osos logos leipw mexri o nun anqrwpos oudeis pwpote yegw adikia oude epainew dikaiosunh allos h doca te kai timh kai dwrea o apo autos gignomai autos de ekateros o eautou dunamis tis draw o o exw yuxw eneimi kai lanqanw qeos te kai anqrwpos oudeis pwpote oute en poihsis oute en idios logos epecerxomai ikanos o logos ws o men megas kakh osos isxw yuxh en eautou dikaiosunh de megas agaqos
english_head: self-styled advocates of justice, from the heroes of old whose discourses survive to the men of the present day, not one has ever censured injustice or commended justice otherwise than in respect of the repute, the honors, and the gifts that accrue from each. But what each one of them is in itself, by its own inherent force, when it is within the soul of the possessor and escapes the eyes of both gods and men, no one has ever adequately set forth in poetry or prose—the proof that the one is the greatest of all evils that the soul contains within itself, while justice is the greatest good.

greek_head: εἰ γὰρ οὕτως ἐλέγετο ἐξ ἀρχῆς ὑπὸ πάντων ὑμῶν καὶ ἐκ νέων ἡμᾶς ἐπείθετε οὐκ ἂν ἀλλήλους ἐφυλάττομεν μὴ
greek_tokens: ei gar outws legw ek arxh upo pas su kai ek neos egw peiqw ou an allhlwn fulassw mh adikew alla autos eautou eimi ekastos aristos fulac deidw mh adikew o megas kakazw sunoikos eimi outos eimi swkraths isos de kai eti outos plew qrasumaxos te kai allos pou tis uper dikaiosunh te kai adikia legw an metastrefw autos o dunamis fortikos os ge egw dokew alla egw oudeis gar su
english_head: For if you had all spoken in this way from the beginning and from our youth up had sought to convince us, we should not now be guarding against one another’s injustice, but each would be his own best guardian, for fear lest by working injustice he should dwell in communion with the greatest of evils. This, Socrates, and perhaps even more than this, Thrasymachus and haply another might say in pleas for and against justice and injustice, inverting their true potencies, as I believe, grossly. But I—

greek_head: δέομαι ἀποκρύπτεσθαι σοῦ ἐπιθυμῶν ἀκοῦσαι τἀναντία ὡς δύναμαι μάλιστα κατατείνας λέγω μὴ οὖν ἡμῖν μόνον
greek_tokens: deomai apokruptw sew epiqumew akew enantios ws dunamai malista katateinw legos mh oun egw monos endeicis o logos ostis dikaiosunh adikia kreisswn alla tis poiew ekateros o exw autos dia eautou o men kakos o de agaqos eimi o de doca afairew wsper glauc diakeleuomai ei gar mh afairew ekaterwqen o alhqhs o de yeudhs prosqew ou o dikaios fhmi epainew su alla o dokew oude o adikos eimi
english_head: for I have no reason to hide anything from you—am laying myself out to the utmost on the theory, because I wish to hear its refutation from you. Do not merely show us by argument that justice is superior to injustice, but make clear to us what each in and of itself does to its possessor, whereby the one is evil and the other good. But do away with the repute of both, as Glaucon urged. For, unless you take away from either the true repute and attach to each the false, we shall say that it is not justice that you are praising but the semblance,

greek_head: ψέγειν ἀλλὰ τὸ δοκεῖν καὶ παρακελεύεσθαι ἄδικον ὄντα λανθάνειν καὶ ὁμολογεῖν Θρασυμάχῳ ὅτι τὸ μὲν δίκαιον
greek_tokens: yegw alla o dokew kai parakeleuomai adikos eimi lanqanw kai omologew qrasumaxos ostis o men dikaios allotrios agaqos sumferw o kreisswn o de adikos eautou men sumferw kai lusitelew o de hsswn asumforos epei oun omologew o megas agaqos eimi dikaiosunh o o te apobainw apo autos eneka acios ktaomai polus de mallon autos eautou oios oraw
english_head: nor injustice that you censure, but the seeming, and that you really are exhorting us to be unjust but conceal it, and that you are at one with Thrasymachus in the opinion that justice is other man’s good, the advantage of the other, and that injustice is advantageous and profitible to oneself but disadvantageous to the inferior. Since, then, you have admitted that justice belongs to the class of those highest goods which are desirable both for their consequences and still more for their own sake, as sight, hearing, intelligence, yes and health too,

greek_head: ἀκούειν φρονεῖν καὶ ὑγιαίνειν δή καὶ ὅσ' ἄλλα ἀγαθὰ γόνιμα τῇ αὑτῶν φύσει ἀλλ' οὐ δόξῃ ἐστίν τοῦτ' οὖν αὐτὸ
greek_tokens: akouw fronew kai ugiainw dh kai osos allos agaqos gonimos o eautou fusis alla ou doca eimi outos oun autos epainew dikaiosunh o autos dia eautou o exw oninhmi kai adikia blaptw misqos de kai doca parihmi allos epainew ws egw o men allos apodexomai an outws epainew dikaiosunh kai yegw adikia doca te peri autos kai misqos egkwmiazw kai loidorew sew de ou an ei mh su keleuw dioti pas o bios
english_head: and all other goods that are productive by their very nature and not by opinion, this is what I would have you praise about justice—the benefit which it and the harm which injustice inherently works upon its possessor. But the rewards and the honors that depend on opinion, leave to others to praise. For while I would listen to others who thus commended justice and disparaged injustice, bestowing their praise and their blame on the reputation and the rewards of either, I could not accept that sort of thing from you unless you say I must, because you have passed

greek_head: οὐδὲν ἄλλο σκοπῶν διελήλυθας ἢ τοῦτο μὴ οὖν ἡμῖν ἐνδείξῃ μόνον τῷ λόγῳ ὅτι δικαιοσύνη ἀδικίας κρεῖττον
greek_tokens: oudeis allos skopaw dierxomai h outos mh oun egw endeicis monos o logos ostis dikaiosunh adikia kreisswn alla kai tis poiew ekateros o exw autos dia eautou ean lanqanw ean mh qeos te kai anqrwpos o men agaqos o de kakos eimi kai egw aekwn aei men dh o fus o te glaukwn kai o adeimantos agamai atar oun kai tote panu ge
english_head: your entire life in the consideration of this very matter. Do not then, I repeat, merely prove to us in argument the superiority of justice to injustice, but show us what it is that each inherently does to its possessor—whether he does or does not escape the eyes of gods and men—whereby the one is good and the other evil. While I had always admired the natural parts of Glaucon and Adeimantus, I was especially

greek_tokens: ezomai kai eipon ou kakos eis su eimi pais ekeinos o anhr o arxh o elegeion poiew o glaukwn erasths eudokimew peri o megaroi maxh eipon pais aristwn kleinos qew genos anhr outos egw eimi filos eu dokew exw panu gar qew pasxw ei mh peiqw adikia dikaiosunh ameinwn eimi outws dunamai eipon uper autos dokew dh egw ws
english_head: pleased by their words on this occasion, and said: It was excellently spoken of you, sons of the man we know, in the beginning of the elegy which the admirer of Glaucon wrote when you distinguished yourselves in the battle of Megara —Sons of Ariston, whose race from a glorious sire is god-like. This, my friends, I think, was well said. For there must indeed be a touch of the god-like in your disposition if you are not convinced that injustice is preferable to justice though you can plead its case in such fashion.

greek_head: ἀληθῶς οὐ πεπεῖσθαι τεκμαίρομαι δὲ ἐκ τοῦ ἄλλου τοῦ ὑμετέρου τρόπου ἐπεὶ κατά γε αὐτοὺς τοὺς λόγους ἠπίστουν

english_head: And I believe that you are really not convinced. I infer this from your general character since from your words alone I should have distrusted you. But the more I trust you the more I am at a loss what to make of the matter. I do not know how I can come to the rescue. For I doubt my ability by reason that you have not accepted the arguments whereby I thought I proved against Thrasymachus that justice is better than injustice. Nor yet again do I know how I can refuse to come to the rescue. For I fear lest

greek_head: ἀπαγορεύειν καὶ μὴ βοηθεῖν ἔτι ἐμπνέοντα καὶ δυνάμενον φθέγγεσθαι κράτιστον οὖν οὕτως ὅπως δύναμαι
greek_tokens: apagoreuw kai mh bohqew eti empnew kai dunamai fqeggomai kratistos oun outws opws dunamai epiepikourew autos o te oun glauc kai e allos deomai pas tropos bohqew kai mh anihmi o logos alla diereunaw tis su eimi ekateros kai peri o wfeleia autos alhqhs poteros exis eipon oun osper egw dokew ostis o zhthma os epixeirew ou faulos alla ocus blepw ws egw
english_head: it be actually impious to stand idly by when justice is reviled and be faint-hearted and not defend her so long as one has breath and can utter his voice. The best thing, then, is to aid her as best I can. Glaucon, then, and the rest besought me by all means to come to the rescue and not to drop the argument but to pursue to the end the investigation as to the nature of each and the truth about their respective advantages. I said then as I thought: The inquiry we are undertaking is no easy one but

greek_head: φαίνεται ἐπειδὴ οὖν ἡμεῖς οὐ δεινοί δοκῶ μοι ἦν δ' ἐγώ τοιαύτην ποιήσασθαι ζήτησιν αὐτοῦ οἵανπερ ἂν εἰ
greek_tokens: fainw epei oun egw ou deinos dokazw egw eimi de egw toioutos poiew zhthsis autos oios an ei prostassw tis gramma mikros proswqen anagignwskw mh panu ocus blepw epeita tis ennoew ostis o autos gramma eimi pou kai alloqi megas te kai en megas ermaion an fainw oimh ekeinos proteros anagignwskw outws episkopew o elasswn ei o autos eimi tugxanw panu men oun fhmi o adeimantos alla tis toioutos eimi
english_head: calls for keen vision, as it seems to me. So, since we are not clever persons, I think we should employ the method of search that we should use if we, with not very keen vision, were bidden to read small letters from a distance, and then someone had observed that these same letters exist elsewhere larger and on a larger surface. We should have accounted it a godsend, I fancy, to be allowed to read those letters first, and examine the smaller, if they are the same. Quite so, said Adeimantus;

english_head: but what analogy to do you detect in the inquiry about justice? I will tell you, I said: there is a justice of one man, we say, and, I suppose, also of an entire city. Assuredly, said he. Is not the city larger than the man? It is larger, he said. Then, perhaps, there would be more justice in the larger object and more easy to apprehend. If it please you, then,

greek_head: πόλεσι ζητήσωμεν ποῖόν τί ἐστιν ἔπειτα οὕτως ἐπισκεψώμεθα καὶ ἐν ἑνὶ ἑκάστῳ τὴν τοῦ μείζονος ὁμοιότητα ἐν τῇ

english_head: let us first look for its quality in states, and then only examine it also in the individual, looking for the likeness of the greater in the form of the less. I think that is a good suggestion, he said. If, then, said I, our argument should observe the origin of a state, we should see also the origin of justice and injustice in it. It may be, said he. And if this is done, we may expect to find more easily what we are seeking?

greek_head: Πολύ γε Δοκεῖ οὖν χρῆναι ἐπιχειρῆσαι περαίνειν οἶμαι μὲν γὰρ
greek_tokens: polus ge dokew oun xrh epixeirew perainw oimh men gar ou oligos ergnumi autos eimi skopaw oun skeptomai fhmi o adeimantos alla mh allos poiew gignomai toinun eimi de egw polis ws oimh epei tugxanw egw ekastos ou autarkhs alla polus eimi endehs h tinw oiomai arxh allos polis oikizw oudeis eimi de os
english_head: Much more. Shall we try it, then, and go through with it? I fancy it is no slight task. Reflect, then. We have reflected, said Adeimantus; proceed and don’t refuse. The origin of the city, then, said I, in my opinion, is to be found in the fact that we do not severally suffice for our own needs, but each of us lacks many things. Do you think any other principle establishes the state? No other, said he. As a result of this,

greek_head: Οὕτω δὴ ἄρα παραλαμβάνων ἄλλος ἄλλον ἐπ' ἄλλου τὸν δ' ἐπ' ἄλλου χρείᾳ πολλῶν δεόμενοι πολλοὺς εἰς μίαν
greek_tokens: outws dh ara paralambanw allos allos epi allos o de epi allos xreia polus deomai polus eis eis eoika ageirw koinwnos te kai bohqos outos o sunoikia tiqhmi polis onoma eimi gar panu men oun metadidwmi dh allos allos ei tis metadidwmi h metalambanw oiomai eautou ameinwn eimi panu ge eimi dh eimi de egw o logos ek arxh poiew polis poihsis de autos ws eoika o hmeteros xreos pws de ou
english_head: then, one man calling in another for one service and another for another, we, being in need of many things, gather many into one place of abode as associates and helpers, and to this dwelling together we give the name city or state, do we not? By all means. And between one man and another there is an interchange of giving, if it so happens, and taking, because each supposes this to be better for himself. Certainly. Come, then, let us create a city from the beginning, in our theory. Its real creator, as it appears, will be our needs. Obviously.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν πρώτη γε καὶ μεγίστη τῶν χρειῶν ἡ τῆς τροφῆς παρασκευὴ τοῦ εἶναί τε καὶ ζῆν ἕνεκα
greek_tokens: alla meis proteros ge kai megas o xreos o o trefw paraskeuh o eimi te kai zaw eneka pantapasi ge deuteros dh oikhsis tritos de esqhs kai o toioutos eimi outos ferw dh eimi de egw pws o polis arkesis epi tosoutos paraskeuh allos tis gewrgos men eis o de oikodomos allos de tis ufanths h kai skutotomos autose prosqew h tis allos o peri o swma qerapeuths panu ge eaw de an o ge anagkaios polis ek tessares h pente androw
english_head: Now the first and chief of our needs is the provision of food for existence and life.Assuredly. The second is housing and the third is raiment and that sort of thing. That is so. Tell me, then, said I, how our city will suffice for the provision of all these things. Will there not be a farmer for one, and a builder, and then again a weaver? And shall we add thereto a cobbler and some other purveyor for the needs of body? Certainly. The indispensable minimum of a city, then, would consist of four or

greek_head: Φαίνεται Τί δὴ οὖν ἕνα ἕκαστον τούτων δεῖ τὸ αὑτοῦ ἔργον
greek_tokens: fainw tis dh oun enos ekastos outos deomai o eautou ergnumi apas koinos katatiqhmi oios o gewrgos enos eimi paraskeuazw sition tessares kai tetraplasios xronos te kai ponos analiskw epi sitos paraskeuazw kai allos koinwnew h amelew eautou monos tetartos meros poiew outos o
english_head: five men. Apparently. What of this, then? Shall each of these contribute his work for the common use of all? I mean shall the farmer, who is one, provide food for four and spend fourfold time and toil on the production of food and share it with the others, or shall he take no thought for them and provide a fourth portion

greek_head: σίτου ἐν τετάρτῳ μέρει τοῦ χρόνου τὰ δὲ τρία τὸ μὲν ἐπὶ τῇ τῆς οἰκίας παρασκευῇ διατρίβειν τὸ δὲ ἱματίου τὸ δὲ
greek_tokens: sitos en tetartos meros o xronos o de treis o men epi o o oikia paraskeuazw diatribw o de imation o de upodhma kai mh allos koinwnew pragma exw alla autos dia eautou o eautou prassw kai o adeimantos fhmi alla isos eimi swkraths outws raios h ekeinos oudeis eimi de egw ma dios atopos ennoew gar kai autos eipon sew ostis proteros men egw fuw ekastos ou panu
english_head: of the food for himself alone in a quarter of the time and employ the other three-quarters, the one in the provision of a house, the other of a garment, the other of shoes, and not have the bother of associating with other people, but, himself for himself, mind his own affairs? And Adeimantus said, But, perhaps, Socrates, the former way is easier. It would not, by Zeus, be at all strange, said I; for now that you have mentioned it, it occurs to me myself that, to begin with, our several natures are not

greek_head: ὅμοιος ἑκάστῳ ἀλλὰ διαφέρων τὴν φύσιν ἄλλος ἐπ' ἄλλου ἔργου πράξει ἢ οὐ δοκεῖ σοι
greek_tokens: omoios ekastos alla diaferw o fus allos epi allos ergnumi prassw h ou dokew su egw tis de poteros kallion prassw an tis eis eimi polus texnh ergazomai h otan eis eis otan eimi de os eis eis alla meis oimh kai ode dhlos ws ean tis tis pareimi ergnumi kairos diollumi dhlos gar ou gar oimh eqelw o prassw o o prassw sxolh perimenw alla anagkh o prassw o prassw
english_head: all alike but different. One man is naturally fitted for one task, and another for another. Don’t you think so? I do. Again, would one man do better working at many tasks or one at one? One at one, he said. And, furthermore, this, I fancy, is obvious—that if one lets slip the right season, the favorable moment in any task, the work is spoiled. Obvious. That, I take it, is because the business will not wait upon the leisure of the workman, but the workman must

greek_head: ἐπακολουθεῖν μὴ ἐν παρέργου μέρει Ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: epakolouqew mh en parergon meros anagkh ek dh outos plew te ekastos gignomai kai kallion kai raios otan eis eis kata fus kai en kairos sxolh o allos agw prassw pantapasi men oun pleiwn dh eimi adeimantos deomai poliths h tessares epi o paraskeuh os legw o gar gewrgos ws eoika ou autos poiew eautou o arotron ei mellw kalos eimi
english_head: attend to it as his main affair, and not as a by-work. He must indeed. The result, then, is that more things are produced, and better and more easily when one man performs one task according to his nature, at the right moment, and at leisure from other occupations. By all means. Then, Adeimantus, we need more than four citizens for the provision of the things we have mentioned. For the farmer, it appears, will not make his own plough if it is to be a good one,

greek_head: οὐδὲ σμινύην οὐδὲ τἆλλα ὄργανα ὅσα περὶ γεωργίαν οὐδ' αὖ ὁ οἰκοδόμος πολλῶν δὲ καὶ τούτῳ δεῖ ὡσαύτως δ' ὁ
greek_tokens: oude sminuh oude allos organon osos peri gewrgia oude au o oikodomos polus de kai outos deomai wsautws de o ufanths te kai o skutotomos h ou alhqhs tektwn dh kai xalkeos kai toioutos tis polus dhmiourgos koinwnos egw o polixnh gignomai suxnos autos poiew panu men oun alla ou an pw panu ge megas tis eaw ei autos boukolos te kai poimhn o te allos nomehs prosqew ina e te
english_head: nor his hoe, nor his other agricultural implements, nor will the builder, who also needs many; and similarly the weaver and cobbler. True. Carpenters, then, and smiths and many similar craftsmen, associating themselves with our hamlet, will enlarge it considerably. Certainly. Yet it still wouldn’t be very large even if we should add to them neat-herds and shepherds and other herders,

greek_head: γεωργοὶ ἐπὶ τὸ ἀροῦν ἔχοιεν βοῦς οἵ τε οἰκοδόμοι πρὸς τὰς ἀγωγὰς μετὰ τῶν γεωργῶν χρῆσθαι ὑποζυγίοις ὑφάνται δὲ
greek_tokens: gewrgos epi o aeirw exw bous e te oikodomos pros o agwgh meta o gewrgew xraomai upozugion ufanths de kai skutotomos derma te kai erion oude ge eimi de os mikros polis an eaw exw pas outos alla meis eimi de egw katoikizw ge autos o polis eis toioutos topos e epeisagwgimos mh dew sxedon tis adunatos adunatos gar prosdehsis ara eti kai allos e ek allos polis autos komizw os deomai dehsis kai meis kenos an eimi o diakonos mhdeis agw os ekeinos
english_head: so that the farmers might have cattle for ploughing, and the builders oxen to use with the farmers for transportation, and the weavers and cobblers hides and fleeces for their use. It wouldn’t be a small city, either, if it had all these. But further, said I, it is practically impossible to establish the city in a region where it will not need imports. It is. There will be a further need, then, of those who will bring in from some other city what it requires. There will. And again, if our servitor goes forth empty-handed, not taking with him any of the things needed by those

greek_head: δέονται παρ' ὧν ἂν κομίζωνται ὧν ἂν αὐτοῖς χρεία κενὸς ἄπεισιν ἦ γάρ
greek_tokens: deomai para os an komizw os an autos xreos kenos apeimi eimi gar dokew egw deomai dh o eoika mh monos eautou poiew ikanos alla kai oios kai osos ekeinos os an deomai deomai gar pleiwn dh gewrgew te kai o allos dhmiourgew deomai egw o polis pleiwn gar kai dh kai o allos diakonos pou o te eisagw kai ecagnumi ekastos outos de eimi emporos eimi gar nai kai emporos dh dew panu ge kai ean men ge kata qalassa o emporia gignomai suxnazw
english_head: from whom they procure what they themselves require, he will come back with empty hands, will he not? I think so. Then their home production must not merely suffice for themselves but in quality and quantity meet the needs of those of whom they have need. It must. So our city will require more farmers and other craftsmen. Yes, more. And also of other ministrants who are to export and import the merchandise. These are traders, are they not? Yes. We shall also need traders, then. Assuredly. And if the trading is carried on by sea,

greek_head: καὶ ἄλλων προσδεήσεται τῶν ἐπιστημόνων τῆς περὶ τὴν θάλατταν ἐργασίας
greek_tokens: kai allos prosdeomai o episthmwn o peri o qalassa ergasia suxnazw mentoi tis de dh en autos o polis pws allhlwn metadidwmi os an ekastos ergazomai os dh eneka kai koinwnia poiew polis oikizw dhlos dh eimi de os ostis pwlew kai wneomai agora dh egw kai nomisma sumbolon o allagh eneka gignomai ek outos panu men oun
english_head: we shall need quite a number of others who are expert in maritime business. Quite a number. But again, within the city itself how will they share with one another the products of their labor? This was the very purpose of our association and establishment of a state. Obviously, he said, by buying and selling. A market-place, then, and money as a token for the purpose of exchange will be the result of this.

greek_head: Ἂν οὖν κομίσας ὁ γεωργὸς εἰς τὴν ἀγοράν τι ὧν ποιεῖ ἤ τις ἄλλος τῶν δημιουργῶν μὴ εἰς τὸν αὐτὸν χρόνον ἥκῃ τοῖς
greek_tokens: an oun komizw o gewrgos eis o agora tis os poiew h tis allos o dhmiourgew mh eis o autos xronos hkw o deomai o para autos allassw argew o eautou dhmiourgia kaqhmai en agora oudamos eimi de os alla eimi e outos oraw eautou epi o diakonia tassw outos en men o orqos oikew polis sxedon tis e asqenhs o swma kai axreios tis allos ergnumi prassw autos gar deomai menw
english_head: By all means. If, then, the farmer or any other craftsman taking his products to the market-place does not arrive at the same time with those who desire to exchange with him, is he to sit idle in the market-place and lose time from his own work? By no means, he said, but there are men who see this need and appoint themselves for this service—in well-conducted cities they are generally those who are weakest in body and those who are useless for any other task. They must wait there in the agora

greek_head: αὐτοὺς περὶ τὴν ἀγορὰν τὰ μὲν ἀντ' ἀργυρίου ἀλλάξασθαι τοῖς τι δεομένοις ἀποδόσθαι τοῖς δὲ ἀντὶ αὖ ἀργυρίου διαλλάττειν
greek_tokens: autos peri o agora o men anti argurion allassw o tis deomai apodidwmi o de anti au argurion diallassw osos tis deomai priamai outos ara eimi de egw o xreos kaphlos egw genesis empoiew o polis h ou kaphlos kalew o pros wnh te kai prasis diakonew idruw en agora o de planhths epi o polis emporos panu men oun
english_head: and exchange money for goods with those who wish to sell, and goods for money with as many as desire to buy. This need, then, said I, creates the class of shopkeepers in our city. Or is not shopkeepers the name we give to those who, planted in the agora, serve us in buying and selling, while we call those who roam from city to city merchants? Certainly. And there are, furthermore, I believe, other servitors who in the things of the mind

greek_head: Ἔτι δή τινες ὡς ἐγᾦμαι εἰσὶ καὶ ἄλλοι διάκονοι οἳ ἂν τὰ μὲν τῆς διανοίας μὴ πάνυ ἀξιοκοινώνητοι ὦσιν τὴν δὲ
greek_tokens: eti dh tis ws oimh eimi kai allos diakonos e an o men o dianoia mh panu aciokoinwnhtos eimi o de o swma isxus ikanos epi o ponos exw e dh pwlew o o isxus xreia o timh outos misqos kalew kalew ws oimh misqwtos eimi gar panu men oun plhrwma dh polis eimi ws eoika kai misqwtos dokew egw ara oun eimi adeimantos hdh egw aucanw o polis wste eimi teleios isos pou oun an pote en autos eaw o te dikaiosunh kai o adikia kai tis ama eggignomai os skeptomai
english_head: are not altogether worthy of our fellowship, but whose strength of body is sufficient for toil; so they, selling the use of this strength and calling the price wages, are designated, I believe, wage-earners, are they not? Certainly. Wage-earners, then, it seems, are the complement that helps to fill up the state. I think so. Has our city, then, Adeimantus, reached its full growth and is it complete? Perhaps. Where, then, can justice and injustice be found in it? And along with which of the constituents that we have considered does it come into the state?

greek_head: Ἐγὼ μέν ἔφη οὐκ ἐννοῶ ὦ Σώκρατες εἰ μή που ἐν αὐτῶν τούτων χρείᾳ τινὶ τῇ πρὸς ἀλλήλους
greek_tokens: egw men fhmi ou ennoew eimi swkraths ei mh pou en autos outos xreia tis o pros allhlwn alla isos eimi de egw kalos legw kai skepteos ge kai ou apoknhteon proteros oun skeptomai tis tropos diaitaw e outws paraskeuazw allos tis h sitos te poiew kai oinon kai imation kai upodhma kai oikodomew oikia qeros men o polus gumnos te kai anupodhtos ergazomai o de
english_head: I cannot conceive, Socrates, he said, unless it be in some need that those very constituents have of one another. Perhaps that is a good suggestion, said I; we must examine it and not hold back. First of all, then, let us consider what will be the manner of life of men thus provided. Will they not make bread and wine and garments and shoes? And they will build themselves houses and carry on their work in summer for the most part unclad and unshod and in winter clothed and

greek_head: χειμῶνος ἠμφιεσμένοι τε καὶ ὑποδεδεμένοι ἱκανῶς θρέψονται δὲ ἐκ μὲν τῶν κριθῶν ἄλφιτα σκευαζόμενοι ἐκ δὲ
greek_tokens: xeimwn amfiamfiennumi te kai upodew ikanos trefw de ek men o kriqaw alfiton skeuazw ek de o pura aleuron o men pessw o de massw maza gennaios kai artos epi kalamos tis paraballw h fullon kaqaros kataklinw epi stibas storennumi milac te kai mursinh euwxew autos te kai o paidia epipinw o oinon stefanow kai umnew o qeos hdews suneimi allhlwn ou uper o ousia poiew
english_head: shod sufficiently? And for their nourishment they will provide meal from their barley and flour from their wheat, and kneading and cooking these they will serve noble cakes and loaves on some arrangement of reeds or clean leaves, and, reclined on rustic beds strewn with bryony and myrtle, they will feast with their children, drinking of their wine thereto, garlanded and singing hymns to the gods in pleasant fellowship, not begetting offspring beyond their means

greek_head: τοὺς παῖδας εὐλαβούμενοι πενίαν ἢ πόλεμον Καὶ ὁ Γλαύκων ὑπολαβών Ἄνευ ὄψου ἔφη ὡς ἔοικας
greek_tokens: o pais eulabeomai penia h polemos kai o glauc upolambanw aneu oyon fhmi ws eoika poiew o anhr estiaw alhqhs eimi de egw legw epilanqanomai ostis kai oyon exw alas te dhlos ostis kai elaia kai turos kai bolbos kai laxanon ge oios dh en agrew eyhma eyw kai traghma pou paraqew autos o te sukon kai erebinqos kai kuamos kai murth kai fhgos spodizw
english_head: lest they fall into poverty or war? Here Glaucon broke in: No relishes apparently, he said, for the men you describe as feasting. True said I; I forgot that they will also have relishes—salt, of course, and olives and cheese and onions and greens, the sort of things they boil in the country, they will boil up together. But for dessert we will serve them figs and chickpeas and beans,

greek_head: πρὸς τὸ πῦρ μετρίως ὑποπίνοντες καὶ οὕτω διάγοντες τὸν βίον ἐν εἰρήνῃ μετὰ ὑγιείας ὡς εἰκός γηραιοὶ τελευτῶντες
greek_tokens: pros o pur metrios upopinw kai outws diagw o bios en eirhnh meta ugieia ws eoika ghraios teleutaw allos toioutos bios o ekgonos paradidwmi kai os ei de us polis eimi swkraths fhmi kataskeuazw tis an autos allos h outos xortazw alla pws xrh eimi de egw eimi glauc o nomizw fhmi epi te klinh katakeimai oimh o mellw mh talaipwrew kai apo trapeza
english_head: and they will toast myrtle-berries and acorns before the fire, washing them down with moderate potations and so, living in peace and health, they will probably die in old age and hand on a like life to their offspring. And he said, If you were founding a city of pigs, Socrates, what other fodder than this would you provide? Why, what would you have, Glaucon? said I. What is customary, he replied; They must recline on couches, I presume, if they are not to be uncomfortable,

greek_head: δειπνεῖν καὶ ὄψα ἅπερ καὶ οἱ νῦν ἔχουσι καὶ τραγήματα Εἶεν ἦν δ' ἐγώ μανθάνω οὐ πόλιν ὡς ἔοικε σκοποῦμεν
greek_tokens: deipnew kai oyon o kai e nun exw kai traghma eien eimi de egw manqanw ou polis ws eoika skopaw monos opws gignomai alla kai trufaw polis isos oun oude kakos exis skopaw gar kai toioutos taxa an kateidon o te dikaiosunh kai adikia oph pote o polis emfuw o men oun alhqinos polis dokew egw eimi os dierxomai wsper ugihs tis ei de au boulomai kai flegmainw polis qewrew oudeis apokwluw
english_head: and dine from tables and have made dishes and sweetmeats such as are now in use. Good, said I, I understand. It is not merely the origin of a city, it seems, that we are considering but the origin of a luxurious city. Perhaps that isn’t such a bad suggestion, either. For by observation of such a city it may be we could discern the origin of justice and injustice in states. The true state I believe to be the one we have described—the healthy state, as it were. But if it is your pleasure that we contemplate also a fevered state, there is nothing to hinder.

greek_head: ταῦτα γὰρ δή τισιν ὡς δοκεῖ οὐκ ἐξαρκέσει οὐδὲ αὕτη ἡ δίαιτα ἀλλὰ κλῖναί τε προσέσονται καὶ τράπεζαι καὶ τἆλλα
greek_tokens: outos gar dh tis ws dokew ou ecarkew oude outos o diaita alla klinh te proseimi kai trapeza kai allos skeuos kai oyon dh kai muron kai qumiama kai etaira kai pemma kai ekastos outos pantodapos kai dh kai o o proteros legw ouketi anagkaion qeteos oikia te kai imation kai upodhma alla o te zwgrafia kinhteos kai o poikilia kai xrusos kai elefas kai pas o toioutos kthteos eimi gar
english_head: For there are some, it appears, who will not be contented with this sort of fare or with this way of life; but couches will have to be added thereto and tables and other furniture, yes, and relishes and myrrh and incense and girls and cakes—all sorts of all of them. And the requirements we first mentioned, houses and garments and shoes, will no longer be confined to necessities, but we must set painting to work and embroidery, and procure gold and ivory and similar adornments, must we not?

greek_head: Ναί ἔφη Οὐκοῦν μείζονά τε αὖ τὴν πόλιν δεῖ ποιεῖν ἐκείνη γὰρ
greek_tokens: nai fhmi oukoun megas te au o polis deomai poiew ekeinos gar o ugieinos ouketi ikanos alla hdh ogkos emplhsteos kai plhqos o ouketi o anagkaion eneka eimi en o polis oios e te qhreuths pas e te mimhths polus men e peri o sxhma te kai xrwma polus de e peri mousikh poihths te kai outos uphreths raywdos upokriths xoreuths ergolabos skeuos te pantodaphs dhmiourgos o te allos
english_head: Yes, he said. Then we shall have to enlarge the city again. For that healthy state is no longer sufficient, but we must proceed to swell out its bulk and fill it up with a multitude of things that exceed the requirements of necessity in states, as, for example, the entire class of huntsmen, and the imitators, many of them occupied with figures and colors and many with music—the poets and their assistants, rhapsodists, actors, chorus-dancers, contractors—and

greek_head: καὶ τῶν περὶ τὸν γυναικεῖον κόσμον καὶ δὴ καὶ διακόνων πλειόνων δεησόμεθα ἢ οὐ δοκεῖ δεήσειν παιδαγωγῶν τιτθῶν
greek_tokens: kai o peri o gunaikeios kosmos kai dh kai diakonos pleiwn dew h ou dokew dew paidagwgew titqh trefw kommwtria epikourew kai au oyopoiew te kai mageiros eti de kai subwths prosdeomai outos gar egw en o proteros polis ou eneimi dew gar oudeis en de outos kai outos prosdehsis dehsis de kai o allos boskhma pampolus ei tis autos edw eimi gar pws gar ou
english_head: the manufacturers of all kinds of articles, especially those that have to do with women’s adornment. And so we shall also want more servitors. Don’t you think that we shall need tutors, nurses wet and dry, beauty-shop ladies, barbers and yet again cooks and chefs? And we shall have need, further, of swineherds; there were none of these creatures in our former city, for we had no need of them, but in this city there will be this further need; and we shall also require other cattle in great numbers if they are to be eaten,

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν καὶ ἰατρῶν ἐν χρείαις ἐσόμεθα πολὺ μᾶλλον οὕτω διαιτώμενοι ἢ ὡς τὸ πρότερον
greek_tokens: oukoun kai iatros en xreia eimi polus mallon outws diaitaw h ws o proteros polus ge kai o xwra ge pou o tote ikanos trefw o tote mikros dh ek ikanos eimi h pws legw outws fhmi oukoun o o pimplhmi xwra egw apotmhteon ei mellw ikanos exw nemw te kai aeirw kai ekeinos au o hmeteros ean kai ekeinos afihmi eautou epi xrhma ktaomai apeiros uperbainw o o anagkaion oros
english_head: shall we not? Yes. Doctors, too, are something whose services we shall be much more likely to require if we live thus than as before? Much. And the territory, I presume, that was then sufficient to feed the then population, from being adequate will become too small. Is that so or not? It is. Then we shall have to cut out a cantle of our neighbor’s land if we are to have enough for pasture and ploughing, and they in turn of ours if they too abandon themselves to the unlimited acquisition of wealth,

greek_head: Πολλὴ ἀνάγκη ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες Πολεμήσομεν δὴ τὸ μετὰ τοῦτο ὦ Γλαύκων ἢ πῶς ἔσται
greek_tokens: polus anagkh fhmi eimi swkraths polemew dh o meta outos eimi glauc h pws eimi outws fhmi kai mhdeis ge pw legw eimi de egw mhte ei tis kakos mhte ei agaqos o polemos ergazomai alla tosoutos monos ostis polemos au genesis euriskw ek os malista o polis kai idios kai dhmosios kakos gignomai otan gignomai panu men oun eti dh eimi filos megas o polis deomai ou tis mikros
english_head: disregarding the limit set by our necessary wants. Inevitably, Socrates. We shall go to war as the next step, Glaucon—or what will happen? What you say, he said. And we are not yet to speak, said I, of any evil or good effect of war, but only to affirm that we have further discovered the origin of war, namely, from those things from which the greatest disasters, public and private, come to states when they come. Certainly. Then, my friend, we must still further enlarge our city

greek_head: ἀλλ' ὅλῳ στρατοπέδῳ ὃ ἐξελθὸν ὑπὲρ τῆς οὐσίας ἁπάσης καὶ ὑπὲρ ὧν νυνδὴ ἐλέγομεν διαμαχεῖται τοῖς ἐπιοῦσιν
greek_tokens: alla oloc stratopedon o ecerxomai uper o ousia apas kai uper os nundi legw diamaxomai o epeimi tis de eimi de os autos ou ikanos ou ei su ge eimi de egw kai egw apas omologew kalos hnika plassw o polis omologew de pou ei mimnhskw adunatos enos polus kalos ergazomai texnh alhqhs legw fhmi
english_head: by no small increment, but by a whole army, that will march forth and fight it out with assailants in defence of all our wealth and the luxuries we have just described. How so? he said; are the citizens themselves not sufficient for it? Not if you, said I, and we all were right in the admission we made when we were molding our city. We surely agreed, if you remember, that it is impossible for one man to do the work of many arts well. True, he said. Well, then, said I,

greek_head: Τί οὖν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἡ περὶ τὸν πόλεμον ἀγωνία οὐ τεχνικὴ δοκεῖ εἶναι
greek_tokens: tis oun eimi de egw o peri o polemos agwnia ou texnikos dokew eimi kai mala fhmi eimi oun tis skutikos deomai mallon khdw h polemikos oudamos alla ara o men skutotomos diakwluw mhte gewrgos epixeirew eimi ama mhte ufanths mhte oikodomos alla skutotomos ina dh egw o o skutikos ergnumi kalos gignomai kai o allos eis ekastos wsautws eis apodidwmi pros o fuw ekastos kai epi os mellw o allos
english_head: don’t you think that the business of fighting is an art and a profession? It is indeed, he said. Should our concern be greater, then, for the cobbler’s art than for the art of war? By no means. Can we suppose, then, that while we were at pains to prevent the cobbler from attempting to be at the same time a farmer, a weaver, or a builder instead of just a cobbler, to the end that we might have the cobbler’s business well done, and similarly assigned to each and every one man one occupation, for which he was fit and naturally adapted and at which he was to work all his days,

greek_head: σχολὴν ἄγων διὰ βίου αὐτὸ ἐργαζόμενος οὐ παριεὶς τοὺς καιροὺς καλῶς ἀπεργάσεσθαι τὰ δὲ δὴ περὶ τὸν πόλεμον
greek_tokens: sxolh agw dia bios autos ergazomai ou parihmi o kairos kalos apergazomai o de dh peri o polemos poteros ou peri pleistos eimi eu apergazomai h outws radios wste kai gewrgew tis ama polemikos eimi kai skutotomew kai allos texnh ostis ergazomai pesseutikos de h kubeutikos ikanos oude an eis gignomai mh autos outos ek pais epithdeuw alla parergon xraomai
english_head: at leisure from other pursuits and not letting slip the right moments for doing the work well, and that yet we are in doubt whether the right accomplishment of the business of war is not of supreme moment? Is it so easy that a man who is cultivating the soil will be at the same time a soldier and one who is practising cobbling or any other trade, though no man in the world could make himself a competent expert at draughts or the dice who did not practise that and nothing else from childhood but treated it as an occasional business? And are we to believe that a man who

greek_head: καὶ ἀσπίδα μὲν λαβὼν ἤ τι ἄλλο τῶν πολεμικῶν ὅπλων τε καὶ ὀργάνων αὐθημερὸν ὁπλιτικῆς ἤ τινος ἄλλης μάχης
greek_tokens: kai aspis men lambanw h tis allos o polemikos oplon te kai organon auqhmeros oplitikos h tis allos maxh o kata polemos ikanos eimi agwnisths o de allos organon oudeis oudeis dhmiourgos oude aqlhths lambanw poihsis oude eimi xrhsimos o mhte o episthmh ekastos lambanw mhte o melw ikanos parexw polus gar an eimi de os o organon eimi acios oukoun eimi de egw osos megas o o fulac ergnumi
english_head: takes in hand a shield or any other instrument of war springs up on that very day a competent combatant in heavy armor or in any other form of warfare—though no other tool will make a man be an artist or an athlete by his taking it in hand, nor will it be of any service to those who have neither acquired the science of it nor sufficiently practised themselves in its use? Great indeed, he said, would be the value of tools in that case. Then, said I, in the same degree that the task of our guardians is the greatest of all,

greek_head: τοσούτῳ σχολῆς τε τῶν ἄλλων πλείστης ἂν εἴη καὶ αὖ τέχνης τε καὶ ἐπιμελείας μεγίστης δεόμενον
greek_tokens: tosoutos sxolazw te o allos pleistos an eaw kai au texnh te kai epimeleia megas deomai oimh egw eimi de os ara oun ou kai fusis epithdeios eis autos o epithdeuma pws de ou hmeteros dh ergnumi an eaw ws eoika eiper oios su eimi eklegw tinw te kai poa fusis epithdeios eis polis fulakh hmeteros mentoi ma dios eimi de egw ou ara faulos pragma araomai omws de ou apodeiliateon osos ge an dunamis pareikw
english_head: it would require more leisure than any other business and the greatest science and training. I think so, said he. Does it not also require a nature adapted to that very pursuit? Of course. It becomes our task, then, it seems, if we are able, to select which and what kind of natures are suited for the guardianship of a state. Yes, ours. Upon my word, said I, it is no light task that we have taken upon ourselves. But we must not faint

greek_head: Οὐ γὰρ οὖν ἔφη Οἴει οὖν τι ἦν δ' ἐγώ διαφέρειν φύσιν γενναίου σκύλακος
greek_tokens: ou gar oun fhmi oiomai oun tis eimi de egw diaferw fus gennaios skulac eis fulakh neaniskos eugenhs o poios legw oios ocus su pou deomai autos ekateros eimi pros aisqhsis kai elafros pros o aisqanomai diwkw kai isxuros au ean deomai airew diamaxomai deomai gar oun fhmi pas outos kai meis andreios ge eiper eu maxomai pws de ou andreios de eimi ara eqelw o mh qumoeidhs eite ippos eite kuw h allos otioun zwion h ou ennoew ws
english_head: so far as our strength allows. No, we mustn’t. Do you think, said I, that there is any difference between the nature of a well-bred hound for this watch-dog’s work and of a well-born lad? What point have you in mind? I mean that each of them must be keen of perception, quick in pursuit of what it has apprehended, and strong too if it has to fight it out with its captive. Why, yes, said he, there is need of all these qualities. And it must, further, be brave if it is to fight well. Of course. And will a creature be ready to be brave that is not high-spirited, whether horse or dog or

greek_head: ἄμαχόν τε καὶ ἀνίκητον θυμός οὗ παρόντος ψυχὴ πᾶσα πρὸς πάντα ἄφοβός τέ ἐστι καὶ ἀήττητος
greek_tokens: amaxos te kai anikhtos qumos e pareimi yuxh pas pros pas afobos su eimi kai ahtthtos ennoew o men toinun o swma oios deomai o fulac eimi dhlos nai kai meis kai o o yuxazw ostis ge qumoeidhs kai outos pws oun eimi de egw eimi glauc ou agrios allhlwn te eimi kai o allos poliths eimi toioutos o fusis ma dios eimi de os ou radios
english_head: anything else? Have you never observed what an irresistible and invincible thing is spirit, the presence of which makes every soul in the face of everything fearless and unconquerable? I have. The physical qualities of the guardian, then, are obvious. Yes. And also those of his soul, namely that he must be of high spirit. Yes, this too. How then, Glaucon, said I, will they escape being savage to one another and to the other citizens if this is to be their nature? Not easily, by Zeus, said he. And yet

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μέντοι δεῖ γε πρὸς μὲν τοὺς οἰκείους πρᾴους αὐτοὺς εἶναι πρὸς δὲ τοὺς πολεμίους χαλεπούς εἰ δὲ μή οὐ
greek_tokens: alla mentoi deomai ge pros men o oikeios praos autos eimi pros de o polemios xalepos ei de mh ou perimenw allos sfazw diollumi alla autos fqanw autos draw alhqhs fhmi tis oun eimi de egw poiew pinw ama praos kai megaloqumos hqos euriskw enantios gar pou qumoeidhs praos fusis fainw alla mentoi outos ge opoteros an steromai fulac agaqos ou mh gignomai outos de adunatos eoika kai outws
english_head: we must have them gentle to their friends and harsh to their enemies; otherwise they will not await their destruction at the hands of others, but will be first themselves in bringing it about. True, he said. What, then, are we to do? said I. Where shall we discover a disposition that is at once gentle and great-spirited? For there appears to be an opposition between the spirited type and the gentle nature. There does. But yet if one lacks either of these qualities, a good guardian he never can be. But these requirements resemble impossibilities, and so

greek_head: δὴ συμβαίνει ἀγαθὸν φύλακα ἀδύνατον γενέσθαι Κινδυνεύει ἔφη
greek_tokens: dh sumbainw agaqos fulac adunatos gignomai kinduneuw fhmi kai egw aporew te kai episkeptomai o emprosqen dikaios ge eimi de egw eimi filos aforaw eis gar protiqhmi eikwn apoleipw pws legw ou ennoew ostis eimi ara fusis oios egw ou oiomai exw enantios outos pou dh eidon men an tis kai en allos zwion ou mentoi hkistos
english_head: the result is that a good guardian is impossible. It seems likely, he said. And I was at a standstill, and after reconsidering what we had been saying, I said, We deserve to be at a loss, my friend, for we have lost sight of the comparison that we set before ourselves. What do you mean? We failed to note that there are after all such natures as we thought impossible, endowed with these opposite qualities. Where? It may be observed in other animals, but especially in that which we

greek_head: ἐν ᾧ ἡμεῖς παρεβάλλομεν τῷ φύλακι οἶσθα γάρ που τῶν γενναίων κυνῶν ὅτι τοῦτο φύσει αὐτῶν τὸ ἦθος πρὸς μὲν
greek_tokens: en os egw paraballw o fulac oida gar pou o gennaios kuwn ostis outos fusis autos o hqos pros men o sunhqhs te kai gnwrimos ws oios te praos eimi pros de o agnws enantion oida mentoi outos men ara eimi de egw dunatos kai ou para fus zhtew toioutos eimi o fulac ou eoika ara oun su dokew eti ode prosdeomai o fulakikos eimi pros o qumoeidhs eti prosgignomai filosofos o fus
english_head: likened to the guardian. You surely have observed in well-bred hounds that their natural disposition is to be most gentle to their familiars and those whom they recognize, but the contrary to those whom they do not know. I am aware of that. The thing is possible, then, said I, and it is not an unnatural requirement that we are looking for in our guardian. It seems not. And does it seem to you that our guardian-to-be will also need, in addition to the being high-spirited, the further quality of having the love of wisdom in his nature? How so? he said; I don’t apprehend your meaning.

greek_head: Πῶς δή ἔφη οὐ γὰρ ἐννοῶ Καὶ τοῦτο ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἐν τοῖς κυσὶν κατόψει ὃ καὶ ἄξιον
greek_tokens: pws dh fhmi ou gar ennoew kai outos eimi de egw en o kuwn katoyis o kai acios qaumazw o qhrion o poios ostis os men an eidon agnws xalepainw oude eis kakos propasxw os de an gnwrimos aspazomai kan mhdeis pwpote upo autos agaqos pasxw h oupw outos qaumazw ou panu fhmi mexri outos prosexw o new ostis de pou draw outos dhlos alla meis komyos ge fainw o paqos autos o
english_head: This too, said I, is something that you will discover in dogs and which is worth our wonder in the creature. What? That the sight of an unknown person angers him before he has suffered any injury, but an acquaintance he will fawn upon though he has never received any kindness from him. Have you never marvelled at that? I never paid any attention to the matter before now, but that he acts in some such way is obvious. But surely that is an exquisite

greek_head: φύσεως καὶ ὡς ἀληθῶς φιλόσοφον Πῇ δή
greek_tokens: fusis kai ws alhqhs filosofos ph dh h eimi de egw oyis oudeis allos filos kai exqros diakrinw h o o men katamanqanw o de agnoew kaitoi pws ou an filomaqhs eaw suneimi te kai agnoia orizw o te oikew kai o allotrios oudamos eimi de os opws ou alla mentoi eipon egw o ge filomaqhs kai filosofos tautos tautos gar fhmi oukoun qarsew tiqhmi kai en anqrwpos ei mellw
english_head: trait of his nature and one that shows a true love of wisdom. In what respect, pray? In respect, said I, that he distinguishes a friendly from a hostile aspect by nothing save his apprehension of the one and his failure to recognize the other. How, I ask you, can the love of learning be denied to a creature whose criterion of the friendly and the alien is intelligence and ignorance? It certainly cannot, he said. But you will admit, said I, that the love of learning and the love of wisdom are the same? The same, he said. Then may we not confidently lay it down in the case of man too, that if he is to be

greek_head: πρὸς τοὺς οἰκείους καὶ γνωρίμους πρᾷός τις ἔσεσθαι φύσει φιλόσοφον καὶ φιλομαθῆ αὐτὸν δεῖν εἶναι
greek_tokens: pros o oikeios kai gnwrimos praos tis eimi fusis filosofos kai filomaqhs autos dew eimi tiqhmi fhmi filosofos dh kai qumoeidhs kai taxus kai isxuros egw o fus eimi o mellw kalos agaqos eimi fulac polis pantapasi men oun fhmi outos men dh an outws uparxw trefw de dh egw outos kai paideuw tis tropos kai ara tis prourgou egw eimi autos skopaw pros o kateidon ouper eneka
english_head: in some sort gentle to friends and familiars he must be by nature a lover of wisdom and of learning? Let us so assume, he replied. The love of wisdom, then, and high spirit and quickness and strength will be combined for us in the nature of him who is to be a good and true guardian of the state. By all means, he said. Such, then, I said, would be the basis of his character. But the rearing of these men and their education, how shall we manage that? And will the consideration of this topic advance us

greek_head: πάντα σκοποῦμεν δικαιοσύνην τε καὶ ἀδικίαν τίνα τρόπον ἐν πόλει γίγνεται ἵνα μὴ ἐῶμεν ἱκανὸν λόγον ἢ συχνὸν
greek_tokens: pas skopaw dikaiosunh te kai adikia tis tropos en polis gignomai ina mh eaw ikanos logos h suxnos dieceimi kai o o glaukwn adelfos panu men oun fhmi egw prosdokaw prourgou eimi eis outos outos o skeyis ma dios eimi de egw eimi filos adeimantos ou ara afeteos oude ei makros tugxanw eimi ou gar oun eimi oun wsper en muqos muqologew te kai sxolh agw logos paideuw o anhr
english_head: in any way towards discerning what is the object of our entire inquiry—the origin of justice and injustice in a state—our aim must be to omit nothing of a sufficient discussion, and yet not to draw it out to tiresome length? And Glaucon’s brother replied, Certainly, I expect that this inquiry will bring us nearer to that end. Certainly, then, my dear Adeimantus, said I, we must not abandon it even if it prove to be rather long. No, we must not. Come, then, just as if we were telling stories or fables and

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ χρή Τίς οὖν ἡ παιδεία ἢ χαλεπὸν εὑρεῖν βελτίω τῆς ὑπὸ
greek_tokens: alla xrh tis oun o paideia h xalepos euriskw beltiwn o upo o polus xronos euriskw eimi de pou o men epi swma gumnastikos o de epi yuxw mousikh eimi gar ara oun ou mousikh proteros arxw paideuw h gumnastikos pws de ou mousikh de eipon tiqhmi logos h ou egw logos de dissos eidos o men alhqhs yeudos de eteros nai
english_head: had ample leisure, let us educate these men in our discourse. So we must. What, then, is our education? Or is it hard to find a better than that which long time has discovered? Which is, I suppose, gymnastics for the body and for the soul music. It is. And shall we not begin education in music earlier than in gymnastics? Of course. And under music you include tales, do you not? I do. And tales are of two species, the one true and the other false? Yes. And education must make use

greek_head: Παιδευτέον δ' ἐν ἀμφοτέροις πρότερον δ' ἐν τοῖς ψευδέσιν
greek_tokens: paideuteos de en amfoteros proteros de en o yeudhs ou manqanw fhmi pws legw ou manqanw eimi de egw ostis proteros o paidion muqos legw outos de pou ws o oloc eipon yeudos en de kai alhqhs proteros de muqos pros o paidia h gumnasion xraomai eimi outos outos dh elegos ostis mousikh proteros apteon h gumnastikos orqos fhmi oukoun oistos ostis arxh pas ergnumi megas allos
english_head: of both, but first of the false? I don’t understand your meaning. Don’t you understand, I said, that we begin by telling children fables, and the fable is, taken as a whole, false, but there is truth in it also? And we make use of fable with children before gymnastics. That is so. That, then, is what I meant by saying that we must take up music before gymnastics. You were right, he said. Do you not know, then, that the beginning in every task is the chief thing, especially for any creature that is young and tender?

greek_head: τε δὴ καὶ νέῳ καὶ ἁπαλῷ ὁτῳοῦν μάλιστα γὰρ δὴ τότε πλάττεται καὶ ἐνδύεται τύπος ὃν ἄν τις βούληται ἐνσημήνασθαι
greek_tokens: te dh kai neos kai apalos ostis malista gar dh tote plassw kai enduw tupos os an tis boulomai enshmainomai ekastos komidh men oun ara oun radios outws parihmi o epitugxanw upo o epitugxanw muqos plassw akouw o pais kai lambanw en o yuxh ws epi o polus enantios doca ekeinos os epeidan teleiow exw oiomai dew autos oude opwsoun parihmi proteros dh egw ws eoika epistathteon o muqopoios
english_head: For it is then that it is best molded and takes the impression that one wishes to stamp upon it. Quite so. Shall we, then, thus lightly suffer our children to listen to any chance stories fashioned by any chance teachers and so to take into their minds opinions for the most part contrary to those that we shall think it desirable for them to hold when they are grown up? By no manner of means will we allow it. We must begin, then, it seems, by a censorship

greek_head: καὶ ὃν μὲν ἂν καλὸν μῦθον ποιήσωσιν ἐγκριτέον ὃν δ' ἂν μή ἀποκριτέον τοὺς δ' ἐγκριθέντας πείσομεν τὰς τροφούς
greek_tokens: kai os men an kalos muqos poiew egkriteon os de an mh apokriteon o de egkrinw peiqw o trofos te kai mhthr legw o pais kai plassw o yuxh autos o muqos polus mallon h o swma o xeir os de nun legw o polus ekblhteon poios dh fhmi en o megas eimi de egw muqos oraw kai o elasswn deomai gar dh o autos tupon eimi kai tautos
english_head: over our storymakers, and what they do well we must pass and what not, reject. And the stories on the accepted list we will induce nurses and mothers to tell to the children and so shape their souls by these stories far rather than their bodies by their hands. But most of the stories they now tell we must reject. What sort of stories? he said. The example of the greater stories, I said, will show us the lesser also. For surely the pattern must be the same and the greater and the less

greek_head: δύνασθαι τούς τε μείζους καὶ τοὺς ἐλάττους ἢ οὐκ οἴει Ἔγωγ' ἔφη ἀλλ' οὐκ ἐννοῶ οὐδὲ τοὺς μείζους τίνας
greek_tokens: dunamai o te megas kai o elasswn h ou oiomai egw fhmi alla ou ennoew oude o megas tis legw os hsiodos te eipon kai omhros egw legw kai e allos poihths outos gar pou muqos o anqrwpos yeudhs suntiqhmi elegos te kai legw poios dh eimi de os kai tis autos memfomai legw osper eimi de egw xrh kai proteros kai malista memfomai allos te kai ean tis mh kalos yeudw tis outos
english_head: must have a like tendency. Don’t you think so? I do, he said; but I don’t apprehend which you mean by the greater, either. Those, I said, that Hesiod and Homer and the other poets related. These, methinks, composed false stories which they told and still tell to mankind. Of what sort? he said; and what in them do you find fault? With that, I said, which one ought first and chiefly to blame, especially if the lie is not a pretty one.

greek_head: Ὅταν εἰκάζῃ τις κακῶς οὐσίαν τῷ λόγῳ περὶ θεῶν τε καὶ ἡρώων οἷοί εἰσιν ὥσπερ γραφεὺς μηδὲν ἐοικότα γράφων
greek_tokens: otan eikazw tis kakos ousia o logos peri qea te kai hrws oios eimi wsper grafeus mhdeis eoika grafw os an omoios boulomai grafw kai gar fhmi orqos exis o ge toioutos memfomai alla pws dh legw kai poios proteros men eimi de egw o megas kai peri o megas yeudos o eipon ou kalos yeudw ws ouranos te ergazomai o fhmi draw autos hsiodos o te au kronos
english_head: What is that? When anyone images badly in his speech the true nature of gods and heroes, like a painter whose portraits bear no resemblance to his models. It is certainly right to condemn things like that, he said; but just what do we mean and what particular things? There is, first of all, I said, the greatest lie about the things of greatest concernment, which was no pretty invention of him who told how Uranus did what Hesiod says he did to Cronos, and how Cronos in turn took his revenge;

greek_head: ὡς ἐτιμωρήσατο αὐτόν τὰ δὲ δὴ τοῦ Κρόνου ἔργα καὶ πάθη ὑπὸ τοῦ ὑέος οὐδ' ἂν εἰ ἦν ἀληθῆ ᾤμην δεῖν ῥᾳδίως
greek_tokens: ws timwrew autos o de dh o kronos ergon kai paqh upo o uios oude an ei eimi alhqhs oiomai dew radios outws legw pros afrwn te kai neos alla malista men sigaw ei de anagkh tis eimi legw dia aporrhtos akouw ws oligistos quw ou xoiros alla tis megas kai aporos quma opws ostis elaxistos sumbainw akew kai gar eimi de os outos ge e logos xalepos
english_head: and then there are the doings and sufferings of Cronos at the hands of his son. Even if they were true I should not think that they ought to be thus lightly told to thoughtless young persons. But the best way would be to bury them in silence, and if there were some necessity for relating them, that only a very small audience should be admitted under pledge of secrecy and after sacrificing, not a pig, but some huge and unprocurable victim, to the end that as few as possible should have heard these tales. Why, yes, said he, such stories are hard sayings. Yes, and they are not to be told,

greek_head: Καὶ οὐ λεκτέοι γ' ἔφην ὦ Ἀδείμαντε ἐν τῇ ἡμετέρᾳ πόλει οὐδὲ λεκτέον νέῳ ἀκούοντι ὡς ἀδικῶν τὰ ἔσχατα
greek_tokens: kai ou lekteos ge fhmi eimi adeimantos en o hmeteros polis oude lekteos neos akouw ws adikew o esxatos oudeis an qaumastos poiew oude au adikew pathr kolazw pas tropos alla draw an osper qea e proteros te kai megas ou ma o dios eimi de os oude autos egw dokew epithdeios eimi legw oude ge eimi de egw o parapan ws qeos qeaw polemew
english_head: Adeimantus, in our city, nor is it to be said in the hearing of a young man, that in doing the utmost wrong he would do nothing to surprise anybody, nor again in punishing his father’s wrong-doings to the limit, but would only be following the example of the first and greatest of the gods. No, by heaven, said he, I do not myself think that they are fit to be told. Neither must we admit at all, said I, that gods war with gods and plot against one another and contend—for it is not true either—

greek_head: τε καὶ ἐπιβουλεύουσι καὶ μάχονται οὐδὲ γὰρ ἀληθῆ εἴ γε δεῖ ἡμῖν τοὺς μέλλοντας τὴν πόλιν φυλάξειν αἴσχιστον
greek_tokens: te kai epibouleuw kai maxomai oude gar alhqhs ei ge deomai egw o mellw o polis fulassw aisxros nomizw o radios allhlwn apexqanomai polus deomai gigantomaxia te muqologhteon autos kai poikilteon kai allos exqra polus kai pantodapos qea te kai hrws pros suggeneus te kai oikeios autos alla ei pws mellw peiqw ws oudeis pwpote poliths eteros eteros apaxqomai oude eimi outos osios toioutos lekteos mallon pros
english_head: if we wish our future guardians to deem nothing more shameful than lightly to fall out with one another; still less must we make battles of gods and giants the subject for them of stories and embroideries, and other enmities many and manifold of gods and heroes toward their kith and kin. But if there is any likelihood of our persuading them that no citizen ever quarrelled with his fellow-citizen and that the very idea of it is an impiety,

greek_head: τὰ παιδία εὐθὺς καὶ γέρουσι καὶ γραυσί καὶ πρεσβυτέροις γιγνομένοις καὶ τοὺς ποιητὰς ἐγγὺς τούτων ἀναγκαστέον
greek_tokens: o paidia euqus kai gerwn kai graus kai presbus gignomai kai o poihths eggus outos anagkasteos logopoiew hra de desmos upo uios kai hfaistos riptw upo pathr mellw o mhthr tuptw amunw kai qeomaxia osos omhros poiew ou paradekteos eis o polis oute en uponoia poiew oute aneu uponoia o gar neos ou oios te krinw ostis te uponoia kai o mh alla o an thlikoutos eimi lambanw en o doca dusekniptos
english_head: that is the sort of thing that ought rather to be said by their elders, men and women, to children from the beginning and as they grow older, and we must compel the poets to keep close to this in their compositions. But Hera’s fetterings by her son and the hurling out of heaven of Hephaestus by his father when he was trying to save his mother from a beating, and the battles of the gods in Homer’s verse are things that we must not admit into our city either wrought in allegory or without allegory. For the young are not able to distinguish what is and what is not allegory, but whatever opinions are taken into the mind at that age are wont to prove

greek_head: τε καὶ ἀμετάστατα φιλεῖ γίγνεσθαι ὧν δὴ ἴσως ἕνεκα περὶ παντὸς ποιητέον ἃ πρῶτα ἀκούουσιν ὅτι κάλλιστα
greek_tokens: te kai ametastatos filew gignomai os dh isos eneka peri pas poihteos o proteros akouw ostis kallista muqologew pros areth akouw exis gar fhmi logos alla ei tis au kai outos erwtaw egw outos ostis su eimi kai tinw e muqos tis an fhmi kai egw eipon eimi adeimantos ou eimi poihths egw te
english_head: indelible and unalterable. For which reason, maybe, we should do our utmost that the first stories that they hear should be so composed as to bring the fairest lessons of virtue to their ears. Yes, that is reasonable, he said; but if again someone should ask us to be specific and say what these compositions may be and what are the tales, what could we name? And I replied, Adeimantus, we are not poets, you and I at present,

greek_head: καὶ σὺ ἐν τῷ παρόντι ἀλλ' οἰκισταὶ πόλεως οἰκισταῖς δὲ τοὺς μὲν τύπους προσήκει εἰδέναι ἐν οἷς δεῖ μυθολογεῖν τοὺς
greek_tokens: kai su en o pareimi alla oikisths polis oikisths de o men tupos proshkw oida en os deomai muqologew o poihths para os ean poiew ou epitrepteon ou meis autos ge poihteos muqos orqos fhmi alla autos dh outos e tupos peri qeologia tinw an eien toiosde pou tis eimi de egw oios tugxanw o qeos eimi aei dhpou apodoteos ean tis autos en epos poiew ean en melos ean en tragwdia deomai gar
english_head: but founders of a state. And to founders it pertains to know the patterns on which poets must compose their fables and from which their poems must not be allowed to deviate; but the founders are not required themselves to compose fables. Right, he said; but this very thing—the patterns or norms of right speech about the gods, what would they be? Something like this, I said. The true quality of God we must always surely attribute to him whether we compose in epic, melic, or tragic verse. We must. And is not God of course good in reality

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἀγαθὸς ὅ γε θεὸς τῷ ὄντι τε καὶ λεκτέον οὕτω Τί μήν
greek_tokens: oukoun agaqos o ge qeos o eimi te kai lekteos outws tis meis alla meis oudeis ge o agaqos blaberos eimi gar ou egw dokew ara oun o mh blaberos blaptw oudamos o de mh blaptw kakos tis poiew oude outos o de ge mhdeis kakos poiew oude an tis eaw kakos aitios pws gar tis de wfelimos o agaqos nai aitios ara eupragia nai ou ara pas ge aitios o agaqos alla o men eu exw aitios o de kakh anaitios
english_head: and always to be spoken of as such? Certainly. But further, no good thing is harmful, is it? I think not. Can what is not harmful harm? By no means. Can that which does not harm do any evil? Not that either. But that which does no evil would not be cause of any evil either? How could it? Once more, is the good beneficent? Yes. It is the cause, then, of welfare? Yes. Then the good is not the cause of all things, but of things that are well it the cause—of things that are ill it is blameless. Entirely so,

greek_head: Παντελῶς γ' ἔφη Οὐδ' ἄρα ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὁ θεός ἐπειδὴ ἀγαθός πάντων ἂν
greek_tokens: pantelhs ge fhmi oude ara eimi de egw o qeos epei agaqos pas an eaw aitios ws e polus legw alla oligos men o anqrwpos aitios polus de anaitios polus gar elasswn agaqos o kakh egw kai o men agaqos oudeis allos aitiateon o de kakh allos atta deomai zhtew o aitios alla ou o qeos alhqhs fhmi dokew egw legw ou ara eimi de egw apodekteos oute omhros oute allos
english_head: he said. Neither, then, could God, said I, since he is good, be, as the multitude say, the cause of all things, but for mankind he is the cause of few things, but of many things not the cause. For good things are far fewer with us than evil, and for the good we must assume no other cause than God, but the cause of evil we must look for in other things and not in God. What you say seems to me most true, he replied. Then, said I, we must not accept

greek_head: ποιητοῦ ταύτην τὴν ἁμαρτίαν περὶ τοὺς θεοὺς ἀνοήτως ἁμαρτάνοντος καὶ λέγοντος
greek_tokens: poihths outos o amartia peri o qeos anohtos amartanw kai legw ws doioi te peiqw katakeimai en zeus oudas khr empleos o men esqlos atar o deilh kai os men an mignumi o zeus didwmi amfoteros allote men te kakazw o ge kurew allote de esqlos os de an mh alla akratos o eteros o de kakos boubrwstis epi xqwn dios elaunw
english_head: from Homer or any other poet the folly of such error as this about the gods when he says Two urns stand on the floor of the palace of Zeus and are filled with Dooms he allots, one of blessings, the other of gifts that are evil, Hom. Il. 24.527-8 and to whomsoever Zeus gives of both commingled— Now upon evil he chances and now again good is his portion, Hom. Il. 24.530 but the man for whom he does not blend the lots, but to whom he gives unmixed evil— Hunger devouring drives him, a wanderer over the wide world, Hom. Il. 24.532

greek_head: οὐδ' ὡς ταμίας ἡμῖν Ζεὺς ἀγαθῶν τε κακῶν τε τέτυκται
greek_tokens: oude ws tamias egw zeus agaqos te kakh te teuxw o de o orkos kai spondh sugxusis os o pandaros sugxew ean tis fhmi dia aqhnh te kai zeus gignomai ou epainew oude qea eris te kai krisis
english_head: nor will we tolerate the saying that Zeus is dispenser alike of good and of evil to mortals. But as to the violation of the oaths and the truce by Pandarus, if anyone affirms it to have been brought about by the action of Athena and Zeus, we will not approve, nor that the strife and contention of the gods

english_head: was the doing of Themis and Zeus; nor again must we permit our youth to hear what Aeschylus says— A god implants the guilty cause in men When he would utterly destroy a house, Aesch. but if any poets compose a Sorrows of Niobe, the poem that contains these iambics, or a tale of the Pelopidae or of Troy, or anything else of the kind, we must either forbid them to say that these woes are the work of God, or they must devise some such interpretation as we now require, and must declare that what God
