english_head: did was righteous and good, and they were benefited by their chastisement. But that they were miserable who paid the penalty, and that the doer of this was God, is a thing that the poet must not be suffered to say; if on the other hand he should say that for needing chastisement the wicked were miserable and that in paying the penalty they were benefited by God, that we must allow. But as to saying that God, who is good, becomes the cause of evil to anyone, we must contend in every way that neither should anyone assert this in his own city if it is to be well governed, nor anyone hear it,

greek_head: μήτε νεώτερον μήτε πρεσβύτερον μήτ' ἐν μέτρῳ μήτε ἄνευ μέτρου μυθολογοῦντα ὡς οὔτε ὅσια ἂν λεγόμενα εἰ λέγοιτο
greek_tokens: mhte neos mhte presbus mhte en metron mhte aneu metron muqologew ws oute osios an legw ei legw oute sumforos egw oute sumfwnos autos eautou sumyhfos sos eimi fhmi outos o nomos kai egw areskw outos men toinun eimi de egw eis an eaw o peri qeos nomos te kai tupon en os dehsis o te legw legw kai o poiew poiew mh pas aitios o qeos alla o agaqos kai mala fhmi apoxraw
english_head: neither younger nor older, neither telling a story in meter or without meter; for neither would the saying of such things, if they are said, be holy, nor would they be profitable to us or concordant with themselves. I cast my vote with yours for this law, he said, and am well pleased with it. This, then, said I, will be one of the laws and patterns concerning the gods to which speakers and poets will be required to conform, that God is not the cause of all things, but only of the good. And an entirely satisfactory one, he said.

greek_head: Τί δὲ δὴ ὁ δεύτερος ὅδε ἆρα γόητα τὸν θεὸν οἴει εἶναι καὶ οἷον ἐξ ἐπιβουλῆς φαντάζεσθαι ἄλλοτε ἐν ἄλλαις ἰδέαις
greek_tokens: tis de dh o deuteros ode ara gohs o qeos oiomai eimi kai oios ek epiboulh fantazomai allote en allos idea tote men autos gignomai kai allassw o eautou eidos eis polus morfh tote de egw apataw kai poiew peri eautou toioutos dokew h aploos te eimi kai pas hkistos o eautou idea ekbainw ou exw fhmi nun ge outws eipon tis de ode ou anagkh eiper tis ecisthmi o eautou
english_head: And what of this, the second. Do you think that God is a wizard and capable of manifesting himself by design, now in one aspect, now in another, at one time himself changing and altering his shape in many transformations and at another deceiving us and causing us to believe such things about him; or that he is simple and less likely than anything else to depart from his own form? I cannot say offhand, he replied. But what of this: If anything went out from its own form, would it not be displaced and changed, either by itself

greek_head: ἰδέας ἢ αὐτὸ ὑφ' ἑαυτοῦ μεθίστασθαι ἢ ὑπ' ἄλλου Ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: idea h autos ufh eautou meqisthmi h upo allos anagkh oukoun upo men allos o ariston exw hkistos alloiow te kai kinew oios swma upo siteomai te kai poths kai ponos kai pas futon upo eilhsis te kai anemos kai o toioutos paqhma ou o ugihs kai isxuros
english_head: or by something else? Necessarily. Is it not true that to be altered and moved by something else happens least to things that are in the best condition, as, for example, a body by food and drink and toil, and plants by the heat of the sun and winds and similar influences—is it not true that the healthiest and strongest is least altered?

greek_head: ἥκιστα ἀλλοιοῦται Πῶς δ' οὔ
greek_tokens: hkistos alloiow pws de ou yuxh de ou o andreios kai fronimos hkistos an tis ecwqen paqos tarassw te kai alloiow nai kai meis pou kai o ge sunqetos pas skeuos te kai oikodomhma kai amfiesma kata o autos logos o eu ergazomai kai eu exw upo xronos te kai o allos paqhma hkistos alloiow eimi dh outos
english_head: Certainly. And is it not the soul that is bravest and most intelligent, that would be least disturbed and altered by any external affection? Yes. And, again, it is surely true of all composite implements, edifices, and habiliments, by parity of reasoning, that those which are well made and in good condition are least liable to be changed by time and other influences. That is so. It is universally true, then, that that which is in the best state by nature or

greek_head: Πᾶν δὴ τὸ καλῶς ἔχον ἢ φύσει ἢ τέχνῃ ἢ ἀμφοτέροις ἐλαχίστην μεταβολὴν ὑπ' ἄλλου ἐνδέχεται
greek_tokens: pas dh o kalos exw h fusis h texnh h amfoteros elaxistos metabolh upo allos endexomai eoika alla meis o qeos ge kai o o qeaomai panth ariston exis pws de ou outos men dh hkistos an polus morfh isxw o qeos hkistos dhta alla ara autos eautou metaballw an kai alloiow dhlos fhmi ostis eiper alloiow poteros oun epi o beltiwn te kai kallion metaballw eautou h epi o xeirwn kai o aisxros eautou
english_head: art or both admits least alteration by something else. So it seems. But God, surely, and everything that belongs to God is in every way in the best possible state. Of course. From this point of view, then, it would be least of all likely that there would be many forms in God. Least indeed. But would he transform and alter himself? Obviously, he said, if he is altered. Then does he change himself for the better and to something fairer, or for the worse and to something uglier than himself?

greek_head: Ἀνάγκη ἔφη ἐπὶ τὸ χεῖρον εἴπερ ἀλλοιοῦται οὐ γάρ που ἐνδεᾶ γε φήσομεν τὸν θεὸν κάλλους ἢ ἀρετῆς εἶναι
greek_tokens: anagkh fhmi epi o xeirwn eiper alloiow ou gar pou endehs ge fhmi o qeos kallos h aretaw eimi orqos eimi de egw legw kai outws exw dokew an tis su eimi adeimantos ekwn eautou xeirwn poiew oph h qea h anqrwpos adunatos fhmi adunatos ara fhmi kai qeaw eqelw eautou alloiow alla ws eoika kalos kai aristos eimi eis o dunatos ekastos autos menos aei aplos en o eautou morfaw apas fhmi anagkh egw dokew
english_head: It must necessarily, said he, be for the worse if he is changed. For we surely will not say that God is deficient in either beauty or excellence. Most rightly spoken, said I. And if that were his condition, do you think, Adeimantus, that any one god or man would of his own will worsen himself in any way? Impossible, he replied. It is impossible then, said I, even for a god to wish to alter himself, but, as it appears, each of them being the fairest and best possible abides for ever simply in his own form. An absolutely necessary conclusion to my thinking. No poet then,

greek_head: Μηδεὶς ἄρα ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ ἄριστε λεγέτω ἡμῖν τῶν ποιητῶν ὡς
greek_tokens: mhdeis ara eimi de egw eimi aristos legw egw o poihths ws qeos cenos eoika allodapos pantoios teleqw epistrwfaw polis mhde prwteus kai qetis katayeudomai mhdeis mhde en tragwdia mhde en o allos poihma eisagw hra alloiow ws iereia ageirw inaxos argeios potamos pais biodwros
english_head: I said, my good friend, must be allowed to tell us that The gods, in the likeness of strangers, Many disguises assume as they visit the cities of mortals. Hom. Od. 17.485-486 Nor must anyone tell falsehoods about Proteus and Thetis, nor in any tragedy or in other poems bring in Hera disguised as a priestess collecting alms for the life-giving sons of Inachus, the Argive stream. Aesch.

greek_head: καὶ ἄλλα τοιαῦτα πολλὰ μὴ ἡμῖν ψευδέσθων μηδ' αὖ ὑπὸ τούτων ἀναπειθόμεναι αἱ μητέρες τὰ παιδία ἐκδειματούντων
greek_tokens: kai allos toioutos polus mh egw yeudw mhde au upo outos anapeiqw o mhthr o paidia ekdeimatow legw o muqos kakos ws ara qeos tis perierxomai nuktwr polus cenos kai pantodapos indallomai ina mh ama men eis qeos blasfhmew ama de o pais apergazomai deilos mh gar fhmi alla ara eimi de egw autos men e qeos eimi oios mh metaballw egw de poiew dokew sfazw pantodapos fainw ecapataw kai gohteuw isos fhmi
english_head: And many similar falsehoods they must not tell. Nor again must mothers under the influence of such poets terrify their children with harmful tales, how that there are certain gods whose apparitions haunt the night in the likeness of many strangers from all manner of lands, lest while they speak evil of the gods they at the same time make cowards of children. They must not, he said. But, said I, may we suppose that while the gods themselves are incapable of change they cause us to fancy that they appear in many shapes deceiving and practising magic upon us? Perhaps, said he. Consider,

greek_head: Τί δέ ἦν δ' ἐγώ ψεύδεσθαι θεὸς ἐθέλοι ἂν ἢ λόγῳ ἢ ἔργῳ φάντασμα προτείνων
greek_tokens: tis de eimi de egw yeudw qeos eqelw an h logos h ergon fantasma proteinw ou oida eimi de os ou oida eimi de egw ostis o ge ws alhqhs yeudos ei oios te outos eipon pas qeos te kai anqrwpos misew pws fhmi legw outws eimi de egw ostis o kurios pou eautou yeudw kai peri o kurios oudeis ekwn eqelw alla pas malista fobew ekei autos ktaomai oude nun pw eimi de os manqanw
english_head: said I; would a god wish to deceive, or lie, by presenting in either word or action what is only appearance? I don’t know, said he. Don’t you know, said I, that the veritable lie, if the expression is permissible, is a thing that all gods and men abhor? What do you mean? he said. This, said I, that falsehood in the most vital part of themselves, and about their most vital concerns, is something that no one willingly accepts, but it is there above all that everyone fears it. I don’t understand yet either. That is because you suspect me of some grand meaning,

greek_head: Οἴει γάρ τί με ἔφην σεμνὸν λέγειν ἐγὼ δὲ λέγω ὅτι τῇ ψυχῇ περὶ τὰ ὄντα ψεύδεσθαί τε καὶ ἐψεῦσθαι καὶ
greek_tokens: oiomai gar tis egw fhmi semnon legw egw de legos ostis o yuxw peri o eimi yeudw te kai yeudw kai amaqhs eimi kai entauqa exw te kai ktaomai o yeudos pas hkistos an dexomai kai misew malista autos en o toioutos polus ge fhmi alla meis orqos ge an o nundi elegos outos ws alhqhs yeudos kalew o en o yuxw agnoia o o yeudw epei o ge en o logos mimhma tis o en o yuxw eimi paqhma kai usteron gignomai eidwlon ou panu
english_head: I said; but what I mean is, that deception in the soul about realities, to have been deceived and to be blindly ignorant and to have and hold the falsehood there, is what all men would least of all accept, and it is in that case that they loathe it most of all. Quite so, he said. But surely it would be most wholly right, as I was just now saying, to describe this as in very truth falsehood—ignorance namely in the soul of the man deceived. For the falsehood in words is a copy of the affection in the soul,

greek_head: ἄκρατον ψεῦδος ἢ οὐχ οὕτω Πάνυ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: akratos yeudos h ou outws panu men oun o men dh o eimi yeudos ou monos upo qea alla kai upo anqrwpos misew dokew egw tis de dh o en o logos yeudos pote kai o xrhsimos wste mh acios eimi misos ara ou pros te o polemios kai o kalew filos otan dia mania h tis anoia kakos tis epixeirew prassw tote apotroph eneka ws farmakon xrhsimos gignomai kai en os nundi
english_head: an after-rising image of it and not an altogether unmixed falsehood. Is not that so? By all means. Essential falsehood, then, is hated not only by gods but by men. I agree. But what of the falsehood in words, when and for whom is it serviceable so as not to merit abhorrence? Will it not be against enemies? And when any of those whom we call friends owing to madness or folly attempts to do some wrong, does it not then become useful

greek_head: ἐλέγομεν ταῖς μυθολογίαις διὰ τὸ μὴ εἰδέναι ὅπῃ τἀληθὲς ἔχει περὶ τῶν παλαιῶν ἀφομοιοῦντες τῷ ἀληθεῖ τὸ ψεῦδος
greek_tokens: legw o muqologia dia o mh oida oph alhqhs exis peri o palaios afomoiow o alhqhs o yeudos ostis malista outws xrhsimos poiew kai mala eimi de os outws exis kata tis dh oun outos o qeaw o yeudos xrhsimos poteros dia o mh oida o palaios afomoiow an yeudw geloios mentoi eaw fhmi poihths men ara yeudhs en qeaw ou en ou egw dokew alla deidw o exqros yeudw
english_head: to avert the evil—as a medicine? And also in the fables of which we were just now speaking owing to our ignorance of the truth about antiquity, we liken the false to the true as far as we may and so make it edifying. We most certainly do, he said. Tell me, then, on which of these grounds falsehood would be serviceable to God. Would he because of his ignorance of antiquity make false likenesses of it? An absurd supposition, that, he said. Then there is no lying poet in God. I think not.

greek_head: Πολλοῦ γε δεῖ Ἀλλὰ δι' οἰκείων ἄνοιαν ἢ μανίαν
greek_tokens: polus ge deomai alla dia oikew anoia h mania alla oudeis fhmi o anohtos kai mainomai qeofilhs ou ara eimi e eneka an qeos yeudw ou eimi panth ara ayeudhs o daimonion te kai o qew pantapasi men oun fhmi komidh ara o qeos aploos kai alhqhs en te ergon kai logos kai oute autos meqisthmi oute allos ecapataw oute kata fantasia oute kata logos oute kata shmeion pomph oute upar oude onar
english_head: Well then, would it be through fear of his enemies that he would lie? Far from it. Would it be because of the folly or madness of his friends? Nay, no fool or madman is a friend of God. Then there is no motive for God to deceive. None. From every point of view the divine and the divinity are free from falsehood. By all means. Then God is altogether simple and true in deed and word, and neither changes himself nor deceives others by visions or words or the sending of signs

greek_head: Οὕτως ἔφη ἔμοιγε καὶ αὐτῷ φαίνεται σοῦ λέγοντος Συγχωρεῖς ἄρα ἔφην τοῦτον δεύτερον τύπον εἶναι ἐν ᾧ
greek_tokens: outws fhmi egw kai autos fainw sew legw sugxwrew ara fhmi outos deuteros tupon eimi en os deomai peri qea kai legw kai poiew ws mhte autos gohs eimi o metaballw eautou mhte egw yeudis paragw en logos h en ergon sugxwrew polus ara omhros epainew alla outos ou epainew o o enupnion pomph upo zeus o agamemnwn oude aisxulos otan fhmi o qetis o apolouw en o eautou
english_head: in waking or in dreams. I myself think so, he said, when I hear you say it. You concur then, I said, this as our second norm or canon for speech and poetry about the gods,—that they are neither wizards in shape-shifting nor do they mislead us by falsehoods in words or deed? I concur. Then, though there are many other things that we praise in Homer, this we will not applaud, the sending of the dream by Zeus to Agamemnon, nor shall we approve of Aeschylus when his Thetis avers that

greek_head: γάμοις ᾄδοντα ἐνδατεῖσθαι τὰς ἑὰς εὐπαιδίας νόσων τ' ἀπείρους καὶ μακραίωνας βίους
greek_tokens: gamos aeidw endateomai o eos eupaidia nosos su apeiros kai makraiwn bios sumpas su eipon qeofilhs emos tuxh paian epeufhmew euqumew egw agos o foibos qew ayeudhs stoma elpizw eimi mantikos bruon texnh o de autos umnew autos en qoinh pareimi autos ode eipon autos eimi o kteinw o pais o emos
english_head: Apollo singing at her wedding, foretold the happy fortunes of her issue Hom. Il. 2.1 — Their days prolonged, from pain and sickness free, And rounding out the tale of heaven’s blessings, Raised the proud paean, making glad my heart. And I believed that Phoebus’ mouth divine, Filled with the breath of prophecy, could not lie. But he himself, the singer, himself who sat At meat with us, himself who promised all, Is now himself the slayer of my son. Aesch. Frag. 350

greek_head: ὅταν τις τοιαῦτα λέγῃ περὶ θεῶν χαλεπανοῦμέν τε καὶ χορὸν οὐ δώσομεν οὐδὲ τοὺς διδασκάλους ἐάσομεν ἐπὶ
greek_tokens: otan tis toioutos legos peri qea xalepainw te kai xoros ou didwmi oude o didaskalos eaw epi paideia xraomai o neos ei mellw egw e fulac qeosebew te kai qeios gignomai kaqa osos anqrwpos epi pleistos oios te pantapasi fhmi egw o tupos outos sugxwrew kai ws nomos an xraomai
english_head: When anyone says that sort of thing about the gods, we shall be wroth with him, we will refuse him a chorus, neither will we allow teachers to use him for the education of the young if our guardians are to be god-fearing men and god-like in so far as that is possible for humanity. By all means, he said, I accept these norms and would use them as canons and laws.

greek_head: Τὰ μὲν δὴ περὶ θεούς ἦν δ' ἐγώ τοιαῦτ' ἄττα ὡς ἔοικεν ἀκουστέον τε καὶ οὐκ ἀκουστέον εὐθὺς ἐκ παίδων τοῖς θεούς
greek_tokens: o men dh peri qeos eimi de egw toioutos atta ws eoika akousteon te kai ou akousteon euqus ek pais o qeos te timaw kai goneus o te allhlwn filios mh peri mikros poiew kai oimh ge fhmi orqos egw fainw tis de dh ei mellw eimi andreios ara ou outos te lekteos kai oios autos poiew hkistos o qanatos deidw
english_head: Concerning the gods then, said I, this is the sort of thing that we must allow or not allow them to hear from childhood up, if they are to honor the gods and their fathers and mothers, and not to hold their friendship with one another in light esteem. That was our view and I believe it right. What then of this? If they are to be brave, must we not extend our prescription to include also the sayings that will make them least likely

greek_head: ἢ ἡγῇ τινά ποτ' ἂν γενέσθαι ἀνδρεῖον ἔχοντα ἐν αὑτῷ τοῦτο τὸ δεῖμα
greek_tokens: h hgeomai tis pote an gignomai andreios exw en eautou outos o demw ma dios eimi de os ou egw tis de en adhs hgeomai eimi te kai deinos eimi oiomai tis qanatos adehs eimi kai en o maxh airew pro hssa te kai douleios qanatos oudamos deomai dh ws eoika egw epistatew kai peri outos o muqos o epixeirew legw kai deomai mh loidorew aplos outws o en adhs alla mallon epainew ws oute alhqhs
english_head: to fear death? Or do you suppose that anyone could ever become brave who had that dread in his heart? No indeed, I do not, he replied. And again if he believes in the reality of the underworld and its terrors, do you think that any man will be fearless of death and in battle will prefer death to defeat and slavery? By no means. Then it seems we must exercise supervision also, in the matter of such tales as these, over those who undertake to supply them and request them not to dispraise in this undiscriminating fashion the life in Hades but rather praise it,

greek_head: ἂν λέγοντας οὔτε ὠφέλιμα τοῖς μέλλουσιν μαχίμοις ἔσεσθαι Δεῖ μέντοι ἔφη
greek_tokens: an legw oute wfelimos o mellw maximos eimi deomai mentoi fhmi ecaleifw ara eimi de egw apo ode o epos arxw pas o toioutos boulomai an eparouros eimi qhteuw allos andris para aklhros os mh biotos polus eaw h pas nekus katafqinw anassw kai o
english_head: since what they now tell us is neither true nor edifying to men who are destined to be warriors. Yes, we must, he said. Then, said I, beginning with this verse we will expunge everything of the same kind: Liefer were I in the fields up above to be serf to another Tiller of some poor plot which yields him a scanty subsistence, Than to be ruler and king over all the dead who have perished, Aesch. Frag. 350 and this:

greek_head: οἰκία δὲ θνητοῖσι καὶ ἀθανάτοισι φανείη σμερδαλέ' εὐρώεντα τά τε στυγέουσι θεοί περ

english_head: Lest unto men and immortals the homes of the dead be uncovered Horrible, noisome, dank, that the gods too hold in abhorrence, Hom. Il. 20.64 and: Ah me! so it is true that e’en in the dwellings of Hades Spirit there is and wraith, but within there is no understanding, Hom. Il. 10.495 and this: Sole to have wisdom and wit, but the others are shadowy phantoms, Hom. Il. 23.103 and: Forth from his limbs unwilling his spirit flitted to Hades, Wailing its doom and its lustihood lost and the May of its manhood, Hom. Il. 16.856

greek_head: καὶ τὸ ψυχὴ δὲ κατὰ χθονός ἠΰτε καπνός

english_head: and: Under the earth like a vapor vanished the gibbering soul, Hom. Il. 23.100 and: Even as bats in the hollow of some mysterious grotto Fly with a flittermouse shriek when one of them falls from the cluster Whereby they hold to the rock and are clinging the one to the other, Flitted their gibbering ghosts. Hom. Od. 24.6-10

greek_head: ταῦτα καὶ τὰ τοιαῦτα πάντα παραιτησόμεθα Ὅμηρόν τε καὶ τοὺς ἄλλους ποιητὰς μὴ χαλεπαίνειν ἂν διαγράφωμεν οὐχ
greek_tokens: outos kai o toioutos pas paraiteomai omhros te kai o allos poihths mh xalepainw an diagrafw ou ws ou poihtikos kai hdus o polus akouw alla osos poihtikos tosoutos hsswn akousteon pais kai anhr os deomai eleuqeros eimi douleios qanatos mallon fobew pantapasi men oun oukoun eti kai o peri outos onoma pas o deinos te kai foberos apoblhteos kwkutos te kai stuc kai
english_head: We will beg Homer and the other poets not to be angry if we cancel those and all similar passages, not that they are not poetic and pleasing to most hearers, but because the more poetic they are the less are they suited to the ears of boys and men who are destined to be free and to be more afraid of slavery than of death. By all means. Then we must further taboo in these matters the entire vocabulary of terror and fear, Cocytus

greek_tokens: eneroi kai alibas kai allos osos outos o tuphs onomazw frissw dh poiew ws oiomai pas o akouw kai isos eu exis pros allos tis egw de uper o fulac fobew mh ek o toioutos frikh qermos kai malakos o deon gignomai egw kai orqos ge fhmi fobew afaireteos ara nai o de enantion tupon outos lekteos te kai poihteos dhlos dh
english_head: named of lamentation loud, abhorred Styx, the flood of deadly hate, the people of the infernal pit and of the charnel-house, and all other terms of this type, whose very names send a shudder through all the hearers every year. And they may be excellent for other purposes, but we are in fear for our guardians lest the habit of such thrills make them more sensitive and soft than we would have them. And we are right in so fearing. We must remove those things then? Yes. And the opposite type to them is what we must require in speech and in verse? Obviously. And shall we also do away with the

greek_head: Καὶ τοὺς ὀδυρμοὺς ἄρα ἐξαιρήσομεν καὶ τοὺς οἴκτους τοὺς τῶν ἐλλογίμων ἀνδρῶν
greek_tokens: kai o odurmos ara ecairew kai o oiktos o o ellogimos androw anagkh fhmi eiper kai o proteros skopaw dh eimi de egw ei orqos ecairew h ou fhmi de dh ostis o epieikhs anhr o epieikhs ouper kai etairos eimi o qnhskw ou deinos ageomai fhmi gar ou ara uper ge ekeinos ws deinos tis pasxw oduromai an ou dhta alla meis kai ode legw ws o toioutos malista autos eautou autarkhs pros o eu zaw kai diaferontws o
english_head: wailings and lamentations of men of repute? That necessarily follows, he said, from the other. Consider, said I, whether we shall be right in thus getting rid of them or not. What we affirm is that a good man will not think that for a good man, whose friend he also is, death is a terrible thing. Yes, we say that. Then it would not be for his friend’s sake as if he had suffered something dreadful that he would make lament. Certainly not. But we also say this, that such a one is most of all men sufficient unto himself

greek_head: ἄλλων ἥκιστα ἑτέρου προσδεῖται Ἀληθῆ ἔφη
greek_tokens: allos hkistos eteros prosdeomai alhqhs fhmi hkistos ara autos deinos sterew uios h adelfos h xrhma h allos tis o toioutos hkistos mentoi hkistos ara kai oduromai ferw de ws praos otan tis autos toioutos sumfora katalambanw polus ge orqos ara an ecairew o qrhnos o onomasths androw gunh de apodidwmi kai oude outos spoudaios
english_head: for a good life and is distinguished from other men in having least need of anybody else. True, he replied. Least of all then to him is it a terrible thing to lose son or brother or his wealth or anything of the sort. Least of all. Then he makes the least lament and bears it most moderately when any such misfortune overtakes him. Certainly. Then we should be right in doing away with the lamentations of men of note and in attributing them to women,

greek_head: καὶ ὅσοι κακοὶ τῶν ἀνδρῶν ἵνα ἡμῖν δυσχεραίνωσιν ὅμοια τούτοις ποιεῖν οὓς δή φαμεν ἐπὶ φυλακῇ τῆς χώρας
greek_tokens: kai osos kakos o androw ina egw dusxerainw omoios outos poiew os dh fhmi epi fulakeus o xwra trefw orqos fhmi palin dh omhros te dew kai o allos poihths mh poiew axilleus qea pais allote epi pleura katakeimai allote de aute uptios allote de prhnhs tote de orqos anisthmi plwizw aluw epi
english_head: and not to the most worthy of them either, and to inferior men, in that those whom we say we are breeding for the guardianship of the land may disdain to act like these. We should be right, said he. Again then we shall request Homer and the other poets not to portray Achilles, the son of a goddess, as, Lying now on his side, and then again on his back, And again on his face, Hom. Il. 24.10-12 and then rising up and Drifting distraught on the shore of the waste unharvested ocean, Hom. Il. 24.10-12

Hom. Il. 22.414-415 And yet more than this shall we beg of them at least not to describe the gods as lamenting and crying,

Il. 18.54 and if they will so picture the gods at least not to have the effrontery to present so unlikely a likeness of the supreme god as to make him say: Out on it, dear to my heart is the man whose pursuit around Troy-town I must behold with my eyes while my spirit is grieving within me, Hom. Il. 22.168 and: Ah, woe is me! of all men to me is Sarpedon the dearest,

english_head: Fated to fall by the hands of Patroclus, Menoitius’ offspring. Hom. Il. 16.433-434 For if, dear Adeimantus, our young men should seriously incline to listen to such tales and not laugh at them as unworthy utterances, still less surely would any man be to think such conduct unworthy of himself and to rebuke himself if it occurred to him to do or say anything of that kind, but without shame or restraint full many a dirge for trifles would he chant and many a lament.

english_head: You say most truly, he replied. But that must not be, as our reasoning but now showed us, in which we must put our trust until someone convinces with a better reason. No, it must not be. Again, they must not be prone to laughter. For ordinarily when one abandons himself to violent laughter his condition provokes a violent reaction. I think so, he said. Then if anyone represents men of worth as overpowered

english_head: by laughter we must accept it, much less if gods. Much indeed, he replied. Then we must not accept from Homer such sayings as these either about the gods: Quenchless then was the laughter that rose from the blessed immortals When they beheld Hephaestus officiously puffing and panting. Hom. Il. 1.599-600 — we must not accept it on your view. If it pleases you

english_head: to call it mine, he said; at any rate we must not accept it. But further we must surely prize truth most highly. For if we were right in what we were just saying and falsehood is in very deed useless to gods, but to men useful as a remedy or form of medicine, it is obvious that such a thing must be assigned to physicians and laymen should have nothing to do with it. Obviously, he replied. The rulers then of the city may, if anybody, fitly lie on account of enemies or citizens for the benefit of the state; no others may have anything to do with it,

greek_head: ἀλλὰ πρός γε δὴ τοὺς τοιούτους ἄρχοντας ἰδιώτῃ ψεύσασθαι ταὐτὸν καὶ μεῖζον ἁμάρτημα φήσομεν ἢ κάμνοντι πρὸς ἰατρὸν

english_head: but for a layman to lie to rulers of that kind we shall affirm to be as great a sin, nay a greater, than it is for a patient not to tell physician or an athlete his trainer the truth about his bodily condition, or for a man to deceive the pilot about the ship and the sailors as to the real condition of himself or a fellow-sailor, and how they fare. Most true, he replied. If then

english_head: the ruler catches anybody else in the city lying, any of the craftsmen Whether a prophet or healer of sickness or joiner of timbers, Hom. Od. 17.383-384 he will chastise him for introducing a practice as subversive and destructive of a state as it is of a ship. He will, he said, if deed follows upon word. Again, will our lads not need the virtue of self-control? Of course. And for the multitude are not the main points of self-control these—to be obedient to their rulers and themselves to be rulers

english_head: over the bodily appetites and pleasures of food, drink, and the rest? I think so. Then, I take it, we will think well said such sayings as that of Homer’s Diomede: Friend, sit down and be silent and hark to the word of my bidding, Hom. Il. 4.412 and what follows: Breathing high spirit the Greeks marched silently fearing their captains, Hom. Il. 3.8 and all similar passages. Yes, well said. But what of this sort of thing? Heavy with wine with the eyes of a dog and the heart of a fleet deer, Hom. Il. 1.225

english_head: and the lines that follow, are these well—and other impertinences in prose or verse of private citizens to their rulers? They are not well. They certainly are not suitable for youth to hear for the inculcation of self-control. But if from another point of view they yield some pleasure we must not be surprised, or what is your view of it? This, he said. Again, to represent the wisest man as saying that this seems to him the fairest thing in the world, When the bounteous tables are standing

Od. 9.8-10 —do you think the hearing of that sort of thing will conduce to a young man’s temperance or self-control? or this: Hunger is the most piteous death that a mortal may suffer. Hom. Od. 12.342 Or to hear how Zeus lightly forgot all the designs which he devised,

english_head: watching while the other gods slept, because of the excitement of his passions, and was so overcome by the sight of Hera that he is not even willing to go to their chamber, but wants to lie with her there on the ground and says that he is possessed by a fiercer desire than when they first consorted with one another, Deceiving their dear parents. Hom. Il. 14.296 Nor will it profit them to hear of Hephaestus’s fettering Ares and Aphrodite for a like motive. No, by Zeus, he said,

english_head: I don’t think it will. But any words or deeds of endurance in the face of all odds attributed to famous men are suitable for our youth to see represented and to hear, such as: He smote his breast and chided thus his heart, Endure, my heart, for worse hast thou endured. Hom. Od. 20.17-18 By all means, he said. It is certain that we cannot allow our men to be acceptors of bribes or greedy for gain.

english_head: By no means. Then they must not chant: Gifts move the gods and gifts persuade dread kings. unknown Nor should we approve Achilles’ attendant Phoenix as speaking fairly when he counselled him if he received gifts for it to defend the Achaeans, but without gifts not to lay aside his wrath; nor shall we think it proper nor admit that Achilles himself was so greedy as to accept gifts from Agamemnon and again to give up a dead body after receiving payment

english_head: but otherwise to refuse. It is not right, he said, to commend such conduct. But, for Homer’s sake, said I, I hesitate to say that it is positively impious to affirm such things of Achilles and to believe them when told by others; or again to believe that he said to Apollo Me thou hast baulked, Far-darter, the most pernicious of all gods, Mightily would I requite thee if only my hands had the power. Hom. Il. 22.15

english_head: And how he was disobedient to the river, who was a god and was ready to fight with him, and again that he said of the locks of his hair, consecrated to her river Spercheius: This let me give to take with him my hair to the hero, Patroclus, Hom. Il. 23.151 who was a dead body, and that he did so we must believe. And again the trailings of Hector’s body round the grave of Patroclus and the slaughter of the living captives upon his pyre, all these we will affirm to be lies,

english_head: nor will we suffer our youth to believe that Achilles, the son of a goddess and of Peleus the most chaste of men, grandson of Zeus, and himself bred under the care of the most sage Cheiron, was of so perturbed a spirit as to be affected with two contradictory maladies, the greed that becomes no free man and at the same time overweening arrogance towards gods and men. You are right, he said. Neither, then, said I, must we believe this or suffer it to be said, that Theseus, the son of Poseidon,

greek_head: ὥρμησαν οὕτως ἐπὶ δεινὰς ἁρπαγάς μηδέ τιν' ἄλλον θεοῦ παῖδά τε καὶ ἥρω τολμῆσαι ἂν δεινὰ καὶ ἀσεβῆ ἐργάσασθαι

english_head: and Peirithous, the son of Zeus, attempted such dreadful rapes, nor that any other child of a god and hero would have brought himself to accomplish the terrible and impious deeds that they now falsely relate of him. But we must constrain the poets either to deny that these are their deeds or that they are the children of gods, but not to make both statements or attempt to persuade our youth that the gods are the begetters of evil, and that heroes are no better than men.

english_head: For, as we were saying, such utterances are both impious and false. For we proved, I take it, that for evil to arise from gods is an impossibility. Certainly. And they are furthermore harmful to those that hear them. For every man will be very lenient with his own misdeeds if he is convinced that such are and were the actions of The near-sown seed of gods, Close kin to Zeus, for whom on Ida’s top Ancestral altars flame to highest heaven, Nor in their life-blood fails the fire divine. Aesch. Niobe Fr. For which cause we must put down such fables, lest they breed

english_head: in our youth great laxity in turpitude. Most assuredly. What type of discourse remains for our definition of our prescriptions and proscriptions? We have declared the right way of speaking about gods and daemons and heroes and that other world. We have. Speech, then, about men would be the remainder. Obviously. It is impossible for us, my friend, to place this here. Why? Because I presume we are going to say that so it is that both poets

greek_head: κακῶς λέγουσιν περὶ ἀνθρώπων τὰ μέγιστα ὅτι εἰσὶν ἄδικοι μὲν εὐδαίμονες πολλοί δίκαιοι δὲ ἄθλιοι καὶ ὡς λυσιτελεῖ
greek_tokens: kakos legw peri anqrwpos o megas ostis eimi adikos men eudaimwn polus dikaios de aqlios kai ws lusitelew o adikew ean lanqanw o de dikaiosunh allotrios men agaqos oikeios de zhmia kai o men toioutos aperaw legw o de enantios outos prosstazw aeidw te kai muqologew h ou oiomai eu men oun fhmi oida oukoun ean omologew orqos egw legw fhmi su omologew o pala zhtew orqos fhmi upolambanw
english_head: and writers of prose speak wrongly about men in matters of greatest moment, saying that there are many examples of men who, though unjust, are happy, and of just men who are wretched, and that there is profit in injustice if it be concealed, and that justice is the other man’s good and your own loss; and I presume that we shall forbid them to say this sort of thing and command them to sing and fable the opposite. Don’t you think so? Nay, I well know it, he said. Then, if you admit that I am right, I will say that you have conceded the original point of our inquiry?

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν περί γε ἀνθρώπων ὅτι τοιούτους δεῖ λόγους λέγεσθαι τότε διομολογησόμεθα ὅταν εὕρωμεν οἷόν ἐστιν
greek_tokens: oukoun peri ge anqrwpos ostis toioutos deomai logos legw tote diomologew otan euriskw oios eimi dikaiosunh kai ws fusis lusitelew o exw ean dokazw ean mh toioutos eimi alhqhs fhmi o men dh logos peri exw telos o de lecis ws egw oimh meta outos skepteos kai egw o te lekteos kai ws lekteos pantelhs skeptomai kai o adeimantos outos eimi de os ou manqanw ostis legw
english_head: Rightly apprehended, he said. Then, as regards men that speech must be of this kind, that is a point that we will agree upon when we have discovered the nature of justice and the proof that it is profitable to its possessor whether he does or does not appear to be just. Most true, he replied. So this concludes the topic of tales. That of diction, I take it, is to be considered next. So we shall have completely examined both the matter and the manner of speech. And Adeimantus said, I don’t understand what

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μέντοι ἦν δ' ἐγώ δεῖ γε ἴσως οὖν τῇδε μᾶλλον εἴσῃ ἆρ' οὐ πάντα ὅσα ὑπὸ μυθολόγων ἢ ποιητῶν λέγεται
greek_tokens: alla mentoi eimi de egw deomai ge isos oun ode mallon eidomai ara ou pas osos upo muqologos h poihths legw dihghsis eimi tugxanw h gignomai h eimi h mellw tis gar fhmi allos ara oun ou htoi aploos dihghsis h dia mimhsis gignomai h dia amfoteros perainw kai outos eimi de os eti deomai safhs manqanw geloios eimi de egw eoika didaskalos eimi kai asafhs wsper oun e adunatos legw ou kata oloc alla apolambanw
english_head: you mean by this. Well, said I, we must have you understand. Perhaps you will be more likely to apprehend it thus. Is not everything that is said by fabulists or poets a narration of past, present, or future things? What else could it be? he said. Do not they proceed either by pure narration or by a narrative that is effected through imitation, or by both? This too, he said, I still need to have made plainer. I seem to be a ridiculous and obscure teacher, I said; so like men who are unable to express themselves

greek_head: μέρος τι πειράσομαί σοι ἐν τούτῳ δηλῶσαι ὃ βούλομαι καί μοι εἰπέ ἐπίστασαι τῆς Ἰλιάδος τὰ πρῶτα ἐν οἷς ὁ
greek_tokens: meros tis peiraw su en outos dhlow o boulomai kai egw eipon efisthmi o ilias o proteros en os o poihths fhmi o men xrush deomai o agamemnwn apoluw o qugathr o de xalepainw o de epei
english_head: I won’t try to speak in wholes and universals but will separate off a particular part and by the example of that try to show you my meaning. Tell me. Do you know the first lines if the Iliad in which the poet says that Chryses implored Agamemnon to release his daughter, and that the king was angry and that Chryses,

greek_head: οὐκ ἐτύγχανεν κατεύχεσθαι τῶν Ἀχαιῶν πρὸς τὸν θεόν Ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: ou tugxanw kateuxomai o axaia pros o qeos egw oistos oun ostis mexri men outos o epos kai elissw pas axaios atreidhs de malista duo kosmhtwr laos legw te autos o poihths kai oude epixeirew egw o dianoia allote trepw ws allos tis o legos h autos o de meta outos wsper autos eimi o xrush legw kai peiraw
english_head: failing of his request, imprecated curses on the Achaeans in his prayers to the god? I do. You know then that as far as these verses, And prayed unto all the Achaeans, Chiefly to Atreus’ sons, twin leaders who marshalled the people, Hom. Il. 1.15 the poet himself is the speaker and does not even attempt to suggest to us that anyone but himself is speaking.

greek_head: ἡμᾶς ὅτι μάλιστα ποιῆσαι μὴ Ὅμηρον δοκεῖν εἶναι τὸν λέγοντα ἀλλὰ τὸν ἱερέα πρεσβύτην ὄντα καὶ τὴν ἄλλην
greek_tokens: egw ostis malista poiew mh omhros dokew eimi o legw alla o iereia presbuths eimi kai o allos dh pas sxedon tis outws poiew dihghsis peri te o en ilion kai peri o en iqakh kai oloc odusseia paqhma panu men oun fhmi oukoun dihghsis men eimi kai otan o rhsis ekastote legos kai otan o metacu o rhsis pws gar ou
english_head: But what follows he delivers as if he were himself Chryses and tries as far as may be to make us feel that not Homer is the speaker, but the priest, an old man. And in this manner he has carried in nearly all the rest of his narration about affairs in Ilion, all that happened in Ithaca, and the entire Odyssey. Quite so, he said. Now, it is narration, is it not, both when he presents the several speeches and the matter between the speeches? Of course. But when he delivers a speech

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ὅταν γέ τινα λέγῃ ῥῆσιν ὥς τις ἄλλος ὤν ἆρ' οὐ τότε ὁμοιοῦν αὐτὸν φήσομεν ὅτι μάλιστα τὴν αὑτοῦ λέξιν
greek_tokens: alla otan ge tis legos reomai os tis allos eimi ara ou tote omoiow autos fhmi ostis malista o eautou lecis ekastos os an proeipon ws erew fhmi tis gar oukoun o ge omoiow eautou allos h kata fwnh h kata sxhma mimeomai eimi ekeinos os an tis omoiow tis meis en dh o toioutos ws eoika outos te kai e allos poihths dia mimhsis o dihghsis poiew panu men oun ei de ge mhdamos eautou apokruptw o poihths pas
english_head: as if he were someone else, shall we not say that he then assimilates thereby his own diction is far as possible to that of the person whom he announces as about to speak? We shall obviously. And is not likening one’s self to another speech or bodily bearing an imitation of him to whom one likens one’s self? Surely. In such case then it appears he and the other poets effect their narration through imitation. Certainly. But if the poet should conceal himself nowhere, then his entire poetizing and narration would have been accomplished without imitation.

greek_head: ἂν αὐτῷ ἄνευ μιμήσεως ἡ ποίησίς τε καὶ διήγησις γεγονυῖα εἴη ἵνα δὲ μὴ εἴπῃς ὅτι οὐκ αὖ μανθάνεις ὅπως ἂν τοῦτο
greek_tokens: an autos aneu mimhsis o poihsis te kai dihghsis gignomai eaw ina de mh eipon ostis ou au manqanw opws an outos gignomai egw frazw ei gar omhros eipon ostis erxomai o xrush o te qugathr lutron ferw kai ikeths o axaia malista de o basilh meta outos mh ws xrush gignomai legw alla eti ws omhros oistos ostis ou an mimhsis eimi alla aploos dihghsis exw de an wde pws frazw de aneu metron ou gar eimi poihtikos erxomai
english_head: And lest you may say again that you don’t understand, I will explain to you how this would be done. If Homer, after telling us that Chryses came with the ransom of his daughter and as a suppliant of the Achaeans but chiefly of the kings, had gone on speaking not as if made or being Chryses but still as Homer, you are aware that it would not be imitation but narration, pure and simple. It would have been somewhat in this wise. I will state it without meter for I am not a poet:

greek_head: ὁ ἱερεὺς ηὔχετο ἐκείνοις μὲν τοὺς θεοὺς δοῦναι ἑλόντας τὴν Τροίαν αὐτοὺς σωθῆναι τὴν δὲ θυγατέρα οἱ λῦσαι δεξαμένους
greek_tokens: o iereus euxomai ekeinos men o qeos didwmi airew o troia autos swzw o de qugathr e luw dexomai apoina kai o qeos aideomai outos de eipon autos e men allos sebomai kai sunainew o de agamemnwn agriainw entellw nun te apeimi kai auqis mh erxomai mh autos o te skhptron kai o o qeaomai stemma ou eparkew prin de luw autos o qugathr en argew fhmi ghraskw meta e apeimi de keleuw kai mh ereqizw
english_head: the priest came and prayed that to them the gods should grant to take Troy and come safely home, but that they should accept the ransom and release his daughter, out of reverence for the god; and when he had thus spoken the others were of reverent mind and approved, but Agamemnon was angry and bade him depart and not come again lest the scepter and the fillets of the god should not avail him. And ere his daughter should be released, he said, she would grow old in Argos with himself, and he ordered him to be off and not vex him if he wished to get home safe.

greek_head: ἵνα σῶς οἴκαδε ἔλθοι ὁ δὲ πρεσβύτης ἀκούσας ἔδεισέν τε καὶ ἀπῄει σιγῇ ἀποχωρήσας δὲ ἐκ τοῦ στρατοπέδου πολλὰ
greek_tokens: ina sos oikade erxomai o de presbuths aekwn deidw te kai apeimi sigaw apoxwrew de ek o stratopedon polus o apollwn euxomai o te epwnumios o qeaomai anakalew kai upomimnhskw kai apaitew ei tis pwpote h en naos oikodomhsis h en iera qusia xarizomai dwreomai os dh xaris kateuxomai tinw o axaios o o dakruon o ekeinos belos outws eimi de egw eimi etairos aneu
english_head: And the old man on hearing this was frightened and departed in silence, and having gone apart from the camp he prayed at length to Apollo, invoking the appellations of the god, and reminding him of and asking requital for any of his gifts that had found favor whether in the building of temples or the sacrifice of victims. In return for these things he prayed that the Achaeans should suffer for his tears by the god’s shafts. It is in this way, my dear fellow, I said, that

greek_head: μιμήσεως ἁπλῆ διήγησις γίγνεται Μανθάνω ἔφη
greek_tokens: mimhsis aploos dihghsis gignomai manqanw fhmi manqanw toinun eimi de egw ostis outos au enantios gignomai otan tis o o poihths o metacu o rhsis ecairew o amoibaios katalimpanw kai outos fhmi manqanw ostis eimi o peri o tragwdia toioutos orqos fhmi upolambanw kai oimh su hdh dhlow o emprosqen ou oios su eimi ostis o poihsis te kai muqologia
english_head: without imitation simple narration results. I understand, he said. Understand then, said I, that the opposite of this arises when one removes the words of the poet between and leaves the alternation of speeches. This too I understand, he said, —it is what happens in tragedy. You have conceived me most rightly, I said, and now I think I can make plain to you what I was unable to before, that there is one kind of poetry and tale-telling which works wholly through imitation,

greek_head: ἡ μὲν διὰ μιμήσεως ὅλη ἐστίν ὥσπερ σὺ λέγεις τραγῳδία τε καὶ κωμῳδία ἡ δὲ δι' ἀπαγγελίας αὐτοῦ τοῦ
greek_tokens: o men dia mimhsis oloc eimi wsper su legw tragwdia te kai kwmwdia o de dia apaggelia autos o poihths euriskw de an autos malista pou en diqurambos o de au dia amfoteros en te o o epos poihsis pollaxou de kai alloqi ei egw manqanw alla sunihmi fhmi o tote boulomai legw kai o pro outos dh anamimnhskw ostis fhmi o men lekteos hdh erw ws de lekteos eti skepteos eimi alla mimnhskw
english_head: as you remarked, tragedy and comedy; and another which employs the recital of the poet himself, best exemplified, I presume, in the dithyramb; and there is again that which employs both, in epic poetry and in many other places, if you apprehend me. I understand now, he said, what you then meant. Recall then also the preceding statement that we were done with the what of speech and still had to consider the ’how.’ I remember.

greek_head: Τοῦτο τοίνυν αὐτὸ ἦν ὃ ἔλεγον ὅτι χρείη διομολογήσασθαι πότερον ἐάσομεν τοὺς ποιητὰς μιμουμένους ἡμῖν
greek_tokens: outos toinun autos eimi o elegos ostis xreos diomologew poteros eaw o poihths mimeomai egw o dihghsis poiew h o men mimeomai o de mh kai opoios ekateros h oude mimeomai manteuomai fhmi skopaw su eite paradexomai tragwdia te kai kwmwdia eis o polis eite kai ou isos eimi de egw isos de kai plew eti outos ou gar dh egw pw oida alla oph an o logos wsper pneuma ferw outos iteon kai kalos ge fhmi legw
english_head: What I meant then was just this, that we must reach a decision whether we are to suffer our poets to narrate as imitators or in part as imitators and in part not, and what sort of things in each case, or not allow them to imitate at all. I divine, he said, that you are considering whether we shall admit tragedy and comedy into our city or not. Perhaps, said I, and perhaps even more than that. For I certainly do not yet know myself, but whithersoever the wind, as it were, of the argument blows, there lies our course.

greek_head: Τόδε τοίνυν ὦ Ἀδείμαντε ἄθρει πότερον μιμητικοὺς ἡμῖν δεῖ εἶναι τοὺς φύλακας ἢ οὔ ἢ καὶ τοῦτο τοῖς ἔμπροσθεν
greek_tokens: ode toinun eimi adeimantos aqrew poteros mimhtikos egw deomai eimi o fulac h ou h kai outos o emprosqen epomai ostis eis ekastos eis men an epithdeuma kalos epithdeuw polus de ou alla ei outos epixeirew polus efaptw pas apotugxanw an wste eimi pou ellogimos tis de ou mellw oukoun kai peri mimhsis o autos logos ostis polus o autos mimeomai eu wsper eis ou dunatos ou gar oun
english_head: Well said, he replied. This then, Adeimantus, is the point we must keep in view, do we wish our guardians to be good mimics or not? Or is this also a consequence of what we said before, that each one could practise well only one pursuit and not many, but if he attempted the latter, dabbling in many things, he would fail of distinction in all? Of course it is. And does not the same rule hold for imitation, that the same man is not able to imitate many things well as he can one? No, he is not. Still less, then, will he be able to combine

greek_head: Σχολῇ ἄρα ἐπιτηδεύσει γέ τι ἅμα τῶν ἀξίων λόγου ἐπιτηδευμάτων καὶ πολλὰ μιμήσεται καὶ ἔσται μιμητικός
greek_tokens: sxolazw ara epithdeusis ge tis ama o acios logos epithdeuma kai polus mimeomai kai eimi mimhtikos epei pou oude o dokew eggus allhlwn eimi duo mimhma dunamai e autos ama eu mimeomai oios kwmwdia kai tragwdia poiew h ou mimhma arti outos kalew egw kai alhqhs ge legw ostis ou dunamai e autos oude meis raywdos ge kai upokriths ama alhqhs alla oude su upokriths kwmwdew te kai tragwdew e
english_head: the practice of any worthy pursuit with the imitation of many things and the quality of a mimic; since, unless I mistake, the same men cannot practise well at once even the two forms of imitation that appear most nearly akin, as the writing of tragedy and comedy? Did you not just now call these two imitations? I did, and you are right in saying that the same men are not able to succeed in both, nor yet to be at once good rhapsodists and actors. True. But

greek_head: αὐτοί πάντα δὲ ταῦτα μιμήματα ἢ οὔ Μιμήματα
greek_tokens: autos pas de outos mimhma h ou mimhma kai eti ge outos eimi adeimantos fainw egw eis mikros katakermatizw o o anqrwpos fusis wste adunatos eimi polus kalos mimeomai h autos ekeinos prassw os dh kai o mimhma eimi afomoiwma alhqhs eimi de os ei ara o proteros logos diaswzw o fulac egw o allos pas dhmiourgia afihmi dew eimi
english_head: neither can the same men be actors for tragedies and comedies—and all these are imitations, are they not? Yes, imitations. And to still smaller coinage than this, in my opinion, Adeimantus, proceeds the fractioning of human faculty, so as to be incapable of imitating many things or of doing the things themselves of which the imitations are likenesses. Most true, he replied. If, then, we are to maintain our original principle, that our guardians, released from all other crafts,

greek_head: δημιουργοὺς ἐλευθερίας τῆς πόλεως πάνυ ἀκριβεῖς καὶ μηδὲν ἄλλο ἐπιτηδεύειν ὅτι μὴ εἰς τοῦτο φέρει οὐδὲν δὴ δέοι ἂν
greek_tokens: dhmiourgos eleuqeria o polis panu akribhs kai mhdeis allos epithdeuw ostis mh eis outos ferw oudeis dh dew an autos allos prassw oude mimeomai ean de mimeomai mimeomai o outos proshkw euqus ek pais andreios swfrwn osios eleuqeros kai o toioutos pas o de aneleuqeros mhte poiew mhte deinos eimi mimeomai mhde allos mhdeis o aisxros ina mh ek o mimhsis o eimi
english_head: are to be expert craftsmen of civic liberty, and pursue nothing else that does not conduce to this, it would not be fitting for these to do nor yet to imitate anything else. But if they imitate they should from childhood up imitate what is appropriate to them—men, that is, who are brave, sober, pious, free and all things of that kind; but things unbecoming the free man they should neither do nor be clever at imitating, nor yet any other shameful thing, lest from the imitation

greek_head: ἀπολαύσωσιν ἢ οὐκ ᾔσθησαι ὅτι αἱ μιμήσεις ἐὰν ἐκ νέων πόρρω διατελέσωσιν εἰς ἔθη τε καὶ φύσιν καθίστανται καὶ
greek_tokens: apolauw h ou aisqanomai ostis o mimhsis ean ek neos prosw diatelew eis eqos te kai fus kaqisthmi kai kata swma kai fwnh kai kata o dianoia kai mala eimi de os ou dh epitrepw eimi de egw os fhmi khdw kai dew autos anhr agaqos gignomai gunh mimeomai anhr eimi h nea h presbus h andris loidorew h pros qeos erizw te kai megalauxew oiomai
english_head: they imbibe the reality. Or have you not observed that imitations, if continued from youth far into life, settle down into habits and (second) nature in the body, the speech, and the thought? Yes, indeed, said he. We will not then allow our charges, whom we expect to prove good men, being men, to play the parts of women and imitate a woman young or old wrangling with her husband, defying heaven, loudly boasting, fortunate in her own conceit, or involved in misfortune

greek_head: εὐδαίμονα εἶναι ἢ ἐν συμφοραῖς τε καὶ πένθεσιν καὶ θρήνοις ἐχομένην κάμνουσαν δὲ ἢ ἐρῶσαν ἢ ὠδίνουσαν πολλοῦ καὶ
greek_tokens: eudaimwn eimi h en sumfora te kai penqos kai qrhnos exw kamnw de h eraw h wdinw polus kai dew pantapasi men oun eimi de os oude ge doulh te kai doulos prassw osos doulos oude outos oude ge anhr kakos ws eoika deilos te kai o enantios prassw os nundi eipon kakhgorew te kai kwmwdew allhlwn kai aisxrologew mequw h
english_head: and possessed by grief and lamentation—still less a woman that is sick, in love, or in labor. Most certainly not, he replied. Nor may they imitate slaves, female and male, doing the offices of slaves. No, not that either. Nor yet, as it seems, bad men who are cowards and who do the opposite of the things we just now spoke of, reviling and lampooning one another, speaking foul words in their cups or when sober

greek_head: καὶ νήφοντας ἢ καὶ ἄλλα ὅσα οἱ τοιοῦτοι καὶ ἐν λόγοις καὶ ἐν ἔργοις ἁμαρτάνουσιν εἰς αὑτούς τε καὶ εἰς ἄλλους οἶμαι
greek_tokens: kai nhfw h kai allos osos e toioutos kai en logos kai en ergon amartanw eis eautou te kai eis allos oimh de oude mainomai eqisteon afomoiow eautou en logos oude en ergon gnwsteos men gar kai mainomai kai ponhros anhr te kai gunh poihteos de oudeis outos oude mimhteos alhqhs fhmi tis de eimi de egw xalkeuw h tis allos dhmiourgew
english_head: and in other ways sinning against themselves and others in word and deed after the fashion of such men. And I take it they must not form the habit of likening themselves to madmen either in words nor yet in deeds. For while knowledge they must have both of mad and bad men and women, they must do and imitate nothing of this kind. Most true, he said. What of this? I said, —are they to imitate smiths and other craftsmen or the rowers of triremes and those who call the time to them or other things

greek_head: ἢ ἐλαύνοντας τριήρεις ἢ κελεύοντας τούτοις ἤ τι ἄλλο τῶν περὶ ταῦτα μιμητέον
greek_tokens: h elaunw trihrhs h keleuw outos h tis allos o peri outos mimhteos kai pws fhmi os ge oude prosexw o new outos oudeis ecesti tis de ippos xremetizw kai tauros mukaomai kai potamos yofew kai qalassa ktupew kai bronth kai pas au o toioutos eimi mimeomai alla apeirhtos autos fhmi mhte mainomai mhte mainomai afomoiow ei ara eimi de egw manqanw o su legw eimi tis eidos lecis te kai dihghsis en os an dihgeomai o o eimi kalos
english_head: connected therewith? How could they, he said, since it will be forbidden them even to pay any attention to such things? Well, then, neighing horses and lowing bulls, and the noise of rivers and the roar of the sea and the thunder and everything of that kind—will they imitate these? Nay, they have been forbidden, he said, to be mad or liken themselves to madmen. If, then, I understand your meaning, said I, there is a form of diction and narrative in which

greek_head: κἀγαθός ὁπότε τι δέοι αὐτὸν λέγειν καὶ ἕτερον αὖ ἀνόμοιον τούτῳ εἶδος οὗ ἂν ἔχοιτο ἀεὶ καὶ ἐν ᾧ διηγοῖτο ὁ ἐναντίως
greek_tokens: agaqos opote tis dew autos legw kai eteros au anomoios outos eidos e an exw aei kai en os dihgeomai o enantios ekeinos fus te kai trefw poios dh fhmi outos o men egw dokew eimi de egw metrios anhr epeidan afikneomai en o dihghsis epi lecis tis h pracis anhr agaqos eqelw ws autos eimi ekeinos apaggellw kai ou aisxunw epi o toioutos mimhsis malista men mimeomai
english_head: the really good and true man would narrate anything that he had to say, and another form unlike this to which the man of the opposite birth and breeding would cleave and which he would tell his story. What are these forms? he said. A man of the right sort, I think, when he comes in the course of his narrative to some word or act of a good man will be willing to impersonate the other in reporting it, and will feel no shame at that kind of mimicry, by preference imitating the good man

greek_head: τὸν ἀγαθὸν ἀσφαλῶς τε καὶ ἐμφρόνως πράττοντα ἐλάττω δὲ καὶ ἧττον ἢ ὑπὸ νόσων ἢ ὑπὸ ἐρώτων ἐσφαλμένον ἢ καὶ ὑπὸ
greek_tokens: o agaqos asfalhs te kai emfrwn prassw elasswn de kai hsswn h upo nosos h upo erws sfallw h kai upo meqh h tis allos sumfora otan de gignomai kata tis eautou anacios ou eqelw spoudazw apeikazw eautou o xeirwn ei mh ara kata braxus otan tis xrhstos poiew alla aisxunw ama men agumnastos eimi o mimeomai o toioutos ama de kai dusxerainw eautou ekmassw te kai
english_head: when he acts steadfastly and sensibly, and less and more reluctantly when he is upset by sickness or love or drunkenness or any other mishap. But when he comes to someone unworthy of himself, he will not wish to liken himself in earnest to one who is inferior, except in the few cases where he is doing something good, but will be embarrassed both because he is unpractised in the mimicry of such characters, and also because he shrinks in distaste from molding and fitting himself the types of baser things.

greek_head: ἐνιστάναι εἰς τοὺς τῶν κακιόνων τύπους ἀτιμάζων τῇ διανοίᾳ ὅτι μὴ παιδιᾶς χάριν

english_head: His mind disdains them, unless it be for jest. Naturally, he said. Then the narrative that he will employ will be the kind that we just now illustrated by the verses of Homer, and his diction will be one that partakes of both, of imitation and simple narration, but there will be a small portion of imitation in a long discourse—or is there nothing in what I say? Yes, indeed, he said, that is the type and pattern of such a speaker. Then, said I,

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὁ μὴ τοιοῦτος αὖ ὅσῳ ἂν φαυλότερος ᾖ πάντα τε μᾶλλον διηγήσεται καὶ οὐδὲν ἑαυτοῦ ἀνάξιον
greek_tokens: oukoun eimi de egw o mh toioutos au osos an faulos eimi pas te mallon dihgeomai kai oudeis eautou anacios oiomai eimi wste pas epixeirhsis mimeomai spoudazw te kai enantion polus kai o nundi legw bronth te kai yofos anemos te kai xalaza kai acwn te kai troxilia kai salpigc kai aulew kai surigc kai pas organon fwnh kai eti kuwn kai probaton kai orneon fqoggos
english_head: the other kind speaker, the more debased he is the less will he shrink from imitating anything and everything. He will think nothing unworthy of himself, so that he will attempt, seriously and in the presence of many, to imitate all things, including those we just now mentioned—claps of thunder, and the noise of wind and hail and axles and pulleys, and the notes of trumpets and flutes and pan-pipes, and the sounds of all instruments, and the cries of dogs, sheep, and birds; and so his style will depend wholly on imitation

greek_head: καὶ ἔσται δὴ ἡ τούτου λέξις ἅπασα διὰ μιμήσεως φωναῖς τε καὶ σχήμασιν ἢ σμικρόν τι διηγήσεως ἔχουσα

english_head: in voice and gesture, or will contain but a little of pure narration. That too follows of necessity, he said. These, then, said I, were the two types of diction of which I was aking. There are those two, he replied. Now does not one of the two involve slight variations, and if we assign a suitable pitch and rhythm to the diction, is not the result that the right speaker speaks almost on the same note and in one cadence—for the changes are slight—

greek_head: ῥυθμῷ ὡσαύτως παραπλησίῳ τινί Κομιδῇ μὲν οὖν ἔφη οὕτως ἔχει
greek_tokens: ruqmos wsautws paraplhsios tis komidh men oun fhmi outws exis tis de o o eteros eidos ou o enantios deomai pas men armonia pas de ruqmos ei mellw au oikeios legw dia o pantodapos morfh o metabolh exw kai sfodra ge outws exis ara oun pas e poihths kai e tis legw h o eteros outos epitugxanw tupon o lecis h o eteros h ek amfoteros tis sugkerannumi anagkh fhmi
english_head: and similarly in a rhythm of nearly the same kind? Quite so. But what of the other type? Does it not require the opposite, every kind of pitch and all rhythms, if it too is to have appropriate expression, since it involves manifold forms of variation? Emphatically so. And do all poets and speakers hit upon one type or the other of diction or some blend which they combine of both?

greek_head: Τί οὖν ποιήσομεν ἦν δ' ἐγώ πότερον εἰς τὴν πόλιν πάντας τούτους παραδεξόμεθα ἢ τῶν ἀκράτων τὸν ἕτερον ἢ

english_head: They must, he said. What, then, said I, are we to do? Shall we admit all of these into the city, or one of the unmixed types, or the mixed type? If my vote prevails, he said, the unmixed imitator of the good. Nay, but the mixed type also is pleasing, Adeimantus, and far most pleasing to boys and their tutors and the great mob is the opposite of your choice. Most pleasing it is. But perhaps, said I, you would affirm it to be ill-suited

greek_head: ἡμετέρᾳ πολιτείᾳ ὅτι οὐκ ἔστιν διπλοῦς ἀνὴρ παρ' ἡμῖν οὐδὲ πολλαπλοῦς ἐπειδὴ ἕκαστος ἓν πράττει
greek_tokens: hmeteros politeia ostis ou eimi diploos anhr para egw oude pollaploos epei ekastos eis prassw ou gar oun armozw oukoun dia outos en monos o toioutos polis o te skutotomos skutotomos euriskw kai ou kubernaw pros o skutotomia kai o gewrgos gewrgos kai ou dikasths pros o gewrgia kai o polemikos polemikos kai ou xrhmatisths pros o polemikos kai pas outws alhqhs fhmi
english_head: to our polity, because there is no twofold or manifold man among us, since every man does one thing. It is not suited. And is this not the reason why such a city is the only one in which we shall find the cobbler a cobbler and not a pilot in addition to his cobbling, and the farmer a farmer and not a judge added to his farming, and the soldier a soldier and not a money-maker in addition to his soldiery, and so of all the rest? True, he said. If a man, then, it seems,

greek_head: Ἄνδρα δή ὡς ἔοικε δυνάμενον ὑπὸ σοφίας παντοδαπὸν γίγνεσθαι καὶ μιμεῖσθαι πάντα χρήματα εἰ ἡμῖν ἀφίκοιτο
greek_tokens: anhr dh ws eoika dunamai upo sofia pantodapos gignomai kai mimeomai pas xrhma ei egw afikneomai eis o polis autos te kai o poihma boulomai epideiknumi proskunew an autos ws ieron kai qaumastos kai hdus eipon de an ostis ou eimi toioutos anhr en o polis para egw oute qemis eggignomai apopempw te eis allos polis muron kata o kefalh kataxew kai erion stefw autos de an o austhros kai ahdhs poihths
english_head: who was capable by his cunning of assuming every kind of shape and imitating all things should arrive in our city, bringing with himself the poems which he wished to exhibit, we should fall down and worship him as a holy and wondrous and delightful creature, but should say to him that there is no man of that kind among us in our city, nor is it lawful for such a man to arise among us, and we should send him away to another city, after pouring myrrh down over his head and crowning him with fillets of wool, but we ourselves, for our souls’ good, should continue to employ

greek_head: χρῴμεθα καὶ μυθολόγῳ ὠφελίας ἕνεκα ὃς ἡμῖν τὴν τοῦ ἐπιεικοῦς λέξιν μιμοῖτο καὶ τὰ λεγόμενα λέγοι ἐν ἐκείνοις
greek_tokens: xraomai kai muqologos wfeleia eneka os egw o o epieikhs lecis mimeomai kai o legw legos en ekeinos o tupon os kata arxh nomoqetew oste o stratiwths epixeirew paideuw kai mala fhmi outws an poiew ei epi egw eaw nun dh eipon egw eimi filos kinduneuw egw o mousikh o peri logos te kai muqos pantelhs diaperainw o te gar lekteos kai ws lekteos eromai kai autos egw dokew fhmi
english_head: the more austere and less delightful poet and tale-teller, who would imitate the diction of the good man and would tell his tale in the patterns which we prescribed in the beginning, when we set out to educate our soldiers. We certainly should do that if it rested with us. And now, my friend, said I, we may say that we have completely finished the part of music that concerns speeches and tales. For we have set forth what is to be said and how it is to be said. I think so too, he replied.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν μετὰ τοῦτο ἦν δ' ἐγώ τὸ περὶ ᾠδῆς τρόπου καὶ μελῶν λοιπόν

english_head: After this, then, said I, comes the manner of song and tunes? Obviously. And having gone thus far, could not everybody discover what we must say of their character in order to conform to what has already been said? I am afraid that everybody does not include me, laughed Glaucon; I cannot sufficiently divine off-hand what we ought to say, though I have a suspicion. You certainly, I presume, said I,

greek_head: λέγειν ὅτι τὸ μέλος ἐκ τριῶν ἐστιν συγκείμενον λόγου τε καὶ ἁρμονίας καὶ ῥυθμοῦ

english_head: have sufficient a understanding of this—that the song is composed of three things, the words, the tune, and the rhythm? Yes, said he, that much. And so far as it is words, it surely in no manner differs from words not sung in the requirement of conformity to the patterns and manner that we have prescribed? True, he said. And again, the music and the rhythm must follow the speech. Of course. But we said we did not require dirges and lamentations in words. We do not. What, then,

greek_head: Τίνες οὖν θρηνώδεις ἁρμονίαι λέγε μοι σὺ γὰρ μουσικός Μειξολυδιστί ἔφη καὶ συντονολυδιστὶ καὶ τοιαῦταί τινες
greek_tokens: tinw oun qrhnwdhs armonia legos egw su gar mousikos micoludisti fhmi kai suntonoludisti kai toioutos tis oukoun outos eimi de egw afaireteos axrhstos gar kai gunh os deomai epieikhs eimi mh ostis anhr panu ge alla meis meqh ge fulac aprephs kai malakia kai argia pws gar ou tinw oun malakos te kai sumpotikos o armonia iasti eimi de os kai ludisti au tis xalaros kalew
english_head: are the dirge-like modes of music? Tell me, for you are a musician. The mixed Lydian, he said, and the tense or higher Lydian, and similar modes. These, then, said I, we must do away with. For they are useless even to women who are to make the best of themselves, let alone to men. Assuredly. But again, drunkenness is a thing most unbefitting guardians, and so is softness and sloth. Yes. What, then, are the soft and convivial modes? There are certain Ionian and also Lydian modes

greek_head: Ταύταις οὖν ὦ φίλε ἐπὶ πολεμικῶν ἀνδρῶν ἔσθ' ὅτι χρήσῃ
greek_tokens: outos oun eimi filos epi polemikos androw esqw ostis xraw oudamos fhmi alla kinduneuw su dwristi leipw kai frugisti ou oida fhmi egw o armonia alla katalimpanw ekeinos o armonia o en te polemikos prassw eimi andreios kai en paomai biaios ergasia prepontws an mimeomai fqoggos te kai proswdia kai apotugxanw h eis trauma h eis
english_head: that are called lax. Will you make any use of them for warriors? None at all, he said; but it would seem that you have left the Dorian and the Phrygian. I don’t know the musical modes, I said, but leave us that mode that would fittingly imitate the utterances and the accents of a brave man who is engaged in warfare or in any enforced business, and who, when he has failed, either meeting wounds or death or having fallen into some other mishap,

greek_head: θανάτους ἰόντος ἢ εἴς τινα ἄλλην συμφορὰν πεσόντος ἐν πᾶσι τούτοις παρατεταγμένως καὶ καρτερούντως ἀμυνομένου
greek_tokens: qanatos eimi h eis tis allos sumfora piptw en pas outos paratassw kai karterountws amunw o tuxh kai allos au en eirhnikos te kai mh biaios alla en ekousios prassw eimi h tis tis peiqw te kai deomai h euxh qeos h didaxh kai nouqethsis anqrwpos h enantion allos deomai h didaskw h metapeiqw eautou epwxato kai ek outos prassw kata new kai mh uperhfanos exw alla swfrwn te kai metrios en pas outos
english_head: in all these conditions confronts fortune with steadfast endurance and repels her strokes. And another for such a man engaged in works of peace, not enforced but voluntary, either trying to persuade somebody of something and imploring him—whether it be a god, through prayer, or a man, by teaching and admonition—or contrariwise yielding himself to another who petitioning or teaching him or trying to change his opinions, and in consequence faring according to his wish, and not bearing himself arrogantly, but in all this acting modestly and moderately

greek_head: πράττοντά τε καὶ τὰ ἀποβαίνοντα ἀγαπῶντα ταύτας δύο ἁρμονίας βίαιον ἑκούσιον δυστυχούντων εὐτυχούντων

english_head: and acquiescing in the outcome. Leave us these two modes—the forced and the voluntary—that will best imitate the utterances of men failing or succeeding, the temperate, the brave—leave us these. Well, said he, you are asking me to leave none other than those I just spoke of. Then, said I, we shall not need in our songs and airs instruments of many strings or whose compass includes all the harmonies. Not in my opinion, said he. Then we shall not maintain makers of triangles and harps and all other

greek_head: πολύχορδα καὶ πολυαρμόνια δημιουργοὺς οὐ θρέψομεν Οὐ φαινόμεθα
greek_tokens: poluxordos kai poluarmonios dhmiourgos ou trefw ou fainw tis de aulopoios h aulhths paradexomai eis o polis h ou outos poluxordos kai autos o panarmonios aulew tugxanw eimi mimhma dhlos dh eimi de os lura dh su eimi de egw kai kiqara leipw kai kata polis xrhsimos kai au kata agros o nomeus surigc an tis eaw ws goun fhmi o logos egw shmainw
english_head: many stringed and poly-harmonic instruments. Apparently not. Well, will you admit to the city flute-makers and flute-players? Or is not the flute the most many-stringed of instruments and do not the pan-harmonics themselves imitate it? Clearly, he said. You have left, said I, the lyre and the cither. These are useful in the city, and in the fields the shepherds would have a little piccolo to pipe on. So our argument indicates, he said.

greek_head: Οὐδέν γε ἦν δ' ἐγώ καινὸν ποιοῦμεν ὦ φίλε κρίνοντες τὸν Ἀπόλλω καὶ τὰ τοῦ Ἀπόλλωνος ὄργανα πρὸ Μαρσύου

english_head: We are not innovating, my friend, in preferring Apollo and the instruments of Apollo to Marsyas and his instruments. No, by heaven! he said, I think not. And by the dog, said I, we have all unawares purged the city which a little while ago we said was wanton. In that we show our good sense, he said. Come then, let us complete the purification. For upon harmonies would follow the consideration of rhythms: we must not pursue complexity nor great variety in the basic movements, but must observe what are the rhythms of a life that is orderly and brave, and after observing them

greek_head: πόδα τῷ τοῦ τοιούτου λόγῳ ἀναγκάζειν ἕπεσθαι καὶ τὸ μέλος ἀλλὰ μὴ λόγον ποδί τε καὶ μέλει οἵτινες δ' ἂν εἶεν οὗτοι
greek_tokens: pous o o toioutos logos anagkazw epomai kai o melos alla mh logos podis te kai melos ostis de an eien outos e ruqmoomai sos ergnumi wsper o armonia frazw alla ma diw fhmi ou exw legw ostis men gar treis atta eimi eidos ek os o basis plekw wsper en o fqoggos tessares oqen o paomai armonia qeaomai an eipon poios de opoios bios mimhma legw ou exw
english_head: require the foot and the air to conform to that kind of man’s speech and not the speech to the foot and the tune. What those rhythms would be, it is for you to tell us as you did the musical modes. Nay, in faith, he said, I cannot tell. For that there are some three forms from which the feet are combined, just as there are four in the notes of the voice whence come all harmonies, is a thing that I have observed and could tell. But which are imitations of which sort of life, I am unable to say.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ ταῦτα μέν ἦν δ' ἐγώ καὶ μετὰ Δάμωνος βουλευσόμεθα τίνες τε ἀνελευθερίας καὶ ὕβρεως ἢ μανίας καὶ
