english_head: Well, said I, on this point we will take counsel with Damon, too, as to which are the feet appropriate to illiberality, and insolence or madness or other evils, and what rhythms we must leave for their opposites; and I believe I have heard him obscurely speaking of a foot that he called the enoplios, a composite foot, and a dactyl and an heroic foot, which he arranged, I know not how, to be equal up and down in the interchange of long and short, and unless I am mistaken he used the term iambic, and there was another foot that he called the trochaic,

greek_head: βραχύτητας προσῆπτε καὶ τούτων τισὶν οἶμαι τὰς ἀγωγὰς τοῦ ποδὸς αὐτὸν οὐχ ἧττον ψέγειν τε καὶ ἐπαινεῖν ἢ τοὺς
greek_tokens: braxuths prosaptw kai outos tis oimh o agwgh o pous autos ou hsswn yegw te kai epainew h o ruqmos autos htoi sunamfoteroi tis ou gar exw legw alla outos men wsper eipon eis damwn anaballw diairew gar ou mikros logos h su oiomai ma diw ou egw alla ode ge ostis o o eusxhmosunh te kai asxhmosunh o euruqmos te kai arruqmos akolouqew dunamai diairew pws de ou
english_head: and he added the quantities long and short. And in some of these, I believe, he censured and commended the tempo of the foot no less than the rhythm itself, or else some combination of the two; I can’t say. But, as I said, let this matter be postponed for Damon’s consideration. For to determine the truth of these would require no little discourse. Do you think otherwise? No, by heaven, I do not. But this you are able to determine—that seemliness and unseemliness are attendant upon the good rhythm and the bad. Of course. And, further, that

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν τὸ εὔρυθμόν γε καὶ τὸ ἄρρυθμον τὸ μὲν τῇ καλῇ λέξει ἕπεται ὁμοιούμενον τὸ δὲ τῇ ἐναντίᾳ καὶ τὸ
greek_tokens: alla meis o euruqmos ge kai o arruqmos o men o kalew lecis epomai omoiow o de o enantios kai o euarmostos kai anarmostos wsautws eiper ruqmos ge kai armonia logos wsper arti legw alla mh logos outos alla meis eimi de os outos ge logos akolouqhteon tis de o tropos o lecis eimi de egw kai o logos ou o o yuxazw hqos epomai pws gar ou o de lecis o allos nai eulogia ara kai euarmostia kai eusxhmosunh kai euruqmia
english_head: good rhythm and bad rhythm accompany, the one fair diction, assimilating itself thereto, and the other the opposite, and so of the apt and the unapt, if, as we were just now saying, the rhythm and harmony follow the words and not the words these. They certainly must follow the speech, he said. And what of the manner of the diction, and the speech? said I. Do they not follow and conform to the disposition of the soul? Of course. And all the rest to the diction? Yes. Good speech, then, good accord, and good grace,

greek_head: εὐηθείᾳ ἀκολουθεῖ οὐχ ἣν ἄνοιαν οὖσαν ὑποκοριζόμενοι καλοῦμεν ὡς εὐήθειαν ἀλλὰ τὴν ὡς ἀληθῶς εὖ τε καὶ
greek_tokens: euhqeia akolouqew ou os anoia eimi upokorizomai kalew ws euhqeia alla o ws alhqhs eu te kai kalos o hqos kataskeuazw dianoia pantapasi men oun fhmi ara oun ou pantaxou outos diwkteos o neos ei mellw o eautou prassw diwkteos men oun
english_head: and good rhythm wait upon good disposition, not that weakness of head which we euphemistically style goodness of heart, but the truly good and fair disposition of the character and the mind. By all means, he said. And must not our youth pursue these everywhere if they are to do what it is truly theirs to do? They must indeed. And there is surely much of these qualities in painting

greek_head: Ἔστιν δέ γέ που πλήρης μὲν γραφικὴ αὐτῶν καὶ πᾶσα ἡ τοιαύτη δημιουργία πλήρης δὲ ὑφαντικὴ καὶ ποικιλία καὶ
greek_tokens: eimi de ge pou plhrhs men grafikos autos kai pas o toioutos dhmiourgia plhrhs de ufantikos kai poikilia kai oikodomia kai pas au o o allos skeuos ergasia eti de o o swma fusis kai o o allos futon en pas gar outos eneimi eusxhmosunh h asxhmosunh kai o men asxhmosunh kai arruqmia kai anarmostia kakologia kai kakohqeia adelfh o de enantios o enantios swfrwn te kai agaqos hqos adelfh te kai mimhma pantelhs men oun fhmi
english_head: and in all similar craftsmanship—weaving is full of them and embroidery and architecture and likewise the manufacture of household furnishings and thereto the natural bodies of animals and plants as well. For in all these there is grace or gracelessness. And gracelessness and evil rhythm and disharmony are akin to evil speaking and the evil temper but the opposites are the symbols and the kin of the opposites, the sober and good disposition. Entirely so, he said.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν τοῖς ποιηταῖς ἡμῖν μόνον ἐπιστατητέον καὶ προσαναγκαστέον τὴν τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ εἰκόνα ἤθους ἐμποιεῖν
greek_tokens: ara oun o poihths egw monos epistathteon kai prosanagkasteon o o agaqos eikwn hqos empoiew o poihma h mh para egw poiew h kai o allos dhmiourgew epistathteon kai diakwluteon o kakohqhs outos kai akolastos kai aneleuqeros kai asxhmos mhte en eoika zwion mhte en oikodomhma mhte en allos mhdeis dhmiourgew empoiew h o mh oios te eimi ou eateos para egw dhmiourgew ina mh en kakia eoika trefw egw e
english_head: Is it, then, only the poets that we must supervise and compel to embody in their poems the semblance of the good character or else not write poetry among us, or must we keep watch over the other craftsmen, and forbid them to represent the evil disposition, the licentious, the illiberal, the graceless, either in the likeness of living creatures or in buildings or in any other product of their art, on penalty, if unable to obey, of being forbidden to practise their art among us, that our guardians may not be bred among symbols of evil, as it were

greek_head: φύλακες ὥσπερ ἐν κακῇ βοτάνῃ πολλὰ ἑκάστης ἡμέρας κατὰ σμικρὸν ἀπὸ πολλῶν δρεπόμενοί τε καὶ νεμόμενοι ἕν
greek_tokens: fulac wsper en kakazw botanh polus ekastos hmeros kata mikros apo polus drepw te kai nemw eis tis sunisthmi lanqanw kakos megas en o eautou yuxw alla ekeinos zhthteos o dhmiourgos o eufuhs dunamai ixneuw o o kalew te kai eusxhmwn fus ina wsper en ugieinos topos oikew e neos apo pas wfelew opoqen an autos apo o kalh ergnumi h pros oyis h pros akoh tis prosballw wsper aura ferw apo
english_head: in a pasturage of poisonous herbs, lest grazing freely and cropping from many such day by day they little by little and all unawares accumulate and build up a huge mass of evil in their own souls. But we must look for those craftsmen who by the happy gift of nature are capable of following the trail of true beauty and grace, that our young men, dwelling as it were in a salubrious region, may receive benefit from all things about them, whence the influence that emanates from works of beauty may waft itself to eye or ear like a breeze that brings from wholesome places health,

greek_head: χρηστῶν τόπων ὑγίειαν καὶ εὐθὺς ἐκ παίδων λανθάνῃ εἰς ὁμοιότητά τε καὶ φιλίαν καὶ συμφωνίαν τῷ καλῷ λόγῳ
greek_tokens: xrhstos topos ugieia kai euqus ek pais lanqanw eis omoioths te kai filios kai sumfwnia o kalos logos agw polus gar an fhmi kallista outws trefw ara oun eimi de egw eimi glauc outos eneka kurios en mousikh trofh ostis malista kataduw eis o entos o yuxazw o te ruqmos kai armonia kai errwmenos aptw autos ferw o eusxhmosunh kai poiew eusxhmwn
english_head: and so from earliest childhood insensibly guide them to likeness, to friendship, to harmony with beautiful reason. Yes, he said, that would be far the best education for them. And is it not for this reason, Glaucon, said I, that education in music is most sovereign, because more than anything else rhythm and harmony find their way to the inmost soul and take strongest hold upon it, bringing with them and imparting grace, if one is rightly trained,

greek_head: ἐάν τις ὀρθῶς τραφῇ εἰ δὲ μή τοὐναντίον καὶ ὅτι αὖ τῶν παραλειπομένων καὶ μὴ καλῶς δημιουργηθέντων ἢ μὴ καλῶς
greek_tokens: ean tis orqos trefw ei de mh enantion kai ostis au o paraleipw kai mh kalos dhmiourgew h mh kalos fus ocus an aisqanomai o ekei trefw ws dew kai orqos dh dusxerainw o men kalos epainew kai xairw kai katadexomai eis o yuxh trefw an apo autos kai
english_head: and otherwise the contrary? And further, because omissions and the failure of beauty in things badly made or grown would be most quickly perceived by one who was properly educated in music, and so, feeling distaste rightly, he would praise beautiful things and take delight in them and receive them into his soul to foster its growth and become himself beautiful and good.

greek_head: γίγνοιτο καλός τε κἀγαθός τὰ δ' αἰσχρὰ ψέγοι τ' ἂν ὀρθῶς καὶ μισοῖ ἔτι νέος ὤν πρὶν λόγον δυνατὸς εἶναι λαβεῖν
greek_tokens: gignomai kalos te agaqos o de aisxros yegw su an orqos kai misew eti neos eimi prin logos dunatos eimi lambanw erxomai de o logos aspazomai an autos gnwrizw dia oikeioths malista o outws trefw egw goun dokew fhmi o toioutos eneka en mousikh eimi o trofh wsper ara eimi de egw gramma peri tote ikanos exw oste o stoixeion mh lanqanw egw oligos eimi en apas os eimi periferw kai oute en mikros oute en
english_head: The ugly he would rightly disapprove of and hate while still young and yet unable to apprehend the reason, but when reason came the man thus nurtured would be the first to give her welcome, for by this affinity he would know her. I certainly think, he said, that such is the cause of education in music. It is, then, said I, as it was when we learned our letters and felt that we knew them sufficiently only when the separate letters did not elude us, appearing as few elements in all the combinations that convey them, and when we did not disregard them

greek_head: μεγάλῳ ἠτιμάζομεν αὐτά ὡς οὐ δέοι αἰσθάνεσθαι ἀλλὰ πανταχοῦ προυθυμούμεθα διαγιγνώσκειν ὡς οὐ πρότερον
greek_tokens: megas atimazw autos ws ou dew aisqanomai alla pantaxou proqumeomai diagignwskw ws ou proteros eimi grammatikos prin outws exw alhqhs oukoun kai eikwn gramma ei pou h en udwr h en katoptron emfainw ou proteros gignwskw prin an autos gignwskw alla eimi o autos texnh te kai meleth pantapasi men oun ara oun o legos pros qea outws oude mousikos proteros
english_head: in small things or great and think it unnecessary to recognize them, but were eager to distinguish them everywhere, in the belief that we should never be literate and letter-perfect till we could do this. True. And is it not also true that if there are any likenesses of letters reflected in water or mirrors, we shall never know them until we know the originals, but such knowledge belongs to the same art and discipline? By all means. Then, by heaven, am I not right in saying that by the same token we shall never be true musicians, either—

greek_head: ἐσόμεθα οὔτε αὐτοὶ οὔτε οὕς φαμεν ἡμῖν παιδευτέον εἶναι τοὺς φύλακας πρὶν ἂν τὰ τῆς σωφροσύνης εἴδη καὶ
greek_tokens: eimi oute autos oute os fhmi egw paideuteos eimi o fulac prin an o o swfrosunos eidos kai andreia kai eleuqerioths kai megaloprepeia kai osos outos adelfh kai o outos au enantios pantaxou periferw gnwrizw kai eneimi en os eneimi aisqanomai kai autos kai eikwn autos kai mhte en mikros mhte en megas atimazw alla o autos oiomai texnh eimi kai meleth polus anagkh fhmi
english_head: neither we nor the guardians that we have undertaken to educate—until we are able to recognize the forms of soberness, courage, liberality, and high-mindedness and all their kindred and their opposites, too, in all the combinations that contain and convey them, and to apprehend them and their images wherever found, disregarding them neither in trifles nor in great things, but believing the knowledge of them to belong to the same art and discipline? The conclusion is inevitable, he said.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὅτου ἂν συμπίπτῃ ἔν τε τῇ ψυχῇ καλὰ ἤθη ἐνόντα καὶ ἐν τῷ εἴδει ὁμολογοῦντα ἐκείνοις καὶ
greek_tokens: oukoun eimi de egw ostis an sumpitnw en te o yuxw kalos hqos eneimi kai en o eidomai omologew ekeinos kai sumfwnew o autos metexw tuphs outos an eaw kalos qeama o dunamai qeaomai polus ge kai meis o ge kalos erasmios pws de ou o dh ostis malista toioutos anqrwpos o ge mousikos eraw an ei de asumfwnos eaw ou an eraw ou an ei ge tis fhmi kata o yuxh elleipw ei mentoi tis kata o swma upomenw an wste eqelw
english_head: Then, said I, when there is a coincidence of a beautiful disposition in the soul and corresponding and harmonious beauties of the same type in the bodily form—is not this the fairest spectacle for one who is capable of its contemplation? Far the fairest. And surely the fairest is the most lovable. Of course. The true musician, then, would love by preference persons of this sort; but if there were disharmony he would not love this. No, he said, not if there was a defect in the soul; but if it were in the body he would bear with it and still be willing to bestow his love.

greek_head: ἀσπάζεσθαι Μανθάνω ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὅτι ἔστιν σοι ἢ γέγονεν παιδικὰ
greek_tokens: aspazomai manqanw eimi de egw ostis eimi su h gignomai paidikon toioutos kai sugxwrew alla ode egw eipon swfrosunos kai hdona uperballw eimi tis koinwnia kai pws fhmi o ge ekfrwn poiew ou hsswn h luph alla o allh aretaw
english_head: I understand, I said, that you have or have had favorites of this sort and I grant your distinction. But tell me this—can there be any communion between soberness and extravagant pleasure? How could there be, he said, since such pleasure puts a man beside himself

greek_head: Οὐδαμῶς Τί δέ ὕβρει τε καὶ ἀκολασίᾳ
greek_tokens: oudamos tis de ubris te kai akolasia pas malista megas de tis kai ocus exis eipon hdona o peri o afrodisios ou exw eimi de os oude ge manikos o de orqos eros fuw kosmion te kai kalew swfrwn te kai mousikos era kai mala eimi de os oudeis ara prosoisteos manikos oude suggenhs akolasia o orqos erws ou prosoisteos
english_head: no less than pain? Or between it and virtue generally? By no means. But is there between pleasure and insolence and licence? Most assuredly. Do you know of greater or keener pleasure than that associated with Aphrodite? I don’t, he said, nor yet of any more insane. But is not the right love a sober and harmonious love of the orderly and the beautiful? It is indeed, said he. Then nothing of madness, nothing akin to licence, must be allowed to come nigh the right love? No. Then this kind of pleasure

greek_head: Οὐ προσοιστέον ἄρα αὕτη ἡ ἡδονή οὐδὲ κοινωνητέον αὐτῆς ἐραστῇ τε καὶ παιδικοῖς ὀρθῶς ἐρῶσί τε καὶ ἐρωμένοις
greek_tokens: ou prosoisteos ara outos o hdona oude koinwnhteon autos erasths te kai paidikon orqos eraw te kai eraomai ou mentoi ma diw fhmi eimi swkraths prosoisteos outws dh ws eoika nomoqethsis en o oikizw polis filew men kai suneimi kai aptw wsper uios paidikon erasths o kalh xaris ean peiqw o de allos outws omilew pros os tis spoudazw opws mhdepote docis makros
english_head: may not come nigh, nor may lover and beloved who rightly love and are loved have anything to do with it? No, by heaven, Socrates, he said, it must not come nigh them. Thus, then, as it seems, you will lay down the law in the city that we are founding, that the lover may kiss and pass the time with and touch the beloved as a father would a son, for honorable ends, if he persuade him. But otherwise he must so associate with the objects of his care that there should never be any suspicion of anything further,

greek_head: τούτων συγγίγνεσθαι εἰ δὲ μή ψόγον ἀμουσίας καὶ ἀπειροκαλίας ὑφέξοντα
greek_tokens: outos suggignomai ei de mh yogos amousia kai apeirokalia upexw outws fhmi ara oun eimi de egw kai sos fainw telos egw exw o peri mousikh logos e goun deomai teleutaw teleutaw deomai de pou teleutaw o mousikh eis o o kalew erwtikos sumfhmi eimi de os meta dh mousikh gumnastikos qrepteos e neanihs tis meis deomai men dh kai outos akribhs trefw ek pais dia
english_head: on penalty of being stigmatized for want of taste and true musical culture. Even so, he said. Do you not agree, then, that our discourse on music has come to an end? It has certainly made a fitting end, for surely the end and consummation of culture be love of the beautiful. I concur, he said. After music our youth are to be educated by gymnastics? Certainly. In this too they must be carefully trained

greek_head: βίου ἔχει δέ πως ὡς ἐγᾦμαι ὧδε σκόπει δὲ καὶ σύ ἐμοὶ μὲν γὰρ οὐ φαίνεται ὃ ἂν χρηστὸν ᾖ σῶμα τοῦτο τῇ
greek_tokens: bios exis de pws ws oimh wde skopaw de kai su egw men gar ou fainw o an xrhstos eimi swma outos o eautou aretaw yuxh agaqos poiew alla enantion yuxh agaqos o eautou aretaw swma parexw ws oios te beltistos sos de pws fainw kai egw fhmi outws oukoun ei o dianoia ikanos qerapeuw paradew autos o peri o swma akribologeomai egw de osos o
english_head: from boyhood through life, and the way of it is this, I believe; but consider it yourself too. For I, for my part, do not believe that a sound body by its excellence makes the soul good, but on the contrary that a good soul by its virtue renders the body the best that is possible. What is your opinion? I think so too. Then if we should sufficiently train the mind and turn over to it the minutiae of the care of the body,

greek_head: τύπους ὑφηγησαίμεθα ἵνα μὴ μακρολογῶμεν ὀρθῶς ἂν ποιοῖμεν
greek_tokens: tupos ufhgeomai ina mh makrologew orqos an poiew panu men oun meqh men dh eipon ostis afekteon autos pas gar pou mallon egxwrew h fulac mequskw mh oida opou gaia eimi geloios gar eimi de os o ge fulac fulac deomai tis de dh sitos peri aqlhths men gar e anhr o megas agwn h ou nai
english_head: and content ourselves with merely indicating the norms or patterns, not to make a long story of it, we should acting rightly? By all means. From intoxication we said that they must abstain. For a guardian is surely the last person in the world to whom it is allowable to get drunk and not know where on earth he is. Yes, he said, it would absurd that a guardian should need a guard. What next about their food? These men are athletes in the greatest of contests, are they not? Yes. Is, then, the bodily habit of the athletes we see about us suitable for such?

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ἡ τῶνδε τῶν ἀσκητῶν ἕξις προσήκουσ' ἂν εἴη τούτοις
greek_tokens: ara oun o ode o askhths ecis prosakouw an eaw outos isos alla eimi de egw upnwdhs outos ge tis kai sfaleros pros ugieia h ou oraw ostis kaqeudw te o bios kai ean mikros ekbazw o tassw diaita megas kai sfodra nosew outos e askhths oraw komyos dh tis eimi de egw askhsis deomai o polemikos aqlhths os ge wsper kuwn agrupnos te anagkh eimi kai ostis malista ocus oraw kai akouw kai polus metabolh en o strateia metaballw udwr
english_head: Perhaps. Nay, said I, that is a drowsy habit and precarious for health. Don’t you observe that they sleep away their lives, and that if they depart ever so little from their prescribed regimen these athletes are liable to great and violent diseases? I do. Then, said I, we need some more ingenious form of training for our athletes of war, since these must be as it were sleepless hounds, and have the keenest possible perceptions of sight and hearing, and in their campaigns undergo many changes

greek_head: τε καὶ τῶν ἄλλων σίτων καὶ εἱλήσεων καὶ χειμώνων μὴ ἀκροσφαλεῖς εἶναι πρὸς ὑγίειαν
greek_tokens: te kai o allos sitos kai eilhsis kai xeimwn mh akrosfalhs eimi pros ugieia fainw egw ara oun o beltistos gumnastikos adelfh tis an eaw o aploos mousikh os oligos proteros dieimi pws legw aploos pou kai epieikhs gumnastikos kai malista o o peri o polemos ph dh kai para omhros eimi de egw o ge toioutos manqanw an tis oida gar ostis epi stratia en o o hrws estiasis oute ixqus autos estiaw kai outos epi
english_head: in their drinking water, their food, and in exposure to the heat of the sun and to storms, without disturbance of their health. I think so. Would not, then, the best gymnastics be akin to the music that we were just now describing? What do you mean? It would be a simple and flexible gymnastic, and especially so in the training for war. In what way? One could learn that, said I, even from Homer. For you are aware that in the banqueting of the heroes on campaign he does not

greek_head: θαλάττῃ ἐν Ἑλλησπόντῳ ὄντας οὔτε ἑφθοῖς κρέασιν ἀλλὰ μόνον ὀπτοῖς ἃ δὴ μάλιστ' ἂν εἴη στρατιώταις εὔπορα
greek_tokens: qalassa en ellhspontos eimi oute efqos kreas alla monos optaw o dh malista an eaw stratiwths euporos pantaxou gar ws epos eipon autos o pur xraomai euporos h aggeion sumperiferw kai mala oude meis hdusma ws oimh omhros pwpote mimnhskw h outos men kai e allos askhths oida ostis o mellw swma eu exw afekteon o toioutos apas kai orqos ge fhmi oida te kai apexw
english_head: feast them on fish, nor on boiled meat, but only on roast, which is what soldiers could most easily procure. For everywhere, one may say, it is of easier provision to use the bare fire than to convey pots and pans along. Indeed it is. Neither, as I believe, does Homer ever make mention of sweet meats. Is not that something which all men in training understand—that if one is to keep his body in good condition he must abstain from such things altogether? They are right,

greek_head: Συρακοσίαν δέ ὦ φίλε τράπεζαν καὶ Σικελικὴν ποικιλίαν ὄψου ὡς ἔοικας οὐκ αἰνεῖς εἴπερ σοι ταῦτα δοκεῖ
greek_tokens: surakosios de eimi filos trapeza kai sikelikos poikilia oyon ws eoika ou ainew eiper su outos dokew orqos exw ou egw dokazw yegw ara kai korinqios korh filos eimi anhr mellw eu swma exw pantapasi men oun oukoun kai attikos pemma o dokew eimi eupaqeia anagkh oloc gar oimh o toioutos sithsis kai diaita o melopoiia te kai aoidh o en o panarmonios kai en pas
english_head: he said, in that they know it and do abstain. Then, my friend, if you think this is the right way, you apparently do not approve of a Syracusan table and Sicilian variety of made dishes. I think not. You would frown, then, on a little Corinthian maid as the chère amie of men who were to keep themselves fit? Most certainly. And also on the seeming delights of Attic pastry? Inevitably. In general, I take it, if we likened that kind of food and regimen to music and song expressed in the pan-harmonic mode and

greek_head: ῥυθμοῖς πεποιημένῃ ἀπεικάζοντες ὀρθῶς ἂν ἀπεικάζοιμεν Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: ruqmos poiew apeikazw orqos an apeikazw pws gar ou oukoun ekei men akolasia o poikilia entiktw entauqa de nosos o de aploths kata men mousikh en yuxh swfrosunos kata de gumnastikos en swma ugieia alhqhs fhmi
english_head: in every variety of rhythm it would be a fair comparison. Quite so. And here variety engendered licentiousness, did it not, but here disease? While simplicity in music begets sobriety in the souls, and in gymnastic training it begets health in bodies. Most true, he said. And when licentiousness

greek_head: Ἀκολασίας δὲ καὶ νόσων πληθυουσῶν ἐν πόλει ἆρ' οὐ δικαστήριά τε καὶ ἰατρεῖα πολλὰ ἀνοίγεται καὶ δικανική
greek_tokens: akolasia de kai nosos plhquw en polis ara ou dikasthrion te kai iatreion polus anoignumi kai dikanikos te kai iatrikos semnunw otan dh kai eleuqeros polus kai sfodra peri autos spoudazw tis gar ou mellw o de kakazw te kai aisxros paideia en polis ara mh tis megas ecis lambanw tekmhrion h o deomai iatros kai dikasths akron mh monos o faulos te kai xeirotexnhs alla kai o en eleuqeros sxhma prospoiew trefw
english_head: and disease multiply in a city, are not many courts of law and dispensaries opened, and the arts of chicane and medicine give themselves airs when even free men in great numbers take them very seriously? How can they help it? he said. Will you be able to find a surer proof of an evil and shameful state of education in a city than the necessity of first-rate physicians and judges, not only for the base and mechanical, but for those who claim to have been bred in the fashion of free men? Do you not think

greek_head: ἢ οὐκ αἰσχρὸν δοκεῖ καὶ ἀπαιδευσίας μέγα τεκμήριον τὸ ἐπακτῷ παρ' ἄλλων ὡς δεσποτῶν τε καὶ
greek_tokens: h ou aisxros dokew kai apaideusia megas tekmhrion o epaktos para allos ws despoths te kai kriths o dikaios anagkazw xraomai kai aporia oikew pas men oun fhmi aisxros eimi dokew su eimi de egw outos aisxros eimi outos otan dh tis mh monos o polus o bios en dikasthrion feugw te kai diwkw katatribw alla kai upo apeirokalia epi autos dh outos peiqw kallwpizw ws deinos
english_head: it disgraceful and a notable mark of bad breeding to have to make use of a justice imported from others, who thus become your masters and judges, from lack of such qualities in yourself? The most shameful thing in the world. Is it? said I, or is this still more shameful—when a man only wears out the better part of his days in the courts of law as defendant or accuser, but from the lack of all true sense of values is led to plume himself on this very thing, as being a smart fellow to put over an unjust act

greek_head: ὢν περὶ τὸ ἀδικεῖν καὶ ἱκανὸς πάσας μὲν στροφὰς στρέφεσθαι πάσας δὲ διεξόδους διεξελθὼν ἀποστραφῆναι λυγιζόμενος
greek_tokens: eimi peri o adikew kai ikanos passw men strofas strefw passw de diecodos diecerxomai apostrefw lugizomai wste mh parexw dikh kai outos mikros te kai oudeis acios eneka agnoew osos kallion kai ameinwn o paraskeuazw o bios eautou mhdeis deomai nustazw dikasths ou alla outos fhmi ekeinos eti aisxros o de iatrikos eimi de egw deomai ostis mh trauma eneka h tis epeteios noshma epipiptw alla dia
english_head: and cunningly to try every dodge and practice, every evasion, and wriggle out of every hold in defeating justice, and that too for trifles and worthless things, because he does not know how much nobler and better it is to arrange his life so as to have no need of a nodding juryman? That is, said he, still more shameful than the other. And to require medicine, said I, not merely for wounds or the incidence of some seasonal maladies,

greek_head: ἀργίαν τε καὶ δίαιταν οἵαν διήλθομεν ῥευμάτων τε καὶ πνευμάτων ὥσπερ λίμνας ἐμπιμπλαμένους φύσας τε καὶ
greek_tokens: argia te kai diaita oios dierxomai reuma te kai pneuma wsper limnh empiplhmi fusas te kai katarroos noshma onoma tiqhmi anagkazw o komyos asklhpias ou aisxros dokew kai mala fhmi ws alhqhs kainos outos kai atopos noshma onoma oios eimi de egw ws oimh ou eimi epi asklhpios
english_head: but, because of sloth and such a regimen as we described, to fill one’s body up with winds and humors like a marsh and compel the ingenious sons of Aesculapius to invent for diseases such names as fluxes and flatulences—don’t you think that disgraceful? Those surely are, he said, new-fangled and monstrous strange names of diseases. There was nothing of the kind, I fancy, said I, in the days of Aesculapius. I infer this from the fact that at Troy his sons

english_head: did not find fault with the damsel who gave to the wounded Eurypylus to drink a posset of Pramnian wine plentifully sprinkled with barley and gratings of cheese,

greek_head: καὶ τυρὸν ἐπιξυσθέντα ἃ δὴ δοκεῖ φλεγματώδη εἶναι οὐκ ἐμέμψαντο τῇ δούσῃ πιεῖν οὐδὲ Πατρόκλῳ τῷ ἰωμένῳ
greek_tokens: kai turos epicuw o dh dokew flegmatwdhs eimi ou memfomai o dew pinw oude patroklos o iaomai epitimaw kai men dh fhmi atopos ge o pwma outws exw ou ei ge ennoew eipon ostis o paidagwgikos o noshma outos o nun iatrikos pro o asklhpiadai ou xraomai os fhmi prin hrodikos gignomai hrodikos de paidotribhs eimi kai noswdhs gignomai mignumi gumnastikos
english_head: inflammatory ingredients of a surety, nor did they censure Patroclus, who was in charge of the case. It was indeed, said he, a strange potion for a man in that condition. Not strange, said I, if you reflect that the former Asclepiads made no use of our modern coddling medication of diseases before the time of Herodicus. But Herodicus was a trainer and became a valetudinarian, and blended

greek_head: ἰατρικῇ ἀπέκναισε πρῶτον μὲν καὶ μάλιστα ἑαυτόν ἔπειτ' ἄλλους ὕστερον πολλούς
greek_tokens: iatrikos apoknaiw proteros men kai malista eautou epeimi allos usteron polus ph dh fhmi makros eimi de egw o qanatos eautou poiew parakolouqew gar o noshma qanasimos eimi oute iaomai oimh oios su eimi eautou en asxolia te pas iatreuw dia bios zaw apoknaiw ei tis o eqw diaita ekbainw dusqanataw de upo sofia eis ghras afikneomai kalos ara o geras fhmi o texnh ferw
english_head: gymnastics and medicine, for the torment first and chiefly of himself and then of many successors. How so? he said. By lingering out his death, said I; for living in perpetual observance of his malady, which was incurable, he was not able to effect a cure, but lived through his days unfit for the business of life, suffering the tortures of the damned if he departed a whit from his fixed regimen, and struggling against death by reason of his science he won the prize of a doting old age. A noble prize indeed for his science, he said.

greek_head: Οἷον εἰκός ἦν δ' ἐγώ τὸν μὴ εἰδότα ὅτι Ἀσκληπιὸς οὐκ ἀγνοίᾳ οὐδὲ ἀπειρίᾳ τούτου τοῦ εἴδους τῆς ἰατρικῆς τοῖς
greek_tokens: oios eoika eimi de egw o mh oida ostis asklhpios ou agnoia oude apeiria outos o eidos o iatrikos o ekgonos ou katadeiknumi autos alla eidoi ostis pas o eunomeomai ergnumi tis ekastos en o polis prostassw o anagkaion ergazomai kai oudeis sxolh dia bios kamnw iatreuw o egw geloios epi men o dhmiourgew aisqanomai epi de o plousios te kai eudaimwn dokew eimi ou aisqanomai pws fhmi
english_head: The appropriate one, said I, for a man who did not know that it was not from ignorance or inacquaintance with this type of medicine that Aesculapius did not discover it to his descendants, but because he knew that for all well-governed peoples there is a work assigned to each man in the city which he must perform, and no one has leisure to be sick and doctor himself all his days. And this we absurdly enough perceive in the case of a craftsman, but don’t see in the case of the rich and so-called fortunate. How so? he said.

greek_head: Τέκτων μέν ἦν δ' ἐγώ κάμνων ἀξιοῖ παρὰ τοῦ ἰατροῦ φάρμακον πιὼν ἐξεμέσαι τὸ νόσημα ἢ κάτω καθαρθεὶς ἢ καύσει
greek_tokens: tektwn men eimi de egw kamnw aciow para o iatros farmakon pinw ecemew o noshma h katos kaqairw h kaiw h tomaw xraw apallassw ean de tis autos makran diaita prostassw pilidion te peri o kefalh peritiqhmi kai o outos epomai taxus eipon ostis ou sxolh kamnw oude lusitelew outws zaw noshma o new prosexw o de prokeimai ergasia amelew kai meta outos xairw
english_head: A carpenter, said I, when he is sick expects his physician to give him a drug which will operate as an emetic on the disease, or to get rid of it by purging or the use of cautery or the knife. But if anyone prescribes for him a long course of treatment with swathings about the head and their accompaniments, he hastily says that he has no leisure to be sick and that such a life of preoccupation with his illess and neglect of the work that lies before him isn’t worth living. And thereupon he bids farewell to that kind of physician,

greek_head: εἰπὼν τῷ τοιούτῳ ἰατρῷ εἰς τὴν εἰωθυῖαν δίαιταν ἐμβάς ὑγιὴς γενόμενος ζῇ τὰ ἑαυτοῦ πράττων ἐὰν δὲ μὴ ἱκανὸν
greek_tokens: eipon o toioutos iatros eis o eqw diaita embas ugihs gignomai zaw o eautou prassw ean de mh ikanos eimi o swma upoferw teleutaw pragma apallassw kai o toioutos men ge fhmi dokew prepw outws iatrikos xraomai
english_head: enters upon his customary way of life, regains his health, and lives attending to his affairs—or, if his body is not equal to strain, he dies and is freed from all his troubles. For such a man, he said, that appears to be the right use of medicine. And is not the reason, I said,

greek_head: Ἆρα ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὅτι ἦν τι αὐτῷ ἔργον ὃ εἰ μὴ πράττοι οὐκ ἐλυσιτέλει ζῆν
greek_tokens: ara eimi de egw ostis eimi tis autos ergnumi o ei mh prassw ou lusitelew zaw dhlos fhmi o de dh plousios os fhmi oudeis exis toioutos ergnumi prokeimai e anagkazw apexw abiwtos oukoun dh legw ge fwkulidhs gar eimi de egw ou akouw pws fhmi dew otan tis hdh bios eimi areth askew oimh de ge fhmi kai proteros mhdeis eipon peri outos autos maxomai alla egw autos didaskw poteros melethteos outos o plousios
english_head: that he had a task and that life wasn’t worth acceptance on condition of not doing his work? Obviously, he said. But the rich man, we say, has no such appointed task, the necessity of abstaining from which renders life intolerable. I haven’t heard of any. Why, haven’t you heard that saying of Phocylides, that after a man has made his pile he ought to practice virtue? Before, too, I fancy, he said. Let us not quarrel with him on that point, I said, but inform ourselves whether this virtue is something for the rich man to practise,

greek_head: καὶ ἀβίωτον τῷ μὴ μελετῶντι ἢ νοσοτροφία τεκτονικῇ μὲν καὶ ταῖς ἄλλαις τέχναις ἐμπόδιον τῇ προσέξει τοῦ νοῦ
greek_tokens: kai abiwtos o mh meletaw h nosotrofia tektonikos men kai o allos texnh empodios o prosecis o neomai o de fwkulidhs parakeleuma oudeis empodizw nai ma o dios eimi de os sxedon ge tis pas malista o ge peraiteros gumnastikos o perissos outos epimeleia o swma kai gar pros oikonomia kai pros strateia kai pros edraios en polis arxh duskolos o de dh megas ostis kai pros maqhsis ostis kai
english_head: and life is intolerable if he does not, or whether we are to suppose that while valetudinarianism is a hindrance to single-minded attention to carpentry and the other arts, it is no obstacle to the fulfilment of Phocylides’ exhortation. Yes, indeed, he said, this excessive care for the body that goes beyond simple gymnastics is the greatest of all obstacles. For it is troublesome in household affairs and military service and sedentary offices in the city. And, chief of all, it puts difficulties in the way of any kind of instruction, thinking, or private meditation,

greek_head: ἐννοήσεις τε καὶ μελέτας πρὸς ἑαυτὸν χαλεπή κεφαλῆς τινας ἀεὶ διατάσεις καὶ ἰλίγγους ὑποπτεύουσα καὶ αἰτιωμένη ἐκ
greek_tokens: ennohsis te kai meleth pros eautou xalepos kefalh tis aei diatasis kai iliggos upopteuw kai aitiaomai ek filosofia eggignomai wste oph outos areth askew kai dokimazw panth empodios kamnw gar oiomai poiew aei kai wdinw mhpote lhgw peri o swma eoika ge fhmi oukoun outos gignwskw fhmi kai asklhpios o men fusis te kai diaita ugieinos exw o swma
english_head: forever imagining headaches and dizziness and attributing their origin to philosophy. So that wherever this kind of virtue is practiced and tested it is in every way a hindrance. For it makes the man always fancy himself sick and never cease from anguishing about his body. Naturally, he said. Then, shall we not say that it was because Asclepius knew this—that for those who were by nature and course of life sound of body

greek_head: νόσημα δέ τι ἀποκεκριμένον ἴσχοντας ἐν αὑτοῖς τούτοις μὲν καὶ ταύτῃ τῇ ἕξει καταδεῖξαι ἰατρικήν φαρμάκοις τε
greek_tokens: noshma de tis apokrinw isxw en eautou outos men kai outos o ecis katadeiknumi iatrikos farmakon te kai tomh o noshma ekballw autos o eqw prostassw diaita ina mh o politikos blaptw o de eidomai dia pas nosew swma ou epixeirew diaita kata mikros apantlew kai epixew makros kai kakos bios anqrwpos poiew kai ekgonos autos ws o eoika eteros toioutos futeuw alla o mh dunamai en o kaqisthmi
english_head: but had some localized disease, that for such, I say, and for this habit he revealed the art of medicine, and, driving out their disease by drugs and surgery, prescribed for them their customary regimen in order not to interfere with their civic duties, but that, when bodies were diseased inwardly and throughout, he did not attempt by diet and by gradual evacuations and infusions to prolong a wretched existence for the man and have him beget in all likelihood similar wretched offspring?

greek_head: περιόδῳ ζῆν μὴ οἴεσθαι δεῖν θεραπεύειν ὡς οὔτε αὑτῷ οὔτε πόλει λυσιτελῆ
greek_tokens: periodos zaw mh oiomai dew qerapeuw ws oute eautou oute polis lusitelhs politikos fhmi legw asklhpios dhlos eimi de egw kai e pais autos ostis toioutos eimi
english_head: But if a man was incapable of living in the established round and order of life, he did not think it worth while to treat him, since such a fellow is of no use either to himself or to the state. A most politic Asclepius you’re telling us of, he said. Obviously, said I, that was his character. And his sons too, don’t you in see that at Troy they approved

greek_head: οὐχ ὁρᾷς ὡς καὶ ἐν Τροίᾳ ἀγαθοὶ πρὸς τὸν πόλεμον ἐφάνησαν καὶ τῇ ἰατρικῇ ὡς ἐγὼ λέγω ἐχρῶντο ἢ οὐ μέμνησαι
greek_tokens: ou oraw ws kai en troia agaqos pros o polemos fainw kai o iatrikos ws egw legos xraomai h ou mimnhskw ostis kai o menelaos ek o trauma e o pandaros ballw aima ekmuzaw epi hpios farmakon passw ostis de xrh meta outos h pinw h esqiw oudeis mallon h o eurupulos prostassw ws ikanos eimi o farmakon iaomai anhr pro o trauma ugieinos te kai
english_head: themselves good fighting-men and practised medicine as I described it? Don’t you remember that in the case of Menelaus too from the wound that Pandarus inflicted They sucked the blood, and soothing simples sprinkled? Hom. Il. 4.218 But what he was to eat or drink thereafter they no more prescribed than for Eurypylus, taking it for granted that the remedies sufficed to heal men who before their wounds were healthy and temperate in diet

greek_head: κοσμίους ἐν διαίτῃ κἂν εἰ τύχοιεν ἐν τῷ παραχρῆμα κυκεῶνα πιόντες νοσώδη δὲ φύσει τε καὶ ἀκόλαστον οὔτε αὐτοῖς
greek_tokens: kosmios en diaita kan ei tugxanw en o paraxrhma kukewn pinw noswdhs de fusis te kai akolastos oute autos oute o allos oiomai lusitelew zaw oude epi outos o texnh dew eimi oude qerapeuteon autos oude ei midas plousios eien panu komyos fhmi legw asklhpios pais prepw eimi de egw kaitoi apeiqew ge egw e tragwdopoios te kai pindaros apollwn men fhmi asklhpios eimi upo de xruseos peiqw plousios anhr qanasimos
english_head: even if they did happen for the nonce to drink a posset; but they thought that the life of a man constitutionally sickly and intemperate was of no use to himself or others, and that the art of medicine should not be for such nor should they be given treatment even if they were richer than Midas. Very ingenious fellows, he said, you make out these sons of Asclepius to be. ’Tis fitting, said I; and yet in disregard of our principles the tragedians and Pindar affirm that Asclepius, though he was the son of Apollo, was bribed by gold

greek_head: ἤδη ὄντα ἰάσασθαι ὅθεν δὴ καὶ κεραυνωθῆναι αὐτόν ἡμεῖς δὲ κατὰ τὰ προειρημένα οὐ πεισόμεθα αὐτοῖς ἀμφότερα
greek_tokens: hdh eimi iaomai oqen dh kai keraunow autos egw de kata o proerew ou pasxw autos amfoteros alla ei men qeaomai eimi ou eimi fhmi aisxrokerdhs ei de aisxrokerdhs ou eimi qeaomai orqos eimi de os outos ge alla peri ode tis legw eimi swkraths ara ou agaqos deomai en o polis ktaomai iatros eien de an pou malista toioutos osos
english_head: to heal a man already at the point of death, and that for this cause he was struck by the lightning. But we in accordance with the aforesaid principles refuse to believe both statements, but if he was the son of a god he was not avaricious, we will insist, and if he was greedy of gain he was not the son of a god. That much, said he, is most certainly true. But what have you to say to this, Socrates, must we not have good physicians in our city? And they would be the most likely to be good who had treated the greatest number of healthy and diseased men,

greek_head: πλείστους μὲν ὑγιεινούς πλείστους δὲ νοσώδεις μετεχειρίσαντο καὶ δικασταὶ αὖ ὡσαύτως οἱ παντοδαπαῖς φύσεσιν
greek_tokens: pleistos men ugieinos pleistos de noswdhs metaxeirizw kai dikasths au wsautws e pantodapos fusis omilew kai mala eipon agaqos legos alla oida os hgeomai toioutos an eipon fhmi alla peiraw eimi de egw su mentoi ou omoios pragma o autos logos arow pws fhmi iatros men eipon deinos an gignomai ei ek pais arxw pros o manqanw o texnh ws pleistos te kai ponhros swma omilew kai autos passw
english_head: and so good judges would be those who had associated with all sorts and conditions of men. Most assuredly I want them good, I said; but do you know whom I regard as such? I’ll know if you tell, he said. Well, I will try, said I. You, however, have put unlike cases in one question. How so? said he. Physicians, it is true, I said, would prove most skilled if, from childhood up, in addition to learning the principles of the art they had familiarized themselves with the greatest possible number of the most sickly bodies,

greek_head: νόσους κάμοιεν καὶ εἶεν μὴ πάνυ ὑγιεινοὶ φύσει οὐ γὰρ οἶμαι σώματι σῶμα θεραπεύουσιν οὐ γὰρ ἂν αὐτὰ
greek_tokens: nosos kamnw kai eien mh panu ugieinos fusis ou gar oimh swma swma qerapeuw ou gar an autos egxwrew kakos eimi pote kai gignomai alla yuxw swma h ou egxwrew kakos gignomai te kai eimi eu tis qerapeuw orqos fhmi
english_head: and if they themselves had suffered all diseases and were not of very healthy constitution. For you see they do not treat the body by the body. If they did, it would not be allowable for their bodies to be or to have been in evil condition. But they treat the body with the mind—and it is not competent for a mind that is or has been evil to treat anything well. Right, he said. But a judge, mark you, my friend,

greek_head: Δικαστὴς δέ γε ὦ φίλε ψυχῇ ψυχῆς ἄρχει ᾗ οὐκ ἐγχωρεῖ ἐκ νέας ἐν πονηραῖς ψυχαῖς τεθράφθαι τε καὶ
greek_tokens: dikasths de ge eimi filos yuxw yuxazw arxw h ou egxwrew ek naus en ponhros yuxh trefw te kai omilew kai pas adikhma autos adikew diecerxomai wste ocews apo eautou tekmairomai o o allos adikhma oios kata swma nosos alla apeiros autos kai akeraios deomai kakh hqos nea eimi gignomai ei mellw kalos agaqos eimi krinw ugihs o dikaios dio dh kai euhqhs neos eimi e epieikhs fainw kai euecapathtos
english_head: rules soul with soul and it is not allowable for a soul to have been bred from youth up among evil souls and to have grown familiar with them, and itself to have run the gauntlet of every kind of wrong-doing and injustice so as quickly to infer from itself the misdeeds of others as it might diseases in the body, but it must have been inexperienced in evil natures and uncontaminated by them while young, if it is to be truly fair and good and judge soundly of justice. For which cause the better sort seem to be simple-minded in youth and are easily deceived by the wicked,

greek_head: ὑπὸ τῶν ἀδίκων ἅτε οὐκ ἔχοντες ἐν ἑαυτοῖς παραδείγματα ὁμοιοπαθῆ τοῖς πονηροῖς
greek_tokens: upo o adikos ate ou exw en eautou paradeigma omoiopaqhs o ponhros kai men dh fhmi sfodra ge autos pasxw o su eimi de egw ou neos alla gerwn deomai o agaqos dikasths eimi oyimaqhs gignomai o adikia oios eimi ou oikeios en o eautou yuxw eneimi aisqanomai alla allotrios en allotrios meletaw en polus xronos diaisqanomai oios fuw kakos episthmh ou
english_head: since they do not have within themselves patterns answering to the affections of the bad. That is indeed their experience, he said. Therefore it is, said I, that the good judge must not be a youth but an old man, a late learner of the nature of injustice, one who has not become aware of it as a property in his own soul, but one who has through the long years trained himself to understand it as an alien thing in alien souls, and to discern how great an evil it is

greek_head: ἐμπειρίᾳ οἰκείᾳ κεχρημένον Γενναιότατος γοῦν ἔφη ἔοικεν εἶναι ὁ τοιοῦτος δικαστής
greek_tokens: empeiria oikeios xraw gennaios goun fhmi eoika eimi o toioutos dikasths kai agaqos ge eimi de egw o su erwtaw o gar exw yuxh agaqos agaqos o de deinos ekeinos kai kaxupoptos o polus autos adikew kai panourgos te kai sofos oiomai eimi otan men omoios omilew deinos fainw eceulabeomai pros o en eautou paradeigma aposkopew otan de agaqos kai presbus hdh plhsiasis abelteros
english_head: by the instrument of mere knowledge and not by experience of his own. That at any rate, he said, appears to be the noblest kind of judge. And what is more, a good one, I said, which was the gist of your question. For he who has a good soul is good. But that cunning fellow quick to suspect evil, and who has himself done many unjust acts and who thinks himself a smart trickster, when he associates with his like does appear to be clever, being on his guard and fixing his eyes on the patterns within himself. But when the time comes for him to mingle with the good and his elders,

greek_head: αὖ φαίνεται ἀπιστῶν παρὰ καιρὸν καὶ ἀγνοῶν ὑγιὲς ἦθος ἅτε οὐκ ἔχων παράδειγμα τοῦ τοιούτου πλεονάκις δὲ πονηροῖς
greek_tokens: au fainw apistew para kairos kai agnoew ugihs hqos ate ou exw paradeigma o toioutos pleonakis de ponhros h xrhstos entugxanw sofos h amaqhs dokew eimi eautou te kai allos pantapasi men oun fhmi alhqhs ou toinun eimi de egw toioutos xrh o dikasths zhtew o agaqos te kai sofos alla o proteros ponhria men gar areth te kai eautou oupote an gignwskw areth de fusis paideuw xronos ama eautou te kai ponhria episthmh
english_head: then on the contrary he appears stupid. He is unseasonably distrustful and he cannot recognize a sound character because he has no such pattern in himself. But since he more often meets with the bad than the good, he seems to himself and to others to be rather wise than foolish. That is quite true, he said. Well then, said I, such a one must not be our ideal of the good and wise judge but the former. For while badness could never come to know both virtue and itself, native virtue through education

greek_head: λήψεται σοφὸς οὖν οὗτος ὥς μοι δοκεῖ ἀλλ' οὐχ ὁ κακὸς γίγνεται
greek_tokens: lambanw sofos oun outos os egw dokew alla ou o kakos gignomai kai egw fhmi sundokew oukoun kai iatrikos oios eipon meta o toioutos dikastikos kata polis nomoqethsis o o poliths su
english_head: will at last acquire the science both of itself and badness. This one, then, as I think, is the man who proves to be wise and not the bad man. And I concur, he said. Then will you not establish by law in your city such an art of medicine as we have described in conjunction with this kind of justice? And these arts will care for the bodies and souls of such of your citizens as are truly well born,

greek_head: τοὺς μὲν εὐφυεῖς τὰ σώματα καὶ τὰς ψυχὰς θεραπεύσουσι τοὺς δὲ μή ὅσοι μὲν κατὰ σῶμα τοιοῦτοι ἀποθνῄσκειν
greek_tokens: o men eufuhs o swma kai o yuxh qerapeuw o de mh osos men kata swma toioutos apoqnhskw eaw o de kata o yuxh kakofuhs kai aniatos autos apokteinw o goun ariston fhmi autos te o pasxw kai o polis outws fainw e de dh neos eimi de egw dhlos ostis eulabeomai su dikastikos eis xreia eimi o aploos ekeinos mousikh xraomai os dh fhmi swfrosunos entiktw tis meis fhmi
english_head: but of those who are not, such as are defective in body they will suffer to die and those who are evil-natured and incurable in soul they will themselves put to death. This certainly, he said, has been shown to be the best thing for the sufferers themselves and for the state. And so your youths, said I, employing that simple music which we said engendered sobriety will, it is clear, guard themselves against falling into the need of the justice of the court-room. Yes, he said. And will not our musician, pursuing the same trail

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν οὐ κατὰ ταὐτὰ ἴχνη ταῦτα ὁ μουσικὸς γυμναστικὴν διώκων ἐὰν ἐθέλῃ αἱρήσει ὥστε μηδὲν ἰατρικῆς δεῖσθαι
greek_tokens: ara oun ou kata tautos ixnos outos o mousikos gumnastikos diwkw ean eqelw airew wste mhdeis iatrikos deomai ostis mh anagkh egw dokew autos ge meis o gumnasion kai o ponos pros o qumoeidhs o fusis blepw ekeinos egeirw ponhsis mallon h pros isxus ou wsper e allos aqlhths rwmh eneka sition kai ponos metaxeirizomai orqos eimi de os ara oun eimi de egw eimi glauc kai e kaqisthmi
english_head: in his use of gymnastics, if he please, get to have no need of medicine save when indispensable? I think so. And even the exercises and toils of gymnastics he will undertake with a view to the spirited part of his nature to arouse that rather than for mere strength, unlike ordinary athletes, who treat diet and exercise only as a means to muscle. Nothing could be truer, he said. Then may we not say, Glaucon, said I, that those who established an education in music and gymnastics

greek_head: μουσικῇ καὶ γυμναστικῇ παιδεύειν οὐχ οὗ ἕνεκά τινες οἴονται καθιστᾶσιν ἵνα τῇ μὲν τὸ σῶμα θεραπεύοιντο τῇ δὲ τὴν
greek_tokens: mousikh kai gumnastikos paideuw ou e eneka tis oiomai kaqisthmi ina o men o swma qerapeuw o de o yuxh alla tis meis fhmi kinduneuw eimi de egw amfoteros o yuxazw eneka o megas kaqisthmi pws dh ou ennoew eipon ws diatiqhmi autos o dianoia e an gumnastikos men dia bios omilew mousikh de mh aptw h au osos an enantion diatiqhmi tis de eimi de os peri legw
english_head: had not the purpose in view that some attribute to them in so instituting, namely to treat the body by one and the soul by the other? But what? he said. It seems likely, I said, that they ordained both chiefly for the soul’s sake. How so? Have you not observed, said I, the effect on the disposition of the mind itself of lifelong devotion to gymnastics with total neglect of music? Or the disposition of those of the opposite habit? In what respect do you mean? he said.

greek_head: Ἀγριότητός τε καὶ σκληρότητος καὶ αὖ μαλακίας τε καὶ ἡμερότητος ἦν δ' ἐγώ
greek_tokens: agrioths te kai sklhroths kai au malakia te kai hmeroths eimi de egw egw fhmi ostis e men gumnastikos akratos xraw agrios o deon apobainw e de mousikh malakos au gignomai h ws kallion autos kai meis eimi de egw o ge agrios o qumoeidhs an o fusis parexw kai orqos men trefw andreios an eaw mallon de epiteinw o deon sklhros te kai xalepos gignomai an ws o eoika dokew egw fhmi
english_head: In respect of savagery and hardness or, on the other hand, of softness and gentleness? I have observed, he said, that the devotees of unmitigated gymnastics turn out more brutal than they should be and those of music softer than is good for them. And surely, said I, this savagery is a quality derived from the high-spirited element in our nature, which, if rightly trained, becomes brave, but if overstrained, would naturally become hard and harsh. I think so, he said. And again, is not the gentleness

greek_head: Τί δέ τὸ ἥμερον οὐχ ἡ φιλόσοφος ἂν ἔχοι φύσις καὶ μᾶλλον μὲν ἀνεθέντος αὐτοῦ μαλακώτερον εἴη τοῦ δέοντος
greek_tokens: tis de o hmeros ou o filosofos an exw fusis kai mallon men anihmi autos malakos eaw o deon kalos de trefw hmeros te kai kosmion eimi outos dew de ge fhmi o fulac amfoteros exw outos o fusis deomai gar oukoun armozw deomai autos pros allhlwn pws de ou kai o men armozw swfrwn te kai andreia o
english_head: a quality which the philosophic nature would yield? This if relaxed too far would be softer than is desirable but if rightly trained gentle and orderly? That is so. But our requirement, we say, is that the guardians should possess both natures. It is. And must they not be harmoniously adjusted to one another? Of course. And the soul of the man thus attuned is sober and brave?

greek_head: ψυχή Πάνυ γε
greek_tokens: yuxh panu ge o de anarmostos deilos kai agroikos kai mala oukoun otan men tis mousikh parexw kataulew kai kataxew o yuxazw dia o aatos wsper dia xwnh os nundi egw legw o glukeios te kai malakos kai qrhnwdhs armonia kai minurizw te kai ganow upo o aoidh diatelew o bios oloc outos o men proteros ei tis qumoeidhs exw wsper sidhron malassw kai xrhsimos
english_head: Certainly. And that of the ill adjusted is cowardiy and rude? It surely is. Now when a man abandons himself to music to play upon him and pour into his soul as it were through the funnel of his ears those sweet, soft, and dirge-like airs of which we were just now speaking, and gives his entire time to the warblings and blandishments of song, the first result is that the principle of high spirit, if he had it,

greek_head: ἐξ ἀχρήστου καὶ σκληροῦ ἐποίησεν ὅταν δ' ἐπέχων μὴ ἀνιῇ ἀλλὰ κηλῇ τὸ δὴ μετὰ τοῦτο ἤδη τήκει καὶ λείβει ἕως
greek_tokens: ek axrhstos kai sklhros poiew otan de epwxato mh aniaomai alla khlew o dh meta outos hdh thkw kai leibw ews an ekthcis o qumos kai ektemnw wsper neuron ek o yuxazw kai poihsis malqakos aixmhths panu men oun fhmi kai ean men ge eimi de egw ek arxh fusis aqumos lambanw taxus outos diaprassw ean de qumoeidhs asqenhs poiew o qumos ocurropos apergazomai apo mikros taxus ereqizw
english_head: is softened like iron and is made useful instead of useless and brittle. But when he continues the practice without remission and is spellbound, the effect begins to be that he melts and liquefies till he completely dissolves away his spirit, cuts out as it were the very sinews of his soul and makes of himself a feeble warrior. Assuredly, he said. And if, said I, he has to begin with a spiritless nature he reaches this result quickly, but if high-spirited, by weakening the spirit he makes it unstable,

greek_head: τε καὶ κατασβεννύμενον ἀκράχολοι οὖν καὶ ὀργίλοι ἀντὶ θυμοειδοῦς γεγένηνται δυσκολίας ἔμπλεῳ
greek_tokens: te kai katasbennumi akraxolos oun kai orgilos anti qumoeidhs gignomai duskolia empleos komidh men oun tis de an au gumnastikos polus poneomai kai euwxew eu mala mousikh de kai filosofia mh aptw ou proteros men eu isxw o swma fronhma te kai qumos empiplhmi kai andreios gignomai autos eautou kai mala ge tis de epeidan allos mhdeis prassw mhde koinwnew mousa
english_head: quickly irritated by slight stimuli, and as quickly quelled. The outcome is that such men are choleric and irascible instead of high-spirited, and are peevish and discontented. Precisely so. On the other hand, if a man toils hard at gymnastics and eats right lustily and holds no truck with music and philosophy, does he not at first get very fit and full of pride and high spirit and become more brave and bold than he was? He does indeed. But what if he does nothing but this and has no contact with the Muse in any way,

greek_head: μηδαμῇ οὐκ εἴ τι καὶ ἐνῆν αὐτοῦ φιλομαθὲς ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ ἅτε οὔτε μαθήματος γευόμενον οὐδενὸς οὔτε ζητήματος οὔτε
greek_tokens: mhdamh ou ei tis kai eneimi autos filomaqhs en o yuxw ate oute maqhma geuw oudeis oute zhthma oute logos metisxw oute o allos mousikh asqenhs te kai kwfos kai tuflos gignomai ate ou egeirw oude trefw oude diakaqairw o aisqhsis autos outws fhmi misologos dh oimh o toioutos gignomai kai amousos kai peiqw men dia logos oudeis eti xraomai bia de kai
english_head: is not the result that even if there was some principle of the love of knowledge in his soul, since it tastes of no instruction nor of any inquiry and does not participate in any discussion or any other form of culture, it becomes feeble, deaf, and blind, because it is not aroused or fed nor are its perceptions purified and quickened? That is so, he said. And so such a man, I take it, becomes a misologist and stranger to the Muses. He no longer makes any use of persuasion by speech but achieves all his ends

greek_head: ἀγριότητι ὥσπερ θηρίον πρὸς πάντα διαπράττεται καὶ ἐν ἀμαθίᾳ καὶ σκαιότητι μετὰ ἀρρυθμίας τε καὶ ἀχαριστίας ζῇ
greek_tokens: agrioths wsper qhraw pros pas diaprassw kai en amaqia kai skaioths meta arruqmia te kai axaristia zaw pantapasi eimi de os outws exis epi dh duh eimi outos ws eoika duo texnh qeos egw an tis fhmi didwmi o anqrwpos mousikh te kai gumnastikos epi o qumoeidhs kai o filosofos ou epi yuxh kai swma ei mh ei parergon alla epi ekeinos opws
english_head: like a beast by violence and savagery, and in his brute ignorance and ineptitude lives a life of disharmony and gracelessness. That is entirely true, he said. For these two, then, it seems there are two arts which I would say some god gave to mankind, music and gymnastics for the service of the high-spirited principle and the love of knowledge in them—not for the soul and the body except incidentally, but for the harmonious adjustment of these two principles

greek_head: ἂν ἀλλήλοιν συναρμοσθῆτον ἐπιτεινομένω καὶ ἀνιεμένω μέχρι τοῦ προσήκοντος
greek_tokens: an allhlwn sunarmozw epiteinw kai anihmi mexri o proshkw kai gar eoika fhmi o kallista ara mousikh gumnastikos kerannumi kai metrios o yuxw prosferw outos orqos an fhmi eimi teleios mousikos kai euarmostos polus mallon h o o xordh allhlwn sunisthmi eoikotws ge fhmi eimi swkraths oukoun kai en o polis egw eimi glauc dehsis o toioutos tis aei epistaton ei mellw o politeia swzw
english_head: by the proper degree of tension and relaxation of each. Yes, so it appears, he said. Then he who best blends gymnastics with music and applies them most suitably to the soul is the man whom we should most rightly pronounce to be the most perfect and harmonious musician, far rather than the one who brings the strings into unison with one another. That seems likely, Socrates, he said. And shall we not also need in our city, Glaucon, a permanent overseer of this kind if its constitution is to be preserved?

greek_head: Δεήσει μέντοι ὡς οἷόν τέ γε μάλιστα Οἱ μὲν δὴ τύποι τῆς παιδείας τε καὶ τροφῆς οὗτοι ἂν
greek_tokens: dehsis mentoi ws oios su ge malista e men dh tupos o paideia te kai trefw outos an eien xoreia gar tis an tis dieceimi o toioutos kai qhra te kai kunhgesion kai gumnikos agwn kai ippikos sxedon gar tis dhlos dh ostis outos epomai deomai autos eimi kai ouketi xalepos euriskw isos eimi de os ou xalepos eien eimi de egw o dh meta outos tis an egw diaireteon eaw ara ou autos outos ostis arxw te kai arxw
english_head: We most certainly shall. Such would be the outlines of their education and breeding. For why should one recite the list of the dances of such citizens, their hunts and chases with hounds, their athletic contests and races? It is pretty plain that they must conform to these principles and there is no longer any difficulty in discovering them. There is, it may be, no difficulty, he said. Very well, said I; what, then, have we next to determine? Is it not which ones among them shall be

greek_head: Τί μήν Οὐκοῦν ὅτι μὲν πρεσβυτέρους τοὺς ἄρχοντας δεῖ εἶναι
greek_tokens: tis meis oukoun ostis men presbus o arxw deomai eimi neos de o arxw dhlos dhlos kai ostis ge o aristos autos kai outos e de gewrgew aristos ara ou gewrgikos gignomai nai nun de epei fulac autos aristos deomai eimi ara ou fulakikos polis nai oukoun fronimos te eis outos deomai uparxw kai dunatos kai eti khdemwn o polis
english_head: the rulers and the ruled? Certainly. That the rulers must be the elder and the ruled the younger is obvious. It is. And that the rulers must be their best? This too. And do not the best of the farmers prove the best farmers? Yes. And in this case, since we want them to be the best of the guardians, must they not be the best guardians, the most regardful of the state? Yes. They must then to begin with be intelligent in such matters and capable,

greek_head: Ἔστι ταῦτα Κήδοιτο δέ γ' ἄν τις μάλιστα τούτου ὃ τυγχάνοι φιλῶν
greek_tokens: eimi outos khdw de ge an tis malista outos o tugxanw filos anagkh kai meis outos ge an malista filew os sumferw hgeomai o autos kai eautou kai otan malista ekeinos men eu prassw oiomai sumbainw kai eautou eu prassw mh de enantion outws fhmi eklekteos ara ek o allos fulac toioutos anhr e an skopaw egw malista fainw para pas o
english_head: and furthermore careful of the interests of the state? That is so. But one would be most likely to be careful of that which he loved. Necessarily. And again, one would be most likely to love that whose interests he supposed to coincide with his own, and thought that when it prospered, he too would prosper and if not, the contrary. So it is, he said. Then we must pick out from the other guardians such men as to our observation appear most inclined through the entire course of their lives to be zealous to do what they think

greek_head: βίον ὃ μὲν ἂν τῇ πόλει ἡγήσωνται συμφέρειν πάσῃ προθυμίᾳ ποιεῖν ὃ δ' ἂν μή μηδενὶ τρόπῳ πρᾶξαι ἂν
greek_tokens: bios o men an o polis hgeomai sumferw paomai proqumia poiew o de an mh mhdeis tropos prassw an eqelw epithdeios gar fhmi dokew dh egw thrhteon autos eimi en apas o hlikia ei fulakikos eimi outos o dogma kai mhte gohteuw mhte biazw ekballw epilanqanomai doca o o poiew dew o o polis beltistos tis fhmi legw o ekbolh egw su fhmi eraomai fainw egw doca eceimi ek dianoia h ekousios h aekousios ekousios men o yeudhs
english_head: for the interest of the state, and who would be least likely to consent to do the opposite. That would be a suitable choice, he said. I think, then, we shall have to observe them at every period of life, to see if they are conservators and guardians of this conviction in their minds and never by sorcery nor by force can be brought to expel from their souls unawares this conviction that they must do what is best for the state. What do you mean by the expelling? he said. I will tell you, said I; it seems to me that the exit of a belief from the mind is either voluntary or involuntary.

greek_head: τοῦ μεταμανθάνοντος ἀκουσίως δὲ πᾶσα ἡ ἀληθής Τὸ μὲν τῆς ἑκουσίου ἔφη μανθάνω τὸ δὲ τῆς ἀκουσίου
greek_tokens: o metamanqanw aekousios de pas o alhqhs o men o ekousios fhmi manqanw o de o aekousios deomai manqanw tis de ou kai su hgeomai fhmi egw o men agaqos aekousios steromai o anqrwpos o de kakh ekousios h ou o men yeudw o alhqeia kakos o de alhqeuw agaqos h ou o o eimi docazw alhqeuw dokew su eimi alla eimi de os orqos legw kai egw dokew akwn alhqhs doca steriskw
english_head: Voluntary is the departure of the false belief from one who learns better, involuntary that of every true belief. The voluntary, he said, I understand, but I need instruction about the involuntary. How now, said I, don’t you agree with me in thinking that men are unwillingly deprived of good things but willingly of evil? Or is it not an evil to be deceived in respect of the truth and a good to possess truth? And don’t you think that to opine the things that are is to possess the truth? Why, yes, said he, you are right, and I agree that men are unwillingly deprived of true opinions. And doesn’t this happen to them by theft, by the spells of sorcery or by force? I don’t understand now either, he said. I must be talking in high tragic style, I said;

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν κλαπέντες ἢ γοητευθέντες ἢ βιασθέντες τοῦτο πάσχουσιν

english_head: by those who have their opinions stolen from them I mean those who are over-persuaded and those who forget, because in the one case time, in the other argument strips them unawares of their beliefs. Now I presume you understand, do you not? Yes. Well, then, by those who are constrained or forced I mean those whom some pain or suffering compels to change their minds. That too I understand and you are right. And the victims of sorcery

english_head: I am sure you too would say are they who alter their opinions under the spell of pleasure or terrified by some fear. Yes, he said: everything that deceives appears to cast a spell upon the mind. Well then, as I was just saying, we must look for those who are the best guardians of the indwelling conviction that what they have to do is what they at any time believe to be best for the state. Then we must observe them from childhood up and propose them tasks in which one would be most likely to forget this principle or be deceived, and he whose memory is sure

greek_head: καὶ δυσεξαπάτητον ἐγκριτέον τὸν δὲ μὴ ἀποκριτέον ἦ γάρ

english_head: and who cannot be beguiled we must accept and the other kind we must cross off from our list. Is not that so? Yes. And again we must subject them to toils and pains and competitions in which we have to watch for the same traits. Right, he said. Then, said I, must we not institute a third kind of competitive test with regard to sorcery and observe them in that? Just as men conduct colts to noises and uproar to see if they are liable to take fright, so we must bring these lads while young into fears

greek_head: αὖ μεταβλητέον βασανίζοντας πολὺ μᾶλλον ἢ χρυσὸν ἐν πυρί εἰ δυσγοήτευτος καὶ εὐσχήμων ἐν πᾶσι φαίνεται
greek_tokens: au metablhteon basanizw polus mallon h xrusos en pur ei dusgohteutos kai eusxhmos en pas fainw fulac eautou eimi agaqos kai mousikh eis manqanw euruqmos te kai euarmostos eautou en pas outos paraxwnnumi oios dh an eimi kai eautou kai polis xrhsimos eaw kai o aei en te pais kai neaniskos kai en anhr basanizw
english_head: and again pass them into pleasures, testing them much more carefully than men do gold in the fire, to see if the man remains immune to such witchcraft and preserves his composure throughout, a good guardian of himself and the culture which he has received, maintaining the true rhythm and harmony of his being in all those conditions, and the character that would make him most useful to himself and to the state. And he who as boy, lad, and man endures the test

greek_head: καὶ ἀκήρατον ἐκβαίνοντα καταστατέον ἄρχοντα τῆς πόλεως καὶ φύλακα καὶ τιμὰς δοτέον καὶ ζῶντι καὶ

english_head: and issues from it unspoiled we must establish as ruler over our city and its guardian, and bestow rewards upon him in life, and in death the allotment of the supreme honors of burial-rites and other memorials. But the man of the other type we must reject. Such, said I, appears to me, Glaucon, the general notion of our selection and appointment of rulers and guardians as sketched in outline, but not drawn out in detail. I too, he said, think much the same. Then would it not truly

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ὡς ἀληθῶς ὀρθότατον καλεῖν τούτους μὲν φύλακας παντελεῖς τῶν τε ἔξωθεν πολεμίων τῶν τε ἐντὸς

english_head: be most proper to designate these as guardians in the full sense of the word, watchers against foemen without and friends within, so that the latter shall not wish and the former shall not be able to work harm, but to name those youths whom we were calling guardians just now, helpers and aids for the decrees of the rulers? I think so, he replied. How, then, said I, might we contrive one of those opportune falsehoods of which we were just now speaking,

greek_head: τι ἓν ψευδομένους πεῖσαι μάλιστα μὲν καὶ αὐτοὺς τοὺς ἄρχοντας εἰ δὲ μή τὴν ἄλλην πόλιν
greek_tokens: tis eis yeudw peiqw malista men kai autos o arxw ei de mh o allos polis poios tis fhmi mhdeis kainos eimi de egw alla foinikikos tis proteros men hdh pollaxou gignomai os fhmi e poihths kai peiqw epi egw de ou gignomai oude oida ei gignomai an peiqw de suxnazw peiqw ws eoika fhmi oknew legw docow de su eimi de egw kai mala eoikotws oknew epeidan eipon legos fhmi kai mh fobew
english_head: so as by one noble lie to persuade if possible the rulers themselves, but failing that the rest of the city? What kind of a fiction do you mean? said he. Nothing unprecedented, said I, but a sort of Phoenician tale, something that has happened ere now in many parts of the world, as the poets aver and have induced men to believe, but that has not happened and perhaps would not be likely to happen in our day and demanding no little persuasion to make it believable. You act like one who shrinks from telling his thought, he said. You will think that I have right good reason for shrinking when I have told, I said.

greek_head: Λέγω δή καίτοι οὐκ οἶδα ὁποίᾳ τόλμῃ ἢ ποίοις λόγοις χρώμενος ἐρῶ καὶ ἐπιχειρήσω πρῶτον μὲν αὐτοὺς τοὺς

english_head: Say on, said he, and don’t be afraid. Very well, I will. And yet I hardly know how to find the audacity or the words to speak and undertake to persuade first the rulers themselves and the soldiers and then the rest of the city, that in good sooth all our training and educating of them were things that they imagined and that happened to them as it were in a dream; but that in reality at that time they were down within the earth being molded and fostered themselves while

greek_head: ὅπλα αὐτῶν καὶ ἡ ἄλλη σκευὴ δημιουργουμένη ἐπειδὴ δὲ παντελῶς ἐξειργασμένοι ἦσαν καὶ ἡ γῆ αὐτοὺς μήτηρ οὖσα
greek_tokens: oplon autos kai o allos skeuh dhmiourgew epei de pantelhs ecergazomai eimi kai o gaia autos mhthr eimi anhkw kai nun deomai ws peri mhthr kai trefw o xwra en h eimi bouleuw te kai amunw autos ean tis epi autos eimi kai uper o allos poliths ws adelfh eimi kai ghgenhs dianoeomai ou etos fhmi pala aisxunw o yeudos legw
english_head: their weapons and the rest of their equipment were being fashioned. And when they were quite finished the earth as being their mother delivered them, and now as if their land were their mother and their nurse they ought to take thought for her and defend her against any attack and regard the other citizens as their brothers and children of the self-same earth. It is not for nothing, he said, that you were so bashful about coming out with your lie. It was quite natural that I should be,

greek_head: Πάνυ ἦν δ' ἐγώ εἰκότως ἀλλ' ὅμως ἄκουε καὶ τὸ λοιπὸν τοῦ μύθου ἐστὲ μὲν γὰρ δὴ πάντες οἱ ἐν τῇ πόλει

english_head: I said; but all the same hear the rest of the story. While all of you in the city are brothers, we will say in our tale, yet God in fashioning those of you who are fitted to hold rule mingled gold in their generation, for which reason they are the most precious—but in the helpers silver, and iron and brass in the farmers and other craftsmen. And as you are all akin, though for the most part you will breed after your kinds,

greek_head: ἔστι δ' ὅτε ἐκ χρυσοῦ γεννηθείη ἂν ἀργυροῦν καὶ ἐξ ἀργύρου χρυσοῦν ἔκγονον καὶ τἆλλα πάντα οὕτως ἐξ ἀλλήλων
greek_tokens: eimi de oste ek xruseos gennaw an argureos kai ek arguros xruseos ekgonos kai allos pas outws ek allhlwn o oun arxw kai proteros kai malista paraggellw o qeos opws mhdeis outws fulac agaqos eimi mhde outws sfodra fulassw mhdeis ws o ekgonos ostis autos outos en o yuxh paramignumi kai ean te sfeteros ekgonos upoxalkos h uposidhros gignomai mhdeis
english_head: it may sometimes happen that a golden father would beget a silver son and that a golden offspring would come from a silver sire and that the rest would in like manner be born of one another. So that the first and chief injunction that the god lays upon the rulers is that of nothing else are they to be such careful guardians and so intently observant as of the intermixture of these metals in the souls of their offspring, and if sons are born to them with an infusion of brass or iron

greek_head: τρόπῳ κατελεήσουσιν ἀλλὰ τὴν τῇ φύσει προσήκουσαν τιμὴν ἀποδόντες ὤσουσιν εἰς δημιουργοὺς ἢ εἰς γεωργούς
greek_tokens: tropos kateleew alla o o fusis prosakouw timh apodidwmi wqew eis dhmiourgos h eis gewrgos kai an au ek outos tis upoxrusos h uparguros fuw timaw anagw o men eis fulakh o de eis epikouria ws xrhsmos eimi tote o polis diafqeirw otan autos o sidhreos fulac h o xalkeos fulacis outos oun o muqos opws an peiqw exis tis mhxanh
english_head: they shall by no means give way to pity in their treatment of them, but shall assign to each the status due to his nature and thrust them out among the artisans or the farmers. And again, if from these there is born a son with unexpected gold or silver in his composition they shall honor such and bid them go up higher, some to the office of guardian, some to the assistanceship, alleging that there is an oracle that the state shall then be overthrown when the man of iron or brass is its guardian. Do you see any way of getting them to believe this tale?

greek_head: Οὐδαμῶς ἔφη ὅπως γ' ἂν αὐτοὶ οὗτοι ὅπως μεντἂν οἱ τούτων ὑεῖς καὶ οἱ ἔπειτα οἵ τ' ἄλλοι ἄνθρωποι οἱ ὕστερον
greek_tokens: oudamos fhmi opws ge an autos outos opws mentoi e outos uios kai e epeita e su allos anqrwpos e usteron alla kai outos eimi de egw eu an exw pros o mallon autos o polis te kai allhlwn khdw sxedon gar tis manqanw o legw kai outos men dh ecis oph an autos o fhmh agw egw de outos o ghgenhs oplizw proagw hgeomai o arxw erxomai de qeaomai o polis opou kalos stratopedeuw oqen o te
english_head: No, not these themselves, he said, but I do, their sons and successors and the rest of mankind who come after. Well, said I, even that would have a good effect making them more inclined to care for the state and one another. For I think I apprehend your meaning. XXII. And this shall fall out as tradition guides. But let us arm these sons of earth and conduct them under the leadership of their rulers. And when they have arrived they must look out for the fairest site in the city for their encampment,

greek_head: ἔνδον μάλιστ' ἂν κατέχοιεν εἴ τις μὴ ἐθέλοι τοῖς νόμοις πείθεσθαι τούς τε ἔξωθεν ἀπαμύνοιεν εἰ πολέμιος ὥσπερ
greek_tokens: endon malista an katexw ei tis mh eqelw o nomos peiqw o te ecwqen apamunw ei polemios wsper lukos epi poimnh tis eimi stratopedeuw de quw os xrh eunh poiew h pws outws fhmi oukoun toioutos oios xeimwn te stegw kai qeros ikanos eimi pws gar ou oikhsis gar fhmi dokew egw legw nai eimi de egw stratiwtikos ge alla ou xrhmatistikos
english_head: a position from which they could best hold down rebellion against the laws from within and repel aggression from without as of a wolf against the fold. And after they have encamped and sacrificed to the proper gods they must make their lairs, must they not? Yes, he said. And these must be of a character keep out the cold in winter and be sufficient in summer? Of course. For I presume you are speaking of their houses. Yes, said I, the houses of soldiers not of money-makers.

greek_head: Πῶς ἔφη αὖ τοῦτο λέγεις διαφέρειν ἐκείνου Ἐγώ σοι ἦν δ' ἐγώ πειράσομαι εἰπεῖν δεινότατον
greek_tokens: pws fhmi au outos legw diaferw ekeinos egw su eimi de egw peiraw eipon deinos gar pou pas kai aisxros poimhn toioutos ge kai outws trefw kuwn epikouros poimnion wste upo akolasia h limos h tis allos kakos eqos autos o kuwn epixeirew o probaton kakourgew kai anti kuwn lukos omoiow deinos eimi de os pws de ou
english_head: What distinction do you intend by that? he said. I will try to tell you, I said. It is surely the most monstrous and shameful thing in the world for shepherds to breed the dogs who are to help them with their flocks in such wise and of such a nature that from indiscipline or hunger or some other evil condition the dogs themselves shall attack the sheep and injure them and be likened to wolves instead of dogs. A terrible thing, indeed, he said.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν φυλακτέον παντὶ τρόπῳ μὴ τοιοῦτον ἡμῖν οἱ ἐπίκουροι ποιήσωσι πρὸς τοὺς πολίτας ἐπειδὴ αὐτῶν κρείττους εἰσίν
greek_tokens: oukoun fulakteos pas tropos mh toioutos egw e epikouros poiew pros o poliths epei autos kreisswn eimi anti summaxos eumenew despoths agrios afomoiow fulakteos fhmi oukoun o megas o eulabeia paraskeuazw an eien ei o eimi kalos paideuw eimi alla meis eimi ge fhmi kai egw eipon outos men ou acios diisxurizomai eimi filos glauc o mentoi arti legw acios ostis deomai
english_head: Must we not then guard by every means in our power against our helpers treating the citizens in any such way and, because they are the stronger, converting themselves from benign assistants into savage masters? We must, he said. And would they not have been provided with the chief safeguard if their education has really been a good one? But it surely has, he said. That, said I, dear Glaucon, we may not properly affirm, but what we were just now saying we may,

greek_head: αὐτοὺς τῆς ὀρθῆς τυχεῖν παιδείας ἥτις ποτέ ἐστιν εἰ μέλλουσι τὸ μέγιστον ἔχειν πρὸς τὸ ἥμεροι εἶναι αὑτοῖς τε καὶ
greek_tokens: autos o orqos tugxanw paideia ostis pote eimi ei mellw o megas exw pros o hmeros eimi eautou te kai o fulassw upo autos kai orqos ge eimi de os pros toinun o paideia outos fhmi an tis new exw dew kai o oikhsis kai o allos ousia toioutos autos paraskeuazw ostis mhte o fulac ws aristos eimi pauw
english_head: that they must have the right education, whatever it is, if they are to have what will do most to make them gentle to one another and to their charges. That is right, he said. In addition, moreover, to such an education a thoughtful man would affirm that their houses and the possessions provided for them ought to be such as not to interfere with the best performance of their own work as guardians and not to incite them to wrong the other citizens.

greek_head: αὐτούς κακουργεῖν τε μὴ ἐπαρεῖ περὶ τοὺς ἄλλους πολίτας Καὶ ἀληθῶς γε φήσει
greek_tokens: autos kakourgew te mh epairw peri o allos poliths kai alhqhs ge fhmi oraw dh eipon egw ei toiosde tis tropos deomai autos zaw te kai oikew ei mellw toioutos eimi proteros men ousia ktaomai mhdeis mhdeis idios an mh pas anagkh epeita eoika kai tamieion mhdeis eimi mhdeis toioutos eis o ou pas o boulomai eiseimi o de epithdeios osos deomai anhr aqlhths polemos swfrwn te kai
english_head: He will rightly affirm that. Consider then, said I, whether, if that is to be their character, their habitations and ways of life must not be something after this fashion. In the first place, none must possess any private property save the indispensable. Secondly, none must have any habitation or treasure-house which is not open for all to enter at will. Their food, in such quantities as are needful for athletes of war sober and brave,

greek_head: ἀνδρεῖοι ταξαμένους παρὰ τῶν ἄλλων πολιτῶν δέχεσθαι μισθὸν τῆς φυλακῆς τοσοῦτον ὅσον μήτε περιεῖναι αὐτοῖς
greek_tokens: andreios tassw para o allos poliths dexomai misqos o fulakeus tosoutos osos mhte perieimi autos eis o eniautos mhte endew foitaw de eis sussition wsper stratopedeuw koinh zaw xrusion de kai argurion eipon autos ostis qew para qea aei en o yuxw exw kai oudeis prosdeomai o anqrwpeios oude osios o ekeinos ktaomai o o qnhtos xruseos ktaomai summignumi miainw dioti polus kai anosios peri o o
english_head: they must receive as an agreed stipend from the other citizens as the wages of their guardianship, so measured that there shall be neither superfluity at the end of the year nor any lack. And resorting to a common mess like soldiers on campaign they will live together. Gold and silver, we will tell them, they have of the divine quality from the gods always in their souls, and they have no need of the metal of men nor does holiness suffer them to mingle and contaminate that heavenly possession with the acquisition of mortal gold, since many impious deeds have been done about

greek_head: πολλῶν νόμισμα γέγονεν τὸ παρ' ἐκείνοις δὲ ἀκήρατον ἀλλὰ μόνοις αὐτοῖς τῶν ἐν τῇ πόλει μεταχειρίζεσθαι καὶ
greek_tokens: polus nomisma gignomai o para ekeinos de akhratos alla monos autos o en o polis metaxeirizomai kai aptw xruseos kai arguros ou qemis oude upo o autos orofos eimi oude periaptw oude pinw ek arguros h xruseos kai outws men swzw su an kai swzw o polis opote de autos gaia te idios kai oikia kai nomisma ktaomai oikonomos men kai gewrgos anti fulac eimi
english_head: the coin of the multitude, while that which dwells within them is unsullied. But for these only of all the dwellers in the city it is not lawful to handle gold and silver and to touch them nor yet to come under the same roof with them, nor to hang them as ornaments on their limbs nor to drink from silver and gold. So living they would save themselves and save their city. But whenever they shall acquire for themselves land of their own and houses and coin, they will be house-holders and farmers instead of guardians, and will be transformed

greek_head: δεσπόται δ' ἐχθροὶ ἀντὶ συμμάχων τῶν ἄλλων πολιτῶν γενήσονται μισοῦντες δὲ δὴ καὶ μισούμενοι καὶ ἐπιβουλεύοντες
greek_tokens: despoths de exqros anti summaxos o allos poliths gignomai misew de dh kai misew kai epibouleuw kai epibouleuw diagw pas o bios polus plew kai mallon deidw o endon h o ecwqen polemios qew hdh tote eggus oleqros autos te kai o allos polis outos oun pas eneka eimi de egw fhmi outws dew kataskeuazw o fulac oikhsis te peri kai o allos kai outos nomoqetew h mh panu ge eimi de os o glauc
english_head: from the helpers of their fellow citizens to their enemies and masters, and so in hating and being hated, plotting and being plotted against they will pass their days fearing far more and rather the townsmen within than the foemen without—and then even then laying the course of near shipwreck for themselves and the state. For all these reasons, said I, let us declare that such must be the provision for our guardians in lodging and other respects and so legislate. Shall we not? By all means, said Glaucon.

greek_head: Καὶ ὁ Ἀδείμαντος ὑπολαβών Τί οὖν ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες ἀπολογήσῃ ἐάν τίς σε φῇ μὴ πάνυ τι εὐδαίμονας ποιεῖν
