greek_tokens: kai o adeimantos upolambanw tis oun fhmi eimi swkraths apologeomai ean tis su fhmi mh panu tis eudaimwn poiew outos o anhr kai outos dia eautou os eimi men o polis o alhqeia e de mhdeis apolauw agaqos o polis oios allos agros te ktaomai kai oikia oikodomew kalos kai megas kai outos prepw kataskeuh ktaomai kai qusia qeaw idios quw kai cenodokew kai dh kai o nundi su legw xrusos te kai arguros ktaomai kai pas osos nomizw o mellw makarios eimi alla atexnos fhmi an wsper epikouros misqwtos en
english_head: And Adeimantus broke in and said, What will be your defence, Socrates, if anyone objects that you are not making these men very happy, and that through their own fault? For the city really belongs to them and yet they get no enjoyment out of it as ordinary men do by owning lands and building fine big houses and providing them with suitable furniture and winning the favor of the gods by private sacrifices and entertaining guests and enjoying too those possessions which you just now spoke of, gold and silver and all that is customary for those who are expecting to be happy? But they seem, one might say, to be established in idleness in the city,

greek_head: τῇ πόλει φαίνονται καθῆσθαι οὐδὲν ἄλλο ἢ φρουροῦντες Ναί ἦν δ' ἐγώ καὶ ταῦτά γε ἐπισίτιοι καὶ οὐδὲ μισθὸν
greek_tokens: o polis fainw kaqhmai oudeis allos h frourew nai eimi de egw kai outos ge episitios kai oude misqos pros o siteomai lambanw wsper e allos wste oude an apodhmew boulomai idios ecesti autos oude etaira didwmi oude analiskw an poi boulomai allote oios dh e eudaimwn dokew eimi analiskw outos kai allos toioutos suxnos o kathgoria apoleipw alla eimi de os eimi kai outos kathgorew
english_head: exactly like hired mercenaries, with nothing to do but keep guard. Yes, said I, and what is more, they serve for board-wages and do not even receive pay in addition to their food as others do, so that they will not even be able to take a journey on their own account, if they wish to, or make presents to their mistresses, or spend money in other directions according to their desires like the men who are thought to be happy. These and many similar counts of the indictment you are omitting. Well, said he, assume these counts too.

greek_head: Τί οὖν δὴ ἀπολογησόμεθα φῄς Ναί
greek_tokens: tis oun dh apologeomai fhmi nai o autos oimos eimi de egw poreuw euriskw ws oimh o lekteos erew gar ostis qaumastos men an oudeis eaw ei kai outos outws eudaimwn eimi ou meis pros outos blepw o polis oikizw opws eis tis egw eqnos eimi diaferontws eudaimwn alla opws ostis malista oloc o polis oiomai gar en o toioutos malista an euriskw dikaiosunh kai au en o kakos oikew
english_head: What then will be our apology you ask? Yes. By following the same path I think we shall find what to reply. For we shall say that while it would not surprise us if these men thus living prove to be the most happy, yet the object on which we fixed our eyes in the establishment of our state was not the exceptional happiness of any one class but the greatest possible happiness of the city as a whole. For we thought that in a state so constituted we should be most likely to discover justice as we should injustice

greek_head: ἀδικίαν κατιδόντες δὲ κρῖναι ἂν ὃ πάλαι ζητοῦμεν νῦν μὲν οὖν ὡς οἰόμεθα τὴν εὐδαίμονα πλάττομεν οὐκ ἀπολαβόντες
greek_tokens: adikia kateidon de krinw an o pala zhtew nun men oun ws oiomai o eudaimwn plassw ou apolambanw oligos en autos toioutos tis tiqhmi alla oloc autika de o enantios skeptomai wsper oun an ei egw andrias grafw proserxomai tis yegw legos ostis ou o kalos o zwion o kallista farmakon prostiqhmi e gar ofqalmos kalos eimi ou ostreion enaleifw eien alla melas metrios an dokew
english_head: in the worst governed state, and that when we had made these out we could pass judgement on the issue of our long inquiry. Our first task then, we take it, is to mold the model of a happy state—we are not isolating a small class in it and postulating their happiness, but that of the city as a whole. But the opposite type of state we will consider presently. It is as if we were coloring a statue and someone approached and censured us, saying that we did not apply the most beautiful pigments to the most beautiful parts of the image, since the eyes, which are the most beautiful part, have not been painted with purple but with black—

greek_head: πρὸς αὐτὸν ἀπολογεῖσθαι λέγοντες Ὦ θαυμάσιε μὴ οἴου δεῖν ἡμᾶς οὕτω καλοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς γράφειν ὥστε
greek_tokens: pros autos apologeomai legw eimi qaumasios mh oiomai dew egw outws kalos ofqalmos grafw wste mhde ofqalmos fainw mhde au allos mera alla aqrew ei o proshkw ekastos apodidwmi o oloc kalos poiew kai dh kai nun mh anagkazw egw toioutos eudaimonia o fulac prosaptw o ekeinos pas mallon
english_head: we should think it a reasonable justification to reply, Don’t expect us, quaint friend, to paint the eyes so fine that they will not be like eyes at all, nor the other parts. But observe whether by assigning what is proper to each we render the whole beautiful. And so in the present case you must not require us to attach to the guardians a happiness that will make them anything but guardians.

greek_head: ἀπεργάσεται ἢ φύλακας ἐπιστάμεθα γὰρ καὶ τοὺς γεωργοὺς ξυστίδας ἀμφιέσαντες καὶ χρυσὸν περιθέντες πρὸς
greek_tokens: apergazomai h fulac efisthmi gar kai o gewrgos custis amfiazw kai xrusos peritiqhmi pros hdona ergazomai keleuw o gaia kai o kerameos kataklinw epi decia pros o pur diapinw te kai euwxew o troxos paratiqhmi osos an epiqumew kerameuw kai o allos pas toioutos tropos makarios poiew ina dh oloc o polis eudaimonew alla egw mh outws nouqetew ws an su peiqw oute o gewrgos gewrgos
english_head: For in like manner we could clothe the farmers in robes of state and deck them with gold and bid them cultivate the soil at their pleasure, and we could make the potters recline on couches from left to right before the fire drinking toasts and feasting with their wheel alongside to potter with when they are so disposed, and we can make all the others happy in the same fashion, so that thus the entire city may be happy. But urge us not to this,

greek_head: ἔσται οὔτε ὁ κεραμεὺς κεραμεὺς οὔτε ἄλλος οὐδεὶς οὐδὲν ἔχων σχῆμα ἐξ ὧν πόλις γίγνεται ἀλλὰ τῶν μὲν ἄλλων
greek_tokens: eimi oute o kerameus kerameus oute allos oudeis oudeis exw sxhma ek os polis gignomai alla o men allos elasswn logos neurorrafos gar faulos gignomai kai diafqeirw kai prospoiew eimi mh eimi polis oudeis deinos fulac de nomos te kai polis mh eimi alla dokew oraw dh ostis pas ardhn polis apollumi kai au o eu oikew kai eudaimonew monos o kairos exw ei men oun egw men fulac ws alhqhs
english_head: since, if we yield, the farmer will not be a farmer nor the potter a potter, nor will any other of the types that constitute state keep its form. However, for the others it matters less. For cobblers who deteriorate and are spoiled and pretend to be the workmen that they are not are no great danger to a state. But guardians of laws and of the city who are not what they pretend to be, but only seem, destroy utterly, I would have you note, the entire state, and on the other hand, they alone are decisive of its good government and happiness. If then we are forming true guardians

greek_head: ποιοῦμεν ἥκιστα κακούργους τῆς πόλεως ὁ δ' ἐκεῖνο λέγων γεωργούς τινας καὶ ὥσπερ ἐν πανηγύρει ἀλλ' οὐκ ἐν πόλει
greek_tokens: poiew hkistos kakourgos o polis o de ekeinos legos gewrgos tis kai wsper en panhguris alla ou en polis estiatwr eudaimwn allos an tis h polis legos skepteos oun poteros pros outos blepw o fulac kaqisthmi opws ostis pleistos autos eudaimonia eggignomai h outos men eis o polis oloc blepw qeateon ei ekeinos eggignomai o de epikouros outos kai o
english_head: and keepers of our liberties, men least likely to harm the commonwealth, but the proponent of the other ideal is thinking of farmers and happy feasters as it were in a festival and not in a civic community, he would have something else in mind than a state. Consider, then, whether our aim in establishing the guardians is the greatest possible happiness among them or whether that is something we must look to see develop in the city as a whole, but these helpers and guardians

greek_head: φύλακας ἐκεῖνο ἀναγκαστέον ποιεῖν καὶ πειστέον ὅπως ὅτι ἄριστοι δημιουργοὶ τοῦ ἑαυτῶν ἔργου ἔσονται καὶ τοὺς
greek_tokens: fulac ekeinos anagkasteos poiew kai peisteon opws ostis aristos dhmiourgos o eautou ergnumi eimi kai o allos apas wsautws kai outws sumpas o polis aucanw kai kalos oikizw eateos opws ekastos o eqnos o fusis apodidwmi o metalambanw eudaimonia alla eimi de os kalos egw dokew legw ara oun eimi de egw kai o outos adelfos docow su metrios legw tis malista
english_head: are to be constrained and persuaded to do what will make them the best craftsmen in their own work, and similarly all the rest. And so, as the entire city develops and is ordered well, each class is to be left, to the share of happiness that its nature comports. Well, he said, I think you are right. And will you then, I said, also think me reasonable in another point akin to this? What pray? Consider whether

greek_head: Τοὺς ἄλλους αὖ δημιουργοὺς σκόπει εἰ τάδε διαφθείρει ὥστε καὶ κακοὺς γίγνεσθαι
greek_tokens: o allos au dhmiourgos skopaw ei ode diafqeirw wste kai kakos gignomai o poios dh outos ploutos eimi de egw kai penia pws dh wde ploutew xutreus dokew su etai eqelw epimeleomai o texnh oudamos fhmi argos de kai amelhs gignomai mallon autos eautou polus ge oukoun kakos xutreus gignomai kai outos fhmi polus kai meis kai organon ge mh exw parexw upo penia h tis allos o eis o texnh o te ergon ponhros
english_head: these are the causes that corrupt other craftsmen too so as positively to spoil them. What causes? Wealth and poverty, said I. How so? Thus! do you think a potter who grew rich would any longer be willing to give his mind to his craft? By no means, said he. But will he become more idle and negligent than he was? Far more. Then he becomes a worse potter? Far worse too. And yet again, if from poverty he is unable to provide himself with tools and other requirements of his art,

greek_head: ἐργάσεται καὶ τοὺς ὑεῖς ἢ ἄλλους οὓς ἂν διδάσκῃ χείρους δημιουργοὺς διδάξεται
greek_tokens: ergazomai kai o uios h allos os an didaskw xeirwn dhmiourgos didaskw pws de ou upo amfoteros dh penia te kai ploutos xeirwn men o o texnh ergon xeirwn de autos fainw eteros dh ws eoika o fulac euriskw o pas tropos fulakteos opws mhpote autos lanqanw eis o polis paraduomai o poios outos
english_head: the work that he turns out will be worse, and he will also make inferior workmen of his sons or any others whom he teaches. Of course. From both causes, then, poverty and wealth, the products of the arts deteriorate, and so do the artisans? So it appears. Here, then, is a second group of things it seems that our guardians must guard against and do all in their power to keep from slipping into the city without their knowledge. What are they?

greek_head: Πλοῦτός τε ἦν δ' ἐγώ καὶ πενία ὡς τοῦ μὲν τρυφὴν καὶ ἀργίαν καὶ νεωτερισμὸν ἐμποιοῦντος τῆς δὲ ἀνελευθερίαν
greek_tokens: ploutos te eimi de egw kai penia ws o men trufh kai argia kai newterismos empoiew o de aneleuqeria kai kakourgia pros o newterismos panu men oun fhmi ode mentoi eimi swkraths skopaw pws egw o polis oios su eimi polemew epeidan xrhma mh ktaomai eimi allos te kan pros megas te kai plousios anagkazw polemew dhlos eimi de egw ostis pros men eis xalepos
english_head: Wealth and poverty, said I, since the one brings luxury, idleness and innovation, and the other illiberality and the evil of bad workmanship in addition to innovation. Assuredly, he said; yet here is a point for your consideration, Socrates, how our city, possessing no wealth, will be able to wage war, especially if compelled to fight a large and wealthy state. Obviously, said I, it would be rather difficult to fight one such,

greek_head: πρὸς δὲ δύο τοιαύτας ῥᾷον Πῶς εἶπες ἦ δ' ὅς
greek_tokens: pros de duo toioutos raios pws eipon eimi de os proteros men pou eipon ean deomai maxomai ara ou plousios anhr maxomai autos eimi polemos aqlhths nai outos ge fhmi tis oun eimi de egw eimi adeimantos eis pukths ws oios te kallista epi outos paraskeuazw duo mh pukths plousios de kai piwn ou an dokew su radios maxomai ou an isos fhmi ama ge oude ei ecesti eimi de egw upofeugw o proteros aei
english_head: but easier to fight two. What did you mean by that? he said. Tell me first, I said, whether, if they have to fight, they will not be fighting as athletes of war against men of wealth? Yes, that is true, he said. Answer me then, Adeimantus. Do you not think that one boxer perfectly trained in the art could easily fight two fat rich men who knew nothing of it? Not at the same time perhaps, said he. Not even, said I, if he were allowed to retreat

greek_head: προσφερόμενον ἀναστρέφοντα κρούειν καὶ τοῦτο ποιοῖ πολλάκις ἐν ἡλίῳ τε καὶ πνίγει ἆρά γε οὐ καὶ πλείους
greek_tokens: prosferw anastrefw krouw kai outos poiew pollakis en hlios te kai pnigw ara ge ou kai plews xeirow an toioutos o toioutos amelei fhmi oudeis an gignomai qaumastos alla ou oiomai puktikos pleos metexw o plousios episthmh te kai empeiria h polemikos egw fhmi radios ara egw e aqlhths ek o eoika diplasios te kai triplasios eautou maxomai sugxwrew su fhmi dokew gar egw orqos legw
english_head: and then turn and strike the one who came up first, and if he repeated the procedure many times under a burning and stifling sun? Would not such a fighter down even a number of such opponents? Doubtless, he said; it wouldn’t be surprising if he did. Well, don’t you think that the rich have more of the skill and practice of boxing than of the art of war? I do, he said. It will be easy, then, for our athletes in all probability to fight with double and triple their number. I shall have to concede the point,

greek_head: Τί δ' ἂν πρεσβείαν πέμψαντες εἰς τὴν ἑτέραν πόλιν τἀληθῆ εἴπωσιν ὅτι Ἡμεῖς μὲν οὐδὲν χρυσίῳ οὐδ'
greek_tokens: tis de an presbeia pempw eis o eteros polis alhqhs eipon ostis egw men oudeis xruseos oude argurion xraomai oude egw qemis su de sumpolemew oun meqo egw exw o o eteros oiomai tis akouw outos airew kuwn polemew stereos te kai isxnos mallon h meta kuwn probaton piwn te kai apalos ou egw dokew alla ean eis eis fhmi polis sunaqroizw
english_head: he said, for I believe you are right. Well then, if they send an embassy to the other city and say what is in fact true: We make no use of gold and silver nor is it lawful for us but it is for you: do you then join us in the war and keep the spoils of the enemy,—do you suppose any who heard such a proposal would choose to fight against hard and wiry hounds rather than with the aid of the hounds against fat and tender sheep? I think not. Yet consider whether the accumulation

greek_head: τὰ τῶν ἄλλων χρήματα ὅρα μὴ κίνδυνον φέρῃ τῇ μὴ πλουτούσῃ
greek_tokens: o o allos xrhma oraw mh kindunos ferw o mh ploutew eudaimwn ei eimi de egw ostis oiomai acios eimi allos tis proseipon polis h o toioutos oios egw kataskeuazw alla tis meis fhmi megas eimi de egw xrh prosagoreuw o allos ekastos gar autos polis eimi pampolus alla ou polis o o paizw duo men kan otioun eimi polemios allhlwn
english_head: of all the wealth of other cities in one does not involve danger for the state that has no wealth. What happy innocence, said I, to suppose that you can properly use the name city of any other than the one we are constructing. Why, what should we say? he said. A greater predication, said I, must be applied to the others. For they are each one of them many cities, not a city, as it goes in the game. There are two at the least at enmity with one another, the city of the rich

greek_head: ἡ μὲν πενήτων ἡ δὲ πλουσίων τούτων δ' ἐν ἑκατέρᾳ πάνυ πολλαί αἷς ἐὰν μὲν ὡς μιᾷ προσφέρῃ παντὸς ἂν
greek_tokens: o men penhs o de plousios outos de en ekateros panu polus os ean men ws eis prosferw pas an amartanw ean de ws polus didwmi o o eteros o eteros xrhma te kai dunamis h kai autos summaxos men aei polus xraw polemios de oligos kai ews an o polis su oikew swfrwn ws arti etazw megas eimi ou o eudokimew legos alla ws alhqhs megas kai ean monos eimi xilioi o propolemew outws gar megas polis eis ou radios oute en ellhn oute en barbaros
english_head: and the city of the poor, and in each of these there are many. If you deal with them as one you will altogether miss the mark, but if you treat them as a multiplicity by offering to the one faction the property, the power, the very persons of the other, you will continue always to have few enemies and many allies. And so long as your city is governed soberly in the order just laid down, it will be the greatest of cities. I do not mean greatest in repute, but in reality, even though it have only a thousand defenders. For a city of this size

greek_head: εὑρήσεις δοκούσας δὲ πολλὰς καὶ πολλαπλασίας τῆς τηλικαύτης ἢ ἄλλως οἴει
greek_tokens: eurhsis dokew de polus kai pollaplasios o thlikoutos h allos oiomai ou ma o diw fhmi oukoun eimi de egw outos an eaw kai kalos oros o hmeteros arxw osos deomai o megeqos o polis poiew kai alizw eimi osos xwra aforizw o allos xairw eaw tis fhmi oros oimh men eimi de egw ode mexri e an eqelw aucanw eimi eis mexri outos aucanw pera de mh
english_head: that is really one you will not easily discover either among Greeks or barbarians—but of those that seem so you will find many and many times the size of this. Or do you think otherwise? No, indeed I don’t, said he. Would not this, then, be the best rule and measure for our governors of the proper size of the city and of the territory that they should mark off for a city of that size and seek no more? What is the measure? I think, said I, that they should let it grow so long as in its growth it consents to remain a unity,

greek_head: Καὶ καλῶς γ' ἔφη Οὐκοῦν καὶ τοῦτο αὖ ἄλλο πρόσταγμα τοῖς φύλαξι
greek_tokens: kai kalos ge fhmi oukoun kai outos au allos prostagma o fulac prosstazw fulassw pas tropos opws mhte mikros o polis eimi mhte megas dokew alla tis ikanos kai eis kai faulos ge fhmi isos autos prosstazw kai outos ge eimi de egw eti faulos ode e kai en o prosqen epimimnhskomai legw ws dew ean o fulac tis faulos ekgonos gignomai eis o allos
english_head: but no further. Excellent, he said. Then is not this still another injunction that we should lay upon our guardians, to keep guard in every way that the city shall not be too small, nor great only in seeming, but that it shall be a sufficient city and one? That behest will perhaps be an easy one for them, he said. And still easier, haply, I said, is this that we mentioned before when we said that if a degenerate offspring was born to the guardians he must be sent away to the other classes,

greek_head: αὐτὸν ἀποπέμπεσθαι ἐάντ' ἐκ τῶν ἄλλων σπουδαῖος εἰς τοὺς φύλακας τοῦτο δ' ἐβούλετο δηλοῦν ὅτι καὶ τοὺς
greek_tokens: autos apopempw ean ek o allos spoudaios eis o fulac outos de boulomai dhlow ostis kai o allos poliths pros o tis fuw pros outos enos pros eis ekastos ergnumi deomai komizw opws an eis o eautou epithdeuw ekastos mh polus alla eis gignomai kai outws dh sumpas o polis eis fuw alla mh polus eimi gar fhmi outos ekeinos mikros outoi eimi de egw eimi agaqos adeimantos ws dokew an tis outos polus kai megas autos prostassw alla
english_head: and likewise if a superior to the others he must be enrolled among the guardians; and the purport of all this was that the other citizens too must be sent to the task for which their natures were fitted, one man to one work, in order that each of them fulfilling his own function may be not many men, but one, and so the entire city may come to be not a multiplicity but a unity. Why yes, he said, this is even more trifling than that. These are not, my good Adeimantus, as one might suppose, numerous and difficult injunctions that

greek_head: πάντα φαῦλα ἐὰν τὸ λεγόμενον ἓν μέγα φυλάττωσι μᾶλλον δ' ἀντὶ μεγάλου ἱκανόν
greek_tokens: pas faulos ean o legw eis megas fulassw mallon de anti megas ikanos tis outos fhmi o paideia eimi de egw kai trofh ean gar eu paideuw metrios anhr gignomai pas outos radios dioraw kai allos ge osos nun egw paraleipw o te o gunaikow ktaomai kai gamos kai paidopoiia ostis
english_head: we are imposing upon them, but they are all easy, provided they guard, as the saying is, the one great thing—or instead of great let us call it sufficient. What is that? he said. Their education and nurture, I replied. For if a right education makes of them reasonable men they will easily discover everything of this kind—and other principles that we now pass over, as that the possession of wives and marriage,

greek_head: δεῖ ταῦτα κατὰ τὴν παροιμίαν πάντα ὅτι μάλιστα κοινὰ τὰ φίλων ποιεῖσθαι
greek_tokens: deomai outos kata o paroimia pas ostis malista koinos o filos poiew orqos gar fhmi gignomai an kai meis eipon politeia ean apac ormhsis eu erxomai wsper kuklos aucanw trofh gar kai paideusis xrhstos swzw fusis agaqos empoiew kai au fusis xrhstos toioutos paideia antilambanw eti beltiwn o proteros fuw eis te allos kai eis o
english_head: and the procreation of children and all that sort of thing should be made as far as possible the proverbial goods of friends that are common. Yes, that would be the best way, he said. And, moreover, said I, the state, if it once starts well, proceeds as it were in a cycle of growth. I mean that a sound nurture and education if kept up creates good natures in the state, and sound natures in turn receiving an education of this sort develop into better men than their predecessors

greek_head: γεννᾶν ὥσπερ καὶ ἐν τοῖς ἄλλοις ζῴοις Εἰκός γ' ἔφη
greek_tokens: genna wsper kai en o allos zwion eoika ge fhmi ws toinun dia braxea eipon outos anqekteon o epimelhths o polis opws an autos mh lanqanw diafqeirw alla para pas autos fulassw o mh newterizw peri gumnastikos te kai mousikh para o tacis alla ws oios te malista fulassw fobew otan tis legos ws o aoidh mallon epifronew anqrwpos ostis aeidw neos amfipelomai
english_head: both for other purposes and for the production of offspring as among animals also. It is probable, he said. To put it briefly, then, said I, it is to this that the overseers of our state must cleave and be watchful against its insensible corruption. They must throughout be watchful against innovations in music and gymnastics counter to the established order, and to the best of their power guard against them, fearing when anyone says that That song is most regarded among men Which hovers newest on the singer’s lips, Hom. Od. 1.351

greek_head: μὴ πολλάκις τὸν ποιητήν τις οἴηται λέγειν οὐκ ᾄσματα νέα ἀλλὰ τρόπον ᾠδῆς νέον καὶ τοῦτο ἐπαινῇ δεῖ δ' οὔτ'
greek_tokens: mh pollakis o poihths tis oiomai legw ou aeisma naus alla tropos aoidh neos kai outos epainew deomai de oute epainew o toioutos oute upolambanw eidos gar kainos mousikh metaballw eulabhteon ws en oloc kinduneuw oudamos gar kinew mousikh tropos aneu politikos nomos o megas os fhmi te damaw kai egw peiqw kai egw toinun fhmi o adeimantos tiqhmi o peiqw
english_head: lest haply it be supposed that the poet means not new songs but a new way of song and is commending this. But we must not praise that sort of thing nor conceive it to be the poet’s meaning. For a change to a new type of music is something to beware of as a hazard of all our fortunes. For the modes of music are never disturbed without unsettling of the most fundamental political and social conventions, as Damon affirms and as I am convinced. Set me too down in the number of the convinced, said Adeimantus.

greek_head: Τὸ δὴ φυλακτήριον ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὡς ἔοικεν ἐνταῦθά που οἰκοδομητέον τοῖς φύλαξιν ἐν μουσικῇ
greek_tokens: o dh fulakthrion eimi de egw ws eoika entauqa pou oikodomhteon o fulac en mousikh o goun paranomia fhmi radios outos lanqanw paraduomai nai fhmi ws en paidih ge meros kai ws kakos oudeis ergazomai oude gar ergazomai fhmi allos ge h kata mikros eisoikizw hrema uporrew pros o hqos te kai o epithdeuma ek de outos eis o pros allhlwn sumbolaion megas ekbainw ek de dh o sumbolaion erxomai epi
english_head: It is here, then, I said, in music, as it seems, that our guardians must build their guard-house and post of watch. It is certain, he said, that this is the kind of lawlessness that easily insinuates itself unobserved. Yes, said I, because it is supposed to be only a form of play and to work no harm. Nor does it work any, he said, except that by gradual infiltration it softly overflows upon the characters and pursuits of men and from these issues forth grown greater to attack their business dealings, and from these relations

greek_head: τοὺς νόμους καὶ πολιτείας σὺν πολλῇ ὦ Σώκρατες ἀσελγείᾳ ἕως ἂν τελευτῶσα πάντα ἰδίᾳ καὶ δημοσίᾳ ἀνατρέψῃ
greek_tokens: o nomos kai politeia sun polus eimi swkraths aselgeia ews an teleutaw pas idios kai dhmosios anatreyis eien eimi de egw outws outos exis dokew egw fhmi oukoun o ek arxh legw o hmeteros pais ennomos euqus paidih meqekteos ws paranomos gignomai autos kai pais toioutos ennomos te kai spoudaios
english_head: it proceeds against the laws and the constitution with wanton licence, Socrates, till finally it overthrows all things public and private. Well, said I, are these things so? I think so, he said. Then, as we were saying in the beginning, our youth must join in a more law-abiding play, since, if play grows lawless and the children likewise,

greek_head: ἐξ αὐτῶν ἄνδρας αὐξάνεσθαι ἀδύνατον ὄν Πῶς δ' οὐχί ἔφη
greek_tokens: ek autos anhr aucanw adunatos eimi pws de ou fhmi otan dh ara kalos arxw pais paizw eunomia dia o mousikh eisdexomai palin enantion h ekeinos eis pas sunepomai te kai aucis epanorqow ei tis kai proteros o polis keimai alhqhs mentoi fhmi kai o mikros ara eipon dokew eimi nomimos eceuriskw outos o e proteros apollumi pas o poios
english_head: it is impossible that they should grow up to be men of serious temper and lawful spirit. Of course, he said. And so we may reason that when children in their earliest play are imbued with the spirit of law and order through their music, the opposite of the former supposition happens—this spirit waits upon them in all things and fosters their growth, and restores and sets up again whatever was overthrown in the other type of state. True, indeed, he said. Then such men rediscover for themselves those seemingly trifling conventions which their predecessors abolished altogether. Of what sort? Such things as

greek_head: Τὰ τοιάδε σιγάς τε τῶν νεωτέρων παρὰ πρεσβυτέροις ἃς πρέπει καὶ κατακλίσεις καὶ ὑπαναστάσεις καὶ γονέων
greek_tokens: o toiosde sigas te o neos para presbus os prepw kai kataklisis kai upanastasis kai gonaw qerapeia kai koura ge kai ampexonh kai upodesis kai oloc o o swma sxhmatismos kai allos osos toioutos h ou oiomai egw nomoqetew de autos oimh euhqhs oute gar pou gignomai oute an menw logos te kai gramma nomoqetew pws gar kinduneuw goun eimi de egw eimi adeimantos ek o paideia
english_head: the becoming silence of the young in the presence of their elders; the giving place to them and rising up before them, and dutiful service of parents, and the cut of the hair and the garments and the fashion of the foot-gear, and in general the deportment of the body and everything of the kind. Don’t you think so? I do. Yet to enact them into laws would, I think, be silly. For such laws are not obeyed nor would they last, being enacted only in words and on paper. How could they? At any rate, Adeimantus, I said, the direction of the education from whence one starts is likely to determine

greek_head: ὅποι ἄν τις ὁρμήσῃ τοιαῦτα καὶ τὰ ἑπόμενα εἶναι ἢ οὐκ ἀεὶ τὸ ὅμοιον ὂν ὅμοιον παρακαλεῖ
greek_tokens: opoi an tis ormhsis toioutos kai o epomai eimi h ou aei o omoios eimi omoios parakalew tis meis kai teleutaw dh oimh fhmi an eis eis tis teleios kai neanikos apobainw autos h agaqos h kai enantion tis gar ou eimi de os egw men toinun eipon dia outos ou an eti o toioutos epixeirew nomoqetew eoikotws ge fhmi tis de eimi pros qea fhmi ode o agoraios sumbolaion te peri kata agora ekastos o pros allhlwn sumballw
english_head: the quality of what follows. Does not like ever summon like? Surely. And the final outcome, I presume, we would say is one complete and vigorous product of good or the reverse. Of course, said he. For my part, then, I said, for these reasons I would not go on to try to legislate on such matters. With good reason, said he. But what, in heaven’s name, said I, about business matters, the deals that men make with one another in the agora—

greek_head: εἰ δὲ βούλει καὶ χειροτεχνικῶν περὶ συμβολαίων καὶ λοιδοριῶν καὶ αἰκίας καὶ δικῶν λήξεως καὶ δικαστῶν
greek_tokens: ei de boulomai kai xeirotexnikos peri sumbolaion kai loidoria kai aikia kai dikh lhcis kai dikasths katastasis kai ei pou tellw tis h prassw h qesis anagkaios eimi h kata agora h limhn h kai o parapan agoranomikos atta h astunomikos h ellimenikos h osos allos toioutos outos tolmaw tis nomoqetew alla ou acios fhmi anhr kalew agaqos epitassw
english_head: and, if you please, contracts with workmen and actions for foul language and assault, the filing of declarations, the impanelling of juries, the payment and exaction of any dues that may be needful in markets or harbors and in general market, police or harbor regulations and the like, can we bring ourselves to legislate about these? Nay, ʼtwould not be fitting, he said, to dictate to good and honorable men. For most of the enactments that are needed about these things

greek_head: τὰ πολλὰ γὰρ αὐτῶν ὅσα δεῖ νομοθετήσασθαι ῥᾳδίως που εὑρήσουσιν
greek_tokens: o polus gar autos osos deomai nomoqetew radios pou euriskw nai eimi filos eipon ean ge qeos autos didwmi swthria o nomos os emprosqen dierxomai ei de mh ge eimi de os polus toioutos tiqhmi aei kai epanorqow o bios diatelew oiomai epilambanw o beltistos legw fhmi egw biow o toioutos wsper o kamnw te kai ou eqelw upo akolasia ekbainw ponhros diaita panu men oun
english_head: they will easily, I presume, discover. Yes, my friend, provided God grants them the preservation of the principles of law that we have already discussed. Failing that, said he, they will pass their lives multiplying such petty laws and amending them in the expectation of attaining what is best. You mean, said I, that the life of such citizens will resemble that of men who are sick, yet from intemperance are unwilling to abandon their unwholesome regimen.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν οὗτοί γε χαριέντως διατελοῦσιν ἰατρευόμενοι γὰρ οὐδὲν περαίνουσιν πλήν γε ποικιλώτερα καὶ μείζω
greek_tokens: kai meis outos ge xarieis diatelew iatreuw gar oudeis perainw plhn ge poikilos kai megas poiew o noshma kai aei elpizw ean tis farmakon sumbouleusis upo outos eimi ugihs panu gar fhmi o outws kamnw o toioutos paqh tis de eimi de egw ode autos ou xarieis o pas exqistos hgeomai o alhqhs legw ostis prin an mequw kai empiplhmi kai afrodisiazw kai argew pauw
english_head: By all means. And truly, said I, these latter go on in a most charming fashion. For with all their doctoring they accomplish nothing except to complicate and augment their maladies. And they are always hoping that some one will recommend a panacea that will restore their health. A perfect description, he said, of the state of such invalids. And isn’t this a charming trait in them, that they hate most in all the world him who tells them the truth that until a man stops drinking and gorging and wenching

greek_head: οὔτε φάρμακα οὔτε καύσεις οὔτε τομαὶ οὐδ' αὖ ἐπῳδαὶ αὐτὸν οὐδὲ περίαπτα οὐδὲ ἄλλο τῶν τοιούτων οὐδὲν ὀνήσει
greek_tokens: oute farmakon oute kaiw oute tomh oude au epwdh autos oude periaptos oude allos o toioutos oudeis onhsis ou panu xarieis fhmi o gar o eu legw xalepainw ou exis xaris ou epaineths ei fhmi egw ws eoika o toioutos androw ou mentoi ma dios oude an o polis ara osper arti legw oloc toioutos poiew ou epainesis h ou fainw su tautos ergazomai outos o polis osos kakos politeuw proagoreuw
english_head: and idling, neither drugs nor cautery nor the knife, no, nor spells nor periapts will be of any avail? Not altogether charming, he said, for there is no grace or charm in being angry with him who speaks well. You do not seem to be an admirer of such people, said I. No, by heaven, I am not. Neither then, if an entire city, as we were just now saying, acts in this way, will it have your approval, or don’t you think that the way of such invalids is precisely that of those cities

greek_head: τοῖς πολίταις τὴν μὲν κατάστασιν τῆς πόλεως ὅλην μὴ κινεῖν ὡς ἀποθανουμένους ὃς ἂν τοῦτο δρᾷ ὃς δ' ἂν
greek_tokens: o poliths o men katastasis o polis oloc mh kinew ws apoqnhskw os an outos draw os de an sfazw outws politeuw hdus qerapeuw kai xarizomai upotrexw kai progignwskw o sfeteros boulhsis kai outos deinos eimi apoplhrow outos ara agaqos te eimi anhr kai sofos o megas kai timaw upo sfazw tautos men oun fhmi egw dokew draw kai oude opwsoun epainew
english_head: which being badly governed forewarn their citizens not to meddle with the general constitution of the state, denouncing death to whosoever attempts that—while whoever most agreeably serves them governed as they are and who curries favor with them by fawning upon them and anticipating their desires and by his cleverness in gratifying them, him they will account the good man, the man wise in worthwhile things, the man they will delight to honor? Yes, he said, I think their conduct is identical, and I don’t approve it in the very least.

greek_head: Τί δ' αὖ τοὺς ἐθέλοντας θεραπεύειν τὰς τοιαύτας πόλεις καὶ προθυμουμένους οὐκ ἄγασαι τῆς ἀνδρείας τε καὶ εὐχερείας
greek_tokens: tis de au o eqelw qerapeuw o toioutos polis kai proqumeomai ou agamai o andreia te kai euxereia egw fhmi plhn ge osos ecapataw upo autos kai oiomai o alhqeia politikos eimi ostis epainew upo o polus pws legw ou suggignwskw eimi de egw o anhr h oiomai oios su eimi andris mh efisthmi metrew eteros
english_head: And what again of those who are willing and eager to serve such states? Don’t you admire their valiance and light-hearted irresponsibility? I do, he said, except those who are actually deluded and suppose themselves to be in truth statesmen because they are praised by the many. What do you mean? Can’t you make allowances for the men? Do you think it possible for a man who does not know how to measure when a multitude of others equally ignorant assure him that he is four cubits tall

greek_head: τοιούτων πολλῶν λεγόντων ὅτι τετράπηχύς ἐστιν αὐτὸν ταῦτα μὴ ἡγεῖσθαι περὶ αὑτοῦ
greek_tokens: toioutos polus legw ostis tetraphxus eimi autos outos mh hgeomai peri eautou ou au fhmi outos ge mh toinun xalepainw kai gar pou eimi pas xarieis e toioutos nomoqetew te oios arti dierxomai kai epanorqow aei oiomai tis pera euriskw peri o en o sumbolaion kakourghma kai peri o nundi egw elegos agnoew ostis o eimi wsper udra temnw
english_head: not to suppose this to be the fact about himself? Why no, he said, I don’t think that. Then don’t be harsh with them. For surely such fellows are the most charming spectacle in the world when they enact and amend such laws as we just now described and are perpetually expecting to find a way of putting an end to frauds in business and in the other matters of which I was speaking because they can’t see that they are in very truth trying to cut off a Hydra’s head.

greek_head: Καὶ μήν ἔφη οὐκ ἄλλο γέ τι ποιοῦσιν Ἐγὼ μὲν τοίνυν ἦν δ' ἐγώ τὸ τοιοῦτον εἶδος νόμων
greek_tokens: kai meis fhmi ou allos ge tis poiew egw men toinun eimi de egw o toioutos eidos nomos peri kai politeia oute en kakos oute en eu politeuw polis oiomai an dew o alhqinos nomoqeths pragmateuomai en o men ostis anwfelhs kai pleos oudeis en de o ostis o men autos kan ostis euriskw o de ostis automatos epeimi ek o emprosqen epithdeuma
english_head: Indeed, he said, that is exactly what they are doing. I, then, said I, should not have supposed that the true lawgiver ought to work out matters of that kind in the laws and the constitution either of an ill-governed or a well-governed state—in the one because they are useless and accomplish nothing, in the other because some of them anybody could discover and others will result spontaneously from the pursuits already described.

greek_head: Τί οὖν ἔφη ἔτι ἂν ἡμῖν λοιπὸν τῆς νομοθεσίας εἴη Καὶ ἐγὼ εἶπον ὅτι Ἡμῖν μὲν οὐδέν τῷ μέντοι Ἀπόλλωνι
greek_tokens: tis oun fhmi eti an egw loipos o nomoqesia eaw kai egw eipon ostis egw men oudeis o mentoi apollwn o en delfoi o ge megas kai kallista kai proteros o nomoqethma o poios eimi de os iera te idrusis kai qusia kai allos qea te kai daimwn kai hrws qerapeia teleutaw te au qhkh kai osos o ekei deomai uphretew ilaos autos exw o gar dh toioutos oute efisthmi egw oikizw te polis
english_head: What part of legislation, then, he said, is still left for us? And I replied, For us nothing, but for the Apollo of Delphi, the chief, the fairest and the first of enactments. What are they? he said. The founding of temples, and sacrifices, and other forms of worship of gods, daemons, and heroes; and likewise the burial of the dead and the services we must render to the dwellers in the world beyond to keep them gracious. For of such matters

greek_head: οὐδενὶ ἄλλῳ πεισόμεθα ἐὰν νοῦν ἔχωμεν οὐδὲ χρησόμεθα ἐξηγητῇ ἀλλ' ἢ τῷ πατρίῳ οὗτος γὰρ δήπου ὁ θεὸς περὶ τὰ
greek_tokens: oudeis allos pasxw ean new exw oude xraw echghths alla h o patrios outos gar dhpou o qeos peri o toioutos pas anqrwpos patrios echghths en mesos o gaia epi o omfalos kaqhmai echgeomai kai kalos ge fhmi legw kai poihteos outws oikizw men toinun eimi de egw hdh an su eaw eimi pais
english_head: we neither know anything nor in the founding of our city if we are wise shall we entrust them to any other or make use of any other interpreter than the God of our fathers. For this God surely is in such matters for all mankind the interpreter of the religion of their fathers who from his seat in the middle and at the very navel of the earth delivers his interpretation. Excellently said, he replied; and that is what we must do.

greek_head: Ἀρίστωνος ἡ πόλις τὸ δὲ δὴ μετὰ τοῦτο σκόπει ἐν αὐτῇ φῶς ποθὲν πορισάμενος ἱκανόν αὐτός τε καὶ τὸν ἀδελφὸν
greek_tokens: aristwn o polis o de dh meta outos skopaw en autos faos pinw porizw ikanos autos te kai o adelfos parakalew kai polemarxos kai o allos ean pws eidon pou pote an eaw o dikaiosunh kai pou o adikia kai tis allhlwn diaferw kai poteros deomai ktaomai o mellw eudaimwn eimi ean lanqanw ean mh pas qeos te kai anqrwpos oudeis legw fhmi o glauc su gar upexw zhtew
english_head: At last, then, son of Ariston, said I, your city may be considered as established. The next thing is to procure a sufficient light somewhere and to look yourself, and call in the aid of your brother and of Polemarchus and the rest, if we may in any wise discover where justice and injustice should be in it, wherein they differ from one another and which of the two he must have who is to be happy, alike whether his condition is known or not known to all gods and men. Nonsense, said Glaucon, you promised that you would carry on the search yourself,

greek_head: ὡς οὐχ ὅσιόν σοι ὂν μὴ οὐ βοηθεῖν δικαιοσύνῃ εἰς δύναμιν παντὶ τρόπῳ
greek_tokens: ws ou osios su eimi mh ou bohqew dikaiosunh eis dunamis pas tropos alhqhs fhmi egw upomimnhskw kai poihteos men ge outws xrh de kai su sullambanw alla fhmi poiew outws elpizw toinun eimi de egw euriskw autos wde oimh egw o polis eiper orqos ge oikizw teleios agaqos eimi anagkh ge fhmi dhlos dh ostis sofos su eimi kai andreia kai swfrwn kai dikaios dhlos oukoun ostis an autos euriskw en autos o upoloipos eimi o ou euriskw
english_head: admitting that it would be impious for you not to come to the aid of justice by every means in your power. A true reminder, I said, and I must do so, but you also must lend a hand. Well, he said, we will. I expect then, said I, that we shall find it in this way. I think our city, if it has been rightly founded is good in the full sense of the word. Necessarily, he said. Clearly, then, it will be wise, brave, sober, and just. Clearly. Then if we find any of these qualities in it, the remainder will be that which we have not found?

greek_head: Τί μήν Ὥσπερ τοίνυν ἄλλων τινῶν τεττάρων εἰ ἕν τι ἐζητοῦμεν
greek_tokens: tis meis wsper toinun allos tis tessares ei eis tis zhtew autos en ostis opote proteros ekeinos gignwskw ikanos an exw egw ei de o treis proteros gnwrizw autos an outos gnwrizw o zhtew dhlos gar ostis ou allos eti eimi h o upoleipw orqos fhmi legw oukoun kai peri outos epei tessares eimi tugxanw wsautws zhthteos dhlos dh kai men dh proteros ge egw dokew en autos katadhlos eimi
english_head: Surely. Take the case of any four other things. If we were looking for any one of them in anything and recognized the object of our search first, that would have been enough for us, but if we had recognized the other three first, that in itself would have made known to us the thing we were seeking. For plainly there was nothing left for it to be but the remainder. Right, he said. And so, since these are four, we must conduct the search in the same way. Clearly. And, moreover,

greek_head: ἡ σοφία καί τι ἄτοπον περὶ αὐτὴν φαίνεται Τί ἦ δ' ὅς
greek_tokens: o sofia kai tis atopos peri autos fainw tis eimi de os sofos men o eimi dokew egw o polis eimi os dierxomai euboulos gar ou nai kai meis outos ge autos o euboulia dhlos ostis episthmh tis eimi ou gar pou amaqia ge alla episthmh eu bouleuw dhlos polus de ge kai pantodapos episthmh en o polis eimi pws gar ou ara oun dia o o tektwn episthmh sofos kai euboulos o polis prosrhteos
english_head: the first thing that I think I clearly see therein is the wisdom, and there is something odd about that, it appears. What? said he. Wise in very deed I think the city that we have described is, for it is well counselled, is it not? Yes. And surely this very thing, good counsel, is a form of wisdom. For it is not by ignorance but by knowledge that men counsel well. Obviously. But there are many and manifold knowledges or sciences in the city. Of course. Is it then owing to the science of her carpenters that

greek_head: Οὐδαμῶς ἔφη διά γε ταύτην ἀλλὰ τεκτονική Οὐκ ἄρα διὰ τὴν ὑπὲρ τῶν ξυλίνων σκευῶν ἐπιστήμην
greek_tokens: oudamos fhmi dia ge outos alla tektonikos ou ara dia o uper o culinos skeuos episthmh bouleuw ws an exw beltistos sofos klhteos polis ou mentoi tis de o uper o ek o xalkeos h tis allos o toioutos oude ostis fhmi oude o uper o karpew o genesis ek o gaia alla gewrgikos dokew egw tis de eimi de egw eimi tis episthmh en o arti ufh egw oikizw para tis o poliths h ou uper o
english_head: a city is to be called wise and well advised? By no means for that, but rather mistress of the arts of building. Then a city is not to be styled wise because of the deliberations of the science of wooden utensils for their best production? No, I grant you. Is it, then, because of that of brass implements or any other of that kind? None whatsoever, he said. Nor yet because of the science of the production of crops from the soil, but the name it takes from that is agricultural. I think so. Then, said I, is there any science in the city just founded by us residing in any of its citizens which does not take counsel about some particular thing

greek_head: ἐν τῇ πόλει τινὸς βουλεύεται ἀλλ' ὑπὲρ αὑτῆς ὅλης ὅντινα τρόπον αὐτή τε πρὸς αὑτὴν καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἄλλας πόλεις
greek_tokens: en o polis tis bouleuw alla uper eautou oloc ostis tropos autos te pros eautou kai pros o allos polis ariston omilew eimi mentoi tis fhmi egw kai en tis outos eimi de os o fulakikos kai en outos o arxw os nundi teleios fulac onomazw dia outos oun o episthmh tis o polis prosagoreuw euboulos fhmi kai o eimi sofos poteros oun eimi de egw en o polis oiomai egw xalkeos
english_head: in the city but about the city as a whole and the betterment of its relations with itself and other states? Why, there is. What is it, said I, and in whom is it found? It is the science of guardianship or government and it is to be found in those rulers to whom we just now gave the name of guardians in the full sense of the word. And what term then do you apply to the city because of this knowledge? Well advised, he said, and truly wise. Which class, then, said I,

greek_head: πλείους ἐνέσεσθαι ἢ τοὺς ἀληθινοὺς φύλακας τούτους Πολύ ἔφη χαλκέας
greek_tokens: plews enennumi h o alhqinos fulac outos polus fhmi xalkeos oukoun fhmi kai o allos osos episthmh exw onomazw tis eimi pas outos outos an eien oligistos polus ge o mikros ara eqnos kai meros eautou kai o en outos episthmh o proisthmi kai arxw oloc sofos an eaw kata fus oikizw polis kai outos ws eoika fusis
english_head: do you suppose will be the more numerous in our city, the smiths or these true guardians? The smiths, by far, he said. And would not these rulers be the smallest of all the groups of those who possess special knowledge and receive distinctive appellations? By far. Then it is by virtue of its smallest class and minutest part of itself, and the wisdom that resides therein, in the part which takes the lead and rules, that a city established on principles of nature would be wise as a whole. And as it appears

greek_head: ὀλίγιστον γίγνεται γένος ᾧ προσήκει ταύτης τῆς ἐπιστήμης μεταλαγχάνειν ἣν μόνην δεῖ τῶν ἄλλων ἐπιστημῶν σοφίαν
greek_tokens: oligistos gignomai genos os proshkw outos o episthmh metalagxanw os monos deomai o allos episthmh sofia kalew alhqhs fhmi legw outos men dh eis o tessares ou oida ostis tropos euriskw autos te kai opou o polis idruw egw goun dokew fhmi apoxrwntws euriskw alla meis andreia ge autos te kai en os kew o polis dia o toioutos klhteos o polis ou panu xalepos eidon pws dh
english_head: these are by nature the fewest, the class to which it pertains to partake of the knowledge which alone of all forms of knowledge deserves the name of wisdom. Most true, he said. This one of our four, then, we have, I know not how, discovered, the thing itself and its place in the state. I certainly think, said he, that it has been discovered sufficiently. But again there is no difficulty in seeing bravery itself and the part of the city in which it resides for which the city is called brave. How so? Who, said I,

greek_head: Τίς ἄν ἦν δ' ἐγώ εἰς ἄλλο τι ἀποβλέψας ἢ δειλὴν ἢ ἀνδρείαν πόλιν εἴποι ἀλλ' ἢ εἰς τοῦτο τὸ μέρος ὃ προπολεμεῖ
greek_tokens: tis an eimi de egw eis allos tis apoblepw h deilos h andreia polis eipon alla h eis outos o meros o propolemew te kai strateuw uper autos oude an eis fhmi eis allos tis ou gar oimh eipon e ge allos en autos h deiloomai h andreios eimi kurios an eien h toios autos eimi h toios ou gar kai andreia ara polis meros tis eautou eimi dia o en ekeinos exw dunamis toioutos o dia pas swzw o peri
english_head: in calling a city cowardly or brave would fix his eyes on any other part of it than that which defends it and wages war in its behalf? No one at all, he said. For the reason, I take it, said I, that the cowardice or the bravery of the other inhabitants does not determine for it the one quality or the other. It does not. Bravery too, then, belongs to a city by virtue of a part of itself owing to its possession in that part of a quality that under all conditions will preserve the conviction

greek_head: τῶν δεινῶν δόξαν ταῦτά τε αὐτὰ εἶναι καὶ τοιαῦτα ἅ τε καὶ οἷα ὁ νομοθέτης παρήγγελλεν ἐν τῇ παιδείᾳ ἢ οὐ τοῦτο
greek_tokens: o deinazw doca outos te autos eimi kai toioutos o te kai oios o nomoqeths paraggellw en o paideia h ou outos andreia kalew ou panu fhmi manqanw o eipon alla auqis eipon swthria egw eipon legos tis eimi o andreia poa dh swthria o o doca o upo nomos dia o paideia gignomai peri o deinazw o su eimi kai oios dia pas de elegos autos swthria o en te luph eimi diaswzw autos kai en
english_head: that things to be feared are precisely those which and such as the lawgiver inculcated in their education. Is not that what you call bravery? I don’t altogether understand what you said, he replied; but say it again. A kind of conservation, I said, is what I mean by bravery. What sort of a conservation? The conservation of the conviction which the law has created by education about fearful things—what and what sort of things are to be feared. And by the phrase under all conditions I mean that the brave man preserves it both in pain

greek_head: ἡδοναῖς καὶ ἐν ἐπιθυμίαις καὶ ἐν φόβοις καὶ μὴ ἐκβάλλειν ᾧ δέ μοι δοκεῖ ὅμοιον εἶναι ἐθέλω ἀπεικάσαι εἰ βούλει
greek_tokens: hdona kai en epiqumia kai en fobos kai mh ekballw os de egw dokew omoios eimi eqelw apeikazw ei boulomai alla boulomai oukoun oida eimi de egw ostis e bafeus epeidan boulomai baptw erion wste eimi alourghs proteros men eklegw ek tosoutos xrwma eis fus o o leukh epeita proparaskeuazw ou oligos paraskeuazw qerapeuw opws dexomai ostis malista o anqos kai outws dh baptw
english_head: and pleasures and in desires and fears and does not expel it from his soul. And I may illustrate it by a similitude if you please. I do. You are aware that dyers when they wish to dye wool so as to hold the purple hue begin by selecting from the many colors there be the one nature of the white and then give it a careful preparatory treatment so that it will take the hue in the best way, and after the treatment, then and then only, dip it in the dye.

greek_head: καὶ ὃ μὲν ἂν τούτῳ τῷ τρόπῳ βαφῇ δευσοποιὸν γίγνεται τὸ βαφέν καὶ ἡ πλύσις οὔτ' ἄνευ ῥυμμάτων οὔτε μετὰ ῥυμμάτων
greek_tokens: kai o men an outos o tropos baptw deusopoios gignomai o baptw kai o plusis oute aneu rumma oute meta rumma dunamai autos o anqos afairew o de an mh oida oios dh gignomai ean tis allos xrwma bapths ean kai outos mh proqerapeuw oida fhmi ostis kai ekplutos kai geloios toioutos toinun eimi de egw upolambanw kata dunamis ergazomai kai egw oste eklegw o stratiwths kai
english_head: And things that are dyed by this process become fast-colored and washing either with or without lyes cannot take away the sheen of their hues. But otherwise you know what happens to them, whether anyone dips other colors or even these without the preparatory treatment. I know, he said, that they present a ridiculous and washed-out appearance. By this analogy, then, said I, you must conceive what we too to the best of our ability were doing when we selected our soldiers and educated them in music

greek_head: ἐπαιδεύομεν μουσικῇ καὶ γυμναστικῇ μηδὲν οἴου ἄλλο μηχανᾶσθαι ἢ ὅπως ἡμῖν ὅτι κάλλιστα τοὺς νόμους πεισθέντες
greek_tokens: paideuw mousikh kai gumnastikos mhdeis oiomai allos mhxanaomai h opws egw ostis kallista o nomos peiqw dexomai wsper bafh ina deusopoios autos o doca gignomai kai peri deinazw kai peri o allos dia o o te fus kai o trofh epithdeios exw kai mh autos ekplunw o bafh o rumma outos deinos eimi ekkluzw o te hdona pas xalestraios deinos eimi outos draw
english_head: and exercises of the body. The sole aim of our contrivance was that they should be convinced and receive our laws like a dye as it were, so that their belief and faith might be fast-colored both about the things that are to be feared and all other things because of the fitness of their nature and nurture, and that so their dyes might not be washed out by those lyes that have such dread power to scour our faiths away, pleasure more potent than any detergent or abstergent

greek_head: καὶ κονίας λύπη τε καὶ φόβος καὶ ἐπιθυμία παντὸς ἄλλου ῥύμματος τὴν δὴ τοιαύτην δύναμιν καὶ σωτηρίαν διὰ
greek_tokens: kai konios luph te kai fobos kai epiqumia pas allos rumma o dh toioutos dunamis kai swthria dia pas doca orqos te kai nomimos deinazw te peri kai mh andreia egw kalew kai tiqhmi ei mh tis su allos legw alla oudeis eimi de os legos dokew gar egw o orqos doca peri o autos outos aneu paideia gignomai o te qhriwdhs kai andrapodwdhs oute panu nomimos hgeomai allos su tis h andreia kalew
english_head: to accomplish this, and pain and fear and desire more sure than any lye. This power in the soul, then, this unfailing conservation of right and lawful belief about things to be and not to be feared is what I call and would assume to be courage, unless you have something different to say. No, nothing, said he; for I presume that you consider mere right opinion about the same matters not produced by education, that which may manifest itself in a beast or a slave, to have little or nothing to do with law and that you would call it by another name than courage.

greek_head: Ἀληθέστατα ἦν δ' ἐγώ λέγεις Ἀποδέχομαι τοίνυν τοῦτο ἀνδρείαν εἶναι
greek_tokens: alhqhs eimi de egw legw apodexomai toinun outos andreia eimi kai gar apodexomai eimi de egw politikos ge kai orqos apodecis auqis de peri autos ean boulomai eti kallion dieimi nun gar ou outos zhtew alla dikaiosunh pros oun o ekeinos zhthsis ws oimh ikanos exis alla kalos fhmi legw duo meis eimi de egw eti loipas o deomai kateidon en o
english_head: That is most true, said I. Well then, he said, I accept this as bravery. Do so, said I, and you will be right with the reservation that it is the courage of a citizen. Some other time, if it please you, we will discuss it more fully. At present we were not seeking this but justice; and for the purpose of that inquiry I believe we have done enough. You are quite right, he said.

greek_head: πόλει ἥ τε σωφροσύνη καὶ οὗ δὴ ἕνεκα πάντα ζητοῦμεν δικαιοσύνη
greek_tokens: polis o te swfrosunos kai e dh eneka pas zhtew dikaiosunh panu men oun pws oun an o dikaiosunh euriskw ina mhketi pragmateuomai peri swfrosunos egw men toinun fhmi oute oida oute an boulomai autos proteros fainw eiper mhketi episkeptomai swfrosunos alla ei egw boulomai xarizomai skopaw proteros outos ekeinos
english_head: Two things still remain, said I, to make out in our city, soberness and the object of the whole inquiry, justice. Quite so. If there were only some way to discover justice so that we need not further concern ourselves about soberness. Well, I, for my part, he said, neither know of any such way nor would I wish justice to be discovered first if that means that we are not to go on to the consideration of soberness. But if you desire to please me, consider this before that. It would certainly

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μέντοι ἦν δ' ἐγώ βούλομαί γε εἰ μὴ ἀδικῶ Σκόπει δή ἔφη
greek_tokens: alla mentoi eimi de egw boulomai ge ei mh adikew skopaw dh fhmi skepteos eipon kai os ge enteuqen eidon sumfwnia tis kai armonia proseoika mallon h o proteros pws kosmos pou tis eimi de egw o swfrosunos eimi kai hdona tis kai epiqumia egkrateia os fhmi kreisswn dh eautou apofainw ou oida ostis tropos kai allos atta toioutos wsper ixnos autos legw eimi gar pas malista fhmi oukoun o men kreisswn eautou geloios o gar eautou kreisswn kai hsswn dhpou an eautou eaw kai o hsswn kreisswn
english_head: be very wrong of me not to desire it, said I. Go on with the inquiry then, he said. I must go on, I replied, and viewed from here it bears more likeness to a kind of concord and harmony than the other virtues did. How so? Soberness is a kind of beautiful order and a continence of certain pleasures and appetites, as they say, using the phrase master of himself I know not how; and there are other similar expressions that as it were point us to the same trail. Is that not so? Most certainly. Now the phrase master of himself is an absurdity, is it not? For he who is master of himself would also be subject to himself,

greek_head: ὁ αὐτὸς γὰρ ἐν ἅπασιν τούτοις προσαγορεύεται Τί δ' οὔ
greek_tokens: o autos gar en apas outos prosagoreuw tis de ou alla eimi de egw fainw egw boulomai legw outos o logos os tis en autos o anqrwpos peri o yuxh o men beltiwn en o de xeirwn kai otan men o beltiwn fusis o xeirwn egkraths eimi outos legw o kreisswn eautou epainew goun otan de upo trefw kakazw h tis omilia kratew upo plhqos o xeirwn mikros o beltiwn
english_head: and he who is subject to himself would be master. For the same person is spoken of in all these expressions. Of course. But, said I, the intended meaning of this way of speaking appears to me to be that the soul of a man within him has a better part and a worse part, and the expression self-mastery means the control of the worse by the naturally better part. It is, at any rate, a term of praise. But when, because of bad breeding or some association, the better part, which is the smaller, is dominated by the multitude of the worse, I think that our speech

greek_head: ὄν τοῦτο δὲ ὡς ἐν ὀνείδει ψέγειν τε καὶ καλεῖν ἥττω ἑαυτοῦ καὶ ἀκόλαστον τὸν οὕτω διακείμενον
greek_tokens: eimi outos de ws en oneidos yegw te kai kalew hsswn eautou kai akolastos o outws diakeimai kai gar eoika fhmi apoblepw toinun eimi de egw pros o nea egw polis kai eurhsis en autos o eteros outos eneimi kreisswn gar autos eautou dikaios fhmi prosagoreuw eiper e o ameinwn o xeirwn arxw swfrwn klhteos kai kreisswn eautou alla apoblepw fhmi kai alhqhs legw kai meis kai o ge polus kai pantodapos epiqumia kai
english_head: censures this as a reproach, and calls the man in this plight unselfcontrolled and licentious. That seems likely, he said. Turn your eyes now upon our new city, said I, and you will find one of these conditions existent in it. For you will say that it is justly spoken of as master of itself if that in which the superior rules the inferior is to be called sober and self-mastered. I do turn my eyes upon it, he said, and it is as you say. And again, the mob of motley

greek_head: ἡδονάς τε καὶ λύπας ἐν παισὶ μάλιστα ἄν τις εὕροι καὶ γυναιξὶ καὶ οἰκέταις καὶ τῶν ἐλευθέρων λεγομένων ἐν τοῖς
greek_tokens: hdona te kai luph en pais malista an tis euriskw kai gunh kai oikeths kai o eleuqeros legw en o polus te kai faulos panu men oun o de ge aploos te kai metrios o dh meta neomai te kai doca orqos logismos agw en oligos te epiteucis kai o beltistos men fusin beltistos de paideuw alhqhs fhmi oukoun kai outos oraw eneimi su en o polis kai kratew autoqi o epiqumia o en o polus te
english_head: appetites and pleasures and pains one would find chiefly in children and women and slaves and in the base rabble of those who are freemen in name. By all means. But the simple and moderate appetites which with the aid of reason and right opinion are guided by consideration you will find in few and those the best born and best educated. True, he said. And do you not find this too in your city and a domination there of the desires

greek_head: καὶ φαύλοις ὑπό τε τῶν ἐπιθυμιῶν καὶ τῆς φρονήσεως τῆς ἐν τοῖς ἐλάττοσί τε καὶ ἐπιεικεστέροις
greek_tokens: kai faulos upo te o epiqumia kai o fronhsis o en o elasswn te kai epieikhs egw fhmi ei ara deomai tis polis prosagoreuw kreisswn hdona te kai epiqumia kai autos eautou kai outos prosrhteos pantapasi men oun fhmi ara oun ou kai swfrwn kata pas outos kai mala fhmi kai meis eiper au en allh polis o autos doca eneimi o
english_head: in the multitude and the rabble by the desires and the wisdom that dwell in the minority of the better? I do, he said. If, then, there is any city that deserves to be described as master of its pleasures and desires and self-mastered, this one merits that designation. Most assuredly, he said. And is it not also to be called sober in all these respects? Indeed it is, he said. And yet again, if there is any city in which

greek_head: τε ἄρχουσι καὶ ἀρχομένοις περὶ τοῦ οὕστινας δεῖ ἄρχειν καὶ ἐν ταύτῃ ἂν εἴη τοῦτο ἐνόν ἢ οὐ δοκεῖ
greek_tokens: te arxw kai arxw peri o ostis deomai arxw kai en outos an eaw outos eneimi h ou dokew kai mala fhmi sfodra en poteros oun fhmi o poliths o swfronew enihmi otan outws exw en o arxw h en o arxw en amfoteros pou fhmi oraw oun eimi de egw ostis epieikhs manteuomai arti ws armonia tis o swfrosunos omoiow tis dh ostis ou wsper o andreia kai o sofia en meros tis
english_head: the rulers and the ruled are of one mind as to who ought to rule, that condition will be found in this. Don’t you think so? I most emphatically do, he said. In which class of the citizens, then, will you say that the virtue of soberness has its seat when this is their condition? In the rulers or in the ruled? In both, I suppose, he said. Do you see then, said I, that our intuition was not a bad one just now that discerned a likeness between soberness and a kind of harmony? Why so? Because its operation is unlike that of courage and wisdom, which residing in separate parts

greek_head: ἑκατέρα ἐνοῦσα ἡ μὲν σοφήν ἡ δὲ ἀνδρείαν τὴν πόλιν παρείχετο οὐχ οὕτω ποιεῖ αὕτη ἀλλὰ δι' ὅλης ἀτεχνῶς
greek_tokens: ekateros eneimi o men sofos o de andreia o polis parexw ou outws poiew outos alla dia oloc atexnos teinw dia pas parexw sunadw o te asqenhs tautos kai o isxuros kai o mesos ei men boulomai fronhsis ei de boulomai isxus ei de kai plhqw h xrhma h allos ostis o toioutos wste orqos an fhmi outos o omonoia swfrosunos eimi xeirwn te kai ameinwn kata fus sumfwnia opoteros deomai arxw kai en polis kai en eis ekastos
english_head: respectively made the city, the one wise and the other brave. That is not the way of soberness, but it extends literally through the entire gamut throughout, bringing about the unison in the same chant of the strongest, the weakest and the intermediate, whether in wisdom or, if you please, in strength, or for that matter in numbers, wealth, or any similar criterion. So that we should be quite right in affirming this unanimity to be soberness, the concord of the naturally superior and inferior

greek_head: Πάνυ μοι ἔφη συνδοκεῖ Εἶεν ἦν δ' ἐγώ τὰ μὲν τρία ἡμῖν ἐν τῇ πόλει κατῶπται
greek_tokens: panu egw fhmi sundokew eien eimi de egw o men treis egw en o polis kaqoraw os ge outwsi doca o de dh loipos eidos dia o an eti aretaw metexw polis tis pote an eaw dhlos gar ostis outos eimi o dikaiosunh dhlos oukoun eimi glauc nun dh egw deomai wsper kunhgeths tis qamnos kuklos periisthmi prosexw o new mh ph diafeugw o dikaiosunh kai afanizw adhlos gignomai
english_head: as to which ought to rule both in the state and the individual. I entirely concur, he said. Very well, said I. We have made out these three forms in our city to the best of our present judgement. What can be the remaining form that would give the city still another virtue? For it is obvious that the remainder is justice. Obvious. Now then, Glaucon, is the time for us like huntsmen to surround the covert and keep close watch that justice may not slip through and get away from us and vanish

greek_head: φανερὸν γὰρ δὴ ὅτι ταύτῃ πῃ ἔστιν ὅρα οὖν καὶ προθυμοῦ κατιδεῖν ἐάν πως πρότερος ἐμοῦ ἴδῃς καὶ ἐμοὶ φράσῃς
greek_tokens: faneros gar dh ostis outos ph eimi oraw oun kai proqumeomai kateidon ean pws proteros egw eidon kai egw frazw ei gar ofeilw fhmi alla mallon ean egw epomai xraomai kai o deiknumi dunamai kaqoraw panu egw metrios xraw epomai eimi de egw euxomai meta egw poiew outos alla monos eimi de os ageomai kai meis eipon egw dusbatos ge tis o topos fainw kai episkios eimi goun skoteinos kai dusdiereunhtos alla gar omws iteon
english_head: from our sight. It plainly must be somewhere hereabouts. Keep your eyes open then and do your best to descry it. You may see it before I do and point it out to me. Would that I could, he said; but I think rather that if you find in me one who can follow you and discern what you point out to him you will be making a very fair use of me. Pray for success then, said I, and follow along with me. That I will do, only lead on, he said. And truly, said I, it appears to be an inaccessible place, lying in deep shadows. It certainly is a dark covert,

greek_head: Ἰτέον γάρ ἔφη Καὶ ἐγὼ κατιδών Ἰοὺ ἰού εἶπον ὦ Γλαύκων κινδυνεύομέν
greek_tokens: iteon gar fhmi kai egw kateidon iou iou eipon eimi glauc kinduneuw tis exw ixnos kai egw dokew ou panu tis ekfeugw egw eu aggellw eimi de os eimi meis eimi de egw blakikos ge egw o paqos o poios pala eimi makarios fainw pro podow egw ek arxh kulindew kai ou oraw ara autos alla eimi katagelastos wsper e en o xeir exw zhtew
english_head: not easy to beat up. But all the same on we must go. Yes, on. And I caught view and gave a hulloa and said, Glaucon, I think we have found its trail and I don’t believe it will get away from us. I am glad to hear that, said he. Truly, said I, we were slackers indeed. How so? Why, all the time, bless your heart, the thing apparently was tumbling about our feet from the start and yet we couldn’t see it, but were most ludicrous, like

greek_head: ἐνίοτε ὃ ἔχουσιν καὶ ἡμεῖς εἰς αὐτὸ μὲν οὐκ ἀπεβλέπομεν πόρρω δέ ποι ἀπεσκοποῦμεν ᾗ δὴ καὶ ἐλάνθανεν ἴσως
greek_tokens: eniote o exw kai egw eis autos men ou apoblepw prosw de poi aposkopew h dh kai lanqanw isos egw pws fhmi legw outws eipon ws dokew egw kai legw autos kai akouw pala ou manqanw egw autos ostis legw tropos tis autos makros fhmi o prooimion o epiqumew akew
english_head: people who sometimes hunt for what they hold in their hands. So we did not turn our eyes upon it, but looked off into the distance, which perhaps was the reason it escaped us. What do you mean? he said. This, I replied, that it seems to me that though we were speaking of it and hearing about it all the time we did not understand ourselves or realize that we were speaking of it in a sense. That is a tedious prologue, he said, for an eager listener.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἄκουε εἴ τι ἄρα λέγω ὃ γὰρ ἐξ ἀρχῆς ἐθέμεθα δεῖν ποιεῖν διὰ παντός ὅτε τὴν πόλιν κατῳκίζομεν
greek_tokens: alla eimi de egw akouw ei tis ara legos o gar ek arxh tiqhmi dew poiew dia pas oste o polis katoikizw outos eimi ws egw dokew htoi outos tis eidos o dikaiosunh tiqhmi de dhpou kai pollakis legw ei mimnhskw ostis enos ekastos eis dew epithdeuw o peri o polis eis o autos o fusis epithdeios fuw eaw legw gar kai meis ostis ge o o eautou prassw kai mh polupragmonew dikaiosunh eimi kai outos allos te polus
english_head: Listen then, said I, and learn if there is anything in what I say. For what we laid down in the beginning as a universal requirement when we were founding our city, this I think, or some form of this, is justice. And what we did lay down, and often said, you recall, was that each one man must perform one social service in the state for which his nature is best adapted. Yes, we said that. And again that to do one’s own business and not to be a busybody is justice,

greek_head: ἀκηκόαμεν καὶ αὐτοὶ πολλάκις εἰρήκαμεν Εἰρήκαμεν γάρ
greek_tokens: akouw kai autos pollakis erw erw gar outos toinun eimi de egw eimi filos kinduneuw tropos tis gignomai o dikaiosunh eimi o o eautou prassw oida oqen tekmairomai ou alla legos fhmi dokew egw eimi de egw o upoloipos en o polis os skeptomai swfrosunos kai andreia kai fronhsis outos eimi o pas ekeinos o dunamis parexw wste eggignomai kai eggignomai ge swthria parexw ewsper an
english_head: is a saying that we have heard from many and have often repeated ourselves. We have. This, then, I said, my friend, if taken in a certain sense appears to be justice, this principle of doing one’s own business. Do you know whence I infer this? No, but tell me, he said. I think that this is the remaining virtue in the state after our consideration of soberness, courage, and intelligence, a quality which made it possible for them all to grow up in the body politic and which when they have sprung up preserves them as long as it is present. And I hardly need to remind you that

greek_head: ἐνῇ καίτοι ἔφαμεν δικαιοσύνην ἔσεσθαι τὸ ὑπολειφθὲν ἐκείνων εἰ τὰ τρία εὕροιμεν
greek_tokens: enihmi kaitoi fhmi dikaiosunh eimi o upoleipw ekeinos ei o treis euriskw kai gar anagkh fhmi alla mentoi eimi de egw ei dew ge krinw tis o polis egw outos malista agaqos apergazomai eggignomai duskritos an eaw poteros o omodocia o arxw te kai arxw h o peri deinazw te kai mh ostis eimi doca ennomos swthria en o stratiwths eggignomai h o en
english_head: we said that justice would be the residue after we had found the other three. That is an unavoidable conclusion, he said. But moreover, said I, if we were required to decide what it is whose indwelling presence will contribute most to making our city good, it would be a difficult decision whether it was the unanimity of rulers and ruled or the conservation in the minds of the soldiers of the convictions produced by law as to what things are or are not to be feared, or the watchful intelligence

greek_head: τοῖς ἄρχουσι φρόνησίς τε καὶ φυλακὴ ἐνοῦσα ἢ τοῦτο μάλιστα ἀγαθὴν αὐτὴν ποιεῖ ἐνὸν καὶ ἐν παιδὶ καὶ ἐν
greek_tokens: o arxw fronhsis te kai fulakh eneimi h outos malista agaqos autos poiew eneimi kai en pais kai en gunh kai doulos kai eleuqeros kai dhmiourgos kai arxw kai arxw ostis o eautou ekastos eis eimi prassw kai ou polupragmonew duskritos fhmi pws de ou enamillos ara ws eoika pros areth polis o te sofia autos kai o swfrosunos kai o andreia o o ekastos en autos o eautou prassw dunamis kai mala fhmi oukoun dikaiosunh o ge outos enamillos an eis areth
english_head: that resides in the guardians, or whether this is the chief cause of its goodness, the principle embodied in child, woman, slave, free, artisan, ruler, and ruled, that each performed his one task as one man and was not a versatile busybody. Hard to decide indeed, he said. A thing, then, that in its contribution to the excellence of a state vies with and rivals its wisdom, its soberness, its bravery, is this principle of everyone in it doing his own task. It is indeed, he said. And is not justice the name you would have to give to the principle that rivals these as conducing to

greek_head: πόλεως θείης Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: polis qeia pantapasi men oun skopaw dh kai ode ei outws docis ara o arxw en o polis o dikh prostacis dikazw tis meis eimi allos ostis mallon efihmi dikazw h outos opws an ekastos mhte exw allotrios mhte o eautou steromai ou alla outos ws dikaios eimi nai kai outos ara ph o o oikew te kai eautou ecis te kai
english_head: the virtue of state? By all means. Consider it in this wise too if so you will be convinced. Will you not assign the conduct of lawsuits in your state to the rulers? Of course. Will not this be the chief aim of their decisions, that no one shall have what belongs to others or be deprived of his own? Nothing else but this. On the assumption that this is just? Yes. From this point of view too, then, the having and doing

greek_head: πρᾶξις δικαιοσύνη ἂν ὁμολογοῖτο Ἔστι ταῦτα
greek_tokens: pracis dikaiosunh an omologew eimi outos ide dh ean sos osper egw sundokew tektwn skutotomos epixeirew ergon ergazomai h skutotomos tektwn h o organon metalambanw allhlwn h timh h kai o autos epixeirew amfoteros prassw pas allos metallassw ara su an tis dokew megas blaptw polis ou panu fhmi alla otan ge oimh dhmiourgos eimi h tis allos xrhmatisths
english_head: of one’s own and what belongs to oneself would admittedly be justice. That is so. Consider now whether you agree with me. A carpenter undertaking to do the work of a cobbler or a cobbler of a carpenter or their interchange of one another’s tools or honors or even the attempt of the same man to do both—the confounding of all other functions would not, think you, greatly injure a state, would it? Not much, he said. But when I fancy one who is by nature an artisan or some kind of money-maker

greek_head: φύσει ἔπειτα ἐπαιρόμενος ἢ πλούτῳ ἢ πλήθει ἢ ἰσχύι ἢ ἄλλῳ τῳ τοιούτῳ εἰς τὸ τοῦ πολεμικοῦ εἶδος ἐπιχειρῇ ἰέναι
greek_tokens: fusis epeita epairw h ploutos h plhqw h isxus h allos tis toioutos eis o o polemikos eidos epixeirew eimi h o polemikos tis eis o o bouleutikos kai fulac anacis eimi kai o allhlwn outos organon metalambanw kai o timh h otan o autos pas outos ama epixeirew prassw tote oimh kai sos dokew outos o outos metabolh kai polupragmosunh oleqros eimi o polis pantapasi men oun o treis ara eimi genos polupragmosunh kai metabolh
english_head: tempted and incited by wealth or command of votes or bodily strength or some similar advantage tries to enter into the class of the soldiers or one of the soldiers into the class of counsellors and guardians, for which he is not fitted, and these interchange their tools and their honors or when the same man undertakes all these functions at once, then, I take it, you too believe that this kind of substitution and meddlesomeness is the ruin of a state. By all means. The interference with one another’s business, then, of three existent classes and the substitution of the one for the other

greek_head: εἰς ἄλληλα μεγίστη τε βλάβη τῇ πόλει καὶ ὀρθότατ' ἂν προσαγορεύοιτο μάλιστα κακουργία
greek_tokens: eis allhlwn megas te blabh o polis kai orqos an prosagoreuw malista kakourgia komidh men oun kakourgia de o megas o eautou polis ou adikia fhmi eimi pws de ou outos men ara adikia palin de wde legw xrhmatistikos epikourikos fulakikos genos oikeiopragia ekastos outos o eautou prassw en polis enantion ekeinos dikaiosunh su an eaw kai o polis dikaios parexw
english_head: is the greatest injury to a state and would most rightly be designated as the thing which chiefly works it harm. Precisely so. And the thing that works the greatest harm to one’s own state, will you not pronounce to be injustice? Of course. This, then, is injustice. Again, let us put it in this way. The proper functioning of the money-making class, the helpers and the guardians, each doing its own work in the state, being the reverse of that just described, would be justice and would render the city just.

greek_head: Οὐκ ἄλλῃ ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ ἦ δ' ὅς ἔχειν ἢ ταύτῃ Μηδέν ἦν δ' ἐγώ πω πάνυ παγίως αὐτὸ λέγωμεν ἀλλ'
greek_tokens: ou allh egw dokew eimi de os exw h outos mhdeis eimi de egw pw panu pagios autos legw alla ean men egw kai eis enos ekastos o anqrwpos eimi o eidos outos omologew kai ekei dikaiosunh eimi sugxwrew hdh tis gar kai erew ei de mh tote allos tis skeptomai nun de ektelew o skeyis os oiomai ei en megas tis o exw dikaiosunh proteros h ekei epixeirew qeaomai raios an en eis anqrwpos kateidon oios eimi kai
english_head: I think the case is thus and no otherwise, said he. Let us not yet affirm it quite fixedly, I said, but if this form when applied to the individual man, accepted there also as a definition of justice, we will then concede the point—for what else will there be to say? But if not, then we will look for something else. But now let us work out the inquiry in which we supposed that, if we found some larger thing that contained justice and viewed it there, we should more easily discover its nature in the individual man.

greek_head: ἔδοξε δὴ ἡμῖν τοῦτο εἶναι πόλις καὶ οὕτω ᾠκίζομεν ὡς ἐδυνάμεθα ἀρίστην εὖ εἰδότες ὅτι ἔν γε τῇ ἀγαθῇ ἂν εἴη
greek_tokens: dokew dh egw outos eimi polis kai outws oikizw ws dunamai aristos eu oida ostis en ge o agaw an eaw o oun egw ekei fainw epanaferw eis o enos kan men omologew kalos ecis ean de tis allos en o eis emfainw palin epaneimi epi o polis basanizw kai
english_head: And we agreed that this larger thing is the city, and so we constructed the best city in our power, well knowing that in the good city it would of course be found. What, then, we thought we saw there we must refer back to the individual and, if it is confirmed, all will be well. But if something different manifests itself in the individual, we will return again

greek_head: τάχ' ἂν παρ' ἄλληλα σκοποῦντες καὶ τρίβοντες ὥσπερ ἐκ πυρείων ἐκλάμψαι ποιήσαιμεν τὴν δικαιοσύνην καὶ φανερὰν
greek_tokens: taxa an para allhlwn skopaw kai tribw wsper ek pureion eklampw poiew o dikaiosunh kai faneros gignomai bebaiow autos para egw autos alla fhmi kaqa odos te legw kai poiew xrh outws ara oun eimi de egw o ge tautos an tis proseipon megas te kai elasswn anomoios tugxanw eimi outos h tautos prosagoreuw h omoios omoios fhmi
english_head: to the state and test it there and it may be that, by examining them side by side and rubbing them against one another, as it were from the fire-sticks we may cause the spark of justice to flash forth, and when it is thus revealed confirm it in our own minds. Well, he said, that seems a sound method and that is what we must do. Then, said I, if you call a thing by the same name whether it is big or little, is it unlike in the way in which it is called the same or like? Like, he said. Then a just man too

greek_head: Καὶ δίκαιος ἄρα ἀνὴρ δικαίας πόλεως κατ' αὐτὸ τὸ τῆς δικαιοσύνης εἶδος οὐδὲν διοίσει ἀλλ' ὅμοιος ἔσται
greek_tokens: kai dikaios ara anhr dikaios polis kata autos o o dikaiosunh eidos oudeis diaferw alla omoios eimi omoios fhmi alla mentoi polis ge dokew eimi dikaios oste en autos trissos genos fusis eneimi o eautou ekastos prassw swfrwn de au kai andreia kai sofos dia o autos outos genos allos atta paqh te kai ecis alhqhs fhmi kai o enos ara eimi filos outws aciow o autos outos
english_head: will not differ at all from a just city in respect of the very form of justice, but will be like it. Yes, like. But now the city was thought to be just because three natural kinds existing in it performed each its own function, and again it was sober, brave, and wise because of certain other affections and habits of these three kinds. True, he said. Then, my friend, we shall thus expect the individual also to have these same forms

greek_head: εἴδη ἐν τῇ αὑτοῦ ψυχῇ ἔχοντα διὰ τὰ αὐτὰ πάθη ἐκείνοις τῶν αὐτῶν ὀνομάτων ὀρθῶς ἀξιοῦσθαι τῇ πόλει
greek_tokens: eidos en o eautou yuxw exw dia o autos paqh ekeinos o autos onoma orqos aciow o polis pas anagkh fhmi eis faulos ge au eimi de egw eimi qaumasios skemma empitnw peri yuxazw eite exis o treis eidos outos en eautou eite mh ou panu egw dokew fhmi eis faulos isos gar eimi swkraths o legw alhqhs ostis xalepos o kalos fainw eimi de egw kai eu ge eimi eimi glauc ws o
english_head: in his soul, and by reason of identical affections of these with those in the city to receive properly the same appellations. Inevitable, he said. Goodness gracious, said I, here is another trifling inquiry into which we have plunged, the question whether the soul really contains these three forms in itself or not. It does not seem to me at all trifling, he said, for perhaps, Socrates, the saying is true that ’fine things are difficult.’ Apparently, said I;

greek_head: ἐμὴ δόξα ἀκριβῶς μὲν τοῦτο ἐκ τοιούτων μεθόδων οἵαις νῦν ἐν τοῖς λόγοις χρώμεθα οὐ μή ποτε λάβωμεν
greek_tokens: emos doca akribhs men outos ek toioutos meqodos oios nun en o logos xraomai ou mh pote lambanw allos gar makros kai plew odos o epi outos agw isos mentoi o ge proerew te kai proskeptomai acios oukoun agaphtos fhmi egw men gar en ge o pareimi ikanos an exw alla mentoi eipon egw kai panu ecarkew mh toinun apokamnw fhmi alla skopaw
english_head: and let me tell you, Glaucon, that in my opinion we shall never in the world apprehend this matter from such methods as we are now employing in discussion. For there is another longer and harder way that conducts to this. Yet we may perhaps discuss it on the level of previous statements and inquiries. May we acquiesce in that? he said. I for my part should be quite satisfied with that for the present. And I surely should be more than satisfied, I replied. Don’t you weary then, he said, but go on with the inquiry. Is it not, then,

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ἡμῖν ἦν δ' ἐγώ πολλὴ ἀνάγκη ὁμολογεῖν ὅτι γε τὰ αὐτὰ ἐν ἑκάστῳ ἔνεστιν ἡμῶν εἴδη τε καὶ ἤθη ἅπερ
greek_tokens: ara oun egw eimi de egw polus anagkh omologew ostis ge o autos en ekastos eneimi egw eidos te kai hqos o en o polis ou gar pou alloqen ekeise afikneomai geloios gar an eaw ei tis oiomai o qumoeidhs mh ek o idiwths en o polis eggignomai e dh kai exw outos o aitios oios e kata o qrakh te kai skuqikos kai sxedon tis kata o anoos topos h o filomaqhs o dh o para egw
english_head: said I, impossible for us to avoid admitting this much, that the same forms and qualities are to be found in each one of us that are in the state? They could not get there from any other source. It would be absurd to suppose that the element of high spirit was not derived in states from the private citizens who are reputed to have this quality as the populations of the Thracian and Scythian lands and generally of northern regions; or the quality of love of knowledge, which would chiefly be attributed to the region where we dwell,
