greek_head: μάλιστ' ἄν τις αἰτιάσαιτο τόπον ἢ τὸ φιλοχρήματον τὸ περὶ τούς τε Φοίνικας εἶναι καὶ τοὺς κατὰ Αἴγυπτον φαίη τις ἂν
greek_tokens: malista an tis aitiaomai topos h o filoxrhmatos o peri o te foinic eimi kai o kata aiguptos fhmi tis an ou hkistos kai mala fhmi outos men dh outws exis eimi de egw kai oudeis xalepos gignwskw ou dhta ode de hdh xalepos ei o autos outos ekastos prassw h treis eimi allos allos manqanw men eteros qumow de allos o en egw epiqumew de au tritos tis o peri o trofh te kai gennhsis hdona kai osos outos
english_head: or the love of money which we might say is not least likely to be found in Phoenicians and the population of Egypt. One certainly might, he replied. This is the fact then, said I, and there is no difficulty in recognizing it. Certainly not. But the matter begins to be difficult when you ask whether we do all these things with the same thing or whether there are three things and we do one thing with one and one with another—learn with one part of ourselves, feel anger with another, and with yet a third desire the pleasures of nutrition

greek_head: ἀδελφά ἢ ὅλῃ τῇ ψυχῇ καθ' ἕκαστον αὐτῶν πράττομεν ὅταν ὁρμήσωμεν ταῦτ' ἔσται τὰ χαλεπὰ διορίσασθαι ἀξίως
greek_tokens: adelfh h oloc o yuxw kaqa ekastos autos prassw otan ormaw outos eimi o xalepos diorizw acios logos kai egw dokew fhmi wde toinun epixeirew autos orizw eite o autos allhlwn eite eteros eimi pws dhlos ostis tautos enantios poiew h pasxw kata tautos ge kai pros tautos ou eqelw ama wste an pou euriskw en autos outos gignomai eimi ostis ou tautos
english_head: and generation and their kind, or whether it is with the entire soul that we function in each case when we once begin. That is what is really hard to determine properly. I think so too, he said. Let us then attempt to define the boundary and decide whether they are identical with one another in this way. How? It is obvious that the same thing will never do or suffer opposites in the same respect in relation to the same thing and at the same time. So that if ever we find these contradictions in the functions of the mind

greek_head: ἦν ἀλλὰ πλείω Εἶεν
greek_tokens: eimi alla plew eien skopaw dh o legos legos fhmi isthmi eipon kai kinew o autos ama kata o autos ara dunatos oudamos eti toinun akribhs omologew mh ph proeimi amfisbhtew ei gar tis legos anqrwpos isthmi kinew de o xeir te kai o kefalh ostis o autos isthmi te kai kinew ama ou an oimh aciow outws
english_head: we shall know that it was not the same thing functioning but a plurality. Very well. Consider, then, what I am saying. Say on, he replied. Is it possible for the same thing at the same time in the same respect to be at rest and in motion? By no means. Let us have our understanding still more precise, lest as we proceed we become involved in dispute. If anyone should say of a man standing still but moving his hands and head that the same man is at the same time at rest and in motion we should not, I take it, regard that as the right way of expressing it, but rather that a part of him is at rest

greek_head: λέγειν δεῖν ἀλλ' ὅτι τὸ μέν τι αὐτοῦ ἕστηκε τὸ δὲ κινεῖται οὐχ οὕτω
greek_tokens: legw dew alla ostis o men tis autos isthmi o de kinew ou outws outws oukoun kai ei eti mallon xarientizomai o outos legos komyeuw ws e ge strobilos oloc isthmi te ama kai kinew otan en o autos phgnumi o kentron periferw h kai allos tis kuklos perieimi en o autos edra outos draw ou an apodexomai ws ou kata tautos eautou
english_head: and a part in motion. Is not that so? It is. Then if the disputant should carry the jest still further with the subtlety that tops at any rate stand still as a whole at the same time that they are in motion when with the peg fixed in one point they revolve, and that the same is true of any other case of circular motion about the same spot—we should reject the statement on the ground that the repose and the movement in such cases were not in relation to the same parts of the objects, but we would say

greek_head: τὰ τοιαῦτα τότε μενόντων τε καὶ φερομένων ἀλλὰ φαῖμεν ἂν ἔχειν αὐτὰ εὐθύ τε καὶ περιφερὲς ἐν αὑτοῖς καὶ κατὰ μὲν
greek_tokens: o toioutos tote menw te kai ferw alla fhmi an exw autos euqus te kai periferhs en eautou kai kata men o euqus isthmi oudamh gar apoklinw kata de o periferhs kuklos kinew kai otan de o euquwria h eis decia h eis aristeros h eis o prosqen h eis o opisqen egklinw ama periferw tote oudamh eimi isthmi kai orqos ge fhmi oudeis ara egw o toioutos legw ekplhcis oude mallon tis pasxw os pote tis an o autos eimi ama kata
english_head: that there was a straight line and a circumference in them and that in respect of the straight line they are standing still since they do not incline to either side, but in respect of the circumference they move in a circle; but that when as they revolve they incline the perpendicular to right or left or forward or back, then they are in no wise at rest. And that would be right, he said. No such remarks then will disconcert us or any whit the more make us believe that it is ever possible for the same thing at the same time in the same respect and the same relation

greek_head: τὸ αὐτὸ πρὸς τὸ αὐτὸ τἀναντία πάθοι ἢ καὶ εἴη ἢ καὶ ποιήσειεν
greek_tokens: o autos pros o autos enantios pasxw h kai eaw h kai poiew oukoun egw ge fhmi alla omws eimi de egw ina mh anagkazw passw o toioutos amfisbhthsis epeceimi kai bebaiow ws ou alhqhs eimi mhkunw upotiqhmi ws outos outws exw eis o prosqen proeimi omologew ean pote allh fainw outos h outos pas egw o apo outos sumbainw luw eimi alla xrh fhmi outos poiew
english_head: to suffer, be, or do opposites. They will not me, I am sure, said he. All the same, said I, that we may not be forced to examine at tedious length the entire list of such contentions and convince ourselves that they are false, let us proceed on the hypothesis that this is so, with the understanding that, if it ever appear otherwise, everything that results from the assumption shall be invalidated. That is what we must do, he said.

greek_head: Ἆρ' ἂν οὖν ἦν δ' ἐγώ τὸ ἐπινεύειν τῷ ἀνανεύειν καὶ τὸ ἐφίεσθαί τινος λαβεῖν τῷ ἀπαρνεῖσθαι καὶ τὸ προσάγεσθαι
greek_tokens: ara an oun eimi de egw o epineuw o ananeuw kai o efihmi tis lambanw o aparneomai kai o prosagw o apwqew pas o toioutos o enantios allhlwn qeia eite poihma eite paqhma oudeis gar outos diaferw alla eimi de os o enantios tis oun eimi de egw diyaw kai peinaw kai oloc o epiqumia kai au o eqelw kai o boulomai ou pas outos
english_head: Will you not then, said I, set down as opposed to one another assent and dissent, and the endeavor after a thing to the rejection of it, and embracing to repelling—do not these and all things like these belong to the class of opposite actions or passions; it will make no difference which? None, said he, but they are opposites. What then, said I, of thirst and hunger and the appetites generally, and again consenting and willing, would you not put them all somewhere in the classes

greek_head: εἰς ἐκεῖνά ποι ἂν θείης τὰ εἴδη τὰ νυνδὴ λεχθέντα οἷον ἀεὶ τὴν τοῦ ἐπιθυμοῦντος ψυχὴν οὐχὶ ἤτοι ἐφίεσθαι φήσεις
greek_tokens: eis ekeinos poi an qeia o eidos o nundi legw oios aei o o epiqumew yuxh ou htoi efihmi fhmi ekeinos e an epiqumew h prosagw outos o an boulomai e gignomai h au kaqa osos eqelw tis e porizw epineuw outos pros eautou wsper tis erwtaw eporegw autos o genesis egw tis de o aboulew kai mh eqelw mhde epiqumew ou eis o apwqew kai apelaunw apo autos kai eis apas enantios ekeinos tiqhmi
english_head: just described? Will you not say, for example, that the soul of one who desires either strives for that which he desires or draws towards its embrace what it wishes to accrue to it; or again, in so far as it wills that anything be presented to it, nods assent to itself thereon as if someone put the question, striving towards its attainment? I would say so, he said. But what of not-willing and not consenting nor yet desiring, shall we not put these under the soul’s rejection and repulsion from itself and

greek_head: Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Τούτων δὴ οὕτως ἐχόντων ἐπιθυμιῶν τι φήσομεν εἶναι
greek_tokens: pws gar ou outos dh outws exw epiqumia tis fhmi eimi eidos kai enarghs autos outos os te diya kalew kai os peina fhmi eimi de os oukoun o men potaomai o de edwdh nai ara oun kaqa osos diya eimi pleiwn an tis h e legw epiqumia en o yuxw eaw oios diya eimi diya ara ge qermos potaomai h yuxroomai h polus h oligos h kai eis logos poiew tis pwma h ean men tis qermoths o
english_head: generally into the opposite class from all the former? Of course. This being so, shall we say that the desires constitute a class and that the most conspicuous members of that class are what we call thirst and hunger? We shall, said he. Is not the one desire of drink, the other of food? Yes. Then in so far as it is thirst, would it be of anything more than that of which we say it is a desire in the soul? I mean is thirst thirst for hot drink or cold or much or little or in a word for a draught of any particular quality, or is it the fact that if heat

greek_head: δίψει προσῇ τὴν τοῦ ψυχροῦ ἐπιθυμίαν προσπαρέχοιτ' ἄν ἐὰν δὲ ψυχρότης τὴν τοῦ θερμοῦ ἐὰν δὲ διὰ πλήθους
greek_tokens: diyos proseimi o o yuxroomai epiqumia prosparexw an ean de yuxroths o o qermos ean de dia plhqos parousia polus o diya eimi o o polus parexw ean de oligos o o oligos autos de o diyaw ou mh pote allos gignomai epiqumia h ouper fuw autos pwma kai au o peinaw brwma outws fhmi autos ge o epiqumia ekastos autos monos ekastos e fuw o de toios h toios o prosgignomai
english_head: is attached to the thirst it would further render the desire—a desire of cold, and if cold of hot? But if owing to the presence of muchness the thirst is much it would render it a thirst for much and if little for little. But mere thirst will never be desire of anything else than that of which it is its nature to be, mere drink, and so hunger of food. That is so, he said; each desire in itself is of that thing only of which it is its nature to be. The epithets belong to the quality—such or such.

greek_head: Μήτοι τις ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἀσκέπτους ἡμᾶς ὄντας θορυβήσῃ ὡς οὐδεὶς ποτοῦ ἐπιθυμεῖ ἀλλὰ χρηστοῦ ποτοῦ καὶ οὐ σίτου
greek_tokens: mhtoi tis eimi de egw askeptos egw eimi qorubew ws oudeis potaomai epiqumew alla xrhstos potaomai kai ou sitos alla xrhstos sitos pas gar ara o agaqos epiqumew ei oun o diya epiqumia eimi xrhstos an eaw eite pwma eite allos ostis eimi epiqumia kai o allos outws isos gar an fhmi dokew tis legw o outos legos alla mentoi eimi de egw osos ge eimi toioutos oios eimi
english_head: Let no one then, said I, disconcert us when off our guard with the objection that everybody desires not drink but good drink and not food but good food, because (the argument will run) all men desire good, and so, if thirst is desire, it would be of good drink or of good whatsoever it is; and so similarly of other desires. Why, he said, there perhaps would seem to be something in that objection. But I need hardly remind you, said I,

greek_head: του τὰ μὲν ποιὰ ἄττα ποιοῦ τινός ἐστιν ὡς ἐμοὶ δοκεῖ τὰ δ' αὐτὰ ἕκαστα αὐτοῦ ἑκάστου μόνον
greek_tokens: tis o men poios atta poiew tis eimi ws egw dokew o de autos ekastos autos ekastos monos ou manqanw fhmi ou manqanw fhmi ostis o megas toioutos eimi oios tis eimi megas panu ge oukoun o elasswn nai o de ge polus megas polus elasswn eimi gar nai ara oun kai o pote megas pote elasswn kai o eimi megas eimi elasswn alla tis meis eimi de os
english_head: that of relative terms those that are somehow qualified are related to a qualified correlate, those that are severally just themselves to a correlate that is just itself. I don’t understand, he said. Don’t you understand, said I, that the greater is such as to be greater than something? Certainly. Is it not than the less? Yes. But the much greater than the much less. Is that not so? Yes. And may we add the one time greater than the one time less and that which will be greater than that which will be less? Surely.

greek_head: Καὶ τὰ πλείω δὴ πρὸς τὰ ἐλάττω καὶ τὰ διπλάσια πρὸς τὰ ἡμίσεα καὶ πάντα τὰ τοιαῦτα καὶ αὖ βαρύτερα πρὸς κουφότερα
greek_tokens: kai o plew dh pros o elasswn kai o diplasios pros o hmisus kai pas o toioutos kai au barus pros koufos kai qassw pros o bradus kai eti ge o qermos pros o yuxros kai pas o outos omoios ara ou outws exis panu men oun tis de o peri o episthmh ou o autos tropos episthmh men autos maqhma autos episthmh eimi h ostis dh deomai qeinw o episthmh episthmh de tis kai poios tis
english_head: And similarly of the more towards the fewer, and the double towards the half and of all like cases, and again of the heavier towards the lighter, the swifter towards the slower, and yet again of the hot towards the cold and all cases of that kind, does not the same hold? By all means. But what of the sciences? Is not the way of it the same? Science which is just that, is of knowledge which is just that, or is of whatsoever we must assume the correlate of science to be. But a particular science of a particular kind is of some particular thing of a particular kind.

greek_head: ποιοῦ τινος καὶ τινός λέγω δὲ τὸ τοιόνδε οὐκ ἐπειδὴ οἰκίας ἐργασίας ἐπιστήμη ἐγένετο διήνεγκε τῶν ἄλλων
greek_tokens: poiew tis kai tis legos de o toiosde ou epei oikia ergasia episthmh gignomai diaferw o allos episthmh wste oikodomikos kalew tis meis ara ou o poios tis eimi oios eteros oudeis o allos nai oukoun epei poiew tis kai autos poios tis gignomai kai o allos outws texnh te kai episthmh eimi outws outos toinun eimi de egw fhmi egw tote boulomai legw ei ara nun manqanw ostis osos eimi oios eimi tis autos men monos autos monos eimi o de poa tis poios atta
english_head: I mean something like this: As there was a science of making a house it differed from other sciences so as to be named architecture. Certainly. Was not this by reason of its being of a certain kind such as no other of all the rest? Yes. And was it not because it was of something of a certain kind that it itself became a certain kind of science? And similarly of the other arts and sciences? That is so. This then, said I, if haply you now understand, is what you must say I then meant, by the statement that of all things that are such as to be of something those that are just themselves only are of things just themselves only,

greek_head: καὶ οὔ τι λέγω ὡς οἵων ἂν ᾖ τοιαῦτα καὶ ἔστιν ὡς ἄρα καὶ τῶν ὑγιεινῶν καὶ νοσωδῶν ἡ ἐπιστήμη ὑγιεινὴ καὶ
greek_tokens: kai ou tis legos ws oios an eimi toioutos kai eimi ws ara kai o ugieinos kai noswdhs o episthmh ugieinos kai noswdhs kai o kakh kai o agaqos kakos kai agaqos alla epei ou autos ouper episthmh eimi gignomai episthmh alla poiew tis outos de eimi ugieinos kai noswdhs poios dh tis sumbainw kai autos gignomai kai outos autos poiew mhketi episthmh aplos kalew alla o poiew tis prosgignomai iatrikos manqanw fhmi kai egw dokew outws exw
english_head: but things of a certain kind are of things of a kind. And I don’t at all mean that they are of the same kind as the things of which they are, so that we are to suppose that the science of health and disease is a healthy and diseased science and that of evil and good, evil and good. I only mean that as science became the science not of just the thing of which science is but of some particular kind of thing, namely, of health and disease, the result was that it itself became some kind of science and this caused it to be no longer called simply science but with the addition of the particular kind, medical science. I understand, he said, and agree that it is so. To return to thirst, then, said I,

greek_head: Τὸ δὲ δὴ δίψος ἦν δ' ἐγώ οὐ τούτων θήσεις τῶν τινὸς εἶναι τοῦτο ὅπερ ἐστίν ἔστι δὲ δήπου δίψος
greek_tokens: o de dh diyos eimi de egw ou outos tiqhmi o tis eimi outos osper eimi eimi de dhpou diyos egw eimi de os pwma ge oukoun poiew men tis pwma poios tis kai diyos diyos de oun autos oute polus oute oligos oute agaqos oute kakos oude eis logos poiew tis alla autos pwma monos autos diyos fuw pantapasi men oun o diyaw ara o yuxh kaqa osos diyaw ou allos
english_head: will you not class it with the things that are of something and say that it is what it is in relation to something—and it is, I presume, thirst? I will, said he, —namely of drink. Then if the drink is of a certain kind, so is the thirst, but thirst that is just thirst is neither of much nor little nor good nor bad, nor in a word of any kind, but just thirst is naturally of just drink only. By all means. The soul of the thirsty then, in so far as it thirsts, wishes nothing else than to drink, and

greek_head: τι βούλεται ἢ πιεῖν καὶ τούτου ὀρέγεται καὶ ἐπὶ τοῦτο ὁρμᾷ Δῆλον δή
greek_tokens: tis boulomai h pinw kai outos oregw kai epi outos ormaw dhlos dh oukoun ei pote tis autos anqelkw diyaw eteros an tis en autos eaw autos o diyaw kai agw wsper qhraw epi o pinw ou gar dh fhmi o ge autos o autos eautou peri o autos ama an enantios prassw ou gar oun wsper ge oimh o tocoths ou kalos exis legw ostis autos ama o xeir o tocon apwqew te kai proselkw alla ostis allos men o apwqew xeir eteros de o prosagw
english_head: yearns for this and its impulse is towards this. Obviously. Then if anything draws it back when thirsty it must be something different in it from that which thirsts and drives it like a beast to drink. For it cannot be, we say, that the same thing with the same part of itself at the same time acts in opposite ways about the same thing. We must admit that it does not. So I fancy it is not well said of the archer that his hands at the same time thrust away the bow and draw it nigh, but we should rather say that there is one hand that puts it away and another that draws it to.

greek_head: Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν ἔφη Πότερον δὴ φῶμέν τινας ἔστιν ὅτε διψῶντας οὐκ ἐθέλειν
greek_tokens: pantapasi men oun fhmi poteros dh fhmi tis eimi oste diyaw ou eqelw pinw kai mala ge fhmi polus kai pollakis tis oun fhmi egw fhmi tis an outos peri ou enihmi men en o yuxw autos o keleuw enihmi de o kwluw pinw allos eimi kai kratew o keleuw egw fhmi dokew ara oun ou o men kwluw o toioutos eggignomai otan
english_head: By all means, he said. Are we to say, then, that some men sometimes though thirsty refuse to drink? We are indeed, he said, many and often. What then, said I, should one affirm about them? Is it not that there is something in the soul that bids them drink and a something that forbids, a different something that masters that which bids? I think so. And is it not the fact that that which inhibits such actions arises when it arises from the calculations of reason,

greek_head: ἐγγένηται ἐκ λογισμοῦ τὰ δὲ ἄγοντα καὶ ἕλκοντα διὰ παθημάτων τε καὶ νοσημάτων παραγίγνεται
greek_tokens: eggignomai ek logismos o de agw kai elkw dia paqhma te kai noshma paragignomai fainw ou dh alogos eimi de egw aciow autos dissos te kai eteros allhlwn eimi o men os logizomai logistikos prosagoreuw o yuxazw o de os eraomai te kai peinaw kai diyaw kai peri o allos epiqumia ptoew alogistos te kai epiqumhtikos plhrwsis tis kai hdona etairos
english_head: but the impulses which draw and drag come through affections and diseases? Apparently. Not unreasonably, said I, shall we claim that they are two and different from one another, naming that in the soul whereby it reckons and reasons the rational and that with which it loves, hungers, thirsts, and feels the flutter and titillation of other desires, the irrational and appetitive—companion of various repletions and pleasures. It would not be unreasonable but quite natural,

greek_head: Οὔκ ἀλλ' εἰκότως ἔφη ἡγοίμεθ' ἂν οὕτως Ταῦτα μὲν τοίνυν ἦν δ' ἐγώ δύο ἡμῖν ὡρίσθω εἴδη ἐν
greek_tokens: ou alla eoikotws fhmi hgeomai an outws outos men toinun eimi de egw duo egw orizw eidos en yuxw eneimi o de dh o qumos kai os qumow poteros tritos h outos poteros an eaw omofuhs isos fhmi o eteros o epiqumhtikos alla eimi de egw pote aekwn tis pisteuw outos ws ara leontios o aglaiwnos aneimi ek peiraieus upo o boreios teixos ektos aisqanomai nekros para o dhmios keimai ama men eidon epiqumew ama de au dusxerainw kai apotrepw eautou kai teos men maxomai te kai parakaluptw
english_head: he said, for us to think this. These two forms, then, let us assume to have been marked off as actually existing in the soul. But now the Thumos or principle of high spirit, that with which we feel anger, is it a third, or would it be identical in nature with one of these? Perhaps, he said, with one of these, the appetitive. But, I said, I once heard a story which I believe, that Leontius the son of Aglaion, on his way up from the Peiraeus under the outer side of the northern wall, becoming aware of dead bodies that lay at the place of public execution at the same time felt a desire to see them and a repugnance and aversion, and that for a time

greek_head: κρατούμενος δ' οὖν ὑπὸ τῆς ἐπιθυμίας διελκύσας τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς προσδραμὼν πρὸς τοὺς νεκρούς Ἰδοὺ ὑμῖν
greek_tokens: kratew de oun upo o epiqumia dielkw o ofqalmos prostrexw pros o nekros idou su fhmi eimi kakodaimwn empiplhmi o kalew qeama akouw fhmi kai autos outos mentoi fhmi o logos shmainw o orgh polemew eniote o epiqumia ws allos eimi allos shmainw gar fhmi oukoun kai alloqi fhmi pollaxou aisqanomai otan
english_head: he resisted and veiled his head, but overpowered in despite of all by his desire, with wide staring eyes he rushed up to the corpses and cried, There, ye wretches, take your fill of the fine spectacle! I too, he said, have heard the story. Yet, surely, this anecdote, I said, signifies that the principle of anger sometimes fights against desires as an alien thing against an alien. Yes, it does, he said. And do we not, said I, on many other occasions observe when his desires constrain a man contrary to his reason

greek_head: βιάζωνταί τινα παρὰ τὸν λογισμὸν ἐπιθυμίαι λοιδοροῦντά τε αὑτὸν καὶ θυμούμενον τῷ βιαζομένῳ ἐν αὑτῷ καὶ ὥσπερ
greek_tokens: biazw tis para o logismos epiqumia loidorew te eautou kai qumow o biazw en eautou kai wsper duo stasiazw summaxos o logos gignomai o qumos o toioutos o de epiqumia autos koinwnew airew logos mh dew antiprassw oimh su ou an fainw gignomai pote en sautou o toioutos aisqanomai oimh de oude en allos ou ma o dios fhmi
english_head: that he reviles himself and is angry with that within which masters him and that as it were in a faction of two parties the high spirit of such a man becomes the ally of his reason? But its making common cause with the desires against the reason when reason whispers low Thou must not—that, I think, is a kind of thing you would not affirm ever to have perceived in yourself, nor, I fancy, in anybody else either.

greek_head: Τί δέ ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὅταν τις οἴηται ἀδικεῖν οὐχ ὅσῳ ἂν γενναιότερος ᾖ τοσούτῳ ἧττον δύναται ὀργίζεσθαι καὶ
greek_tokens: tis de eimi de egw otan tis oiomai adikew ou osos an gennaios eimi tosoutos hsswn dunamai orgizw kai peina kai rigos kai allos otioun o toioutos pasxw upo ekeinos os an oiomai dikaios outos draw kai o legos ou eqelw pros outos autos egeirw o qumos alhqhs fhmi tis de otan adikew tis hgeomai ou en outos zew te kai xalepainw kai summaxomai o dokew dikaios kai dia o peinaw kai dia o rigew kai pas o toioutos pasxw
english_head: No, by heaven, he said. Again, when a man thinks himself to be in the wrong, is it not true that the nobler he is the less is he capable of anger though suffering hunger and cold and whatsoever else at the hands of him whom he believes to be acting justly therein, and as I say his spirit refuses to be aroused against such a one? True, he said. But what when a man believes himself to be wronged, does not his spirit in that case seethe and grow fierce (and also because of his suffering hunger,

greek_head: ὑπομένων καὶ νικᾷ καὶ οὐ λήγει τῶν γενναίων πρὶν ἂν ἢ διαπράξηται ἢ τελευτήσῃ ἢ ὥσπερ κύων ὑπὸ νομέως ὑπὸ
greek_tokens: upomenw kai nikaw kai ou lhgw o gennaios prin an h diaprassw h teleutaw h wsper kuw upo nomeus upo o logos o para eautou anakalew praunw panu men oun fhmi eoika outos os legw kaitoi ge en o hmeteros polis o epikouros wsper kuwn tiqhmi uphkoos o arxw wsper poimhn polis kalos gar eimi de egw noew o boulomai legw alla eimi pros outos kai ode enqumeomai
english_head: cold and the like) and make itself the ally of what he judges just, and in noble souls it endures and wins the victory and will not let go until either it achieves its purpose, or death ends all, or, as a dog is called back by a shepherd, it is called back by the reason within and calmed. Your similitude is perfect, he said, and it confirms our former statements that the helpers are as it were dogs subject to the rulers who are as it were the shepherds of the city. You apprehend my meaning excellently, said I. But do you also

greek_head: Τὸ ποῖον Ὅτι τοὐναντίον ἢ ἀρτίως ἡμῖν φαίνεται περὶ τοῦ
greek_tokens: o poios ostis enantion h artios egw fainw peri o qumoeidhs tote men gar epiqumhtikos tis autos oiomai eimi nun de polus dew fhmi alla polus mallon autos en o o yuxazw isthmi tiqhmi o oplon pros o logistikos pantapasi fhmi ara oun eteros eimi kai outos h logistikos tis eidos wste mh treis alla duo eidos eimi en yuxw logistikos kai epiqumhtikos h kaqa en o polis sunoxwka autos treis
english_head: take note of this? Of what? That what we now think about the spirited element is just the opposite of our recent surmise. For then we supposed it to be a part of the appetitive, but now, far from that, we say that, in the factions of the soul, it much rather marshals itself on the side of the reason. By all means, he said. Is it then distinct from this too, or is it a form of the rational, so that there are not three but two kinds in the soul, the rational and the appetitive, or just as in the city there were

greek_head: ὄντα γένη χρηματιστικόν ἐπικουρητικόν βουλευτικόν οὕτως καὶ ἐν ψυχῇ τρίτον τοῦτό ἐστι τὸ θυμοειδές ἐπίκουρον ὂν

english_head: three existing kinds that composed its structure, the moneymakers, the helpers, the counsellors, so also in the soul there exists a third kind, this principle of high spirit, which is the helper of reason by nature unless it is corrupted by evil nurture? We have to assume it as a third, he said. Yes, said I, provided it shall have been shown to be something different from the rational, as it has been shown to be other than the appetitive. That is not hard to be shown, he said; for that much one can see in children, that they are from their very birth chock-full of rage and high spirit, but as for reason,

english_head: some of them, to my thinking, never participate in it, and the majority quite late. Yes, by heaven, excellently said, I replied; and further, one could see in animals that what you say is true. And to these instances we may add the testimony of Homer quoted above: He smote his breast and chided thus his heart. Hom. Od. 20.17 For there Homer has clearly represented that in us

english_head: which has reflected about the better and the worse as rebuking that which feels unreasoning anger as if it were a distinct and different thing. You are entirely right, he said. Through these waters, then, said I, we have with difficulty made our way and we are fairly agreed that the same kinds equal in number are to be found in the state and in the soul of each one of us. That is so. Then does not the necessity of our former postulate immediately follow, that as and whereby the state was wise so and thereby is the individual wise? Surely. And so whereby and as

english_head: the individual is brave, thereby and so is the state brave, and that both should have all the other constituents of virtue in the same way? Necessarily. Just too, then, Glaucon, I presume we shall say a man is in the same way in which a city was just. That too is quite inevitable. But we surely cannot have forgotten this, that the state was just by reason of each of the three classes found in it fulfilling its own function. I don’t think we have forgotten, he said. We must remember, then, that each of us also in whom the several parts within him

greek_head: τὰ αὑτοῦ ἕκαστον τῶν ἐν αὐτῷ πράττῃ οὗτος δίκαιός τε ἔσται καὶ τὰ αὑτοῦ πράττων

english_head: perform each their own task—he will be a just man and one who minds his own affair. We must indeed remember, he said. Does it not belong to the rational part to rule, being wise and exercising forethought in behalf of the entire soul, and to the principle of high spirit to be subject to this and its ally? Assuredly. Then is it not, as we said, the blending of music and gymnastics that will render them concordant, intensifying

english_head: and fostering the one with fair words and teachings and relaxing and soothing and making gentle the other by harmony and rhythm? Quite so, said he. And these two thus reared and having learned and been educated to do their own work in the true sense of the phrase, will preside over the appetitive part which is the mass of the soul in each of us and the most insatiate by nature of wealth. They will keep watch upon it, lest, by being filled and infected with the so-called pleasures associated with the body and so waxing big and strong, it may not keep to its own work

english_head: but may undertake to enslave and rule over the classes which it is not fitting that it should, and so overturn the entire life of all. By all means, he said. Would not these two, then, best keep guard against enemies from without also in behalf of the entire soul and body, the one taking counsel, the other giving battle, attending upon the ruler, and by its courage executing the ruler’s designs? That is so. Brave, too, then, I take it, we call

english_head: each individual by virtue of this part in him, when, namely, his high spirit preserves in the midst of pains and pleasures the rule handed down by the reason as to what is or is not to be feared. Right, he said. But wise by that small part that ruled in him and handed down these commands, by its possession in turn within it of the knowledge of what is beneficial for each and for the whole, the community composed of the three. By all means. And again, was he not sober

english_head: by reason of the friendship and concord of these same parts, when, namely, the ruling principle and its two subjects are at one in the belief that the reason ought to rule, and do not raise faction against it? The virtue of soberness certainly, said he, is nothing else than this, whether in a city or an individual. But surely, now, a man is just by that which and in the way we have so often described. That is altogether necessary. Well then, said I, has our idea of justice in any way lost the edge of its contour so as to look like anything else than precisely what it showed itself to be in the state? I think not, he said.

english_head: We might, I said, completely confirm your reply and our own conviction thus, if anything in our minds still disputes our definition—by applying commonplace and vulgar tests to it. What are these? For example, if an answer were demanded to the question concerning that city and the man whose birth and breeding was in harmony with it, whether we believe that such a man, entrusted with a deposit of gold or silver, would withhold it and embezzle it, who do you suppose would think that he would be more likely so to act

english_head: than men of a different kind? No one would, he said. And would not he be far removed from sacrilege and theft and betrayal of comrades in private life or of the state in public? He would. And, moreover, he would not be in any way faithless either in the keeping of his oaths or in other agreements. How could he? Adultery, surely, and neglect of parents and of the due service of the gods would pertain to anyone rather than to such a man. To anyone indeed,

english_head: he said. And is not the cause of this to be found in the fact that each of the principles within him does its own work in the matter of ruling and being ruled? Yes, that and nothing else. Do you still, then, look for justice to be anything else than this potency which provides men and cities of this sort? No, by heaven, he said, I do not. Finished, then, is our dream and perfected —the surmise we spoke of, that, by some Providence, at the very beginning of our foundation of the state,

english_head: we chanced to hit upon the original principle and a sort of type of justice. Most assuredly. It really was, it seems, Glaucon, which is why it helps, a sort of adumbration of justice, this principle that it is right for the cobbler by nature to cobble and occupy himself with nothing else, and the carpenter to practice carpentry, and similarly all others. But the truth of the matter was, as it seems,

english_head: that justice is indeed something of this kind, yet not in regard to the doing of one’s own business externally, but with regard to that which is within and in the true sense concerns one’s self, and the things of one’s self—it means that a man must not suffer the principles in his soul to do each the work of some other and interfere and meddle with one another, but that he should dispose well of what in the true sense of the word is properly his own, and having first attained to self-mastery and beautiful order within himself, and having harmonized these three principles, the notes or intervals of three terms quite literally the lowest, the highest, and the mean,

english_head: and all others there may be between them, and having linked and bound all three together and made of himself a unit, one man instead of many, self-controlled and in unison, he should then and then only turn to practice if he find aught to do either in the getting of wealth or the tendance of the body or it may be in political action or private business, in all such doings believing and naming the just and honorable action to be that which preserves and helps to produce this condition of soul, and wisdom the science

english_head: that presides over such conduct; and believing and naming the unjust action to be that which ever tends to overthrow this spiritual constitution, and brutish ignorance, to be the opinion that in turn presides over this. What you say is entirely true, Socrates. Well, said I, if we should affirm that we had found the just man and state and what justice really is in them, I think we should not be much mistaken. No indeed, we should not, he said. Shall we affirm it, then? Let us so affirm. So be it, then, said I; next after this, I take it, we must consider injustice. Obviously.

english_head: Must not this be a kind of civil war of these three principles, their meddlesomeness and interference with one another’s functions, and the revolt of one part against the whole of the soul that it may hold therein a rule which does not belong to it, since its nature is such that it befits it to serve as a slave to the ruling principle? Something of this sort, I fancy, is what we shall say, and that the confusion of these principles and their straying from their proper course is injustice and licentiousness and cowardice and brutish ignorance and, in general, all turpitude. Precisely this,

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἦν δ' ἐγώ καὶ τὸ ἄδικα πράττειν καὶ τὸ ἀδικεῖν καὶ αὖ τὸ δίκαια ποιεῖν ταῦτα πάντα τυγχάνει ὄντα κατάδηλα

english_head: he replied. Then, said I, to act unjustly and be unjust and in turn to act justly the meaning of all these terms becomes at once plain and clear, since injustice and justice are so. How so? Because, said I, these are in the soul what the healthful and the diseaseful are in the body; there is no difference. In what respect? he said. Healthful things surely engender health and diseaseful disease. Yes. Then does not doing just acts engender justice

greek_head: τὸ δ' ἄδικα ἀδικίαν Ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: o de adikos adikia anagkh eimi de o men ugieia poiew o en o swma kata fus kaqisthmi kratew te kai kratew upo allhlwn o de nosos para fus arxw te kai arxw allos upo allos eimi gar oukoun au fhmi o dikaiosunh empoiew o en o yuxw kata fus kaqisthmi kratew te kai kratew upo allhlwn o de adikia para fus arxw te kai arxw allos upo allos komidh fhmi areth men ara ws eoika ugieia su tis an eaw kai
english_head: and unjust injustice? Of necessity. But to produce health is to establish the elements in a body in the natural relation of dominating and being dominated by one another, while to cause disease is to bring it about that one rules or is ruled by the other contrary to nature. Yes, that is so. And is it not likewise the production of justice in the soul to establish its principles in the natural relation of controlling and being controlled by one another, while injustice is to cause the one to rule or be ruled by the other contrary to nature? Exactly so, he said. Virtue, then, as it seems, would be a kind of health

greek_head: κάλλος καὶ εὐεξία ψυχῆς κακία δὲ νόσος τε καὶ αἶσχος καὶ ἀσθένεια
greek_tokens: kallos kai euecia yuxazw kakia de nosos te kai aisxos kai asqeneia eimi outws ara oun ou kai o men kalos epithdeuma eis aretaw ktaomai ferw o de aisxros eis kakia anagkh o dh loipos hdh ws eoika egw eimi skeptomai
english_head: and beauty and good condition of the soul, and vice would be disease, ugliness, and weakness. It is so. Then is it not also true that beautiful and honorable pursuits tend to the winning of virtue and the ugly to vice? Of necessity. And now at last, it seems, it remains for us to consider whether it is profitable to do justice

greek_head: πότερον αὖ λυσιτελεῖ δίκαιά τε πράττειν καὶ καλὰ ἐπιτηδεύειν καὶ εἶναι δίκαιον ἐάντε λανθάνῃ ἐάντε μὴ τοιοῦτος

english_head: and practice honorable pursuits and be just, whether one is known to be such or not, or whether injustice profits, and to be unjust, if only a man escape punishment and is not bettered by chastisement. Nay, Socrates, he said, I think that from this point on our inquiry becomes an absurdity—if, while life is admittedly intolerable with a ruined constitution of body even though accompanied by all the food and drink and wealth and power in the world, we are yet to be asked to suppose that, when the very nature and constitution of that whereby we live is disordered

greek_head: διαφθειρομένης βιωτὸν ἄρα ἔσται ἐάνπερ τις ποιῇ ὃ ἂν βουληθῇ ἄλλο πλὴν τοῦτο ὁπόθεν κακίας μὲν καὶ ἀδικίας
greek_tokens: diafqeirw biwtos ara eimi ean tis poiew o an boulomai allos plhn outos opoqen kakia men kai adikia apallassw dikaiosunh de kai areth ktaomai epei fainw ge eimi ekateros oios egw dierxomai geloios gar eimi de egw alla omws epei entauqa erxomai osos oios te safhs kateidon ostis outos outws exis ou xrh apokamnw hkistos nh o dios fhmi pas apokmhteon
english_head: and corrupted, life is going to be worth living, if a man can only do as he pleases, and pleases to do anything save that which will rid him of evil and injustice and make him possessed of justice and virtue—now that the two have been shown to be as we have described them. Yes, it is absurd, said I; but nevertheless, now that we have won to this height, we must not grow weary in endeavoring to discover with the utmost possible clearness that these things are so. That is the last thing in the world we must do, he said.

greek_head: Δεῦρό νυν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἵνα καὶ ἴδῃς ὅσα καὶ εἴδη ἔχει ἡ κακία ὡς ἐμοὶ δοκεῖ ἅ γε δὴ καὶ ἄξια θέας
greek_tokens: deuro nun eimi de egw ina kai eidon osos kai eidos exis o kakia ws egw dokew o ge dh kai acios qea epomai fhmi monos legos kai meis eimi de egw wsper apo skopia egw fainw epei entauqa anabainw o logos eis men eimi eidos o aretaw apeiros de o kakia tessares de en autos atta os kai acios epimimnhskomai pws legw fhmi osos eimi de egw politeia tropos eimi eidos exw tosoutos kinduneuw kai yuxazw tropos eimi posos dh
english_head: Come up here then, said I, that you may see how many are the kinds of evil, I mean those that it is worth while to observe and distinguish. I am with you, he said; only do you say on. And truly, said I, now that we have come to this height of argument I seem to see as from a point of outlook that there is one form of excellence, and that the forms of evil are infinite, yet that there are some four among them that it is worth while to take note of. What do you mean? he said. As many as are the varieties of political constitutions that constitute specific types, so many, it seems likely,

greek_head: Πέντε μέν ἦν δ' ἐγώ πολιτειῶν πέντε δὲ ψυχῆς Λέγε ἔφη τίνες
greek_tokens: pente men eimi de egw politeia pente de yuxazw legos fhmi tinw legos eipon ostis eis men outos os egw dierxomai politeia eaw an tropos eponomazw de an kai dixaw eggignomai men gar anhr eis en o arxw diaferw basileia an kalew pleiwn de aristokratia alhqhs fhmi outos men toinun eimi de egw eis eidos legos oute gar an
english_head: are the characters of soul. How many, pray? There are five kinds of constitutions, said I, and five kinds of soul. Tell me what they are, he said. I tell you, said I, that one way of government would be the constitution that we have just expounded, but the names that might be applied to it are two. If one man of surpassing merit rose among the rulers, it would be denominated royalty; if more than one, aristocracy. True, he said. Well, then, I said, this is one of the forms I have in mind.

greek_head: πλείους οὔτε εἷς ἐγγενόμενοι κινήσειεν ἂν τῶν ἀξίων λόγου νόμων τῆς πόλεως τροφῇ τε καὶ παιδείᾳ χρησάμενος ᾗ
greek_tokens: plews oute eis eggignomai kinew an o acios logos nomos o polis trefw te kai paideia xraw h dierxomai ou gar eoika fhmi
english_head: For neither would a number of such men, nor one if he arose among them, alter to any extent worth mentioning the laws of our city—if he preserved the breeding and the education that we have described. It is not likely, he said.

greek_head: Ἀγαθὴν μὲν τοίνυν τὴν τοιαύτην πόλιν τε καὶ πολιτείαν καὶ ὀρθὴν καλῶ καὶ ἄνδρα τὸν τοιοῦτον κακὰς δὲ τὰς ἄλλας
greek_tokens: agaqos men toinun o toioutos polis te kai politeia kai orqos kalew kai anhr o toioutos kakos de o allos kai amartanw eiper outos orqos peri te polis dioikhsis kai peri idiwths yuxazw tropos kataskeuh en tessares ponhria eidos eimi poa dh outos fhmi kai egw men eimi o efechs era os egw fainw
english_head: To such a city, then, or constitution I apply the terms good and right—and to the corresponding kind of man; but the others I describe as bad and mistaken, if this one is right, in respect both to the administration of states and to the formation of the character of the individual soul, they falling under four forms of badness. What are these, he said. And I was going on to enumerate them in what seemed to me the order of their evolution

greek_head: ἕκασται ἐξ ἀλλήλων μεταβαίνειν ὁ δὲ Πολέμαρχος σμικρὸν γὰρ ἀπωτέρω τοῦ Ἀδειμάντου καθῆστο ἐκτείνας
greek_tokens: ekastos ek allhlwn metabainw o de polemarxos mikros gar apwteros o adeimantos kaqhmai ekteinw o xeir kai lambanw o imation anwqen autos para o wmos ekeinos te prosagw kai proteinw eautou legw atta proskuptw os allos men oudeis katakouw ode de afihmi oun fhmi h tis draw hkistos ge fhmi o adeimantos megas hdh legos kai egw tis malista fhmi su ou afihmi sos eimi de os
english_head: from one another, when Polemarchus—he sat at some little distance from Adeimantus—stretched forth his hand, and, taking hold of his garment from above by the shoulder, drew the other toward him and, leaning forward himself, spoke a few words in his ear, of which we overheard nothing else save only this, Shall we let him off, then, he said, or what shall we do? By no means, said Adeimantus, now raising his voice. What, pray, said I, is it that you are not letting off? You,

greek_head: Ὅτι ἐγὼ εἶπον τί μάλιστα Ἀπορρᾳθυμεῖν ἡμῖν δοκεῖς ἔφη καὶ εἶδος ὅλον οὐ τὸ
greek_tokens: ostis egw eipon tis malista aporraqumew egw dokew fhmi kai eidos oloc ou o elaxistos ekkleptw o logos ina mh dierxomai kai lanqanw oiomai eipon autos faulos ws ara peri gunaikow te kai pais pas dhlos ostis koinos o filos eimi oukoun orqos fhmi eimi adeimantos nai eimi de os alla o orqos outos wsper allos logos deomai tis o tropos o koinwnia polus gar an gignomai
english_head: said he. And for what reason, pray? said I. We think you are a slacker, he said, and are trying to cheat us out of a whole division, and that not the least, of the argument to avoid the trouble of expounding it, and expect to get away with it by observing thus lightly that, of course, in respect to women and children it is obvious to everybody that the possessions of friends will be in common. Well, isn’t that right, Adeimantus? I said. Yes, said he, but this word right, like other things, requires defining as to the way and manner of such a community. There might be many ways. Don’t, then, pass over the one

greek_head: μὴ οὖν παρῇς ὅντινα σὺ λέγεις ὡς ἡμεῖς πάλαι περιμένομεν οἰόμενοί σέ που μνησθήσεσθαι παιδοποιίας τε πέρι πῶς
greek_tokens: mh oun pareimi ostis su legw ws egw pala perimenw oiomai sos pou mimnhskw paidopoiia te peri pws paidopoiew kai gignomai pws trefw kai oloc outos os legw koinwnia gunaikow te kai pais megas gar tis oiomai ferw kai oloc eis politeia orqos h mh orqos gignomai nun oun epei allos epilambanw politeia prin outos ikanos diairew dokew egw outos
english_head: that you have in mind. For we have long been lying in wait for you, expecting that you would say something both of the procreation of children and their bringing up, and would explain the whole matter of the community of women and children of which you speak. We think that the right or wrong management of this makes a great difference, all the difference in the world, in the constitution of a state; so now, since you are beginning on another constitution before sufficiently defining this, we are firmly resolved,

greek_head: ὃ σὺ ἤκουσας τὸ σὲ μὴ μεθιέναι πρὶν ἂν ταῦτα πάντα ὥσπερ τἆλλα διέλθῃς
greek_tokens: o su akouw o sos mh meqihmi prin an outos pas wsper allos dierxomai kai egw toinun o glauc fhmi koinwnos o yhfos outos tiqhmi amelei fhmi o qrasumaxos pas outos dokew egw nomizw eimi swkraths oios eimi de egw ergazomai epilambanw egw osos logos palin wsper ek arxh kinew peri o politeia os ws hdh dierxomai egw xairw agaph ei tis eaw outos apodexomai ws tote erw o nun su parakalew
english_head: as you overheard, not to let you go till you have expounded all this as fully as you did the rest. Set me down, too, said Glaucon, as voting this ticket. Surely, said Thrasymachus, you may consider it a joint resolution of us all, Socrates. What a thing you have done, said I, in thus challenging me! What a huge debate you have started afresh, as it were, about this polity, in the supposed completion of which I was rejoicing, being only too glad to have it accepted

greek_head: οὐκ ἴστε ὅσον ἑσμὸν λόγων ἐπεγείρετε ὃν ὁρῶν ἐγὼ παρῆκα τότε μὴ παράσχοι πολὺν ὄχλον
greek_tokens: ou oida osos esmos logos epegeirw os oraw egw parihmi tote mh parexw polus oxlos tis de eimi de os o qrasumaxos xrusoxoew oiomai ode nun enqade afikneomai alla ou logos akouw nai eipon metrios ge metron de ge fhmi eimi swkraths o glauc toioutos logos akouw oloc o bios new exw alla o men hmeteros ea su de peri os erwtaw mhdamos apokamnw h
english_head: as I then set it forth! You don’t realize what a swarm of arguments you are stirring up by this demand, which I foresaw and evaded to save us no end of trouble. Well, said Thrasymachus,do you suppose this company has come here to prospect for gold and not to listen to discussions? Yes, I said, in measure. Nay, Socrates, said Glaucon, the measure of listening to such discussions is the whole of life for reasonable men. So don’t consider us, and do not you yourself grow weary

greek_head: σοι δοκεῖ διεξιών τίς ἡ κοινωνία τοῖς φύλαξιν ἡμῖν παίδων τε πέρι καὶ γυναικῶν ἔσται καὶ τροφῆς νέων ἔτι ὄντων τῆς
greek_tokens: su dokew dieceimi tis o koinwnia o fulac egw pais te peri kai gunaikow eimi kai trefw neos eti eimi o en o metacu xronos gignomai genesis te kai paideia o dh epiponos dokew eimi peiraw oun eipon tis tropos deomai gignomai autos ou radios eimi eudaimwn eimi de egw dierxomai polus gar apistia exis eti mallon o emprosqen os dierxomai kai gar ws dunatos legw apistew an kai ei ostis malista gignomai ws ariston an eaw outos kai outos apistew
english_head: in explaining to us what we ask or, your views as to how this communion of wives and children among our guardians will be managed, and also about the rearing of the children while still young in the interval between birth and formal schooling which is thought to be the most difficult part of education. Try, then, to tell us what must be the manner of it. It is not an easy thing to expound, my dear fellow, said I, for even more than the provisions that precede it, it raises many doubts. For one might doubt whether what is proposed is possible and, even conceding the possibility, one might still be sceptical whether it is best.

greek_head: διὸ δὴ καὶ ὄκνος τις αὐτῶν ἅπτεσθαι μὴ εὐχὴ δοκῇ εἶναι ὁ λόγος ὦ φίλε ἑταῖρε
greek_tokens: dio dh kai oknos tis autos aptw mh euxh dokazw eimi o logos eimi filos etairos mhdeis eimi de os oknew oute gar agnwmwn oute apistos oute dusnous e akouw kai egw eipon eimi aristos eimi pou boulomai egw paraqarsunw legw egw fhmi pas toinun eimi de egw enantion poiew pisteuw men gar egw egw oida o legos kalos exw o paramuqia en gar fronimos te kai filos peri o megas te kai
english_head: For which reason one as it were, shrinks from touching on the matter lest the theory be regarded as nothing but a wish-thought, my dear friend. Do not shrink, he said, for your hearers will not be inconsiderate nor distrustful nor hostile. And I said, My good fellow, is that remark intended to encourage me? It is, he said. Well, then, said I, it has just the contrary effect. For, if I were confident that I was speaking with knowledge, it would be an excellent encouragement.

greek_head: φίλων τἀληθῆ εἰδότα λέγειν ἀσφαλὲς καὶ θαρραλέον ἀπιστοῦντα δὲ καὶ ζητοῦντα ἅμα τοὺς λόγους ποιεῖσθαι ὃ δὴ
greek_tokens: filos alhqhs oida legw asfalhs kai qarsaleos apistew de kai zhtew ama o logos poiew o dh
english_head: For there is both safety and security in speaking the truth with knowledge about our greatest and dearest concerns to those who are both wise and dear. But to speak when one doubts himself and is seeking while he talks is

greek_head: ἐγὼ δρῶ φοβερόν τε καὶ σφαλερόν οὔ τι γέλωτα ὀφλεῖν παιδικὸν γὰρ τοῦτό γε ἀλλὰ μὴ σφαλεὶς τῆς ἀληθείας οὐ
greek_tokens: egw draw foberos te kai sfaleros ou tis gelws oflew paidikon gar outos ge alla mh sfallw o alhqeia ou monos autos alla kai o filos sunepispaw keimai peri o hkistos deomai sfallw proskunew de adrasteia eimi glauc xaris e mellw legw elpizw gar oun elasswn amarthma aekousios tis foneus gignomai h apatewn kalh te kai agaqos kai dikaios nomimos peri outos oun o kinduneuma kinduneuw en exqros kreisswn h
english_head: a fearful and slippery venture. The fear is not of being laughed at, for that is childish, but, lest, missing the truth, I fall down and drag my friends with me in matters where it most imports not to stumble. So I salute Nemesis, Glaucon, in what I am about to say. For, indeed, I believe that involuntary homicide is a lesser fault than to mislead opinion about the honorable, the good, and the just. This is a risk that it is better to run with enemies

greek_head: φίλοις ὥστε εὖ με παραμυθῇ Καὶ ὁ Γλαύκων γελάσας Ἀλλ' ὦ Σώκρατες ἔφη ἐάν τι
greek_tokens: filos wste eu egw paramuqeomai kai o glauc gelaw alla eimi swkraths fhmi ean tis pasxw plhmmelhs upo o logos afihmi su wsper fonos kai kaqaros eimi kai mh apatewn egw alla qarsew legos alla mentoi eipon kaqaros ge kai ekei o afihmi ws o nomos legw eoika de ge eiper ekei enqade legos toinun fhmi outos ge eneka legw dh fhmi egw xrh anapalin au nun o tote isos
english_head: than with friends, so that your encouragement is none. And Glaucon, with a laugh, said, Nay, Socrates, if any false note in the argument does us any harm, we release you as in a homicide case, and warrant you pure of hand and no deceiver of us. So speak on with confidence. Well, said I, he who is released in that case is counted pure as the law bids, and, presumably, if there, here too. Speak on, then, he said, for all this objection. We must return then, said I, and say now what perhaps ought to have been said in due sequence there.

greek_head: ἔδει ἐφεξῆς λέγειν τάχα δὲ οὕτως ἂν ὀρθῶς ἔχοι μετὰ ἀνδρεῖον δρᾶμα παντελῶς διαπερανθὲν τὸ γυναικεῖον αὖ
greek_tokens: dew efechs legw taxa de outws an orqos exw meta andreios drama pantelhs diaperainw o gunaikeios au perainw allos te kai epei su outws prokalew anqrwpos gar fusi kai paideuw ws egw dierxomai kata emos doca ou este allos orqos pais te kai gunaikow kthsis te kai xreos h kata ekeinos o ormh eimi os o proteros ormaw epixeirew de pou ws agelh fulac o anhr kaqisthmi o logos nai
english_head: But maybe this way is right, that after the completion of the male drama we should in turn go through with the female, especially since you are so urgent. For men, then, born and bred as we described there is in my opinion no other right possession and use of children and women than that which accords with the start we gave them. Our endeavor, I believe, was to establish these men in our discourse as the guardians of a flock? Yes.

greek_head: Ἀκολουθῶμεν τοίνυν καὶ τὴν γένεσιν καὶ τροφὴν παραπλησίαν ἀποδιδόντες καὶ σκοπῶμεν εἰ ἡμῖν πρέπει ἢ οὔ
greek_tokens: akolouqew toinun kai o genesis kai trofh paraplhsios apodidwmi kai skopaw ei egw prepw h ou pws fhmi wde o qhlus o fulac kuwn poteros sumfulassw oiomai dew o an e arshn fulassw kai sunqhreuw kai allos koinh prassw h o men oikourew endon ws adunatos dia o o skulac tokos te kai trofh o de ponew te kai pas epimeleia exw peri o poimnion
english_head: Let us preserve the analogy, then, and assign them a generation and breeding answering to it, and see if it suits us or not. In what way? he said. In this. Do we expect the females of watch-dogs to join in guarding what the males guard and to hunt with them and share all their pursuits or do we expect the females to stay indoors as being incapacitated by the bearing and the breeding of the whelps while the males toil and have all the care of the flock? They have all things in common,

greek_head: Κοινῇ ἔφη πάντα πλὴν ὡς ἀσθενεστέραις χρώμεθα τοῖς δὲ ὡς ἰσχυροτέροις
greek_tokens: koinh fhmi pas plhn ws asqenhs xraomai o de ws isxuros oios su oun fhmi egw epi o autos xraomai tis zwion an mh o autos trofh te kai paideia apodidwmi ou oios te ei ara o gunh epi tautos xraw kai o anhr tautos kai didakteon autos
english_head: he replied, except that we treat the females as weaker and the males as stronger. Is it possible, then, said I, to employ any creature for the same ends as another if you do not assign it the same nurture and education? It is not possible. If, then, we are to use the women for the same things as the men,

greek_head: Ναί Μουσικὴ μὴν ἐκείνοις γε καὶ γυμναστικὴ ἐδόθη
greek_tokens: nai mousikh meis ekeinos ge kai gumnastikos didwmi nai kai o gunh ara outos o texnh kai o peri o polemos apodoteos kai xrhsteon kata tautos eoika ek os legw fhmi isos dh eipon para o eqos geloios an fainw polus peri o nun legw ei prassw h legw kai mala fhmi tis eimi de egw geloios autos oraw h dhlos dh ostis gumnas o gunh en o palaistra gumnazw meta
english_head: we must also teach them the same things. Yes. Now music together with gymnastic was the training we gave the men. Yes. Then we must assign these two arts to the women also and the offices of war and employ them in the same way. It would seem likely from what you say, he replied. Perhaps, then, said I, the contrast with present custom would make much in our proposals look ridiculous if our words are to be realized in fact. Yes, indeed, he said. What then, said I, is the funniest thing you note in them? Is it not obviously the women exercising unclad in the palestra

greek_head: τῶν ἀνδρῶν οὐ μόνον τὰς νέας ἀλλὰ καὶ ἤδη τὰς πρεσβυτέρας ὥσπερ τοὺς γέροντας ἐν τοῖς γυμνασίοις ὅταν ῥυσοὶ
greek_tokens: o androw ou monos o naus alla kai hdh o presbus wsper o gerwn en o gumnasion otan rusos kai mh hdus o oyis omws filogumnastew nh o dios fhmi geloios gar an os ge en o paristhmi fainw oukoun eimi de egw epei ormaw legw ou fobhteos o o xarieis skwmma osos kai oios an eipon eis o toioutos metabolh gignomai kai peri o gumnasion
english_head: together with the men, not only the young, but even the older, like old men in gymnasiums, when, though wrinkled and unpleasant to look at, they still persist in exercising? Yes, on my word, he replied, it would seem ridiculous under present conditions. Then, said I, since we have set out to speak our minds, we must not fear all the jibes with which the wits would greet so great a revolution, and the sort of things they would say about gymnastics

greek_head: καὶ περὶ μουσικὴν καὶ οὐκ ἐλάχιστα περὶ τὴν τῶν ὅπλων σχέσιν καὶ ἵππων ὀχήσεις
greek_tokens: kai peri mousikh kai ou elaxistos peri o o oplon sxesis kai ippos oxhsis orqos fhmi legw alla epei legw arxw poreuteos pros o traxus o nomos dew te outos mh o eautou prassw alla spoudazw kai upomimnhskw ostis ou polus xronos ek e o ellhn dokew aisxros eimi kai geloios o nun o polus o barbaros gumnos anhr oraw kai oste arxw o gumnasis proteros men krhs epeita lakedaimonios
english_head: and culture, and most of all about the bearing of arms and the bestriding of horses. You’re right, he said. But since we have begun we must go forward to the rough part of our law, after begging these fellows not to mind their own business but to be serious, and reminding them that it is not long since the Greeks thought it disgraceful and ridiculous, as most of the barbarians do now, for men to be seen naked. And when the practice of athletics began, first with the Cretans

greek_head: ἐξῆν τοῖς τότε ἀστείοις πάντα ταῦτα κωμῳδεῖν ἢ οὐκ οἴει Ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: ecesti o tote asteios pas outos kwmwdew h ou oiomai egw alla epei oimh xraomai ameinwn o apodunw o sugkaluptw pas o toioutos fainw kai o en o ofqalmos dh geloios ekrew upo o en o logos mhnuw ariston kai outos endeiknumi ostis mataios os geloios allos tis hgeomai h o kakos kai o gelwtopoiew epixeirew pros allos tis oyis apoblepw ws geloios h o o afrwn
english_head: and then with the Lacedaemonians, it was open to the wits of that time to make fun of these practices, don’t you think so? I do. But when, I take it, experience showed that it is better to strip than to veil all things of this sort, then the laughter of the eyes faded away before that which reason revealed to be best, and this made it plain that he talks idly who deems anything else ridiculous but evil, and who tries to raise a laugh by looking to any other pattern of absurdity than

greek_head: τε καὶ κακοῦ καὶ καλοῦ αὖ σπουδάζει πρὸς ἄλλον τινὰ σκοπὸν στησάμενος ἢ τὸν τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ
greek_tokens: te kai kakos kai kalew au spoudazw pros allos tis skopos isthmi h o o agaqos pantapasi men oun fhmi ara oun ou proteros men outos peri autos anomologhteon ei dunatos h ou kai doteos amfisbhthsis eite tis filopaismwn eite spoudastikos eqelw amfisbhtew poteros
english_head: that of folly and wrong or sets up any other standard of the beautiful as a mark for his seriousness than the good. Most assuredly, said he. Then is not the first thing that we have to agree upon with regard to these proposals whether they are possible or not? And we must throw open the debate to anyone who wishes either in jest or earnest to raise the question

greek_head: δυνατὴ φύσις ἡ ἀνθρωπίνη ἡ θήλεια τῇ τοῦ ἄρρενος γένους κοινωνῆσαι εἰς ἅπαντα τὰ ἔργα ἢ οὐδ' εἰς ἕν ἢ εἰς τὰ μὲν
greek_tokens: dunatos fusis o anqrwpinos o qhlus o o arshn genos koinwnew eis apas o ergon h oude eis eis h eis o men oios te eis de o ou kai outos dh o peri o polemos poteros eimi ara ou outws an kallista tis arxw ws o eoika kai kallista teleutaw polus ge fhmi boulomai oun eimi de egw egw pros egw autos uper o allos amfisbhtew ina mh erhmos o o eteros logos poliorkew
english_head: whether female human nature is capable of sharing with the male all tasks or none at all, or some but not others, and under which of these heads this business of war falls. Would not this be that best beginning which would naturally and proverbially lead to the best end? Far the best, he said. Shall we then conduct the debate with ourselves in behalf of those others so that the case of the other side may not be taken defenceless and go by default?

greek_head: Οὐδέν ἔφη κωλύει Λέγωμεν δὴ ὑπὲρ αὐτῶν ὅτι Ὦ Σώκρατές τε καὶ
greek_tokens: oudeis fhmi kwluw legw dh uper autos ostis eimi swkraths te kai glauc oudeis deomai su allos amfisbhtew autos gar en arxh o katoikisis os oikizw polis omologew dew kata fus ekastos enos eis o eautou prassw omologew oimh pws gar ou eimi oun opws ou pampolus diaferw gunh anhr o fus pws de ou diaferw oukoun allos kai ergnumi ekateros proshkw prostassw
english_head: Nothing hinders, he said. Shall we say then in their behalf: There is no need, Socrates and Glaucon, of others disputing against you, for you yourselves at the beginning of the foundation of your city agreed that each one ought to mind as his own business the one thing for which he was fitted by nature? We did so agree, I think; certainly! Can it be denied then that there is by nature a great difference between men and women? Surely there is. Is it not fitting, then, that a different function should be appointed

greek_head: τὸ κατὰ τὴν αὑτοῦ φύσιν Τί μήν
greek_tokens: o kata o eautou fus tis meis pws oun ou amartanw nuni kai enantios su autos legw faskw au o anhr kai o gunh dew o autos prassw pleistos xwrizw fus exw ecis tis eimi qaumasios pros outos apologeomai ws men ecaifnhs fhmi ou panu radios alla sew dew te kai deomai kai o uper egw logos ostis pote eimi ermhneuw outos eimi eimi de egw eimi glauc kai allos polus
english_head: for each corresponding to this difference of nature? Certainly. How, then, can you deny that you are mistaken and in contradiction with yourselves when you turn around and affirm that the men and the women ought to do the same thing, though their natures are so far apart? Can you surprise me with an answer to that question? Not easily on this sudden challenge, he replied: but I will and do beg you to lend your voice to the plea in our behalf, whatever it may be. These and many similar difficulties, Glaucon, said I,

greek_head: τοιαῦτα ἃ ἐγὼ πάλαι προορῶν ἐφοβούμην τε καὶ ὤκνουν ἅπτεσθαι τοῦ νόμου τοῦ περὶ τὴν τῶν γυναικῶν καὶ παίδων
greek_tokens: toioutos o egw pala prooraw fobew te kai oknew aptw o nomos o peri o o gunaikow kai pais ktaomai kai trofh ou ma o dios fhmi ou gar eukolos eoika ou gar eipon alla dh wde exis antomai tis eis kolumbhqra mikros empitnw antomai eis o megas pelagos mesos omws ge neomai oudeis hsswn panu men oun oukoun kai egw neusteon kai peirateon swzw ek o logos htoi delfis tis elpizw egw upolambanw an h tis allos aporos swthria
english_head: I foresaw and feared, and so shrank from touching on the law concerning the getting and breeding of women and children. It does not seem an easy thing, by heaven, he said, no, by heaven. No, it is not, said I; but the fact is that whether one tumbles into a little diving-pool or plump into the great sea he swims all the same. By all means. Then we, too, must swim and try to escape out of the sea of argument in the hope that either some dolphin will take us on its back or some other desperate rescue.

greek_head: Ἔοικεν ἔφη Φέρε δή ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἐάν πῃ εὕρωμεν τὴν ἔξοδον ὁμολογοῦμεν
greek_tokens: eoika fhmi ferw dh eimi de egw ean ph euriskw o ecodos omologew gar dh allos fus allos dew epithdeuw gunh de kai anhr allos eimi o de allos fusis o autos fhmi nun dew epithdeuw outos egw kathgorew komidh ge
english_head: So it seems, he said. Come then, consider, said I, if we can find a way out. We did agree that different natures should have differing pursuits and that the nature of men and women differ. And yet now we affirm that these differing natures should have the same pursuits. That is the indictment. It is. What a grand thing, Glaucon, said I,

greek_head: Ἦ γενναία ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ Γλαύκων ἡ δύναμις τῆς ἀντιλογικῆς τέχνης
greek_tokens: eimi gennaios eimi de egw eimi glauc o dunamis o antilogikos texnh tis dh ostis eipon dokew egw eis autos kai akwn polus empitnw kai oiomai ou erizw alla dialegw dia o mh dunamai kata eidos diairew o legw episkopew alla kata autos o onoma diwkw o legw o enantiwsis eris ou dialektos pros allhlwn xraomai eimi gar dh fhmi peri polus outos o paqos alla mhs kai pros egw outos teinw en o pareimi
english_head: is the power of the art of contradiction! Why so? Because, said I, many appear to me to fall into it even against their wills, and to suppose that they are not wrangling but arguing, owing to their inability to apply the proper divisions and distinctions to the subject under consideration. They pursue purely verbal oppositions, practising eristic, not dialectic on one another. Yes, this does happen to many, he said; but does this observation apply to us too at present?

greek_head: Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν ἦν δ' ἐγώ κινδυνεύομεν γοῦν ἄκοντες ἀντιλογίας ἅπτεσθαι
greek_tokens: pantapasi men oun eimi de egw kinduneuw goun akwn antilogia aptw pws o mh o autos fus ostis ou o autos deomai epithdeuma tugxanw panu andreios te kai eristikos kata o onoma diwkw episkeptomai de oude oph tis eidos o o eteros te kai o autos fusis kai pros tis teinw orizw tote oste o epithdeuma allh fusis allos o de autos o autos apodidwmi ou gar oun fhmi episkeptomai
english_head: Absolutely, said I; at any rate I am afraid that we are unawares slipping into contentiousness. In what way? The principle that natures not the same ought not to share in the same pursuits we are following up most manfully and eristically in the literal and verbal sense but we did not delay to consider at all what particular kind of diversity and identity of nature we had in mind and with reference to what we were trying to define it when we assigned different pursuits to different natures and the same to the same. No, we didn’t consider that, he said.

greek_head: Τοιγάρτοι εἶπον ἔξεστιν ἡμῖν ὡς ἔοικεν ἀνερωτᾶν ἡμᾶς αὐτοὺς εἰ ἡ αὐτὴ φύσις φαλακρῶν καὶ κομητῶν καὶ οὐχ
greek_tokens: toigar eipon ecesti egw ws eoika anerwtaw egw autos ei o autos fusis falakra kai komhths kai ou o enantios kai epeidan omologew enantios eimi ean falakroomai skutotomew mh eaw komhths ean de au komaw mh o eteros geloios mentoi eaw fhmi ara kata allos tis eipon egw geloios h ostis tote ou pantws o autos kai o eteros fus tiqhmi alla ekeinos o eidos o alloiwsis te kai omoiwsis monos
english_head: Wherefore, by the same token, I said, we might ask ourselves whether the natures of bald and long-haired men are the same and not, rather, contrary. And, after agreeing that they were opposed, we might, if the bald cobbled, forbid the long-haired to do so, or vice versa. That would be ridiculous, he said. Would it be so, said I, for any other reason than that we did not then posit likeness and difference of nature in any and every sense, but were paying heed solely to the kind of diversity

greek_head: ἐφυλάττομεν τὸ πρὸς αὐτὰ τεῖνον τὰ ἐπιτηδεύματα οἷον ἰατρικὸν μὲν καὶ ἰατρικὴν τὴν ψυχὴν ὄντα τὴν αὐτὴν φύσιν
greek_tokens: fulassw o pros autos teinw o epithdeuma oios iatrikos men kai iatrikos o yuxh eimi o autos fus exw legw h ou oiomai egw iatrikos de ge kai tektonikos allos pantws pou oukoun eimi de egw kai o o androw kai o o gunaikow genos ean men pros texnh tis h allos epithdeuma diaferw fainw outos dh fhmi ekateros dew apodidwmi ean de autos outos fainw diaferw o o men qhlus tiktw
english_head: and homogeneity that was pertinent to the pursuits themselves? We meant, for example, that a man and a woman who have a physician’s mind have the same nature. Don’t you think so? I do. But that a man physician and a man carpenter have different natures? Certainly, I suppose. Similarly, then, said I, if it appears that the male and the female sex have distinct qualifications for any arts or pursuits, we shall affirm that they ought to be assigned respectively to each. But if it appears that they differ only in just this respect that the female bears

greek_head: τὸ δὲ ἄρρεν ὀχεύειν οὐδέν τί πω φήσομεν μᾶλλον ἀποδεδεῖχθαι ὡς πρὸς ὃ ἡμεῖς λέγομεν διαφέρει γυνὴ ἀνδρός ἀλλ'
greek_tokens: o de arshn oxeuw oudeis tis pw fhmi mallon apodeiknumi ws pros o egw legw diaferw gunh anhr alla eti oiomai dew o autos epithdeuw o te fulac egw kai o gunh autos kai orqos ge fhmi oukoun meta outos keleuw o o enantios legw
english_head: and the male begets, we shall say that no proof has yet been produced that the woman differs from the man for our purposes, but we shall continue to think that our guardians and their wives ought to follow the same pursuits. And rightly, said he. Then, is it not the next thing to bid our opponent tell us

greek_head: τοῦτο αὐτὸ διδάσκειν ἡμᾶς πρὸς τίνα τέχνην ἢ τί ἐπιτήδευμα τῶν περὶ πόλεως κατασκευὴν οὐχ ἡ αὐτὴ ἀλλὰ ἑτέρα φύσις
greek_tokens: outos autos didaskw egw pros tis texnh h tis epithdeuma o peri polis kataskeuh ou o autos alla eteros fusis gunh te kai anhr dikaios goun taxa toinun an osper su oligos proteros legw eipon an kai allos ostis en men o paraxrhma ikanos eipon ou radios episkeptomai de oudeis xalepos eipon gar an boulomai oun deomai o o toioutos antilegw akolouqew
english_head: precisely for what art or pursuit concerned with the conduct of a state the woman’s nature differs from the man’s? That would be at any rate fair. Perhaps, then, someone else, too, might say what you were saying a while ago, that it is not easy to find a satisfactory answer on a sudden, but that with time for reflection there is no difficulty. He might say that. Shall we, then, beg the raiser of such objections to follow us,

greek_head: ἡμῖν ἐάν πως ἡμεῖς ἐκείνῳ ἐνδειξώμεθα ὅτι οὐδέν ἐστιν ἐπιτήδευμα ἴδιον γυναικὶ πρὸς διοίκησιν πόλεως
greek_tokens: egw ean pws egw ekeinos endeiknumi ostis oudeis eimi epithdeuma idios gunh pros dioikhsis polis panu ge eimi dh fhmi pros autos apokrinw ara outws legw o men eufuhs pros tis eimi o de afuhs en os o men radios tis manqanw o de xalepos kai o men apo braxus maqhsis epi polus euretikos eaw e manqanw o de polus maqhsis tugxanw kai meleth mhde o manqanw swzw kai o men o o swma ikanos uphretew o dianoia o
english_head: if we may perhaps prove able to make it plain to him that there is no pursuit connected with the administration of a state that is peculiar to woman? By all means. Come then, we shall say to him, answer our question. Was this the basis of your distinction between the man naturally gifted for anything and the one not so gifted—that the one learned easily, the other with difficulty; that the one with slight instruction could discover much for himself in the matter studied, but the other, after much instruction and drill, could not even remember what he had learned; and that the bodily faculties of the one adequately served his mind,

greek_head: δὲ ἐναντιοῖτο ἆρ' ἄλλα ἄττα ἐστὶν ἢ ταῦτα οἷς τὸν εὐφυῆ πρὸς ἕκαστα καὶ τὸν μὴ ὡρίζου
greek_tokens: de enantioomai ara allos atta eimi h outos os o eufuhs pros ekastos kai o mh orizw oudeis eimi de os allos fhmi oida tis oun upo anqrwpos meletaw en os ou pas outos o o androw genos diaferontws exis h o o gunaikow h makrologew o te ufantikos legw kai o o popanon te kai eyhma qerapeia en os dh tis dokew o gunaikeios genos eimi e kai katagelastos
english_head: while, for the other, the body was a hindrance? Were there any other points than these by which you distinguish the well endowed man in every subject and the poorly endowed? No one, said he, will be able to name any others. Do you know, then, of anything practised by mankind in which the masculine sex does not surpass the female on all these points? Must we make a long story of it by alleging weaving and the watching of pancakes

greek_head: ἐστι πάντων ἡττώμενον Ἀληθῆ ἔφη λέγεις ὅτι πολὺ κρατεῖται ἐν ἅπασιν ὡς
greek_tokens: eimi pas hssaomai alhqhs fhmi legw ostis polus kratew en apas ws epos eipon o genos o genos gunh mentoi polus polus androw beltiwn eis polus o de oloc exis ws su legw oudeis ara eimi eimi filos epithdeuma o polis dioikew gunh dioti gunh oude anhr dioti anhr alla omoios diaspeirw o fusis en amfw o zwion kai pas men metexw gunh epithdeuma kata
english_head: and the boiling pot, whereon the sex plumes itself and wherein its defeat will expose it to most laughter? You are right, he said, that the one sex is far surpassed by the other in everything, one may say. Many women, it is true, are better than many men in many things, but broadly speaking, it is as you say. Then there is no pursuit of the administrators of a state that belongs to a woman because she is a woman or to a man because he is a man. But the natural capacities are distributed alike among both creatures, and women naturally share in all pursuits and men in all—

greek_head: φύσιν πάντων δὲ ἀνήρ ἐπὶ πᾶσι δὲ ἀσθενέστερον γυνὴ ἀνδρός
greek_tokens: fus pas de anhr epi pas de asqenhs gunh anhr panu ge eimi oun anhr pas prosstazw gunh de oudeis kai pws alla eimi gar oimh ws fhmi kai gunh iatrikos o de ou kai mousikh o de amousos fusis tis meis
english_head: yet for all the woman is weaker than the man. Assuredly. Shall we, then, assign them all to men and nothing to women? How could we? We shall rather, I take it, say that one woman has the nature of a physician and another not, and one is by nature musical, and another unmusical? Surely. Can we, then, deny that one woman is naturally athletic

greek_head: Καὶ γυμναστικὴ δ' ἄρα οὔ οὐδὲ πολεμική ἡ δὲ ἀπόλεμος καὶ οὐ φιλογυμναστική
greek_tokens: kai gumnastikos de ara ou oude polemikos o de apolemos kai ou filogumnastikos oimh egw tis de filosofos te kai misosofos kai qumoeidhs o de aqumos eimi kai outos eimi ara kai fulakikos gunh o de ou h ou toioutos kai o androw o fulakikos fus eklegw toioutos men oun kai gunh ara kai anhr o autos fusis eis fulakh polis plhn osos asqenhs o de isxuros eimi fainw
english_head: and warlike and another unwarlike and averse to gymnastics? I think not. And again, one a lover, another a hater, of wisdom? And one high-spirited, and the other lacking spirit? That also is true. Then it is likewise true that one woman has the qualities of a guardian and another not. Were not these the natural qualities of the men also whom we selected for guardians? They were. The women and the men, then, have the same nature in respect to the guardianship of the state, save in so far as the one is weaker, the other stronger. Apparently.

greek_head: Καὶ γυναῖκες ἄρα αἱ τοιαῦται τοῖς τοιούτοις ἀνδράσιν ἐκλεκτέαι συνοικεῖν τε καὶ συμφυλάττειν ἐπείπερ εἰσὶν ἱκαναὶ
greek_tokens: kai gunh ara o toioutos o toioutos anhr eklekteos sunoikew te kai sumfulassw epei eimi ikanos kai suggeneus autos o fus panu ge o de epithdeuma ou o autos apodoteos o autos fusis o autos hkw ara eis o proteros periferw kai omologew mh para fus eimi o o fulac gunh mousikh te kai gumnastikos apodidwmi pantapasi men oun ou ara adunatos ge oude euxh omoios nomoqetew
english_head: Women of this kind, then, must be selected to cohabit with men of this kind and to serve with them as guardians since they are capable of it and akin by nature. By all means. And to the same natures must we not assign the same pursuits? The same. We come round, then, to our previous statement, and agree that it does not run counter to nature to assign music and gymnastics to the wives of the guardians.

greek_head: ἐπείπερ κατὰ φύσιν ἐτίθεμεν τὸν νόμον ἀλλὰ τὰ νῦν παρὰ ταῦτα γιγνόμενα παρὰ φύσιν μᾶλλον ὡς ἔοικε γίγνεται
greek_tokens: epei kata fus tiqhmi o nomos alla o nun para outos gignomai para fus mallon ws eoika gignomai eoika oukoun o episkeyis egw eimi ei dunatos ge kai beltistos legw eimi gar kai ostis men dh dunatos diomologew nai ostis de dh beltistos o meta outos deomai diomologew dhlos oukoun pros ge o fulakikos gunh gignomai ou allos men egw anhr poihsis paideia allos de gunh allos
english_head: By all means. Our legislation, then, was not impracticable or utopian, since the law we proposed accorded with nature. Rather, the other way of doing things, prevalent today, proves, as it seems, unnatural. Apparently. The object of our inquiry was the possibility and the desirability of what we were proposing. It was. That it is possible has been admitted. Yes. The next point to be agreed upon is that it is the best way. Obviously. For the production of a guardian, then, education will not be one thing for our men and another for our women, especially since

greek_head: τε καὶ τὴν αὐτὴν φύσιν παραλαβοῦσα Οὐκ ἄλλη
greek_tokens: te kai o autos fus paralambanw ou allos pws oun exis doca o toiosde peri tis dh o upolambanw para sautou o men ameinwn anhr o de xeirwn h pas omoios hgeomai oudamos en oun o polis os oikizw poteros oiomai egw ameinwn anhr ecergazomai o fulac tugxanw eis dierxomai paideia h o skutotomos o skutikos paideuw geloios fhmi erwtaw manqanw fhmi tis de o allos poliths ou outos
english_head: the nature which we hand over to it is the same. There will be no difference. How are you minded, now, in this matter? In what? In the matter of supposing some men to be better and some worse, or do you think them all alike? By no means. In the city, then, that we are founding, which do you think will prove the better men, the guardians receiving the education which we have described or the cobblers educated by the art of cobbling? An absurd question, he said.

greek_head: ἄριστοι Πολύ γε
greek_tokens: aristos polus ge tis de o gunh o gunaikow ou outos eimi beltistos kai outos fhmi polus eimi de tis polis ameinwn h gunh te kai anhr ws aristos eggignomai ou eimi outos de mousikh te kai gumnastikos paragignomai ws
english_head: I understand, said I; and are not these the best of all the citizens? By far. And will not these women be the best of all the women? They, too, by far. Is there anything better for a state than the generation in it of the best possible women and men? There is not. And this, music and gymnastics

greek_head: ἡμεῖς διήλθομεν ἀπεργάσονται Πῶς δ' οὔ
greek_tokens: egw dierxomai apergazomai pws de ou ou monos ara dunatos alla kai ariston polis nomimos tiqhmi outws apoduteon dh o o fulac gunh epei areth anti imation amfiazw kai koinwnhteon polemos te kai o allos fulakeus o peri o polis kai ou allos prakteos outos de autos o elafros o gunh h o anhr doteos dia o o genos asqeneia o
english_head: applied as we described will effect. Surely. Then the institution we proposed is not only possible but the best for the state. That is so. The women of the guardians, then, must strip, since they will be clothed with virtue as a garment, and must take their part with the men in war and the other duties of civic guardianship and have no other occupation. But in these very duties lighter tasks must be assigned to the women than to the men

greek_head: δὲ γελῶν ἀνὴρ ἐπὶ γυμναῖς γυναιξί τοῦ βελτίστου ἕνεκα γυμναζομέναις ἀτελῆ τοῦ γελοίου σοφίας δρέπων καρπόν
greek_tokens: de gelaw anhr epi gumnos gunh o beltistos eneka gumnazw atelhs o geloios sofia drepw karpos oudeis oida ws eoika epi os gelaw oude ostis prassw kallista gar dh outos kai legw kai legw ostis o men wfelimos kalos o de blaberos aisxros pantapasi men oun outos men toinun eis wsper kuma fhmi diafeugw o gunaikeios peri nomos legw wste mh pantapasi katakluzw tiqhmi ws deomai koinh pas epithdeuw o te
english_head: because of their weakness as a class. But the man who ridicules unclad women, exercising because it is best that they should, plucks the unripe fruit of laughter and does not know, it appears, the end of his laughter nor what he would be at. For the fairest thing that is said or ever will be said is this, that the helpful is fair and the harmful foul. Assuredly. In this matter, then, of the regulation of women, we may say that we have surmounted one of the waves of our paradox
