greek_head: φύλακας ἡμῖν καὶ τὰς φυλακίδας ἀλλά πῃ τὸν λόγον αὐτὸν αὑτῷ ὁμολογεῖσθαι ὡς δυνατά τε καὶ ὠφέλιμα λέγει
greek_tokens: fulac egw kai o fulakis alla ph o logos autos eautou omologew ws dunatos te kai wfelimos legw kai mala fhmi ou mikros kuma diafeugw fhmi ge eimi de egw ou megas autos eimi otan o meta outos eidon legos dh eidon fhmi outos eimi de egw epomai nomos kai o emprosqen o allos ws oimh ode tis o gunh outos o androw outos pas passw
english_head: and have not been quite swept away by it in ordaining that our guardians and female guardians must have all pursuits in common, but that in some sort the argument concurs with itself in the assurance that what it proposes is both possible and beneficial. It is no slight wave that you are thus escaping. You will not think it a great one, I said, when you have seen the one that follows. Say on then and show me, said he. This, said I, and all that precedes has for its sequel, in my opinion, the following law. What? That these women shall all be common to all the men,

greek_head: εἶναι κοινάς ἰδίᾳ δὲ μηδενὶ μηδεμίαν συνοικεῖν καὶ τοὺς παῖδας αὖ κοινούς καὶ μήτε γονέα ἔκγονον εἰδέναι τὸν αὑτοῦ
greek_tokens: eimi koinos idios de mhdeis mhdeis sunoikew kai o pais au koinos kai mhte goneus ekgonos oida o eautou mhte pais goneus polus fhmi outos ekeinos megas pros apistia kai o dunatew peri kai o wfelimos ou oimh eimi de egw peri ge o wfelimos amfisbhtew an ws ou megas agaqos koinos men o gunh eimi koinos de o pais eiper oios te alla oimh peri o ei dunatos h mh pleistos an amfisbhthsis gignomai
english_head: and that none shall cohabit with any privately; and that the children shall be common, and that no parent shall know its own offspring nor any child its parent. This is a far bigger paradox than the other, and provokes more distrust as to its possibility and its utility. I presume, said I, that there would be no debate about its utility, no denial that the community of women and children would be the greatest good, supposing it possible. But I take it that its possibility or the contrary

greek_head: Περὶ ἀμφοτέρων ἦ δ' ὅς εὖ μάλ' ἂν ἀμφισβητηθείη Λέγεις ἦν δ' ἐγώ λόγων σύστασιν ἐγὼ δ' ᾤμην ἔκ γε
greek_tokens: peri amfoteros eimi de os eu mala an amfisbhtew legw eimi de egw logos sustasis egw de oiomai ek ge o eteros apodidraskw ei su dokew wfelimos eimi loipos de dh egw eimi peri o dunatew kai mh alla ou lanqanw eimi de os apodidraskw alla amfoteros peri didhmi logos ufekteon eimi de egw dikh tososde mentoi xarizomai
english_head: would be the chief topic of contention. Both, he said, would be right sharply debated. You mean, said I, that I have to meet a coalition of arguments. But I expected to escape from one of them, and that if you agreed that the thing was beneficial, it would remain for me to speak only of its feasibility. You have not escaped detection, he said, in your attempted flight, but you must render an account of both. I must pay the penalty, I said, yet do me this much grace:

greek_head: μοι ἔασόν με ἑορτάσαι ὥσπερ οἱ ἀργοὶ τὴν διάνοιαν εἰώθασιν ἑστιᾶσθαι ὑφ' ἑαυτῶν ὅταν μόνοι πορεύωνται καὶ
greek_tokens: egw eaw egw eortazw wsper e argos o dianoia eqw estiaw ufh eautou otan monos poreuw kai gar e toioutos pou prin eceuriskw tis tropos eimi tis os epiqumew outos parihmi ina mh kamnw bouleuw peri o dunatew kai mh tiqhmi ws uparxw eimi o boulomai hdh o loipas diatassw kai xairw dieceimi oios draw gignomai argos kai allos yuxh eti
english_head: Permit me to take a holiday, just as men of lazy minds are wont to feast themselves on their own thoughts when they walk alone. Such persons, without waiting to discover how their desires may be realized, dismiss that topic to save themselves the labor of deliberating about possibilities and impossibilities, assume their wish fulfilled, and proceed to work out the details in imagination, and take pleasure in portraying what they will do when it is realized, thus making still more idle a mind that is idle without that. I too now succumb to this weakness

greek_head: ἀργοτέραν ποιοῦντες ἤδη οὖν καὶ αὐτὸς μαλθακίζομαι καὶ ἐκεῖνα μὲν ἐπιθυμῶ ἀναβαλέσθαι καὶ ὕστερον ἐπισκέψασθαι
greek_tokens: argos poiew hdh oun kai autos malqakizomai kai ekeinos men epiqumew anaballw kai usteron episkeptomai h dunatos nun de ws dunaths eimi tiqhmi skeptomai an egw paraiew pws diatassw autos e arxw gignomai kai ostis pas sumforos an eaw prassw o te polis kai o fulac outos peiraw su proteros sundiaskopew usteron de ekeinos eiper parihmi alla parihmi fhmi kai skopaw oimh toinun eimi de egw eiper eimi e arxw acios
english_head: and desire to postpone and examine later the question of feasibility, but will at present assume that, and will, with your permission, inquire how the rulers will work out the details in practice, and try to show that nothing could be more beneficial to the state and its guardians than the effective operation of our plan. This is what I would try to consider first together with you, and thereafter the other topic, if you allow it. I do allow it, he said: proceed with the inquiry. I think, then, said I, that the rulers,

greek_head: τούτου τοῦ ὀνόματος οἵ τε τούτοις ἐπίκουροι κατὰ ταὐτά τοὺς μὲν ἐθελήσειν ποιεῖν τὰ ἐπιταττόμενα τοὺς δὲ ἐπιτάξειν
greek_tokens: outos o onoma e te outos epikouros kata tautos o men eqelw poiew o epitassw o de epitassw o men autos peiqw o nomos o de kai mimeomai osos an ekeinos epitrepw eoika fhmi su men toinun eimi de egw o nomoqeths autos wsper o anhr eklegw outws kai o gunh eklegw paradidwmi kaqa osos oios te omofuhs e de ate oikia te kai sussition koinos exw idios de oudeis oudeis toioutos ktaomai
english_head: if they are to deserve that name, and their helpers likewise, will, the one, be willing to accept orders, and the other, to give them, in some things obeying our laws, and imitating them in others which we leave to their discretion. Presumably. You, then, the lawgiver, I said, have picked these men and similarly will select to give over to them women as nearly as possible of the same nature. And they, having houses and meals in common, and no private possessions of that kind,

greek_head: ὁμοῦ δὴ ἔσονται ὁμοῦ δὲ ἀναμεμειγμένων καὶ ἐν γυμνασίοις καὶ ἐν τῇ ἄλλῃ τροφῇ ὑπ' ἀνάγκης οἶμαι τῆς
greek_tokens: omos dh eimi omos de anamignumi kai en gumnasion kai en o allh trefw upo anagkh oimh o emfutos agnumi pros o allhlwn meicis h ou anagkaion su dokazw legw ou gewmetrikos ge eimi de os alla erwtikos anagkh o kinduneuw ekeinos drimus eimi pros o peiqw te kai elkw o polus laos kai mala eipon alla meta dh outos eimi glauc ataktos men mignumi allhlwn h allos otioun poiew oute
english_head: will dwell together, and being commingled in gymnastics and in all their life and education, will be conducted by innate necessity to sexual union. Is not what I say a necessary consequence? Not by the necessities of geometry, he said, but by those of love, which are perhaps keener and more potent than the other to persuade and constrain the multitude. They are, indeed, I said; but next, Glaucon, disorder and promiscuity in these unions or

greek_head: ὅσιον ἐν εὐδαιμόνων πόλει οὔτ' ἐάσουσιν οἱ ἄρχοντες Οὐ γὰρ δίκαιον ἔφη
greek_tokens: osios en eudaimwn polis oute eaw e arxw ou gar dikaios fhmi dhlos dh ostis gamos o meta outos poiew ieros eis dunamis ostis malista eien de an ieros e wfelimos pantapasi men oun
english_head: in anything else they do would be an unhallowed thing in a happy state and the rulers will not suffer it. It would not be right, he said. Obviously, then, we must arrange marriages, sacramental so far as may be. And the most sacred marriages would be those that were most beneficial.

greek_head: Πῶς οὖν δὴ ὠφελιμώτατοι ἔσονται τόδε μοι λέγε ὦ Γλαύκων ὁρῶ γάρ σου ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ καὶ κύνας θηρευτικοὺς
greek_tokens: pws oun dh wfelimos eimi ode egw legos eimi glauc oraw gar su en o oikia kai kuwn qhreutikos kai o gennaios ornis mala suxnos ara oun eimi pros zeus prosexw tis o outos gamos te kai paidopoiia o poios fhmi proteros men autos outos kaiper eimi gennaios ara ou eimi tis kai gignomai aristos eimi poteros oun ek apas omoios gennaw h proqumeomai ostis malista ek o ariston ek o ariston
english_head: By all means. How, then, would the greatest benefit result? Tell me this, Glaucon. I see that you have in your house hunting-dogs and a number of pedigree cocks. Have you ever considered something about their unions and procreations? What? he said. In the first place, I said, among these themselves, although they are a select breed, do not some prove better than the rest? They do. Do you then breed from all indiscriminately, or are you careful to breed from the best?

greek_head: Τί δ' ἐκ τῶν νεωτάτων ἢ ἐκ τῶν γεραιτάτων ἢ ἐξ ἀκμαζόντων ὅτι μάλιστα
greek_tokens: tis de ek o neos h ek o geraios h ek akmazw ostis malista ek akmazw kai an mh outws gennaw polus su hgeomai xeirwn eimi o te o ornis kai o o kuwn genos egw fhmi tis de ippos oiomai eimi de egw kai o allos zwion eimi allh ph exw atopos mentoi eimi de os eaw babai eimi de egw eimi filos etairos ws ara sfodra egw deomai akron eimi o arxw eiper kai peri o o anqrwpos genos wsautws exis
english_head: From the best. And, again, do you breed from the youngest or the oldest, or, so far as may be, from those in their prime? From those in their prime. And if they are not thus bred, you expect, do you not, that your birds and hounds will greatly degenerate? I do, he said. And what of horses and other animals? I said; is it otherwise with them? It would be strange if it were, said he. Gracious, said I, dear friend, how imperative, then, is our need of the highest skill in our rulers, if the principle holds also for mankind.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὲν δὴ ἔχει ἔφη ἀλλὰ τί δή Ὅτι ἀνάγκη αὐτοῖς ἦν δ' ἐγώ φαρμάκοις πολλοῖς χρῆσθαι
greek_tokens: alla men dh exis fhmi alla tis dh ostis anagkh autos eimi de egw farmakon polus xraomai iatros de pou mh deomai men swma farmakon alla diaita eqelw upakouw kai faulos ecarkew ageomai eimi otan de dh kai farmakeuw deomai oida ostis andreios deomai o iatros alhqhs alla pros tis legw pros ode eimi de egw suxnazw o yeudw kai o apath kinduneuw egw dew xraomai o arxw epi wfeleia
english_head: Well, it does, he said, but what of it? This, said I, that they will have to employ many of those drugs of which we were speaking. We thought that an inferior physician sufficed for bodies that do not need drugs but yield to diet and regimen. But when it is necessary to prescribe drugs we know that a more enterprising and venturesome physician is required. True; but what is the pertinency? This, said I: it seems likely that our rulers will have to make considerable use of falsehood and deception

greek_head: τῶν ἀρχομένων ἔφαμεν δέ που ἐν φαρμάκου εἴδει πάντα τὰ τοιαῦτα χρήσιμα εἶναι
greek_tokens: o arxw fhmi de pou en farmakon eidomai pas o toioutos xrhsimos eimi kai orqos ge fhmi en o gamos toinun kai paidopoiia eoika o orqos outos gignomai ou elaxistos pws dh deomai men eipon ek o omologew o aristos o aristos suggignomai ws pleistakis o de faulos o faulos enantion kai o men o ekgonos trefw
english_head: for the benefit of their subjects. We said, I believe, that the use of that sort of thing was in the category of medicine. And that was right, he said. In our marriages, then, and the procreation of children, it seems there will be no slight need of this kind of right. How so? It follows from our former admissions, I said, that the best men must cohabit with the best women in as many cases as possible and the worst with the worst in the fewest,

greek_head: τῶν δὲ μή εἰ μέλλει τὸ ποίμνιον ὅτι ἀκρότατον εἶναι καὶ ταῦτα πάντα γιγνόμενα λανθάνειν πλὴν αὐτοὺς τοὺς ἄρχοντας
greek_tokens: o de mh ei mellw o poimnion ostis akros eimi kai outos pas gignomai lanqanw plhn autos o arxw ei au o agelh o fulac ostis malista astasiastos eimi orqos fhmi oukoun dh eorth tis nomoqethteos en os sunagnumi o te numfh kai o numfios kai qusia kai umnos
english_head: and that the offspring of the one must be reared and that of the other not, if the flock is to be as perfect as possible. And the way in which all this is brought to pass must be unknown to any but the rulers, if, again, the herd of guardians is to be as free as possible from dissension. Most true, he said. We shall, then, have to ordain certain festivals and sacrifices, in which we shall bring together the brides and the bridegrooms, and our poets must compose hymns

greek_head: ποιητέοι τοῖς ἡμετέροις ποιηταῖς πρέποντες τοῖς γιγνομένοις γάμοις τὸ δὲ πλῆθος τῶν γάμων ἐπὶ τοῖς ἄρχουσι ποιήσομεν
greek_tokens: poihteos o hmeteros poihths prepw o gignomai gamos o de plhqos o gamos epi o arxw poiew ina ws malista diaswzw o autos ariqmos o androw pros polemos te kai nosos kai pas o toioutos aposkopew kai mhte megas egw o polis kata o dunatos mhte mikros gignomai orqos fhmi klhros dh tis oimh poihteos komyos wste o faulos ekeinos aitiaomai epi ekastos sunercis tuxh alla mh o arxw kai mala fhmi
english_head: suitable to the marriages that then take place. But the number of the marriages we will leave to the discretion of the rulers, that they may keep the number of the citizens as nearly as may be the same, taking into account wars and diseases and all such considerations, and that, so far as possible, our city may not grow too great or too small. Right, he said. Certain ingenious lots, then, I suppose, must be devised so that the inferior man at each conjugation may blame chance and not the rulers. Yes, indeed, he said.

greek_head: Καὶ τοῖς ἀγαθοῖς γέ που τῶν νέων ἐν πολέμῳ ἢ ἄλλοθί που γέρα δοτέον καὶ ἆθλα ἄλλα τε καὶ ἀφθονεστέρα ἡ
greek_tokens: kai o agaqos ge pou o neos en polemos h alloqi pou geras doteos kai aqlon allos te kai afqonos o ecousia o o gunaikow sugkoimhsis ina kai ama meta profasis ws pleistos o pais ek o toioutos speirw orqos oukoun kai o aei gignomai ekgonos paralambanw o epi outos efisthmi arxh eite androw eite gunaikow eite amfoteros koinos men gar pou kai arxh gunh te kai anhr nai
english_head: And on the young men, surely, who excel in war and other pursuits we must bestow honors and prizes, and, in particular, the opportunity of more frequent intercourse with the women, which will at the same time be a plausible pretext for having them beget as many of the children as possible. Right. And the children thus born will be taken over by the officials appointed for this, men or women or both, since, I take it, the official posts too are common to women and men.

greek_head: Τὰ μὲν δὴ τῶν ἀγαθῶν δοκῶ λαβοῦσαι εἰς τὸν σηκὸν οἴσουσιν παρά τινας τροφοὺς χωρὶς οἰκούσας ἔν τινι μέρει
greek_tokens: o men dh o agaqos dokazw lambanw eis o shkos ferw para tis trofos xwris oikew en tis meros o polis o de o xeirwn kai ean tis o eteros anaphros gignomai en aporrhtos te kai adhlos katakruptw ws prepw eiper mellw fhmi kaqaros o genos o fulac eimi oukoun kai trefw outos epimeleomai o te mhthr epi o shkos agw otan spargaw pas mhxanh
english_head: The offspring of the good, I suppose, they will take to the pen or créche, to certain nurses who live apart in a quarter of the city, but the offspring of the inferior, and any of those of the other sort who are born defective, they will properly dispose of in secret, so that no one will know what has become of them. That is the condition, he said, of preserving the purity of the guardians’ breed. They will also supervise the nursing of the children, conducting the mothers to the pen when their breasts are full, but employing every device

greek_head: μηχανώμενοι ὅπως μηδεμία τὸ αὑτῆς αἰσθήσεται καὶ ἄλλας γάλα ἐχούσας ἐκπορίζοντες ἐὰν μὴ αὐταὶ ἱκαναὶ ὦσι καὶ
greek_tokens: mhxanaomai opws mhdeis o eautou aisqanomai kai allos gala exw ekporizw ean mh autos ikanos eimi kai autos outos epimeleomai opws metrios xronos qhlazw agrupnia de kai o allos ponos titqh te kai trefw paradidwmi polus rastwnh fhmi legw o paidopoiia o o fulac gunh prepw gar eimi de egw o de efechs dierxomai o protiqhmi fhmi gar dh ek akmazw dew o ekgonos gignomai alhqhs
english_head: to prevent anyone from recognizing her own infant. And they will provide others who have milk if the mothers are insufficient. But they will take care that the mothers themselves shall not suckle too long, and the trouble of wakeful nights and similar burdens they will devolve upon the nurses, wet and dry. You are making maternity a soft job for the women of the guardians. It ought to be, said I, but let us pursue our design. We said that the offspring should come from parents in their prime.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν σοι συνδοκεῖ μέτριος χρόνος ἀκμῆς τὰ εἴκοσι ἔτη γυναικί ἀνδρὶ δὲ τὰ τριάκοντα
greek_tokens: ara oun su sundokew metrios xronos akmazw o eikosi eths gunh andris de o triakonta o poios autos fhmi gunh men eimi de egw arxw apo eikosieths mexri tettarakontaetis tiktw o polis andris de epeidan o ocus dromos akmh pareimi o apo outos genna o polis mexri pentekaipenthkontaeths
english_head: True. Do you agree that the period of the prime may be fairly estimated at twenty years for a woman and thirty for a man? How do you reckon it? he said. The women, I said, beginning at the age of twenty, shall bear for the state to the age of forty, and the man shall beget for the state from the time he passes his prime in swiftness in running to the age of fifty-five.

greek_head: Ἀμφοτέρων γοῦν ἔφη αὕτη ἀκμὴ σώματός τε καὶ φρονήσεως
greek_tokens: amfoteros goun fhmi outos akmh swma te kai fronhsis oukoun ean presbus outos ean neos o eis o koinos gennhsis aptw oute osios oute dikaios fhmi o amarthma ws pais fituw o polis os an lanqanw gennaw ou upo qusia oude upo euxos fus os epi ekastos o gamos euxomai kai iereia kai iereus kai sumpas o polis ek agaqos ameinwn kai ek wfelimos
english_head: That is, he said, the maturity and prime for both of body and mind. Then, if anyone older or younger than the prescribed age meddles with procreation for the state, we shall say that his error is an impiety and an injustice, since he is begetting for the city a child whose birth, if it escapes discovery, will not be attended by the sacrifices and the prayers which the priests and priestesses and the entire city prefer at the ceremonial marriages, that ever better offspring may spring from good sires and from fathers helpful to the state

greek_head: ὠφελιμωτέρους ἀεὶ τοὺς ἐκγόνους γίγνεσθαι ἀλλ' ὑπὸ σκότου μετὰ δεινῆς ἀκρατείας γεγονώς
greek_tokens: wfelimos aei o ekgonos gignomai alla upo skotos meta deinazw akrateia gignomai orqos fhmi o autos de ge eipon nomos ean tis o eti gennaw mh sunerdw arxw aptw o en hlikia gunaikow noqos gar kai anegguos kai anieros fhmi autos pais o polis kaqisthmi orqos fhmi otan de dh oimh o te gunh kai e anhr o genna ekbazw o hlikia afihmi pou eleuqeros autos suggignomai
english_head: sons more helpful still. But this child will be born in darkness and conceived in foul incontinence. Right, he said. And the same rule will apply, I said, if any of those still within the age of procreation goes in to a woman of that age with whom the ruler has not paired him. We shall say that he is imposing on the state a base-born, uncertified, and unhallowed child. Most rightly, he said. But when, I take it, the men and the women have passed the age of lawful procreation, we shall leave the men free to form such relations

greek_head: ᾧ ἂν ἐθέλωσι πλὴν θυγατρὶ καὶ μητρὶ καὶ ταῖς τῶν θυγατέρων παισὶ καὶ ταῖς ἄνω μητρός καὶ γυναῖκας αὖ
greek_tokens: os an eqelw plhn qugathr kai mhthr kai o o qugathr pais kai o anoos mhthr kai gunh au plhn uios kai pathr kai o outos eis o katos kai epi o anoos kai outos ge hdh pas diakeleuomai proqumeomai malista men mhde eis faos ekferw kuhma mhde ge eis ean gignomai ean de tis biaw outws tiqhmi ws ou eimi trefw o toioutos kai outos men ge fhmi metrios legw pathr de kai
english_head: with whomsoever they please, except daughter and mother and their direct descendants and ascendants, and likewise the women, save with son and father, and so on, first admonishing them preferably not even to bring to light anything whatever thus conceived, but if they are unable to prevent a birth to dispose of it on the understanding that we cannot rear such an offspring. All that sounds reasonable, he said; but how are they to distinguish one another’s fathers and daughters,

greek_head: θυγατέρας καὶ ἃ νυνδὴ ἔλεγες πῶς διαγνώσονται ἀλλήλων Οὐδαμῶς ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἀλλ' ἀφ' ἧς ἂν ἡμέρας τις αὐτῶν
greek_tokens: qugathr kai o nundi legw pws diagignwskw allhlwn oudamos eimi de egw alla apo eis an hmeros tis autos numfios gignomai meta ekeinos dekatos meis kai ebdomos dh o an gignomai ekgonos outos pas proserew o men arshn uios o de qhlus qugathr kai ekeinos ekeinos pathr kai outws dh o outos ekgonos pais pais kai ekeinos au ekeinos pappos te kai thqh o de en ekeinos o xronos gignomai en os o mhthr kai e pathr autos gennaw
english_head: and the other degrees of kin that you have just mentioned? They won’t, said I, except that a man will call all male offspring born in the tenth and in the seventh month after he became a bridegroom his sons, and all female, daughters, and they will call him father. And, similarly, he will call their offspring his grandchildren and they will call his group grandfathers and grandmothers. And all children born in the period in which their fathers and mothers were procreating will regard one another as brothers and sisters.

greek_head: ἀδελφάς τε καὶ ἀδελφούς ὥστε ὃ νυνδὴ ἐλέγομεν ἀλλήλων μὴ ἅπτεσθαι ἀδελφοὺς δὲ καὶ ἀδελφὰς δώσει ὁ νόμος συνοικεῖν
greek_tokens: adelfh te kai adelfos wste o nundi legw allhlwn mh aptw adelfos de kai adelfh didwmi o nomos sunoikew ean o klhros outos sumpitnw kai o puqios prosanairew orqos eimi de os o men dh koinwnia eimi glauc outos te kai toioutos gunaikow te kai pais o fulac su o polis ws de epomai te o allh politeia kai makros beltistos deomai dh o meta outos bebaiow para o logos h pws poiew
english_head: This will suffice for the prohibitions of intercourse of which we just now spoke. But the law will allow brothers and sisters to cohabit if the lot so falls out and the Delphic oracle approves. Quite right, said he. This, then, Glaucon, is the manner of the community of wives and children among the guardians. That it is consistent with the rest of our polity and by far the best way is the next point that we must get confirmed

greek_head: Οὕτω νὴ Δία ἦ δ' ὅς Ἆρ' οὖν οὐχ ἥδε ἀρχὴ τῆς ὁμολογίας ἐρέσθαι ἡμᾶς
greek_tokens: outws nh dios eimi de os ara oun ou ode arxh o omologia eromai egw autos tis pote o megas agaqos exw eipon eis polis kataskeuh e deomai stoxazomai o nomoqeths tiqhmi o nomos kai tis megas kakos eita episkeptomai ara o nundi dierxomai eis men o o agaqos ixnos egw armozw o de o kakos anarmostew pas malista fhmi exw oun tis megas kakos polis h ekeinos o an autos
english_head: by the argument. Is not that so? It is, indeed, he said. Is not the logical first step towards such an agreement to ask ourselves what we could name as the greatest good for the constitution of a state and the proper aim of a lawgiver in his legislation, and what would be the greatest evil, and then to consider whether the proposals we have just set forth fit into the footprints of the good and do not suit those of the evil? By all means, he said. Do we know of any greater evil for a state than the thing that distracts it

greek_head: διασπᾷ καὶ ποιῇ πολλὰς ἀντὶ μιᾶς ἢ μεῖζον ἀγαθὸν τοῦ ὃ ἂν συνδῇ τε καὶ ποιῇ μίαν
greek_tokens: diaspaw kai poiew polus anti eis h megas agaqos o o an sundeomai te kai poiew eis ou exw oukoun o men hdona te kai luph koinwnia sundeomai otan ostis malista pas e poliths o autos gignomai te kai apollumi paraplhsios xairw kai lupew pantapasi men oun fhmi o de ge o toioutos idiwsis dialuw otan e men perialgew e de perixarhs gignomai epi o autos
english_head: and makes it many instead of one, or a greater good than that which binds it together and makes it one? We do not. Is not, then, the community of pleasure and pain the tie that binds, when, so far as may be, all the citizens rejoice and grieve alike at the same births and deaths? By all means, he said. But the individualization of these feelings is a dissolvent, when some grieve exceedingly and others rejoice at the same happenings

greek_head: παθήμασι τῆς πόλεώς τε καὶ τῶν ἐν τῇ πόλει Τί δ' οὔ
greek_tokens: paqhma o polis te kai o en o polis tis de ou ara oun ek ode o toiosde gignomai otan mh ama fqeggomai en o polis o toiosde rhma o te emos kai o ou emos kai peri o allotrios kata tautos komidh men oun en ostis dh polis pleistos epi o autos kata tautos outos legw o emos kai o ou emos outos ariston dioikew polus ge kai ostis dh eggus eis anqrwpos exis oios otan pou egw daktulos tis plhssw pas o koinwnia o kata o swma pros o yuxh teinw eis eis suntacis o o arxw
english_head: to the city and its inhabitants? Of course. And the chief cause of this is when the citizens do not utter in unison such words as mine and not mine, and similarly with regard to the word alien?Precisely so. That city, then, is best ordered in which the greatest number use the expression mine and not mine of the same things in the same way. Much the best. And the city whose state is most like that of an individual man. For example, if the finger of one of us is wounded, the entire community of bodily connections stretching to the soul for integration

greek_head: ἐν αὐτῇ ᾔσθετό τε καὶ πᾶσα ἅμα συνήλγησεν μέρους πονήσαντος ὅλη καὶ οὕτω δὴ λέγομεν ὅτι ὁ ἄνθρωπος τὸν
greek_tokens: en autos aisqanomai te kai pas ama sunalgew meros ponew oloc kai outws dh legw ostis o anqrwpos o daktulos algew kai peri allos ostis o o anqrwpos o autos logos peri te luph ponew meros kai peri hdona raizw o autos gar fhmi kai outos o erwtaw o toioutos eggus o ariston politeuw polis oikew eis dh oimh pasxw o poliths otioun h agaqos
english_head: with the dominant part is made aware, and all of it feels the pain as a whole, though it is a part that suffers, and that is how we come to say that the man has a pain in his finger. And for any other member of the man the same statement holds, alike for a part that labors in pain or is eased by pleasure. The same, he said, and, to return to your question, the best governed state most nearly resembles such an organism. That is the kind of a state,

greek_head: ἢ κακὸν ἡ τοιαύτη πόλις μάλιστά τε φήσει ἑαυτῆς εἶναι τὸ πάσχον καὶ ἢ συνησθήσεται ἅπασα ἢ συλλυπήσεται
greek_tokens: h kakos o toioutos polis malista te fhmi eautou eimi o pasxw kai h sunhdomai apas h sullupew anagkh fhmi o ge eunomos oraw an eaw eimi de egw epaneimi egw epi o hmeteros polis kai o o logos omologhma skopaw en autos ei autos malista exis eite kai allos tis mallon oukoun xrh fhmi
english_head: then, I presume, that, when anyone of the citizens suffers aught of good or evil, will be most likely to speak of the part that suffers as its own and will share the pleasure or the pain as a whole. Inevitably, he said, if it is well governed. It is time, I said, to return to our city and observe whether it, rather than any other, embodies the qualities agreed upon in our argument. We must, he said. Well, then,

greek_head: Τί οὖν ἔστι μέν που καὶ ἐν ταῖς ἄλλαις πόλεσιν ἄρχοντές τε καὶ δῆμος ἔστι δὲ καὶ ἐν ταύτῃ
greek_tokens: tis oun eimi men pou kai en o allos polis arxw te kai dhmos eimi de kai en outos eimi poliths men dh pas outos allhlwn proserew pws de ou alla pros o poliths tis o en o allos dhmos o arxw prosagoreuw en men o polus despoths en de o dhmokrateomai autos onoma outos arxw tis de o en o hmeteros dhmos pros o poliths tis o arxw fhmi eimi
english_head: there are to be found in other cities rulers and the people as in it, are there not? There are. Will not all these address one another as fellow-citizens? Of course. But in addition to citizens, what does the people in other states call its rulers. In most cities, masters. In democratic cities, just this, rulers. But what of the people in our city. In addition to citizens,

greek_head: Σωτῆράς τε καὶ ἐπικούρους ἔφη Τί δ' οὗτοι τὸν δῆμον
greek_tokens: swthr te kai epikouros fhmi tis de outos o dhmos misqodoths te kai trofeus e de en o allos arxw o dhmos doulos fhmi tis de e arxw allhlwn sunarxw fhmi tis de e hmeteros sumfulac exis oun eipon o arxw o en o allos polis ei tis tis exis proseipon o sunarxw o men ws oikew o de ws allotrios kai polus ge oukoun o men oikew ws eautou nomizw te kai legw
english_head: what do they call their rulers? Saviors and helpers, he said. And what term do these apply to the people? Payers of their wage and supporters. And how do the rulers in other states denominate the populace? Slaves, he said. And how do the rulers describe one another? Co-rulers, he said. And ours? Co-guardians. Can you tell me whether any of the rulers in other states would speak of some of their co-rulers as belonging and others as outsiders? Yes, many would. And such a one thinks and speaks of the one that belongs as his own, doesn’t he, and of the outsider as not his own? That is so. But what of your guardians. Could any of them think or speak of

greek_head: τὸν δ' ἀλλότριον ὡς οὐχ ἑαυτοῦ Οὕτω
greek_tokens: o de allotrios ws ou eautou outws tis de e para sos fulac esqw ostis autos exw an o sumfulac nomizw tis h proseipon ws allotrios oudamos fhmi pas gar os an entugxanw h ws adelfos h ws adelfh h ws pathr h ws mhthr h uios h qugathr h outos ekgonos h progonos nomizw entugxanw kallista eimi de egw legw alla eti kai ode eipon poteros autos o onoma monos oikeios nomoqethsis h
english_head: his co-guardian as an outsider? By no means, he said; for no matter whom he meets, he will feel that he is meeting a brother, a sister, a father, a mother, a son, a daughter, or the offspring or forebears of these. Excellent, said I; but tell me this further,

greek_head: καὶ τὰς πράξεις πάσας κατὰ τὰ ὀνόματα πράττειν περί τε τοὺς πατέρας ὅσα νόμος περὶ πατέρας αἰδοῦς τε πέρι καὶ
greek_tokens: kai o prassw passw kata o onoma prassw peri te o pathr osos nomos peri pathr aidw te peri kai khdemonia kai o uphkoon dew eimi o gonaw h mhte pros qea mhte pros anqrwpos autos ameinwn eimi ws oute osios oute dikaios prassw an ei allos prassw h outos outos su h allos fhmh ek apas o poliths umnew euqus peri o o pais ous kai peri pathr os an autos tis apofainw kai peri o allos suggenhs
english_head: will it be merely the names of this kinship that you have prescribed for them or must all their actions conform to the names in all customary observance toward fathers and in awe and care and obedience for parents, if they look for the favor of either gods or men, since any other behaviour would be neither just nor pious? Shall these be the unanimous oracular voices that they hear from all the people, or shall some other kind of teaching beset the ears of your children from their birth, both concerning what is due to those who are pointed out as their fathers

greek_head: Αὗται ἔφη γελοῖον γὰρ ἂν εἴη εἰ ἄνευ ἔργων οἰκεῖα ὀνόματα διὰ τῶν στομάτων μόνον φθέγγοιντο
greek_tokens: outos fhmi geloios gar an eaw ei aneu ergnumi oikeios onoma dia o stoma monos fqeggomai pas ara polis malista en autos sumfwnew eis tis h eu h kakos prassw o nundi legw o rhma o ostis o emos eu prassw h ostis o emos kakos alhqhs au eimi de os
english_head: and to their other kin? These, he said; for it would be absurd for them merely to pronounce with their lips the names of kinship without the deeds. Then, in this city more than in any other, when one citizen fares well or ill, men will pronounce in unison the word of which we spoke: It is mine that does well; it is mine that does ill. That is most true, he said.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν μετὰ τούτου τοῦ δόγματός τε καὶ ῥήματος ἔφαμεν συνακολουθεῖν τάς τε ἡδονὰς καὶ τὰς λύπας κοινῇ
greek_tokens: oukoun meta outos o dogma te kai rhma fhmi sunakolouqew o te hdona kai o luph koinh kai orqos ge fhmi oukoun malista o autos koinwnew egw e poliths o dh emos onomazw outos de koinwnew outws dh luph te kai hdona malista koinwnia exw polus ge ara oun outos aitios pros o allh kaqisthmi o o gunaikow te kai pais koinwnia o fulac polus men oun malista fhmi
english_head: And did we not say that this conviction and way of speech brings with it a community in pleasures and pains? And rightly, too. Then these citizens, above all others, will have one and the same thing in common which they will name mine, and by virtue of this communion they will have their pleasures and pains in common. Quite so. And is not the cause of this, besides the general constitution of the state, the community of wives and children among the guardians? It will certainly be the chief cause, he said.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν μέγιστόν γε πόλει αὐτὸ ὡμολογήσαμεν ἀγαθόν ἀπεικάζοντες εὖ οἰκουμένην πόλιν σώματι πρὸς μέρος αὑτοῦ
greek_tokens: alla meis megas ge polis autos omologew agaqos apeikazw eu oikew polis swma pros meros eautou luph te peri kai hdona ws exis kai orqos ge fhmi omologew o megas ara agaqos o polis aitios egw fainw o koinwnia o epikouros o te pais kai o gunaikow kai mala fhmi kai men dh kai o prosqen ge omologew fhmi gar pou oute oikia outos idios dew eimi oute gaia oute
english_head: But we further agreed that this unity is the greatest blessing for a state, and we compared a well governed state to the human body in its relation to the pleasure and pain of its parts. And we were right in so agreeing. Then it is the greatest blessing for a state of which the community of women and children among the helpers has been shown to be the cause. Quite so, he said. And this is consistent with what we said before. For we said, I believe, that these helpers must not possess houses of their own or

greek_head: τι κτῆμα ἀλλὰ παρὰ τῶν ἄλλων τροφὴν λαμβάνοντας μισθὸν τῆς φυλακῆς κοινῇ πάντας ἀναλίσκειν εἰ μέλλοιεν
greek_tokens: tis kthma alla para o allos trofh lambanw misqos o fulakeus koinh pas analiskw ei mellw ontws fulac eimi orqos fhmi ara oun ou osper legos o te prosqen erw kai o nun legw eti mallon apergazomai autos alhqinos fulac kai poiew mh diaspaw o polis o emos onomazw mh o autos alla allos allos o men eis o eautou oikia elkw ostis an dunamai xwris o allos ktaomai
english_head: land or any other property, but that they should receive from the other citizens for their support the wage of their guardianship and all spend it in common. That was the condition of their being true guardians. Right, he said. Is it not true, then, as I am trying to say, that those former and these present prescriptions tend to make them still more truly guardians and prevent them from distracting the city by referring mine not to the same but to different things, one man dragging off to his own house anything he is able to acquire apart from the rest,

greek_head: τὸν δὲ εἰς τὴν ἑαυτοῦ ἑτέραν οὖσαν καὶ γυναῖκά τε καὶ παῖδας ἑτέρους ἡδονάς τε καὶ ἀλγηδόνας ἐμποιοῦντας ἰδίων
greek_tokens: o de eis o eautou eteros eimi kai gunh te kai pais eteros hdona te kai alghdwn empoiew idios eimi idios alla eis dogma o oikew peri epi o autos teinw pas eis o dunatos omopaqew luph te kai hdona eimi komidh men oun fhmi tis de dikh te kai egklhma pros allhlwn ou oixomai ek autos ws epos eipon dia o mhdeis idios ktaomai plhn o swma o de allos koinos oqen dh uparxw
english_head: and another doing the same to his own separate house, and having women and children apart, thus introducing into the state the pleasures and pains of individuals? They should all rather, we said, share one conviction about their own, tend to one goal, and so far as practicable have one experience of pleasure and pain. By all means, he said. Then will not law-suits and accusations against one another vanish, one may say, from among them, because they have nothing in private possession but their bodies, but all else in common?

greek_head: τούτοις ἀστασιάστοις εἶναι ὅσα γε διὰ χρημάτων ἢ παίδων καὶ συγγενῶν κτῆσιν ἄνθρωποι στασιάζουσιν
greek_tokens: outos astasiastos eimi osos ge dia xrhma h pais kai suggenhs ktaomai anqrwpos stasiazw polus anagkh fhmi apallassw kai meis oude biaios ge oude aikia dikh dikaios an eien en autos hlic men gar alizw amunw kalos kai dikaios pou fhmi anagkh swma epimeleia tiqhmi orqos fhmi
english_head: So that we can count on their being free from the dissensions that arise among men from the possession of property, children, and kin. They will necessarily be quit of these, he said. And again, there could not rightly arise among them any law-suit for assault or bodily injury. For as between age-fellows we shall say that self-defence is honorable and just, thereby compelling them to keep their bodies in condition. Right, he said.

greek_head: Καὶ γὰρ τόδε ὀρθὸν ἔχει ἦν δ' ἐγώ οὗτος ὁ νόμος εἴ πού τίς τῳ θυμοῖτο ἐν τῷ τοιούτῳ πληρῶν τὸν θυμὸν ἧττον
greek_tokens: kai gar ode orqos exis eimi de egw outos o nomos ei pou tis tis qumow en o toioutos plhrhs o qumos hsswn epi megas an eimi isthmi panu men oun presbus meis neos pas arxw te kai kolazw prostassw dhlos kai meis ostis ge neos presbus an mh arxw prostassw oute allos biazw epixeirhsis pote oute tuptw ws o eoika oimh de oude allos atimaw ikanos gar o fulac kwluw deos te kai aidws aidws men ws
english_head: And there will be the further advantage in such a law that an angry man, satisfying his anger in such wise, would be less likely to carry the quarrel to further extremes. Assuredly. As for an older man, he will always have the charge of ruling and chastising the younger. Obviously. Again, it is plain that the young man, except by command of the rulers, will probably not do violence to an elder or strike him, or, I take it, dishonor him in any other way. Two guardians sufficient to prevent that

greek_head: γονέων μὴ ἅπτεσθαι εἴργουσα δέος δὲ τὸ τῷ πάσχοντι τοὺς ἄλλους βοηθεῖν τοὺς μὲν ὡς ὑεῖς τοὺς δὲ ὡς ἀδελφούς
greek_tokens: gonaw mh aptw ergw deos de o o pasxw o allos bohqew o men ws uios o de ws adelfos o de ws pathr sumbainw gar outws fhmi pantaxh dh ek o nomos eirhnh pros allhlwn e anhr agnumi polus ge outos meis en eautou mh stasiazw oudeis deinos mh pote o allos polis pros outos h pros allhlwn dixostatew ou gar oun o ge meis mikros o kakh dia aprepeia oknew
english_head: there are, fear and awe, awe restraining him from laying hands on one who may be his parent, and fear in that the others will rush to the aid of the sufferer, some as sons, some as brothers, some as fathers. That is the way it works out, he said. Then in all cases the laws will leave these men to dwell in peace together. Great peace. And if these are free from dissensions among themselves, there is no fear that the rest of the city will ever start faction against them or with one another. No, there is not.

greek_head: καὶ λέγειν ὧν ἀπηλλαγμένοι ἂν εἶεν κολακείας τε πλουσίων πένητες ἀπορίας τε καὶ ἀλγηδόνας ὅσας ἐν παιδοτροφίᾳ
greek_tokens: kai legw os apallassw an eien kolakeia te plousios penhs aporia te kai alghdwn osos en paidotrofia kai xrhmatismos dia trofh oikeths anagkh isxw o men daneizw o de ecarneomai o de pantws porizw tiqhmi para gunh te kai oikeths tamieuw paradidwmi osos te eimi filos peri autos kai oios pasxw dhlos te dh kai agennhs kai ou acios legw
english_head: But I hesitate, so unseemly are they, even to mention the pettiest troubles of which they would be rid, the flatterings of the rich, the embarrassments and pains of the poor in the bringing-up of their children and the procuring of money for the necessities of life for their households, the borrowings, the repudiations, all the devices with which they acquire what they deposit with wives and servitors to husband, and all the indignities that they endure in such matters, which are obvious and

greek_head: Δῆλα γάρ ἔφη καὶ τυφλῷ Πάντων τε δὴ τούτων ἀπαλλάξονται ζήσουσί τε τοῦ
greek_tokens: dhlos gar fhmi kai tuflos pas te dh outos apallassw zaw te o makarisths bios os e olumpionikai zaw makarios ph dia mikros pou meros eudaimonizw ekeinos os outos uparxw o te gar ode nikh kallos o su ek o dhmosios trofh teleios nikh te gar nikaw sumpas o polis swthria trefw te kai o allos pas osos bios deomai autos te kai pais anadew kai geras dexomai
english_head: ignoble and not deserving of mention. Even a blind man can see these, he said. From all these, then, they will be finally free, and they will live a happier life than that men count most happy, the life of the victors at Olympia. How so? The things for which those are felicitated are a small part of what is secured for these. Their victory is fairer and their public support more complete. For the prize of victory that they win is the salvation of the entire state, the fillet that binds their brows is the public support of themselves and their children—

greek_head: παρὰ τῆς αὑτῶν πόλεως ζῶντές τε καὶ τελευτήσαντες ταφῆς ἀξίας μετέχουσιν
greek_tokens: para o eautou polis zaw te kai teleutaw tafeus acios metexw kai mala fhmi kalos mimnhskw oun eimi de egw ostis en o prosqen ou oida ostis logos egw epiplhssw ostis o fulac ou eudaimwn
english_head: they receive honor from the city while they live and when they die a worthy burial. A fair guerdon, indeed, he said. Do you recall, said I, that in the preceding argument the objection of somebody or other rebuked us for not making our guardians happy,

greek_head: ποιοῖμεν οἷς ἐξὸν πάντα ἔχειν τὰ τῶν πολιτῶν οὐδὲν ἔχοιεν ἡμεῖς δέ που εἴπομεν ὅτι τοῦτο μέν εἴ που παραπίπτοι
greek_tokens: poiew os ecesti pas exw o o poliths oudeis exw egw de pou eipon ostis outos men ei pou parapiptw eis auqis skeptomai nun de o men fulac fulac poiew o de polis ws oios su eimi eudaimwn alla ou eis eis eqnos apoblepw en autos outos eudaimwn plassw mimnhskw fhmi tis oun nun egw o o epikouros bios eiper o ge o olumpionikwn polus te kallos kai ameinwn fainw
english_head: since, though it was in their power to have everything of the citizens, they had nothing, and we, I believe, replied that this was a consideration to which we would return if occasion offered, but that at present we were making our guardians guardians and the city as a whole as happy as possible, and that we were not modelling our ideal of happiness with reference to any one class? I do remember, he said. Well then, since now the life of our helpers has been shown to be fairer and better than that of the victors at Olympia,

greek_head: μή πῃ κατὰ τὸν τῶν σκυτοτόμων φαίνεται βίον ἤ τινων ἄλλων δημιουργῶν ἢ τὸν τῶν γεωργῶν
greek_tokens: mh ph kata o o skutotomos fainw bios h tis allos dhmiourgew h o o gewrgew ou egw dokew fhmi alla mentoi o ge kai ekei elegos dikaios kai entauqa eipon ostis ei outws o fulac epixeirhsis eudaimwn gignomai wste mhde fulac eimi mhde arkesis autos bios outws metrios kai bebaios kai ws egw fhmi aristos alla anohtos te kai meirakiwdhs doca empitnw eudaimonia peri ormhsis
english_head: need we compare it with the life of cobblers and other craftsmen and farmers? I think not, he said. But further, we may fairly repeat what I was saying then also, that if the guardian shall strive for a kind of happiness that will unmake him as a guardian and shall not be content with the way of life that is moderate and secure and, as we affirm, the best, but if some senseless and childish opinion about happiness shall beset him and impel him to use his power to appropriate

greek_head: αὐτὸν διὰ δύναμιν ἐπὶ τὸ ἅπαντα τὰ ἐν τῇ πόλει οἰκειοῦσθαι γνώσεται τὸν Ἡσίοδον ὅτι τῷ ὄντι ἦν σοφὸς λέγων πλέον
greek_tokens: autos dia dunamis epi o apas o en o polis oikeiow gignwskw o hsiodos ostis o eimi eimi sofos legos pleos eimi pws hmisus pas egw men fhmi sumboulos xraomai menw epi outos o bios sugxwrew ara eimi de egw o o gunaikow koinwnia o anhr os dierxomai paideia te peri kai pais kai fulakeus o allos poliths kata te polis menw eis polemos te eimi kai sumfulassw dew kai
english_head: everything in the city for himself, then he will find out that Hesiod was indeed wise, who said that the half was in some sort more than the whole. Hes. WD 40 If he accepts my counsel, he said, he will abide in this way of life. You accept, then, as we have described it, this partnership of the women with our men in the matter of education and children and the guardianship of the other citizens, and you admit that both within the city and when they go forth to war they ought to keep guard together and hunt together as it were like hounds,

greek_head: συνθηρεύειν ὥσπερ κύνας καὶ πάντα πάντῃ κατὰ τὸ δυνατὸν κοινωνεῖν καὶ ταῦτα πραττούσας τά τε βέλτιστα πράξειν καὶ
greek_tokens: sunqhreuw wsper kuwn kai pas panth kata o dunatos koinwnew kai outos prassw o te beltistos prassw kai ou para fus o o qhlus pros o arshn h fuw pros allhlwn koinwnew sugxwrew fhmi oukoun eimi de egw ekeinos loipos diairew ei ara kai en anqrwpos dunatos wsper en allos zwion outos o koinwnia eggignomai kai oph dunatos fqanw fhmi eipon h mellw upolambanw
english_head: and have all things in every way, so far as possible, in common, and that so doing they will do what is for the best and nothing that is contrary to female human nature in comparison with male or to their natural fellowship with one another. I do admit it, he said. Then, I said, is not the thing that it remains to determine this, whether, namely, it is possible for such a community to be brought about among men as it is in the other animals, and in what way it is possible? You have anticipated, he said, the point I was about to raise. For as for their wars, I said,

greek_head: Περὶ μὲν γὰρ τῶν ἐν τῷ πολέμῳ οἶμαι ἔφην δῆλον ὃν τρόπον πολεμήσουσιν
greek_tokens: peri men gar o en o polemos oimh fhmi dhlos os tropos polemew pws eimi de os ostis koinh strateuw kai pros ge agnumi o pais eis o polemos osos adroomai ina wsper e o allos dhmiourgew qeaomai outos o teleiow dehsis dhmiourgew
english_head: the manner in which they will conduct them is too obvious for discussion. How so, said he. It is obvious that they will march out together, and, what is more, will conduct their children to war when they are sturdy, in order that, like the children of other craftsmen, they may observe the processes of which they must be masters in their maturity; and in addition to looking on

greek_head: πρὸς δὲ τῇ θέᾳ διακονεῖν καὶ ὑπηρετεῖν πάντα τὰ περὶ τὸν πόλεμον καὶ θεραπεύειν πατέρας τε καὶ μητέρας ἢ οὐκ
greek_tokens: pros de o qea diakonew kai uphretew pas o peri o polemos kai qerapeuw pathr te kai mhthr h ou aisqanomai o peri o texnh oios o o kerameos pais ws polus xronos diakonew qewrew prin aptw o kerameuw kai mala eimi oun ekeinos epimelhs paideuteos h o fulac o eautou empeiria te kai qea o proshkw katagelastos mentoi fhmi eaw alla meis kai maxomai ge pas zwion diaferontws
english_head: they must assist and minister in all the business of war and serve their fathers and mothers. Or have you never noticed the practice in the arts, how for example the sons of potters look on as helpers a long time before they put their hands to the clay? They do, indeed. Should these then be more concerned than our guardians to train the children by observation and experience of what is to be their proper business? That would be ridiculous, he said. But, further, when it comes to fighting,

greek_head: παρόντων ὧν ἂν τέκῃ Ἔστιν οὕτω κίνδυνος δέ ὦ Σώκρατες οὐ σμικρὸς
greek_tokens: pareimi os an tiktw eimi outws kindunos de eimi swkraths ou mikros sfallw oios dh en polemos filew pros eautou pais apollumi poiew kai o allos polis adunatos analambanw alhqhs eimi de egw legw alla su proteros men hgeomai paraskeuasteos o mh pote kinduneuw oudamos tis de ei pou kinduneuteos ou en os beltiwn eimi katorqow dhlos dh
english_head: every creature will do better in the presence of its offspring? That is so, but the risk, Socrates, is not slight, in the event of disasters such as may happen in war, that, losing their children as well as themselves, they make it impossible for the remnant of the state to recover. What you say is true, I replied; but, in the first place, is it your idea that the one thing for which we must provide is the avoidance of all danger? By no means. And, if they are to take chances, should it not be for something success in which will make them better?

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ σμικρὸν οἴει διαφέρειν καὶ οὐκ ἄξιον κινδύνου θεωρεῖν ἢ μὴ τὰ περὶ τὸν πόλεμον παῖδας τοὺς ἄνδρας
greek_tokens: alla mikros oiomai diaferw kai ou acios kindunos qewrew h mh o peri o polemos pais o anhr polemikos eimi ou alla diaferw pros o legw outos men ara uparkteos qewros polemos o pais poiew prosmhxanaomai de autos asfaleia kai kalos ecis eimi gar nai oukoun eimi de egw proteros men autos e pathr osos anqrwpos ou amaqhs eimi alla gnwmonikos o
english_head: Clearly. Do you think it makes a slight difference and not worth some risk whether men who are to be warriors do or do not observe war as boys? No, it makes a great difference for the purpose of which you speak. Starting, then, from this assumption that we are to make the boys spectators of war, we must further contrive security for them and all will be well, will it not? Yes. To begin with, then, said I, will not the fathers be, humanly speaking, not ignorant of war

greek_head: στρατειῶν ὅσαι τε καὶ μὴ ἐπικίνδυνοι Εἰκός ἔφη
greek_tokens: strateia osos te kai mh epikindunos eoika fhmi eis men ara o agnumi eis de o eulabeomai orqos kai arxw ge pou eimi de egw ou o faulos autos efisthmi alla o empeiria te kai hlikia ikanos hgemwn te kai paidagwgos eimi prepw gar alla gar fhmi kai para doca polus polus dh gignomai kai mala pros toinun o toioutos eimi filos pterow xrh paidia eimi euqus ina an tis deomai petomai apofeugw
english_head: and shrewd judges of which campaigns are hazardous and which not? Presumably, he said. They will take the boys with them to the one and avoid the others? Rightly. And for officers, I presume, said I, they will put in charge of them not those who are good for nothing else but men who by age and experience are qualified to serve at once as leaders and as caretakers of children. Yes, that would be the proper way. Still, we may object, it is the unexpected that happens to many in many cases. Yes, indeed. To provide against such chances, then, we must wing the children from the start so that if need arises they may fly away and escape.

greek_head: Πῶς λέγεις ἔφη Ἐπὶ τοὺς ἵππους ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἀναβιβαστέον ὡς νεωτάτους
greek_tokens: pws legw fhmi epi o ippos eimi de egw anabibasteon ws neos kai didaskw ippeuw epi ippos akteon epi o qea mh qumoeidhs mhde maxhtikos alla ostis podwkhs kai euhnios outws gar kallista te qeaomai o eautou ergnumi kai asfalhs an tis deomai swzw meta presbus hgemwn epomai orqos fhmi egw dokew legw
english_head: What do you mean? he said. We must mount them when very young, said I, and first have them taught to ride, and then conduct them to the scene of war, not on mettlesome war-steeds, but on the swiftest and gentlest horses possible; for thus they will have the best view of their own future business and also, if need arises, will most securely escape to safety in the train of elder guides. I think you are right, he said.

greek_head: Τί δὲ δή εἶπον τὰ περὶ τὸν πόλεμον πῶς ἑκτέον σοι τοὺς στρατιώτας πρὸς αὑτούς τε καὶ τοὺς πολεμίους ἆρ'
greek_tokens: tis de dh eipon o peri o polemos pws ekteon su o stratiwths pros eautou te kai o polemios ara orqos egw katafainw h ou legos fhmi poa au autos men eipon o leipw tacis h oplon apoballw h tis o toioutos poiew dia kakh ara ou dhmiourgos tis deomai kaqisthmi h gewrgos panu men oun o de zaw eis o polemios aliskomai ara ou dwrea didwmi o airew xraomai o agra ostis an boulomai
english_head: But now what of the conduct of war? What should be the attitude of the soldiers to one another and the enemy? Am I right in my notions or not? Tell me what notions, he said. Anyone of them who deserts his post, or flings away his weapons, or is guilty of any similar act of cowardice, should be reduced to the artisan or farmer class, should he not? By all means. And anyone who is taken alive by the enemy we will make a present of to his captors, shall we not, to deal with their catch

And don’t you agree that the one who wins the prize of valor and distinguishes himself shall first be crowned by his fellows in the campaign, by the lads and boys each in turn? I do. And be greeted with the right hand? That, too. But I presume you wouldn’t go as far as this? What? That he should kiss and be kissed by everyone? By all means, he said, and I add to the law the provision that during that

Excellent, said I, and we have already said that the opportunity of marriage will be more readily provided for the good man, and that he will be more frequently selected than the others for participation in that sort of thing, in order that as many children as possible may be born from such stock. We have, he replied. But, furthermore, we may cite Homer

english_head: too for the justice of honoring in such ways the valiant among our youth. For Homer says that Ajax, who had distinguished himself in the war, was honored with the long chine, assuming that the most fitting meed for a brave man in the prime of his youth is that from which both honor and strength will accrue to him. Most rightly, he said. We will then, said I, take Homer as our guide in this at least. We, too, at sacrifices and on other like occasions, will reward the good so far as they have proved themselves good with hymns and the other privileges of which we have just spoken,

Very well; and of those who die on campaign, if anyone’s death has been especially glorious, shall we not, to begin with, affirm that he belongs to the golden race? By all means. That will surely be right, he said. But again, how will our soldiers conduct themselves toward enemies? In what respect? First, in the matter of making slaves of the defeated, do you think it right for Greeks to reduce Greek cities to slavery, or rather that so far as they are able, they should not suffer any other city to do so, but should accustom Greeks

english_head: to spare Greeks, foreseeing the danger of enslavement by the barbarians? Sparing them is wholly and altogether the better, said he. They are not, then, themselves to own Greek slaves, either, and they should advise the other Greeks not to? By all means, he said; at any rate in that way they would be more likely to turn against the barbarians and keep their hands from one another. And how about stripping the dead after victory of anything except their weapons: is that well? Does it not furnish a pretext to cowards

And don’t you think it illiberal and greedy to plunder a corpse, and is it not the mark of a womanish and petty spirit to deem the body of the dead an enemy when the real foeman has flown away and left behind only the instrument with which he fought?

english_head: Do you see any difference between such conduct and that of the dogs who snarl at the stones that hit them but don’t touch the thrower? Not the slightest. We must abandon, then, the plundering of corpses and the refusal to permit their burial. By heaven, we certainly must, he said. And again, we will not take weapons to the temples for dedicatory offerings, especially the weapons of Greeks,

english_head: if we are at all concerned to preserve friendly relations with the other Greeks. Rather we shall fear that there is pollution in bringing such offerings to the temples from our kind unless in a case where the god bids otherwise. Most rightly, he said. And in the matter of devastating the land of Greeks and burning their houses, how will your soldiers deal with their enemies. I would gladly hear your opinion of that. In my view,

english_head: said I, they ought to do neither, but confine themselves to taking away the annual harvest. Shall I tell you why? Do. In my opinion, just as we have the two terms, war and faction, so there are also two things, distinguished by two differentiae. The two things I mean are the friendly and kindred on the one hand and the alien and foreign on the other. Now the term employed for the hostility of the friendly is faction, and for that of the alien is war. What you say is in nothing beside the mark, he replied. Consider, then,

greek_head: Ὅρα δὴ καὶ εἰ τόδε πρὸς τρόπου λέγω φημὶ γὰρ τὸ μὲν Ἑλληνικὸν γένος αὐτὸ αὑτῷ οἰκεῖον εἶναι καὶ συγγενές

english_head: if this goes to the mark. I affirm that the Hellenic race is friendly to itself and akin, and foreign and alien to the barbarian. Rightly, he said. We shall then say that Greeks fight and wage war with barbarians, and barbarians with Greeks, and are enemies by nature, and that war is the fit name for this enmity and hatred. Greeks, however, we shall say, are still by nature the friends of Greeks when they act in this way, but that Greece is sick in that case and divided by faction,

english_head: and faction is the name we must give to that enmity. I will allow you that habit of speech, he said. Then observe, said I, that when anything of this sort occurs in faction, as the word is now used, and a state is divided against itself, if either party devastates the land and burns the houses of the other such factional strife is thought to be an accursed thing and neither party to be true patriots. Otherwise, they would never have endured thus to outrage their nurse and mother. But the moderate and reasonable thing is thought to be that the victors

english_head: shall take away the crops of the vanquished, but that their temper shall be that of men who expect to be reconciled and not always to wage war. That way of feeling, he said, is far less savage than the other. Well, then, said I, is not the city that you are founding to be a Greek city? It must be, he said. Will they then not be good and gentle? Indeed they will. And won’t they be philhellenes, lovers of Greeks, and will they not regard all Greece as their own and not renounce their part in the holy places common to all Greeks ? Most certainly. Will they not then regard any difference with Greeks

english_head: who are their own people as a form of faction and refuse even to speak of it as war? Most certainly. And they will conduct their quarrels always looking forward to a reconciliation? By all means. They will correct them, then, for their own good, not chastising them with a view to their enslavement or their destruction, but acting as correctors, not as enemies. They will, he said. They will not, being Greeks, ravage Greek territory nor burn habitations, and they will not admit that in any city all the population are their enemies, men, women and children, but will say that only a few at any time are their foes,

greek_head: καὶ διὰ ταῦτα πάντα οὔτε τὴν γῆν ἐθελήσουσιν κείρειν αὐτῶν ὡς φίλων τῶν πολλῶν οὔτε οἰκίας ἀνατρέπειν

english_head: those, namely, who are to blame for the quarrel. And on all these considerations they will not be willing to lay waste the soil, since the majority are their friends, nor to destroy the houses, but will carry the conflict only to the point of compelling the guilty to do justice by the pressure of the suffering of the innocent. I, he said, agree that our citizens ought to deal with their Greek opponents on this wise, while treating barbarians as Greeks now treat Greeks. Shall we lay down this law also, then,

english_head: dismissing everything else. This is a sudden assault, indeed, said I, that you have made on my theory, without any regard for my natural hesitation. Perhaps you don’t realize that when I have hardly escaped the first two waves, you are now rolling up against me the great third wave of paradox, the worst of all. When you have seen and heard that, you will be very ready to be lenient, recognizing that I had good reason after all for shrinking and fearing to enter upon the discussion of so paradoxical a notion. The more such excuses you offer, he said, the less

Oh, nothing, I replied, only this: if we do discover what justice is, are we to demand that the just man shall differ from it in no respect,

greek_head: ἐστίν ἢ ἀγαπήσομεν ἐὰν ὅτι ἐγγύτατα αὐτῆς ᾖ καὶ πλεῖστα τῶν ἄλλων ἐκείνης μετέχῃ

english_head: but shall conform in every way to the ideal? Or will it suffice us if he approximate to it as nearly as possible and partake of it more than others? That will content us, he said. A pattern, then, said I, was what we wanted when we were inquiring into the nature of ideal justice and asking what would be the character of the perfectly just man, supposing him to exist, and, likewise, in regard to injustice and the completely unjust man. We wished to fix our eyes upon them as types and models, so that whatever we discerned in them of happiness or the reverse would necessarily apply to ourselves

In that, he said, you speak truly. Do you think, then, that he would be any the less a good painter, who, after portraying a pattern of the ideally beautiful man and omitting no touch required for the perfection of the picture, should not be able to prove that it is actually possible for such a man to exist? Not I, by Zeus, he said. Then were not we,

greek_head: λόγῳ ἀγαθῆς πόλεως Πάνυ γε
greek_tokens: logos agaqos polis panu ge hsswn tis oun oiomai egw eu legw outos eneka ean mh exw apodeiknumi ws dunatos outws polis oikew ws legw ou dhta fhmi o men toinun alhqhs eimi de egw outws ei de dh kai outos proqumeomai deomai shs xaris apodeiknumi ph malista kai kata tis dunatos an eaw palin egw pros o toioutos apodeicis o autos diomologew o poios
english_head: as we say, trying to create in words the pattern of a good state? Certainly. Do you think, then, that our words are any the less well spoken if we find ourselves unable to prove that it is possible for a state to be governed in accordance with our words? Of course not, he said. That, then, said I, is the truth of the matter. But if, to please you, we must do our best to show how most probably and in what respect these things would be most nearly realized, again, with a view to such a demonstration, grant me the same point. What?

greek_head: Ἆρ' οἷόν τέ τι πραχθῆναι ὡς λέγεται ἢ φύσιν ἔχει πρᾶξιν λέξεως ἧττον ἀληθείας ἐφάπτεσθαι κἂν εἰ μή τῳ
greek_tokens: ara oios su tis prassw ws legw h fus exis pracis lecis hsswn alhqeia efaptw kan ei mh tis dokew alla su poteros omologew outws h ou omologew fhmi outos men dh mh anagkazw egw oios o logos dierxomai toioutos pantapasi kai o ergon dew gignomai an apofainw alla ean oios te gignomai euriskw ws an eggus o erw polis oikew fainw egw
english_head: Is it possible for anything to be realized in deed as it is spoken in word, or is it the nature of things that action should partake of exact truth less than speech, even if some deny it? Do you admit it or not? I do, he said. Then don’t insist, said I, that I must exhibit as realized in action precisely what we expounded in words. But if we can discover how a state might be constituted most nearly answering to our description, you must say that we have discovered that possibility of realization which you demanded.

greek_head: ἐξηυρηκέναι ὡς δυνατὰ ταῦτα γίγνεσθαι ἃ σὺ ἐπιτάττεις ἢ οὐκ ἀγαπήσεις τούτων τυγχάνων ἐγὼ μὲν γὰρ ἂν ἀγαπῴην
greek_tokens: eceuriskw ws dunatos outos gignomai o su epitassw h ou agaphsis outos tugxanw egw men gar an agapaw kai gar egw fhmi o de dh meta outos ws eoika peirw zhtew te kai apodeiknumi tis pote nun kakos en o polis prassw dia o ou outws oikew kai tis an mikros metaballw erxomai eis outos o tropos o politeia polis malista men eis ei de mh duo ei de mh ostis oligistos o ariqmos kai mikros o dunamis
english_head: Will you not be content if you get this? I for my part would. And I too, he said. Next, it seems, we must try to discover and point out what it is that is now badly managed in our cities, and that prevents them from being so governed, and what is the smallest change that would bring a state to this manner of government, preferably a change in one thing, if not, then in two, and, failing that, the fewest possible in number and the slightest in potency.

greek_head: Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν ἔφη Ἑνὸς μὲν τοίνυν ἦν δ' ἐγώ μεταβαλόντος δοκοῦμέν μοι
greek_tokens: pantapasi men oun fhmi eis men toinun eimi de egw metaballw dokew egw exw deiknumi ostis metapiptw an ou mentoi mikros ge oude radios dunatew de tis fhmi epi autos dh eimi de egw eimi o o megas proseikazw kuma eromai de oun ei kai mellw gelws te atexnos wsper kuma ekgelaw kai adocia katakluzw skopaw de o mellw legw legos fhmi ean mh eimi de egw h e filosofos basileuw en
english_head: By all means, he said. There is one change, then, said I, which I think that we can show would bring about the desired transformation. It is not a slight or an easy thing but it is possible. What is that? said he. I am on the very verge, said I, of what we likened to the greatest wave of paradox. But say it I will, even if, to keep the figure, it is likely to wash us away on billows of laughter and scorn. Listen. I am all attention, he said. Unless, said I, either philosophers become kings

greek_head: ταῖς πόλεσιν ἢ οἱ βασιλῆς τε νῦν λεγόμενοι καὶ δυνάσται φιλοσοφήσωσι γνησίως τε καὶ ἱκανῶς καὶ τοῦτο εἰς ταὐτὸν
greek_tokens: o polis h e basileus te nun legw kai dunasths filosofew gnhsios te kai ikanos kai outos eis tautos sumpitnw dunamis te politikos kai filosofia o de nun poreuw xwris epi ekateros o polus fusis ek anagkh apokleiw ou eimi kakh paula eimi filos glauc o polis dokazw de oude o anqrwpinos genos
english_head: in our states or those whom we now call our kings and rulers take to the pursuit of philosophy seriously and adequately, and there is a conjunction of these two things, political power and philosophic intelligence, while the motley horde of the natures who at present pursue either apart from the other are compulsorily excluded, there can be no cessation of troubles, dear Glaucon, for our states, nor, I fancy, for the human race either. Nor, until this happens, will this constitution which we have been expounding in theory

greek_head: οὐδὲ αὕτη ἡ πολιτεία μή ποτε πρότερον φυῇ τε εἰς τὸ δυνατὸν καὶ φῶς ἡλίου ἴδῃ ἣν νῦν λόγῳ διεληλύθαμεν
greek_tokens: oude outos o politeia mh pote proteros fuw te eis o dunatos kai faos hlios eidon os nun logos dierxomai alla outos eimi o egw pala oknos entiqhmi legw oraw ws polus para doca erw xalepos gar eidon ostis ou an allos tis eudaimonew oute idios oute dhmosios kai os eimi swkraths fhmi toioutos ekballw rhma te kai logos os eipon ageomai epi sos panu polus te kai
english_head: ever be put into practice within the limits of possibility and see the light of the sun. But this is the thing that has made me so long shrink from speaking out, because I saw that it would be a very paradoxical saying. For it is not easy to see that there is no other way of happiness either for private or public life. Whereupon he, Socrates, said he, after hurling at us such an utterance and statement as that, you must expect to be attacked by a great multitude of our men of light and leading, who forthwith will, so to speak, cast off their garments

greek_head: οὐ φαύλους νῦν οὕτως οἷον ῥίψαντας τὰ ἱμάτια γυμνοὺς λαβόντας ὅτι ἑκάστῳ παρέτυχεν ὅπλον θεῖν διατεταμένους
greek_tokens: ou faulos nun outws oios riptw o imation gumnos lambanw ostis ekastos paratugxanw oplon qew diateinw ws qaumasios ergazomai os ei mh amunw o logos kai ekfeucis o eimi twqazw didwmi dikh oukoun su egw eimi de egw outos aitios kalos ge fhmi egw poa alla o su ou prodidwmi alla amunw os dunamai dunamai de eunoia te kai o parakeleuomai kai isos an allos tis emmelhs su
english_head: and strip and, snatching the first weapon that comes to hand, rush at you with might and main, prepared to do dreadful deeds. And if you don’t find words to defend yourself against them, and escape their assault, then to be scorned and flouted will in very truth be the penalty you will have to pay. And isn’t it you, said I, that have brought this upon me and are to blame? And a good thing, too, said he; but I won’t let you down, and will defend you with what I can. I can do so with my good will and my encouragement, and perhaps I might answer your questions more suitably than another.

greek_head: ἀποκρινοίμην ἀλλ' ὡς ἔχων τοιοῦτον βοηθὸν πειρῶ τοῖς ἀπιστοῦσιν ἐνδείξασθαι ὅτι ἔχει ᾗ σὺ λέγεις
greek_tokens: apokrinw alla ws exw toioutos bohqos peiraw o apistew endeiknumi ostis exis h su legw peirateon eimi de egw epei kai su outws megas summaxia parexw anagkaion oun egw dokew ei mellw ph ekfeugw os legw diorizw pros autos o filosofos tis legw tolmaw fainw dew arxw ina diadhlos gignomai dunamai tis amunw endeiknumi
english_head: So, with such an aid to back you, try to make it plain to the doubters that the truth is as you say. I must try, I replied, since you proffer so strong an alliance. I think it requisite, then, if we are to escape the assailants you speak of, that we should define for them whom we mean by the philosophers, who we dare to say ought to be our rulers. When these are clearly discriminated it will be possible to defend ourselves by showing that to them by their very nature belong the study of philosophy

greek_head: ὅτι τοῖς μὲν προσήκει φύσει ἅπτεσθαί τε φιλοσοφίας ἡγεμονεύειν τ' ἐν πόλει τοῖς δ' ἄλλοις μήτε ἅπτεσθαι
greek_tokens: ostis o men proshkw fusis aptw te filosofia hgemoneuw su en polis o de allos mhte aptw akolouqew te o hgeomai oraw an eaw fhmi orizw eimi dh akolouqew egw ode ean autos amos ge ph ikanos echgeomai age fhmi anamimnhskw oun su eimi de egw dehsis h mimnhskw ostis os an fhmi filew tis deomai fainw autos ean orqos legw ou o men filew ekeinos o de mh alla pas stergw
english_head: and political leadership, while it befits the other sort to let philosophy alone and to follow their leader. It is high time, he said, to produce your definition. Come, then, follow me on this line, if we may in some fashion or other explain our meaning. Proceed, he said. Must I remind you, then, said I, or do you remember, that when we affirm that a man is a lover of something, it must be apparent that he is fond of all of it? It will not do to say that some of it he likes and some does not. I think you will have to remind me, he said,

greek_head: Ἀναμιμνῄσκειν ἔφη ὡς ἔοικεν δεῖ οὐ γὰρ πάνυ γε ἐννοῶ
greek_tokens: anamimnhskw fhmi ws eoika deomai ou gar panu ge ennoew allos eipon prepw eimi glauc legw o legw andris de erwtikos ou prepw amnhmonew ostis pas e en wra o filopais kai erwtikos amos ge ph daknw te kai kinew dokew acios eimi epimeleia te kai o aspazomai h ou outws poiew pros o kalos o men ostis simos epixaris kalew epainew ufh su o de o grupos basilikos fhmi eimi o de dh dia
english_head: for I don’t apprehend at all. That reply, Glaucon, said I, befitted another rather than you. It does not become a lover to forget that all adolescents in some sort sting and stir the amorous lover of youth and appear to him deserving of his attention and desirable. Is not that your reaction to the fair? One, because his nose is tip-tilted, you will praise as piquant, the beak of another you pronounce right-royal, the intermediate type you say strikes the harmonious mean,

greek_head: μέσου τούτων ἐμμετρώτατα ἔχειν μέλανας δὲ ἀνδρικοὺς ἰδεῖν λευκοὺς δὲ θεῶν παῖδας εἶναι μελιχλώρους δὲ καὶ
greek_tokens: meshs outos emmetros exw melas de andrikos eidon leukos de qea pais eimi melixlwros de kai onoma oiomai tis allos poihma eimi h erasths upokorizomai te kai euxerhs ferw o wxroths ean epi wra eimi kai eis logos passw profasis profasizomai te
english_head: the swarthy are of manly aspect, the white are children of the gods divinely fair, and as for honey-hued, do you suppose the very word is anything but the euphemistic invention of some lover who can feel no distaste for sallowness when it accompanies the blooming time of youth? And, in short, there is no pretext

greek_head: καὶ πάσας φωνὰς ἀφίετε ὥστε μηδένα ἀποβάλλειν τῶν ἀνθούντων ἐν ὥρᾳ
greek_tokens: kai passw fwnh afihmi wste mhdeis apoballw o anqew en wra ei boulomai fhmi epi egw legw peri o erwtikos ostis outws poiew sugxwrew o logos xaris tis de eimi de egw o filoinos ou o autos outos poiew oraw pas oinon epi pas profasis aspazomai kai mala kai meis filotimos ge ws oimh kaqoraw ostis an mh strathgew dunamai trittuarxew kan mh upo megas
english_head: you do not allege and there is nothing you shrink from saying to justify you in not rejecting any who are in the bloom of their prime. If it is your pleasure, he said, to take me as your example of this trait in lovers, I admit it for the sake of the argument. Again, said I, do you not observe the same thing in the lovers of wine? They welcome every wine on any pretext. They do, indeed. And so I take it you have observed that men who are covetous of honor, if they can’t get themselves elected generals, are captains of a company. And if they can’t be honored

greek_head: καὶ σεμνοτέρων τιμᾶσθαι ὑπὸ σμικροτέρων καὶ φαυλοτέρων τιμώμενοι ἀγαπῶσιν ὡς ὅλως τιμῆς ἐπιθυμηταὶ ὄντες
greek_tokens: kai semnos timaw upo mikros kai faulos timaw agapaw ws oloc timaw epiqumhths eimi komidh men oun outos dh fhmi h mh ara os an tis epiqumhtikos legw pas o eidos outos fhmi epiqumew h o men o de ou pas fhmi oukoun kai o filosofos sofia fhmi epiqumhths eimi ou o men o de ou alla pas alhqhs o ara peri o maqhma dusxerainw allos te
english_head: by great men and dignitaries, are satisfied with honor from little men and nobodies. But honor they desire and must have. Yes, indeed. Admit, then, or reject my proposition. When we say a man is keen about something, shall we say that he has an appetite for the whole class or that he desires only a part and a part not? The whole, he said. Then the lover of wisdom, too, we shall affirm, desires all wisdom, not a part and a part not.

greek_head: καὶ νέον ὄντα καὶ μήπω λόγον ἔχοντα τί τε χρηστὸν καὶ μή οὐ φήσομεν φιλομαθῆ οὐδὲ φιλόσοφον εἶναι ὥσπερ
greek_tokens: kai neos eimi kai mhpw logos exw tis te xrhstos kai mh ou fhmi filomaqhs oude filosofos eimi wsper o peri o sition dusxerhs oute peinaw fhmi oute epiqumew siteomai oude filositos alla kakositos eimi kai orqos ge fhmi o de dh euxerhs eqelw pas maqhma geuw kai asmenos epi o manqanw eimi kai aplhstos exw outos de en dikh fhmi filosofos eimi gar
english_head: Certainly. The student, then, who is finical about his studies, especially when he is young and cannot yet know by reason what is useful and what is not, we shall say is not a lover of learning or a lover of wisdom, just as we say that one who is dainty about his food is not really hungry, has not an appetite for food, and is not a lover of food, but a poor feeder. We shall rightly say so. But the one who feels no distaste in sampling every study, and who attacks his task of learning gladly and cannot get enough of it, him we shall justly pronounce the lover of wisdom, the philosopher, shall we not? To which Glaucon replied,

greek_head: Καὶ ὁ Γλαύκων ἔφη Πολλοὶ ἄρα καὶ ἄτοποι ἔσονταί σοι τοιοῦτοι οἵ τε γὰρ φιλοθεάμονες πάντες ἔμοιγε δοκοῦσι τῷ
greek_tokens: kai o glauc fhmi polus ara kai atopos eimi su toioutos e te gar filoqeamwn pas egw dokew o katamanqanw xairw toioutos eimi e te filhkoos atopos tis eimi os ge en filosofos tiqhmi e pros men logos kai toioutos diatribh ekwn ou an eqelw erxomai wsper de apomisqow o ous epakouw pas xoros periqew o dionusia oute o kata polis oute o kata kwmh apoleipw outos oun pas kai allos toioutos tis
english_head: You will then be giving the name to a numerous and strange band, for all the lovers of spectacles are what they are, I fancy, by virtue of their delight in learning something. And those who always want to hear some new thing are a very queer lot to be reckoned among philosophers. You couldn’t induce them to attend a serious debate or any such entertainment, but as if they had farmed out their ears to listen to every chorus in the land, they run about to all the Dionysiac festivals, never missing one, either in the towns or in the country-villages. Are we to designate all these, then, and similar folk

greek_head: μαθητικοὺς καὶ τοὺς τῶν τεχνυδρίων φιλοσόφους φήσομεν Οὐδαμῶς εἶπον ἀλλ' ὁμοίους μὲν φιλοσόφοις
greek_tokens: maqhtikos kai o o texnudrion filosofos fhmi oudamos eipon alla omoios men filosofos o de alhqinos fhmi tis legw o o alhqeia eimi de egw filoqeamwn kai outos men ge fhmi orqos alla pws autos legw oudamos eimi de egw radios pros ge allos sos de oimh omologew egw o toiosde o poios epei eimi enantion kalos aisxros duo autos eimi
english_head: and all the practitioners of the minor arts as philosophers? Not at all, I said; but they do bear a certain likeness to philosophers. Whom do you mean, then, by the true philosophers? Those for whom the truth is the spectacle of which they are enamored, said I. Right again, said he; but in what sense do you mean it? It would be by no means easy to explain it to another, I said, but I think that you will grant me this. What? That since the fair and honorable is the opposite of the base and ugly, they are two.

greek_head: Πῶς δ' οὔ Οὐκοῦν ἐπειδὴ δύο καὶ ἓν ἑκάτερον
greek_tokens: pws de ou oukoun epei duo kai eis ekateros kai outos kai peri dh dikaios kai adikos kai agaqos kai kakos kai pas o eidos peri o autos logos autos men eis ekastos eimi o de o pracis kai swma kai allhlwn koinwnia pantaxou fantazomai polus fainw ekastos orqos fhmi legw outos toinun eimi de egw diairew xwris men os nundi legw filoqeamwn te kai filotexnos kai praktikos
english_head: Of course. And since they are two, each is one. That also. And in respect of the just and the unjust, the good and the bad, and all the ideas or forms, the same statement holds, that in itself each is one, but that by virtue of their communion with actions and bodies and with one another they present themselves everywhere, each as a multiplicity of aspects. Right, he said. This, then, said I, is my division. I set apart and distinguish those of whom you were just speaking, the lovers of spectacles and the arts,

greek_head: καὶ χωρὶς αὖ περὶ ὧν ὁ λόγος οὓς μόνους ἄν τις ὀρθῶς προσείποι φιλοσόφους
greek_tokens: kai xwris au peri os o logos os monos an tis orqos proseipon filosofos pws fhmi legw e men pou eimi de egw filhkoos kai filoqeamwn o te kalos fwnh aspazomai kai xroia kai sxhma kai pas o ek o toioutos dhmiourgew autos de o kalew adunatos autos o dianoia o fus eidon te kai aspazomai exis gar oun dh fhmi outws e de dh epi autos o kalos dunatos eimi te kai oraw kaqa eautou ara ou spanios an eien
english_head: and men of action, and separate from them again those with whom our argument is concerned and who alone deserve the appellation of philosophers or lovers of wisdom. What do you mean? he said. The lovers of sounds and sights, I said, delight in beautiful tones and colors and shapes and in everything that art fashions out of these, but their thought is incapable of apprehending and taking delight in the nature of the beautiful in itself. Why, yes, he said, that is so. And on the other hand, will not those be few who would be able to approach beauty itself and contemplate it in and by itself?

greek_head: Καὶ μάλα Ὁ οὖν καλὰ μὲν πράγματα νομίζων αὐτὸ δὲ κάλλος
greek_tokens: kai mala o oun kalos men pragma nomizw autos de kallos mhte nomizw mhte an tis hgeomai epi o gignwskw autos dunamai epomai onar h upar dokew su zaw skopaw de o oneirwssw ara ou ode eimi ean en upnon tis ean egeirw o omoios tis mh omoios alla autos hgeomai eimi os eoika egw goun an eimi de os fhmi oneirwssw o toioutos tis de o enantios outos hgeomai su tis autos kalos
english_head: They would, indeed. He, then, who believes in beautiful things, but neither believes in beauty itself nor is able to follow when someone tries to guide him to the knowledge of it—do you think that his life is a dream or a waking? Just consider. Is not the dream state, whether the man is asleep or awake, just this: the mistaking of resemblance for identity? I should certainly call that dreaming, he said. Well, then, take the opposite case: the man whose thought recognizes a beauty in itself,

greek_head: καὶ δυνάμενος καθορᾶν καὶ αὐτὸ καὶ τὰ ἐκείνου μετέχοντα καὶ οὔτε τὰ μετέχοντα αὐτὸ οὔτε αὐτὸ τὰ μετέχοντα
