greek_tokens: kai dunamai kaqoraw kai autos kai o ekeinos metexw kai oute o metexw autos oute autos o metexw hgeomai upar h onar au kai outos dokew su zaw kai mala fhmi upar oukoun outos men o dianoia ws gignwskw gnwmh an orqos fhmi eimi o de doca ws docazw panu men oun tis oun ean egw xalepainw outos os fhmi docazw alla ou gignwskw kai amfisbhtew ws ou alhqhs legw
english_head: and is able to distinguish that self-beautiful and the things that participate in it, and neither supposes the participants to be it nor it the participants—is his life, in your opinion, a waking or a dream state? He is very much awake, he replied. Could we not rightly, then, call the mental state of the one as knowing, knowledge, and that of the other as opining, opinion? Assuredly. Suppose, now, he who we say opines but does not know should be angry and challenge our statement as not true.

greek_head: ἕξομέν τι παραμυθεῖσθαι αὐτὸν καὶ πείθειν ἠρέμα ἐπικρυπτόμενοι ὅτι οὐχ ὑγιαίνει
greek_tokens: exw tis paramuqeomai autos kai peiqw hrema epikruptw ostis ou ugiainw deomai ge su dh fhmi eimi dh skopaw tis erew pros autos h boulomai wde punqanomai para autos legw ws ei tis oida oudeis autos fqonos alla asmenos an eidon oida tis alla egw eipon ode o gignwskw gignwskw tis h oudeis su oun egw uper ekeinos apokrinw apokrinw fhmi ostis gignwskw tis poteros eimi h ou eimi
english_head: Can we find any way of soothing him and gently winning him over, without telling him too plainly that he is not in his right mind? We must try, he said. Come, then, consider what we are to say to him, or would you have us question him in this fashion—premising that if he knows anything, nobody grudges it him, but we should be very glad to see him knowing something—but tell us this: Does he who knows know something or nothing? Do you reply in his behalf. I will reply, he said, that he knows something. Is it something that is or is not? That is. How could

greek_head: Ὄν πῶς γὰρ ἂν μὴ ὄν γέ τι γνωσθείη Ἱκανῶς οὖν τοῦτο ἔχομεν κἂν εἰ πλεοναχῇ σκοποῖμεν
greek_tokens: eimi pws gar an mh eimi ge tis gignwskw ikanos oun outos exw kan ei pleonaxh skopaw ostis o men pantelhs eimi pantelhs gnwstos mh eimi de mhdamh panth agnwstos ikanos eien ei de dh tis outws exis ws eimi te kai mh eimi ou metacu an kew o eilikrinhs eimi kai o au mhdamh eimi metacu oukoun epi men o eimi gnwsis eimi agnwsia de ek anagkh epi mh eimi epi de o metacu outos metacu tis kai zhthteos
english_head: that which is not be known? We are sufficiently assured of this, then, even if we should examine it from every point of view, that that which entirelyis is entirely knowable, and that which in no way is is in every way unknowable. Most sufficiently. Good. If a thing, then, is so conditioned as both to be and not to be, would it not lie between that which absolutely and unqualifiedly is and that which in no way is? Between. Then if knowledge pertains to that which is and ignorance of necessity to that which is not,

greek_head: ἀγνοίας τε καὶ ἐπιστήμης εἴ τι τυγχάνει ὂν τοιοῦτον Πάνυ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: agnoia te kai episthmh ei tis tugxanw eimi toioutos panu men oun ara oun legw tis doca eimi pws gar ou poteros allos dunamis episthmh h o autos allos epi allos ara tassw doca kai epi allos episthmh kata o dunamis ekateros o eautou outws oukoun episthmh men epi o eimi fuw gignwskw ws eimi o eimi mallon de wde egw dokew proteros anagkaion eimi diairew pws
english_head: for that which lies between we must seek for something between nescience and science, if such a thing there be. By all means. Is there a thing which we call opinion? Surely. Is it a different faculty from science or the same? A different. Then opinion is set over one thing and science over another, each by virtue of its own distinctive power or faculty. That is so. May we say, then, that science is naturally related to that which is, to know that and how that which is is? But rather, before we proceed, I think we must draw the following distinctions. What ones?

greek_head: Φήσομεν δυνάμεις εἶναι γένος τι τῶν ὄντων αἷς δὴ καὶ ἡμεῖς δυνάμεθα ἃ δυνάμεθα καὶ ἄλλο πᾶν ὅτι περ ἂν δύνηται
greek_tokens: fhmi dunamis eimi genos tis o eimi os dh kai egw dunamai o dunamai kai allos pas ostis per an dunamai oios legos oyis kai akoh o dunamis eimi ei ara manqanw o boulomai legw o eidos alla manqanw fhmi akouw dh o egw fainw peri autos dunamis gar egw oute tis xroia oraw oute sxhma oute tis o toioutos oios kai allos polus pros o apoblepw enioi diorizw para emautou o men allos eimi o de allos dunamis
english_head: Shall we say that faculties, powers, abilities are a class of entities by virtue of which we and all other things are able to do what we or they are able to do? I mean that sight and hearing, for example, are faculties, if so be that you understand the class or type that I am trying to describe. I understand, he said. Hear, then, my notion about them. In a faculty I cannot see any color or shape or similar mark such as those on which in many other cases I fix my eyes in discriminating in my thought one thing

greek_head: δ' εἰς ἐκεῖνο μόνον βλέπω ἐφ' ᾧ τε ἔστι καὶ ὃ ἀπεργάζεται καὶ ταύτῃ ἑκάστην αὐτῶν δύναμιν ἐκάλεσα καὶ τὴν μὲν
greek_tokens: de eis ekeinos monos blepw epi os te eimi kai o apergazomai kai outos ekastos autos dunamis kalew kai o men epi o autos tassw kai o autos apergazomai o autos kalew o de epi eteros kai eteros apergazomai allos tis de su pws poiew outws fhmi deuro dh palin eimi de egw eimi aristos episthmh poteros dunamis tis fhmi eimi autos h eis tis genos tiqhmi eis outos fhmi pas ge dunamis errwmenos
english_head: from another. But in the case of a faculty I look to one thing only—that to which it is related and what it effects, and it is in this way that I come to call each one of them a faculty, and that which is related to the same thing and accomplishes the same thing I call the same faculty, and that to another I call other. How about you, what is your practice? The same, he said. To return, then, my friend, said I, to science or true knowledge, do you say that it is a faculty and a power,

greek_head: Τί δέ δόξαν εἰς δύναμιν ἢ εἰς ἄλλο εἶδος οἴσομεν Οὐδαμῶς ἔφη ᾧ γὰρ δοξάζειν δυνάμεθα οὐκ ἄλλο τι
greek_tokens: tis de doca eis dunamis h eis allos eidos ferw oudamos fhmi os gar docazw dunamai ou allos tis h doca eimi alla men dh oligos ge proteros omologew mh o autos eimi episthmh te kai doca pws gar an fhmi o ge anamarthtos o mh anamarthtos tautos tis new exw tiqhmi kalos eimi de egw kai dhlos ostis eteros episthmh doca
english_head: or in what class do you put it? Into this, he said, the most potent of all faculties. And opinion—shall we assign it to some other class than faculty. By no means, he said, for that by which we are able to opine is nothing else than the faculty of opinion. But not long ago you agreed that science and opinion are not identical. How could any rational man affirm the identity of the infallible with the fallible? Excellent, said I, and we are plainly agreed

greek_head: ὁμολογεῖται ἡμῖν Ἕτερον
greek_tokens: omologew egw eteros epi eteros ara eteros tis dunamai ekateros autos fuw anagkh episthmh men ge pou epi o eimi o eimi gignwskw ws exis nai doca de fhmi docazw nai eimi tautos osper episthmh gignwskw kai eimi gnwstos te kai docastos o autos h adunatos adunatos fhmi ek o omologew eiper epi allos allos dunamis fuw dunamis de amfoteros eimi doca te
english_head: that opinion is a different thing from scientific knowledge. Yes, different. Each of them, then, since it has a different power, is related to a different object. Of necessity. Science, I presume, to that which is, to know the condition of that which is. But opinion, we say, opines. Yes. Does it opine the same thing that science knows, and will the knowable and the opinable be identical, or is that impossible? Impossible by our admissions, he said. If different faculties are naturally related to different objects

greek_head: καὶ ἐπιστήμη ἄλλη δὲ ἑκατέρα ὥς φαμεν ἐκ τούτων δὴ οὐκ ἐγχωρεῖ γνωστὸν καὶ δοξαστὸν ταὐτὸν εἶναι
greek_tokens: kai episthmh allos de ekateros os fhmi ek outos dh ou egxwrew gnwstos kai docastos tautos eimi oukoun ei o eimi gnwstos allos tis an docastos h o eimi eaw allos ara oun o mh eimi docazw h adunatos kai docazw o ge mh eimi ennoew de ou o docazw epi tis ferw o doca h oios te au docazw men docazw de mhdeis adunatos alla eis ge tis docazw o docazw nai alla meis mh eimi ge ou eis tis alla mhdeis orqos an
english_head: and both opinion and science are faculties, but each different from the other, as we say—these admissions do not leave place for the identity of the knowable and the opinable. Then, if that which is is knowable, something other than that which is would be the opinable. Something else. Does it opine that which is not, or is it impossible even to opine that which is not? Reflect: Does not he who opines bring his opinion to bear upon something or shall we reverse ourselves and say that it is possible to opine, yet opine nothing? That is impossible. Then he who opines opines some one thing. Yes. But surely that which is not could not be designated as some one thing, but

greek_head: προσαγορεύοιτο Πάνυ γε
greek_tokens: prosagoreuw panu ge mh eimi meis agnoia ek anagkh apodidwmi eimi de gignwskw orqos fhmi ou ara eimi oude mh eimi docazw ou gar oute ara agnoia oute gnwsis doca an eaw ou eoika ara oun ektos outos eimi uperbainw h gignwskw safhneia h agnoia asafeia oudeteros alla ara eimi de egw gnwsis men su fainw doca skotwdhs agnoia de fanos kai polus ge fhmi
english_head: most rightly as nothing at all. To that which is not we of necessity assigned nescience, and to that which is, knowledge. Rightly, he said. Then neither that which is nor that which is not is the object of opinion. It seems not. Then opinion would be neither nescience nor knowledge. So it seems. Is it then a faculty outside of these, exceeding either knowledge in lucidity or ignorance in obscurity? It is neither. But do you deem opinion something darker than knowledge but brighter than ignorance? Much so, he said. And does it lie within the boundaries

greek_head: Ἐντὸς δ' ἀμφοῖν κεῖται Ναί
greek_tokens: entos de amfw kew nai metacu ara an eaw outos doca komidh men oun oukoun fhmi en o prosqen ei tis fainw oios ama eimi te kai mh eimi o toioutos metacu kew o eilikrinhs eimi te kai o pantws mh eimi kai oute episthmh oute agnoia epi autos eimi alla o metacu au fainw agnoia kai episthmh orqos nun de ge fainw metacu outos o dh kalew doca fainw
english_head: of the two? Yes. Then opinion would be between the two. Most assuredly. Were we not saying a little while ago that if anything should turn up such that it both is and is not, that sort of thing would lie between that which purely and absolutely is and that which wholly is not, and that the faculty correlated with it would be neither science or nescience, but that which should appear to hold a place correspondingly between nescience and science. Right. And now there has turned up between these two the thing that we call opinion. There has.

greek_head: Ἐκεῖνο δὴ λείποιτ' ἂν ἡμῖν εὑρεῖν ὡς ἔοικε τὸ ἀμφοτέρων μετέχον τοῦ εἶναί τε καὶ μὴ εἶναι καὶ οὐδέτερον εἰλικρινὲς
greek_tokens: ekeinos dh leipw an egw euriskw ws eoika o amfoteros metexw o eimi te kai mh eimi kai oudeteros eilikrinhs orqos an prosagoreuw ina ean fainw docastos autos eimi en dikh prosagoreuw o men akron o akra o de metacu o metacu apodidwmi h ou outws outws outos dh upokeimai legw egw fhmi kai apokrinw
english_head: It would remain, then, as it seems, for us to discover that which partakes of both, of to be and not to be, and that could not be rightly designated either in its exclusive purity; so that, if it shall be discovered, we may justly pronounce it to be the opinable, thus assigning extremes to extremes and the intermediate to the intermediate. Is not that so? It is. This much premised, let him tell me,

greek_head: ὁ χρηστὸς ὃς αὐτὸ μὲν καλὸν καὶ ἰδέαν τινὰ αὐτοῦ κάλλους μηδεμίαν ἡγεῖται ἀεὶ μὲν κατὰ ταὐτὰ ὡσαύτως
greek_tokens: o xrhstos os autos men kalos kai idea tis autos kallos mhdeis hgeomai aei men kata tautos wsautws exw polus de o kalos nomizw ekeinos o filoqeamos kai oudamh anexw an tis eis o kalos fhmi eimi kai dikaios kai allos outws outos gar dh eimi aristos fhmi o polus kalh mhs tis eimi o ou aisxros fainw kai o dikaios o ou adikos kai o osios o ou anosios
english_head: I will say, let him answer me, that good fellow who does not think there is a beautiful in itself or any idea of beauty in itself always remaining the same and unchanged, but who does believe in many beautiful things—the lover of spectacles, I mean, who cannot endure to hear anybody say that the beautiful is one and the just one, and so of other things—and this will be our question: My good fellow, is there any one of these many fair-and-honorable things that will not sometimes appear ugly and base? And of the just things, that will not seem unjust? And of the pious things, that will not seem impious? No, it is inevitable, he said, that they would appear

greek_head: Οὔκ ἀλλ' ἀνάγκη ἔφη καὶ καλά πως αὐτὰ καὶ αἰσχρὰ φανῆναι καὶ ὅσα ἄλλα ἐρωτᾷς
greek_tokens: ou alla anagkh fhmi kai kalos pws autos kai aisxros fainw kai osos allos erwtaw tis de o polus diplasios hsswn tis hmisus h diplasios fainw oudeis kai megas dh kai mikros kai koufos kai barus mh tis mallon o an fhmi outos proserew h enantios ou alla aei fhmi ekastos amfoteros exw poteros oun eimi mallon h ou eimi ekastos o polus outos o an tis fhmi autos eimi o en o estiasis fhmi epamfoterizw eoika
english_head: to be both beautiful in a way and ugly, and so with all the other things you asked about. And again, do the many double things appear any the less halves than doubles? None the less. And likewise of the great and the small things, the light and the heavy things—will they admit these predicates any more than their opposites? No, he said, each of them will always hold of, partake of, both. Then is each of these multiples rather than it is not that which one affirms it to be? They are like those jesters who palter with us in a double sense at banquets, he replied, and resemble the children’s riddle

greek_head: καὶ τῷ τῶν παίδων αἰνίγματι τῷ περὶ τοῦ εὐνούχου τῆς βολῆς πέρι τῆς νυκτερίδος ᾧ καὶ ἐφ' οὗ αὐτὸν αὐτὴν αἰνίττονται
greek_tokens: kai o o pais ainigma o peri o eunouxos o antibolew peri o nukteris os kai epi e autos autos ainissomai ballw kai gar outos epamfoterizw kai oute eimi oute mh eimi oudeis autos dunatos pagios noew oute amfoteros oute oudeteros exis oun autos eimi de egw ostis xraw h opoi tiqhmi kallias qesis o metacu ousia te kai o mh eimi oute gar pou skotwdhs mh eimi pros o mallon mh eimi
english_head: about the eunuch and his hitting of the bat—with what and as it sat on what they signify that he struck it. For these things too equivocate, and it is impossible to conceive firmly any one of them to be or not to be or both or neither. Do you know what to do with them, then? said I, and can you find a better place to put them than that midway between existence or essence and the not-to-be? For we shall surely not discover a darker region than not-being that they should still more not be,

greek_head: φανήσεται οὔτε φανότερα ὄντος πρὸς τὸ μᾶλλον εἶναι Ἀληθέστατα ἔφη
greek_tokens: fainw oute fanos eimi pros o mallon eimi alhqhs fhmi euriskw ara ws eoika ostis o o polus polus nomimos kalew te peri kai o allos metacu pou kulindew o te mh eimi kai o eimi eilikrinhs euriskw proomologew de ge ei tis toioutos fainw docastos autos alla ou gnwstos dew legw o metacu dunamis o metacu planhtos aliskomai omologew
english_head: nor brighter than being that they should still more be. Most true, he said. We would seem to have found, then, that the many conventions of the many about the fair and honorable and other things are tumbled about in the mid-region between that which is not and that which is in the true and absolute sense. We have so found it. But we agreed in advance that, if anything of that sort should be discovered, it must be denominated opinable, not knowable, the wanderer between being caught by the faculty that is betwixt and between. We did. We shall affirm, then, that those who view many beautiful things

greek_head: Τοὺς ἄρα πολλὰ καλὰ θεωμένους αὐτὸ δὲ τὸ καλὸν μὴ ὁρῶντας μηδ' ἄλλῳ ἐπ' αὐτὸ ἄγοντι δυναμένους ἕπεσθαι
greek_tokens: o ara polus kalos qeaomai autos de o kalos mh oraw mhde allos epi autos agw dunamai epomai kai polus dikaios autos de o dikaios mh kai pas outws docazw fhmi apas gignwskw de os docazw oudeis anagkh fhmi tis de au o autos ekastos qeaomai kai aei kata tautos wsautws eimi ara ou gignwskw alla ou docazw anagkh kai outos oukoun kai aspazomai te kai filew outos men outos
english_head: but do not see the beautiful itself and are unable to follow another’s guidance to it, and many just things, but not justice itself, and so in all cases—we shall say that such men have opinions about all things, but know nothing of the things they opine. Of necessity. And, on the other hand, what of those who contemplate the very things themselves in each case, ever remaining the same and unchanged—shall we not say that they know and do not merely opine? That, too, necessarily follows. Shall we not also say that the one welcomes to his thought and loves the things

greek_head: φήσομεν ἐφ' οἷς γνῶσίς ἐστιν ἐκείνους δὲ ἐφ' οἷς δόξα ἢ οὐ μνημονεύομεν ὅτι φωνάς τε καὶ χρόας καλὰς καὶ τὰ
greek_tokens: fhmi epi os gnwsis eimi ekeinos de epi os doca h ou mnhmoneuw ostis fwnh te kai xroia kalos kai o toioutos fhmi outos filew te kai qeaomai autos de o kalos oude anexw os tis eimi mimnhskw mh oun tis plhmmelew filodocos kalew autos mallon h filosofos kai ara egw sfodra xalepainw an outws legw ou an ge egw peiqw fhmi o gar alhqhs xalepainw ou qemis o autos ara ekastos o eimi aspazomai filosofos alla ou filodocos klhteos pantapasi men oun
english_head: subject to knowledge and the other those to opinion? Do we not remember that we said that those loved and regarded tones and beautiful colours and the like, but they could not endure the notion of the reality of the beautiful itself? We do remember. Shall we then offend their ears if we call them doxophilists rather than philosophers and will they be very angry if we so speak? Not if they heed my counsel, he said, for to be angry with truth is not lawful. Then to those who in each and every kind welcome the true being, lovers of wisdom and not lovers of opinion is the name we must give. By all means.

greek_head: Οἱ μὲν δὴ φιλόσοφοι ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ Γλαύκων καὶ οἱ μὴ διὰ μακροῦ τινος διεξελθόντες λόγου μόγις πως ἀνεφάνησαν
greek_tokens: e men dh filosofos eimi de egw eimi glauc kai e mh dia makros tis diecerxomai logos mogis pws anafainw e eimi ekateros isos gar fhmi dia braxus ou radios ou fainw eipon egw goun eti dokew an beltiwn fainw ei peri outos monos dew erw kai mh polus o loipas dierxomai mellw kaqoraw tis diaferw bios
english_head: So now, Glaucon, I said, our argument after winding a long and weary way has at last made clear to us who are the philosophers or lovers of wisdom and who are not. Yes, he said, a shorter way is perhaps not feasible. Apparently not, I said. I, at any rate, think that the matter would have been made still plainer if we had had nothing but this to speak of, and if there were not so many things left which our purpose of discerning the difference between the just and

greek_head: δίκαιος ἀδίκου Τί οὖν ἔφη τὸ μετὰ τοῦτο ἡμῖν
greek_tokens: dikaios adikos tis oun fhmi o meta outos egw tis de allos eimi de egw h o echs epei filosofos men e o aei kata tautos wsautws exw dunamai efaptw e de mh alla en polus kai pantoios isxw planaw ou filosofos poteros dh deomai polis hgemwn eimi pws oun legw an autos fhmi metrios legw opoteros an eimi de egw dunatos fainw fulassw nomos te kai epithdeuma polis outos kaqisthmi
english_head: the unjust life requires us to discuss. What, then, he said, comes next? What else, said I, but the next in order? Since the philosophers are those who are capable of apprehending that which is eternal and unchanging, while those who are incapable of this but lose themselves and wander amid the multiplicities of multifarious things, are not philosophers, which of the two kinds ought to be the leaders in a state? What, then, he said, would be a fair statement of the matter? Whichever, I said, appear competent to guard the laws and pursuits of society,

greek_head: φύλακας Ὀρθῶς ἔφη
greek_tokens: fulac orqos fhmi ode de eimi de egw ara dhlos eite tuflos eite ocus oraw xrh fulac threw otioun kai pws fhmi ou dhlos eimi oun dokew tis tuflos diaferw e o eimi o eimi ekastos sterew o gnwsis kai mhdeis enarghs en o yuxw exw paradeigma mhde dunamai wsper grafeus eis o alhqhs apoblepw ekeise aei anaferw te
english_head: these we should establish as guardians. Right, he said. Is this, then, said I, clear, whether the guardian who is to keep watch over anything ought to be blind or keen of sight? Of course it is clear, he said. Do you think, then, that there is any appreciable difference between the blind and those who are veritably deprived of the knowledge of the veritable being of things, those who have no vivid pattern in their souls and so cannot, as painters look to their models, fix their eyes on the absolute truth, and always with reference to that ideal and in the exactest possible contemplation of it

greek_head: καὶ θεώμενοι ὡς οἷόν τε ἀκριβέστατα οὕτω δὴ καὶ τὰ ἐνθάδε νόμιμα καλῶν τε πέρι καὶ δικαίων καὶ ἀγαθῶν τίθεσθαί
greek_tokens: kai qeaomai ws oios te akribhs outws dh kai o enqade nomimos kalh te peri kai dikaios kai agaqos tiqhmi te ean deomai tiqhmi kai o keimai fulassw swzw ou ma o dios eimi de os ou polus tis diaferw outos oun mallon fulac isthmi h o gignwskw men ekastos o eimi empeiria de mhdeis ekeinos elleipw mhde en allos mhdeis meros aretaw usterew atopos mentoi fhmi eaw allos airew ei ge allos mh elleipw outos gar autos sxedon tis o megas an proexw
english_head: establish in this world also the laws of the beautiful, the just and the good, when that is needful, or guard and preserve those that are established? No, by heaven, he said, there is not much difference. Shall we, then, appoint these blind souls as our guardians, rather than those who have learned to know the ideal reality of things and who do not fall short of the others in experience and are not second to them in any part of virtue? It would be strange indeed, he said, to choose others than the philosophers, provided they were not deficient in those other respects, for this very knowledge

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν τοῦτο δὴ λέγωμεν τίνα τρόπον οἷοί τ' ἔσονται οἱ αὐτοὶ κἀκεῖνα καὶ ταῦτα ἔχειν
greek_tokens: oukoun outos dh legw tis tropos oios su eimi e autos ekeinos kai outos exw panu men oun o toinun arxw outos o logos legw o fus autos proteros deomai katamanqanw kai oimh ean ekeinos ikanos omologew omologew kai ostis oios te outos exw e autos ostis te ou allos polis hgemwn deomai eimi h outos pws outos men dh o filosofos fusis peri omologew
english_head: of the ideal would perhaps be the greatest of superiorities. Then what we have to say is how it would be possible for the same persons to have both qualifications, is it not? Quite so. Then, as we were saying at the beginning of this discussion, the first thing to understand is the nature that they must have from birth; and I think that if we sufficiently agree on this we shall also agree that the combination of qualities that we seek belongs to the same persons, and that we need no others for guardians of states than these. How so? We must accept as agreed this trait of the philosophical nature,

greek_head: ἡμῖν ὅτι μαθήματός γε ἀεὶ ἐρῶσιν ὃ ἂν αὐτοῖς δηλοῖ ἐκείνης τῆς οὐσίας τῆς ἀεὶ οὔσης καὶ μὴ πλανωμένης ὑπὸ γενέσεως
greek_tokens: egw ostis maqhma ge aei eraw o an autos dhlow ekeinos o ousia o aei eimi kai mh planaw upo genesis kai fqora omologew kai meis eimi de egw kai ostis pas autos kai oute mikros oute megas oute timios oute atimos meros ekwn afihmi wsper en o prosqen peri te o filotimos kai erwtikos dierxomai orqos fhmi legw ode toinun meta outos skopaw ei anagkh exw pros
english_head: that it is ever enamored of the kind of knowledge which reveals to them something of that essence which is eternal, and is not wandering between the two poles of generation and decay. Let us take that as agreed. And, further, said I, that their desire is for the whole of it and that they do not willingly renounce a small or a great, a more precious or a less honored, part of it. That was the point of our former illustration drawn from lovers and men covetous of honor. You are right, he said. Consider, then, next whether the men who are to meet our requirements

greek_head: τούτῳ ἐν τῇ φύσει οἳ ἂν μέλλωσιν ἔσεσθαι οἵους ἐλέγομεν Τὸ ποῖον
greek_tokens: outos en o fusis e an mellw eimi oios legw o poios o ayeudeia kai o ekwn eimi mhdamh prosdexomai o yeudos alla misew o de alhqeia stergw eoika ge fhmi ou monos ge eimi filos eoika alla kai pas anagkh o erwtikos tis fusis exw pas o suggenhs te kai oikew o paidikon agaph orqos fhmi eimi oun oikeios sofia tis alhqeia an euriskw kai pws eimi de os eimi oun dunatos eimi o autos fus filosofos te kai
english_head: must not have this further quality in their natures. What quality? The spirit of truthfulness, reluctance to admit falsehood in any form, the hatred of it and the love of truth. It is likely, he said. It is not only likely, my friend, but there is every necessity that he who is by nature enamored of anything should cherish all that is akin and pertaining to the object of his love. Right, he said. Could you find anything more akin to wisdom than truth? Impossible, he said. Then can the same nature be a lover of wisdom and of falsehood?

greek_head: φιλοψευδῆ Οὐδαμῶς γε
greek_tokens: filoyeudhs oudamos ge o ara o eimi filomaqhs pas alhqeia deomai euqus ek neos ostis malista oregw pantelhs ge alla meis ostis ge eis eis tis o epiqumia sfodra repw oida pou ostis eis allos outos asqenhs wsper reuma ekeise apooxeteuw tis meis os dh pros o maqhma kai pas o toioutos rew peri o o yuxazw oimh hdona autos kaqa eautou eien an o de dia o swma ekleipw ei mh peplasmenws alla
english_head: By no means. Then the true lover of knowledge must, from childhood up, be most of all a striver after truth in every form. By all means. But, again, we surely are aware that when in a man the desires incline strongly to any one thing, they are weakened for other things. It is as if the stream had been diverted into another channel. Surely. So, when a man’s desires have been taught to flow in the channel of learning and all that sort of thing, they will be concerned, I presume, with the pleasures of the soul in itself, and will be indifferent to those of which the body is the instrument, if the man is a true and not a sham philosopher.

greek_head: ἀληθῶς φιλόσοφός τις εἴη Μεγάλη ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: alhqhs filosofos tis eaw megas anagkh swfrwn meis o ge toioutos kai oudamh filoxrhmatos os gar eneka xrhma meta polus dapanh spoudazw allos tis mallon h outos proshkw spoudazw outws
english_head: That is quite necessary. Such a man will be temperate and by no means greedy for wealth; for the things for the sake of which money and great expenditure are eagerly sought others may take seriously, but not he. It is so. And there is this further point to be considered in distinguishing

greek_head: Καὶ μήν που καὶ τόδε δεῖ σκοπεῖν ὅταν κρίνειν μέλλῃς φύσιν φιλόσοφόν τε καὶ μή
greek_tokens: kai meis pou kai ode deomai skopaw otan krinw mellw fus filosofos te kai mh o poios mh su lanqanw metexw aneleuqeria enantios gar pou mikrologia yuxw mellw o oloc kai pas aei eporegw qeios te kai anqrwpinos alhqhs fhmi h oun uparxw dianoia megaloprepeia kai qewrios pas men xronos pas de ousia oios te oiomai outos megas tis dokew eimi o anqrwpinos bios adunatos eimi de os
english_head: the philosophical from the unphilosophical nature. What point? You must not overlook any touch of illiberality. For nothing can be more contrary than such pettiness to the quality of a soul that is ever to seek integrity and wholeness in all things human and divine. Most true, he said. Do you think that a mind habituated to thoughts of grandeur and the contemplation of all time and all existence can deem this life of man a thing of great concern? Impossible, said he.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν καὶ θάνατον οὐ δεινόν τι ἡγήσεται ὁ τοιοῦτος Ἥκιστά γε
greek_tokens: oukoun kai qanatos ou deinos tis ageomai o toioutos hkistos ge deilos dh kai aneleuqeros fusis filosofia alhqinos ws eoika ou an meteimi ou egw dokew tis oun o kosmios kai mh filoxrhmatos mhde aneleuqeros mhde alazwn mhde deilos esqw oph an dussumbolos h adikos gignomai ou eimi kai outos dh yuxh skopaw filosofos kai mh euqus neos eimi episkeyis ei ara dikaios te kai hmeros h duskoinwnhtos kai agrios panu men oun
english_head: Hence such a man will not suppose death to be terrible? Least of all. Then a cowardly and illiberal spirit, it seems, could have no part in genuine philosophy. I think not. What then? Could a man of orderly spirit, not a lover of money, not illiberal, nor a braggart nor a coward, ever prove unjust, or a driver of hard bargains? Impossible. This too, then, is a point that in your discrimination of the philosophic and unphilosophic soul you will observe—whether the man is from youth up just and gentle or unsocial and savage. Assuredly. Nor will you overlook this,

greek_head: Οὐ μὴν οὐδὲ τόδε παραλείψεις ὡς ἐγᾦμαι Τὸ ποῖον
greek_tokens: ou meis oude ode paraleiyis ws oimh o poios eumaqhs h dusmaqhs h prosdokaw pote tis tis ikanos an stergw o prassw an algos te prassw kai mogis mikros anuw ou an gignomai tis de ei mhdeis os manqanw swzw dunamai lhqh eimi pleos ara an oios su eaw episthmh mh kenos eimi kai pws anonhtos dh ponew ou oiomai anagkazw teleutaw eautou te misew kai o toioutos pracis pws de ou
english_head: I fancy. What? Whether he is quick or slow to learn. Or do you suppose that anyone could properly love a task which he performed painfully and with little result from much toil? That could not be. And if he could not keep what he learned, being steeped in oblivion, could he fail to be void of knowledge? How could he? And so, having all his labor for naught, will he not finally be constrained to loathe himself and that occupation?

greek_head: Ἐπιλήσμονα ἄρα ψυχὴν ἐν ταῖς ἱκανῶς φιλοσόφοις μή ποτε ἐγκρίνωμεν ἀλλὰ μνημονικὴν αὐτὴν ζητῶμεν δεῖν εἶναι
greek_tokens: epilhsmwn ara yuxh en o ikanos filosofos mh pote egkrinw alla mnhmonikos autos zhtew dew eimi pantapasi men oun alla ou meis o ge o amousos te kai asxhmwn fusis allote poi an fhmi elkw h eis ametrios tis meis alhqeia de ametrios hgeomai suggeneus eimi h emmetria emmetria emmetros ara kai euxaris zhtew pros o allos dianoia fusis os epi o o eimi idea ekastos o autofuhs euagwgos parecis pws de ou
english_head: Of course. The forgetful soul, then, we must not list in the roll of competent lovers of wisdom, but we require a good memory. By all means. But assuredly we should not say that the want of harmony and seemliness in a nature conduces to anything else than the want of measure and proportion. Certainly. And do you think that truth is akin to measure and proportion or to disproportion? To proportion. Then in addition to our other requirements we look for a mind endowed with measure and grace, whose native disposition will make it easily guided

greek_head: Τί οὖν μή πῃ δοκοῦμέν σοι οὐκ ἀναγκαῖα ἕκαστα διεληλυθέναι καὶ ἑπόμενα ἀλλήλοις τῇ μελλούσῃ τοῦ ὄντος ἱκανῶς
greek_tokens: tis oun mh ph dokew su ou anagkaion ekastos dierxomai kai epomai allhlwn o mellw o eimi ikanos te kai teleios yuxw metalambanw
english_head: to the aspect of the ideal reality in all things. Assuredly. Tell me, then, is there any flaw in the argument? Have we not proved the qualities enumerated to be necessary and compatible with one another for the soul that is to have a sufficient and perfect apprehension of reality?

greek_head: Ἀναγκαιότατα μὲν οὖν ἔφη Ἔστιν οὖν ὅπῃ μέμψῃ τοιοῦτον ἐπιτήδευμα ὃ μή ποτ' ἄν
greek_tokens: anagkaios men oun fhmi eimi oun oph memfomai toioutos epithdeuma o mh pote an tis oios te gignomai ikanos epithdeuw ei mh fusis eaw mnhmwn eumaqhs megaloprephs euxaris filos te kai suggenhs alhqeia dikaiosunh andreia swfrosunos oude an o mwmos fhmi o ge toioutos memfomai alla eimi de egw teleiow o toioutos paideia te kai hlikia ara ou monos an o polis epitrepw
english_head: Nay, most necessary, he said. Is there any fault, then, that you can find with a pursuit which a man could not properly practise unless he were by nature of good memory, quick apprehension, magnificent, gracious, friendly and akin to truth, justice, bravery and sobriety? Momus himself, he said, could not find fault with such a combination. Well, then, said I, when men of this sort are perfected by education and maturity of age, would you not entrust the state solely to them? And Adeimantus said, No one, Socrates,

greek_head: Καὶ ὁ Ἀδείμαντος Ὦ Σώκρατες ἔφη πρὸς μὲν ταῦτά σοι οὐδεὶς ἂν οἷός τ' εἴη ἀντειπεῖν ἀλλὰ γὰρ τοιόνδε τι
greek_tokens: kai o adeimantos eimi swkraths fhmi pros men outos su oudeis an oios su eaw anteipon alla gar toiosde tis pasxw e akouw ekastote o nun legw hgeomai dia apeiria o erwtaw kai apokrinw upo o logos para ekastos o erwthma mikros paragw aqroizw o mikros epi teleutaw o logos megas o sfalma kai enantion o proteros anafainw kai wsper upo o pesseuw deinazw e mh teleutaw apokleiw kai ou exw ostis
english_head: would be able to controvert these statements of yours. But, all the same, those who occasionally hear you argue thus feel in this way: They think that owing to their inexperience in the game of question and answer they are at every question led astray a little bit by the argument, and when these bits are accumulated at the conclusion of the discussion mighty is their fall and the apparent contradiction of what they at first said; and that just as by expert draught-players the unskilled are finally shut in and cannot make a move,

greek_head: φέρωσιν οὕτω καὶ σφεῖς τελευτῶντες ἀποκλείεσθαι καὶ οὐκ ἔχειν ὅτι λέγωσιν ὑπὸ πεττείας αὖ ταύτης τινὸς ἑτέρας οὐκ ἐν
greek_tokens: ferw outws kai sfeis teleutaw apokleiw kai ou exw ostis legw upo pesseia au outos tis eteros ou en yhfos alla en logos epei o ge alhqhs oudeis tis mallon outos exw legos de eis o pareimi apoblepw nun gar fhmi an tis su logos men ou exw kaqa ekastos o erwtaw enantioomai ergon de oraw osos an epi filosofia ormaw mh o paideuw eneka aptw neos eimi
english_head: so they are finally blocked and have their mouths stopped by this other game of draughts played not with counters but with words; yet the truth is not affected by that outcome. I say this with reference to the present case, for in this instance one might say that he is unable in words to contend against you at each question, but that when it comes to facts he sees that of those who turn to philosophy, not merely touching upon it to complete their education

greek_head: ἀπαλλάττωνται ἀλλὰ μακρότερον ἐνδιατρίψωσιν τοὺς μὲν πλείστους καὶ πάνυ ἀλλοκότους γιγνομένους ἵνα μὴ παμπονήρους
greek_tokens: apallassw alla makros endiatribw o men pleistos kai panu allokotos gignomai ina mh pamponhros eipon o de epieikhs dokew omws outos ge upo o epithdeuma e su epainew pasxw axrhstos o polis gignomai kai egw aekwn oiomai oun eipon o outos legw yeudw ou oida eimi de os alla o sos dokew hdews an akouw akouw an ostis egw fainw alhqhs legw
english_head: and dropping it while still young, but lingering too long in the study of it, the majority become cranks, not to say rascals, and those accounted the finest spirits among them are still rendered useless to society by the pursuit which you commend. And I, on hearing this, said, Do you think that they are mistaken in saying so? I don’t know, said he,

greek_head: Πῶς οὖν ἔφη εὖ ἔχει λέγειν ὅτι οὐ πρότερον κακῶν παύσονται αἱ πόλεις πρὶν ἂν ἐν αὐταῖς οἱ φιλόσοφοι
greek_tokens: pws oun fhmi eu exis legw ostis ou proteros kakh pauw o polis prin an en autos e filosofos arxw os axrhstos omologew autos eimi erwtaw eimi de egw erwthma deomai apokrisls dia eikwn legw su de ge fhmi oimh ou eqw dia eikwn legw eien eipon skwptw emballw egw eis logos outws
english_head: but I would gladly hear your opinion. You may hear, then, that I think that what they say is true. How, then, he replied, can it be right to say that our cities will never be freed from their evils until the philosophers, whom we admit to be useless to them, become their rulers? Your question, I said, requires an answer expressed in a comparison or parable. And you, he said, of course, are not accustomed to speak in comparisons! So, said I, you are making fun of me after driving me into such an impasse of argument. But, all the same, hear my comparison

greek_head: δυσαπόδεικτον ἄκουε δ' οὖν τῆς εἰκόνος ἵν' ἔτι μᾶλλον ἴδῃς ὡς γλίσχρως εἰκάζω οὕτω γὰρ χαλεπὸν τὸ πάθος
greek_tokens: dusapodeiktos akouw de oun o eikwn ina eti mallon eidon ws glisxros eikazw outws gar xalepos o paqos o epieikhs o pros o polis pasxw wste oude eimi eis oudeis allos toioutos pasxw alla deomai ek polus autos sunagw eikazw kai apologeomai uper autos oios e grafeus tragelafos kai o toioutos mignumi grafw noew gar toioutos gignomai eite polus naos peri eite eis nauklhros megeqos men kai
english_head: so that you may still better see how I strain after imagery. For so cruel is the condition of the better sort in relation to the state that there is no single thing like it in nature. But to find a likeness for it and a defence for them one must bring together many things in such a combination as painters mix when they portray goat-stags and similar creatures. Conceive this sort of thing happening either on many ships or on one: Picture a shipmaster in height and strength surpassing all others on the ship,

greek_head: ῥώμῃ ὑπὲρ τοὺς ἐν τῇ νηὶ πάντας ὑπόκωφον δὲ καὶ ὁρῶντα ὡσαύτως βραχύ τι καὶ γιγνώσκοντα περὶ ναυτικῶν ἕτερα
greek_tokens: rwmh uper o en o naias pas upokwfos de kai oraw wsautws braxus tis kai gignwskw peri nautikon eteros toioutos o de nauths stasiazw pros allhlwn peri o kubernhsis ekastos oiomai dew kubernaw mhte manqanw pwpote o texnh mete exw apodeiknumi didaskalos eautou mhde xronos en os manqanw pros de outos faskw mhde didaktos eimi alla kai o legw ws didaktos etoimos katatemnw autos de autos aei o
english_head: but who is slightly deaf and of similarly impaired vision, and whose knowledge of navigation is on a par with his sight and hearing. Conceive the sailors to be wrangling with one another for control of the helm, each claiming that it is his right to steer though he has never learned the art and cannot point out his teacher or any time when he studied it. And what is more, they affirm that it cannot be taught at all, but they are ready to make mincemeat of anyone who says that it can be taught,

greek_head: ναυκλήρῳ περικεχύσθαι δεομένους καὶ πάντα ποιοῦντας ὅπως ἂν σφίσι τὸ πηδάλιον ἐπιτρέψῃ ἐνίοτε δ' ἂν μὴ πείθωσιν
greek_tokens: nauklhros perixew deomai kai pas poiew opws an sfeis o phdalion epitrepw eniote de an mh peiqw alla allos mallon o men allos h apokteinw h ekballw ek o naos o de gennaios nauklhros mandragoras h meqh h tis allos sumpodizw o naos arxw xraomai o eneimi kai pinw te kai euwxew plew ws o eoika o toioutos pros de outos epainew
english_head: and meanwhile they are always clustered about the shipmaster importuning him and sticking at nothing to induce him to turn over the helm to them. And sometimes, if they fail and others get his ear, they put the others to death or cast them out from the ship, and then, after binding and stupefying the worthy shipmaster with mandragora or intoxication or otherwise, they take command of the ship, consume its stores and, drinking and feasting, make such a voyage of it as is to be expected from such, and as if that were not enough, they praise and celebrate as a navigator,

greek_head: ναυτικὸν μὲν καλοῦντας καὶ κυβερνητικὸν καὶ ἐπιστάμενον τὰ κατὰ ναῦν ὃς ἂν συλλαμβάνειν δεινὸς ᾖ ὅπως
greek_tokens: nautikon men kalew kai kubernhtikos kai efisthmi o kata naus os an sullambanw deinos eimi opws arxw h peiqw h biazw o nauklhros o de mh toioutos yegw ws axrhstos o de alhqinos kubernhths peri mhde epaiw ostis anagkh autos o epimeleia poiew eniautos kai wra kai ouranos kai astron kai pneuma kai pas o o texnh proshkw ei mellw o eimi naos arxikos eimi opws de kubernhsis
english_head: a pilot, a master of shipcraft, the man who is most cunning to lend a hand in persuading or constraining the shipmaster to let them rule, while the man who lacks this craft they censure as useless. They have no suspicions that the true pilot must give his attention to the time of the year, the seasons, the sky, the winds, the stars, and all that pertains to his art if he is to be a true ruler of a ship, and that he does not believe that there is any art or science of seizing the helm

greek_head: ἐάντε τινες βούλωνται ἐάντε μή μήτε τέχνην τούτου μήτε μελέτην οἰόμενοι δυνατὸν εἶναι λαβεῖν ἅμα καὶ τὴν κυβερνητικήν
greek_tokens: ean tis boulomai ean mh mhte texnh outos mhte melw oiomai dunatos eimi lambanw ama kai o kubernhtikos toioutos dh peri o naus gignomai o ws alhqhs kubernhtikos ou hgeomai an o eimi metewroskopos
english_head: with or without the consent of others, or any possibility of mastering this alleged art and the practice of it at the same time with the science of navigation. With such goings-on aboard ship do you not think that the real pilot would in very deed be called a star-gazer, an idle babbler,

greek_head: τε καὶ ἀδολέσχην καὶ ἄχρηστόν σφισι καλεῖσθαι ὑπὸ τῶν ἐν ταῖς οὕτω κατεσκευασμέναις ναυσὶ πλωτήρων
greek_tokens: te kai adolesxhs kai axrhstos sfeis kalew upo o en o outws kataskeuazw naus plwthr kai mala fhmi o adeimantos ou dh eimi de egw oimh deomai su ecetazw o eikwn eidon ostis o polis pros o alhqinos filosofos o diaqesis eoika alla manqanw o legos kai mala fhmi proteros men toinun ekeinos o qaumazw ostis e filosofos ou timaw en o polis didaskw te o eikwn kai peiraw peiqw ostis polus an qaumastos eimi
english_head: a useless fellow, by the sailors in ships managed after this fashion? Quite so, said Adeimantus. You take my meaning, I presume, and do not require us to put the comparison to the proof and show that the condition we have described is the exact counterpart of the relation of the state to the true philosophers. It is indeed, he said. To begin with, then, teach this parable to the man who is surprised that philosophers are not honored in our cities, and try to convince him that it would be far more surprising

greek_head: εἰ ἐτιμῶντο Ἀλλὰ διδάξω ἔφη
greek_tokens: ei timaw alla didaskw fhmi kai ostis toinun alhqhs legw ws axrhstos o polus e epieikhs o en filosofia o mentoi axrhstia o mh xraomai keleuw aitiaomai alla mh o epieikhs ou gar exis fus kubernaw nauths deomai arxw ufh eautou oude o sofos epi o o plousios qura eimi alla o outos komyeuw yeudw o de alhqhs fuw ean plousios ean penhs kamnw anagkaion
english_head: if they were honored. I will teach him, he said. And say to him further: You are right in affirming that the finest spirit among the philosophers are of no service to the multitude. But bid him blame for this uselessness, not the finer spirits, but those who do not know how to make use of them. For it is not the natural course of things that the pilot should beg the sailors to be ruled by him or that wise men should go to the doors of the rich. The author of that epigram was a liar. But the true nature of things is that whether the sick man be rich or poor he must needs go to the door of the physician,

greek_head: εἶναι ἐπὶ ἰατρῶν θύρας ἰέναι καὶ πάντα τὸν ἄρχεσθαι δεόμενον ἐπὶ τὰς τοῦ ἄρχειν δυναμένου οὐ τὸν ἄρχοντα δεῖσθαι
greek_tokens: eimi epi iatros qura eimi kai pas o arxw deomai epi o o arxw dunamai ou o arxw deomai o arxw arxw e an o alhqeia tis ofelos eimi alla o nun politikos arxw apeikazw os arti legw nauths ou amartanw kai o upo outos axrhstos legw kai metewrolesxhs o ws alhqhs kubernhths orqos fhmi ek te toinun outos kai en outos ou radios eudokimew o beltistos epithdeuma upo o enantios epithdeuw
english_head: and everyone who needs to be governed to the door of the man who knows how to govern, not that the ruler should implore his natural subjects to let themselves be ruled, if he is really good for anything. But you will make no mistake in likening our present political rulers to the sort of sailors we are just describing, and those whom these call useless and star-gazing ideologists to the true pilots. Just so, he said. Hence, and under these conditions, we cannot expect that the noblest pursuit should be highly esteemed by those whose way of life is quite the contrary.

greek_head: πολὺ δὲ μεγίστη καὶ ἰσχυροτάτη διαβολὴ γίγνεται φιλοσοφίᾳ διὰ τοὺς τὰ τοιαῦτα φάσκοντας ἐπιτηδεύειν οὓς δὴ σὺ φῂς
greek_tokens: polus de megas kai isxuros diabolh gignomai filosofia dia o o toioutos faskw epithdeuw os dh su fhmi o egkalew o filosofia legw ws pamponhros e pleistos o eimi epi autos e de epieikhs axrhstos kai egw sugxwrew alhqhs su legw eimi gar nai oukoun o men o epeikazw axrhstia o aitios dierxomai kai mala o de o polus ponhria o anagkh boulomai o meta outos dierxomai kai ostis oude outos filosofia aitios
english_head: But far the greatest and chief disparagement of philosophy is brought upon it by the pretenders to that way of life, those whom you had in mind when you affirmed that the accuser of philosophy says that the majority of her followers are rascals and the better sort useless, while I admitted that what you said was true. Is not that so? Yes. Have we not, then, explained the cause of the uselessness of the better sort? We have. Shall we next set forth the inevitableness of the degeneracy of the majority, and try to show if we can that philosophy

greek_head: ἂν δυνώμεθα πειραθῶμεν δεῖξαι Πάνυ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: an dunamai peiraw deiknumi panu men oun akouw dh kai legw ekeiqen anamimnhskw oqen dieimi o fus oios anagkh fuw o kalos te agaqos
english_head: is not to be blamed for this either? By all means. Let us begin, then, what we have to say and hear by recalling the starting-point of our description of the nature which he who is to be

greek_head: ἐσόμενον ἡγεῖτο δ' αὐτῷ εἰ νῷ ἔχεις πρῶτον μὲν ἀλήθεια ἣν διώκειν αὐτὸν πάντως καὶ πάντῃ ἔδει ἢ ἀλαζόνι ὄντι
greek_tokens: eimi ageomai de autos ei egw exis proteros men alhqeia os diwkw autos pantws kai panth dew h alazwn eimi mhdamh meteimi filosofia alhqinos eimi gar outws legw oukoun eis men outos sfodra outws para doca o nun dokew peri autos kai mala fhmi ara oun dh ou metrios apologeomai ostis pros o eimi fuw eaw amillaomai o ge ontws filomaqhs kai ou
english_head: a scholar and gentleman must have from birth. The leader of the choir for him, if you recollect, was truth. That he was to seek always and altogether, on pain of being an impostor without part or lot in true philosophy. Yes, that was said. Is not this one point quite contrary to the prevailing opinion about him? It is indeed, he said. Will it not be a fair plea in his defence to say that it was the nature of the real lover of knowledge to strive emulously for true being and that he would not linger over

greek_head: ἐπιμένοι ἐπὶ τοῖς δοξαζομένοις εἶναι πολλοῖς ἑκάστοις ἀλλ' ἴοι καὶ οὐκ ἀμβλύνοιτο οὐδ' ἀπολήγοι τοῦ ἔρωτος πρὶν
greek_tokens: epimenw epi o docazw eimi polus ekastos alla eimi kai ou amblunw oude apolhgw o erws prin autos o eimi ekastos o fusis aptw os proshkw yuxazw efaptw o toioutos proshkw de suggeneus os plhsiazw kai mignumi o eimi ontws gennaw new kai alhqeia gignwskw te kai alhqhs zwh kai trefw kai outws lhgw wdis prin de ou ws oios su fhmi metrios tis oun outos tis meteimi yeudos agaph h pas enantion misew
english_head: the many particulars that are opined to be real, but would hold on his way, and the edge of his passion would not be blunted nor would his desire fail till he came into touch with the nature of each thing in itself by that part of his soul to which it belongs to lay hold on that kind of reality—the part akin to it, namely—and through that approaching it, and consorting with reality really, he would beget intelligence and truth, attain to knowledge and truly live and grow, and so find surcease from his travail of soul, but not before? No plea could be fairer. Well, then, will such a man love falsehood,

greek_head: Μισεῖν ἔφη Ἡγουμένης δὴ ἀληθείας οὐκ ἄν ποτε οἶμαι φαμὲν αὐτῇ
greek_tokens: misew fhmi hgeomai dh alhqeia ou an pote oimh fhmi autos xoros kakh akolouqew pws gar alla ugihs te kai dikaios hqos os kai swfrosunos epomai orqos fhmi kai dh o allos o filosofos fusis xoros tis deomai palin ek arxh anagkazw tassw mimnhskw gar pou ostis sumbainw proshkw outos andreia megaloprepeia eumaqeia mnhmh kai sew epilambanw ostis pas men anagkazw
english_head: or, quite the contrary, hate it? Hate it, he said. When truth led the way, no choir of evils, we, I fancy, would say, could ever follow in its train. How could it? But rather a sound and just character, which is accompanied by temperance. Right, he said. What need, then, of repeating from the beginning our proof of the necessary order of the choir that attends on the philosophical nature? You surely remember that we found pertaining to such a nature courage, grandeur of soul, aptness to learn, memory. And when you interposed

greek_head: ὁμολογεῖν οἷς λέγομεν ἐάσας δὲ τοὺς λόγους εἰς αὐτοὺς ἀποβλέψας περὶ ὧν ὁ λόγος φαίη ὁρᾶν αὐτῶν
greek_tokens: omologew os legw eaw de o logos eis autos apoblepw peri os o logos fhmi oraw autos o men axrhstos o de polus kakos pas kakia o diabolh o aitios episkopew epi outos nun gignomai tis poqh e polus kakos kai outos dh eneka palin analambanw o o alhqhs filosofos fus kai ek anagkh orizw
english_head: the objection that though everybody will be compelled to admit our statements, yet, if we abandoned mere words and fixed our eyes on the persons to whom the words referred, everyone would say that he actually saw some of them to be useless and most of them base with all baseness, it was in our search for the cause of this ill-repute that we came to the present question: Why is it that the majority are bad? And, for the sake of this, we took up again the nature of the true philosophers and defined what it must necessarily be?

greek_head: Ἔστιν ἔφη ταῦτα Ταύτης δή ἦν δ' ἐγώ τῆς φύσεως δεῖ θεάσασθαι τὰς
greek_tokens: eimi fhmi outos outos dh eimi de egw o fusis deomai qeaomai o fqora ws diollumi en polus mikros de tis ekfeugw os dh kai ou ponhros axrhstos de kalew kai meta
english_head: That is so, he said. We have, then, I said, to contemplate the causes of the corruption of this nature in the majority, while a small part escapes, even those whom men call not bad but useless; and after that in turn

greek_head: τοῦτο αὖ τὰς μιμουμένας ταύτην καὶ εἰς τὸ ἐπιτήδευμα καθισταμένας αὐτῆς οἷαι οὖσαι φύσεις ψυχῶν εἰς ἀνάξιον
greek_tokens: outos au o mimeomai outos kai eis o epithdeuma kaqisthmi autos oios eimi fusis yuxos eis anacios kai megas eautou afikneomai epithdeuma pollaxh plhmmelew pantaxh kai epi pas doca oios legw filosofia prosaptw tis de fhmi o diafqora legw egw su eipon an oios te gignomai peiraw dierxomai ode men oun oimh pas egw omologhsis toioutos fus kai pas exw osos prostassw nundi ei teleios
english_head: we are to observe those who imitate this nature and usurp its pursuits and see what types of souls they are that thus entering upon a way of life which is too high for them and exceeds their powers, by the many discords and disharmonies of their conduct everywhere and among all men bring upon philosophy the repute of which you speak. Of what corruptions are you speaking? I will try, I said, to explain them to you if I can. I think everyone will grant us this point, that a nature such as we just now postulated

greek_head: μέλλοι φιλόσοφος γενέσθαι ὀλιγάκις ἐν ἀνθρώποις φύεσθαι καὶ ὀλίγας ἢ οὐκ οἴει
greek_tokens: mellw filosofos gignomai oligakis en anqrwpos fuw kai oligos h ou oiomai sfodra ge outos dh o oligos skopaw ws polus oleqros kai megas tinw dh o men pas qaumastos akew ostis eis ekastos os epainew o fusis apollumi o exw yuxh kai apospaw filosofia legos de andreia swfrosunos kai pas o dierxomai atopos fhmi akew
english_head: for the perfect philosopher is a rare growth among men and is found in only a few. Don’t you think so? Most emphatically. Observe, then, the number and magnitude of the things that operate to destroy these few. What are they? The most surprising fact of all is that each of the gifts of nature which we praise tends to corrupt the soul of its possessor and divert it from philosophy. I am speaking of bravery, sobriety, and the entire list. That does sound like a paradox, said he.

greek_head: Ἔτι τοίνυν ἦν δ' ἐγώ πρὸς τούτοις τὰ λεγόμενα ἀγαθὰ πάντα φθείρει καὶ ἀποσπᾷ κάλλος καὶ πλοῦτος καὶ ἰσχὺς
greek_tokens: eti toinun eimi de egw pros outos o legw agaqos pas fqeirw kai apospaw kallos kai ploutos kai isxus swma kai suggeneia errwmenos en polis kai pas o outos oikeios exis gar o tupon os legos exw fhmi kai hdews ge an akribhs o legw puqw lambanw toinun eimi de egw oloc autos orqos kai su eudhlos te fainw kai ou atopos docis o proerew peri autos pws oun fhmi keleuw
english_head: Furthermore, said I, all the so-called goods corrupt and divert, beauty and wealth and strength of body and powerful family connections in the city and all things akin to them—you get my general meaning? I do, he said, and I would gladly hear a more precise statement of it. Well, said I, grasp it rightly as a general proposition and the matter will be clear and the preceding statement will not seem to you so strange. How do you bid me proceed? he said.

greek_head: Παντός ἦν δ' ἐγώ σπέρματος πέρι ἢ φυτοῦ εἴτε ἐγγείων εἴτε τῶν ζῴων ἴσμεν ὅτι τὸ μὴ τυχὸν τροφῆς ἧς προσήκει
greek_tokens: pas eimi de egw sperma peri h futon eite eggaios eite o zwion oida ostis o mh tugxanw trefw eis proshkw ekastos mhde oraw mhde topos osos an errwmenos eimi tosoutos pleiwn endew o prepw agaqos gar pou kakos enantios h o mh agaqos pws de ou exis dh oimh logos o aristos fus en allotrios eimi trefw kakos apallassw o faulos exis
english_head: We know it to be universally true of every seed and growth, whether vegetable or animal, that the more vigorous it is the more it falls short of its proper perfection when deprived of the food, the season, the place that suits it. For evil is more opposed to the good than to the not-good. Of course. So it is, I take it, natural that the best nature should fare worse than the inferior under conditions of nurture unsuited to it. It is. Then, said I, Adeimantus,

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ Ἀδείμαντε καὶ τὰς ψυχὰς οὕτω φῶμεν τὰς εὐφυεστάτας κακῆς παιδαγωγίας τυχούσας
greek_tokens: oukoun eimi de egw eimi adeimantos kai o yuxh outws fhmi o eufuhs kakazw paidagwgia tugxanw diaferontws kakos gignomai h oiomai o megas adikhma kai o akratos ponhria ek faulos alla ou ek neanikew fusis trefw diollumi gignomai asqenhs de fus megas oute agaqos oute kakh aitios pote eimi ou alla eimi de os outws
english_head: shall we not similarly affirm that the best endowed souls become worse than the others under a bad education? Or do you suppose that great crimes and unmixed wickedness spring from a slight nature and not from a vigorous one corrupted by its nurture, while a weak nature will never be the cause of anything great, either for good or evil? No, he said, that is the case.

greek_head: Ἣν τοίνυν ἔθεμεν τοῦ φιλοσόφου φύσιν ἂν μὲν οἶμαι μαθήσεως προσηκούσης τύχῃ εἰς πᾶσαν ἀρετὴν ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: os toinun eqemen o filosofos fus an men oimh maqhsis proshkw tuxh eis pas areth anagkh aucanw afikneomai ean de mh en proshkw speirw te kai futeuw trefw eis pas enantios au ean mh tis autos bohqew qea tuxh h kai su hgeomai wsper e polus diafqeirw tis eimi upo sofisths neos diafqeirw de tis sofisths idiwtikos ostis kai acios logos alla ou autos o outos legw megas
english_head: Then the nature which we assumed in the philosopher, if it receives the proper teaching, must needs grow and attain to consummate excellence, but, if it be sown and planted and grown in the wrong environment, the outcome will be quite the contrary unless some god comes to the rescue. Or are you too one of the multitude who believe that there are young men who are corrupted by the sophists, and that there are sophists in private life who corrupt to any extent worth mentioning, and that it is not rather the very men who talk in this strain

greek_head: μὲν εἶναι σοφιστάς παιδεύειν δὲ τελεώτατα καὶ ἀπεργάζεσθαι οἵους βούλονται εἶναι καὶ νέους καὶ πρεσβυτέρους καὶ ἄνδρας
greek_tokens: men eimi sofisths paideuw de teleios kai apergazomai oios boulomai eimi kai neos kai presbus kai anhr kai gunh pote dh eimi de os otan eipon sugkaqezomai aqroos polus eis ekklhsia h eis dikasthrion h qeatron h stratopeka h tis allos koinos plhqos sullogos sun polus qorubos o men yegw o legw h prassw o de epainew uperballontws ekateros kai ekboaw kai krotew pros de
english_head: who are the chief sophists and educate most effectively and mould to their own heart’s desire young and old, men and women? When? said he. Why, when, I said, the multitude are seated together in assemblies or in court-rooms or theaters or camps or any other public gathering of a crowd, and with loud uproar censure some of the things that are said and done and approve others, both in excess, with full-throated clamor

greek_head: αὐτοῖς αἵ τε πέτραι καὶ ὁ τόπος ἐν ᾧ ἂν ὦσιν ἐπηχοῦντες διπλάσιον θόρυβον παρέχωσι τοῦ ψόγου καὶ ἐπαίνου ἐν
greek_tokens: autos o te petra kai o topos en os an eimi ephxew diplasios qorubos parexw o yogos kai epainos en dh o toioutos o neos o legw tis oiomai kardia isxw h poa an autos paideia idiwtikos antexw os ou katakluzw upo o toioutos yogos h epainos oixomai ferw kata roos h an outos ferw kai fhmi te o autos outos kalos kai aisxros eimi kai epithdeuw o an outos kai eimi toioutos
english_head: and clapping of hands, and thereto the rocks and the region round about re-echoing redouble the din of the censure and the praise. In such case how do you think the young man’s heart, as the saying is, is moved within him? What private teaching do you think will hold out and not rather be swept away by the torrent of censure and applause, and borne off on its current, so that he will affirm the same things that they do to be honorable and base,

greek_head: Πολλή ἦ δ' ὅς ὦ Σώκρατες ἀνάγκη Καὶ μήν ἦν δ' ἐγώ οὔπω τὴν μεγίστην ἀνάγκην εἰρήκαμεν
greek_tokens: polus eimi de os eimi swkraths anagkh kai meis eimi de egw oupw o megas anagkh erw poa fhmi os ergon prostiqhmi logos mh peiqw outos e paideuths te kai sofisths h ou oida ostis o mh peiqw atimia te kai xrhma kai qanatos kolazw kai mala fhmi sfodra tis oun allos sofisths oiomai h poios idiwtikos logos enantios outos teinw kratew
english_head: and will do as they do, and be even such as they? That is quite inevitable, Socrates, he said. And, moreover, I said, we have not yet mentioned the chief necessity and compulsion. What is it? said he. That which these educators and sophists impose by action when their words fail to convince. Don’t you know that they chastise the recalcitrant with loss of civic rights and fines and death? They most emphatically do, he said. What other sophist, then, or what private teaching do you think

greek_head: Οἶμαι μὲν οὐδένα ἦ δ' ὅς Οὐ γάρ ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἀλλὰ καὶ τὸ ἐπιχειρεῖν πολλὴ ἄνοια
greek_tokens: oimh men oudeis eimi de os ou gar eimi de egw alla kai o epixeirew polus anoia oute gar gignomai oute gignomai oude oun mh gignomai alloios hqos pros areth para o outos paideia paideuw anqrwpeios eimi etairos qew mentoi kata o paroimia ecairew logos eu gar xrh oida oti an swzw te
english_head: will prevail in opposition to these? None, I fancy, said he. No, said I, the very attempt is the height of folly. For there is not, never has been and never will be, a divergent type of character and virtue created by an education running counter to theirs—humanly speaking, I mean, my friend; for the divine, as the proverb says, all rules fail. And you may be sure that, if anything is saved and turns out well

greek_head: καὶ γένηται οἷον δεῖ ἐν τοιαύτῃ καταστάσει πολιτειῶν θεοῦ μοῖραν αὐτὸ σῶσαι λέγων οὐ κακῶς ἐρεῖς
greek_tokens: kai gignomai oios deomai en toioutos kaqisthmi politeia qeaomai moira autos swzw legos ou kakos erew oude egw allos fhmi dokew eti toinun su eimi de egw pros outos kai ode dokew o poios ekastos o misqarnew idiwths os dh outos sofisths kalew kai antitexnos hgeomai mh allos paideuw h outos o o polus dogma o docazw otan aqroizw kai sofia outos kalew oionper an ei qremma megas kai isxuros trefw o orgas tis kai
english_head: in the present condition of society and government, in saying that the providence of God preserves it you will not be speaking ill. Neither do I think otherwise, he said. Then, said I, think this also in addition. What? Each of these private teachers who work for pay, whom the politicians call sophists and regard as their rivals, inculcates nothing else than these opinions of the multitude which they opine when they are assembled and calls this knowledge wisdom. It is as if a man were acquiring the knowledge of the humors and desires of a great strong beast which he had in his keeping,

greek_head: ἐπιθυμίας κατεμάνθανεν ὅπῃ τε προσελθεῖν χρὴ καὶ ὅπῃ ἅψασθαι αὐτοῦ καὶ ὁπότε χαλεπώτατον ἢ πρᾳότατον καὶ
greek_tokens: epiqumia katamanqanw oph te proserxomai xrh kai oph aptw autos kai opote xalepos h praos kai ek tinw gignomai kai fwnh dh epi os ekastos eqw fqeggomai kai oios au allos fqeggomai hmerow te kai agriainw katamanqanw de outos pas sunousia te kai xronos tribw sofia te kalew kai ws texnh sunisthmi epi didaskalia trepw mhdeis eidoi o alhqeia outos o dogma te kai epiqumia ostis kalos h aisxros
english_head: how it is to be approached and touched, and when and by what things it is made most savage or gentle, yes, and the several sounds it is wont to utter on the occasion of each, and again what sounds uttered by another make it tame or fierce, and after mastering this knowledge by living with the creature and by lapse of time should call it wisdom, and should construct thereof a system and art and turn to the teaching of it, knowing nothing in reality about which of these opinions and desires is honorable or base, good or evil, just or unjust,

greek_head: ἢ ἀγαθὸν ἢ κακὸν ἢ δίκαιον ἢ ἄδικον ὀνομάζοι δὲ πάντα ταῦτα ἐπὶ ταῖς τοῦ μεγάλου ζῴου δόξαις οἷς μὲν χαίροι
greek_tokens: h agaqos h kakos h dikaios h adikos onomazw de pas outos epi o o megas zwion doca os men xairw ekeinos agaqos kalh os de axqomai kakos allos de mhdeis exw logos peri autos alla anagkaion dikaios kalew kai kalos o de o anagkaion kai agaqos fus osos diaferw o eimi mhte oraw eaw mhte allos dunatos deiknumi toioutos dh eimi pros zeus ou atopos an su dokew eimi paideuths egw fhmi eimi oun tis outos dokew diaferw o o o polus kai
english_head: but should apply all these terms to the judgements of the great beast, calling the things that pleased it good, and the things that vexed it bad, having no other account to render of them, but should call what is necessary just and honorable, never having observed how great is the real difference between the necessary and the good, and being incapable of explaining it to another. Do you not think, by heaven, that such a one would be a strange educator? I do, he said. Do you suppose that there is any difference between such a one and the man who thinks

greek_head: παντοδαπῶν συνιόντων ὀργὴν καὶ ἡδονὰς κατανενοηκέναι σοφίαν ἡγούμενος εἴτ' ἐν γραφικῇ εἴτ' ἐν μουσικῇ εἴτε δὴ
greek_tokens: pantodaphs suneimi orgh kai hdona katanoew sofia hgeomai eite en grafikos eite en mousikh eite dh en politikos ostis men gar an tis outos omilew epideiknumi h poihsis h tis allos dhmiourgia h polis diakonia kurios eautou poa o polus pera o anagkaion o diomhdeios legw anagkh poiew autos outos o an outos epainew ws de kai agaqos kai kalos outos o alhqeia hdh pwpote tis hdousas autos logos didwmi ou katagelastos
english_head: that it is wisdom to have learned to know the moods and the pleasures of the motley multitude in their assembly, whether about painting or music or, for that matter, politics? For if a man associates with these and offers and exhibits to them his poetry or any other product of his craft or any political. service, and grants the mob authority over himself more than is unavoidable, the proverbial necessity of Diomede will compel him to give the public what it likes, but that what it likes is really good and honorable, have you ever heard an attempted proof of this that is not simply ridiculous?

greek_head: Οἶμαι δέ γε ἦ δ' ὅς οὐδ' ἀκούσομαι Ταῦτα τοίνυν πάντα ἐννοήσας ἐκεῖνο ἀναμνήσθητι αὐτὸ
greek_tokens: oimh de ge eimi de os oude akouw outos toinun pas ennoew ekeinos anamimnhskw autos o kalos alla mh o polus kalos h autos tis ekastos kai
english_head: No, he said, and I fancy I never shall hear it either. Bearing all this in mind, recall our former question. Can the multitude possibly tolerate or believe in the reality of the beautiful in itself as opposed to the multiplicity of beautiful things, or can they believe in anything conceived in its essence as opposed to the many particulars? Not in the least, he said. Philosophy, then, the love of wisdom,

greek_head: μὴ τὰ πολλὰ ἕκαστα ἔσθ' ὅπως πλῆθος ἀνέξεται ἢ ἡγήσεαι εἶναι
greek_tokens: mh o polus ekastos esqw opws plhqos anexw h ageomai eimi hkistos ge fhmi filosofos men ara eimi de egw plhqos adunatos eimi adunatos kai o filosofew ara anagkh yegw upo autos anagkh kai upo outos dh o idiwths osos prosomilew oxlos areskw autos epiqumew dhlos ek dh outos tis oraw swthria filosofos fusis wste en o epithdeuma menw pros telos erxomai ennoew de
english_head: is impossible for the multitude. Impossible. It is inevitable, then, that those who philosophize should be censured by them. Inevitable. And so likewise by those laymen who, associating with the mob, desire to curry favor with it. Obviously. From this point of view do you see any salvation that will suffer the born philosopher to abide in the pursuit and persevere to the end? Consider it in the light of what we said before.

greek_head: ἐκ τῶν ἔμπροσθεν ὡμολόγηται γὰρ δὴ ἡμῖν εὐμάθεια καὶ μνήμη καὶ ἀνδρεία καὶ μεγαλοπρέπεια ταύτης εἶναι τῆς
greek_tokens: ek o emprosqen omologew gar dh egw eumaqeia kai mnhmh kai andreia kai megaloprepeia outos eimi o fusis nai oukoun euqus en pais o toioutos proteros eimi en apas allos te kai ean o swma fuw prosferhs o yuxw tis de ou mellw fhmi boulomai dh oimh autos xraomai epeidan presbus gignomai epi o eautou pragma e te oikeios kai e poliths pws de ou
english_head: We agreed that quickness in learning, memory, courage and magnificence were the traits of this nature. Yes. Then even as a boy among boys such a one will take the lead in all things, especially if the nature of his body matches the soul. How could he fail to do so? he said. His kinsmen and fellow-citizens, then, will desire, I presume, to make use of him when he is older for their own affairs. Of course.

greek_head: Ὑποκείσονται ἄρα δεόμενοι καὶ τιμῶντες προκαταλαμβάνοντες καὶ προκολακεύοντες τὴν μέλλουσαν αὐτοῦ δύναμιν
greek_tokens: upokeimai ara deomai kai timaw prokatalambanw kai prokolakeuw o mellw autos dunamis filew goun fhmi outws gignomai tis oun oiomai eimi de egw o toioutos en o toioutos poiew allos te kai ean tuxh megas polis eimi kai en outos plousios te kai gennaios kai eti eueidhs kai megas ara ou plhrow amhxanos elpis hgeomai kai o o ellhn kai o o barbaros ikanos eimi
english_head: Then they will fawn upon him with petitions and honors, anticipating and flattering the power that will be his. That certainly is the usual way. How, then, do you think such a youth will behave in such conditions, especially if it happen that he belongs to a great city and is rich and well-born therein, and thereto handsome and tall? Will his soul not be filled with unbounded ambitious hopes, and will he not think himself capable of managing the affairs of both Greeks and barbarians,

greek_head: πράττειν καὶ ἐπὶ τούτοις ὑψηλὸν ἐξαρεῖν αὑτόν σχηματισμοῦ καὶ φρονήματος κενοῦ ἄνευ νοῦ ἐμπιμπλάμενον
greek_tokens: prassw kai epi outos uyhlos ecaeirw eautou sxhmatismos kai fronhma kenos aneu neomai empiplhmi kai mala fhmi o dh outws diatiqhmi ean tis hrema proserxomai alhqhs legos ostis noos ou eneimi autos deomai de o de ou kthtos mh douleuw o ktaomai autos ara eupeths oiomai eimi eisakouw dia tosoutos kakh polus ge deomai eimi de os ean de oun eimi de egw dia o eu fuw kai o suggenhs
english_head: and thereupon exalt himself, haughty of mien and stuffed with empty pride and void of sense He surely will, he said. And if to a man in this state of mind someone gently comes and tells him what is the truth, that he has no sense and sorely needs it, and that the only way to get it is to work like a slave to win it, do you think it will be easy for him to lend an ear to the quiet voice in the midst of and in spite of these evil surroundings Far from it, said he. And even supposing, said I, that owing to a fortunate disposition and his affinity for the words of admonition

greek_head: τῶν λόγων εἰσαισθάνηταί τέ πῃ καὶ κάμπτηται καὶ ἕλκηται πρὸς φιλοσοφίαν τί οἰόμεθα δράσειν ἐκείνους τοὺς ἡγουμένους
greek_tokens: o logos eisaisqanomai su ph kai kamptw kai elkw pros filosofia tis oiomai draw ekeinos o hgeomai apollumi autos o xreia te kai etaireia ou pas men ergnumi pas de epos legw te kai prassw kai peri autos opws an mh peiqw kai peri o peiqw opws an mh oios su eimi kai idios epibouleuw kai dhmosios eis agwn kaqisthmi
english_head: one such youth apprehends something and is moved and drawn towards philosophy, what do we suppose will be the conduct of those who think that they are losing his service and fellowship? Is there any word or deed that they will stick at to keep him from being persuaded and to incapacitate anyone who attempts it, both by private intrigue and public prosecution in the court?

greek_head: Πολλή ἦ δ' ὅς ἀνάγκη Ἔστιν οὖν ὅπως ὁ τοιοῦτος φιλοσοφήσει
greek_tokens: polus eimi de os anagkh eimi oun opws o toioutos filosofew ou panu oraw oun eimi de egw ostis ou kakos legw ws ara kai autos o o filosofos fusis mera otan en kakazw trefw gignomai aitios tropos tis o ekpitnw ek o epithdeuma kai o legw agaqos ploutos te kai pas o toioutos paraskeuh ou gar alla orqos fhmi legw outos dh eipon eimi qaumasios oleqros te kai diafqora
english_head: That is inevitable, he said. Is there any possibility of such a one continuing to philosophize? None at all, he said. Do you see, then, said I, that we were not wrong in saying that the very qualities that make up the philosophical nature do, in fact, become, when the environment and nurture are bad, in some sort the cause of its backsliding, and so do the so-called goods— riches and all such instrumentalities? No, he replied, it was rightly said. Such, my good friend, and so great as regards the noblest pursuit,

greek_head: τοσαύτη τε καὶ τοιαύτη τῆς βελτίστης φύσεως εἰς τὸ ἄριστον ἐπιτήδευμα ὀλίγης καὶ ἄλλως γιγνομένης ὡς ἡμεῖς φαμεν
greek_tokens: tosoutos te kai toioutos o beltistos fusis eis o ariston epithdeuma oligos kai allos gignomai ws egw fhmi kai ek outos dh o androw kai e o megas kakos ergazomai o polis gignomai kai o idiwths kai e agaqos e an outos tugxanw rew mikros de fusis oudeis megas oudepote oudeis oute idiwths oute polis draw alhqhs eimi de os outos men dh outws ekpitnw os malista proshkw
english_head: is the destruction and corruption of the most excellent nature, which is rare enough in any case, as we affirm. And it is from men of this type that those spring who do the greatest harm to communities and individuals, and the greatest good when the stream chances to be turned into that channel, but a small nature never does anything great to a man or a city. Most true, said he.

greek_head: ἔρημον καὶ ἀτελῆ φιλοσοφίαν λείποντες αὐτοί τε βίον οὐ προσήκοντα οὐδ' ἀληθῆ ζῶσιν τὴν δέ ὥσπερ ὀρφανὴν συγγενῶν
greek_tokens: erhmos kai atelhs filosofia leipw autos te bios ou proshkw oude alhqhs zaw o de wsper orfanos suggenhs allos epeiserxomai anacios aisxunw te kai oneidos periaptw oios kai su fhmi oneidizw o oneidizw ws e suneimi autos e men oudeis e de polus polus kakh acios eimi kai gar oun fhmi o ge legw outos eoikotws ge eimi de egw legw kaqoraw gar allos anqrwpion kenos o xwra outos gignomai kalh
english_head: Those, then, to whom she properly belongs, thus falling away and leaving philosophy forlorn and unwedded, themselves live an unreal and alien life, while other unworthy wooers rush in and defile her as an orphan bereft of her kin, and attach to her such reproaches as you say her revilers taunt her with, declaring that some of her consorts are of no account and the many accountable for many evils. Why, yes, he replied, that is what they do say. And plausibly, said I; for other mannikins, observing that the place is unoccupied and full of fine terms and pretensions,

greek_head: δὲ ὀνομάτων καὶ προσχημάτων μεστήν ὥσπερ οἱ ἐκ τῶν εἱργμῶν εἰς τὰ ἱερὰ ἀποδιδράσκοντες ἅσμενοι καὶ οὗτοι
greek_tokens: de onoma kai prosxhma mestos wsper e ek o eirgmos eis o iera apodidraskw asmenos kai outos ek o texnh ekphdaw eis o filosofia e an komyos eimi tugxanw peri o eautou texnion omws gar dh pros ge o allos texnh kaiper outws prassw filosofia o aciwma megaloprephs leipw e dh efihmi polus atelhs men o fusis upo de o texnh te kai dhmiourgia wsper o swma lwbaomai outws
english_head: just as men escape from prison to take sanctuary in temples, so these gentlemen joyously bound away from the mechanical arts to philosophy, those that are most cunning in their little craft. For in comparison with the other arts the prestige of philosophy even in her present low estate retains a superior dignity; and this is the ambition and aspiration of that multitude of pretenders unfit by nature, whose souls are bowed and mutilated by their vulgar occupations

greek_head: καὶ τὰς ψυχὰς συγκεκλασμένοι τε καὶ ἀποτεθρυμμένοι διὰ τὰς βαναυσίας τυγχάνουσιν ἢ οὐκ ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: kai o yuxh sugklaw te kai apoqruptw dia o banausia tugxanw h ou anagkh kai mala fhmi dokew oun tis eimi de egw diaferw autos eidon argurion ktaomai xalkeos falakroomai kai mikros newsti men ek desmh luw en balaneion de louw neouogon imation exw ws numfios paraskeuazw dia penia kai erhmia o despoths o qugathr mellw gamew
english_head: even as their bodies are marred by their arts and crafts. Is not that inevitable? Quite so, he said. Is not the picture which they present, I said, precisely that of a little bald-headed tinker who has made money and just been freed from bonds and had a bath and is wearing a new garment and has got himself up like a bridegroom and is about to marry his master’s daughter

greek_head: Οὐ πάνυ ἔφη διαφέρει Ποῖ' ἄττα οὖν εἰκὸς γεννᾶν τοὺς τοιούτους οὐ νόθα καὶ
greek_tokens: ou panu fhmi diaferw poa atta oun eoika genna o toioutos ou noqos kai faulos polus anagkh tis de o anacios paideusis otan autos plhsiazw omilew mh kata acios poa atta fhmi genna dianohma te kai doca ara ou ws alhqhs proshkw akew sofisma kai oudeis gnhsios oude fronhsis acios alhqinos exw pantelhs men oun fhmi pansmikros dh tis fhmi egw eimi adeimantos leipw o
english_head: who has fallen into poverty and abandonment? There is no difference at all, he said. Of what sort will probably be the offspring of such parents? Will they not be bastard and base? Inevitably. And so when men unfit for culture approach philosophy and consort with her unworthily, what sort of ideas and opinions shall we say they beget? Will they not produce what may in very deed be fairly called sophisms, and nothing that is genuine or that partakes of true intelligence? Quite so, he said. There is a very small remnant, then, Adeimantus, I said,

greek_head: κατ' ἀξίαν ὁμιλούντων φιλοσοφίᾳ ἤ που ὑπὸ φυγῆς καταληφθὲν γενναῖον καὶ εὖ τεθραμμένον ἦθος ἀπορίᾳ τῶν
greek_tokens: kata acios omilew filosofia h pou upo fugh katalambanw gennaios kai eu trefw hqos aporia o diafqeirw kata fus menw epi autos h en mikros polis otan megas yuxh fuw kai atimaw o o polis upereidon braxus de pou tis kai apo allos texnh dikaios atimazw eufuhs epi autos an erxomai eaw de an kai o o hmeteros etairos qeagous xalinos oios katasxaw kai gar
english_head: of those who consort worthily with philosophy, some well-born and well-bred nature, it may be, held in check by exile, and so in the absence of corrupters remaining true to philosophy, as its quality bids, or it may happen that a great soul born in a little town scorns and disregards its parochial affairs; and a small group perhaps might by natural affinity be drawn to it from other arts which they justly disdain; and the bridle of our companion Theages also might operate as a restraint. For in the case of Theages all other conditions were at hand

greek_head: Θεάγει τὰ μὲν ἄλλα πάντα παρεσκεύασται πρὸς τὸ ἐκπεσεῖν φιλοσοφίας ἡ δὲ τοῦ σώματος νοσοτροφία ἀπείργουσα αὐτὸν
greek_tokens: qeagei o men allos pas paraskeuazw pros o ekpitnw filosofia o de o swma nosotrofia apeirgw autos o politikos kataxew o de hmeteros ou acios legw o daimonion shmeion h gar pou tis allos h oudeis o emprosqen gignomai kai outos dh o oligos e gignomai kai geuw ws hdus kai makarios o kthma kai o polus au ikanos eidon o mania kai ostis oudeis oudeis ugihs ws epos eipon peri o o polis prassw oude eimi
english_head: for his backsliding from philosophy, but his sickly habit of body keeping him out of politics holds him back. My own case, the divine sign, is hardly worth mentioning—for I suppose it has happened to few or none before me. And those who have been of this little company and have tasted the sweetness and blessedness of this possession and who have also come to understand the madness of the multitude sufficiently and have seen that there is nothing, if I may say so, sound or right in any present politics, and that there is no ally

greek_head: σύμμαχος μεθ' ὅτου τις ἰὼν ἐπὶ τὴν τῷ δικαίῳ βοήθειαν σῴζοιτ' ἄν ἀλλ' ὥσπερ εἰς θηρία ἄνθρωπος ἐμπεσών οὔτε
greek_tokens: summaxos meqo ostis tis eimi epi o o dikaios bohqeia swzw an alla wsper eis qhrion anqrwpos empitnw oute sunadikew eqelw oute ikanos eimi eis pas agrios antexw prin tis o polis h filos oninhmi proapollumai anwfelhs eautou te kai o allos an gignomai outos pas logismos lambanw hsuxia exw kai o eautou prassw oios en xeimwn koniortos kai zalh upo pneuma ferw upo teixew afisthmi oraw o allos katapimplhmi anomia agapaw ei ph autos kaqaros adikia te kai anosios
english_head: with whose aid the champion of justice could escape destruction, but that he would be as a man who has fallen among wild beasts, unwilling to share their misdeeds and unable to hold out singly against the savagery of all, and that he would thus, before he could in any way benefit his friends or the state come to an untimely end without doing any good to himself or others,—for all these reasons I say the philosopher remains quiet, minds his own affair, and, as it were, standing aside under shelter of a wall in a storm and blast of dust and sleet and seeing others filled full of lawlessness, is content if in any way

greek_head: ἔργων τόν τε ἐνθάδε βίον βιώσεται καὶ τὴν ἀπαλλαγὴν αὐτοῦ μετὰ καλῆς ἐλπίδος ἵλεώς τε καὶ εὐμενὴς ἀπαλλάξεται
greek_tokens: ergnumi o te enqade bios biow kai o apallagh autos meta kalew elpis ilaos te kai eumenhs apallassw
english_head: he may keep himself free from iniquity and unholy deeds through this life and take his departure with fair hope, serene and well content when the end comes. Well, he said, that is no very slight thing

greek_head: Ἀλλά τοι ἦ δ' ὅς οὐ τὰ ἐλάχιστα ἂν διαπραξάμενος ἀπαλλάττοιτο
