Quite so, he said. Yes, by Zeus, said I, my friend. In most of them, when there is occasion to spend the money of others, you will discover the existence of drone-like appetites. Most emphatically. Such a man, then, would not be free from internal dissension. He would not be really one, but in some sort a double man. Yet for the most part,

greek_head: κρατούσας ἂν ἔχοι βελτίους χειρόνων Ἔστιν οὕτω
greek_tokens: kratew an exw beltiwn xeirwn eimi outws dia outos dh oimh eusxhmwn an polus o toioutos eaw omonohtikos de kai armozw o yuxazw alhqhs areth prosw poi ekfeugw an autos dokew egw kai meis antagwnisths ge idios en polis o feidwlos
english_head: his better desires would have the upper hand over the worse. It is so. And for this reason, I presume, such a man would be more seemly, more respectable, than many others; but the true virtue of a soul in unison and harmony with itself would escape him and dwell afar. I think so. And again, the thrifty stingy man would be a feeble competitor personally

greek_head: φαῦλος ἤ τινος νίκης ἢ ἄλλης φιλοτιμίας τῶν καλῶν χρήματά τε οὐκ ἐθέλων εὐδοξίας ἕνεκα καὶ τῶν τοιούτων ἀγώνων
greek_tokens: faulos h tis nikh h allos filotimia o kalh xrhma te ou eqelw eudocia eneka kai o toioutos agwnos analiskw deidw o epiqumia o analwtikos egeirw kai sumparakalew epi summaxia te kai filoneikia oligos tis eautou polemew oligarxikos o polus hssaomai kai ploutew kai mala fhmi eti oun eimi de egw apistew mh kata o oligarxew polis omoioths o feidwlos te kai xrhmatisths
english_head: in the city for any prize of victory or in any other honorable emulation. He is unwilling to spend money for fame and rivalries of that sort, and, fearing to awaken his prodigal desires and call them into alliance for the winning of the victory, he fights in true oligarchical fashion with a small part of his resources and is defeated for the most part and—finds himself rich! Yes indeed, he said. Have we any further doubt, then, I said, as to the correspondence and resemblance between the thrifty and money-making man

greek_head: τετάχθαι Οὐδαμῶς ἔφη
greek_tokens: tassw oudamos fhmi dhmokratia dh ws eoika meta outos skepteos tis te gignomai tropos gignomai te poios tis exis ina au o o toioutos anhr tropos gignwskw paristhmi autos eis krisis omoios goun an fhmi egw autos poreuw oukoun eimi de egw metaballw men tropos tis toiosde ek oligarxia eis dhmokratia dia aplhstia o prokeimai agaqos o ws plousios dew gignomai pws dh
english_head: and the oligarchical state? None, he said. We have next to consider, it seems, the origin and nature of democracy, that we may next learn the character of that type of man and range him beside the others for our judgement. That would at least be a consistent procedure. Then, said I, is not the transition from oligarchy to democracy effected in some such way as this—by the insatiate greed for that which it set before itself as the good, the attainment of the greatest possible wealth?

greek_head: Ἅτε οἶμαι ἄρχοντες ἐν αὐτῇ οἱ ἄρχοντες διὰ τὸ πολλὰ κεκτῆσθαι οὐκ ἐθέλουσιν εἴργειν νόμῳ τῶν νέων ὅσοι ἂν
greek_tokens: ate oimh arxw en autos e arxw dia o polus ktaomai ou eqelw ergw nomos o neos osos an akolastos gignomai mh ecesti autos analiskw te kai apollumi o eautou ina wneomai o o toioutos kai eisdaneizw eti plousios kai entimos gignomai pas ge mallon oukoun dhlos hdh outos en polis ostis ploutos timaw kai swfrosunos ama ikanos ktaomai en o poliths
english_head: In what way? Why, since its rulers owe their offices to their wealth, they are not willing to prohibit by law the prodigals who arise among the youth from spending and wasting their substance. Their object is, by lending money on the property of such men, and buying it in, to become still richer and more esteemed. By all means. And is it not at once apparent in a state that this honoring of wealth is incompatible with a sober and temperate citizenship,

greek_head: ἀδύνατον ἀλλ' ἀνάγκη ἢ τοῦ ἑτέρου ἀμελεῖν ἢ τοῦ ἑτέρου Ἐπιεικῶς ἔφη δῆλον
greek_tokens: adunatos alla anagkh h o eteros amelew h o eteros epieikhs fhmi dhlos paramelew dh en o oligarxia kai efihmi akolastainw ou agennhs eniote anqrwpos penhs anagkazw gignomai mala ge kaqhmai dh oimh outos en o polis kentrow te kai ecoplizw e men ofeilw xrea e de atimos gignomai e de amfoteros misew te kai epibouleuw o ktaomai o eautou kai o allos newterismos
english_head: but that one or the other of these two ideals is inevitably neglected. That is pretty clear, he said. And such negligence and encouragement of licentiousness in oligarchies not infrequently has reduced to poverty men of no ignoble quality. It surely has. And there they sit, I fancy, within the city, furnished with stings, that is, arms, some burdened with debt, others disfranchised, others both, hating and conspiring against the acquirers of their estates and the rest of the citizens,

greek_head: ἐρῶντες Ἔστι ταῦτα
greek_tokens: eraw eimi outos e de dh xrhmatisths egkuptw oude dokew outos oraw o loipazw o aei upeikw enihmi argurion titrwskw kai o pathr ekgonos tokos pollaplasios
english_head: and eager for revolution. ’Tis so. But these money-makers with down-bent heads, pretending not even to see them, but inserting the sting of their money into any of the remainder who do not resist, and harvesting from them in interest as it were a manifold progeny of the parent sum,

greek_head: κομιζόμενοι πολὺν τὸν κηφῆνα καὶ πτωχὸν ἐμποιοῦσι τῇ πόλει
greek_tokens: komizw polus o khfhn kai ptwxos empoiew o polis pws gar fhmi ou polus kai oute ge ekeinos eimi de egw o toioutos kakos ekkaiw eqelw aposbennumi ergw o eautou oph tis boulomai trepw oute ode h au kata eteros nomos o toioutos luw kata dh tis os meta ekeinos eimi deuteros kai anagkazw aretaw epimeleomai o poliths ean gar epi o eautou kindunos
english_head: foster the drone and pauper element in the state. They do indeed multiply it, he said. And they are not willing to quench the evil as it bursts into flame either by way of a law prohibiting a man from doing as he likes with his own, or in this way, by a second law that does away with such abuses. What law? The law that is next best, and compels the citizens to pay heed to virtue. For if a law commanded that most voluntary contracts should be at the contractor’s risk,

greek_head: τὰ πολλά τις τῶν ἑκουσίων συμβολαίων προστάττῃ συμβάλλειν χρηματίζοιντο μὲν ἂν ἧττον ἀναιδῶς ἐν τῇ πόλει
greek_tokens: o polus tis o ekousios sumbolaion prostassw sumballw xrhmatizw men an hsswn anaidhs en o polis elasswn de en autos fuw o toioutos kakh oios nundi eipon kai polus ge eimi de os nun de ge fhmi egw dia pas o toioutos o men dh arxw outws diatiqhmi en o polis e arxw sfazw de autos kai o eautou ara ou trufaw men o neos kai aponos kai pros o o swma kai pros
english_head: the pursuit of wealth would be less shameless in the state and fewer of the evils of which we spoke just now would grow up there. Much fewer, he said. But as it is, and for all these reasons, this is the plight to which the rulers in the state reduce their subjects, and as for themselves and their off-spring, do they not make the young spoiled wantons averse to toil of body and mind,

greek_head: τὰ τῆς ψυχῆς μαλακοὺς δὲ καρτερεῖν πρὸς ἡδονάς τε καὶ λύπας καὶ ἀργούς
greek_tokens: o o yuxazw malakos de karterew pros hdona te kai luph kai argos tis meis eautou de plhn xrhmatismos o allos amelew kai oudeis plew epimeleia poiew aretaw h o penhs ou gar oun outws dh paraskeuazw otan paraballw allhlwn e te arxw kai e arxw h en odaw poreia h en allos tis koinwnia h kata qewria h kata strateia h sumplous gignomai h sustratiwths h kai en autos o
english_head: and too soft to stand up against pleasure and pain, and mere idlers? Surely. And do they not fasten upon themselves the habit of neglect of everything except the making of money, and as complete an indifference to virtue as the paupers exhibit? Little they care. And when, thus conditioned, the rulers and the ruled are brought together on the march, in wayfaring, or in some other common undertaking, either a religious festival, or a campaign, or as shipmates or fellow-soldiers

greek_head: κινδύνοις ἀλλήλους θεώμενοι μηδαμῇ ταύτῃ καταφρονῶνται οἱ πένητες ὑπὸ τῶν πλουσίων ἀλλὰ πολλάκις ἰσχνὸς ἀνὴρ
greek_tokens: kindunos allhlwn qeaomai mhdamh outos katafronew e penhs upo o plousios alla pollakis isxnos anhr penhs hlioomai paratassw en maxh plousios skiatrofew polus exw sarc allotrios eidon asqma te kai aporia mestos ara oiomai autos ou hgeomai kakia o sfeteros ploutew o toioutos kai allos allos paraggellw otan idios suggignomai ostis anhr hmeteros
english_head: or, for that matter, in actual battle, and observe one another, then the poor are not in the least scorned by the rich, but on the contrary, do you not suppose it often happens that when a lean, sinewy, sunburnt pauper is stationed in battle beside a rich man bred in the shade, and burdened with superfluous flesh, and sees him panting and helpless—do you not suppose he will think that such fellows keep their wealth by the cowardice of the poor, and that when the latter are together in private,

greek_head: εἰσὶ γὰρ οὐδέν Εὖ οἶδα μὲν οὖν ἔφη ἔγωγε ὅτι οὕτω ποιοῦσιν
greek_tokens: eimi gar oudeis eu oida men oun fhmi egw ostis outws poiew oukoun wsper swma noswdhs mikros roph ecwqen deomai proslambanw pros o kamnw eniote de kai aneu o ecw stasiazw autos eautou outws dh kai o kata tautos ekeinos diakeimai polis apo mikros profasis ecwqen epagw h o eteros ek oligarxew polis summaxia h o eteros ek dhmokrateomai nosew te kai autos eautou maxomai eniote de kai aneu o ecw stasiazw
english_head: one will pass the word to another our men are good for nothing? Nay, I know very well that they do, said he. And just as an unhealthy body requires but a slight impulse from outside to fall into sickness, and sometimes, even without that, all the man is one internal war, in like manner does not the corresponding type of state need only a slight occasion, the one party bringing in allies from an oligarchical state, or the other from a democratic, to become diseased and wage war with itself, and sometimes even

greek_head: Καὶ σφόδρα γε Δημοκρατία δὴ οἶμαι γίγνεται ὅταν οἱ πένητες νικήσαντες
greek_tokens: kai sfodra ge dhmokratia dh oimh gignomai otan e penhs nikaw o men apokteinw o eteros o de ekballw o de loipos ek isos metadidwmi politeia te kai arxh kai ws o polus apo klhros o arxh en autos gignomai eimi gar fhmi outos o katastasis dhmokratia ean kai dia oplon gignomai ean kai dia fobos upecerxomai o eteros tis dh oun eimi de egw outos tropos oikew kai poa
english_head: apart from any external impulse faction arises? Most emphatically. And a democracy, I suppose, comes into being when the poor, winning the victory, put to death some of the other party, drive out others, and grant the rest of the citizens an equal share in both citizenship and offices—and for the most part these offices are assigned by lot. Why, yes, he said, that is the constitution of democracy alike whether it is established by force of arms or by terrorism resulting in the withdrawal of one of the parties. What, then, said I, is the manner of their life

greek_head: τις ἡ τοιαύτη αὖ πολιτεία δῆλον γὰρ ὅτι ὁ τοιοῦτος ἀνὴρ δημοκρατικός τις ἀναφανήσεται
greek_tokens: tis o toioutos au politeia dhlos gar ostis o toioutos anhr dhmokratikos tis anafainw dhlos fhmi oukoun proteros men dh eleuqeros kai eleuqeria o polis mestos kai parrhsia gignomai kai ecousia en autos poiew ostis tis boulomai legw ge dh fhmi opou de ge ecousia dhlos ostis idios ekastos an kataskeuh o eautou bios kataskeuazw en autos ostis ekastos areskw dhlos
english_head: and what is the quality of such a constitution? For it is plain that the man of this quality will turn out to be a democratic sort of man. It is plain, he said. To begin with, are they not free? and is not the city chock-full of liberty and freedom of speech? and has not every man licence to do as he likes? So it is said, he replied. And where there is such licence, it is obvious that everyone would arrange a plan for leading his own life in the way that pleases him. Obvious. All sorts and conditions of men,

greek_head: Παντοδαποὶ δὴ ἂν οἶμαι ἐν ταύτῃ τῇ πολιτείᾳ μάλιστ' ἐγγίγνοιντο ἄνθρωποι
greek_tokens: pantodapos dh an oimh en outos o politeia malista eggignomai anqrwpos pws gar ou kinduneuw eimi de egw kalos outos o politeia eimi wsper imation poikilos pas anqos poikillw outws kai outos pas hqos poikillw kalos an fainw kai isos men eimi de egw kai outos wsper e pais te kai o gunh o poikilos qeaomai kalos an polus krinw kai mala fhmi
english_head: then, would arise in this polity more than in any other? Of course. Possibly, said I, this is the most beautiful of polities as a garment of many colors, embroidered with all kinds of hues, so this, decked and diversified with every type of character, would appear the most beautiful. And perhaps, I said, many would judge it to be the most beautiful, like boys and women when they see bright-colored things.

greek_head: Καὶ ἔστιν γε ὦ μακάριε ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἐπιτήδειον ζητεῖν ἐν αὐτῇ πολιτείαν
greek_tokens: kai eimi ge eimi makarios eimi de egw epithdeios zhtew en autos politeia tis dh ostis pas genos politeia exis dia o ecousia kai kinduneuw o boulomai polis kataskeuazw o nundi egw epoizw anagkaion eimi eis dhmokrateomai erxomai polis os an autos areskos tropos outos eklegw wsper eis pantopwlion afikneomai politeia kai eklegw outws katoikizw
english_head: Yes indeed, he said. Yes, said I, and it is the fit place, my good friend, in which to look for a constitution. Why so? Because, owing to this licence, it includes all kinds, and it seems likely that anyone who wishes to organize a state, as we were just now doing, must find his way to a democratic city and select the model that pleases him, as if in a bazaar of constitutions, and after making his choice, establish his own. Perhaps at any rate, he said,

greek_head: Ἴσως γοῦν ἔφη οὐκ ἂν ἀποροῖ παραδειγμάτων Τὸ δὲ μηδεμίαν ἀνάγκην εἶπον εἶναι ἄρχειν ἐν ταύτῃ
greek_tokens: isos goun fhmi ou an aforaw paradeigma o de mhdeis anagkh eipon eimi arxw en outos o polis mhde an eimi ikanos arxw mhde au arxw ean mh boulomai mhde polemew polemew mhde eirhnh agw o allos agw ean mh epiqumew eirhnh mhde au ean tis arxw nomos su diakwluw h dikazw mhdeis hsswn kai
english_head: he would not be at a loss for patterns. And the freedom from all compulsion to hold office in such a city, even if you are qualified, or again, to submit to rule, unless you please, or to make war when the rest are at war, or to keep the peace when the others do so, unless you desire peace; and again, the liberty, in defiance of any law that forbids you, to hold office and sit on juries none the less,

greek_head: ἄρχειν καὶ δικάζειν ἐὰν αὐτῷ σοι ἐπίῃ ἆρ' οὐ θεσπεσία καὶ ἡδεῖα ἡ τοιαύτη διαγωγὴ ἐν τῷ παραυτίκα
greek_tokens: arxw kai dikazw ean autos su efhmai ara ou qespesios kai hdus o toioutos diagwgh en o parautika isos fhmi en ge outos tis de o praoths enioi o dikazw ou komyos h oupw eidon en toioutos politeia anqrwpos katayhfizomai qanatos h fugh oudeis hsswn autos menw te kai anastrefw en mesos kai ws oute frontizw oute oraw oudeis perinostew wsper hrws kai polus ge fhmi
english_head: if it occurs to you to do so, is not all that a heavenly and delicious entertainment for the time being? Perhaps, he said, for so long. And is not the placability of some convicted criminals exquisite? Or have you never seen in such a state men condemned to death or exile who none the less stay on, and go to and fro among the people, and as if no one saw or heeded him, the man slips in and out like a revenant? Yes, many, he said. And the tolerance of democracy,

greek_head: Ἡ δὲ συγγνώμη καὶ οὐδ' ὁπωστιοῦν σμικρολογία αὐτῆς ἀλλὰ καταφρόνησις ὧν ἡμεῖς ἐλέγομεν σεμνύνοντες ὅτε τὴν
greek_tokens: o de suggnwmh kai oude opwsoun mikrologia autos alla katafronhsis os egw legw semnunw oste o polis oikizw ws ei mh tis uperballw fus exw oupote an gignomai anhr agaqos ei mh pas eimi euqus paizw en kalew kai epithdeuw o toioutos pas ws megaloprephs katapatew apas autos oudeis frontizw ek opoios an tis epithdeuma epi o politikos eimi prassw alla timaw
english_head: its superiority to all our meticulous requirements, its disdain or our solemn pronouncements made when we were founding our city, that except in the case of transcendent natural gifts no one could ever become a good man unless from childhood his play and all his pursuits were concerned with things fair and good,—how superbly it tramples under foot all such ideals, caring nothing from what practices and way of life a man turns to politics, but honoring him

greek_head: ἐὰν φῇ μόνον εὔνους εἶναι τῷ πλήθει Πάνυ γ' ἔφη γενναία
greek_tokens: ean fhmi monos eunous eimi o plhqw panu ge fhmi gennaios outos te dh fhmi exw an kai outos allos adelfh dhmokratia kai eaw ws eoika hdus politeia kai anarxos kai poikilos isoths tis omoios isos te kai anisos dianemw kai mala fhmi gnwrimos legw aqrew dh eimi de egw tis o toioutos idios h proteros skepteos wsper o politeia skeptomai tis tropos gignomai nai fhmi ara oun ou wde o feidwlos ekeinos kai oligarxikos
english_head: if only he says that he loves the people! It is a noble polity, indeed! he said. These and qualities akin to these democracy would exhibit, and it would, it seems, be a delightful form of government, anarchic and motley, assigning a kind of equality indiscriminately to equals and unequals alike! Yes, he said, everybody knows that. Observe, then, the corresponding private character. Or must we first, as in the case of the polity, consider the origin of the type? Yes, he said. Is not this, then, the way of it? Our thrifty oligarchical man

greek_head: γένοιτ' ἂν οἶμαι ὑὸς ὑπὸ τῷ πατρὶ τεθραμμένος ἐν τοῖς ἐκείνου ἤθεσι
greek_tokens: gignomai an oimh us upo o pathr trefw en o ekeinos hqos tis gar ou bia dh kai outos arxw o en eautou hdona osos analwtikos men xrhmatistikos de mh o dh ou anagkh kalew dhlos fhmi boulomai oun eimi de egw ina mh skoteinos dialegw proteros orizw o te anagkaios epiqumia kai o mh boulomai eimi de os oukoun os te ou an oios su eimi apotrepw dikaios
english_head: would have a son bred in his father’s ways. Why not? And he, too, would control by force all his appetites for pleasure that are wasters and not winners of wealth, those which are denominated unnecessary. Obviously. And in order not to argue in the dark, shall we first define our distinction between necessary and unnecessary appetites? Let us do so. Well, then, desires that we cannot divert or suppress may be properly called necessary,

greek_head: ἂν ἀναγκαῖαι καλοῖντο καὶ ὅσαι ἀποτελούμεναι ὠφελοῦσιν ἡμᾶς τούτων γὰρ ἀμφοτέρων ἐφίεσθαι ἡμῶν τῇ φύσει
greek_tokens: an anagkh kalew kai osos apotelew wfelew egw outos gar amfoteros efihmi egw o fusis anagkh h ou kai mala
english_head: and likewise those whose satisfaction is beneficial to us, may they not? For our nature compels us to seek their satisfaction.

greek_head: Δικαίως δὴ τοῦτο ἐπ' αὐταῖς ἐροῦμεν τὸ ἀναγκαῖον Δικαίως
greek_tokens: dikaios dh outos epi autos erew o anagkaion dikaios tis de os ge tis apallassw an ei meletaw ek neos kai pros oudeis agaqos eneimi draw o de kai enantion passw outos ei mh anagkaios fhmi eimi ara ou kalos an legw kalos men oun proairew dh tis paradeigma ekateros o eimi ina tupon lambanw autos oukoun xrh ara oun ou o o esqiw mexri ugieia te kai euecia
english_head: Is not that so ? Most assuredly. Then we shall rightly use the word necessary of them? Rightly. And what of the desires from which a man could free himself by discipline from youth up, and whose presence in the soul does no good and in some cases harm? Should we not fairly call all such unnecessary? Fairly indeed. Let us select an example of either kind, so that we may apprehend the type. Let us do so. Would not the desire of eating to keep in health and condition and the appetite

greek_head: καὶ αὐτοῦ σίτου τε καὶ ὄψου ἀναγκαῖος ἂν εἴη Οἶμαι
greek_tokens: kai autos sitos te kai oyon anagkaios an eaw oimh o men ge pou o sitos kata amfoteros anagkh h te wfelimos h te mh pauw zaw dunatos nai o de oyon ei ph tis wfeleia pros euecia parexw panu men oun tis de o pera outos kai alloios edesma h toioutos epiqumia dunatos de kolazw ek neos kai paideuw ek o polus apallassw kai blaberos men swma blaberos de yuxw pros te fronhsis kai o swfronew ara
english_head: for mere bread and relishes be necessary? I think so. The appetite for bread is necessary in both respects, in that it is beneficial and in that if it fails we die. Yes. And the desire for relishes, so far as it conduces to fitness? By all means. And should we not rightly pronounce unnecessary the appetite that exceeds these and seeks other varieties of food, and that by correction and training from youth up can be got rid of in most cases and is harmful to the body and a hindrance to the soul’s attainment of

greek_head: γε ὀρθῶς οὐκ ἀναγκαία ἂν καλοῖτο Ὀρθότατα μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: ge orqos ou anagkh an kalew orqos men oun oukoun kai analwtikos fhmi eimi outos ekeinos de xrhmatistikos dia o xrhsimos pros o ergon eimi tis meis outws dh kai peri afrodisios kai o allos fhmi outws ara oun kai os nundi khfhn onomazw outos legw o o toioutos hdona kai epiqumia gemw kai
english_head: intelligence and sobriety? Nay, most rightly. And may we not call the one group the spendthrift desires and the other the profitable, because they help production? Surely. And we shall say the same of sexual and other appetites? The same. And were we not saying that the man whom we nicknamed the drone is the man who teems with such pleasures and appetites, and who is governed by his unnecessary desires, while the one who is ruled

greek_head: ἀρχόμενον ὑπὸ τῶν μὴ ἀναγκαίων τὸν δὲ ὑπὸ τῶν ἀναγκαίων φειδωλόν τε καὶ ὀλιγαρχικόν
greek_tokens: arxw upo o mh anagkaion o de upo o anagkaion feidwlos te kai oligarxikos alla tis meis palin toinun eimi de egw legw ws ek oligarxikos dhmokratikos gignomai fainw de egw o ge polus wde gignomai pws otan neos trefw ws nundi legw apaideutos te kai feidwlos geuw khfhn meli kai suggignomai aiqw qhr kai deinos pantodapos hdona kai poikillw kai pantoios exw dunamai skeuazw entauqa pou
english_head: by his necessary appetites is the thrifty oligarchical man? Why, surely. To return, then, said I, we have to tell how the democratic man develops from the oligarchical type. I think it is usually in this way. How? When a youth, bred in the illiberal and niggardly fashion that we were describing, gets a taste of the honey of the drones and associates with fierce and cunning creatures who know how to purvey pleasures of every kind and variety and condition, there you must doubtless conceive is the beginning

greek_head: οἴου εἶναι ἀρχὴν αὐτῷ μεταβολῆς ὀλιγαρχικῆς τῆς ἑαυτῷ εἰς δημοκρατικήν
greek_tokens: oiomai eimi arxh autos metaboleus oligarxikos o eautou eis dhmokratikos polus anagkh fhmi ara oun wsper o polis metaballw bohqew o eteros meros summaxia ecwqen omoios omoios outws kai o neanihs metaballw bohqew au eidos epiqumia ecwqen o eteros o para ekeinos suggenhs te kai omoios pantapasi men oun kai ean men ge oimh antiboaw tis o en eautou oligarxikos summaxia h poqen para o pathr h kai o
english_head: of the transformation of the oligarchy in his soul into democracy. Quite inevitably, he said. May we not say that just as the revolution in the city was brought about by the aid of an alliance from outside, coming to the support of the similar and corresponding party in the state, so the youth is revolutionized when a like and kindred group of appetites from outside comes to the aid of one of the parties in his soul? By all means, he said. And if, I take it, a counter-alliance comes to the rescue of the oligarchical part of his soul, either it may be from his father

greek_head: ἄλλων οἰκείων νουθετούντων τε καὶ κακιζόντων στάσις δὴ καὶ ἀντίστασις καὶ μάχη ἐν αὐτῷ πρὸς αὑτὸν τότε γίγνεται
greek_tokens: allos oikew nouqetew te kai kakizw stasis dh kai antistasis kai maxh en autos pros eautou tote gignomai tis meis kai pote men oimh o dhmokratikos upoxwrew o oligarxikos kai tis o epiqumia o men diafqeirw o de kai ekpitnw aidw tis eggignomai en o o neos yuxw kai katakosmew palin gignomai gar eniote fhmi auqis de oimh o ekpitnw epiqumia allos upotrefw
english_head: or from his other kin, who admonish and reproach him, then there arises faction and counter-faction and internal strife in the man with himself. Surely. And sometimes, I suppose, the democratic element retires before the oligarchical, some of its appetites having been destroyed and others expelled, and a sense of awe and reverence grows up in the young man’s soul and order is restored. That sometimes happens, he said. And sometimes, again, another brood of desires akin to those expelled

greek_head: συγγενεῖς δι' ἀνεπιστημοσύνην τροφῆς πατρὸς πολλαί τε καὶ ἰσχυραὶ ἐγένοντο
greek_tokens: suggeneus dia anepisthmosunh trefw pathr polus te kai isxuros gignomai filew goun fhmi outws gignomai oukoun elkw te pros o autos omilia kai laqrh suggignomai plhqos entiktw tis meis teleutaw dh oimh katalambanw o o neos o yuxazw akropolis aisqanomai kenos maqhma te kai epithdeuma kalh kai logos alhqhs e dh aristos frouros te kai fulac en androw qeofilhs eimi dianoia
english_head: are stealthily nurtured to take their place, owing to the father’s ignorance of true education, and wax numerous and strong. Yes, that is wont to be the way of it. And they tug and pull back to the same associations and in secret intercourse engender a multitude. Yes indeed. And in the end, I suppose, they seize the citadel of the young man’s soul, finding it empty and unoccupied by studies and honorable pursuits and true discourses, which are the best watchmen

greek_head: Καὶ πολύ γ' ἔφη Ψευδεῖς δὴ καὶ ἀλαζόνες οἶμαι λόγοι τε καὶ δόξαι ἀντ'
greek_tokens: kai polus ge fhmi yeudhs dh kai alazwn oimh logos te kai doca anti ekeinos anatrexw katexw o autos topos o toioutos sfodra ge fhmi ara oun ou palin te eis ekeinos o lwtofagoi erxomai faneros katoikew kai ean para oikew tis bohqeia o feidwlos autos o yuxazw afikneomai kleiw e alazwn logos ekeinos o o basilikos teixos en autos pulh oute autos o summaxia parihmi oute presbis presbus
english_head: and guardians in the minds of men who are dear to the gods. Much the best, he said. And then false and braggart words and opinions charge up the height and take their place and occupy that part of such a youth. They do indeed. And then he returns, does he not, to those Lotus-eaters and without disguise lives openly with them. And if any support comes from his kin to the thrifty element in his soul, those braggart discourses close the gates of the royal fortress within him

greek_head: λόγους ἰδιωτῶν εἰσδέχονται αὐτοί τε κρατοῦσι μαχόμενοι καὶ τὴν μὲν αἰδῶ ἠλιθιότητα ὀνομάζοντες ὠθοῦσιν ἔξω
greek_tokens: logos idiwths eisdexomai autos te kratew maxomai kai o men aidw hliqioths onomazw wqew ecw atimos fugas swfrosunos de anandria kalew te kai prophlakizw ekballw metrioths de kai kosmios dapanh ws agroikia kai aneleuqeria eimi peiqw uperorizw meta polus kai anwfelhs epiqumia sfodra ge outos de ge pou kenow kai kaqairw o o
english_head: and refuse admission to the auxiliary force itself, and will not grant audience as to envoys to the words of older friends in private life. And they themselves prevail in the conflict, and naming reverence and awe folly thrust it forth, a dishonored fugitive. And temperance they call want of manhood and banish it with contumely, and they teach that moderation and orderly expenditure are rusticity and illiberality, and they combine with a gang of unprofitable and harmful appetites to drive them over the border. They do indeed. And when they have emptied

greek_head: κατεχομένου τε ὑπ' αὐτῶν καὶ τελουμένου ψυχὴν μεγάλοισι τέλεσι τὸ μετὰ τοῦτο ἤδη ὕβριν καὶ ἀναρχίαν καὶ ἀσωτίαν
greek_tokens: katexw te upo autos kai tellw yuxh megas telesis o meta outos hdh ubris kai anarxia kai aswtia kai anaideia lampros meta polus xoros katagw stefanow egkwmiazw kai upokorizomai ubris men eupaideusia kalew anarxia de eleuqeria aswtia
english_head: and purged of all these the soul of the youth that they have thus possessed and occupied, and whom they are initiating with these magnificent and costly rites, they proceed to lead home from exile insolence and anarchy and prodigality and shamelessness, resplendent in a great attendant choir and crowned with garlands, and in celebration of their praises they euphemistically denominate insolence good breeding, licence liberty, prodigality magnificence,

greek_head: δὲ μεγαλοπρέπειαν ἀναίδειαν δὲ ἀνδρείαν ἆρ' οὐχ οὕτω πως ἦν δ' ἐγώ νέος ὢν μεταβάλλει ἐκ τοῦ ἐν ἀναγκαίοις
greek_tokens: de megaloprepeia anaideia de andreia ara ou outws pws eimi de egw neos eimi metaballw ek o en anagkaion epiqumia trefw o o mh anagkaion kai anwfelhs hdona eleuqerwsis te kai anesis kai mala ge eimi de os enarghs zaw dh oimh meta outos o toioutos oudeis mallon eis anagkaios h mh anagkaios hdona analiskw kai xrhma kai ponos kai diatribh alla ean eutuxhs eimi kai mh pera ekbakxeuw alla tis kai presbus gignomai o polus
english_head: and shamelessness manly spirit. And is it not in some such way as this, said I, that in his youth the transformation takes place from the restriction to necessary desires in his education to the liberation and release of his unnecessary and harmful desires? Yes, your description is most vivid, said he. Then, in his subsequent life, I take it, such a one expends money and toil and time no more on his necessary than on his unnecessary pleasures. But if it is his good fortune that the period of storm and stress does not last too long, and as he grows older

greek_head: θορύβου παρελθόντος μέρη τε καταδέξηται τῶν ἐκπεσόντων καὶ τοῖς ἐπεισελθοῦσι μὴ ὅλον ἑαυτὸν ἐνδῷ εἰς ἴσον δή τι
greek_tokens: qorubos parerxomai mera te katadexomai o ekpitnw kai o epeiserxomai mh oloc eautou endidwmi eis isos dh tis kaqisthmi o hdona diagw o parapiptw aei wsper lagxanw o eautou arxh paradidwmi ews an plhrow kai auqis allh oudeis atimazw alla ek isos trefw panu men oun kai logos ge eimi de egw alhqhs ou prosdexomai oude parihmi eis o frourion ean tis legos ws o men eimi o
english_head: the fiercest tumult within him passes, and he receives back a part of the banished elements and does not abandon himself altogether to the invasion of the others, then he establishes and maintains all his pleasures on a footing of equality, forsooth, and so lives turning over the guard-house of his soul to each as it happens along until it is sated, as if it had drawn the lot for that office, and then in turn to another, disdaining none but fostering them all equally. Quite so. And he does not accept or admit into the guard-house the words of truth when anyone tells him

greek_head: καλῶν τε καὶ ἀγαθῶν ἐπιθυμιῶν ἡδοναί αἱ δὲ τῶν πονηρῶν καὶ τὰς μὲν χρὴ ἐπιτηδεύειν καὶ τιμᾶν τὰς δὲ κολάζειν
greek_tokens: kalh te kai agaqos epiqumia hdona o de o ponhros kai o men xrh epithdeuw kai timaw o de kolazw te kai doulow alla en pas outos ananeuw te kai omoios fhmi apas eimi kai timhteos ek isos sfodra gar fhmi outws diakeimai outos draw oukoun eimi de egw kai diazaw o kaqa hmeros outws xarizomai o prospitnw epiqumia tote men mequw kai kataulew auqis de udropoths kai katisxnainw
english_head: that some pleasures arise from honorable and good desires, and others from those that are base, and that we ought to practise and esteem the one and control and subdue the others; but he shakes his head at all such admonitions and avers that they are all alike and to be equally esteemed. Such is indeed his state of mind and his conduct. And does he not, said I, also live out his life in this fashion, day by day indulging the appetite of the day, now wine-bibbing and abandoning himself to the lascivious pleasing of the flute and again drinking only water and dieting;

greek_head: τοτὲ δ' αὖ γυμναζόμενος ἔστιν δ' ὅτε ἀργῶν καὶ πάντων ἀμελῶν τοτὲ δ' ὡς ἐν φιλοσοφίᾳ διατρίβων πολλάκις δὲ
greek_tokens: tote de au gumnazw eimi de oste argew kai pas amelew tote de ws en filosofia diatribw pollakis de politeuw kai anaphdaw ostis an tuxh legw te kai prassw kan pote tis polemikos zhlwsis outos ferw h xrhmatistikos epi outos au kai oute tis tacis oute anagkh epeimi autos o bios alla hdus te dh kai eleuqerios kai makarios kalh o bios outos xraomai autos dia pas
english_head: and at one time exercising his body, and sometimes idling and neglecting all things, and at another time seeming to occupy himself with philosophy. And frequently he goes in for politics and bounces up and says and does whatever enters his head. And if military men excite his emulation, thither he rushes, and if moneyed men, to that he turns, and there is no order or compulsion in his existence, but he calls this life of his the life of pleasure and freedom and happiness and

greek_head: Παντάπασιν ἦ δ' ὅς διελήλυθας βίον ἰσονομικοῦ τινος ἀνδρός
greek_tokens: pantapasi eimi de os dierxomai bios isonomikos tis anhr oimh de ge eimi de egw kai pantodapos te kai pleistos hqos mestos kai o kalos te kai poikilos wsper ekeinos o polis outos o anhr eimi os polus an kai polus zhlow o bios paradeigma politeia te kai tropos pleistos en autos exw outos gar fhmi eimi
english_head: cleaves to it to the end. That is a perfect description, he said, of a devotee of equality. I certainly think, said I, that he is a manifold man stuffed with most excellent differences, and that like that city he is the fair and many-colored one whom many a man and woman would count fortunate in his life, as containing within himself the greatest number of patterns of constitutions and qualities. Yes, that is so, he said.

greek_head: Τί οὖν τετάχθω ἡμῖν κατὰ δημοκρατίαν ὁ τοιοῦτος ἀνήρ ὡς δημοκρατικὸς ὀρθῶς ἂν προσαγορευόμενος
greek_tokens: tis oun tassw egw kata dhmokratia o toioutos anhr ws dhmokratikos orqos an prosagoreuw tassw fhmi o kalos dh eimi de egw politeia te kai o kalos anhr loipas an egw eaw dierxomai turannis te kai turannos komidh ge fhmi ferw dh tis tropos turannis eimi filos etairos gignomai ostis men gar ek dhmokratia metaballw sxedon dhlos dhlos ara oun tropos tis o autos ek te oligarxia dhmokratia
english_head: Shall we definitely assert, then, that such a man is to be ranged with democracy and would properly be designated as democratic? Let that be his place, he said. And now, said I, the fairest polity and the fairest man remain for us to describe, the tyranny and the tyrant. Certainly, he said. Come then, tell me, dear friend, how tyranny arises. That it is an outgrowth of democracy is fairly plain. Yes, plain. Is it, then, in a sense, in the same way in which democracy arises out of oligarchy that tyranny arises from democracy?

greek_head: γίγνεται καὶ ἐκ δημοκρατίας τυραννίς Πῶς
greek_tokens: gignomai kai ek dhmokratia turannis pws o protiqhmi eimi de egw agaqos kai dia o o oligarxia kaqisthmi outos de eimi uperploutos eimi gar nai o ploutos toinun aplhstia kai o o allos ameleia dia xrhmatismos autos apollumi alhqhs fhmi ara oun kai o dhmokratia orizw agaqos o outos aplhstia kai outos kataluw legw de autos tis orizw o eleuqeria eipon outos gar pou en dhmokrateomai
english_head: How is that? The good that they proposed to themselves and that was the cause of the establishment of oligarchy—it was wealth, was it not? Yes. Well, then, the insatiate lust for wealth and the neglect of everything else for the sake of money-making was the cause of its undoing. True, he said. And is not the avidity of democracy for that which is its definition and criterion of good the thing which dissolves it too? What do you say its criterion to be? Liberty, I replied; for you may hear it said that this is best managed in a democratic city,

greek_head: πόλει ἀκούσαις ἂν ὡς ἔχει τε κάλλιστον καὶ διὰ ταῦτα ἐν μόνῃ ταύτῃ ἄξιον οἰκεῖν ὅστις φύσει ἐλεύθερος
greek_tokens: polis aekwn an ws exis te kalos kai dia outos en monos outos acios oikew ostis fusis eleuqeros legw gar dh fhmi kai polus outos o rhma ara oun eimi de egw osper eimi nundi era o o toioutos aplhstia kai o o allos ameleia kai outos o politeia meqisthmi te kai paraskeuazw turannis dew pws fhmi otan oimh dhmokrateomai polis eleuqeria diyaw
english_head: and for this reason that is the only city in which a man of free spirit will care to live. Why, yes, he replied, you hear that saying everywhere. Then, as I was about to observe, is it not the excess and greed of this and the neglect of all other things that revolutionizes this constitution too and prepares the way for the necessity of a dictatorship? How? he said. Why, when a democratic city athirst for liberty gets bad cupbearers

greek_head: κακῶν οἰνοχόων προστατούντων τύχῃ καὶ πορρωτέρω τοῦ δέοντος ἀκράτου αὐτῆς μεθυσθῇ τοὺς ἄρχοντας δή ἂν μὴ
greek_tokens: kakh oinoxoos prostatew tuxh kai prosw o deon akratos autos mequskw o arxw dh an mh panu praos eimi kai polus parexw o eleuqeria kolazw aitiaomai ws miaros te kai oligarxikos draw gar fhmi outos o de ge eipon o arxw kathkoos prophlakizw ws eqelodoulos te kai oudeis eimi o de arxw men arxw arxw de arxw omoios idios te kai dhmosios epainew te kai timaw ara ou anagkh en toioutos
english_head: for its leaders and is intoxicated by drinking too deep of that unmixed wine, and then, if its so-called governors are not extremely mild and gentle with it and do not dispense the liberty unstintedly,it chastises them and accuses them of being accursed oligarchs. Yes, that is what they do, he replied. But those who obey the rulers, I said, it reviles as willing slaves and men of naught, but it commends and honors in public and private rulers who resemble subjects and subjects who are like rulers.

greek_head: πόλει ἐπὶ πᾶν τὸ τῆς ἐλευθερίας ἰέναι Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: polis epi pas o o eleuqeria eimi pws gar ou kai kataduw ge eimi de egw eimi filos eis te o idios oikia kai teleutaw mexri o qhraw o anarxia emfuw pws eimi de os o toioutos legw oios fhmi pathr men eqizw pais omoios gignomai kai fobew o uios uios de pathr kai mhte aisxunw mhte deidw o goneus ina dh eleuqeros eimi metoikos de
english_head: Is it not inevitable that in such a state the spirit of liberty should go to all lengths? Of course. And this anarchical temper, said I, my friend, must penetrate into private homes and finally enter into the very animals. Just what do we mean by that? he said. Why, I said, the father habitually tries to resemble the child and is afraid of his sons, and the son likens himself to the father and feels no awe or fear of his parents,

greek_head: ἀστῷ καὶ ἀστὸν μετοίκῳ ἐξισοῦσθαι καὶ ξένον ὡσαύτως Γίγνεται γὰρ οὕτως ἔφη
greek_tokens: astos kai astos metoikos ecisow kai cenos wsautws gignomai gar outws fhmi outos te eimi de egw kai mikros toiosde allos gignomai didaskalos te en o toioutos foithths fobew kai qwpeuw foithths te didaskalos oligwrew outws de kai paidagwgew kai oloc e men neos presbus apeikazw kai diamillaomai kai en logos kai en ergon e de gerwn sugkaqihmi o neos eutrapelia te kai xarientismos
english_head: so that he may be forsooth a free man. And the resident alien feels himself equal to the citizen and the citizen to him, and the foreigner likewise. Yes, these things do happen, he said. They do, said I, and such other trifles as these. The teacher in such case fears and fawns upon the pupils, and the pupils pay no heed to the teacher or to their overseers either. And in general the young ape their elders and vie with them in speech and action, while the old, accommodating themselves to the young,

greek_head: ἐμπίμπλανται μιμούμενοι τοὺς νέους ἵνα δὴ μὴ δοκῶσιν ἀηδεῖς εἶναι μηδὲ δεσποτικοί
greek_tokens: empiplhmi mimeomai o neos ina dh mh dokazw ahdew eimi mhde despotikos panu men oun fhmi o de ge eimi de egw esxatos eimi filos o eleuqeria o plhqos osos gignomai en o toioutos polis otan dh e wneomai kai o wneomai mhdeis hsswn eleuqeros eimi o priamai en gunh de pros anhr kai anhr pros gunh osos o isonomia kai eleuqeria gignomai oligos epilanqanomai eipon
english_head: are full of pleasantry and graciousness, imitating the young for fear they may be thought disagreeable and authoritative. By all means, he said. And the climax of popular liberty, my friend, I said, is attained in such a city when the purchased slaves, male and female, are no less free than the owners who paid for them. And I almost forgot to mention the spirit of freedom and equal rights in the relation of men to women and women to men.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν κατ' Αἰσχύλον ἔφη ἐροῦμεν ὅτι νῦν ἦλθ' ἐπὶ στόμα
greek_tokens: oukoun kata aisxulos fhmi erew ostis nun erxomai epi stoma panu ge eipon kai egw outws legos o men gar o qhraw o upo o anqrwpos osos eleuqeros eimi entauqa h en allh ou an tis peiqw apeiros atexnos gar o te kuwn kata o paroimia oios o despoina gignomai te dh kai ippos kai onos panu eleuqeros kai semnos eqizw poreuw kata o odos emballw o aei apantaw ean mh ecisthmi kai allos pas outws
english_head: Shall we not, then, said he, in Aeschylean phrase, say whatever rises to our lips? Certainly, I said, so I will. Without experience of it no one would believe how much freer the very beasts subject to men are in such a city than elsewhere. The dogs literally verify the adage and like their mistresses become. And likewise the horses and asses are wont to hold on their way with the utmost freedom and dignity, bumping into everyone who meets them and who does not step aside. And so all things everywhere are just bursting with the spirit of liberty.

greek_head: μεστὰ ἐλευθερίας γίγνεται Τὸ ἐμόν γ' ἔφη ἐμοὶ λέγεις ὄναρ αὐτὸς γὰρ εἰς ἀγρὸν
greek_tokens: mestos eleuqeria gignomai o emos ge fhmi egw legw onar autos gar eis agros poreuw qama autos pasxw o de dh kefalaios eimi de egw pas outos sunaqroizw ennoew ws apalos o yuxh o poliths poiew wste kan otioun douleios tis prosferw aganaktew kai mh anexw teleutaw gar pou oistos ostis oude o nomos frontizw grafw h agrafos ina dh mhdamh
english_head: It is my own dream you are telling me, he said; for it often happens to me when I go to the country. And do you note that the sum total of all these items when footed up is that they render the souls of the citizens so sensitive that they chafe at the slightest suggestion of servitude and will not endure it? For you are aware that they finally pay no heed even to the laws written or unwritten,

greek_head: μηδεὶς αὐτοῖς ᾖ δεσπότης Καὶ μάλ' ἔφη οἶδα
greek_tokens: mhdeis autos eimi despoths kai mala fhmi oida outos men toinun eimi de egw eimi filos o arxh outwsi kalos kai neanikos oqen turannis fuw ws egw dokew neanikos dhta fhmi alla tis o meta outos tautos eimi de egw osper en o oligarxia noshma eggignomai apollumi autos outos kai en outos pleos te kai isxuros ek o ecousia eggignomai katadoulow dhmokratia kai o eimi o agan tis poiew megas filew eis enantion metabolh antapodidwmi en wra te kai en
english_head: so that forsooth they may have no master anywhere over them. I know it very well, said he. This, then, my friend, said I, is the fine and vigorous root from which tyranny grows, in my opinion. Vigorous indeed, he said; but what next? The same malady, I said, that, arising in oligarchy, destroyed it, this more widely diffused and more violent as a result of this licence, enslaves democracy. And in truth, any excess is wont to bring about a corresponding reaction to the opposite in the seasons,

greek_head: φυτοῖς καὶ ἐν σώμασιν καὶ δὴ καὶ ἐν πολιτείαις οὐχ ἥκιστα Εἰκός ἔφη
greek_tokens: futon kai en swma kai dh kai en politeia ou hkistos eoika fhmi o gar agan eleuqeria eoika ou eis allos tis h eis agan douleios metaballw kai idiwths kai polis eoika gar eoikotws toinun eipon ou ek allos politeia turannis kaqisthmi h ek dhmokratia ek oimh o akros eleuqeria douleios pleistos te kai agrios exis gar fhmi logos alla ou outos oimh eimi de egw erwtaw alla poios
english_head: in plants, in animal bodies, and most especially in political societies. Probably, he said. And so the probable outcome of too much freedom is only too much slavery in the individual and the state. Yes, that is probable. Probably, then, tyranny develops out of no other constitution than democracy—from the height of liberty, I take it, the fiercest extreme of servitude. That is reasonable, he said. That, however, I believe, was not your question, but what identical malady

greek_head: νόσημα ἐν ὀλιγαρχίᾳ τε φυόμενον ταὐτὸν καὶ ἐν δημοκρατίᾳ δουλοῦται αὐτήν
greek_tokens: noshma en oligarxia te fuw tautos kai en dhmokratia doulow autos alhqhs fhmi legw ekeinos toinun fhmi elegos o o argew te kai dapanhros androw genos o men andreios hgeomai autos o de anandros epomai os dh afomoiow khfhn o men kentron exw o de akentros kai orqos ge fhmi outos toinun eimi de egw tarassw en paomai politeia eggignomai oios peri swma flegma te kai xolh os dh kai
english_head: arising in democracy as well as in oligarchy enslaves it? You say truly, he replied. That then, I said, was what I had in mind, the class of idle and spendthrift men, the most enterprising and vigorous portion being leaders and the less manly spirits followers. We were likening them to drones, some equipped with stings and others stingless. And rightly too, he said. These two kinds, then, I said, when they arise in any state, create a disturbance like that produced in the body by phlegm and gall.

greek_head: δεῖ τὸν ἀγαθὸν ἰατρόν τε καὶ νομοθέτην πόλεως μὴ ἧττον ἢ σοφὸν μελιττουργὸν πόρρωθεν εὐλαβεῖσθαι μάλιστα μὲν
greek_tokens: deomai o agaqos iatros te kai nomoqeths polis mh hsswn h sofos melittourgos proswqen eulabeomai malista men opws mh eggignomai an de eggignomai opws ostis taxistos sun autos o khrion ektetmhsesqon nai ma dios eimi de os pantapasi ge wde toinun eimi de egw lambanw ina eukrinhs eidon o boulomai pws trixh diisthmi o logos dhmokrateomai polis
english_head: And so a good physician and lawgiver must be on his guard from afar against the two kinds, like a prudent apiarist, first and chiefly to prevent their springing up, but if they do arise to have them as quickly as may be cut out, cells and all. Yes, by Zeus, he said, by all means. Then let us take it in this way, I said, so that we may contemplate our purpose more distinctly. How? Let us in our theory make a tripartite division of the democratic state, which is in fact its structure. One such class,

greek_head: ὥσπερ οὖν καὶ ἔχει ἓν μὲν γάρ που τὸ τοιοῦτον γένος ἐν αὐτῇ ἐμφύεται δι' ἐξουσίαν οὐκ ἔλαττον ἢ ἐν τῇ ὀλιγαρχουμένῃ
greek_tokens: wsper oun kai exis eis men gar pou o toioutos genos en autos emfuw dia ecousia ou elasswn h en o oligarxew eimi outws polus de ge drimus en outos h en ekeinos pws ekei men dia o mh entimos eimi alla apelaunw o arxh agumnastos kai ou errwmenos gignomai en dhmokratia de outos pou o proisthmi autos ektos oligos kai o men drimus autos legw te kai prassw o de allos peri o bhma prosizw bombew te kai ou anexw o
english_head: as we have described, grows up in it because of the licence, no less than in the oligarchic state. That is so. But it is far fiercer in this state than in that. How so? There, because it is not held in honor, but is kept out of office, it is not exercised and does not grow vigorous. But in a democracy this is the dominating class, with rare exceptions, and the fiercest part of it makes speeches and transacts business, and the remainder swarms and settles about the speaker’s stand and keeps up a buzzing and

greek_head: ἄλλα λέγοντος ὥστε πάντα ὑπὸ τοῦ τοιούτου διοικεῖται ἐν τῇ τοιαύτῃ πολιτείᾳ χωρίς τινων ὀλίγων
greek_tokens: allos legw wste pas upo o toioutos dioikew en o toioutos politeia xwris tis oligos mala ge eimi de os allos toinun toiosde aei apokrinw ek o plhqos o poios xrhmatizw pou pas e kosmios fusis ws o polus plousios gignomai eoika pleistos dh oimh o khfhn meli kai euporos enteuqen blissw pws gar an fhmi para ge o mikros exw tis blittw plousios dh oimh e toioutos kalew khfhn botanh sxedon tis fhmi
english_head: tolerates no dissent, so that everything with slight exceptions is administered by that class in such a state. Quite so, he said. And so from time to time there emerges or is secreted from the multitude another group of this sort. What sort? he said. When all are pursuing wealth the most orderly and thrifty natures for the most part become the richest. It is likely. Then they are the most abundant supply of honey for the drones, and it is the easiest to extract. Why, yes, he said, how could one squeeze it out of those who have little? The capitalistic class is, I take it, the name by which they are designated—the pasture of the drones. Pretty much so, he said.

greek_head: Δῆμος δ' ἂν εἴη τρίτον γένος ὅσοι αὐτουργοί τε καὶ ἀπράγμονες οὐ πάνυ πολλὰ κεκτημένοι ὃ δὴ πλεῖστόν τε
greek_tokens: dhmos de an eaw tritos genos osos autourgos te kai apragmwn ou panu polus ktaomai o dh pleistos te kai kurios en dhmokratia otan aqroizw eimi gar fhmi alla ou qama eqelw poiew outos ean mh meli tis metalambanw oukoun metalambanw eimi de egw aei kaqa osos dunamai e proisthmi o exw o ousia afairew dianemw o dhmos o pleistos autos exw
english_head: And the third class, composing the people, would comprise all quiet cultivators of their own farms who possess little property. This is the largest and most potent group in a democracy when it meets in assembly. Yes, it is, he said, but it will not often do that, unless it gets a share of the honey. Well, does it not always share, I said, to the extent that the men at the head find it possible, in distributing to the people what they take from the well-to-do, to keep the lion’s share for themselves? Why, yes, he said, it shares

greek_head: Μεταλαμβάνει γὰρ οὖν ἦ δ' ὅς οὕτως Ἀναγκάζονται δὴ οἶμαι ἀμύνεσθαι λέγοντές τε ἐν τῷ δήμῳ
greek_tokens: metalambanw gar oun eimi de os outws anagkazw dh oimh amunw legw te en o dhmos kai prassw oph dunamai outos os afairew pws gar ou aitios dh exw upo o eteros kan mh epiqumew newterizw ws epibouleuw o dhmos kai eimi oligarxikos tis meis oukoun kai teleutaw epeidan oraw o dhmos ou ekwn alla agnoew te kai ecapataw upo o
english_head: in that sense. And so, I suppose, those who are thus plundered are compelled to defend themselves by speeches in the assembly and any action in their power. Of course. And thereupon the charge is brought against them by the other party, though they may have no revolutionary designs, that they are plotting against the people, and it is said that they are oligarchs. Surely. And then finally, when they see the people, not of its own will but through misapprehension, and being misled

greek_head: διαβαλλόντων ἐπιχειροῦντα σφᾶς ἀδικεῖν τότ' ἤδη εἴτε βούλονται εἴτε μή ὡς ἀληθῶς ὀλιγαρχικοὶ γίγνονται οὐχ
greek_tokens: diaballw epixeirew sfazw adikew tote hdh eite boulomai eite mh ws alhqhs oligarxikos gignomai ou ekwn alla kai outos o kakos ekeinos o khfhn entiktw kentaw autos komidh men oun eisaggelia dh kai krisis kai agwn peri allhlwn gignomai kai mala oukoun enos tis aei dhmos eqw diaferontws proisthmi eautou kai outos trefw te kai aucanw megas eqw gar
english_head: by the calumniators, attempting to wrong them, why then, whether they wish it or not, they become in very deed oligarchs, not willingly, but this evil too is engendered by those drones which sting them. Precisely. And then there ensue impeachments and judgements and lawsuits on either side. Yes, indeed. And is it not always the way of a demos to put forward one man as its special champion and protector and cherish and magnify him? Yes, it is. This, then, is plain,

greek_head: Τοῦτο μὲν ἄρα ἦν δ' ἐγώ δῆλον ὅτι ὅτανπερ φύηται τύραννος ἐκ προστατικῆς ῥίζης καὶ οὐκ ἄλλοθεν ἐκβλαστάνει
greek_tokens: outos men ara eimi de egw dhlos ostis otan fuw turannos ek prostatikos riza kai ou alloqen ekblastanw kai mala dhlos tis arxh oun metaboleus ek prostaths epi turannos h dhlos ostis epeidan tautos arxw draw o prostaths o en o muqos os peri o en arkadia o o zeus o lukaion ieron legw tis fhmi ws ara o geuw o anqrwpinos splagxnon en allos allos iereion eis egkatatemnw anagkh dh
english_head: said I, that when a tyrant arises he sprouts from a protectorate root and from nothing else. Very plain. What, then, is the starting-point of the transformation of a protector into a tyrant? Is it not obviously when the protector’s acts begin to reproduce the legend that is told of the shrine of Lycaean Zeus in Arcadia? What is that? he said. The story goes that he who tastes of the one bit of human entrails minced up with those of other victims

greek_head: τούτῳ λύκῳ γενέσθαι ἢ οὐκ ἀκήκοας τὸν λόγον Ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: outos lukos gignomai h ou akouw o logos egw ara oun outws kai os an dhmos proisthmi lambanw sfodra peiqw oxlos mh apexw emfulios aima alla adikos epaitiaomai oios dh filew eis dikasthrion agw miaifonew bios anhr afanizw glwssa te kai stoma anosios geuw fonos suggenhs kai andrhlatew kai
english_head: is inevitably transformed into a wolf. Have you not heard the tale? I have. And is it not true that in like manner a leader of the people who, getting control of a docile mob, does not withhold his hand from the shedding of tribal blood, but by the customary unjust accusations brings a citizen into court and assassinates him, blotting out a human life, and with unhallowed tongue and lips that have tasted kindred blood,

greek_head: ἀποκτεινύῃ καὶ ὑποσημαίνῃ χρεῶν τε ἀποκοπὰς καὶ γῆς ἀναδασμόν ἆρα τῷ τοιούτῳ ἀνάγκη δὴ τὸ μετὰ τοῦτο
greek_tokens: apokteinw kai uposhmainw xrea te apokoph kai gaia anadasmos ara o toioutos anagkh dh o meta outos kai meiromai h apollumi upo o exqra h turanneuw kai lukos ek anqrwpos gignomai polus anagkh fhmi outos dh fhmi o stasiazw gignomai pros o exw o ousia outos ara oun ekpitnw men kai katerxomai bia o exqra turannos apergazomai katerxomai dhlos
english_head: banishes and slays and hints at the abolition of debts and the partition of lands—is it not the inevitable consequence and a decree of fate that such a one be either slain by his enemies or become a tyrant and be transformed from a man into a wolf? It is quite inevitable, he said. He it is, I said, who becomes the leader of faction against the possessors of property. Yes, he. May it not happen that he is driven into exile and, being restored in defiance of his enemies, returns a finished tyrant? Obviously. And if they are unable

greek_head: Ἐὰν δὲ ἀδύνατοι ἐκβάλλειν αὐτὸν ὦσιν ἢ ἀποκτεῖναι διαβάλλοντες τῇ πόλει βιαίῳ δὴ θανάτῳ ἐπιβουλεύουσιν
greek_tokens: ean de adunatos ekballw autos eimi h apokteinw diaballw o polis biaios dh qanatos epibouleuw apokteinw laqrh filew goun eimi de os outws gignomai o dh turannikos aithma o poluqrulhtos epi outos pas e eis outos probainw eceuriskw aitew o dhmos fulac tis o swma ina sos autos eimi o o dhmos bohqos kai mala fhmi didwmi dh oimh deidw men uper ekeinos qarsew de uper eautou
english_head: to expel him or bring about his death by calumniating him to the people, they plot to assassinate him by stealth. That is certainly wont to happen, said he. And thereupon those who have reached this stage devise that famous petition of the tyrant—to ask from the people a bodyguard to make their city safe for the friend of democracy.

greek_head: Καὶ μάλα Οὐκοῦν τοῦτο ὅταν ἴδῃ ἀνὴρ χρήματα ἔχων καὶ μετὰ τῶν
greek_tokens: kai mala oukoun outos otan eidon anhr xrhma exw kai meta o xrhma aitios misodhmos eimi tote dh outos eimi etairos kata o kroisos gignomai xrhsmos poluyhfis para ermos feugw oude menos oude aideomai kakos eimi ou gar an fhmi deuteros auqis aideomai o de ge oimh eimi de egw katalambanw qanatos didwmi anagkh o de dh prostaths ekeinos autos dhlos dh ostis megas
english_head: They do indeed, he said. And the people grant it, I suppose, fearing for him but unconcerned for themselves. Yes, indeed. And when he sees this, the man who has wealth and with his wealth the repute of hostility to democracy, then in the words of the oracle delivered to Croesus, By the pebble-strewn strand of the Hermos Swift is his flight, he stays not nor blushes to show the white feather. Hdt. 1.55 No, for he would never get a second chance to blush. And he who is caught, methinks, is delivered to his death. Inevitably. And then obviously that protector does not lie prostrate, mighty with far-flung limbs, Hom. Il. 16.776 in Homeric overthrow, but

greek_head: μεγαλωστὶ οὐ κεῖται ἀλλὰ καταβαλὼν ἄλλους πολλοὺς ἕστηκεν ἐν τῷ δίφρῳ τῆς πόλεως τύραννος ἀντὶ προστάτου
greek_tokens: megalwsti ou kew alla kataballw allos polus isthmi en o difros o polis turannos anti prostaths apotelew tis de ou mellw fhmi dierxomai dh o eudaimonia eimi de egw o te anhr kai o polis en h an o toioutos brotos eggignomai panu men oun fhmi dierxomai ara oun eipon ou o men proteros hmeros te kai xronos prosgelaw te kai aspazomai pas os an peritugxanw kai
english_head: overthrowing many others towers in the car of state transformed from a protector into a perfect and finished tyrant. What else is likely? he said. Shall we, then, portray the happiness, said I, of the man and the state in which such a creature arises? By all means let us describe it, he said. Then at the start and in the first days does he not smile upon all men and greet everybody he meets and deny that he is a tyrant,

greek_head: οὔτε τύραννός φησιν εἶναι ὑπισχνεῖταί τε πολλὰ καὶ ἰδίᾳ καὶ δημοσίᾳ χρεῶν τε ἠλευθέρωσε καὶ γῆν διένειμε δήμῳ τε
greek_tokens: oute turannos fhmi eimi upisxneomai te polus kai idios kai dhmosios xrea te eleuqerow kai gaia dianemw dhmos te kai o peri eautou kai pas ilaos te kai praos eimi prospoiew anagkh fhmi otan de ge oimh pros o ecw exqros o men katallassw o de kai diafqeirw kai hsuxia ekeinos gignomai proteros men polemos tis aei kinew ina en xreia hgemwn o dhmos eimi eoika ge
english_head: and promise many things in private and public, and having freed men from debts, and distributed lands to the people and his own associates, he affects a gracious and gentle manner to all? Necessarily, he said. But when, I suppose, he has come to terms with some of his exiled enemies and has got others destroyed and is no longer disturbed by them, in the first place he is always stirring up some war so that the people may be in need of a leader. That is likely.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν καὶ ἵνα χρήματα εἰσφέροντες πένητες γιγνόμενοι πρὸς τῷ καθ' ἡμέραν ἀναγκάζωνται εἶναι καὶ ἧττον αὐτῷ
greek_tokens: oukoun kai ina xrhma eisferw penhs gignomai pros o kaqa hmeros anagkazw eimi kai hsswn autos epibouleuw dhlos kai an ge tis oimh upopteuw eleuqeros fronhma exw mh epitrepw autos arxw opws an outos meta profasis apollumi endidwmi o polemios outos pas eneka turannos aei anagkh polemos tarassw anagkh outos dh poiew etoimos mallon apexqanomai o
english_head: And also that being impoverished by war-taxes they may have to devote themselves to their daily business and be less likely to plot against him? Obviously. And if, I presume, he suspects that there are free spirits who will not suffer his domination, his further object is to find pretexts for destroying them by exposing them to the enemy? From all these motives a tyrant is compelled to be always provoking wars? Yes, he is compelled to do so. And by such conduct

greek_head: πολίταις Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: poliths pws gar ou oukoun kai tis o sugkaqisthmi kai en dunamis eimi parrhsiazomai kai pros autos kai pros allhlwn epiplhssw o gignomai e an tugxanw andrikos eimi eoika ge upecairew dh outos pas deomai o turannos ei mellw arxw ews an mhte filos mhte exqra leipw mhdeis ostis tis ofelos dhlos ocews ara deomai oraw autos tis andreios tis megalofrwn
english_head: will he not the more readily incur the hostility of the citizens? Of course. And is it not likely that some of those who helped to establish and now share in his power, voicing their disapproval of the course of events, will speak out frankly to him and to one another—such of them as happen to be the bravest? Yes, it is likely. Then the tyrant must do away with all such if he is to maintain his rule, until he has left no one of any worth, friend or foe. Obviously. He must look sharp to see, then,

greek_head: τίς φρόνιμος τίς πλούσιος καὶ οὕτως εὐδαίμων ἐστίν ὥστε τούτοις ἅπασιν ἀνάγκη αὐτῷ εἴτε βούλεται εἴτε μή πολεμίῳ
greek_tokens: tis fronimos tis plousios kai outws eudaimwn eimi wste outos apas anagkh autos eite boulomai eite mh polemios eimi kai epibouleuw ews an kaqairw o polis kalos ge fhmi kaqarmos nai eimi de egw o enantion h e iatros o swma e men gar o xeiristos afairew leipw o beltistos o de enantion ws eoika gar autos fhmi anagkh eiper arxw
english_head: who is brave, who is great-souled, who is wise, who is rich and such is his good fortune that, whether he wishes it or not, he must be their enemy and plot against them all until he purge the city. A fine purgation, he said. Yes, said I, just the opposite of that which physicians practise on our bodies. For while they remove the worst and leave the best, he does the reverse. Yes, for apparently he must, he said, if he is to keep his power. Blessed, then, is the necessity that binds him,

greek_head: Ἐν μακαρίᾳ ἄρα εἶπον ἐγώ ἀνάγκῃ δέδεται ἣ προστάττει αὐτῷ ἢ μετὰ φαύλων τῶν πολλῶν οἰκεῖν καὶ ὑπὸ τούτων
greek_tokens: en makarios ara eipon egw anagkh dew o prostassw autos h meta faulos o polus oikew kai upo outos misew h mh zaw en toioutos eimi de os ara oun ou osos an mallon o poliths apexqanomai outos draw tosoutos pleiwn kai pistos doruforos dew pws gar ou tinw oun e pistos kai pinw autos metapempw automatos fhmi polus hkw petomai ean o misqos didwmi khfhn eimi de egw nh o kuwn dokew au tis egw
english_head: said I, which bids him dwell for the most part with base companions who hate him, or else forfeit his life. Such it is, he said. And would he not, the more he offends the citizens by such conduct, have the greater need of more and more trustworthy bodyguards? Of course. Whom, then, may he trust, and whence shall he fetch them? Unbidden, he said, they will wing their way to him in great numbers if he furnish their wage. Drones, by the dog, I said, I think you are talking of again,

greek_head: λέγειν ξενικούς τε καὶ παντοδαπούς Ἀληθῆ γάρ ἔφη δοκῶ σοι
greek_tokens: legw cenikos te kai pantodapos alhqhs gar fhmi dokazw su tis de autoqen ara ou an eqelw pws o doulos afairew o poliths eleuqerow o peri eautou doruforos poiew sfodra ge fhmi epei su kai pistos autos outos eimi eimi makarios eimi de egw legw turannos xrhma ei toioutos
english_head: an alien and motley crew. You think rightly, he said. But what of the home supply, would he not choose to employ that? How? By taking their slaves from the citizens, emancipating them and enlisting them in his bodyguard. Assuredly, he said, since these are those whom he can most trust. Truly, said I, this tyrant business is a blessed thing on your showing, if such are the friends and trusties

greek_head: φίλοις τε καὶ πιστοῖς ἀνδράσι χρῆται τοὺς προτέρους ἐκείνους ἀπολέσας
greek_tokens: filos te kai piston anhr xraomai o proteros ekeinos apollumi alla meis fhmi toioutos ge xraomai kai qaumazw dh eipon outos e etairos autos kai suneimi e neos poliths e de epieikhs misew te kai feugw tis de ou mellw ou etos eimi de egw o te tragwdia oloc sofos dokew eimi kai o euripidhs diaferw en autos tis dh ostis kai outos puknazw dianoia exw fqeggomai ws
english_head: he must employ after destroying his former associates. But such are indeed those he does make use of, he said. And these companions admire him, I said, and these new citizens are his associates, while the better sort hate and avoid him. Why should they not? Not for nothing, said I, is tragedy in general esteemed wise, and Euripides beyond other tragedians. Why, pray? Because among other utterances of pregnant thought he said,

greek_head: ἄρα σοφοὶ τύραννοί εἰσι τῶν σοφῶν συνουσίᾳ καὶ ἔλεγε δῆλον ὅτι τούτους εἶναι τοὺς σοφοὺς οἷς σύνεστιν
greek_tokens: ara sofos turannos eimi o sofos sunousia kai elegos dhlos ostis outos eimi o sofos os suneimi kai ws isoqeos ge fhmi o turannis egkwmiazw kai eteros polus kai outos kai e allos poihths toigar fhmi ate sofos eimi e o tragwdia poihths suggignwskw egw te kai ekeinos osos egw eggus politeuw ostis autos eis o politeia ou paradexomai ate turannis umnhths oimh egw fhmi suggignwskw osos ge autos
english_head: Tyrants are wise by converse with the wise. He meant evidently that these associates of the tyrant are the wise. Yes, he and the other poets, he said, call the tyrant’s power likest God’s and praise it in many other ways. Wherefore, said I, being wise as they are, the poets of tragedy will pardon us and those whose politics resemble ours for not admitting them into our polity, since they hymn the praises of tyranny. I think, he said, that the subtle minds

greek_head: κομψοί Εἰς δέ γε οἶμαι τὰς ἄλλας περιιόντες πόλεις συλλέγοντες
greek_tokens: komyos eis de ge oimh o allos perieimi polis sullegw o oxlos kalos fwnh kai megas kai piqanos misqow eis turannis te kai dhmokratia elkw o politeia mala ge oukoun kai proseti outos misqos lambanw kai timaw malista men wsper o eoika upo turannos deuteros de upo dhmokratia osos de an anw eimi pros
english_head: among them will pardon us. But going about to other cities, I fancy, collecting crowds and hiring fine, loud, persuasive voices, they draw the polities towards tyrannies or democracies. Yes, indeed. And, further, they are paid and honored for this, chiefly, as is to be expected, by tyrants, and secondly by democracy. But the higher they go, breasting constitution hill, the more their honor fails,

greek_head: τὸ ἄναντες τῶν πολιτειῶν μᾶλλον ἀπαγορεύει αὐτῶν ἡ τιμή ὥσπερ ὑπὸ ἄσθματος ἀδυνατοῦσα πορεύεσθαι
greek_tokens: o anaantomai o politeia mallon apagoreuw autos o timh wsper upo asqma adunatew poreuw panu men oun alla dh eipon entauqa men ekbainw legw de palin ekeinos o o turannos stratopedon o kalos te kai polus kai poikilos kai oudepote tautos pinw trefw dhlos fhmi ostis ean te iera xrhma eimi en o polis outos analwsis opoi pote an aei ecarkew o o apodidwmi elasswn eisfora anagkazw o dhmos eisferw
english_head: as it were from lack of breath unable to proceed. Quite so. But this, said I, is a digression. Let us return to that fair, multitudinous, diversified and ever-changing bodyguard of the tyrant and tell how it will be supported. Obviously, he said, if there are sacred treasures in the city he will spend these as long as they last and the property of those he has destroyed, thus requiring smaller contributions from the populace.

greek_head: Τί δ' ὅταν δὴ ταῦτα ἐπιλίπῃ Δῆλον ἔφη ὅτι ἐκ τῶν πατρῴων θρέψεται αὐτός τε καὶ
greek_tokens: tis de otan dh outos epileipw dhlos fhmi ostis ek o patrwos trefw autos te kai e sumpoths te kai etairos kai etaira manqanw eimi de egw ostis o dhmos o gennaw o turannos qreyis autos te kai etairos polus autos fhmi anagkh pws de legw eipon ean de aganaktew te kai legos o dhmos ostis oute dikaios trefw upo pathr uios hbaw alla enantion upo uios pathr oute outos autos eneka
english_head: But what when these resources fail? Clearly, he said, his father’s estate will have to support him and his wassailers, his fellows and his she-fellows. I understand, I said, that the people which begot the tyrant will have to feed him and his companions. It cannot escape from that, he said. And what have you to say, I said, in case the people protests and says that it is not right that a grown-up son should be supported by his father, but the reverse,

greek_head: ἐγέννησέν τε καὶ κατέστησεν ἵνα ἐπειδὴ μέγας γένοιτο τότε αὐτὸς δουλεύων τοῖς αὑτοῦ δούλοις τρέφοι ἐκεῖνόν τε καὶ
greek_tokens: gennaw te kai kaqisthmi ina epei megas gignomai tote autos douleuw o eautou doulos trefw ekeinos te kai o doulos meta sugklus allos alla ina apo o plousios te kai kalh agaqos legw en o polis eleuqerow ekeinos proisthmi kai nun keleuw apeimi ek o polis autos te kai o etairos wsper pathr uios ek oikia meta oxlhros sumpoths ecelaunw gignwskw ge nh dios eimi de os tote hdh o dhmos oios oios
english_head: and that it did not beget and establish him in order that, when he had grown great, it, in servitude to its own slaves, should feed him and the slaves together with a nondescript rabble of aliens, but in order that, with him for protector, it might be liberated from the rule of the rich and the so-called better classes, and that it now bids him and his crew depart from the city as a father expels from his house a son together with troublesome revellers? The demos, by Zeus, he said, will then learn to its cost

greek_head: θρέμμα γεννῶν ἠσπάζετό τε καὶ ηὖξεν καὶ ὅτι ἀσθενέστερος ὢν ἰσχυροτέρους ἐξελαύνει
greek_tokens: qremma genna aspazomai te kai aucanw kai ostis asqenhs eimi isxuros ecelaunw pws eimi de egw legw tolmhsis o pathr biazw kan mh peiqw tuptw o turannos nai fhmi afairew ge o oplon patraloias eimi de egw legw turannos kai xalepos ghroboskos kai ws eoika outos dh omologew an hdh turannis eaw kai o legw o dhmos feugw an kapnos
english_head: what it is and what a creature it begot and cherished and bred to greatness, and that in its weakness it tries to expel the stronger. What do you mean? said I; will the tyrant dare to use force against his father, and, if he does not yield, to strike him? Yes, he said, after he has once taken from him his arms. A very parricide, said I, you make the tyrant out to be, and a cruel nurse of old age, and, as it seems, this is at last tyranny open and avowed, and, as the saying goes, the demos trying to escape the smoke of submission to the free would have plunged

greek_head: δουλείας ἐλευθέρων εἰς πῦρ δούλων δεσποτείας ἂν ἐμπεπτωκὼς εἴη ἀντὶ τῆς πολλῆς ἐκείνης καὶ ἀκαίρου ἐλευθερίας
greek_tokens: douleios eleuqeros eis pur doulos despoteios an empitnw eaw anti o polus ekeinos kai akairos eleuqeria o xalepos te kai pikros doulos douleios amfiisxw kai mala fhmi outos outws gignomai tis oun eipon ou emmelhs egw eromai ean fhmi ikanos dierxomai ws metabainw turannis ek dhmokratia gignomai te oios eimi panu men oun ikanos fhmi
english_head: into the fire of enslavement to slaves, and in exchange for that excessive and unseasonable liberty has clothed itself in the garb of the most cruel and bitter servile servitude. Yes indeed, he said, that is just what happens. Well, then, said I, shall we not be fairly justified in saying that we have sufficiently described the transformation of a democracy into a tyranny and the nature of the tyranny itself? Quite sufficiently, he said.

greek_head: Αὐτὸς δὴ λοιπός ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὁ τυραννικὸς ἀνὴρ σκέψασθαι πῶς τε μεθίσταται ἐκ δημοκρατικοῦ γενόμενός τε ποῖός
greek_tokens: autos dh loipos eimi de egw o turannikos anhr skeptomai pws te meqisthmi ek dhmokratikos gignomai te poios tis eimi kai tis tropos zaw aqlios h makarios loipos gar oun eti outos fhmi oistos oun eimi de egw o pinw eti o poios o o epiqumia oios te kai osos eimi ou egw dokew ikanos diairew outos dh endehs exw asafhs
english_head: There remains for consideration, said I, the tyrannical man himself—the manner of his development out of the democratic type and his character and the quality of his life, whether wretched or happy. Why, yes, he still remains, he said. Do you know, then, what it is that I still miss? What? In the matter of our desires I do not think we sufficiently distinguished their nature and number. And so long as this is lacking

greek_head: ἔσται ἡ ζήτησις οὗ ζητοῦμεν Οὐκοῦν ἦ δ' ὅς ἔτ' ἐν καλῷ
greek_tokens: eimi o zhthsis e zhtew oukoun eimi de os etai en kalos panu men oun kai skopaw ge o en autos boulomai eidon eimi de ode o mh anagkaion hdona te kai epiqumia dokew tis egw eimi paranomos o kinduneuw men eggignomai pas kolazw de upo te o nomos kai o beltiwn epiqumia meta logos enioi men anqrwpos h pantapasi apallassw h oligos leipw kai asqenew
english_head: our inquiry will lack clearness. Well, said he, will our consideration of them not still be opportune? By all means. And observe what it is about them that I wish to consider. It is this. Of our unnecessary pleasures and appetites there are some lawless ones, I think, which probably are to be found in us all, but which, when controlled by the laws and the better desires in alliance with reason, can in some men be altogether got rid of, or so nearly so that only a few weak ones remain,

greek_head: τῶν δὲ ἰσχυρότεραι καὶ πλείους Λέγεις δὲ καὶ τίνας ἔφη ταύτας
greek_tokens: o de isxuros kai plews legw de kai tis fhmi outos o peri o upnon eimi de egw egeirw otan o men allos o yuxazw eudw osos logistikos kai hmeros kai arxw ekeinos o de qhriwdhs te kai agrios h sitos h meqh pimplhmi skirtaw te kai apwqew o upnon zhtew eimi kai apopimplhmi o eautou hqos oistos ostis pas en o toioutos tolmaw poiew ws apo pas luw te kai apallassw aisxunh kai fronhsis mhthr te gar epixeirew
english_head: while in others the remnant is stronger and more numerous. What desires do you mean? he said. Those, said I, that are awakened in sleep when the rest of the soul, the rational, gentle and dominant part, slumbers, but the beastly and savage part, replete with food and wine, gambols and, repelling sleep, endeavors to sally forth and satisfy its own instincts. You are aware that in such case there is nothing it will not venture to undertake as being released from all sense of shame and all reason. It does not shrink from attempting to lie with a mother

greek_head: μείγνυσθαι ὡς οἴεται οὐδὲν ὀκνεῖ ἄλλῳ τε ὁτῳοῦν ἀνθρώπων καὶ θεῶν καὶ θηρίων μιαιφονεῖν τε ὁτιοῦν βρώματός
greek_tokens: mignumi ws oiomai oudeis oknew allos te ostis anqrwpos kai qea kai qhraw miaifonew te otioun brwma te apexw mhdeis kai eis logos oute anoia oudeis elleipw oute anaisxuntia alhqhs fhmi legw otan de ge oimh ugieinos tis exis autos eautou kai swfrwn kai eis o upnon eimi o logistikos men egeirw eautou kai estiaw logos kalh kai skeyis eis sunnoia
english_head: in fancy or with anyone else, man, god or brute. It is ready for any foul deed of blood; it abstains from no food, and, in a word, falls short of no extreme of folly and shamelessness. Most true, he said. But when, I suppose, a man’s condition is healthy and sober, and he goes to sleep after arousing his rational part and entertaining it with fair words and thoughts, and attaining to clear self-consciousness, while he has neither starved

greek_head: αὐτὸς αὑτῷ ἀφικόμενος τὸ ἐπιθυμητικὸν δὲ μήτε ἐνδείᾳ δοὺς μήτε πλησμονῇ ὅπως ἂν κοιμηθῇ καὶ μὴ παρέχῃ θόρυβον
greek_tokens: autos eautou afikneomai o epiqumhtikos de mhte endeia didwmi mhte plhsmonh opws an koimaw kai mh parexw qorubos
english_head: nor indulged to repletion his appetitive part, so that it may be lulled to sleep

greek_head: τῷ βελτίστῳ χαῖρον ἢ λυπούμενον ἀλλ' ἐᾷ αὐτὸ καθ' αὑτὸ μόνον καθαρὸν σκοπεῖν καὶ ὀρέγεσθαί του αἰσθάνεσθαι ὃ μὴ
greek_tokens: o beltistos xairw h lupew alla eaw autos kaqa eautou monos kaqaros skopaw kai oregw tis aisqanomai o mh oida h tis o gignomai h eimi h kai mellw wsautws de kai o qumoeidhs praunw kai mh tis eis orgas erxomai kinew o qumos kaqeudw alla hsuxaw men o duo eidos o tritos de kinew en os o fronew eggignomai outws anapauw oistos ostis o su alhqeia en o toioutos malista aptw kai hkistos paranomos tote o oyis
english_head: and not disturb the better part by its pleasure or pain, but may suffer that in isolated purity to examine and reach out towards and apprehend some of the things unknown to it, past, present or future; and when he has in like manner tamed his passionate part, and does not after a quarrel fall asleep with anger still awake within him, but if he has thus quieted the two elements in his soul and quickened the third, in which reason resides, and so goes to his rest, you are aware that in such case he is most likely to apprehend truth, and

greek_head: φαντάζονται τῶν ἐνυπνίων Παντελῶς μὲν οὖν ἔφη οἶμαι οὕτως
greek_tokens: fantazomai o enupnion pantelhs men oun fhmi oimh outws outos men toinun epi pleos ecagw eipon o de boulomai gignwskw ode eimi ws ara deinos tis kai agrios kai anomos epiqumia eidos ekastos eneimi kai panu dokew egw eneimi metrios eimi outos de ara en o upnon gignomai endhlos ei oun tis dokazw legw kai sugxwrew aqrew alla sugxwrew o toinun dhmotikos anamimnhskw oios fhmi eimi
english_head: the visions of his dreams are least likely to be lawless. I certainly think so, he said. This description has carried us too far, but the point that we have to notice is this, that in fact there exists in every one of us, even in some reputed most respectable, a terrible, fierce and lawless brood of desires, which it seems are revealed in our sleep. Consider, then, whether there is anything in what I say, and whether you admit it. Well, I do. Now recall our characterization of the democratic man.

greek_head: ἦν δέ που γεγονὼς ἐκ νέου ὑπὸ φειδωλῷ πατρὶ τεθραμμένος τὰς χρηματιστικὰς ἐπιθυμίας τιμῶντι μόνας τὰς δὲ μὴ
greek_tokens: eimi de pou gignomai ek neos upo feidwlos pathr trefw o xrhmatistikos epiqumia timaw monos o de mh anagkaios alla paidih te kai kallwpismos eneka gignomai atimazw eimi gar nai suggignomai de komyos anhr kai mestos os arti dierxomai epiqumia ormaw eis ubris te pas kai o ekeinos eidos misos o o pathr feidwlia fus de o diafqeirw beltiwn exw agw amfoterwse
english_head: His development was determined by his education from youth under a thrifty father who approved only the acquisitive appetites and disapproved the unnecessary ones whose object is entertainment and display. Is not that so? Yes. And by association with more sophisticated men, teeming with the appetites we have just described, he is impelled towards every form of insolence and outrage, and to the adoption of their way of life by his hatred of his father’s niggardliness. But since his nature is better than that of his corrupters,

greek_head: κατέστη εἰς μέσον ἀμφοῖν τοῖν τρόποιν καὶ μετρίως δή ὡς ᾤετο ἑκάστων ἀπολαύων οὔτε ἀνελεύθερον οὔτε παράνομον
greek_tokens: kaqisthmi eis mesos amfw o tropos kai metrios dh ws oiomai ekastos apolauw oute aneleuqeros oute paranomos bios zaw dhmotikos ek oligarxikos gignomai eimi gar fhmi kai eimi outos o doca peri o toioutos tiqhmi toinun eimi de egw palin o toioutos hdh presbus gignomai neos uios en o outos au hqos trefw tiqhmi tiqhmi toinun kai o autos ekeinos peri autos gignomai o kai peri o pathr autos agw te eis pas
english_head: being drawn both ways he settles down in a compromise between the two tendencies, and indulging and enjoying each in moderation, forsooth, as he supposes, he lives what he deems a life that is neither illiberal nor lawless, now transformed from an oligarch to a democrat. That was and is our belief about this type. Assume, then, again, said I, that such a man when he is older has a son bred in turn in his ways of life. I so assume. And suppose the experience of his father

greek_head: παρανομίαν ὀνομαζομένην δ' ὑπὸ τῶν ἀγόντων ἐλευθερίαν ἅπασαν βοηθοῦντά τε ταῖς ἐν μέσῳ ταύταις ἐπιθυμίαις
greek_tokens: paranomia onomazw de upo o agw eleuqeria apas bohqew te o en mesos outos epiqumia pathr te kai o allos oikeios o de au parabohqew otan de elpizw e deinos magos te kai turannopoios outos mh allos o neos katexw erws tis autos mhxanaomai empoiew proisthmi o argew kai o
english_head: to be repeated in his case. He is drawn toward utter lawlessness, which is called by his seducers complete freedom. His father and his other kin lend support to these compromise appetites while the others lend theirs to the opposite group. And when these dread magi and king-makers come to realize that they have no hope of controlling the youth in any other way, they contrive to engender in his soul a ruling passion to be the protector

greek_head: ἕτοιμα διανεμομένων ἐπιθυμιῶν ὑπόπτερον καὶ μέγαν κηφῆνά τινα ἢ τί ἄλλο οἴει εἶναι τὸν τῶν τοιούτων ἔρωτα
