greek_tokens: etoimos dianemw epiqumia upopteros kai megas khfhn tis h tis allos oiomai eimi o o toioutos erws oudeis egw eimi de os allos h outos oukoun otan dh peri autos bombew o allos epiqumia qumiama te gemw kai muron kai stefanos kai oinon kai o en o toioutos sunousia hdona anihmi epi o esxatos aucanw te kai trefw poqos kentron empoiew o khfhn tote dh doruforew te upo mania kai
english_head: of his idle and prodigal appetites, a monstrous winged drone. Or do you think the spirit of desire in such men is aught else? Nothing but that, he said. And when the other appetites, buzzing about it, replete with incense and myrrh and chaplets and wine, and the pleasures that are released in such revelries, magnifying and fostering it to the utmost, awaken in the drone the sting of unsatisfied yearnings, why then this protector of the soul has madness for his body-guard and runs amuck, and if it finds in the man

greek_head: οἰστρᾷ οὗτος ὁ προστάτης τῆς ψυχῆς καὶ ἐάν τινας ἐν αὐτῷ δόξας ἢ ἐπιθυμίας λάβῃ ποιουμένας χρηστὰς καὶ ἔτι ἐπαισχυνομένας
greek_tokens: oistraw outos o prostaths o yuxazw kai ean tis en autos doca h epiqumia lambanw poiew xrhstos kai eti epaisxunomai apokteinw te kai ecw wqew para eautou ews an kaqairw swfrosunos mania de plhrwsis epaktos pantelhs fhmi turannikos anhr legw genesis ara oun eimi de egw kai o pala dia o toioutos turannos o eros legw kinduneuw fhmi oukoun eimi filos eipon kai mequskw anhr turannikos tis
english_head: any opinions or appetites accounted worthy and still capable of shame, it slays them and thrusts them forth until it purges him of sobriety, and fills and infects him with frenzy brought in from outside. A perfect description, he said, of the generation of the tyrannical man. And is not this analogy, said I, the reason why Love has long since been called a tyrant? That may well be, he said. And does not a drunken man, my friend, I said,

greek_head: φρόνημα ἴσχει Ἴσχει γάρ
greek_tokens: fronhma isxw isxw gar kai meis o ge mainomai kai upokinew ou monos anqrwpos alla kai qea epixeirew te kai elpizw dunatos eimi arxw kai mala fhmi turannikos de eimi de egw eimi daimonios anhr akribhs gignomai otan h fusis h epithdeuma h amfoteros mequstikos te kai erwtikos kai melagxolikos gignomai pantelhs men oun gignomai men ws eoika outws kai toioutos anhr zaw de dh pws
english_head: have something of this tyrannical temper? Yes, he has. And again the madman, the deranged man, attempts and expects to rule over not only men but gods. Yes indeed, he does, he said. Then a man becomes tyrannical in the full sense of the word, my friend, I said, when either by nature or by habits or by both he has become even as the drunken, the erotic, the maniacal. Assuredly. Such, it seems, is his origin and character, but what is his manner of life? As the wits say,

greek_head: Τὸ τῶν παιζόντων ἔφη τοῦτο σὺ καὶ ἐμοὶ ἐρεῖς Λέγω δή ἔφην οἶμαι γὰρ τὸ μετὰ τοῦτο ἑορταὶ γίγνονται
greek_tokens: o o paizw fhmi outos su kai egw erew legos dh fhmi oimh gar o meta outos eorth gignomai para autos kai kwmos kai qaleia kai etaira kai o toioutos pas os an eros turannos endon oikew diakubernaw o o yuxazw apas anagkh fhmi ara oun ou polus kai deinos parablastanw epiqumia hmeros te kai nuc ekastos polus deomai polus mentoi taxus ara analiskw ean tis eimi prosodos pws de ou
english_head: you shall tell me. I do, I said; for, I take it, next there are among them feasts and carousals and revellings and courtesans and all the doings of those whose souls are entirely swayed by the indwelling tyrant Eros. Inevitably, he said. And do not many and dread appetites shoot up beside this master passion every day and night in need of many things? Many indeed. And so any revenues there may be are quickly expended. Of course. And after this

greek_head: Καὶ μετὰ τοῦτο δὴ δανεισμοὶ καὶ τῆς οὐσίας παραιρέσεις Τί μήν
greek_tokens: kai meta outos dh daneismos kai o ousia parairesis tis meis otan de dh panth epileipw ara ou anagkh men o epiqumia boa puknos te kai sfodros enneosseuw o de wsper upo kentron elaunw o te allos epiqumia kai diaferontws upo autos o erws passw o allos wsper doruforos hgeomai oistraw kai skopaw tis tis exis os dunatos afairew apataw h
english_head: there are borrowings and levyings upon the estate? Of course. And when all these resources fail, must there not come a cry from the frequent and fierce nestlings of desire hatched in his soul, and must not such men, urged, as it were by goads, by the other desires, and especially by the ruling passion itself as captain of their bodyguard—to keep up the figure—must they not run wild and look to see who has aught that can be taken from him by deceit

greek_head: βιασάμενον Σφόδρα γ' ἔφη
greek_tokens: biaw sfodra ge fhmi anagkaion dh pantaxoqen ferw h megas wdis te kai odunh sunoxwka anagkaion ara oun wsper o en autos hdona epigignomai o arxaios pleos exw kai o ekeinos afairew outws kai autos aciwsis neos eimi pathr te kai mhthr pleos exw kai afairew ean o eautou meros analwsis aponemw o patrwos alla tis meis fhmi
english_head: or violence? Most certainly. And so he is compelled to sweep it in from every source or else be afflicted with great travail and pain. He is. And just as the new, upspringing pleasures in him got the better of the original passions of his soul and robbed them, so he himself, though younger, will claim the right to get the better of his father and mother, and, after spending his own share, to seize and convert to his own use a portion of his father’s estate. Of course, he said, what else? And if they resist him,

greek_head: Ἂν δὲ δὴ αὐτῷ μὴ ἐπιτρέπωσιν ἆρ' οὐ τὸ μὲν πρῶτον ἐπιχειροῖ ἂν κλέπτειν καὶ ἀπατᾶν τοὺς γονέας
greek_tokens: an de dh autos mh epitrepw ara ou o men proteros epixeirew an kleptw kai apath o goneus pantws opote de mh dunamai arpazw an kai biazw meta outos oimh fhmi antexw dh kai maxomai eimi qaumasios gerwn te kai graus ara eulabeomai an kai feidomai mh tis draw o turannikos ou panu eimi de os egw qarsew peri o gonaw o toioutos alla eimi adeimantos pros zeus eneka newsti filos kai ou anagkh etaira gignomai o pala filos kai anagkh
english_head: would he not at first attempt to rob and steal from his parents and deceive them? Certainly. And if he failed in that, would he not next seize it by force? I think so, he said. And then, good sir, if the old man and the old woman clung to it and resisted him, would he be careful to refrain from the acts of a tyrant? I am not without my fears, he said, for the parents of such a one. Nay, Adeimantus, in heaven’s name, do you suppose that, for the sake of a newly found belle amie bound to him by no necessary tie, such a one would strike the dear mother,

greek_head: μητέρα ἢ ἕνεκα ὡραίου νεωστὶ φίλου γεγονότος οὐκ ἀναγκαίου τὸν ἄωρόν τε καὶ ἀναγκαῖον πρεσβύτην πατέρα
greek_tokens: mhthr h eneka wraios newsti filos gignomai ou anagkaion o awros te kai anagkaion presbuths pathr kai o filos arxaios dokew an su o toioutos plhgh te didwmi kai katadoulow an autos upo ekeinos ei eis o autos oikia agw nai ma dios eimi de os sfodra ge makarios eimi de egw eoika eimi o turannikos uios tiktw panu ge fhmi
english_head: his by necessity and from his birth? Or for the sake of a blooming new-found bel ami, not necessary to his life, he would rain blows upon the aged father past his prime, closest of his kin and oldest of his friends? And would he subject them to those new favorites if he brought them under the same roof? Yes, by Zeus, he said. A most blessed lot it seems to be, said I, to be the parent of a tyrant son. It does indeed, he said. And again, when the resources of his father and mother are exhausted and fail such a one,

greek_head: Τί δ' ὅταν δὴ τὰ πατρὸς καὶ μητρὸς ἐπιλείπῃ τὸν τοιοῦτον πολὺ δὲ ἤδη συνειλεγμένον ἐν αὐτῷ ᾖ τὸ τῶν ἡδονῶν
greek_tokens: tis de otan dh o pathr kai mhthr epileipw o toioutos polus de hdh sullegw en autos eimi o o hdona smhnos ou proteros men oikia tis efaptw toixos h tis oye nuktwr eimi o imation meta de outos ieron tis newkorew kai en outos dh pas os pala exw doca ek pais peri kalh te kai aisxros o dikaios poiew o newsti ek douleios luw doruforew o erws kratew meta ekeinos o proteros men onar
english_head: and the swarm of pleasures collected in his soul is grown great, will he not first lay hands on the wall of someone’s house or the cloak of someone who walks late at night, and thereafter he will make a clean sweep of some temple, and in all these actions the beliefs which he held from boyhood about the honorable and the base, the opinions accounted just, will be overmastered by the opinions newly emancipated and released, which, serving as bodyguards of the ruling passion, will prevail in alliance with it—I mean the opinions that formerly were freed from restraint in sleep,

greek_head: ἐλύοντο ἐν ὕπνῳ ὅτε ἦν αὐτὸς ἔτι ὑπὸ νόμοις τε καὶ πατρὶ δημοκρατούμενος ἐν ἑαυτῷ τυραννευθεὶς δὲ ὑπὸ Ἔρωτος
greek_tokens: eluw en upnon oste eimi autos eti upo nomos te kai pathr dhmokrateomai en eautou turanneuw de upo erws oios oligakis gignomai onar upar toioutos aei gignomai oute tis fonos deinos apexw oute brwma oute ergnumi
english_head: when, being still under the control of his father and the laws, he maintained the democratic constitution in his soul. But now, when under the tyranny of his ruling passion, he is continuously and in waking hours what he rarely became in sleep, and he will refrain from no atrocity of murder nor from any food or deed,

greek_head: ἀλλὰ τυραννικῶς ἐν αὐτῷ ὁ Ἔρως ἐν πάσῃ ἀναρχίᾳ καὶ ἀνομίᾳ ζῶν ἅτε αὐτὸς ὢν μόναρχος τὸν ἔχοντά τε αὐτὸν
greek_tokens: alla turannikos en autos o eros en paomai anarxia kai anomia zaw ate autos eimi monarxos o exw te autos wsper polis agnumi epi pas tolma oqen eautou te kai o peri eautou qorubos qreyis o men ecwqen eiserxomai apo kakazw omilia o de endoqen upo o autos tropos kai eautou anihmi kai eleuqerow h ou outos o bios o toioutos outos men oun fhmi kai an men ge eimi de egw oligos e toioutos en polis
english_head: but the passion that dwells in him as a tyrant will live in utmost anarchy and lawlessness, and, since it is itself sole autocrat, will urge the polity, so to speak, of him in whom it dwells to dare anything and everything in order to find support for himself and the hubbub of his henchmen, in part introduced from outside by evil associations, and in part released and liberated within by the same habits of life as his. Is not this the life of such a one? It is this, he said. And if, I said, there are only a few of this kind in a city,

greek_head: ὦσι καὶ τὸ ἄλλο πλῆθος σωφρονῇ ἐξελθόντες ἄλλον τινὰ δορυφοροῦσι τύραννον ἢ μισθοῦ ἐπικουροῦσιν ἐάν που
greek_tokens: eimi kai o allos plhqos swfronew ecerxomai allos tis doruforew turannos h misqos epiepikourew ean pou polemos eimi ean de en eirhnh te kai hsuxia gignomai autos dh en o polis kakos draw mikros polus o poios dh legw oios kleptw toixwruxew ballantiotomew lwpodutew ierosulew andrapodizw eimi de oste sukofantew ean dunatos eimi legw kai yeudomarturew kai dwrodokew
english_head: and the others, the multitude as a whole, are sober-minded, the few go forth into exile and serve some tyrant elsewhere as bodyguard or become mercenaries in any war there may be. But if they spring up in time of peace and tranquillity they stay right there in the city and effect many small evils. What kind of evils do you mean? Oh, they just steal, break into houses, cut purses, strip men of their garments, plunder temples, and kidnap, and if they are fluent speakers they become sycophants and bear false witness and take bribes.

greek_head: Σμικρά γ' ἔφη κακὰ λέγεις ἐὰν ὀλίγοι ὦσιν οἱ τοιοῦτοι Τὰ γὰρ σμικρά ἦν δ' ἐγώ πρὸς τὰ μεγάλα σμικρά ἐστιν
greek_tokens: mikros ge fhmi kakos legw ean oligos eimi e toioutos o gar mikros eimi de egw pros o megas mikros eimi kai outos dh pas pros turannos ponhria te kai aqlioths polis o legw oude iktar ballis otan gar dh polus en polis gignomai e toioutos kai allos e sunepomai autos kai aisqanomai eautou o plhqos tote outos eimi e o turannos gennaw meta dhmos anoia ekeinos os an autos malista autos en eautou megas kai pleistos
english_head: Yes, small evils indeed, he said, if the men of this sort are few. Why, yes, I said, for small evils are relatively small compared with great, and in respect of the corruption and misery of a state all of them together, as the saying goes, don’t come within hail of the mischief done by a tyrant. For when men of this sort and their followers become numerous in a state and realize their numbers, then it is they who, in conjunction with the folly of the people, create a tyrant out of that one of them who has

greek_head: ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ τύραννον ἔχῃ Εἰκότως γ' ἔφη τυραννικώτατος γὰρ ἂν εἴη
greek_tokens: en o yuxw turannos exis eoikotws ge fhmi turannikos gar an eaw oukoun ean men ekwn ufihmi ean de mh epitrepw o polis wsper tote mhthr kai pathr kolazw outws palin o patris ean oios su eimi kolazw epeisagw neos etairos kai upo outos dh douleuw o pala filos mhtris te krhs fhmi kai patris ecis te kai qreyis kai outos dh o telos an eaw o epiqumia o toioutos anhr
english_head: the greatest and mightiest tyrant in his own soul. Naturally, he said, for he would be the most tyrannical. Then if the people yield willingly—’tis well, but if the city resists him, then, just as in the previous case the man chastized his mother and his father, so now in turn will he chastize his fatherland if he can, bringing in new boon companions beneath whose sway he will hold and keep enslaved his once dear motherland—as the Cretans name her—and fatherland. And this would be the end of such a man’s desire.

greek_head: Τοῦτο ἦ δ' ὅς παντάπασί γε Οὐκοῦν ἦν δ' ἐγώ οὗτοί γε τοιοίδε γίγνονται ἰδίᾳ καὶ
greek_tokens: outos eimi de os pantapasi ge oukoun eimi de egw outos ge toiosde gignomai idios kai prin arxw proteros men os an suneimi h kolac eautou suneimi kai pas etoimos uphretew h ean tis tis deomai
english_head: Yes, he said, this, just this. Then, said I, is not this the character of such men in private life and before they rule the state: to begin with they associate with flatterers, who are ready to do anything to serve them,

greek_head: αὐτοὶ ὑποπεσόντες πάντα σχήματα τολμῶντες ποιεῖν ὡς οἰκεῖοι διαπραξάμενοι δὲ ἀλλότριοι
greek_tokens: autos upopiptw pas sxhma tolmaw poiew ws oikeios diaprassw de allotrios kai sfodra ge en pas ara o bios zaw filos men oudepote oudeis aei de tis despozw h douleuw allos eleuqeria de kai filios alhqhs turannikos fusis aei ageustos panu men oun ara oun ou orqos an o toioutos apistos kalew pws de ou kai meis adikos ge ws oios te malista eiper orqos
english_head: or, if they themselves want something, they themselves fawn and shrink from no contortion or abasement in protest of their friendship, though, once the object gained, they sing another tune. Yes indeed, he said. Throughout their lives, then, they never know what it is to be the friends of anybody. They are always either masters or slaves, but the tyrannical nature never tastes freedom or true friendship. Quite so. May we not rightly call such men faithless? Of course. Yes, and unjust to the last degree,

greek_head: ἐν τοῖς πρόσθεν ὡμολογήσαμεν περὶ δικαιοσύνης οἷόν ἐστιν
greek_tokens: en o prosqen omologew peri dikaiosunh oios eimi alla meis eimi de os orqos ge kefalaiow toinun eimi de egw o kakos eimi de pou oios onar dierxomai os an upar toioutos eimi panu men oun oukoun outos gignomai os an turannikos fusis eimi monarxew kai osos an plew xronos en turannis biaw tosoutos mallon toioutos anagkh fhmi diadexomai o logos o glauc ara oun eimi de egw os an fainw ponhros kai
english_head: if we were right in our previous agreement about the nature of justice. But surely, he said, we were right. Let us sum up, then, said I, the most evil type of man. He is, I presume, the man who, in his waking hours, has the qualities we found in his dream state. Quite so. And he is developed from the man who, being by nature most of a tyrant, achieves sole power, and the longer he lives as an actual tyrant the stronger this quality becomes. Inevitably, said Glaucon, taking up the argument. And shall we find, said I, that the man who is shown to be the most evil

greek_head: ἀθλιώτατος φανήσεται καὶ ὃς ἂν πλεῖστον χρόνον καὶ μάλιστα τυραννεύσῃ μάλιστά τε καὶ πλεῖστον χρόνον
greek_tokens: aqlios fainw kai os an pleistos xronos kai malista turanneuw malista te kai pleistos xronos toioutos gignomai o alhqeia o de polus polus kai dokew anagkh fhmi outos goun outws exw allos tis oun eimi de egw o ge turannikos kata o turanneuw polis an eaw omoioths dhmotikos de kata dhmokrateomai kai e allos outws tis meis oukoun ostis polis pros polis aretaw kai eudaimonia outos kai anhr pros anhr
english_head: will also be the most miserable, and the man who is most of a tyrant for the longest time is most and longest miserable in sober truth? Yet the many have many opinions. That much, certainly, he said, must needs be true. Does not the tyrannical man, said I, correspond to the tyrannical state in similitude, the democratic to the democratic and the others likewise? Surely. And may we not infer that the relation of state to state in respect of virtue and happiness

greek_head: Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Τί οὖν ἀρετῇ τυραννουμένη πόλις πρὸς βασιλευομένην
greek_tokens: pws gar ou tis oun aretaw turanneuw polis pros basileuw oios o proteros dierxomai pas enantion fhmi o men gar aristos o de kakos ou eromai eipon opoteros legw dhlos gar alla eudaimonia te au kai aqlioths wsautws h allos krinw kai mh ekplhssw pros o turannos enos eimi blepw mhde ei tis oligos peri ekeinos alla ws xrh oloc
english_head: is the same as that of the man to the man? Of course. What is, then, in respect of virtue, the relation of a city ruled by a tyrant to a royal city as we first described it? They are direct contraries, he said; the one is the best, the other the worst. I’ll not ask which is which, I said, because that is obvious. But again in respect of happiness and wretchedness, is your estimate the same or different? And let us not be dazzled by fixing our eyes on that one man, the tyrant, or a few of his court, but let us enter into and survey the entire city,

greek_head: τὴν πόλιν εἰσελθόντας θεάσασθαι καταδύντες εἰς ἅπασαν καὶ ἰδόντες οὕτω δόξαν ἀποφαινώμεθα
greek_tokens: o polis eiserxomai qeaomai kataduw eis apas kai eidon outws doca apofainw alla orqos fhmi prokalew kai dhlos pas ostis turanneuw men ou eimi aqlios basileuw de ou eudaimwn ara oun eimi de egw kai peri o androw o autos outos
english_head: as is right, and declare our opinion only after we have so dived to its uttermost recesses and contemplated its life as a whole. That is a fair challenge, he said, and it is clear to everybody that there is no city more wretched than that in which a tyrant rules, and none more happy than that governed by a true king. And would it not also be a fair challenge, said I,

greek_head: προκαλούμενος ὀρθῶς ἂν προκαλοίμην ἀξιῶν κρίνειν περὶ αὐτῶν ἐκεῖνον ὃς δύναται τῇ διανοίᾳ εἰς ἀνδρὸς ἦθος ἐνδὺς
greek_tokens: prokalew orqos an prokalew acia krinw peri autos ekeinos os dunamai o dianoia eis anhr hqos enduw dieidon kai mh kaqa pas ecwqen oraw ekplhssw upo o o turannikos prostasis os pros o ecw sxhmatizw alla ikanos dioraw ei oun oiomai dew ekeinos pas egw akouw o dunatew men krinw sunoikew de en o autos kai paragignomai en te o kata oikia pracis ws pros ekastos o oikeios exis en os
english_head: to ask you to accept as the only proper judge of the two men the one who is able in thought to enter with understanding into the very soul and temper of a man, and who is not like a child viewing him from outside, overawed by the tyrants’ great attendance, and the pomp and circumstance which they assume in the eyes of the world, but is able to see through it all? And what if I should assume, then, that the man to whom we ought all to listen is he who has this capacity of judgement and who has lived under the same roof with a tyrant and has witnessed his conduct in his own home and observed in person

greek_head: μάλιστα γυμνὸς ἂν ὀφθείη τῆς τραγικῆς σκευῆς καὶ ἐν αὖ τοῖς δημοσίοις κινδύνοις καὶ ταῦτα πάντα ἰδόντα κελεύοιμεν
greek_tokens: malista gumnos an oraw o tragikos skeuazw kai en au o dhmosios kindunos kai outos pas eidon keleuw ecaggellw pws exis eudaimonia kai aqlioths o turannos pros o allos orqos an fhmi kai outos prokalew boulomai oun eimi de egw prospoiew egw eimi o dunaths an krinw kai hdh entugxanw toioutos ina exw ostis apokrinw o erwtaw panu ge
english_head: his dealings with his intimates in each instance where he would best be seen stripped of his vesture of tragedy, and who had likewise observed his behavior in the hazards of his public life—and if we should ask the man who has seen all this to be the messenger to report on the happiness or misery of the tyrant as compared with other men? That also would be a most just challenge, he said. Shall we, then, make believe, said I, that we are of those who are thus able to judge and who have ere now lived with tyrants, so that we may have someone to answer our questions? By all means.

greek_head: Ἴθι δή μοι ἔφην ὧδε σκόπει τὴν ὁμοιότητα ἀναμιμνῃσκόμενος τῆς τε πόλεως καὶ τοῦ ἀνδρός οὕτω καθ' ἕκαστον
greek_tokens: eimi dh egw fhmi wde skopaw o omoioths anamimnhskw o te polis kai o anhr outws kaqa ekastos en meros aqrew o paqhma ekateros legos o poios fhmi proteros men eimi de egw ws polis eipon eleuqeros h doulh o turanneuw erew ws oios su fhmi malista doulh kai meis oraw ge en autos despoths kai eleuqeros oraw fhmi mikros ge tis outos o de oloc ws epos eipon en autos kai o epieikhs atimos te kai aqlios doulos
english_head: Come, then, said I, examine it thus. Recall the general likeness between the city and the man, and then observe in turn what happens to each of them. What things? he said. In the first place, said I, will you call the state governed by a tyrant free or enslaved, speaking of it as a state? Utterly enslaved, he said. And yet you see in it masters and freemen. I see, he said, a small portion of such, but the entirety, so to speak, and the best part of it, is shamefully and wretchedly enslaved. If, then, I said,

greek_head: Εἰ οὖν εἶπον ὅμοιος ἀνὴρ τῇ πόλει οὐ καὶ ἐν ἐκείνῳ ἀνάγκη τὴν αὐτὴν τάξιν ἐνεῖναι καὶ πολλῆς μὲν δουλείας τε
greek_tokens: ei oun eipon omoios anhr o polis ou kai en ekeinos anagkh o autos tacis enihmi kai polus men douleios te kai aneleuqeria gemw o yuxh autos kai outos autos o mera douleuw o eimi epieikhs mikros de kai o moxqhros kai manikos despozw anagkh fhmi tis oun doulh h eleuqeros o toioutos fhmi eimi yuxh doulh dhpou egw oukoun o ge au doulh kai turanneuw polis hkistos poiew o boulomai polus ge
english_head: the man resembles the state, must not the same proportion obtain in him, and his soul teem with boundless servility and illiberality, the best and most reasonable parts of it being enslaved, while a small part, the worst and the most frenzied, plays the despot? Inevitably, he said. Then will you say that such a soul is enslaved or free? Enslaved, I should suppose. Again, does not the enslaved and tyrannized city least of all do what it really wishes? Decidedly so. Then the tyrannized soul—

greek_head: Καὶ ἡ τυραννουμένη ἄρα ψυχὴ ἥκιστα ποιήσει ἃ ἂν βουληθῇ ὡς περὶ ὅλης εἰπεῖν ψυχῆς ὑπὸ δὲ οἴστρου ἀεὶ
greek_tokens: kai o turanneuw ara yuxh hkistos poihsis o an boulomai ws peri oloc eipon yuxazw upo de oistros aei elkw bia taraxh kai metameleia mestos eimi pws gar ou plousios de h penomai anagkh o turanneuw polis eimi penomai
english_head: to speak of the soul as a whole—also will least of all do what it wishes, but being always perforce driven and drawn by the gadfly of desire it will be full of confusion and repentance. Of course. And must the tyrannized city

greek_head: Καὶ ψυχὴν ἄρα τυραννικὴν πενιχρὰν καὶ ἄπληστον ἀνάγκη ἀεὶ εἶναι
greek_tokens: kai yuxh ara turannikos penixros kai aplhstos anagkh aei eimi outws eimi de os tis de fobos gemw ara ou anagkh o te toioutos polis o te toioutos anhr polus ge odurmos te kai stenagmos kai qrhnos kai alghdwn oiomai en tis allh plews euriskw oudamos en andris de hgeomai o toioutos en allos tis plew eimi h en o mainomai upo epiqumia te kai erws outos o turannikos pws gar an fhmi
english_head: be rich or poor? Poor. Then the tyrant soul also must of necessity always be needy and suffer from unfulfilled desire. So it is, he said. And again, must not such a city, as well as such a man, be full of terrors and alarms? It must indeed. And do you think you will find more lamentations and groans and wailing and anguish in any other city? By no means. And so of man, do you think these things will more abound in any other than in this tyrant type, that is maddened by its desires and passions? How could it be so? he said. In view of all these

greek_head: Εἰς πάντα δὴ οἶμαι ταῦτά τε καὶ ἄλλα τοιαῦτα ἀποβλέψας τήν τε πόλιν τῶν πόλεων ἀθλιωτάτην ἔκρινας
greek_tokens: eis pas dh oimh outos te kai allos toioutos apoblepw o te polis o polis aqlios krinw oukoun orqos fhmi kai mala eimi de egw alla peri o anhr au o turannikos tis legw eis tautos outos apoblepw makros fhmi aqlios eimi o allos apas outos eimi de egw ouketi orqos legw pws eimi de os oupw fhmi oimh outos eimi o toioutos malista alla tis meis ode isos su eti docis eimi outos aqlios poios
english_head: and other like considerations, then, I take it, you judged that this city is the most miserable of cities. And was I not right? he said. Yes, indeed, said I. But of the tyrant man, what have you to say in view of these same things? That he is far and away the most miserable of all, he said. I cannot admit, said I, that you are right in that too. How so? said he. This one, said I, I take it, has not yet attained the acme of misery. Then who has? Perhaps you will regard the one I am about to name as still more wretched.

greek_head: Ὃς ἄν ἦν δ' ἐγώ τυραννικὸς ὢν μὴ ἰδιώτην βίον καταβιῷ ἀλλὰ δυστυχὴς ᾖ καὶ αὐτῷ ὑπό τινος συμφορᾶς
greek_tokens: os an eimi de egw turannikos eimi mh idiwths bios katabiazomai alla dustuxhs eimi kai autos upo tis sumfora ekporizw wste turannos gignomai tekmairomai su fhmi ek o proerew alhqhs legw nai eimi de egw alla ou oiomai xrh o toioutos alla eu mala o toioutos logos skopaw peri gar su o megas o skeyis agaqos te bios kai kakos orqos eimi de os skopaw dh ei ara tis legos dokew gar egw dew ennoew
english_head: What one? The one, said I, who, being of tyrannical temper, does not live out his life in private station but is so unfortunate that by some unhappy chance he is enabled to become an actual tyrant. I infer from what has already been said, he replied, that you speak truly. Yes, said I, but it is not enough to suppose such things. We must examine them thoroughly by reason and an argument such as this. For our inquiry concerns the greatest of all things, the good life or the bad life. Quite right, he replied. Consider, then, if there is anything in what I say.

greek_head: ἐκ τῶνδε περὶ αὐτοῦ σκοποῦντας Ἐκ τίνων
greek_tokens: ek ode peri autos skopaw ek tinw ek eis ekastos o idiwths osos plousios en polis andrapodon polus ktaomai outos gar outos ge prosomoios exw o turannos o polus arxw diaferw de o ekeinos plhqos diaferw gar oistos oun ostis outos adehs exw kai ou fobew o oikeths tis gar an fobew oudeis eipon alla o aitios ennoew nai ostis ge pas o polis eis ekastos bohqew o idiwths
english_head: For I think we must get a notion of the matter from these examples. From which? From individual wealthy private citizens in our states who possess many slaves. For these resemble the tyrant in being rulers over many, only the tyrant’s numbers are greater. Yes, they are. You are aware, then, that they are unafraid and do not fear their slaves? What should they fear? Nothing, I said; but do you perceive the reason why? Yes, because the entire state

greek_head: Καλῶς ἦν δ' ἐγώ λέγεις τί δέ εἴ τις θεῶν ἄνδρα ἕνα ὅτῳ ἔστιν ἀνδράποδα πεντήκοντα ἢ καὶ πλείω ἄρας ἐκ
greek_tokens: kalos eimi de egw legw tis de ei tis qea anhr enos ostis eimi andrapodon penthkonta h kai plew airw ek o polis autos te kai gunh kai pais qeia eis erhmia meta o allos ousia te kai o oikeths opou autos mhdeis o eleuqeros mellw bohqew en poios an tis kai oposos fobos oiomai gignomai autos peri te eautou kai pais kai gunh mh apollumi upo o oikeths en pas eimi de os egw
english_head: is ready to defend each citizen. You are right, I said. But now suppose some god should catch up a man who has fifty or more slaves and waft him with his wife and children away from the city and set him down with his other possessions and his slaves in a solitude where no freeman could come to his rescue. What and how great would be his fear, do you suppose, lest he and his wife and children be destroyed by the slaves? The greatest in the world, he said, if you ask me.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἀναγκάζοιτο ἄν τινας ἤδη θωπεύειν αὐτῶν τῶν δούλων καὶ ὑπισχνεῖσθαι πολλὰ καὶ ἐλευθεροῦν οὐδὲν
greek_tokens: oukoun anagkazw an tis hdh qwpeuw autos o doulos kai upisxneomai polus kai eleuqerow oudeis deomai kai kolac autos an qerapwn anafainw polus anagkh fhmi autos h apollumi tis de ei kai allos eimi de egw o qeos kuklos katoikizw geitwn polus autos e mh anexw ei tis allos allos despozw aciow alla ei pou tis toioutos lambanw o esxatos timwrew timwria
english_head: And would he not forthwith find it necessary to fawn upon some of the slaves and make them many promises and emancipate them, though nothing would be further from his wish? And so he would turn out to be the flatterer of his own servants. He would certainly have to, he said, or else perish. But now suppose, said I, that god established round about him numerous neighbors who would not tolerate the claim of one man to be master of another, but would inflict the utmost penalties on any such person on whom they could lay their hands. I think, he said,

greek_head: Ἔτι ἄν ἔφη οἶμαι μᾶλλον ἐν παντὶ κακοῦ εἴη κύκλῳ φρουρούμενος ὑπὸ πάντων πολεμίων
greek_tokens: eti an fhmi oimh mallon en pas kakos eaw kuklos frourew upo pas polemios ara oun ou en toioutos men desmwthrion dew o turannos fusis eimi oios dierxomai polus kai pantodaphs fobos kai erws mestos lixnos de eimi autos o yuxh monos o en o polis oute apodhmew ecesti oudamose oute qewrew osos dh kai e allos eleuqeros epiqumhths eimi kataduw de en o oikia o polus ws gunh zaw
english_head: that his plight would be still more desperate, encompassed by nothing but enemies. And is not that the sort of prison-house in which the tyrant is pent, being of a nature such as we have described and filled with multitudinous and manifold terrors and appetites? Yet greedy and avid of spirit as he is, he only of the citizens may not travel abroad or view any of the sacred festivals that other freemen yearn to see, but he must live for the most part cowering in the recesses of his house like a woman,

greek_head: φθονῶν καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις πολίταις ἐάν τις ἔξω ἀποδημῇ καί τι ἀγαθὸν ὁρᾷ
greek_tokens: fqonew kai o allos poliths ean tis ecw apodhmew kai tis agaqos oraw pantapasi men oun fhmi oukoun o toioutos kakos plew karpow anhr os an kakos en eautou politeuw os nundi su aqlios krinw o turannikos ws mh idiwths katabiazomai alla anagkazw upo tis tuxh turanneuw kai eautou eimi akratwr allos epixeirhsis arxw wsper ei tis kamnw swma kai akratwr eautou mh idiwteuw alla agwnizomai
english_head: envying among the other citizens anyone who goes abroad and sees any good thing. Most certainly, he said. And does not such a harvest of ills measure the difference between the man who is merely ill-governed in his own soul, the man of tyrannical temper, whom you just now judged to be most miserable, and the man who, having this disposition, does not live out his life in private station but is constrained by some ill hap to become an actual tyrant, and while unable to control himself attempts to rule over others, as if a man with a sick and incontinent body should not live the private life but should be compelled

greek_head: πρὸς ἄλλα σώματα καὶ μαχόμενος ἀναγκάζοιτο διάγειν τὸν βίον
greek_tokens: pros allos swma kai maxomai anagkazw diagw o bios pantapasi fhmi omoios te kai alhqhs legw eimi swkraths oukoun eimi de egw eimi filos glauc pantelhs o paqos aqlios kai o upo sew krinw xalepos zaw xalepos eti zaw o turanneuw komidh ge fhmi eimi ara o alhqeia kan ei mh tis dokew o o eimi turannos o eimi doulos o megas qwpeia kai douleios
english_head: to pass his days in contention and strife with other persons? Your analogy is most apt and true, Socrates, he said. Is not that then, dear Glaucon, said I, a most unhappy experience in every way? And is not the tyrant’s life still worse than that which was judged by you to be the worst? Precisely so, he said. Then it is the truth, though some may deny it, that the real tyrant is really enslaved

greek_head: καὶ κόλαξ τῶν πονηροτάτων καὶ τὰς ἐπιθυμίας οὐδ' ὁπωςτιοῦν ἀποπιμπλάς ἀλλὰ πλείστων ἐπιδεέστατος καὶ πένης
greek_tokens: kai kolac o ponhros kai o epiqumia oude opwsoun apopimplhmi alla pleistos epidehs kai penhs o alhqeia fainw ean tis oloc yuxh efisthmi qeaomai kai fobos gemos dia pas o bios sfadasmos te kai odunh plhrhs eiper o o polis diaqesis eis arxw eoika eoika de eimi gar kai mala fhmi
english_head: to cringings and servitudes beyond compare, a flatterer of the basest men, and that, so far from finding even the least satisfaction for his desires, he is in need of most things, and is a poor man in very truth, as is apparent if one knows how to observe a soul in its entirety; and throughout his life he teems with terrors and is full of convulsions and pains, if in fact he resembles the condition of the city which he rules; and he is like it, is he not?

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν καὶ πρὸς τούτοις ἔτι ἀποδώσομεν τῷ ἀνδρὶ καὶ ἃ τὸ πρότερον εἴπομεν ὅτι ἀνάγκη καὶ εἶναι καὶ ἔτι μᾶλλον
greek_tokens: oukoun kai pros outos eti apodidwmi o andris kai o o proteros eipon ostis anagkh kai eimi kai eti mallon gignomai autos h proteros dia o arxh fqoneros apistos adikos afilos anosios kai pas kakia pandokew te kai trefw kai ek apas outos malista men autos dustuxew eimi epeita de kai o pimplhmi eautou toioutos apergazomai oudeis su fhmi o new exw anteraw eimi dh egw fhmi egw nun hdh wsper o dia pas
english_head: Yes, indeed, he said. And in addition, shall we not further attribute to him all that we spoke of before, and say that he must needs be, and, by reason of his rule, come to be still more than he was, envious, faithless, unjust, friendless, impious, a vessel and nurse of all iniquity, and so in consequence be himself most unhappy make all about him so? No man of sense will gainsay that, he said. Come then, said I,

greek_head: κριτὴς ἀποφαίνεται καὶ σὺ οὕτω τίς πρῶτος κατὰ τὴν σὴν δόξαν εὐδαιμονίᾳ καὶ τίς δεύτερος καὶ τοὺς ἄλλους ἑξῆς
greek_tokens: kriths apofainw kai su outws tis proteros kata o shs doca eudaimonia kai tis deuteros kai o allos echs pente eimi krinw basilikos timokratikos oligarxikos dhmokratikos turannikos alla radios fhmi o krisis kaqa gar eiserxomai egw wsper xoros krinon aretaw kai kakia kai eudaimonia kai o enantios misqow oun khruc eimi de egw h autos aneipein ostis o aristwn us o ariston te kai dikaios eudaimwn
english_head: now at last, even as the judge of last instance pronounces, so do you declare who in your opinion is first in happiness and who second, and similarly judge the others, all five in succession, the royal, the timocratic, the oligarchic, the democratic, and the tyrannical man. Nay, he said, the decision is easy. For as if they were choruses I judge them in the order of their entrance, and so rank them in respect of virtue and vice, happiness and its contrary. Shall we hire a herald, then, said I, or shall I myself make proclamation that the son of Ariston pronounced the best man and the most righteous to be the happiest,

greek_head: ἔκρινε τοῦτον δ' εἶναι τὸν βασιλικώτατον καὶ βασιλεύοντα αὑτοῦ τὸν δὲ κάκιστόν τε καὶ ἀδικώτατον
greek_tokens: krinw outos de eimi o basilikos kai basileuw eautou o de kakos te kai adikos aqlios outos de au tugxanw eimi os an turannikos eimi eautou te ostis malista turanneuw kai o polis anaerw su fhmi eimi oun prosanagoreuw eipon ean lanqanw toioutos eimi ean mh pas anqrwpos te kai qeos prosanagoreuw fhmi eien dh eipon outos men egw o apodeicis eis an eaw
english_head: and that he is the one who is the most kingly and a king over himself; and declared that the most evil and most unjust is the most unhappy, who again is the man who, having the most of the tyrannical temper in himself, become, most of a tyrant over himself and over the state? Let it have been so proclaimed by you, he said. Shall I add the clause alike whether their character is known to all men and gods or is not known? Add that to the proclamation, he said. Very good, said I; this, then, would be one of our proofs,

greek_head: δευτέραν δὲ ἰδὲ τήνδε ἐάν τι δόξῃ εἶναι Τίς αὕτη
greek_tokens: deuteros de ide ode ean tis doca eimi tis outos epei wsper polis eimi de egw diairew kata treis eidos outws kai yuxh eis ekastos trixh logistikos dexomai ws egw dokew kai eteros apodeicis tis outos ode treis eimi trissos kai hdona egw fainw eis ekastos eis idios epiqumia te wsautws kai arxh pws legw fhmi o men fhmi eimi os manqanw anqrwpos o de os qumow o de tritos dia polueidia eis ou exw onoma proseipon
english_head: but examine this second one and see if there is anything in it. What is it? Since, said I, corresponding to the three types in the city, the soul also is tripartite, it will admit, I think, of another demonstration also. What is that? The following: The three parts have also, it appears to me, three kinds of pleasure, one peculiar to each, and similarly three appetites and controls. What do you mean? he said. One part, we say, is that with which a man learns, one is that with which he feels anger. But the third part, owing to its manifold forms, we could not easily designate by any one distinctive name,

greek_head: ἰδίῳ αὐτοῦ ἀλλὰ ὃ μέγιστον καὶ ἰσχυρότατον εἶχεν ἐν αὑτῷ τούτῳ ἐπωνομάσαμεν ἐπιθυμητικὸν γὰρ αὐτὸ
greek_tokens: idios autos alla o megas kai isxuros exw en eautou outos eponomazw epiqumhtikos gar autos kalew dia sfodroths o te peri o edwdh epiqumia kai posis kai afrodisios kai osos allos outos akolouqos kai filoxrhmatos dh ostis dia xrhma malista apotelew
english_head: but gave it the name of its chief and strongest element; for we called it the appetitive part because of the intensity of its appetites concerned with food and drink and love and their accompaniments, and likewise the money-loving part, because money is the chief instrument

greek_head: αἱ τοιαῦται ἐπιθυμίαι Καὶ ὀρθῶς γ' ἔφη
greek_tokens: o toioutos epiqumia kai orqos ge fhmi ara oun kai o hdona autos kai filios ei fhmi eimi o kerdos malista an eis eis kefalaios apereidw o logos wste tis egw autos dhlow opote outos o yuxazw o meros legw kai kalew autos filoxrhmatos kai filokerdhs orqos an kalew egw goun dokew fhmi tis de o qumoeidhs ou pros o kratew mentoi fhmi kai nikh kai eudokimew aei oloc ormaw
english_head: for the gratification of such desires. And rightly, he said. And if we should also say that its pleasure and its love were for gain or profit, should we not thus best bring it together under one head in our discourse so as to understand each other when we speak of this part of the soul, and justify our calling it the money-loving and gain-loving part? I, at any rate, think so, he said. And, again, of the high-spirited element, do we not say that it is wholly set on predominance and victory and good repute?

greek_head: Καὶ μάλα Εἰ οὖν φιλόνικον αὐτὸ καὶ φιλότιμον προσαγορεύοιμεν
greek_tokens: kai mala ei oun filoneikos autos kai filotimos prosagoreuw eimi emmelhs an exw emmelhs men oun alla meis os ge manqanw pas dhlos ostis pros o oida o alhqeia oph exis pas aei teinw kai xrhma te kai doca hkistos outos outos melos polus ge filomaqhs dh kai filosofos kalew autos kata tropos an kalew pws gar ou oukoun eimi de egw kai arxw en o yuxh o men
english_head: Yes, indeed. And might we not appropriately designate it as the ambitious part and that which is covetous of honor? Most appropriately. But surely it is obvious to everyone that all the endeavor of the part by which we learn is ever towards knowledge of the truth of things, and that it least of the three is concerned for wealth and reputation. Much the least. Lover of learning and lover of wisdom would be suitable designations for that. Quite so, he said. Is it not also true, I said,

greek_head: τοῦτο τῶν δὲ τὸ ἕτερον ἐκείνων ὁπότερον ἂν τύχῃ Οὕτως ἔφη
greek_tokens: outos o de o eteros ekeinos opoteros an tuxh outws fhmi dia outos dh kai anqrwpos legw o proteros trissos genos eimi filosofos filoneikos filokerdhs komidh ge kai hdona dh treis eidos upokeimai eis ekastos outos panu ge oistos oun eimi de egw ostis ei eqelw trew toioutos anqrwpos en meros ekastos anerwtaw tis outos o bios hdus o eautou ekastos malista egkwmiazw o te
english_head: that the ruling principle of men’s souls is in some cases this faculty and in others one of the other two, as it may happen? That is so, he said. And that is why we say that the primary classes of men also are three, the philosopher or lover of wisdom, the lover of victory and the lover of gain. Precisely so And also that there are three forms of pleasure, corresponding respectively to each? By all means. Are you aware, then said I, that if you should choose to ask men of these three classes, each in turn, which is the most pleasurable of these lives, each will chiefly commend his own? The financier

greek_head: χρηματιστικὸς πρὸς τὸ κερδαίνειν τὴν τοῦ τιμᾶσθαι ἡδονὴν ἢ τὴν τοῦ μανθάνειν οὐδενὸς ἀξίαν φήσει εἶναι εἰ μὴ εἴ τι
greek_tokens: xrhmatistikos pros o kerdainw o o timaw hdona h o o manqanw oudeis acios fhmi eimi ei mh ei tis autos argurion poiew alhqhs fhmi tis de o filotimos eimi de egw ou o men apo o xrhma hdona fortikos tis hgeomai kai au o apo o manqanw ostis mh maqhma timh ferw kapnos kai fluaria outws fhmi exis o de filosofos eimi de egw tis oiomai o allos
english_head: will affirm that in comparison with profit the pleasures of honor or of learning area of no value except in so far as they produce money. True, he said. And what of the lover of honor? I said; does he not regard the pleasure that comes from money as vulgar and low, and again that of learning, save in so far as the knowledge confers honor, mere fume and moonshine? It is so, he said. And what, said I, are we to suppose the philosopher thinks of the other pleasures

greek_head: ἡδονὰς νομίζειν πρὸς τὴν τοῦ εἰδέναι τἀληθὲς ὅπῃ ἔχει καὶ ἐν τοιούτῳ τινὶ ἀεὶ εἶναι μανθάνοντα τῆς ἡδονῆς οὐ πάνυ
greek_tokens: hdona nomizw pros o o oida alhqhs oph exis kai en toioutos tis aei eimi manqanw o hdona ou panu prosw kai kalew o eimi anagkh ws oudeis o allos deomai ei mh anagkh eimi eu fhmi deomai oida oste dh oun eipon amfisbhtew ekastos o eidos o hdona kai autos o bios mh ostis pros o kallion kai aisxros zaw mhde o xeirwn kai ameinwn alla pros autos o
english_head: compared with the delight of knowing the truth and the reality, and being always occupied with that while he learns? Will he not think them far removed from true pleasure, and call them literally the pleasures of necessity, since he would have no use for them if necessity were not laid upon him? We may be sure of that, he said. Since, then, there is contention between the several types of pleasure and the lives themselves, not merely as to which is the more honorable or the more base, or the worse or the better, but which is actually the more pleasurable or free from pain,

greek_head: ἥδιον καὶ ἀλυπότερον πῶς ἂν εἰδεῖμεν τίς αὐτῶν ἀληθέστατα λέγει
greek_tokens: hdus kai alupos pws an oida tis autos alhqhs legw ou panu fhmi egw exw eipon alla wde skopaw tis xrh krinw o mellw kalos krinw ara ou empeiria te kai fronhsis kai logos h outos exw an tis beltiwn krithrion kai pws an fhmi skopaw dh treis eimi o androw tis empeiros pas os eipon hdona poteros o filokerdhs manqanw autos o alhqeia oios eimi empeiros dokew su eimi o
english_head: how could we determine which of them speaks most truly? In faith, I cannot tell, he said. Well, consider it thus: By what are things to be judged, if they are to be judged rightly? Is it not by experience, intelligence and discussion? Or could anyone name a better criterion than these? How could he? he said. Observe, then. Of our three types of men, which has had the most experience of all the pleasures we mentioned? Do you think that the lover of gain by study of the very nature of truth has more experience

greek_head: ἀπὸ τοῦ εἰδέναι ἡδονῆς ἢ ὁ φιλόσοφος τῆς ἀπὸ τοῦ κερδαίνειν Πολύ ἔφη διαφέρει τῷ μὲν γὰρ ἀνάγκη γεύεσθαι τῶν
greek_tokens: apo o oida hdona h o filosofos o apo o kerdainw polus fhmi diaferw o men gar anagkh geuw o eteros ek pais arxw o de filokerdew oph fuw o eimi manqanw o hdona outos ws glukus eimi ou anagkh geuw oude empeiros gignomai mallon de kai proqumeomai ou radios polus ara eimi de egw diaferw o ge filokerdhs o filosofos empeiria amfoteros o hdona
english_head: of the pleasure that knowledge yields than the philosopher has of that which results from gain? There is a vast difference, he said; for the one, the philosopher, must needs taste of the other two kinds of pleasure from childhood; but the lover of gain is not only under no necessity of tasting or experiencing the sweetness of the pleasure of learning the true natures of things, but he cannot easily do so even if he desires and is eager for it. The lover of wisdom, then, said I, far surpasses the lover of gain in experience of both kinds of pleasure.

greek_head: Πολὺ μέντοι Τί δὲ τοῦ φιλοτίμου ἆρα μᾶλλον ἄπειρός ἐστι τῆς ἀπὸ
greek_tokens: polus mentoi tis de o filotimos ara mallon apeiros eimi o apo o timaw hdona h ekeinos o apo o fronew alla timh men fhmi ean ecergazomai epi o ekastos ormaw pas autos epomai kai gar o plousios upo polus timaw kai o andreios kai sofos wste apo ge o timaw oios eimi pas o hdona empeiros o de o eimi qea oios hdona exis adunatos allos geuw plhn o filosofos
english_head: Yes, far. And how does he compare with the lover of honor? Is he more unacquainted with the pleasure of being honored than that other with that which comes from knowledge? Nay, honor, he said, if they achieve their several objects, attends them all; for the rich man is honored by many and the brave man and the wise, so that all are acquainted with the kind of pleasure that honor brings; but it is impossible for anyone except the lover of wisdom to have savored the delight that the contemplation of true being and reality brings.

greek_head: Ἐμπειρίας μὲν ἄρα εἶπον ἕνεκα κάλλιστα τῶν ἀνδρῶν κρίνει οὗτος
greek_tokens: empeiria men ara eipon eneka kallista o androw krinon outos polus ge kai meis meta ge fronhsis monos empeiros gignomai eimi tis meis alla meis kai dia e ge deomai organon krinw ou o filokerdhs outos organon oude o filotimos alla o filosofos o poios dia logos pou fhmi dew krinw eimi gar nai logos de outos malista organon pws de ou oukoun ei men ploutos kai kerdos ariston krinw o
english_head: Then, said I, so far as experience goes, he is the best judge of the three. By far. And again, he is the only one whose experience will have been accompanied by intelligence. Surely. And yet again, that which is the instrument, or ὄργανον, of judgement is the instrument, not of the lover of gain or of the lover of honor, but of the lover of wisdom. What is that? It was by means of words and discussion that we said the judgement must be reached; was it not? Yes. And they are the instrument mainly of the philosopher. Of course. Now if wealth and profit were the best criteria by which things are judged,

greek_head: κρινόμενα ἃ ἐπῄνει ὁ φιλοκερδὴς καὶ ἔψεγεν ἀνάγκη ἂν ἦν ταῦτα ἀληθέστατα εἶναι
greek_tokens: krinw o epainew o filokerdhs kai yegw anagkh an eimi outos alhqhs eimi polus ge ei de timaw te kai nikh kai andreia ara ou o o filotimos te kai filoneikos dhlos epei de empeiria kai fronhsis kai logos anagkh fhmi o o filosofos te kai o filologos epainew alhqhs eimi
english_head: the things praised and censured by the lover of gain would necessarily be truest and most real. Quite necessarily. And if honor, victory and courage, would it not be the things praised by the lover of honor and victory? Obviously. But since the tests are experience and wisdom and discussion, what follows? Of necessity, he said, that the things approved by the lover of wisdom and discussion are most valid and true.

greek_head: Τριῶν ἄρ' οὐσῶν τῶν ἡδονῶν ἡ τούτου τοῦ μέρους τῆς ψυχῆς ᾧ μανθάνομεν ἡδίστη ἂν εἴη καὶ ἐν ᾧ ἡμῶν τοῦτο
greek_tokens: treis ara eimi o hdona o outos o meros o yuxazw os manqanw hdus an eaw kai en os egw outos arxw o outos bios hdus pws de ou mellw fhmi kurios goun epaineths eimi epainew o eautou bios o fronimos tis de deuteros eipon bios kai tis deuteros hdona fhmi o kriths eimi dhlos ostis o o polemikos te kai filotimos eggus gar autos eimi h o o xrhmatisths ustatos dh o o filokerdhs ws eoika tis meis eimi de os
english_head: There being, then, three kinds of pleasure, the pleasure of that part of the soul whereby we learn is the sweetest, and the life of the man in whom that part dominates is the most pleasurable. How could it be otherwise? he said. At any rate the man of intelligence speaks with authority when he commends his own life. And to what life and to what pleasure, I said, does the judge assign the second place? Obviously to that of the warrior and honor-loving type, for it is nearer to the first than is the life of the money-maker. And so the last place belongs to the lover of gain, as it seems. Surely, said he.

greek_head: Ταῦτα μὲν τοίνυν οὕτω δύ' ἐφεξῆς ἂν εἴη καὶ δὶς νενικηκὼς ὁ δίκαιος τὸν ἄδικον τὸ δὲ τρίτον ὀλυμπικῶς τῷ σωτῆρί τε
greek_tokens: outos men toinun outws duh efechs an eaw kai dis nikaw o dikaios o adikos o de tritos olumpikws o swthr te kai o olumpios zeus aqrew ostis oude panalhqhs eimi o o allos hdona plhn o o fronimos oude kaqaros alla skiagrafew tis ws egw dokazw egw o sofos tis akouw kaitoi outos an eaw megas te kai kurios o ptwma polus ge alla pws legw
english_head: That, then, would be two points in succession and two victories for the just man over the unjust. And now for the third in the Olympian fashion to the saviour and to Olympian Zeus—observe that other pleasure than that of the intelligence is not altogether even real or pure, but is a kind of scene-painting, as I seem to have heard from some wise man; and yet this would be the greatest and most decisive overthrow. Much the greatest. But what do you mean? I shall discover it, I said,

greek_head: Ὧδ' εἶπον ἐξευρήσω σοῦ ἀποκρινομένου ζητῶν ἅμα Ἐρώτα δή ἔφη
greek_tokens: wde eipon eceuriskw sew apokrinw zhtew ama erwtaw dh fhmi legos dh eimi de egw ou enantion fhmi luph hdona kai mala oukoun kai o mhte xairw mhte lupew eimi tis eimi mentoi metacu outos amfw en mesos eimi hsuxia tis peri outos o yuxazw h ou outws autos legw outws eimi de os ara oun mnhmoneuw eimi de egw o o kamnw logos os legw otan kamnw poios ws oudeis ara eimi hdus o ugiainw alla sfazw
english_head: if you will answer my questions while I seek. Ask, then, he said. Tell me, then, said I, do we not say that pain is the opposite of pleasure? We certainly do. And is there not such a thing as a neutral state There is. Is it not intermediate between them, and in the mean, being a kind of quietude of the soul in these respects? Or is not that your notion of it? It is that, said he. Do you not recall the things men say in sickness? What sort of things? Why, that after all there is nothing sweeter than to be well,

greek_head: ἐλελήθει πρὶν κάμνειν ἥδιστον ὄν Μέμνημαι ἔφη
greek_tokens: lanqanw prin kamnw hdus eimi mimnhskw fhmi oukoun kai o periwdunia tis exw akouw legw ws oudeis hdus o pauw odunaw akouw kai en allos ge oimh polus toioutos aisqanomai gignomai o anqrwpos en os otan lupew o mh lupew kai o hsuxia o toioutos egkwmiazw ws hdus ou o xairw outos gar fhmi tote hdus isos kai agaphtos gignomai hsuxia
english_head: though they were not aware that it is the highest pleasure before they were Ill. I remember, he said. And do you not hear men afflicted with severe pain saying that there is no greater pleasure than the cessation of this suffering? I do. And you perceive, I presume, many similar conditions in which men while suffering pain praise freedom from pain and relief from that as the highest pleasure, and not positive delight. Yes, he said, for this in such cases is perhaps what is felt as pleasurable and acceptable—peace.

greek_head: Καὶ ὅταν παύσηται ἄρα εἶπον χαίρων τις ἡ τῆς ἡδονῆς ἡσυχία λυπηρὸν ἔσται
greek_tokens: kai otan pauw ara eipon xairw tis o o hdona hsuxia luphros eimi isos fhmi o metacu ara nundi amfoteros fhmi eimi o hsuxia outos pote amfoteros eimi luph te kai hdona eoika eimi kai dunatos o mhdeteros eimi amfoteros gignomai ou egw dokew kai meis o ge hdus en yuxw gignomai kai o luphros kinhsis tis amfoteros eimi h ou nai
english_head: And so, I said, when a man’s delight comes to an end, the cessation of pleasure will be painful. It may be so, he said. What, then,we just now described as the intermediate state between the two—this quietude—will sometimes be both pain and pleasure. It seems so Is it really possible for that which is neither to become both? I think not. And further, both pleasure and pain arising in the soul are a kind of motion, are they not?

greek_head: Τὸ δὲ μήτε λυπηρὸν μήτε ἡδὺ οὐχὶ ἡσυχία μέντοι καὶ ἐν μέσῳ τούτοιν ἐφάνη ἄρτι
greek_tokens: o de mhte luphros mhte hdus ou hsuxia mentoi kai en mesos outos fainw arti fainw gar pws oun orqos eimi o mh algew hdus hgeomai h o mh xairw aniaros oudamos ou eimi ara outos alla fainw eimi de egw para o algeinos hdus kai para o hdus algeinos tote o hsuxia kai oudeis ugihs outos o fantasma pros hdona alhqeia alla gohteia tis ws goun o logos fhmi shmainw
english_head: Yes. And did we not just now see that to feel neither pain nor pleasure is a quietude of the soul and an intermediate state between the two? Yes, we did. How, then, can it be right to think the absence of pain pleasure, or the absence of joy painful? In no way. This is not a reality, then, but an illusion, said I; in such case the quietude in juxtaposition with the pain appears pleasure, and in juxtaposition with the pleasure pain. And these illusions have no real bearing on the truth of pleasure, but are a kind of jugglery. So at any rate our argument signifies, he said. Take a look, then,

greek_head: Ἰδὲ τοίνυν ἔφην ἐγώ ἡδονάς αἳ οὐκ ἐκ λυπῶν εἰσίν ἵνα μὴ πολλάκις οἰηθῇς ἐν τῷ παρόντι οὕτω τοῦτο πεφυκέναι
greek_tokens: ide toinun fhmi egw hdona o ou ek luph eimi ina mh pollakis oiomai en o pareimi outws outos fuw hdona men paula luph eimi luph de hdona pou dh fhmi kai poa legw polus men eipon kai allos malista de ei eqelw ennoew o peri o osmas hdona outos gar ou prolupeomai ecaifnhs amhxanos o megeqos gignomai pauw te luph oudeis katalimpanw alhqhs fhmi
english_head: said I, at pleasures which do not follow on pain, so that you may not haply suppose for the present that it is the nature of pleasure to be a cessation from pain and pain from pleasure. Where shall I look, he said, and what pleasures do you mean? There are many others, I said, and especially, if you please to note them, the pleasures connected with smell. For these with no antecedent pain suddenly attain an indescribable intensity, and their cessation leaves no pain after them. Most true, he said. Let us not believe, then,

greek_head: Μὴ ἄρα πειθώμεθα καθαρὰν ἡδονὴν εἶναι τὴν λύπης ἀπαλλαγήν μηδὲ λύπην τὴν ἡδονῆς
greek_tokens: mh ara peiqw kaqaros hdona eimi o luph apallagh mhde luph o hdona mh gar alla mentoi eipon o ge dia o swma epi o yuxh teinw kai legw hdona sxedon o pleistos te kai megas outos o eidos eimi luph tis apallagh eimi gar oukoun kai o pro mellw outos ek prosdokia gignomai prohsqhsis te kai proluphsis kata tautos exw kata tautos
english_head: that the riddance of pain is pure pleasure or that of pleasure pain. No, we must not. Yet, surely, said I, the affections that find their way through the body to the soul and are called pleasures are, we may say, the most and the greatest of them, of this type, in some sort releases from pain.? Yes, they are. And is not this also the character of the anticipatory pleasures and pains that precede them and arise from the expectation of them? It is.

greek_head: Οἶσθ' οὖν ἦν δ' ἐγώ οἷαί εἰσιν καὶ ᾧ μάλιστα ἐοίκασιν Τῷ ἔφη
greek_tokens: oistos oun eimi de egw oios eimi kai os malista eoika o fhmi nomizw tis eipon en o fusis eimi o men anoos o de katos o de mesos egw oiomai oun an tis ek o katos ferw pros mesos allos tis oiomai h anoos ferw kai en mesos isthmi aforaw oqen ferw alloqi pou an hgeomai eimi h en o anoos mh oraw o alhqhs anoos ma diw ou egw fhmi allos oimh oiomai an o toioutos
english_head: Do you know, then, what their quality is and what they most resemble? What? he said. Do you think that there is such a thing in nature as up and down and in the middle? I do. Do you suppose, then, that anyone who is transported from below to the center would have any other opinion than that he was moving upward? And if he took his stand at the center and looked in the direction from which he had been transported, do you think he would suppose himself to be anywhere but above, never having seen that which is really above? No, by Zeus, he said, I do not think that such a person would have any other notion.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' εἰ πάλιν γ' ἔφην φέροιτο κάτω τ' ἂν οἴοιτο φέρεσθαι καὶ ἀληθῆ οἴοιτο
greek_tokens: alla ei palin ge fhmi ferw katos su an oiomai ferw kai alhqhs oiomai pws gar ou oukoun outos pasxw an pas dia o mh empeiros eimi o alhqinos anoos te eimi kai en mesos kai katos dhlos dh qaumazw an oun ei kai e apeiros alhqeia peri polus te allos mh ugihs doca exw pros te hdona kai luph kai o metacu outos outws diakeimai wste otan men epi o
english_head: And if he were borne back, I said, he would both think himself to be moving downward and would think truly. Of course. And would not all this happen to him because of his non-acquaintance with the true and real up and down and middle? Obviously. Would it surprise you, then, said I, if similarly men without experience of truth and reality hold unsound opinions about many other matters, and are so disposed towards pleasure and pain and the intermediate neutral condition that, when they are moved in the direction of the painful,

greek_head: λυπηρὸν φέρωνται ἀληθῆ τε οἴονται καὶ τῷ ὄντι λυποῦνται ὅταν δὲ ἀπὸ λύπης ἐπὶ τὸ μεταξύ σφόδρα μὲν οἴονται
greek_tokens: luphros ferw alhqhs te oiomai kai o eimi lupew otan de apo luph epi o metacu sfodra men oiomai pros plhrwsis te kai hdona gignomai wsper pros melas faios aposkopew apeiria leukon kai pros o alupon outws luph aforaw apeiria hdona apataw ma dios eimi de os ou an qaumazw alla polus mallon ei mh outws exis wde ge oun eipon ennoew ou peina kai diya kai o
english_head: they truly think themselves to be, and really are, in a state of pain, but, when they move from pain to the middle and neutral state, they intensely believe that they are approaching fulfillment and pleasure, and just as if, in ignorance of white, they were comparing grey with black, so, being inexperienced in true pleasure, they are deceived by viewing painlessness in its relation to pain? No, by Zeus, he said, it would not surprise me, but far rather if it were not so. In this way, then, consider it. Are not hunger and thirst and similar states inanitions or emptinesses

greek_head: τοιαῦτα κενώσεις τινές εἰσιν τῆς περὶ τὸ σῶμα ἕξεως Τί μήν
greek_tokens: toioutos kenwsis tis eimi o peri o swma ecis tis meis agnoia de kai afrosunh ara ou kenoths eimi o peri yuxh au ecis mala ge oukoun plhrow an o te trefw metalambanw kai o new isxw pws de ou plhrwsis de alhqhs o hsswn h o mallon eimi dhlos ostis o mallon poteros oun hgeomai o genos mallon kaqaros ousia metexw o oios sitos te kai potaomai kai oyon kai sumpas trefw h o doca te alhqhs eidos kai episthmh kai neomai kai
english_head: of the bodily habit? Surely. And is not ignorance and folly in turn a kind of emptiness of the habit of the soul? It is indeed. And he who partakes of nourishment and he who gets, wisdom fills the void and is filled? Of course. And which is the truer filling and fulfillment, that of the less or of the more real being? Evidently that of the more real. And which of the two groups or kinds do you think has a greater part in pure essence, the class of foods, drinks, and relishes and nourishment generally, or the kind of true opinion,

greek_head: συλλήβδην αὖ πάσης ἀρετῆς ὧδε δὲ κρῖνε τὸ τοῦ ἀεὶ ὁμοίου ἐχόμενον καὶ ἀθανάτου καὶ ἀληθείας καὶ αὐτὸ τοιοῦτον
greek_tokens: sullhbdhn au pas aretaw wde de krinw o o aei omoios exw kai aqanatos kai alhqeia kai autos toioutos eimi kai en toioutos gignomai mallon eimi su dokew h o mhdepote omoios kai qnhtos kai autos toioutos kai en toioutos gignomai polus fhmi diaferw o o aei omoios o oun aei omoios ausia ousia tis mallon h episthmh metexw oudamos tis de alhqeia oude outos ei de alhqeia hsswn ou kai ousia anagkh
english_head: knowledge and reason, and, in sum, all the things that are more excellent? Form your judgement thus. Which do you think more truly is, that which clings to what is ever like itself and immortal and to the truth, and that which is itself of such a nature and is born in a thing of that nature, or that which clings to what is mortal and never the same and is itself such and is born in such a thing? That which cleaves to what is ever the same far surpasses, he said. Does the essence of that which never abides the same partake of real essence any more than of knowledge? By no means. Or of truth and reality? Not of that, either. And if a thing has less of truth has it not also less of real essence or existence? Necessarily. And is it not generally true

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ὅλως τὰ περὶ τὴν τοῦ σώματος θεραπείαν γένη γῶν γενῶν αὖ τῶν περὶ τὴν τῆς ψυχῆς θεραπείαν ἧττον
greek_tokens: oukoun oloc o peri o o swma qerapeia genos goun genos au o peri o o yuxazw qerapeia hsswn alhqeia te kai ousia metexw polus ge swma de autos yuxazw ou oiomai outws egw oukoun o o mallon eimi plhrow kai autos mallon eimi ontws mallon plhrow h o o hsswn eimi kai autos hsswn eimi pws gar ou ei ara o plhrow o fusis proshkw hdus eimi o o eimi kai o eimi plhrow mallon mallon
english_head: that the kinds concerned with the service of the body partake less of truth and reality than those that serve the soul? Much less. And do you not think that the same holds of the body itself in comparison with the soul? I do. Then is not that which is fulfilled of what more truly is, and which itself more truly is, more truly filled and satisfied than that which being itself less real is filled with more unreal things? Of course. If, then, to be filled with what befits nature is pleasure, then that which is more really filled with real things

greek_head: ὄντως τε καὶ ἀληθεστέρως χαίρειν ἂν ποιοῖ ἡδονῇ ἀληθεῖ τὸ δὲ τῶν ἧττον ὄντων μεταλαμβάνον ἧττόν τε ἂν ἀληθῶς
greek_tokens: ontws te kai alhqhs xairw an poiew hdona alhqhs o de o hsswn eimi metalambanw hsswn te an alhqhs kai bebaios plhrow kai apistos an hdona kai hsswn alhqhs metalambanw anagkaios fhmi
english_head: would more really and truly cause us to enjoy a true pleasure, while that which partakes of the less truly existent would be less truly and surely filled and would partake of a less trustworthy and less true pleasure. Most inevitably, he said. Then those who have no experience

greek_head: Οἱ ἄρα φρονήσεως καὶ ἀρετῆς ἄπειροι εὐωχίαις δὲ καὶ τοῖς τοιούτοις ἀεὶ συνόντες κάτω ὡς ἔοικεν καὶ μέχρι πάλιν
greek_tokens: e ara fronhsis kai aretaw apeiros euwxia de kai o toioutos aei suneimi katos ws eoika kai mexri palin pros o metacu ferw te kai outos planaw dia bios uperbainw de outos pros o alhqhs anoos oute anablepw pwpote oute ferw oude o eimi o eimi plhrow oude bebaios te kai kaqaros hdona geuw alla boskhma dikh katos aei blepw kai kuptw eis gaia kai eis trapeza boskw xortazw kai oxeuw
english_head: of wisdom and virtue but are ever devoted to feastings and that sort of thing are swept downward, it seems, and back again to the center, and so sway and roam to and fro throughout their lives, but they have never transcended all this and turned their eyes to the true upper region nor been wafted there, nor ever been really filled with real things, nor ever tasted stable and pure pleasure, but with eyes ever bent upon the earth and heads bowed down over their tables they feast like cattle,

greek_head: καὶ ἕνεκα τῆς τούτων πλεονεξίας λακτίζοντες καὶ κυρίττοντες ἀλλήλους σιδηροῖς κέρασί τε καὶ ὁπλαῖς ἀποκτεινύασι δι'
greek_tokens: kai eneka o outos pleonecia laktizw kai kurissw allhlwn sidhreos keras te kai oplh apokteinw dia aplhstia ate ou o eimi oude o eimi oude o stegw eautou pimplhmi pantelhs fhmi o glauc o o polus eimi swkraths xrhsmwdew bios ara oun ou anagkh kai hdona suneimi mignumi luph eidwlon o alhqhs hdona kai skiagrafew
english_head: grazing and copulating, ever greedy for more of these delights; and in their greed kicking and butting one another with horns and hooves of iron they slay one another in sateless avidity, because they are vainly striving to satisfy with things that are not real the unreal and incontinent part of their souls. You describe in quite oracular style, Socrates, said Glaucon, the life of the multitude. And are not the pleasures with which they dwell inevitably commingled with pains, phantoms of true pleasure, illusions of scene-painting, so colored by contrary juxtaposition

greek_head: ὑπὸ τῆς παρ' ἀλλήλας θέσεως ἀποχραινομέναις ὥστε σφοδροὺς ἑκατέρας φαίνεσθαι καὶ ἔρωτας ἑαυτῶν λυττῶντας
greek_tokens: upo o para allhlwn qesis apoxrainw wste sfodros ekateros fainw kai erws eautou lussaw o afrwn entiktw kai perimaxhtos eimi wsper o o elenh eidwlon upo o en troia sthsixoros fhmi gignomai perimaxhtos agnoia o alhqhs polus anagkh fhmi toioutos tis autos eimi tis de peri o qumoeidhs ou eteros toioutos anagkh gignomai os an autos outos diaprassw h fqonos dia filotimia h bia dia filoneikia h qumos dia duskolia
english_head: as to seem intense in either kind, and to beget mad loves of themselves in senseless souls, and to be fought for, as Stesichorus says the wraith of Helen was fought for at Troy through ignorance of the truth? It is quite inevitable, he said, that it should be so. So, again, must not the like hold of the high-spirited element, whenever a man succeeds in satisfying that part of his nature—his covetousness of honor by envy, his love of victory by violence, his ill-temper by indulgence in anger,

greek_head: πλησμονὴν τιμῆς τε καὶ νίκης καὶ θυμοῦ διώκων ἄνευ λογισμοῦ τε καὶ νοῦ
greek_tokens: plhsmonh timaw te kai nikh kai qumos diwkw aneu logismos te kai neomai toioutos eimi de os anagkh kai peri outos eimi tis oun eimi de egw qarsew legw ostis kai peri o filokerdhs kai o filoneikos osos epiqumia eimi o men an o episthmh kai logos epomai kai meta outos o hdona diwkw os an o fronimos echgeomai lambanw o alhqhs te lambanw ws oios te autos alhqhs
english_head: pursuing these ends without regard to consideration and reason? The same sort of thing, he said, must necessarily happen in this case too. Then, said I, may we not confidently declare that in both the gain-loving and the contentious part of our nature all the desires that wait upon knowledge and reason, and, pursuing their pleasures in conjunction with them, take only those pleasures which reason approves, will, since they follow truth, enjoy the truest pleasures, so far as that is possible for them, and also the pleasures that are proper to them and their own,

greek_head: λαβεῖν ἅτε ἀληθείᾳ ἑπομένων καὶ τὰς ἑαυτῶν οἰκείας εἴπερ τὸ βέλτιστον ἑκάστῳ τοῦτο καὶ οἰκειότατον
greek_tokens: lambanw ate alhqeia epomai kai o eautou oikeios eiper o beltistos ekastos outos kai oikeios alla meis fhmi oikeios ge o filosofos ara epomai apas o yuxazw kai mh stasiazw ekastos o meros uparxw eis te allos o eautou prassw kai dikaios eimi kai dh kai o hdona o eautou ekastos kai o beltistos kai eis o dunatos o
english_head: if for everything that which is best may be said to be most its own? But indeed, he said, it is most truly its very own. Then when the entire soul accepts the guidance of the wisdom-loving part and is not filled with inner dissension, the result for each part is that it in all other respects keeps to its own task and is just, and likewise that each enjoys its own proper pleasures and the best pleasures and,

greek_head: ἀληθεστάτας καρποῦσθαι Κομιδῇ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: alhqhs karpow komidh men oun otan de ara o eteros tis krathsis uparxw autos mhte o eautou hdona eceuriskw o te allos anagkazw allotrios kai mh alhqhs hdona diwkw outws fhmi oukoun o pleistos filosofia te kai logos afisthmi malista an toioutos ecergazomai polus ge pleistos de logos afisthmi ou osper nomos te kai tacis dhlos dh fainw de pleistos afisthmi ou o erwtikos te
english_head: so far as such a thing is possible, the truest. Precisely so. And so when one of the other two gets the mastery the result for it is that it does not find its own proper pleasure and constrains the others to pursue an alien pleasure and not the true. That is so, he said. And would not that which is furthest removed from philosophy and reason be most likely to produce this effect? Quite so, he said. And is not that furthest removed from reason which is furthest from law and order? Obviously. And was it not made plain that the furthest removed are the erotic and tyrannical appetites? Quite so.

greek_head: καὶ τυραννικαὶ ἐπιθυμίαι Πολύ γε
greek_tokens: kai turannikos epiqumia polus ge elaxistos de o basilikos te kai kosmios nai pleistos dh oimh alhqhs hdona kai oikeios o turannos afisthmi o de oligistos anagkh kai ahdhs ara eipon o turannos biow o de basileus hdus polus anagkh oistos oun eimi de egw osos ahdhs zaw turannos basileus an eipon fhmi treis hdona ws eoika eimi eis men gnhsios duo
english_head: And least so the royal and orderly? Yes. Then the tyrant’s place, I think, will be fixed at the furthest remove from true and proper pleasure, and the king’s at the least. Necessarily. Then the tyrant’s life will be least pleasurable and the king’s most. There is every necessity of that. Do you know, then, said I, how much less pleasurably the tyrant lives than the king? I’ll know if you tell me, he said. There being as it appears three pleasures, one genuine and two spurious,

greek_head: δὲ νόθαιν τῶν νόθων εἰς τὸ ἐπέκεινα ὑπερβὰς ὁ τύραννος φυγὼν νόμον τε καὶ λόγον δούλαις τισὶ δορυφόροις ἡδοναῖς
greek_tokens: de noqos o noqos eis o epekeina uperbainw o turannos feugw nomos te kai logos doulh tis doruforos hdona sunoikew kai oposos elassow oude panu radios eipon plhn isos wde pws fhmi apo o oligarxikos tritos pou o turannos afisthmi en mesos gar autos o dhmotikos eimi nai oukoun kai hdona tritos eidwlon pros alhqeia apo ekeinos sunoikew an ei o prosqen alhqhs outws o de ge oligarxikos apo o basilikos au tritos ean
english_head: the tyrant in his flight from law and reason crosses the border beyond the spurious, cohabits with certain slavish, mercenary pleasures, and the measure of his inferiority is not easy to express except perhaps thus. How? he said. The tyrant, I believe, we found at the third remove from the oligarch, for the democrat came between. Yes. And would he not also dwell with a phantom of pleasure in respect of reality three stages removed from that other, if all that we have said is true? That is so. And the oligarch in turn is at the third remove from the royal man

greek_head: εἰς ταὐτὸν ἀριστοκρατικὸν καὶ βασιλικὸν τιθῶμεν Τρίτος γάρ
greek_tokens: eis tautos aristokratikos kai basilikos tiqhmi tritos gar triplasios ara eimi de egw triplasios ariqmos alhqhs hdona afisthmi turannos fainw epipedos ara fhmi ws eoika o eidwlon kata o o mhkos ariqmos hdona turannikos an eaw komidh ge kata de dunamis kai tritos auch dhlos dh apostasis osos afisthmi gignomai dhlos fhmi o ge logistikos oukoun ean tis metastrefw alhqeia hdona o basileus
english_head: if we assume the identity of the aristocrat and the king. Yes, the third. Three times three, then, by numerical measure is the interval that separates the tyrant from true pleasure. Apparently. The phantom of the tyrant’s pleasure is then by longitudinal mensuration a plane number. Quite so. But by squaring and cubing it is clear what the interval of this separation becomes. It is clear, he said, to a reckoner. Then taking it the other way about,

greek_head: τοῦ τυράννου ἀφεστηκότα λέγῃ ὅσον ἀφέστηκεν ἐννεακαιεικοσικαιεπτακοσιοπλασιάκις ἥδιον αὐτὸν ζῶντα εὑρήσει
greek_tokens: o turannos afisthmi legos osos afisthmi enneakaieikosikaieptakosioplasiakis hdus autos zaw eurhsis teleiow o pollaplasiwsis o de turannos aniaros o autos outos afisthmi amhxanos fhmi logismos kataforew o diaforoths
english_head: if one tries to express the extent of the interval between the king and the tyrant in respect of true pleasure he will find on completion of the multiplication that he lives 729 times as happily and that the tyrant’s life is more painful by the same distance. An overwhelming and baffling calculation, he said, of the difference between the just and

greek_head: τοῖν ἀνδροῖν τοῦ τε δικαίου καὶ τοῦ ἀδίκου πρὸς ἡδονήν τε καὶ λύπην
greek_tokens: o anhr o te dikaios kai o adikos pros hdona te kai luph kai mentoi kai alhqhs kai proshkw ge eimi de egw bios ariqmos eiper autos proshkw hmeros kai nuc kai meis kai eniautos alla meis fhmi proshkw oukoun ei tosoutos hdona nikaw o agaqos te kai dikaios o kakos te kai adikos amhxanos dh osos pleiwn nikaw eusxhmosunh te bios kai kallos kai aretaw amhxanos mentoi nh dios fhmi
english_head: the unjust man in respect of pleasure and pain! And what is more, it is a true number and pertinent to the lives of men if days and nights and months and years pertain to them. They certainly do, he said. Then if in point of pleasure the victory of the good and just man over the bad and unjust is so great as this, he will surpass him inconceivably in decency and beauty of life and virtue. Inconceivably indeed, by Zeus, he said. Very good, said I. And now that we have come to this point in the argument,

greek_head: Εἶεν δή εἶπον ἐπειδὴ ἐνταῦθα λόγου γεγόναμεν ἀναλάβωμεν τὰ πρῶτα λεχθέντα δι' ἃ δεῦρ' ἥκομεν ἦν δέ που
greek_tokens: eien dh eipon epei entauqa logos gignomai analambanw o proteros legw dia o deuro hkw eimi de pou legw lusitelew adikew o teleios men adikos docazw de dikaios h ou outws legw outws men oun nun dh fhmi autos dialegw epei diomologew o te adikew kai o dikaios prassw os ekateros exis dunamis pws fhmi eikwn plassw o yuxazw logos ina oida o ekeinos legos oios legw
english_head: let us take up again the statement with which we began and that has brought us to this pass. It was, I believe, averred that injustice is profitable to the completely unjust man who is reputed just. Was not that the proposition? Yes, that. Let us, then, reason with its proponent now that we have agreed on the essential nature of injustice and just conduct. How? he said. By fashioning in our discourse a symbolic image of the soul, that the maintainer of that proposition may see precisely what it is that he was saying.

greek_head: Ποίαν τινά ἦ δ' ὅς Τῶν τοιούτων τινά ἦν δ' ἐγώ οἷαι μυθολογοῦνται παλαιαὶ
greek_tokens: poa tis eimi de os o toioutos tis eimi de egw oios muqologew palaios gignomai fusis o te ximaira kai o skulla kai kerberos kai allos tis suxnos legw sumfuw idea polus eis eis gignomai legw gar fhmi plassw toinun eis men idea qhrion poikilos kai polukefalos hmeros de qhraw exw kefalh kuklos kai agrios kai dunatew metaballw kai fuw ek eautou pas outos
english_head: What sort of an image? he said. One of those natures that the ancient fables tell of, said I, as that of the Chimaera or Scylla or Cerberus, and the numerous other examples that are told of many forms grown together in one. Yes, they do tell of them. Mould, then, a single shape of a manifold and many-headed beast that has a ring of heads of tame and wild beasts and can change them and cause to spring forth from itself all such growths.

greek_head: Δεινοῦ πλάστου ἔφη τὸ ἔργον ὅμως δέ ἐπειδὴ εὐπλαστότερον κηροῦ καὶ τῶν τοιούτων λόγος πεπλάσθω
greek_tokens: deinos plasths fhmi o ergnumi omws de epei euplastos khroomai kai o toioutos logos plassw eis dh toinun allos idea lewn eis de anqrwpos polus de megas eimi o proteros kai deuteros o deuteros outos fhmi raios kai plassw sunaptw toinun autos eis eis treis eimi wste ph sumfuw allhlwn sunaptw fhmi periplassw dh autos ecwqen eis eikwn o o anqrwpos wste o mh dunamai o entos oraw alla o
english_head: It is the task of a cunning artist, he said, but nevertheless, since speech is more plastic than wax and other such media, assume that it has been so fashioned. Then fashion one other form of a lion and one of a man and let the first be far the largest and the second second in size. That is easier, he said, and is done. Join the three in one, then, so as in some sort to grow together. They are so united, he said. Then mould about them outside the likeness of one, that of the man, so that to anyone who is unable

greek_head: ἔξω μόνον ἔλυτρον ὁρῶντι ἓν ζῷον φαίνεσθαι ἄνθρωπον Περιπέπλασται ἔφη
greek_tokens: ecw monos elutron oraw eis zwion fainw anqrwpos periplassw fhmi legw dh o legw ws lusitelew outos adikew o anqrwpos dikaios de prassw ou sumferw ostis oudeis allos fhmi h lusitelew autos o pantodapos qhraw euwxew poiew isxuros kai o lewn kai o peri o lewn o
english_head: to look within but who can see only the external sheath it appears to be one living creature, the man. The sheath is made fast about him, he said. Let us, then say to the speaker who avers that it pays this man to be unjust, and that to do justice is not for his advantage, that he is affirming nothing else than that it profits him to feast and make strong the multifarious beast and the lion and all that pertains to the lion,

greek_head: δὲ ἄνθρωπον λιμοκτονεῖν καὶ ποιεῖν ἀσθενῆ ὥστε ἕλκεσθαι ὅπῃ ἂν ἐκείνων ὁπότερον ἄγῃ καὶ μηδὲν ἕτερον ἑτέρῳ
greek_tokens: de anqrwpos limoktonew kai poiew asqenhs wste elkw oph an ekeinos opoteros agh kai mhdeis eteros eteros suneqizw mhde filos poiew alla eaw autos en eautou daknw te kai maxomai esqiw allhlwn pantapasi gar fhmi outos an legos o o adikew epainew oukoun au o o dikaios legos lusitelew fhmi an dew outos prassw kai outos legw oqen o anqrwpos o entos
english_head: but to starve the man and so enfeeble him that he can be pulled about whithersoever either of the others drag him, and not to familiarize or reconcile with one another the two creatures but suffer them to bite and fight and devour one another. Yes, he said, that is precisely what the panegyrist of injustice will be found to say. And on the other hand he who says that justice is the more profitable affirms that all our actions and words should tend to give the man within us

greek_head: ἄνθρωπος ἔσται ἐγκρατέστατος καὶ τοῦ πολυκεφάλου θρέμματος ἐπιμελήσεται ὥσπερ γεωργός τὰ μὲν ἥμερα τρέφων
greek_tokens: anqrwpos eimi egkraths kai o polukefalos qremma epimeleomai wsper gewrgos o men hmeros trefw kai tiqaseuw o de agrios apokwluw fuw summaxos poiew o o lewn fus kai koinh pas khdw filos poiew allhlwn te kai eautou outws qreyis komidh gar au legw outos o o dikaios epainew kata pas tropos dh o men o dikaios egkwmiazw alhqhs
