english_head: complete domination over the entire man and make him take charge of the many-headed beast—like a farmer who cherishes and trains the cultivated plants but checks the growth of the wild—and he will make an ally of the lion’s nature, and caring for all the beasts alike will first make them friendly to one another and to himself, and so foster their growth. Yes, that in turn is precisely the meaning of the man who commends justice. From every point of view, then, the panegyrist of justice

greek_head: ἂν λέγοι ὁ δὲ τὰ ἄδικα ψεύδοιτο πρός τε γὰρ ἡδονὴν καὶ πρὸς εὐδοξίαν καὶ ὠφελίαν σκοπουμένῳ ὁ μὲν ἐπαινέτης
greek_tokens: an legos o de o adikos yeudw pros te gar hdona kai pros eudocia kai wfeleia skopaw o men epaineths o dikaios alhqeuw o de yekths oudeis ugihs oude eidoi yegw ostis yegw ou egw dokew eimi de os oudamh ge peiqw toinun autos praos ou gar ekwn amartanw erwtaw eimi makarios ou kai o kalos kai aisxros nomimos dia o toioutos an fhmi gignomai o men kalos o upo
english_head: speaks truly and the panegyrist of injustice falsely. For whether we consider pleasure, reputation, or profit, he who commends justice speaks the truth, while there is no soundness or real knowledge of what he censures in him who disparages it. None whatever, I think, said he. Shall we, then, try to persuade him gently, for he does not willingly err, by questioning him thus: Dear friend, should we not also say that the things which law and custom deem fair or foul have been accounted so for a like reason—

greek_head: τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ μᾶλλον δὲ ἴσως τὰ ὑπὸ τῷ θείῳ τὰ θηριώδη ποιοῦντα τῆς φύσεως αἰσχρὰ δὲ τὰ ὑπὸ τῷ ἀγρίῳ τὸ ἥμερον
greek_tokens: o anqrwpos mallon de isos o upo o qeios o qhriwdhs poiew o fusis aisxros de o upo o agrios o hmeros doulow sumfhmi h pws ean egw fhmi peiqw eimi oun eipon ostis lusitelew ek outos o logos xrusion lambanw adikos eiper toiosde tis gignomai lambanw o xrusion ama katadoulow o beltistos eautou
english_head: the fair and honorable things being those that subject the brutish part of our nature to that which is human in us, or rather, it may be, to that which is divine, while the foul and base are the things that enslave the gentle nature to the wild? Will he assent or not? He will if he is counselled by me. Can it profit any man in the light of this thought to accept gold unjustly if the result is to be that by the acceptance he enslaves the best part of himself to the worst?

greek_head: τῷ μοχθηροτάτῳ ἢ εἰ μὲν λαβὼν χρυσίον ὑὸν ἢ θυγατέρα ἐδουλοῦτο καὶ ταῦτ' εἰς ἀγρίων τε καὶ κακῶν ἀνδρῶν οὐκ
greek_tokens: o moxqhros h ei men lambanw xrusion uios h qugathr doulow kai outos eis agrios te kai kakh androw ou an autos lusitelew oude an pampolus epi outos lambanw ei de o eautou qeios upo o aqeos te kai miaros doulow kai mhdeis eleaw ou ara aqlios eimi kai polus
english_head: Or is it conceivable that, while, if the taking of the gold enslaved his son or daughter and that too to fierce and evil men, it would not profit him, no matter how large the sum, yet that, if the result is to be the ruthless enslavement of the divinest part of himself to the most despicable and godless part, he is not to be deemed wretched

greek_head: ἐπὶ δεινοτέρῳ ὀλέθρῳ χρυσὸν δωροδοκεῖ ἢ Ἐριφύλη ἐπὶ τῇ τοῦ ἀνδρὸς ψυχῇ τὸν ὅρμον δεξαμένη
greek_tokens: epi deinos oleqros xrusos dwrodokew h erifulh epi o o anhr yuxw o ormos dexomai polus mentoi eimi de os o glauc egw gar su uper ekeinos apokrinw oukoun kai o akolastainw oiomai dia toioutos pala yegw ostis anihmi en o toioutos o deinos o megas ekeinos kai polueidhs qremma pera o deon dhlos fhmi o de auqadeia kai duskolia yegw ou otan o leontwdhs
english_head: and is not taking the golden bribe much more disastrously than Eriphyle did when she received the necklace as the price of her husband’s life? Far more, said Glaucon, for I will answer you in his behalf. And do you not think that the reason for the old objection to licentiousness is similarly because that sort of thing emancipates that dread, that huge and manifold beast overmuch? Obviously, he said. And do we not censure self-will

greek_head: τε καὶ ὀφεῶδες αὔξηται καὶ συντείνηται ἀναρμόστως Πάνυ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: te kai ofewdhs aucanw kai sunteinw anarmostos panu men oun trufh de kai malqakia ou epi o autos outos xalasis te kai anesis yegw otan en autos deilia empoiew tis meis kolakeia de kai aneleuqeria ou otan tis o autos outos o qumoeidhs upo o oxlwdhs qhrion poiew kai eneka xrhma kai o ekeinos aplhstia prophlakizw eqizw ek neos anti lewn piqhkos gignomai
english_head: and irascibility when they foster and intensify disproportionately the element of the lion and the snake in us? By all means. And do we not reprobate luxury and effeminacy for their loosening and relaxation of this same element when they engender cowardice in it? Surely. And flattery and illiberality when they reduce this same high-spirited element under the rule of the mob-like beast and habituate it for the sake of wealth and the unbridled lusts of the beast to endure all manner of contumely from youth up and become an ape instead of a lion?

greek_head: Καὶ μάλα ἔφη Βαναυσία δὲ καὶ χειροτεχνία διὰ τί οἴει ὄνειδος φέρει
greek_tokens: kai mala fhmi banausia de kai xeirotexnia dia tis oiomai oneidos ferw h dia allos tis fhmi h otan tis asqenhs fusis exis o o beltistos eidos wste mh an dunamai arxw o en eautou qremma alla qerapeuw ekeinos kai o qwpeuma autos monos dunamai manqanw eoika fhmi oukoun ina kai o toioutos upo omoios arxw oios o beltistos doulos autos fhmi dew eimi ekeinos o beltistos
english_head: Yes, indeed, he said. And why do you suppose that base mechanic handicraft is a term of reproach? Shall we not say that it is solely when the best part is naturally weak in a man so that it cannot govern and control the brood of beasts within him but can only serve them and can learn nothing but the ways of flattering them? So it seems, he said. Then is it not in order that such an one may have a like government with the best man that we say he ought to be the slave

greek_head: καὶ ἔχοντος ἐν αὑτῷ τὸ θεῖον ἄρχον οὐκ ἐπὶ βλάβῃ τῇ τοῦ δούλου οἰόμενοι δεῖν ἄρχεσθαι αὐτόν ὥσπερ Θρασύμαχος
greek_tokens: kai exw en eautou o qew arxw ou epi blabh o o doulos oiomai dew arxw autos wsper qrasumaxos oiomai o arxw alla ws ameinwn eimi pas upo qeios kai fronimos arxw malista men oikew exw en eautou ei de mh ecwqen efisthmi ina eis dunamis pas omoios eimi kai filos o autos kubernaw kai orqos ge fhmi
english_head: of that best man who has within himself the divine governing principle, not because we suppose, as Thrasymachus did in the case of subjects, that the slave should be governed for his own harm, but on the ground that it is better for everyone to be governed by the divine and the intelligent, preferably indwelling and his own, but in default of that imposed from without, in order that we all so far as possible may be akin and friendly because our governance and guidance are the same? Yes, and rightly so, he said.

greek_head: Δηλοῖ δέ γε ἦν δ' ἐγώ καὶ ὁ νόμος ὅτι τοιοῦτον βούλεται πᾶσι τοῖς ἐν τῇ πόλει σύμμαχος ὤν καὶ ἡ τῶν
greek_tokens: dhlow de ge eimi de egw kai o nomos ostis toioutos boulomai pas o en o polis summaxos eimi kai o o pais arxh o mh eaw eleuqeros eimi ews an en autos wsper en polis politeia kaqisthmi kai o beltistos
english_head: And it is plain, I said, that this is the purpose of the law, which is the ally of all classes in the state, and this is the aim of our control of children, our not leaving them free before we have established, so to speak, a constitutional government within them and, by fostering the best element in them

greek_head: θεραπεύσαντες τῷ παρ' ἡμῖν τοιούτῳ ἀντικαταστήσωμεν φύλακα ὅμοιον καὶ ἄρχοντα ἐν αὐτῷ καὶ τότε δὴ ἐλεύθερον
greek_tokens: qerapeuw o para egw toioutos antikaqisthmi fulac omoios kai arxw en autos kai tote dh eleuqeros afihmi dhlow gar eimi de os ph dh oun fhmi eimi glauc kai kata tis logos lusitelew adikew h akolastainw h tis aisxros poiew ek os ponhros men eimi plew de xrhma h allos tis dunamis ktaomai oudamh eimi de os ph de adikew lanqanw kai mh didwmi dikh lusitelew
english_head: with the aid of the like in ourselves, have set up in its place a similar guardian and ruler in the child, and then, and then only, we leave it free. Yes, that is plain, he said. In what way, then, Glaucon, and on what principle, shall we say that it profits a man to be unjust or licentious or do any shameful thing that will make him a worse man, but otherwise will bring him more wealth or power? In no way, he said. And how that it pays him to escape detection in wrongdoing and not pay the penalty?

greek_head: ἢ οὐχὶ ὁ μὲν λανθάνων ἔτι πονηρότερος γίγνεται τοῦ δὲ μὴ λανθάνοντος καὶ κολαζομένου τὸ μὲν θηριῶδες κοιμίζεται
greek_tokens: h ou o men lanqanw eti ponhros gignomai o de mh lanqanw kai kolazw o men qhriwdhs koimizw kai hmerow o de hmeros eleuqerow kai oloc o yuxh eis o beltistos fus kaqisthmi timios ecis lambanw swfrosunos te kai dikaiosunh meta fronhsis ktaomai h swma isxus te kai kallos meta ugieia lambanw tosoutos osos yuxh swma timios pantapasi men oun fhmi
english_head: Or is it not true that he who evades detection becomes a still worse man, while in the one who is discovered and chastened the brutish part is lulled and tamed and the gentle part liberated, and the entire soul, returning to its nature at the best, attains to a much more precious condition in acquiring sobriety and righteousness together with wisdom, than the body does when it gains strength and beauty conjoined with health, even as the soul is more precious than the body? Most assuredly, he said.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ὅ γε νοῦν ἔχων πάντα τὰ αὑτοῦ εἰς τοῦτο συντείνας βιώσεται πρῶτον μὲν τὰ μαθήματα τιμῶν ἃ τοιαύτην
greek_tokens: oukoun o ge new exw pas o eautou eis outos sunteinw biow proteros men o maqhma timaw o toioutos autos o yuxh apergazomai o de allos atimazw dhlos fhmi epeita ge eipon o o swma ecis kai trofh ou opws o qhriwdhs kai alogos hdona epitrepw entauqa trepw zaw alla oude pros ugieia blepw oude outos presbeuw opws isxuros h ugihs h kalos eimi ean mh
english_head: Then the wise man will bend all his endeavors to this end throughout his life; he will, to begin with, prize the studies that will give this quality to his soul and disprize the others. Clearly, he said. And then, I said, he not only will not abandon the habit and nurture of his body to the brutish and irrational pleasure and live with his face set in that direction, but he will not even make health his chief aim, nor give the first place to the ways of becoming strong or healthy or beautiful unless these things are likely to bring with them soberness of spirit,

greek_head: καὶ σωφρονήσειν μέλλῃ ἀπ' αὐτῶν ἀλλ' ἀεὶ τὴν ἐν τῷ σώματι ἁρμονίαν τῆς ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ ἕνεκα συμφωνίας ἁρμοττόμενος
greek_tokens: kai swfronew mellw apo autos alla aei o en o swma armonia o en o yuxw eneka sumfwnia armozw fainw pantapasi men oun fhmi ean mellw o alhqeia mousikos eimi oukoun eipon kai o en o o xrhma ktaomai suntacis te kai sumfwnia kai o ogkos o plhqos ou ekplhssw upo o o polus makarismos apeiros auchsis aperantos kakos exw ou oiomai fhmi
english_head: but he will always be found attuning the harmonies of his body for the sake of the concord in his soul. By all means, he replied, if he is to be a true musician. And will he not deal likewise with the ordering and harmonizing of his possessions? He will not let himself be dazzled by the felicitations of the multitude and pile up the mass of his wealth without measure, involving himself in measureless ills. No, I think not, he said.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ἀποβλέπων γε εἶπον πρὸς τὴν ἐν αὑτῷ πολιτείαν καὶ φυλάττων μή τι παρακινῇ αὑτοῦ τῶν ἐκεῖ διὰ πλῆθος
greek_tokens: alla apoblepw ge eipon pros o en eautou politeia kai fulassw mh tis parakinew eautou o ekei dia plhqos ousia h dia oligoths outws kubernaw prosqew kai analwsis o ousia kaqa osos an oios su eimi komidh men oun fhmi
english_head: He will rather, I said, keep his eyes fixed on the constitution in his soul, and taking care and watching lest he disturb anything there either by excess or deficiency of wealth, will so steer his course and add to or detract from his wealth on this principle, so far as may be. Precisely so, he said. And in the matter of honors and office too this will be his guiding principle:

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν καὶ τιμάς γε εἰς ταὐτὸν ἀποβλέπων τῶν μὲν μεθέξει καὶ γεύσεται ἑκών ἃς ἂν ἡγῆται ἀμείνω αὑτὸν
greek_tokens: alla meis kai timh ge eis tautos apoblepw o men meqecis kai geuw ekwn os an hgeomai ameinwn eautou poiew os de an luw o uparxw ecis feugw idios kai dhmosios ou ara fhmi o ge politikos eqelw prassw ean outos khdw nh o kuwn eimi de egw en ge o eautou polis kai mala ou mentoi isos en ge o patris ean mh qeia tis sumbainw tuxh manqanw fhmi en h nun dierxomai oikizw polis legw o en logos keimai epei gaia ge oudamos oimh
english_head: He will gladly take part in and enjoy those which he thinks will make him a better man, but in public and private life he will shun those that may overthrow the established habit of his soul. Then, if that is his chief concern, he said, he will not willingly take part in politics. Yes, by the dog, said I, in his own city he certainly will, yet perhaps not in the city of his birth, except in some providential conjuncture. I understand, he said; you mean the city whose establishment we have described, the city whose home is in the ideal;

greek_head: αὐτὴν εἶναι Ἀλλ' ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἐν οὐρανῷ ἴσως παράδειγμα ἀνάκειται
greek_tokens: autos eimi alla eimi de egw en ouranos isos paradeigma anakeimai o boulomai oraw kai oraw eautou katoikizw diaferw de oudeis eite pou eimi eite eimi o gar outos monos an prassw allos de oudeis eoika ge fhmi
english_head: for I think that it can be found nowhere on earth. Well, said I, perhaps there is a pattern of it laid up in heaven for him who wishes to contemplate it and so beholding to constitute himself its citizen. But it makes no difference whether it exists now or ever will come into being. The politics of this city only will be his and of none other. That seems probable, he said.

greek_head: Καὶ μήν ἦν δ' ἐγώ πολλὰ μὲν καὶ ἄλλα περὶ αὐτῆς ἐννοῶ ὡς παντὸς ἄρα μᾶλλον ὀρθῶς ᾠκίζομεν τὴν πόλιν
greek_tokens: kai meis eimi de egw polus men kai allos peri autos ennoew ws pas ara mallon orqos oikizw o polis ou hkistos de enqumeomai peri poihsis legos o poios fhmi o mhdamh paradexomai autos osos mimhtikos pas gar mallon ou paradekteos nun kai enarghs ws egw dokew fainw epei xwris ekastos diairew o o yuxazw
english_head: And truly, I said, many other considerations assure me that we were entirely right in our organization of the state, and especially, I think, in the matter of poetry. What about it? he said. In refusing to admit at all so much of it as is imitative; for that it is certainly not to be received is, I think,

greek_head: εἴδη Πῶς λέγεις
greek_tokens: eidos pws legw ws men pros su erw ou gar egw katarrew pros o o tragwdia poihths kai o allos apas o mimhtikos lwbh eoika eimi pas o toioutos o o akouw dianoia osos mh exw farmakon o oida autos oios tugxanw eimi ph dh fhmi dianoeomai legw rhteos eimi de egw kaitoi filios ge tis egw kai aidws ek pais exw peri omhros apokwluw legw eoika men
english_head: still more plainly apparent now that we have distinguished the several parts of the soul. What do you mean? Why, between ourselves—for you will not betray me to the tragic poets and all other imitators—that kind of art seems to be a corruption of the mind of all listeners who do not possess, as an antidote a knowledge of its real nature. What is your idea in saying this? he said. I must speak out, I said, though a certain love and reverence for Homer that has possessed me from a boy would stay me from speaking.

greek_head: γὰρ τῶν καλῶν ἁπάντων τούτων τῶν τραγικῶν πρῶτος διδάσκαλός τε καὶ ἡγεμὼν γενέσθαι ἀλλ' οὐ γὰρ πρό γε τῆς
greek_tokens: gar o kalh apas outos o tragikos proteros didaskalos te kai hgemwn gignomai alla ou gar pro ge o alhqeia timhteos anhr alla o legos rhteos panu men oun fhmi akouw dh mallon de apokrinw erwtaw mimhsis oloc exw an egw eipon ostis pote eimi oude gar su autos panu tis sunnoew tis boulomai eimi eimi pou ara fhmi egw sunnoew oudeis ge eimi de egw atopos epei polus su ocus
english_head: For he appears to have been the first teacher and beginner of all these beauties of tragedy. Yet all the same we must not honor a man above truth, but, as I say, speak our minds. By all means, he said. Listen, then, or rather, answer my question. Ask it, he said. Could you tell me in general what imitation is? For neither do I myself quite apprehend what it would be at. It is likely, then, he said, that I should apprehend! It would be nothing strange, said I, since it often happens

greek_head: βλεπόντων ἀμβλύτερον ὁρῶντες πρότεροι εἶδον Ἔστιν ἔφη οὕτως ἀλλὰ σοῦ παρόντος οὐδ' ἂν προθυμηθῆναι
greek_tokens: blepw amblus oraw proteros eidon eimi fhmi outws alla sew pareimi oude an proqumeomai oios te eimi eipon ei tis egw katafainw alla autos oraw boulomai oun enqende arxw episkopew ek o eqw meqodos eidos gar pou tis eis ekastos eqw tiqhmi peri ekastos o polus os tautos onoma epiferw h ou manqanw manqanw qazw dh kai nun ostis boulomai o polus oios ei
english_head: that the dimmer vision sees things in advance of the keener. That is so, he said; but in your presence I could not even be eager to try to state anything that appears to me, but do you yourself consider it. Shall we, then, start the inquiry at this point by our customary procedure? We are in the habit, I take it, of positing a single idea or form in the case of the various multiplicities to which we give the same name. Do you not understand? I do. In the present case, then, let us take any multiplicity you please;

greek_head: 'θέλεις πολλαί πού εἰσι κλῖναι καὶ τράπεζαι Πῶς δ' οὔ
greek_tokens: eqelw polus pou eimi klinh kai trapeza pws de ou alla idea ge pou peri outos o skeuos duo eis men klinh eis de trapeza nai oukoun kai eqw legw ostis o dhmiourgos ekateros o skeuos pros o idea blepw outws poiew o men o klinh o de o trapeza os egw xraomai kai allos kata tautos ou gar pou o ge idea autos dhmiourgew oudeis o dhmiourgew pws gar oudamos alla oraw dh kai ode tis kalew o dhmiourgos
english_head: for example, there are many couches and tables. Of course. But these utensils imply, I suppose, only two ideas or forms, one of a couch and one of a table. Yes. And are we not also in the habit of saying that the craftsman who produces either of them fixes his eyes on the idea or form, and so makes in the one case the couches and in the other the tables that we use, and similarly of other things? For surely no craftsman makes the idea itself. How could he? By no means. But now consider

greek_head: Τὸν ποῖον Ὃς πάντα ποιεῖ ὅσαπερ εἷς ἕκαστος τῶν χειροτεχνῶν
greek_tokens: o poios os pas poiew osos eis ekastos o xeirotexnhs deinos tis legw kai qaumastos anhr oupw ge alla taxa mallon fhmi o autos gar outos xeirotexnhs ou monos pas oios te skeuos poiew alla kai o ek o gaia fuw apas poiew kai zwion pas ergazomai o te allos kai eautou kai pros outos gaia kai ouranos kai qeos kai pas o en ouranos kai o en adhs upo gaia apas ergazomai
english_head: what name you would give to this craftsman. What one? Him who makes all the things that all handicraftsmen severally produce. A truly clever and wondrous man you tell of. Ah, but wait, and you will say so indeed, for this same handicraftsman is not only able to make all implements, but he produces all plants and animals, including himself, and thereto earth and heaven and the gods and all things in heaven and in Hades under the earth. A most marvellous sophist,

greek_head: Πάνυ θαυμαστόν ἔφη λέγεις σοφιστήν Ἀπιστεῖς ἦν δ' ἐγώ καί μοι εἰπέ τὸ παράπαν οὐκ ἄν
greek_tokens: panu qaumastos fhmi legw sofisths apistew eimi de egw kai egw eipon o parapan ou an su dokew eimi toioutos dhmiourgos h tis men tropos gignomai an outos apas poihths tis de ou an h ou aisqanomai ostis kan autos oios su eaw pas outos poiew tropos ge tis kai tis fhmi o tropos outos ou xalepos eimi de egw alla pollaxh kai taxus dhmiourgew taxistos de pou ei eqelw lambanw katoptron
english_head: he said. Are you incredulous? said I. Tell me, do you deny altogether the possibility of such a craftsman, or do you admit that in a sense there could be such a creator of all these things, and in another sense not? Or do you not perceive that you yourself would be able to make all these things in a way? And in what way, I ask you, he said. There is no difficulty, said I, but it is something that the craftsman can make everywhere and quickly. You could do it most quickly if you should choose to take a mirror and carry it about everywhere.

greek_head: περιφέρειν πανταχῇ ταχὺ μὲν ἥλιον ποιήσεις καὶ τὰ ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ ταχὺ δὲ γῆν ταχὺ δὲ σαυτόν τε καὶ τἆλλα ζῷα καὶ
greek_tokens: periferw pantaxh taxus men hlios poihsis kai o en o ouranos taxus de gaia taxus de sautou te kai allos zwion kai skeuos kai futas kai pas osos nundi legw nai fhmi fainw ou mentoi eimi ge pou o alhqeia kalos eimi de egw kai eis deon erxomai o logos o toioutos gar oimh dhmiourgew kai o zwgrafos eimi eimi gar pws gar ou alla fhmi ou alhqhs oimh autos poiew o poiew kaitoi tropos ge tis kai o zwgrafos klinh poiew h ou nai fhmi fainw ge kai outos
english_head: You will speedily produce the sun and all the things in the sky, and speedily the earth and yourself and the other animals and implements and plants and all the objects of which we just now spoke. Yes, he said, the appearance of them, but not the reality and the truth. Excellent, said I, and you come to the aid of the argument opportunely. For I take it that the painter too belongs to this class of producers, does he not? Of course. But you will say, I suppose, that his creations are not real and true. And yet, after a fashion, the painter too makes a couch, does he not? Yes, he said, the appearance of one, he too.

greek_head: Τί δὲ ὁ κλινοποιός οὐκ ἄρτι μέντοι ἔλεγες ὅτι οὐ τὸ εἶδος ποιεῖ ὃ δή φαμεν εἶναι ὃ ἔστι κλίνη ἀλλὰ κλίνην τινά
greek_tokens: tis de o klinopoios ou arti mentoi legw ostis ou o eidos poiew o dh fhmi eimi o eimi klinh alla klinh tis elegos gar oukoun ei mh o eimi poiew ou an o eimi poiew alla tis toioutos oios o eimi eimi de ou teleios de eimi eimi o o klinourgos ergnumi h allos tis xeirotexnhs ei tis fhmi kinduneuw ou an alhqhs legw oukoun fhmi os ge an dokew o peri o toiosde logos diatribw mhdeis ara qaumazw ei kai outos amudros tis tugxanw eimi pros alhqeia
english_head: What of the cabinet-maker? Were you not just now saying that he does not make the idea or form which we say is the real couch, the couch in itself, but only some particular couch? Yes, I was. Then if he does not make that which really is, he could not be said to make real being but something that resembles real being but is not that. But if anyone should say that being in the complete sense belongs to the work of the cabinet-maker or to that of any other handicraftsman, it seems that he would say what is not true. That would be the view, he said, of those who are versed in this kind of reasoning. We must not be surprised, then, if this too is only a dim adumbration in comparison with reality.

greek_head: Μὴ γάρ Βούλει οὖν ἔφην ἐπ' αὐτῶν τούτων ζητήσωμεν τὸν
greek_tokens: mh gar boulomai oun fhmi epi autos outos zhtew o mimhths outos tis pote eimi ei boulomai fhmi oukoun trissos tis klinh outos gignomai eis men o en o fusis eimi os fhmi an ws oimh qeos ergazomai h tinw allos oudeis oimh eis de ge os o tektwn nai fhmi eis de os o zwgrafos eimi gar eimi zwgrafos dh klinopoios qeos trew outos epistath treis eidos klinh nai trew
english_head: No, we must not. Shall we, then, use these very examples in our quest for the true nature of this imitator? If you please, he said. We get, then, these three couches, one, that in nature which, I take it, we would say that God produces, or who else? No one, I think. And then there was one which the carpenter made. Yes, he said. And one which the painter. Is not that so? So be it. The painter, then, the cabinet-maker, and God, there are these three presiding over three kinds of couches. Yes,three.

greek_head: Ὁ μὲν δὴ θεός εἴτε οὐκ ἐβούλετο εἴτε τις ἀνάγκη ἐπῆν μὴ πλέον ἢ μίαν ἐν τῇ φύσει ἀπεργάσασθαι αὐτὸν κλίνην
greek_tokens: o men dh qeos eite ou boulomai eite tis anagkh epeimi mh pleos h eis en o fusis apergazomai autos klinh outws poiew eis monos autos ekeinos o eimi klinh duo de toioutos h plews oute futeuw upo o qeaomai oute mh fuw pws dh fhmi ostis eimi de egw ei duo monos poiew palin an eis anafainw eis ekeinos an au amfoteros o eidos exw kai eaw an o eimi klinh ekeinos alla ou o duo orqos fhmi
english_head: Now God,whether because he so willed or because some compulsion was laid upon him not to make more than one couch in nature, so wrought and created one only, the couch which really and in itself is. But two or more such were never created by God and never will come into being. How so? he said. Because, said I, if he should make only two, there would again appear one of which they both would possess the form or idea, and that would be the couch that really is in and of itself, and not the other two. Right, he said. God, then, I take it, knowing this and wishing

greek_head: Ταῦτα δὴ οἶμαι εἰδὼς ὁ θεός βουλόμενος εἶναι ὄντως κλίνης ποιητὴς ὄντως οὔσης ἀλλὰ μὴ κλίνης τινὸς μηδὲ
greek_tokens: outos dh oimh eidoi o qeos boulomai eimi ontws klinh poihths ontws eimi alla mh klinh tis mhde klinopoios tis eis fusis autos efuw eoika boulomai oun outos men futourgos outos prosagoreuw h tis toioutos dikaios goun fhmi epei fusis ge kai outos kai allos pas poiew tis de o tektwn ara ou dhmiourgos klinh nai eimi kai o zwgrafos dhmiourgos kai poihths o toioutos oudamos alla tis autos klinh fhmi eimi
english_head: to be the real author of the couch that has real being and not of some particular couch, nor yet a particular cabinet-maker, produced it in nature unique. So it seems. Shall we, then, call him its true and natural begetter, or something of the kind? That would certainly be right, he said, since it is by and in nature that he has made this and all other things. And what of the carpenter? Shall we not call him the creator of a couch? Yes. Shall we also say that the painter is the creator and maker of that sort of thing? By no means. What will you say he is in relation to the couch?

greek_head: Τοῦτο ἦ δ' ὅς ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ μετριώτατ' ἂν προσαγορεύεσθαι μιμητὴς οὗ ἐκεῖνοι δημιουργοί
greek_tokens: outos eimi de os egw dokew metrios an prosagoreuw mimhths e ekeinos dhmiourgos eien eimi de egw o o tritos ara gennhma apo o fusis mimhths kalew panu men oun fhmi outos ara eimi kai o tragwdopoios eiper mimhths eimi tritos tis apo basileus kai o alhqeia fuw kai pas e allos mimhths kinduneuw o men dh mimhths omologew eipon de egw peri
english_head: This, said he, seems to me the most reasonable designation for him, that he is the imitator of the thing which those others produce. Very good, said I; the producer of the product three removes from nature you call the imitator? By all means, he said. This, then, will apply to the maker of tragedies also, if he is an imitator and is in his nature three removes from the king and the truth, as are all other imitators. It would seem so. We are in agreement, then, about the imitator.

greek_head: τοῦ ζωγράφου τόδε πότερα ἐκεῖνο αὐτὸ τὸ ἐν τῇ φύσει ἕκαστον δοκεῖ σοι ἐπιχειρεῖν μιμεῖσθαι ἢ τὰ τῶν δημιουργῶν
greek_tokens: o zwgrafos ode poteros ekeinos autos o en o fusis ekastos dokew su epixeirew mimeomai h o o dhmiourgew ergon o o dhmiourgew fhmi ara oios eimi h oios fainw outos gar eti diorizw pws legw fhmi wde klinh ean ek plagion autos qea ean katantikru h oph mh tis diaferw autos eautou h diaferw men oudeis fainw de alloios kai allos wsautws outws fhmi fainw diaferw de oudeis
english_head: But tell me now this about the painter. Do you think that what he tries to imitate is in each case that thing itself in nature or the works of the craftsmen? The works of the craftsmen, he said. Is it the reality of them or the appearance? Define that further point. What do you mean? he said. This: Does a couch differ from itself according as you view it from the side or the front or in any other way? Or does it differ not at all in fact though it appears different, and so of other things? That is the way of it, he said: it appears other but differs not at all.

greek_head: Τοῦτο δὴ αὐτὸ σκόπει πρὸς πότερον ἡ γραφικὴ πεποίηται περὶ ἕκαστον πότερα πρὸς τὸ ὄν ὡς ἔχει μιμήσασθαι ἢ
greek_tokens: outos dh autos skopaw pros poteros o grafikos poiew peri ekastos poteros pros o eimi ws exis mimeomai h pros o fainw ws fainw fantasma h alhqeia eimi mimhsis fantasma fhmi prosw ara pou o alhqhs o mimhtikos eimi kai ws eoika dia outos pas apergazomai ostis mikros tis ekastos efaptw kai outos eidwlon oios o zwgrafos fhmi zwgrafew egw skutotomos tektwn o allos dhmiourgos
english_head: Consider, then, this very point. To which is painting directed in every case, to the imitation of reality as it is or of appearance as it appears? Is it an imitation of a phantasm or of the truth? Of a phantasm, he said. Then the mimetic art is far removed from truth, and this, it seems, is the reason why it can produce everything, because it touches or lays hold of only a small part of the object and that a phantom; as, for example, a painter, we say, will paint us a cobbler, a carpenter, and other craftsmen,

greek_head: περὶ οὐδενὸς τούτων ἐπαΐων τῶν τεχνῶν ἀλλ' ὅμως παῖδάς γε καὶ ἄφρονας ἀνθρώπους εἰ ἀγαθὸς εἴη ζωγράφος
greek_tokens: peri oudeis outos epaiw o texnh alla omws pais ge kai afrwn anqrwpos ei agaqos eaw zwgrafos grafw an tektwn kai proswqen epideiknumi ecapataw an o dokew ws alhqhs tektwn eimi tis de ou alla gar oimh eimi filos ode deomai peri pas o toioutos dianoeomai epeidan tis egw apaggellw peri tis ws entugxanw anqrwpos passw efisthmi o dhmiourgia kai allos pas osos eis ekastos oida oudeis ostis ou
english_head: though he himself has no expertness in any of these arts, but nevertheless if he were a good painter, by exhibiting at a distance his picture of a carpenter he would deceive children and foolish men, and make them believe it to be a real carpenter. Why not? But for all that, my friend, this, I take it, is what we ought to bear in mind in all such cases: When anyone reports to us of someone, that he has met a man who knows all the crafts and everything else that men severally know,

greek_head: ἀκριβέστερον ὁτουοῦν ἐπισταμένῳ ὑπολαμβάνειν δεῖ τῷ τοιούτῳ ὅτι εὐήθης τις ἄνθρωπος καί ὡς ἔοικεν ἐντυχὼν
greek_tokens: akribhs ostis efisthmi upolambanw deomai o toioutos ostis euhqhs tis anqrwpos kai ws eoika entugxanw gohs tis kai mimhths ecapataw wste dokew autos pansofos eimi dia o autos mh oios su eimi episthmh kai anepisthmosunh kai mimhsis ecetazw alhqhs fhmi oukoun eimi de egw meta outos episkepteos o te tragwdia kai o hgemwn autos omhros epei tis
english_head: and that there is nothing that he does not know more exactly than anybody else, our tacit rejoinder must be that he is a simple fellow, who apparently has met some magician or sleight-of-hand man and imitator and has been deceived by him into the belief that he is all-wise, because of his own inability to put to the proof and distinguish knowledge, ignorance and imitation. Most true, he said. Then, said I, have we not next to scrutinize tragedy and its leader Homer, since some people tell us that these poets know all the arts

greek_head: ἀκούομεν ὅτι οὗτοι πάσας μὲν τέχνας ἐπίστανται πάντα δὲ τὰ ἀνθρώπεια τὰ πρὸς ἀρετὴν καὶ κακίαν καὶ τά γε θεῖα
greek_tokens: akouw ostis outos passw men texnh efisthmi pas de o anqrwpeios o pros areth kai kakia kai o ge qeios anagkh gar o agaqos poihths ei mellw peri os an poiew kalos poiew oida ara poiew h mh oios te eimi poiew deomai dh episkeptomai poteros mimhths outos outos entugxanw ecapataw kai o ergon autos oraw
english_head: and all things human pertaining to virtue and vice, and all things divine? For the good poet, if he is to poetize things rightly, must, they argue, create with knowledge or else be unable to create. So we must consider whether these critics have not fallen in with such imitators and been deceived by them, so that looking upon their works

greek_head: οὐκ αἰσθάνονται τριττὰ ἀπέχοντα τοῦ ὄντος καὶ ῥᾴδια ποιεῖν μὴ εἰδότι τὴν ἀλήθειαν φαντάσματα γὰρ ἀλλ' οὐκ ὄντα
greek_tokens: ou aisqanomai trissos apexw o eimi kai radia poiew mh oida o alhqeia fantasma gar alla ou eimi poiew h tis kai legw kai o eimi e agaqos poihths oida peri os dokew o polus eu legw panu men oun fhmi ecetasteon oiomai oun ei tis amfoteros dunamai poiew o te mimeomai kai o eidwlon epi o o eidwlon dhmiourgia eautou afihmi an spoudazw kai outos proisthmi o
english_head: they cannot perceive that these are three removes from reality, and easy to produce without knowledge of the truth. For it is phantoms, not realities, that they produce. Or is there something in their claim, and do good poets really know the things about which the multitude fancy they speak well? We certainly must examine the matter, he said. Do you suppose, then, that if a man were able to produce both the exemplar and the semblance, he would be eager to abandon himself to the fashioning of phantoms and set this in the forefront

greek_head: ἑαυτοῦ βίου ὡς βέλτιστον ἔχοντα Οὐκ ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: eautou bios ws beltistos exw ou egw alla eiper ge oimh episthmos eaw o alhqeia outos peri o kai mimeomai polus proteros en o ergon an spoudazw h epi o mimhma kai peiraw an polus kai kalos ergon eautou katalimpanw mnhmeion kai eimi proqumeomai an mallon o egkwmiazw h o egkwmiazw oimh fhmi ou gar ek isos o te timh kai o wfeleia o men toinun allos peri mh apaitew logos omhros
english_head: of his life as the best thing he had? I do not. But, I take it, if he had genuine knowledge of the things he imitates he would far rather devote himself to real things than to the imitation of them, and would endeavor to leave after him many noble deeds and works as memorials of himself, and would be more eager to be the theme of praise than the praiser. I think so, he said; for there is no parity in the honor and the gain. Let us not, then, demand a reckoning from Homer

greek_head: ἢ ἄλλον ὁντινοῦν τῶν ποιητῶν ἐρωτῶντες εἰ ἰατρικὸς ἦν τις αὐτῶν ἀλλὰ μὴ μιμητὴς μόνον ἰατρικῶν λόγων τίνας
greek_tokens: h allos ostis o poihths erwtaw ei iatrikos eimi tis autos alla mh mimhths monos iatrikos logos tis ugihs poihths tis o palaios h o neos legw poiew wsper asklhpios h tis maqhths iatrikos katalimpanw wsper ekeinos o ekgonos mhde au peri o allos texnh autos erwtaw alla eaw peri de os megas te kai kalos epixeirew legw omhros polemos te peri kai strathgia kai dioikhsis polis kai
english_head: or any other of the poets on other matters by asking them, if any one of them was a physician and not merely an imitator of a physician’s talk, what men any poet, old or new, is reported to have restored to health as Asclepius did, or what disciples of the medical art he left after him as Asclepius did his descendants; and let us dismiss the other arts and not question them about them; but concerning the greatest and finest things of which Homer undertakes to speak, wars and generalship

greek_head: παιδείας πέρι ἀνθρώπου δίκαιόν που ἐρωτᾶν αὐτὸν πυνθανομένους Ὦ φίλε Ὅμηρε εἴπερ μὴ τρίτος ἀπὸ τῆς ἀληθείας
greek_tokens: paideia peri anqrwpos dikaios pou erwtaw autos punqanomai eimi filos omhros eiper mh tritos apo o alhqeia ei aretaw peri eidwlon dhmiourgos os dh mimhths orizw alla kai deuteros kai oios te eimi gignwskw poios epithdeuma beltiwn h xeirwn anqrwpos poiew idios kai dhmosios legos egw tis o polis dia sos beltiwn oikew wsper dia lukourgos lakedaimwn kai dia allos polus
english_head: and the administration of cities and the education of men, it surely is fair to question him and ask, Friend Homer, if you are not at the third remove from truth and reality in human excellence, being merely that creator of phantoms whom we defined as the imitator, but if you are even in the second place and were capable of knowing what pursuits make men better or worse in private or public life, tell us what city was better governed owing to you, even as Lacedaemon was because of Lycurgus, and many other cities

greek_head: πολλαὶ μεγάλαι τε καὶ σμικραί σὲ δὲ τίς αἰτιᾶται πόλις νομοθέτην ἀγαθὸν γεγονέναι καὶ σφᾶς ὠφεληκέναι Χαρώνδαν
greek_tokens: polus megas te kai mikros sos de tis aitiaomai polis nomoqeths agaqos gignomai kai sfazw wfelew xarwndhs men gar italia kai sikelia kai egw solwn sos de tis ecis tis eipon ou oimh fhmi o glauc oukoun legw ge oude upo autos omhridai
english_head: great and small because of other legislators. But what city credits you with having been a good legislator and having benefited them? Italy and Sicily say this of Charondas and we of Solon. But who says it of you? Will he be able to name any? I think not, said Glaucon; at any rate none is mentioned even by the Homerids themselves. Well, then,

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ δή τις πόλεμος ἐπὶ Ὁμήρου ὑπ' ἐκείνου ἄρχοντος ἢ συμβουλεύοντος εὖ πολεμηθεὶς μνημονεύεται
greek_tokens: alla dh tis polemos epi omhros upo ekeinos arxw h sumbouleuw eu polemew mnhmoneuw oudeis alla oios dh eis o ergon sofos anhr polus epinoia kai eumhxanos eis texnh h tis allos prassw legw wsper au qalhs te peri o milhsios kai anaxarsis o skuqos oudamos toioutos oudeis alla dh ei mh dhmosios idios tis hgemwn paideia autos zaw legw omhros gignomai e ekeinos agapaw epi
english_head: is there any tradition of a war in Homer’s time that was well conducted by his command or counsel? None. Well, then, as might be expected of a man wise in practical affairs, are many and ingenious inventions for the arts and business of life reported of Homer as they are of Thales the Milesian and Anacharsis the Scythian? Nothing whatever of the sort. Well, then, if no public service is credited to him, is Homer reported while he lived to have been a guide in education to men who took pleasure in associating with him

greek_head: συνουσίᾳ καὶ τοῖς ὑστέροις ὁδόν τινα παρέδοσαν βίου Ὁμηρικήν ὥσπερ Πυθαγόρας αὐτός τε διαφερόντως ἐπὶ
greek_tokens: sunousia kai o usteron odos tis paradidwmi bios omhrikos wsper puqagoras autos te diaferontws epi outos agapaw kai e usteros eti kai nun puqagoreios tropos eponomazw o bios diafainw ph dokew eimi en o allos oude au fhmi toioutos oudeis legw o gar krewfulos eimi swkraths isos o o omhros etairos o onoma an geloios eti pros paideia fainw ei o legw peri omhros alhqhs legw gar ws polus tis ameleia peri
english_head: and transmitted to posterity a certain Homeric way of life just as Pythagoras was himself especially honored for this, and his successors, even to this day, denominating a certain way of life the Pythagorean, are distinguished among their contemporaries? No, nothing of this sort either is reported; for Creophylos, Socrates, the friend of Homer, would perhaps be even more ridiculous than his name as a representative of Homeric culture and education, if what is said about Homer is true. For the tradition is that Homer was completely neglected in his own lifetime by that friend of the flesh.

greek_head: αὐτὸν ἦν ἐπ' αὐτοῦ ἐκείνου ὅτε ἔζη Λέγεται γὰρ οὖν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἀλλ' οἴει ὦ Γλαύκων εἰ
greek_tokens: autos eimi epi autos ekeinos oste zaw legw gar oun eimi de egw alla oiomai eimi glauc ei o eimi oios su eimi paideuw anqrwpos kai beltiwn apergazomai omhros ate peri outos ou mimeomai alla gignwskw dunamai ou ara an polus etairos poiew kai timaw kai agapaw upo autos alla prwtagoras men ara o abdhriths kai prodikos o kews kai allos pampolus dunamai o epi eautou paristhmi idios suggignomai
english_head: Why, yes, that is the tradition, said I; but do you suppose, Glaucon, that, if Homer had really been able to educate men and make them better and had possessed not the art of imitation but real knowledge, he would not have acquired many companions and been honored and loved by them? But are we to believe that while Protagoras of Abdera and Prodicus of Ceos and many others are able by private teaching

greek_head: ὡς οὔτε οἰκίαν οὔτε πόλιν τὴν αὑτῶν διοικεῖν οἷοί τ' ἔσονται ἐὰν μὴ σφεῖς αὐτῶν ἐπιστατήσωσιν τῆς παιδείας
greek_tokens: ws oute oikia oute polis o eautou dioikew oios su eimi ean mh sfeis autos epistatew o paideia kai epi outos o sofia outws sfodra filew wste monos ou epi o kefalh periferw autos e etairos omhros de ara e epi ekeinos eiper oios su eimi pros areth oninhmi anqrwpos h hsiodos raywdew an perieimi eaw kai ou mallon an autos antexw h o xruseos kai
english_head: to impress upon their contemporaries the conviction that they will not be capable of governing their homes or the city unless they put them in charge of their education, and make themselves so beloved for this wisdom that their companions all but carry them about on their shoulders, yet, forsooth, that Homer’s contemporaries, if he had been able to help men to achieve excellence, would have suffered him or Hesiod to roam about rhapsodizing and would not have clung to them far rather than to their gold, and constrained them to dwell with them in their homes,

greek_head: ἠνάγκαζον παρὰ σφίσιν οἴκοι εἶναι ἢ εἰ μὴ ἔπειθον αὐτοὶ ἂν ἐπαιδαγώγουν ὅπῃ ᾖσαν ἕως ἱκανῶς παιδείας μεταλάβοιεν
greek_tokens: anagkazw para sfeis eoika eimi h ei mh peiqw autos an paidagwgew oph aeidw ews ikanos paideia metalambanw pantapasi fhmi dokew egw eimi swkraths alhqhs legw oukoun tiqhmi apo omhros arxw pas o poihtikos mimhths eidwlon aretaw eimi kai o allos peri os poiew o de alhqeia ou aptw alla wsper nundi legw o zwgrafos skutotomos poihsis dokew
english_head: or failing to persuade them, would themselves have escorted them wheresoever they went until they should have sufficiently imbibed their culture? What you say seems to me to be altogether true, Socrates, he said. Shall we, then, lay it down that all the poetic tribe, beginning with Homer, are imitators of images of excellence and of the other things that they create, and do not lay hold on truth? but, as we were just now saying, the painter will fashion,

greek_head: εἶναι αὐτός τε οὐκ ἐπαΐων περὶ σκυτοτομίας καὶ τοῖς μὴ ἐπαΐουσιν ἐκ τῶν χρωμάτων δὲ καὶ σχημάτων θεωροῦσιν
greek_tokens: eimi autos te ou epaiw peri skutotomia kai o mh epaiw ek o xrwma de kai sxhma qewrew panu men oun outws dh oimh kai o poihtikos fhmi xrwma atta ekastos o texnh o onoma kai rhma epixrwmatizw autos ou epaiw alla h mimeomai wste eteros toioutos ek o logos qewrew dokew ean peri skutotomia tis legos en metron kai ruqmos kai armonia panu eu dokew legw ean peri strathgia ean peri allos
english_head: himself knowing nothing of the cobbler’s art, what appears to be a cobbler to him and likewise to those who know nothing but judge only by forms and colors? Certainly. And similarly, I suppose, we shall say that the poet himself, knowing nothing but how to imitate, lays on with words and phrases the colors of the several arts in such fashion that others equally ignorant, who see things only through words, will deem his words most excellent,

greek_head: ὁτουοῦν οὕτω φύσει αὐτὰ ταῦτα μεγάλην τινὰ κήλησιν ἔχειν ἐπεὶ γυμνωθέντα γε τῶν τῆς μουσικῆς χρωμάτων
greek_tokens: ostis outws fusis autos outos megas tis khlhsis exw epei gumnow ge o o mousikh xrwma o o poihths autos epi eautou legw oimh su oida oios fainw qeaomai gar pou egw fhmi oukoun eimi de egw eoika o o wraios proswpon kalh de mh oios gignomai eidon otan autos o anqos proleipw pantapasi eimi de os eimi dh ode aqrew o o eidwlon poihths o mimhths fhmi o men eimi oudeis epaiw o de fainw ou
english_head: whether he speak in rhythm, meter and harmony about cobbling or generalship or anything whatever. So mighty is the spell that these adornments naturally exercise; though when they are stripped bare of their musical coloring and taken by themselves, I think you know what sort of a showing these sayings of the poets make. For you, I believe, have observed them. I have, he said. Do they not, said I, resemble the faces of adolescents, young but not really beautiful, when the bloom of youth abandons them? By all means, he said. Come, then, said I, consider this point: The creator of the phantom, the imitator, we say, knows nothing of the reality but only the appearance.

greek_head: οὕτως Ναί
greek_tokens: outws nai mh toinun hmisews autos katalimpanw erw alla ikanos eidon legos fhmi zwgrafos fhmi hnia te grafw kai xalinos nai poihsis de ge skutotomos kai xalkeus panu ge ara oun epaiw oios deomai o hnia eimi kai o xalinos o grafeus h oude o poiew o te xalkeus kai o skuteus alla ekeinos os outos efisthmi xraomai monos o ippikos alhqhs ara oun ou peri pas outws fhmi exw pws
english_head: Is not that so? Yes. Let us not, then, leave it half said but consider it fully. Speak on, he said. The painter, we say, will paint both reins and a bit. Yes. But the maker will be the cobbler and the smith. Certainly. Does the painter, then, know the proper quality of reins and bit? Or does not even the maker, the cobbler and the smith, know that, but only the man who understands the use of these things, the horseman? Most true. And shall we not say

greek_head: Περὶ ἕκαστον ταύτας τινὰς τρεῖς τέχνας εἶναι χρησομένην ποιήσουσαν μιμησομένην
greek_tokens: peri ekastos outos tis trew texnh eimi xraw poiew mimeomai nai oukoun areth kai kallos kai orqoths ekastos skeuos kai zwion kai pracis ou pros allos tis h o xreia eimi pros os an ekastos eimi poiew h fuw outws polus ara anagkh o xraomai ekastos empeiros te eimi kai aggelos gignomai o poihths oios agaqos h kakos poiew en o xreia os xraomai oios aulhths pou aulopoios
english_head: that the same holds true of everything? What do you mean? That there are some three arts concerned with everything, the user’s art, the maker’s, and the imitator’s. Yes. Now do not the excellence, the beauty, the rightness of every implement, living thing, and action refer solely to the use for which each is made or by nature adapted? That is so. It quite necessarily follows, then, that the user of anything is the one who knows most of it by experience, and that he reports to the maker the good or bad effects in use of the thing he uses.

greek_head: ἐξαγγέλλει περὶ τῶν αὐλῶν οἳ ἂν ὑπηρετῶσιν ἐν τῷ αὐλεῖν καὶ ἐπιτάξει οἵους δεῖ ποιεῖν ὁ δ' ὑπηρετήσει
greek_tokens: ecaggellw peri o aulew e an uphretew en o aulew kai epitacis oios deomai poiew o de uphrethsis pws de ou oukoun o men eidoi ecaggellw peri xrhstos kai ponhros aulew o de pisteuw poihsis nai o autos ara skeuos o men poihths pistis orqos ecis peri kallos te kai ponhria suneimi o oida kai anagkazw
english_head: As, for example, the flute-player reports to the flute-maker which flutes respond and serve rightly in flute-playing, and will order the kind that must be made, and the other will obey and serve him. Of course. The one, then, possessing knowledge, reports about the goodness or the badness of the flutes, and the other, believing, will make them. Yes. Then in respect of the same implement the maker will have right belief about its excellence and defects from association with the man who knows and being compelled to listen to him,

greek_head: ἀκούειν παρὰ τοῦ εἰδότος ὁ δὲ χρώμενος ἐπιστήμην Πάνυ γε
greek_tokens: akouw para o oida o de xraomai episthmh panu ge o de mimhths poteros ek o xraomai episthmh ecis peri os an grafw eite kalos kai orqos eite mh h doca orqos dia o ek anagkh suneimi o oida kai epitassw oios xrh grafw oudeteros oute ara eimi oute orqos docasis o mimhths peri os an mimeomai pros kallos h ponhria ou eoika xarieis an eaw o en o poihsis mimhtikos pros sofia peri os an poiew ou panu
english_head: but the user will have true knowledge. Certainly. And will the imitator from experience or use have knowledge whether the things he portrays are or are not beautiful and right, or will he, from compulsory association with the man who knows and taking orders from him for the right making of them, have right opinion? Neither. Then the imitator will neither know nor opine rightly concerning the beauty or the badness of his imitations. It seems not. Most charming, then, would be the state of mind of the poetical imitator in respect of true wisdom about his creations. Not at all.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' οὖν δὴ ὅμως γε μιμήσεται οὐκ εἰδὼς περὶ ἑκάστου ὅπῃ πονηρὸν ἢ χρηστόν ἀλλ' ὡς ἔοικεν οἷον φαίνεται
greek_tokens: alla oun dh omws ge mimeomai ou eidoi peri ekastos oph ponhros h xrhstos alla ws eoika oios fainw kalos eimi o polus te kai mhdeis oida outos mimeomai tis gar allos outos men dh os ge fainw epieikhs egw diomologew o te mimhtikos mhdeis oida acios logos peri os mimeomai alla eimi paidih tis kai ou spoudh o mimhsis o te o tragikos poihsis aptw en iambeios kai en epos pas eimi mimhtikos ws oios te malista panu men oun
english_head: Yet still he will none the less imitate, though in every case he does not know in what way the thing is bad or good. But, as it seems, the thing he will imitate will be the thing that appears beautiful to the ignorant multitude. Why, what else? On this, then, as it seems, we are fairly agreed, that the imitator knows nothing worth mentioning of the things he imitates, but that imitation is a form of play, not to be taken seriously, and that those who attempt tragic poetry, whether in iambics or heroic verse, are all altogether imitators. By all means.

greek_head: Πρὸς Διός ἦν δ' ἐγώ τὸ δὲ δὴ μιμεῖσθαι τοῦτο οὐ περὶ τρίτον μέν τί ἐστιν ἀπὸ τῆς ἀληθείας ἦ γάρ
greek_tokens: pros zeus eimi de egw o de dh mimeomai outos ou peri tritos men tis eimi apo o alhqeia eimi gar nai pros de dh poios tis eimi o o anqrwpos exw o dunamis os exis o poios tis peri legw o toiosde tautos pou egw megeqos egguqen te kai proswqen dia o oyis ou isos fainw ou gar kai tautos kampulh te kai euqhs en udwr te qeaomai kai ecw kai koilos te dh kai ecexw dia o peri o xrwma au planh o oyis kai pas tis taraxh dhlos
english_head: In heaven’s name, then, this business of imitation is concerned with the third remove from truth, is it not? Yes. And now again, to what element in man is its function and potency related? Of what are you speaking? Of this: The same magnitude, I presume, viewed from near and from far does not appear equal. Why, no. And the same things appear bent and straight to those who view them in water and out, or concave and convex, owing to similar errors of vision about colors, and there is

greek_head: ἡμῖν ἐνοῦσα αὕτη ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ ᾧ δὴ ἡμῶν τῷ παθήματι τῆς φύσεως ἡ σκιαγραφία ἐπιθεμένη γοητείας οὐδὲν ἀπολείπει
greek_tokens: egw eneimi outos en o yuxw os dh egw o paqhma o fusis o skiagrafia epitiqhmi gohteia oudeis apoleipw kai o qaumatopoiia kai o allos polus toioutos mhxanh alhqhs ara oun ou o metrew kai ariqmew kai isthmi bohqeia xarieis pros autos fainw wste mh arxw en egw o fainw megas h elasswn h pleos h barus alla o logizomai kai metrew h kai isthmi pws gar ou
english_head: obviously every confusion of this sort in our souls. And so scene-painting in its exploitation of this weakness of our nature falls nothing short of witchcraft, and so do jugglery and many other such contrivances. True. And have not measuring and numbering and weighing proved to be most gracious aids to prevent the domination in our soul of the apparently greater or less or more or heavier, and to give the control to that which has reckoned and numbered or even weighed?

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν τοῦτό γε τοῦ λογιστικοῦ ἂν εἴη τοῦ ἐν ψυχῇ ἔργον
greek_tokens: alla meis outos ge o logistikos an eaw o en yuxw ergnumi outos gar oun outos de pollakis metrew kai shmainw megas atta eimi h elasswn eteros eteros h isos enantios fainw ama peri tautos nai oukoun fhmi o autos ama peri tautos enantios docazw adunatos eimi kai orqos ge fhmi
english_head: Certainly. But this surely would be the function of the part of the soul that reasons and calculates. Why, yes, of that. And often when this has measured and declares that certain things are larger or that some are smaller than the others or equal, there is at the same time an appearance of the contrary. Yes. And did we not say that it is impossible for the same thing at one time to hold contradictory opinions about the same thing?

greek_head: Τὸ παρὰ τὰ μέτρα ἄρα δοξάζον τῆς ψυχῆς τῷ κατὰ τὰ μέτρα οὐκ ἂν εἴη ταὐτόν
greek_tokens: o para o metron ara docazw o yuxazw o kata o metron ou an eaw tautos ou gar oun alla meis o metron ge kai logismos pisteuw beltistos an eaw o yuxazw tis meis o ara outos enantioomai o faulos an tis eaw en egw anagkh outos toinun diomologew boulomai elegos ostis o grafikos kai oloc o mimhtikos prosw men o alhqeia eimi o eautou ergnumi apergazomai prosw de au fronhsis eimi o
english_head: And we were right in affirming that. The part of the soul, then, that opines in contradiction of measurement could not be the same with that which conforms to it. Why, no. But, further, that which puts its trust in measurement and reckoning must be the best part of the soul. Surely. Then that which opposes it must belong to the inferior elements of the soul. Necessarily. This, then, was what I wished to have agreed upon when I said that poetry, and in general the mimetic art, produces a product that is far removed from truth in the accomplishment of its task, and associates with the part in us

greek_head: ἐν ἡμῖν προσομιλεῖ τε καὶ ἑταίρα καὶ φίλη ἐστὶν ἐπ' οὐδενὶ ὑγιεῖ οὐδ' ἀληθεῖ
greek_tokens: en egw prosomilew te kai etaira kai filos eimi epi oudeis ugihs oude alhqhs pantapasi eimi de os faulos ara faulos suggignomai faulos gennaw o mimhtikos eoika poteros eimi de egw o kata o oyis monos h kai kata o akoh os dh poihsis onomazw eoika ge fhmi kai outos mh toinun eimi de egw o eoika monos pisteuw ek o grafikos alla kai epi autos au erxomai o dianoia
english_head: that is remote from intelligence, and is its companion and friend for no sound and true purpose. By all means, said he. Mimetic art, then, is an inferior thing cohabiting with an inferior and engendering inferior offspring. It seems so. Does that, said I, hold only for vision or does it apply also to hearing and to what we call poetry? Presumably, he said, to that also. Let us not, then, trust solely to the plausible analogy from painting, but let us approach in turn

greek_head: τοῦτο ᾧ προσομιλεῖ ἡ τῆς ποιήσεως μιμητική καὶ ἴδωμεν φαῦλον ἢ σπουδαῖόν ἐστιν
greek_tokens: outos os prosomilew o o poihsis mimhtikos kai eidon faulos h spoudaios eimi alla xrh wde dh proqew prassw fhmi anqrwpos mimeomai o mimhtikos biaios h ekousios prassw kai ek o prassw h eu oiomai h kakos prassw kai en outos dh pas h lupew h xairw mh tis allos eimi para outos oudeis ara oun en apas outos omonohtikos anqrwpos diakeimai
english_head: that part of the mind to which mimetic poetry appeals and see whether it is the inferior or the nobly serious part. So we must. Let us, then, put the question thus: Mimetic poetry, we say, imitates human beings acting under compulsion or voluntarily, and as a result of their actions supposing themselves to have fared well or ill and in all this feeling either grief or joy. Did we find anything else but this? Nothing. Is a man, then, in all this

greek_head: ἢ ὥσπερ κατὰ τὴν ὄψιν ἐστασίαζεν καὶ ἐναντίας εἶχεν ἐν ἑαυτῷ δόξας ἅμα περὶ τῶν αὐτῶν οὕτω καὶ ἐν ταῖς
greek_tokens: h wsper kata o oyis stasiazw kai enantios exw en eautou doca ama peri o autos outws kai en o pracis stasiazw te kai maxomai autos eautou anamimnhskw de ostis outos ge nun oudeis deomai egw diomologew en gar o anoos logos ikanos pas outos diomologew ostis murw toioutos enantiwma ama gignomai o yuxh gemos egw orqos fhmi orqos gar eimi de egw alla o tote apoleipw nun egw
english_head: of one mind with himself, or just as in the domain of sight there was faction and strife and he held within himself contrary opinions at the same time about the same things, so also in our actions there is division and strife of the man with himself? But I recall that there is no need now of our seeking agreement on this point, for in our former discussion we were sufficiently agreed that our soul at any one moment teems with countless such self-contradictions. Rightly, he said. Yes, rightly, said I; but what we then omitted must now, I think,

greek_head: δοκεῖ ἀναγκαῖον εἶναι διεξελθεῖν Τὸ ποῖον ἔφη
greek_tokens: dokew anagkaion eimi diecerxomai o poios fhmi anhr eimi de egw epieikhs toiosde tuxh metexw uios apollumi h tis allos os peri pleistos poiew legw pou kai tote ostis radios ferw o allos panu ge nun de ge ode episkeptomai poteros oudeis axqomai h outos men adunatos metriaw de pws pros luph outws mallon fhmi o ge alhqhs
english_head: be set forth. What is that? he said. When a good and reasonable man, said I, experiences such a stroke of fortune as the loss of a son or anything else that he holds most dear, we said, I believe, then too, that he will bear it more easily than the other sort. Assuredly. But now let us consider this: Will he feel no pain, or, since that is impossible, shall we say that he will in some sort be moderate in his grief? That, he said, is rather the truth.

greek_head: Τόδε νῦν μοι περὶ αὐτοῦ εἰπέ πότερον μᾶλλον αὐτὸν οἴει τῇ λύπῃ μαχεῖσθαί τε καὶ ἀντιτείνειν ὅταν ὁρᾶται ὑπὸ
greek_tokens: ode nun egw peri autos eipon poteros mallon autos oiomai o luph maxomai te kai antiteinw otan oraw upo o omoios h otan en erhmia monos autos kaqa eautou gignomai polus pou fhmi diaferw otan oraw monow de ge oimh polus men tolmhsis fqeggomai o ei tis autos akouw aisxunw an polus de poihsis o ou an dexomai tis eidon draw outws exis fhmi oukoun o men antiteinw diakeleuomai logos kai nomos
english_head: Tell me now this about him: Do you think he will be more likely to resist and fight against his grief when he is observed by his equals or when he is in solitude alone by himself? He will be much more restrained, he said, when he is on view. But when left alone, I fancy, he will permit himself many utterances which, if heard by another, would put him to shame, and will do many things which he would not consent to have another see him doing. So it is, he said. Now is it not reason and law

greek_head: ἐστίν τὸ δὲ ἕλκον ἐπὶ τὰς λύπας αὐτὸ τὸ πάθος Ἀληθῆ
greek_tokens: eimi o de elkw epi o luph autos o paqos alhqhs enantios de agwgeus gignomai en o anqrwpos peri o autos ama duo fhmi autos anagkaion eimi pws de ou oukoun o men eteros o nomos etoimos peiqw h o nomos echgeomai pws legw pou o nomos ostis kalos ostis malista hsuxia agw en o sumfora kai mh aganaktew ws oute boulomai eimi o agaqos te kai kakos o toioutos oute eis o prosqen oudeis probainw o xalepos ferw oute tis o
english_head: that exhorts him to resist, while that which urges him to give way to his grief is the bare feeling itself? True. And where there are two opposite impulses in a man at the same time about the same thing we say that there must needs be two things in him. Of course. And is not the one prepared to follow the guidance of the law as the law leads and directs? How so? The law, I suppose, declares that it is best to keep quiet as far as possible in calamity and not to chafe and repine, because we cannot know what is really good and evil in such things and it advantages us nothing to take them hard,

greek_head: ἀνθρωπίνων ἄξιον ὂν μεγάλης σπουδῆς ὅ τε δεῖ ἐν αὐτοῖς ὅτι τάχιστα παραγίγνεσθαι ἡμῖν τούτῳ ἐμποδὼν γιγνόμενον
greek_tokens: anqrwpinos acios eimi megas spoudazw o te deomai en autos ostis taxistos paragignomai egw outos empodwn gignomai o lupew tis eimi de os legw o bouleuw eimi de egw peri o gignomai kai wsper en ptwsis kubos pros o piptw tiqhmi o eautou pragma oph o logos airew beltistos an exw alla mh prosptaiw kaqa pais exw o plhssw en o boa diatribw alla aei eqizw o yuxh ostis
english_head: and nothing in mortal life is worthy of great concern, and our grieving checks the very thing we need to come to our aid as quickly as possible in such case. What thing, he said, do you mean? To deliberate, I said, about what has happened to us, and, as it were in the fall of the dice, to determine the movements of our affairs with reference to the numbers that turn up, in the way that reason indicates would be the best, and, instead of stumbling like children, clapping one’s hands to the stricken spot and wasting the time in wailing,

greek_head: τάχιστα γίγνεσθαι πρὸς τὸ ἰᾶσθαί τε καὶ ἐπανορθοῦν τὸ πεσόν τε καὶ νοσῆσαν ἰατρικῇ θρηνῳδίαν ἀφανίζοντα
greek_tokens: taxistos gignomai pros o iaomai te kai epanorqow o piptw te kai nosew iatrikos qrhnwdia afanizw orqos goun an tis fhmi pros o tuxh outws prosferw oukoun fhmi o men beltistos outos o logismos eqelw epomai dhlos dh o de pros o anamnhsis te o paqos kai pros o odurmos agw kai aplhstos exw autos ara ou alogistos te fhmi eimi kai argos kai deilia filos fhmi men oun
english_head: ever to accustom the soul to devote itself at once to the curing of the hurt and the raising up of what has fallen, banishing threnody by therapy. That certainly, he said, would be the best way to face misfortune and deal with it. Then, we say, the best part of us is willing to conform to these precepts of reason. Obviously. And shall we not say that the part of us that leads us to dwell in memory on our suffering and impels us to lamentation, and cannot get enough of that sort of thing, is the irrational and idle part of us, the associate of cowardice? Yes, we will say that. And does not

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν τὸ μὲν πολλὴν μίμησιν καὶ ποικίλην ἔχει τὸ ἀγανακτητικόν τὸ δὲ φρόνιμόν τε καὶ ἡσύχιον ἦθος παραπλήσιον
greek_tokens: oukoun o men polus mimhsis kai poikilos exis o aganakthtikos o de fronimos te kai hsuxios hqos paraplhsios eimi aei autos eautou oute radios mimeomai oute mimeomai eupeths katamanqanw allos te kai panhguris kai pantodapos anqrwpos eis qeatron sullegw allotrios gar pou paqos o mimhsis autos gignomai
english_head: the fretful part of us present many and varied occasions for imitation, while the intelligent and temperate disposition, always remaining approximately the same, is neither easy to imitate nor to be understood when imitated, especially by a nondescript mob assembled in the theater? For the representation imitates a type

greek_head: Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν Ὁ δὴ μιμητικὸς ποιητὴς δῆλον ὅτι οὐ πρὸς τὸ τοιοῦτον
greek_tokens: pantapasi men oun o dh mimhtikos poihths dhlos ostis ou pros o toioutos o yuxazw fuw te kai o sofia autos outos areskw phgnumi ei mellw eudokimew en o polus alla pros o aganakthtikos te kai poikilos hqos dia o eumimhtos eimi dhlos oukoun dikaios an autos hdh epilambanw kai tiqhmi antistrofos autos o zwgrafos kai gar o faulos poiew pros alhqeia eoika autos kai o pros eteros toioutos
english_head: that is alien to them. By all means. And is it not obvious that the nature of the mimetic poet is not related to this better part of the soul and his cunning is not framed to please it, if he is to win favor with the multitude, but is devoted to the fretful and complicated type of character because it is easy to imitate? It is obvious. This consideration, then, makes it right for us to proceed to lay hold of him and set him down as the counterpart of the painter; for he resembles him in that his creations are inferior in respect of reality; and the fact that his appeal is to the inferior part of the soul

greek_head: ὁμιλεῖν τῆς ψυχῆς ἀλλὰ μὴ πρὸς τὸ βέλτιστον καὶ ταύτῃ ὡμοίωται καὶ οὕτως ἤδη ἂν ἐν δίκῃ οὐ παραδεχοίμεθα
greek_tokens: omilew o yuxazw alla mh pros o beltistos kai outos omoiow kai outws hdh an en dikh ou paradexomai eis mellw eunomeomai polis ostis outos egeirw o yuxazw kai trefos kai isxuros poa apollumi o logistikos wsper en polis otan tis moxqhros egkratew poa paradidwmi o polis o de xarieis fqeirw tautos kai o mimhtikos poihths fhmi kakos politeia idios ekastos o yuxw empoiew o anohtos autos
english_head: and not to the best part is another point of resemblance. And so we may at last say that we should be justified in not admitting him into a well-ordered state, because he stimulates and fosters this element in the soul, and by strengthening it tends to destroy the rational part, just as when in a state one puts bad men in power and turns the city over to them and ruins the better sort. Precisely in the same manner we shall say that the mimetic poet sets up in each individual soul a vicious constitution by fashioning phantoms far removed from reality, and by currying favor with the senseless element

greek_head: χαριζόμενον καὶ οὔτε τὰ μείζω οὔτε τὰ ἐλάττω διαγιγνώσκοντι ἀλλὰ τὰ αὐτὰ τοτὲ μὲν μεγάλα ἡγουμένῳ τοτὲ δὲ
greek_tokens: xarizomai kai oute o megas oute o elasswn diagignwskw alla o autos tote men megas hgeomai tote de mikros eidwlon eidwlopoiew o de alhqhs prosw panu afisthmi panu men oun ou mentoi pw o ge megas kathgorew autos o gar kai o epieikhs ikanos eimi lwbaomai ektos panu tis oligos pandeinos pou tis de ou mellw eiper ge draw autos akouw skopaw e gar pou beltistos egw akroaomai omhros h allos tis o tragwdopoios mimeomai
english_head: that cannot distinguish the greater from the less, but calls the same thing now one, now the other. By all means. But we have not yet brought our chief accusation against it. Its power to corrupt, with rare exceptions, even the better sort is surely the chief cause for alarm. How could it be otherwise, if it really does that? Listen and reflect. I think you know that the very best of us, when we hear Homer or some other of the makers of tragedy

greek_head: τινὰ τῶν ἡρώων ἐν πένθει ὄντα καὶ μακρὰν ῥῆσιν ἀποτείνοντα ἐν τοῖς ὀδυρμοῖς ἢ καὶ ᾄδοντάς τε καὶ κοπτομένους
greek_tokens: tis o hrws en penqos eimi kai makran reomai apoteinw en o odurmos h kai aeidw te kai koptw oistos ostis xairw te kai endidwmi egw autos epomai sumpasxw kai spoudazw epainew ws agaqos poihths os an egw ostis malista outws diaqew oida pws de ou otan de oikew tis egw khdos gignomai ennoew au ostis epi o enantios kallwpizw an dunamai hsuxia agw
english_head: imitating one of the heroes who is in grief, and is delivering a long tirade in his lamentations or chanting and beating his breast, feel pleasure, and abandon ourselves and accompany the representation with sympathy and eagerness, and we praise as an excellent poet the one who most strongly affects us in this way. I do know it, of course. But when in our own lives some affliction comes to us, you are also aware that we plume ourselves upon the opposite, on our ability to remain calm and endure,

greek_head: καὶ καρτερεῖν ὡς τοῦτο μὲν ἀνδρὸς ὄν ἐκεῖνο δὲ γυναικός ὃ τότε ἐπῃνοῦμεν
greek_tokens: kai karterew ws outos men anhr eimi ekeinos de gunh o tote epainew ennoew fhmi eimi kalos oun eimi de egw outos o epainos exis o oraw toioutos anhr oios eautou tis mh aciow eimi alla aisxunw an mh bdelussomai alla xairw te kai epainew ou ma o diw fhmi ou eulogos eoika
english_head: in the belief that this is the conduct of a man, and what we were praising in the theatre that of a woman. I do note that. Do you think, then, said I, that this praise is rightfully bestowed when, contemplating a character that we would not accept but would be ashamed of in ourselves, we do not abominate it but take pleasure and approve? No, by Zeus, he said, it does not seem reasonable.

greek_head: Ναί ἦν δ' ἐγώ εἰ ἐκείνῃ γ' αὐτὸ σκοποίης Πῇ
greek_tokens: nai eimi de egw ei ekeinos ge autos skopaw ph ei enqumeomai ostis o bia katexw tote en o oikeios sumfora kai peinaw o dakruw te kai apoduromai ikanos kai apopimplhmi fusis eimi toioutos oios outos epiqumew tote eimi outos o upo o poihths pimplhmi kai xairw o de fusis beltistos egw ate ou ikanos paideuw logos oude eqos aneimi o fulakh o
english_head: Oh yes, said I, if you would consider it in this way. In what way? If you would reflect that the part of the soul that in the former case, in our own misfortunes, was forcibly restrained, and that has hungered for tears and a good cry and satisfaction, because it is its nature to desire these things, is the element in us that the poets satisfy and delight, and that the best element in our nature, since it has never been properly educated by reason or even by habit, then relaxes its guard over the plaintive part,

greek_head: θρηνώδους τούτου ἅτε ἀλλότρια πάθη θεωροῦν καὶ ἑαυτῷ οὐδὲν αἰσχρὸν ὂν εἰ ἄλλος ἀνὴρ ἀγαθὸς φάσκων εἶναι ἀκαίρως
greek_tokens: qrhnwdhs outos ate allotrios paqh qewrew kai eautou oudeis aisxros eimi ei allos anhr agaqos faskw eimi akairos penqew outos epainew kai eleaw alla ekeinos kerdainw hgeomai o hdona kai ou an dexomai autos sterew katafronew oloc o poihma logizomai gar oimh oligos tis meteimi ostis apolauw anagkh apo o allotrios eis o oikeios trefw gar en ekeinos isxuros o eleeinos ou radios en o eautou paqos katexw
english_head: inasmuch as this is contemplating the woes of others and it is no shame to it to praise and pity another who, claiming to be a good man, abandons himself to excess in his grief; but it thinks this vicarious pleasure is so much clear gain, and would not consent to forfeit it by disdaining the poem altogether. That is, I think, because few are capable of reflecting that what we enjoy in others will inevitably react upon ourselves. For after feeding fat the emotion of pity there, it is not easy to restrain it in our own sufferings.

greek_head: Ἀληθέστατα ἔφη Ἆρ' οὖν οὐχ ὁ αὐτὸς λόγος καὶ περὶ τοῦ γελοίου ὅτι
greek_tokens: alhqhs fhmi ara oun ou o autos logos kai peri o geloios ostis os autos aisxunw gelwtopoiew en mimhsis de kwmwdikos h kai idios akouw sfodra xairw kai mh misew ws ponhros tautos poiew osper en o eleos o gar o logos au katexw en sautou boulomai gelwtopoiew fobew doca bwmoloxia tote au anihmi kai ekei neanikos poiew lanqanw pollakis en o oikew ekferw wste kwmwdopoios gignomai kai mala fhmi
english_head: Most true, he said. Does not the same principle apply to the laughable, namely,that if in comic representations, or for that matter in private talk, you take intense pleasure in buffooneries that you would blush to practise yourself, and do not detest them as base, you are doing the same thing as in the case of the pathetic? For here again what your reason, for fear of the reputation of buffoonery, restrained in yourself when it fain would play the clown, you release in turn, and so, fostering its youthful impudence, let yourself go so far that often ere you are aware you become yourself

greek_head: Καὶ περὶ ἀφροδισίων δὴ καὶ θυμοῦ καὶ περὶ πάντων τῶν ἐπιθυμητικῶν τε καὶ λυπηρῶν καὶ ἡδέων ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ ἃ δή
greek_tokens: kai peri afrodisios dh kai qumos kai peri pas o epiqumhtikos te kai luphros kai hdus en o yuxw o dh fhmi paomai prassw egw epomai ostis toioutos egw o poihtikos mimhsis ergazomai trefos gar outos ardw deon auxmaw kai arxw egw kaqisthmi deon arxw autos ina beltiwn te kai eudaimwn anti xeirwn kai aqlios gignomai ou exw allos fainw eimi de os
english_head: a comedian in private. Yes, indeed, he said. And so in regard to the emotions of sex and anger, and all the appetites and pains and pleasures of the soul which we say accompany all our actions, the effect of poetic imitation is the same. For it waters and fosters these feelings when what we ought to do is to dry them up, and it establishes them as our rulers when they ought to be ruled, to the end that we may be better and happier men instead of worse and more miserable. I cannot deny it, said he.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν εἶπον ὦ Γλαύκων ὅταν Ὁμήρου ἐπαινέταις ἐντύχῃς λέγουσιν ὡς τὴν Ἑλλάδα πεπαίδευκεν οὗτος ὁ
greek_tokens: oukoun eipon eimi glauc otan omhros epaineths entugxanw legw ws o ellas paideuw outos o poihths kai pros dioikhsis te kai paideia o anqrwpinos pragma acios analambanw manqanw te kai kata outos o poihths pas o eautou bios kataskeuazw zaw
english_head: Then, Glaucon, said I, when you meet encomiasts of Homer who tell us that this poet has been the educator of Hellas, and that for the conduct and refinement of human life he is worthy of our study and devotion, and that we should order our entire lives by the guidance of this poet,

greek_head: φιλεῖν μὲν χρὴ καὶ ἀσπάζεσθαι ὡς ὄντας βελτίστους εἰς ὅσον δύνανται καὶ συγχωρεῖν Ὅμηρον ποιητικώτατον εἶναι
greek_tokens: filew men xrh kai aspazomai ws eimi beltistos eis osos dunamai kai sugxwrew omhros poihtikos eimi kai proteros o tragwdopoios oida de ostis osos monos umnos qeaw kai egkwmios o agaqos poihsis paradekteos eis polis ei de o hdunw mousa paradexomai en melos h epos hdona su kai luph en o polis basileuw anti nomos te kai o koinh aei dokew eimi beltistos logos alhqhs fhmi
english_head: we must love and salute them as doing the best they can, and concede to them that Homer is the most poetic of poets and the first of tragedians, but we must know the truth, that we can admit no poetry into our city save only hymns to the gods and the praises of good men. For if you grant admission to the honeyed muse in lyric or epic, pleasure and pain will be lords of your city instead of law and that which shall from time to time have approved itself to the general reason as the best. Most true, he said.

greek_head: Ταῦτα δή ἔφην ἀπολελογήσθω ἡμῖν ἀναμνησθεῖσιν περὶ ποιήσεως ὅτι εἰκότως ἄρα τότε αὐτὴν ἐκ τῆς πόλεως ἀπεστέλλομεν
greek_tokens: outos dh fhmi apologeomai egw anamimnhskw peri poihsis ostis eoikotws ara tote autos ek o polis apostellw toioutos eimi o gar logos egw airew proseipon de autos mh kai tis sklhroths egw kai agroikia katagignwskw ostis palaios men tis diafora filosofia te kai poihtikos kai gar o lakeruza pros despoths kuw ekeinos kraugazw kai megas en afrwn keneagoria
english_head: Let us, then, conclude our return to the topic of poetry and our apology, and affirm that we really had good grounds then for dismissing her from our city, since such was her character. For reason constrained us. And let us further say to her, lest she condemn us for harshness and rusticity, that there is from of old a quarrel between philosophy and poetry. For such expressions as the yelping hound barking at her master and mighty in the idle babble Unknown

greek_head: καὶ ὁ τῶν διασόφων ὄχλος κρατῶν καὶ οἱ λεπτῶς μεριμνῶντες ὅτι ἄρα πένονται
greek_tokens: kai o o diasofos oxlos kras kai e leptos merimnaw ostis ara penomai kai allos murios shmeion palaios enantiwsis outos omws de erw ostis egw ge ei tis exw logos eipon o pros hdona poihtikos kai o mimhsis ws xrh autos eimi en polis eunomeomai asmenos an katadexomai ws sunoida ge egw autos khlew upo autos alla gar o dokew alhqhs ou osios prodidwmi eimi gar eimi filos ou khlew upo autos
english_head: of fools, and the mob that masters those who are too wise for their own good, Unknown and the subtle thinkers who reason that after all they are poor, and countless others are tokens of this ancient enmity. But nevertheless let it be declared that, if the mimetic and dulcet poetry can show any reason for her existence in a well-governed state, we would gladly admit her, since we ourselves are very conscious of her spell. But all the same it would be impious to betray what we believe to be the truth.

greek_head: καὶ σύ καὶ μάλιστα ὅταν δι' Ὁμήρου θεωρῇς αὐτήν Πολύ γε
greek_tokens: kai su kai malista otan dia omhros qewrew autos polus ge oukoun dikaios eimi outws kateimi apologeomai en melos h tis allos metron panu men oun didwmi de ge pou an kai o prostaths autos osos mh poihtikos filopoihths de aneu metron logos uper autos eipon ws ou monos hdus alla kai wfelimos pros o politeia kai o bios o anqrwpinos eimi kai eumenhs akouw
english_head: Is not that so, friend? Do not you yourself feel her magic and especially when Homer is her interpreter? Greatly. Then may she not justly return from this exile after she has pleaded her defence, whether in lyric or other measure? By all means. And we would allow her advocates who are not poets but lovers of poetry to plead her cause in prose without metre, and show that she is not only delightful but beneficial to orderly government and all the life of man. And we shall listen benevolently,

greek_head: κερδανοῦμεν γάρ που ἐὰν μὴ μόνον ἡδεῖα φανῇ ἀλλὰ καὶ ὠφελίμη
greek_tokens: kerdainw gar pou ean mh monos hdus fainw alla kai wfelimos pws de ou mellw fhmi kerdainw ei de ge mh eimi filos etairos wsper e pote tis eramai ean hgeomai mh wfelimos eimi o erws bia men omws de apexw kai egw outws dia o eggignomai men erws o toioutos poihsis upo o o kalh politeia
english_head: for it will be clear gain for us if it can be shown that she bestows not only pleasure but benefit. How could we help being the gainers? said he. But if not, my friend, even as men who have fallen in love, if they think that the love is not good for them, hard though it be, nevertheless refrain, so we, owing to the love of this kind of poetry inbred in us by our education in these fine polities of ours,

greek_head: τροφῆς εὖνοι μὲν ἐσόμεθα φανῆναι αὐτὴν ὡς βελτίστην καὶ ἀληθεστάτην ἕως δ' ἂν μὴ οἵα τ' ᾖ ἀπολογήσασθαι
greek_tokens: trefw eunous men eimi fainw autos ws beltistos kai alhqhs ews de an mh oios su eimi apologeomai akrosaomeq' autos epaeidw egw autos outos o logos os legw kai outos o epwdh eulabeomai palin empitnw eis o paidikon te kai o o polus erws aeidw de oun ws ou spoudasteos epi o toioutos poihsis ws alhqeia te aptw kai spoudaios alla eulabhteon
english_head: will gladly have the best possible case made out for her goodness and truth, but so long as she is unable to make good her defence we shall chant over to ourselves as we listen the reasons that we have given as a counter-charm to her spell, to preserve us from slipping back into the childish loves of the multitude; for we have come to see that we must not take such poetry seriously as a serious thing that lays hold on truth, but that he who lends an ear to it must be on his guard

greek_head: αὐτὴν ὂν τῷ ἀκροωμένῳ περὶ τῆς ἐν αὑτῷ πολιτείας δεδιότι καὶ νομιστέα ἅπερ εἰρήκαμεν περὶ ποιήσεως
greek_tokens: autos eimi o akroaomai peri o en eautou politeia deidw kai nomisteos o erw peri poihsis pantapasi eimi de os sumfhmi megas gar fhmi o agwn eimi filos glauc megas ou osos dokew o xrhstos h kakos gignomai wste oute timaw epairw oute xrhma oute arxh oudeis oude ge poihtikos acios amelew dikaiosunh te kai o allos aretaw sumfhmi su fhmi ek os dierxomai oimh de kai allos ostis
english_head: fearing for the polity in his soul and must believe what we have said about poetry. By all means, he said, I concur. Yes, for great is the struggle, I said, dear Glaucon, a far greater contest than we think it, that determines whether a man prove good or bad, so that not the lure of honor or wealth or any office, no, nor of poetry either, should incite us to be careless of righteousness and all excellence. I agree with you, he replied, in view of what we have set forth, and I think that anyone else would do so too.

greek_head: Καὶ μήν ἦν δ' ἐγώ τά γε μέγιστα ἐπίχειρα ἀρετῆς καὶ προκείμενα ἆθλα οὐ διεληλύθαμεν
greek_tokens: kai meis eimi de egw o ge megas epixeira aretaw kai prokeimai aqlon ou dierxomai amhxanos tis fhmi legw megeqos ei o erw megas eimi allos tis de an eimi de egw en ge oligos xronos megas gignomai pas gar outos ge o ek pais mexri presbuths xronos pros pas oligos pou tis an eaw oudeis men oun fhmi tis oun oiomai aqanatos pragma uper tosoutos dew
english_head: And yet, said I, the greatest rewards of virtue and the prizes proposed for her we have not set forth. You must have in mind an inconceivable magnitude, he replied, if there are other things greater than those of which we have spoken.? For surely the whole time from the boy to the old man would be small compared with all time. Nay, it is nothing, he said. What then? Do you think that an immortal thing ought to be seriously concerned for such a little time,

greek_head: χρόνου ἐσπουδακέναι ἀλλ' οὐχ ὑπὲρ τοῦ παντός Οἶμαι ἔγωγ' ἔφη ἀλλὰ τί τοῦτο λέγεις
