greek_tokens: xronos spoudazw alla ou uper o pas oimh egw fhmi alla tis outos legw ou aisqanomai eimi de egw ostis aqanatos egw o yuxh kai oudepote apollumi kai os emblepw egw kai qaumazw eipon ma diw ou egw su de outos exis legw ei mh adikew ge fhmi oimh de kai su oudeis gar xalepos egw fhmi sew de an hdews akouw o ou xalepos outos akouw an eimi de egw legos monos fhmi agaqos tis eipon kai kakos kalew egw
english_head: and not rather for all time? I think so, he said; but what is this that you have in mind? Have you never perceived, said I, that our soul is immortal and never perishes? And he, looking me full in the face in amazement, said, No, by Zeus, not I; but are you able to declare this? I certainly ought to be, said I, and I think you too can, for it is nothing hard. It is for me, he said; and I would gladly hear from you this thing that is not hard. Listen, said I. Just speak on, he replied. You speak of good

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ὥσπερ ἐγὼ περὶ αὐτῶν διανοῇ Τὸ ποῖον
greek_tokens: ara oun wsper egw peri autos dianoeomai o poios o men apollumi kai diafqeirw pas o kakos eimi o de swzw kai wfelew o agaqos egw fhmi tis de kakos ekastos tis kai agaqos legw oios ofqalmos
english_head: and evil, do you not? I do. Is your notion of them the same as mine? What is it? That which destroys and corrupts in every case is the evil; that which preserves and benefits is the good. Yes, I think so, he said. How about this: Do you say that there is for everything its special good and evil,

greek_head: ὀφθαλμίαν καὶ σύμπαντι τῷ σώματι νόσον σίτῳ τε ἐρυσίβην σηπεδόνα τε ξύλοις χαλκῷ δὲ καὶ σιδήρῳ ἰόν
greek_tokens: ofqalmia kai sumpanti o swma nosos sitos te erusibh shpedwn te culon xalkeos de kai sidhron eimi kai osper legos sxedon pas sumfuton ekastos kakos te kai noshma egw fhmi oukoun otan tis tis outos prosgignomai ponhros te poiew os prosgignomai kai teleutaw oloc dialuw kai apollumi pws gar ou o sumfuton ara kakos ekastos kai o ponhria ekastos apollumi h ei mh outos apollumi ou an allos ge autos eti
english_head: as for example for the eyes ophthalmia, for the entire body disease, for grain mildew, rotting for wood, rust for bronze and iron, and, as I say, for practically everything its congenital evil and disease? I do, he said. Then when one of these evils comes to anything does it not make the thing to which it attaches itself bad, and finally disintegrate and destroy it? Of course. Then the congenital evil of each thing and its own vice destroys it, or if that is not going to destroy it, nothing else

greek_head: διαφθείρειεν οὐ γὰρ τό γε ἀγαθὸν μή ποτέ τι ἀπολέσῃ οὐδὲ αὖ τὸ μήτε κακὸν μήτε ἀγαθόν
greek_tokens: diafqeirw ou gar o ge agaqos mh pote tis apollumi oude au o mhte kakos mhte agaqos pws gar an fhmi ean ara tis euriskw o eimi os eimi men kakos o poiew autos moxqhros outos mentoi ou oios te autos luw apollumi ou hdh eimi ostis o fuw outws oleqros ou eimi outws fhmi eoika tis oun eimi de egw yuxw ara ou eimi o poiew autos autos kakos kai mala fhmi o nundi dieimi pas adikia te kai
english_head: remains that could; for obviously the good will never destroy anything, nor yet again will that which is neutral and neither good nor evil. How could it? he said. If, then, we discover anything that has an evil which vitiates it, yet is not able to dissolve and destroy it, shall we not thereupon know that of a thing so constituted there can be no destruction? That seems likely, he said. Well, then, said I, has not the soul something that makes it evil? Indeed it has, he said, all the things that we were just now enumerating,

greek_head: ἀκολασία καὶ δειλία καὶ ἀμαθία Ἦ οὖν τι τούτων αὐτὴν διαλύει τε καὶ ἀπόλλυσι καὶ
greek_tokens: akolasia kai deilia kai amaqia eimi oun tis outos autos dialuw te kai apollumi kai ennoew mh ecapataw oiomai o adikos anqrwpos kai anohtos otan lambanw adikew tote apollumi upo o adikia ponhria eimi yuxazw alla wde poiew wsper swma o swma ponhria nosos eimi thkw kai diollumi kai agos eis o mhde swma eimi kai o nundi legw
english_head: injustice and licentiousness and cowardice and ignorance. Does any one of these things dissolve and destroy it? And reflect, lest we be misled by supposing that when an unjust and foolish man is taken in his injustice he is then destroyed by the injustice, which is the vice of soul. But conceive it thus: Just as the vice of body which is disease wastes and destroys it so that it no longer is a body at all, in like manner in all the examples of which we spoke it is the specific evil which,

greek_head: ἅπαντα ὑπὸ τῆς οἰκείας κακίας τῷ προσκαθῆσθαι καὶ ἐνεῖναι διαφθειρούσης εἰς τὸ μὴ εἶναι ἀφικνεῖται οὐχ οὕτω
greek_tokens: apas upo o oikeios kakia o proskaqhmai kai enihmi diafqeirw eis o mh eimi afikneomai ou outws nai eimi dh kai yuxh kata o autos tropos skopaw ara eneimi en autos adikia kai o allos kakia o enihmi kai proskaqhmai fqeirw autos kai marainw ews an eis qanatos agw o swma xwrisis oudamos fhmi outos ge alla mentoi ekeinos ge alogos eimi de egw o men allos ponhria apollumi tis o de eautou mh alogos
english_head: by attaching itself to the thing and dwelling in it with power to corrupt, reduces it to nonentity. Is not that so? Yes. Come, then, and consider the soul in the same way. Do injustice and other wickedness dwelling in it, by their indwelling and attachment to it, corrupt and wither it till they bring it to death and separate it from the body? They certainly do not do that, he said. But surely, said I, it is unreasonable to suppose that the vice of something else destroys a thing while its own does not. Yes, unreasonable. For observe, Glaucon,

greek_head: Ἐννόει γάρ ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ Γλαύκων ὅτι οὐδ' ὑπὸ τῆς τῶν σιτίων πονηρίας ἣ ἂν ᾖ αὐτῶν ἐκείνων εἴτε παλαιότης
greek_tokens: ennoew gar eimi de egw eimi glauc ostis oude upo o o siteomai ponhria o an eimi autos ekeinos eite palaioths eite saproths eite ostis eimi ou oiomai dew swma apollumi alla ean men empoiew o autos ponhria o siteomai o swma swma moxqhria fhmi autos dia ekeinos upo o eautou kakia nosos eimi apollumi upo
english_head: said I, that we do not think it proper to say of the body either that it is destroyed by the badness of foods themselves, whether it be staleness or rottenness or whatever it is; but when the badness of the foods themselves engenders in the body the defect of body, then we shall say that it is destroyed owing to these foods, but by its own vice, which is disease.

greek_head: δὲ σιτίων πονηρίας ἄλλων ὄντων ἄλλο ὂν τὸ σῶμα ὑπ' ἀλλοτρίου κακοῦ μὴ ἐμποιήσαντος τὸ ἔμφυτον κακόν οὐδέποτε
greek_tokens: de siteomai ponhria allos eimi allos eimi o swma upo allotrios kakos mh empoiew o emfutos kakos oudepote aciow diafqeirw orqos au fhmi legw kata o autos toinun logos eimi de egw ean mh swma ponhria yuxw yuxazw ponhria empoiew mh pote aciaw upo allotrios kakos aneu o idios ponhria yuxh apollumi o eteros kakazw eteros exis gar fhmi logos h toinun outos ecelegxw ostis ou kalos legw h
english_head: But the body being one thing and the foods something else, we shall never expect the body to be destroyed by their badness, that is by an alien evil that has not produced in it the evil that belongs to it by nature. You are entirely right, he replied. On the same principle, said I, if the badness of the body does not produce in the soul the soul’s badness we shall never expect the soul to be destroyed by an alien evil apart from its own defect—one thing, that is, by the evil of another. That is reasonable, he said. Either, then, we must refute this

greek_head: ἕως ἂν ᾖ ἀνέλεγκτα μή ποτε φῶμεν ὑπὸ πυρετοῦ μηδ' αὖ ὑπ' ἄλλης νόσου μηδ' αὖ ὑπὸ σφαγῆς μηδ' εἴ τις ὅτι
greek_tokens: ews an eimi anelegktos mh pote fhmi upo puretew mhde au upo allos nosos mhde au upo sfageus mhde ei tis ostis mikros oloc o swma katatemnw eneka outos mhdeis mallon pote yuxh apollumi prin an tis apodeicis ws dia outos o paqhma o swma autos ekeinos adikos kai anosios gignomai allotrios de kakos en allos gignomai o de idios ekastos mh eggignomai
english_head: and show that we are mistaken, or, so long as it remains unrefuted, we must never say that by fever or any other disease, or yet by the knife at the throat or the chopping to bits of the entire body, there is any more likelihood of the soul perishing because of these things, until it is proved that owing to these affections of the body the soul itself becomes more unjust and unholy. But when an evil of something else occurs in a different thing and the evil that belongs to the thing is not engendered in it,

greek_head: μήτε ψυχὴν μήτε ἄλλο μηδὲν ἐῶμεν φάναι τινὰ ἀπόλλυσθαι
greek_tokens: mhte yuxh mhte allos mhdeis eaw fainw tis apollumi alla mentoi fhmi outos ge oudeis pote deicis ws o apoqnhskw adikos o yuxh dia o qanatos gignomai ean de ge tis fhmi egw omose o logos tolmaw eimi kai legw ws ponhros kai adikos gignomai o apoqnhskw ina dh mh anagkazw aqanatos o yuxh omologew aciow pou ei alhqhs legw o outos legos o adikia eimi qanasimos o exw wsper nosos kai upo
english_head: we must not suffer it to be said that the soul or anything else is in this way destroyed. But you may be sure, he said, that nobody will ever prove this, that the souls of the dying are made more unjust by death. But if anyone, said I, dares to come to grips with the argument and say, in order to avoid being forced to admit the soul’s immortality, that a dying man does become more wicked and unjust, we will postulate that, if what he says is true, injustice must be fatal

greek_head: αὐτοῦ τοῦ ἀποκτεινύντος τῇ ἑαυτοῦ φύσει ἀποθνῄσκειν τοὺς λαμβάνοντας αὐτό τοὺς μὲν μάλιστα θᾶττον τοὺς δ'
greek_tokens: autos o apokteinw o eautou fusis apoqnhskw o lambanw autos o men malista qasswn o de hsswn sxolaios alla mh wsper nun dia outos upo allos dikh epitiqhmi apoqnhskw e adikos ma diw eimi de os ou ara pandeinos fainw o adikia ei qanasimos eimi o lambanw apallagh gar an eaw kakh alla mallon oimh autos fainw pas enantion
english_head: to its possessor as if it were a disease, and that those who catch it die because it kills them by its own inherent nature, those who have most of it quickest, and those who have less more slowly, and not, as now in fact happens, that the unjust die owing to this but by the action of others who inflict the penalty. Nay, by Zeus, he said, injustice will not appear a very terrible thing after all if it is going to be fatal to its possessor, for that would be a release from all troubles. But I rather think it will prove to be quite the contrary,

greek_head: τοὺς ἄλλους ἀποκτεινῦσαν εἴπερ οἷόν τε τὸν δ' ἔχοντα καὶ μάλα ζωτικὸν παρέχουσαν καὶ πρός γ' ἔτι τῷ ζωτικῷ
greek_tokens: o allos apokteinw eiper oios te o de exw kai mala zwtikos parexw kai pros ge eti o zwtikos agrupnos outws prosw pou ws eoika skhnaw o qanasimos eimi kalos eimi de egw legw opote gar dh mh ikanos o ge oikeios ponhria kai o oikew kakos apokteinw kai apollumi yuxh sxolazw o ge epi allos oleqros tassw kakos yuxh h tis allos apollumi plhn epi os tassw sxolazw ge fhmi os ge o eoika oukoun opote mhde ufh eis apollumi kakos mhte
english_head: something that kills others when it can, but renders its possessor very lively indeed, and not only lively but wakeful, so far, I ween, does it dwell from deadliness. You say well, I replied; for when the natural vice and the evil proper to it cannot kill and destroy the soul, still less will the evil appointed for the destruction of another thing destroy the soul or anything else, except that for which it is appointed. Still less indeed, he said, in all probability. Then since it is not destroyed by any evil whatever,

greek_head: οἰκείου μήτε ἀλλοτρίου δῆλον ὅτι ἀνάγκη αὐτὸ ἀεὶ ὂν εἶναι εἰ δ' ἀεὶ ὄν ἀθάνατον
greek_tokens: oikew mhte allotrios dhlos ostis anagkh autos aei eimi eimi ei de aei eimi aqanatos anagkh fhmi outos men toinun eimi de egw outws exw ei de exis ennoew ostis aei an eien o autos oute gar an pou elasswn gignomai mhdeis apollumi oute au plews ei gar otioun o aqanatos pleos gignomai oistos ostis ek o qnhtos an gignomai kai pas an eaw teleutaw aqanatos alhqhs legw alla eimi de egw mhte outos oiomai o gar logos ou
english_head: either its own or alien, it is evident that it must necessarily exist always, and that if it always exists it is immortal. Necessarily, he said. Let this, then, I said, be assumed to be so. But if it is so, you will observe that these souls must always be the same. For if none perishes they could not, I suppose, become fewer nor yet more numerous. For if any class of immortal things increased you are aware that its increase would come from the mortal and all things would end by becoming immortal. You say truly. But, said I, we must not suppose this,

greek_head: ἐάσει μήτε γε αὖ τῇ ἀληθεστάτῃ φύσει τοιοῦτον εἶναι ψυχήν ὥστε πολλῆς ποικιλίας καὶ ἀνομοιότητός τε καὶ
greek_tokens: eaw mhte ge au o alhqhs fusis toioutos eimi yuxh wste polus poikilia kai anomoioths te kai diafora gemw autos pros eautou pws legw fhmi ou radios eimi de egw aidios eimi sunqetos te ek polus kai mh o kalos xraw sunqesis ws nun egw fainw o yuxh oukoun eoika ge ostis men toinun aqanatos yuxh kai o arti logos kai e allos anagkazw an oios de eimi o alhqeia ou lwbaomai
english_head: for reason will not suffer it nor yet must we think that in its truest nature the soul is the kind of thing that teems with infinite diversity and unlikeness and contradiction in and with itself. How am I to understand that? he said. It is not easy, said I, for a thing to be immortal that is composed of many elements not put together in the best way, as now appeared to us to be the case with the soul. It is not likely. Well, then, that the soul is immortal our recent argument and our other proofs would constrain us to admit. But to know its true nature

greek_head: δεῖ αὐτὸ θεάσασθαι ὑπό τε τῆς τοῦ σώματος κοινωνίας καὶ ἄλλων κακῶν ὥσπερ νῦν ἡμεῖς θεώμεθα ἀλλ' οἷόν
greek_tokens: deomai autos qeaomai upo te o o swma koinwnia kai allos kakh wsper nun egw qew alla oios eimi kaqaros gignomai toioutos ikanos logismos diaqeateon kai polus ge kallion autos eurhsis kai enarghs dikaiosunh te kai adikia dioraw kai pas o nun dierxomai nun de eipon men alhqhs peri autos oios en o pareimi fainw qeaomai mentoi diakeimai autos wsper e o
english_head: we must view it not marred by communion with the body and other miseries as we now contemplate it, but consider adequately in the light of reason what it is when it is purified, and then you will find it to be a far more beautiful thing and will more clearly distinguish justice and injustice and all the matters that we have now discussed. But though we have stated the truth of its present appearance, its condition as we have now contemplated it

greek_head: θαλάττιον Γλαῦκον ὁρῶντες οὐκ ἂν ἔτι ῥᾳδίως αὐτοῦ ἴδοιεν τὴν ἀρχαίαν φύσιν ὑπὸ τοῦ τά τε παλαιὰ τοῦ σώματος
greek_tokens: qalassios glaukos oraw ou an eti radios autos eidon o arxaios fus upo o o te palaios o swma mera o men ekklaw o de suntribw kai pantws lwbaomai upo o kuma allos de prosfuw ostreon te kai fukia kai petra wste pas mallon qhrion eoika h oios eimi fusis outws kai o yuxh egw qew diakeimai upo murw kakh alla deomai eimi glauc ekeise blepw poi eimi de os
english_head: resembles that of the sea-god Glaucus whose first nature can hardly be made out by those who catch glimpses of him, because the original members of his body are broken off and mutilated and crushed and in every way marred by the waves, and other parts have attached themselves to him, accretions of shells and sea-weed and rocks, so that he is more like any wild creature than what he was by nature—even such, I say, is our vision of the soul marred by countless evils. But we must look elsewhere, Glaucon. Where? said he. To its love of wisdom.

greek_head: Εἰς τὴν φιλοσοφίαν αὐτῆς καὶ ἐννοεῖν ὧν ἅπτεται καὶ οἵων ἐφίεται ὁμιλιῶν ὡς συγγενὴς οὖσα τῷ τε θείῳ καὶ
greek_tokens: eis o filosofia autos kai ennoew os aptw kai oios efihmi omilia ws suggenhs eimi o te qeios kai aqanatos kai o aei eimi kai oios an gignomai o toioutos pas efepw kai upo outos o ormaw ekkomizw ek o pontos en os nun eimi kai perikrouw petra
english_head: And we must note the things of which it has apprehensions, and the associations for which it yearns, as being itself akin to the divine and the immortal and to eternal being, and so consider what it might be if it followed the gleam unreservedly and were raised by this impulse out of the depths of this sea in which it is now sunk, and were cleansed and scraped free of the rocks and barnacles which,

greek_head: τε καὶ ὄστρεα ἃ νῦν αὐτῇ ἅτε γῆν ἑστιωμένῃ γεηρὰ καὶ πετρώδη πολλὰ καὶ ἄγρια περιπέφυκεν ὑπὸ τῶν εὐδαιμόνων
greek_tokens: te kai ostreon o nun autos ate gaia estiaw gehros kai petrwdhs polus kai agrios perifuomai upo o eudaimwn legw estiasis kai tote an tis eidon autos o alhqhs fus eite polueidhs eite monoeidhs eite oph exis kai opws nun de o en o anqrwpinos bios paqh te kai eidos ws oimh epieikhs autos dierxomai pantapasi men oun fhmi oukoun eimi de egw o te allos apoluw en o logos
english_head: because it now feasts on earth, cling to it in wild profusion of earthy and stony accretion by reason of these feastings that are accounted happy. And then one might see whether in its real nature it is manifold or single in its simplicity, or what is the truth about it and how. But for the present we have, I think, fairly well described its sufferings and the forms it assumes in this human life of ours. We certainly have, he said. Then, said I, we have met all the other demands

greek_head: καὶ οὐ τοὺς μισθοὺς οὐδὲ τὰς δόξας δικαιοσύνης ἐπῃνέκαμεν ὥσπερ Ἡσίοδόν τε καὶ Ὅμηρον ὑμεῖς ἔφατε ἀλλ' αὐτὸ
greek_tokens: kai ou o misqos oude o doca dikaiosunh epainew wsper hsiodos te kai omhros su fhmi alla autos dikaiosunh autos yuxw ariston euriskw kai poihteos eimi autos o dikaios ean exis o gughs daktulion ean mh kai pros toioutos daktulion o aidhs kunaw alhqhs fhmi legw ara oun eimi de egw eimi glauc nun hdh anepifqonos eimi pros ekeinos kai o misqos o dikaiosunh kai o
english_head: of the argument, and we have not invoked the rewards and reputes of justice as you said Homer and Hesiod do, but we have proved that justice in itself is the best thing for the soul itself, and that the soul ought to do justice whether it possess the ring of Gyges or not, or the helmet of Hades to boot. Most true, he said. Then, said I, Glaucon, there can no longer be any objection, can there, to our assigning to justice and

greek_head: ἄλλῃ ἀρετῇ ἀποδοῦναι ὅσους τε καὶ οἵους τῇ ψυχῇ παρέχει παρ' ἀνθρώπων τε καὶ θεῶν ζῶντός τε ἔτι τοῦ ἀνθρώπου
greek_tokens: allh aretaw apodidwmi osos te kai oios o yuxw paraxew para anqrwpos te kai qea zaw te eti o anqrwpos kai epeidan teleutaw pantapasi men oun eimi de os ara oun apodidwmi egw o daneizw en o logos tis malista didwmi su o dikaios dokew adikos eimi kai o adikos dikaios su gar aitew kan ei mh dunatos eaw outos lanqanw kai qeos kai anqrwpos omws doteos eimi o logos eneka ina autos dikaiosunh pros adikia autos
english_head: virtue generally, in addition, all the various rewards and wages that they bring to the soul from men and gods, both while the man still lives and, after his death? There certainly can be none, he said. Will you, then, return to me what you borrowed in the argument? What, pray? I granted to you that the just man should seem and be thought to be unjust and the unjust just; for you thought that, even if the concealment of these things from gods and men was an impossibility in fact, nevertheless, it ought to be conceded for the sake of the argument, in order that the decision might be made

greek_head: κριθείη ἢ οὐ μνημονεύεις Ἀδικοίην μεντἄν ἔφη εἰ μή
greek_tokens: krinw h ou mnhmoneuw adikew mentoi fhmi ei mh epei toinun eimi de egw krinw eimi palin apaitew uper dikaiosunh wsper exis doca kai para qea kai para anqrwpos kai egw omologew peri autos dokew outws ina kai o nikhthrios komizw apo o dokew ktaomai o didwmi o exw autos epei kai o apo o eimi agaqos didwmi fainw kai ou ecapataw o o eimi lambanw autos
english_head: between absolute justice and absolute injustice. Or do you not remember? It would be unjust of me, he said, if I did not. Well, then, now that they have been compared and judged, I demand back from you in behalf of justice the repute that she in fact enjoys from gods and men, and I ask that we admit that she is thus esteemed in order that she may gather in the prizes which she wins from the seeming and bestows on her possessors, since she has been proved to bestow the blessings that come from the reality and not to deceive those who truly seek and win her. That is a just demand, he said.

greek_head: Δίκαια ἔφη αἰτῇ Οὐκοῦν ἦν δ' ἐγώ πρῶτον μὲν τοῦτο ἀποδώσετε ὅτι
greek_tokens: dikaios fhmi aitew oukoun eimi de egw proteros men outos apodidwmi ostis qeos ge ou lanqanw ekateros autos oios eimi apodidwmi fhmi ei de mh lanqanw o men qeofilhs an eaw o de qeomishs wsper kai kata arxh omologew eimi outos o de qeofilhs ou omologew osos ge apo qea
english_head: Then, said I, will not the first of these restorations be that the gods certainly are not unaware of the true character of each of the two, the just and the unjust? We will restore that, he said. And if they are not concealed, the one will be dear to the gods and the other hateful to them, as we agreed in the beginning. That is so. And shall we not agree that all things that come from the gods

greek_head: γίγνεται πάντα γίγνεσθαι ὡς οἷόν τε ἄριστα εἰ μή τι ἀναγκαῖον αὐτῷ κακὸν ἐκ προτέρας ἁμαρτίας ὑπῆρχεν
greek_tokens: gignomai pas gignomai ws oios te ariston ei mh tis anagkaion autos kakos ek proteros amartia uparxw panu men oun outws ara upolhpteon peri o dikaios anhr ean en penia gignomai ean en nosos h tis allos o dokew kakh ws outos outos eis agaqos tis teleutaw zaw h kai apoqnhskw ou gar dh upo ge qea pote amelew os an proqumeomai eqelw dikaios gignomai kai epithdeuw
english_head: work together for the best for him that is dear to the gods, apart from the inevitable evil caused by sin in a former life? By all means. This, then, must be our conviction about the just man, that whether he fall into poverty or disease or any other supposed evil, for him all these things will finally prove good, both in life and in death. For by the gods assuredly that man will never be neglected who is willing and eager to be righteous, and by the practice of virtue to be likened unto god

greek_head: ἀρετὴν εἰς ὅσον δυνατὸν ἀνθρώπῳ ὁμοιοῦσθαι θεῷ Εἰκός γ' ἔφη τὸν τοιοῦτον μὴ ἀμελεῖσθαι ὑπὸ τοῦ
greek_tokens: areth eis osos dunatos anqrwpos omoiow qeaw eoika ge fhmi o toioutos mh amelew upo o omoios oukoun peri o adikos enantios outos deomai dianoeomai sfodra ge o men dh para qea toioutos atta an eaw nikhthrios o dikaios kata goun emos doca fhmi tis de eimi de egw para anqrwpos ara ou wde exis ei deomai o eimi tiqhmi ou e men deinos te kai adikos draw osper e dromeus osos an qew eu apo o katos apo de o anoos mh o men proteros ocews apophdaw teleutaw
english_head: so far as that is possible for man. It is reasonable, he said, that such a one should not be neglected by his like. And must we not think the opposite of the unjust man? Most emphatically. Such then are the prizes of victory which the gods bestow upon the just. So I think, at any rate, he said. But what, said I, does he receive from men? Is not this the case, if we are now to present the reality? Do not your smart but wicked men fare as those racers do who run well from the scratch but not back from the turn? They bound nimbly away at the start, but in the end

greek_head: δὲ καταγέλαστοι γίγνονται τὰ ὦτα ἐπὶ τῶν ὤμων ἔχοντες καὶ ἀστεφάνωτοι ἀποτρέχοντες οἱ δὲ τῇ ἀληθείᾳ δρομικοὶ
greek_tokens: de katagelastos gignomai o ous epi o wmos exw kai astefanwtos apotrexw e de o alhqeia dromikos eis telos erxomai o te aqlon lambanw kai stefanow ou outws kai peri o dikaios o polus sumbainw pros o telos ekastos pracis kai omilia kai o bios eudokimew te kai o aqlon para o anqrwpos ferw kai mala anexw ara legw egw peri outos o autos legw
english_head: are laughed to scorn and run off the field uncrowned and with their ears on their shoulders. But the true runners when they have come to the goal receive the prizes and bear away the crown. Is not this the usual outcome for the just also, that towards the end of every action and association and of life as a whole they have honor and bear away the prizes from men? So it is indeed. Will you, then, bear with me if I say of them

greek_head: περὶ τῶν ἀδίκων ἐρῶ γὰρ δὴ ὅτι οἱ μὲν δίκαιοι ἐπειδὰν πρεσβύτεροι γένωνται ἐν τῇ αὑτῶν πόλει ἄρχουσί τε ἂν
greek_tokens: peri o adikos eraomai gar dh ostis e men dikaios epeidan presbus gignomai en o eautou polis arxw te an boulomai o arxh gamew te opoqen an boulomai ekdidwmi te eis os an eqelw kai pas o su peri ekeinos egw nun legos peri ode kai au kai peri o adikos ostis e polus autos kai ean neos eimi lanqanw epi telos o dromos airew katagelastos eimi kai gerwn gignomai aqlios prophlakizw upo cenos te
english_head: all that you said of the unjust? For I am going to say that the just, when they become older, hold the offices in their own city if they choose, marry from what families they will, and give their children in marriage to what families they please, and everything that you said of the one I now repeat of the other; and in turn I will say of the unjust that the most of them, even if they escape detection in youth, at the end of their course are caught and derided, and their old age is made miserable by the contumelies of strangers and townsfolk.

greek_head: καὶ ἀστῶν μαστιγούμενοι καὶ ἃ ἄγροικα ἔφησθα σὺ εἶναι ἀληθῆ λέγων εἶτα στρεβλώσονται καὶ ἐκκαυθήσονται
greek_tokens: kai astu mastigow kai o agroikos fhmi su eimi alhqhs legos eita streblow kai ekkaiw pas ekeinos oiomai kai egw akouw ws pasxw alla o legos oraw ei anexw kai panu fhmi dikaios gar legw o men toinun eimi de egw zaw o dikaios para qea te
english_head: They are lashed and suffer all things which you truly said are unfit for ears polite. Suppose yourself to have heard from me a repetition of all that they suffer. But, as I say, consider whether you will bear with me. Assuredly, he said, for what you say is just. Such then while he lives are the prizes, the wages, and the gifts

english_head: that the just man receives from gods and men in addition to those blessings which justice herself bestowed. And right fair and abiding rewards, he said. Well, these, I said, are nothing in number and magnitude compared with those that await both after death. And we must listen to the tale of them, said I, in order that each may have received in full what is due to be said of him by our argument. Tell me, he said,

greek_head: Λέγοις ἄν ἔφη ὡς οὐ πολλὰ ἄλλ' ἥδιον ἀκούοντι Ἀλλ' οὐ μέντοι σοι ἦν δ' ἐγώ Ἀλκίνου γε ἀπόλογον
greek_tokens: legos an fhmi ws ou polus allos hdus akouw alla ou mentoi su eimi de egw alkinoos ge apologos eraomai alla alkimos men anhr hros o sanduc o genos pamfulos os pote en polemos teleutaw anairew dekataios o nekros hdh diafqeirw ugihs men anairew komizw de oikade mellw qaptw dwdekataios epi o purazw keimai anabiow anabiow de legw o ekei eidon fhmi de epei e ekbainw o yuxh poreuw
english_head: since there are not many things to which I would more gladly listen. It is not, let me tell you, said I, the tale to Alcinous told that I shall unfold, but the tale of a warrior bold, Er, the son of Armenius, by race a Pamphylian. He once upon a time was slain in battle, and when the corpses were taken up on the tenth day already decayed, was found intact, and having been brought home, at the moment of his funeral, on the twelfth day as he lay upon the pyre, revived, and after coming to life related what, he said, he had seen in the world beyond. He said that when his soul went forth from his body he journeyed with a great company

greek_head: μετὰ πολλῶν καὶ ἀφικνεῖσθαι σφᾶς εἰς τόπον τινὰ δαιμόνιον ἐν ᾧ τῆς τε γῆς δύ' εἶναι χάσματα ἐχομένω ἀλλήλοιν καὶ

english_head: and that they came to a mysterious region where there were two openings side by side in the earth, and above and over against them in the heaven two others, and that judges were sitting between these, and that after every judgement they bade the righteous journey to the right and upwards through the heaven with tokens attached to them in front of the judgement passed upon them, and the unjust to take the road to the left and downward, they too wearing behind signs

english_head: of all that had befallen them, and that when he himself drew near they told him that he must be the messenger to mankind to tell them of that other world, and they charged him to give ear and to observe everything in the place. And so he said that here he saw, by each opening of heaven and earth, the souls departing after judgement had been passed upon them, while, by the other pair of openings, there came up from the one in the earth souls full of squalor and dust, and from the second there came down from heaven a second procession of souls clean and pure,

english_head: and that those which arrived from time to time appeared to have come as it were from a long journey and gladly departed to the meadow and encamped there as at a festival, and acquaintances greeted one another, and those which came from the earth questioned the others about conditions up yonder, and those from heaven asked how it fared with those others. And they told their stories to one another, the one lamenting

english_head: and wailing as they recalled how many and how dreadful things they had suffered and seen in their journey beneath the earth—it lasted a thousand years—while those from heaven related their delights and visions of a beauty beyond words. To tell it all, Glaucon, would take all our time, but the sum, he said, was this. For all the wrongs they had ever done to anyone and all whom they had severally wronged they had paid the penalty in turn tenfold for each, and the measure of this was by periods of a hundred years each,

english_head: so that on the assumption that this was the length of human life the punishment might be ten times the crime; as for example that if anyone had been the cause of many deaths or had betrayed cities and armies and reduced them to slavery, or had been participant in any other iniquity, they might receive in requital pains tenfold for each of these wrongs, and again if any had done deeds of kindness and been just

english_head: and holy men they might receive their due reward in the same measure; and other things not worthy of record he said of those who had just been born and lived but a short time; and he had still greater requitals to tell of piety and impiety towards the gods and parents and of self-slaughter. For he said that he stood by when one was questioned by another Where is Ardiaeus the Great? Now this Ardiaeos had been tyrant in a certain city of Pamphylia just a thousand years before that time and had put to death his old father

english_head: and his elder brother, and had done many other unholy deeds, as was the report. So he said that the one questioned replied, He has not come, said he, nor will he be likely to come here. For indeed this was one of the dreadful sights we beheld; when we were near the mouth and about to issue forth and all our other sufferings were ended, we suddenly caught sight of him and of others, the most of them, I may say, tyrants. But there were some

english_head: of private station, of those who had committed great crimes. And when these supposed that at last they were about to go up and out, the mouth would not receive them, but it bellowed when anyone of the incurably wicked or of those who had not completed their punishment tried to come up. And thereupon, he said, savage men of fiery aspect who stood by and took note of the voice laid hold on them and bore them away. But Ardiaeus

english_head: and others they bound hand and foot and head and flung down and flayed them and dragged them by the wayside, carding them on thorns and signifying to those who from time to time passed by for what cause they were borne away, and that they were to be hurled into Tartarus. And then, though many and manifold dread things had befallen them, this fear exceeded all—lest each one should hear the voice when he tried to go up, and each went up most gladly when it had kept silence. And the judgements and penalties were somewhat after this manner,

english_head: and the blessings were their counterparts. But when seven days had elapsed for each group in the meadow, they were required to rise up on the eighth and journey on, and they came in four days to a spot whence they discerned, extended from above throughout the heaven and the earth, a straight light like a pillar, most nearly resembling the rainbow, but brighter and purer. To this they came

english_head: after going forward a day’s journey, and they saw there at the middle of the light the extremities of its fastenings stretched from heaven; for this light was the girdle of the heavens like the undergirders of triremes, holding together in like manner the entire revolving vault. And from the extremities was stretched the spindle of Necessity, through which all the orbits turned. Its staff and its hook were made of adamant, and the whorl of these and other kinds was commingled. And the nature of the whorl was this:

The third had the whitest color, and the fourth was of a slightly ruddy hue; the sixth was second in whiteness. The staff turned as a whole in a circle with the same movement, but within the whole as it revolved the seven inner circles revolved gently in the opposite direction to the whole, and of these seven the eighth moved most swiftly,

english_head: who sat round about at equal intervals, each one on her throne, the Fates, daughters of Necessity, clad in white vestments with filleted heads, Lachesis, and Clotho, and Atropos, who sang in unison with the music of the Sirens, Lachesis singing the things that were, Clotho the things that are, and Atropos the things that are to be. And Clotho with the touch of her right hand helped to turn the outer circumference of the spindle, pausing from time to time. Atropos with her left hand in like manner helped to turn the inner circles, and Lachesis

english_head: alternately with either hand lent a hand to each. Now when they arrived they were straight-way bidden to go before Lachesis, and then a certain prophet first marshalled them in orderly intervals, and thereupon took from the lap of Lachesis lots and patterns of lives and went up to a lofty platform and spoke, This is the word of Lachesis, the maiden daughter of Necessity, Souls that live for a day, now is the beginning of another cycle of mortal generation where birth is the beacon of death.

greek_head: οὐχ ὑμᾶς δαίμων λήξεται ἀλλ' ὑμεῖς δαίμονα αἱρήσεσθε πρῶτος δ' ὁ λαχὼν πρῶτος αἱρείσθω βίον ᾧ συνέσται ἐξ
greek_tokens: ou su daimwn lagxanw alla su daimwn airew proteros de o lagxanw proteros airew bios os suneimi ek anagkh areth de adespotos os timaw kai atimazw pleos kai elasswn autos ekastos ecis aitios airew qeos anaitios outos eipon riptw epi pas o klhros o de para eautou piptw ekastos anairew plhn e e de ou eaw o de anairew dhlos eimi opostos lagxanw
english_head: No divinity shall cast lots for you, but you shall choose your own deity. Let him to whom falls the first lot first select a life to which he shall cleave of necessity. But virtue has no master over her, and each shall have more or less of her as he honors her or does her despite. The blame is his who chooses: God is blameless. So saying, the prophet flung the lots out among them all, and each took up the lot that fell by his side, except himself; him they did not permit. And whoever took up a lot saw plainly what number he had drawn.

greek_head: μετὰ δὲ τοῦτο αὖθις τὰ τῶν βίων παραδείγματα εἰς τὸ πρόσθεν σφῶν θεῖναι ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν πολὺ πλείω τῶν
greek_tokens: meta de outos auqis o o bios paradeigma eis o prosqen sfazw qeinw epi o gaia polus plew o pareimi eimi de pantodapos zwion te gar pas bios kai dh kai o anqrwpinos apas turannis te gar en autos eimi o men diatelew o de kai metacu diafqeirw kai eis penia te kai fugas kai eis ptwxeia teleutaw eimi de kai dokimos androw bios o men epi eidos kai kata kallos kai o allos isxus
english_head: And after this again the prophet placed the patterns of lives before them on the ground, far more numerous than the assembly. They were of every variety, for there were lives of all kinds of animals and all sorts of human lives, for there were tyrannies among them, some uninterrupted till the end and others destroyed midway and issuing in penuries and exiles and beggaries; and there were lives of men of repute for their forms and beauty and bodily strength otherwise

greek_head: τε καὶ ἀγωνίαν τοὺς δ' ἐπὶ γένεσιν καὶ προγόνων ἀρεταῖς καὶ ἀδοκίμων κατὰ ταῦτα ὡσαύτως δὲ καὶ γυναικῶν ψυχῆς
greek_tokens: te kai agwnia o de epi genesis kai progonos areth kai adokimos kata outos wsautws de kai gunaikow yuxazw de tacis ou enihmi dia o anagkaios exw allos airew bios alloios gignomai o de allos allhlwn te kai ploutos kai penia o de nosos o de ugieia mignumi o de kai mesow outos enqa dh ws eoika eimi filos glauc o pas kindunos anqrwpos kai dia outos malista
english_head: and prowess and the high birth and the virtues of their ancestors, and others of ill repute in the same things, and similarly of women. But there was no determination of the quality of soul, because the choice of a different life inevitably determined a different character. But all other things were commingled with one another and with wealth and poverty and sickness and health and the intermediate conditions. —And there, dear Glaucon, it appears, is the supreme hazard for a man.

greek_head: ἐπιμελητέον ὅπως ἕκαστος ἡμῶν τῶν ἄλλων μαθημάτων ἀμελήσας τούτου τοῦ μαθήματος καὶ ζητητὴς καὶ μαθητὴς
greek_tokens: epimelhteon opws ekastos egw o allos maqhma amelew outos o maqhma kai zhthths kai maqhths eimi ean poqen oios su eimi manqanw kai eceuriskw tis autos poihsis dunatos kai episthmwn bios kai xrhstos kai ponhros diagignwskw o beltiwn ek o dunaths aei pantaxou airew analogizomai pas o nundi erw kai suntiqhmi allhlwn kai diairew pros areth bios pws exis oida tis kallos penia h ploutos kerannumi kai
english_head: And this is the chief reason why it should be our main concern that each of us, neglecting all other studies, should seek after and study this thing—if in any way he may be able to learn of and discover the man who will give him the ability and the knowledge to distinguish the life that is good from that which is bad, and always and everywhere to choose the best that the conditions allow, and, taking into account all the things of which we have spoken and estimating the effect on the goodness of his life of their conjunction or their severance, to know how beauty commingled with poverty or wealth and combined with

greek_head: μετὰ ποίας τινὸς ψυχῆς ἕξεως κακὸν ἢ ἀγαθὸν ἐργάζεται καὶ τί εὐγένειαι καὶ δυσγένειαι καὶ ἰδιωτεῖαι καὶ ἀρχαὶ καὶ
greek_tokens: meta poa tis yuxazw ecis kakos h agaqos ergazomai kai tis eugeneia kai dusgeneia kai idiwteia kai arxh kai isxus kai asqeneia kai eumaqeia kai dusmaqia kai pas o toioutos o fusis peri yuxh eimi kai o epikthtos tis sugkerannumi pros allhlwn ergazomai wste ek apas autos dunatos eimi sullogizomai airew pros o o yuxazw fus apoblepw o te xeirwn kai o ameinwn
english_head: what habit of soul operates for good or for evil, and what are the effects of high and low birth and private station and office and strength and weakness and quickness of apprehension and dullness and all similar natural and acquired habits of the soul, when blended and combined with one another, so that with consideration of all these things he will be able to make a reasoned choice between the better and the worse life,

greek_head: βίον χείρω μὲν καλοῦντα ὃς αὐτὴν ἐκεῖσε ἄξει εἰς τὸ ἀδικωτέραν γίγνεσθαι ἀμείνω δὲ ὅστις εἰς τὸ δικαιοτέραν τὰ δὲ
greek_tokens: bios xeirwn men kalew os autos ekeise agnumi eis o adikos gignomai ameinwn de ostis eis o dikaios o de allos pas xairw eaw oraw gar ostis zaw te kai teleutaw outos kratistos airesis adamantinos dh
english_head: with his eyes fixed on the nature of his soul, naming the worse life that which will tend to make it more unjust and the better that which will make it more just. But all other considerations he will dismiss, for we have seen that this is the best choice,

greek_head: δεῖ ταύτην τὴν δόξαν ἔχοντα εἰς Ἅιδου ἰέναι ὅπως ἂν ᾖ καὶ ἐκεῖ ἀνέκπληκτος ὑπὸ πλούτων τε καὶ τῶν τοιούτων κακῶν
greek_tokens: deomai outos o doca exw eis adhs eimi opws an eimi kai ekei anekplhktos upo ploutos te kai o toioutos kakh kai mh empitnw eis turannis kai allos toioutos prassw polus men ergazomai kai anhkestos kakos eti de autos megas paqh alla gignwskw o mesos aei o toioutos bios airew kai feugw o uperballw ekaterwse kai en ode o bios kata o dunatos kai en pas o epeita outws gar
english_head: both for life and death. And a man must take with him to the house of death an adamantine faith in this, that even there he may be undazzled by riches and similar trumpery, and may not precipitate himself into tyrannies and similar doings and so work many evils past cure and suffer still greater himself, but may know how always to choose in such things the life that is seated in the mean and shun the excess in either direction, both in this world so far as may be and in all the life to come;

greek_head: εὐδαιμονέστατος γίγνεται ἄνθρωπος Καὶ δὴ οὖν καὶ τότε ὁ ἐκεῖθεν ἄγγελος ἤγγελλε τὸν
greek_tokens: eudaimwn gignomai anqrwpos kai dh oun kai tote o ekeiqen aggelos aggellw o men profaw outws eipon kai teleutaios epeimi sun egw airew suntonos zaw kew bios agaphtos ou kakos mhte o arxw airesis amelew mhte o teleutaw aqumew eipon de outos o proteros lagxanw fhmi euqus epeimi o megas turannis airew kai upo afrosunh te kai laimargia ou pas ikanos anaskeptomai airew alla
english_head: for this is the greatest happiness for man. And at that time also the messenger from that other world reported that the prophet spoke thus: Even for him who comes forward last, if he make his choice wisely and live strenuously, there is reserved an acceptable life, no evil one. Let not the foremost in the choice be heedless nor the last be discouraged. When the prophet had thus spoken he said that the drawer of the first lot at once sprang to seize the greatest tyranny, and that in his folly and greed he chose it

greek_head: αὐτὸν λαθεῖν ἐνοῦσαν εἱμαρμένην παίδων αὑτοῦ βρώσεις καὶ ἄλλα κακά ἐπειδὴ δὲ κατὰ σχολὴν σκέψασθαι κόπτεσθαί
greek_tokens: autos lanqanw eneimi meiromai pais eautou bibrwskw kai allos kakos epei de kata sxolh skeptomai koptw te kai oduromai o airesis ou emmenw o proerew upo o profhths ou gar eautou aitiaomai o kakh alla tuxh te kai daimwn kai pas mallon anti eautou eimi de autos o ek o ouranos hkw en tassw politeia en o proteros bios biow eqos
english_head: without sufficient examination, and failed to observe that it involved the fate of eating his own children, and other horrors, and that when he inspected it at leisure he beat his breast and bewailed his choice, not abiding by the forewarning of the prophet. For he did not blame himself for his woes, but fortune and the gods and anything except himself. He was one of those who had come down from heaven, a man who had lived in a well-ordered polity in his former existence,

greek_head: ἄνευ φιλοσοφίας ἀρετῆς μετειληφότα ὡς δὲ καὶ εἰπεῖν οὐκ ἐλάττους εἶναι ἐν τοῖς τοιούτοις ἁλισκομένους τοὺς ἐκ τοῦ
greek_tokens: aneu filosofia aretaw metalambanw ws de kai eipon ou elasswn eimi en o toioutos aliskomai o ek o ouranos hkw ate ponos agumnastos o de ek o gaia o polus ate autos te ponew allos te oraw ou ek epidromew o airesis poiew dio dh kai metabolh o kakh kai o agaqos o polus o yuxos gignomai kai dia o o klhros tuxh epei ei tis aei opote eis o enqade bios afikneomai ugihs filosofew
english_head: participating in virtue by habit and not by philosophy; and one may perhaps say that a majority of those who were thus caught were of the company that had come from heaven, inasmuch as they were unexercised in suffering. But the most of those who came up from the earth, since they had themselves suffered and seen the sufferings of others, did not make their choice precipitately. For which reason also there was an interchange of good and evil for most of the souls, as well as because of the chances of the lot. Yet if at each return to the life of this world

greek_head: καὶ ὁ κλῆρος αὐτῷ τῆς αἱρέσεως μὴ ἐν τελευταίοις πίπτοι κινδυνεύει ἐκ τῶν ἐκεῖθεν ἀπαγγελλομένων οὐ μόνον
greek_tokens: kai o klhros autos o airesis mh en teleutaios piptw kinduneuw ek o ekeiqen apaggellw ou monos enqade eudaimonew an alla kai o enqende ekeise kai deuro palin poreia ou an xqonios kai traxus poreuw alla leia te kai ouranios outos gar dh fhmi o qea acios eimi eidon ws ekastos
english_head: a man loved wisdom sanely, and the lot of his choice did not fall out among the last, we may venture to affirm, from what was reported thence, that not only will he be happy here but that the path of his journey thither and the return to this world will not be underground and rough but smooth and through the heavens. For he said that it was a sight worth seeing to observe how the several souls selected their lives.

greek_head: αἱ ψυχαὶ ᾑροῦντο τοὺς βίους ἐλεινήν τε γὰρ ἰδεῖν εἶναι καὶ γελοίαν καὶ θαυμασίαν κατὰ συνήθειαν γὰρ τοῦ προτέρου
greek_tokens: o yuxh airew o bios eleeinos te gar eidon eimi kai geloios kai qaumasios kata sunhqeia gar o proteros bios o polus airew eidon men gar yuxh fhmi o pote orfeus gignomai kuknos bios airew misos o gunaikeios genos dia o upo ekeinos qanatos ou eqelw en gunh gennaw gignomai eidon de o qamurou ahdwn airew eidon de kai kuknos metaballw eis anqrwpinos bios airesis kai allos zwion mousikh wsautws
english_head: He said it was a strange, pitiful, and ridiculous spectacle, as the choice was determined for the most part by the habits of their former lives. He saw the soul that had been Orpheus’, he said, selecting the life of a swan, because from hatred of the tribe of women, owing to his death at their hands, it was unwilling to be conceived and born of a woman. He saw the soul of Thamyras choosing the life of a nightingale; and he saw a swan changing to the choice of the life of man, and similarly other musical animals.

greek_head: εἰκοστὴν δὲ λαχοῦσαν ψυχὴν ἑλέσθαι λέοντος βίον εἶναι δὲ τὴν Αἴαντος τοῦ Τελαμωνίου φεύγουσαν ἄνθρωπον γενέσθαι
greek_tokens: eikosth de lagxanw yuxh airew lewn bios eimi de o aias o telamwn feugw anqrwpos gignomai mimnhskw o o oplon krisis o de epi outos agamemnwn exqra de kai outos o anqrwpinos genos dia o paqh aetos diallassw bios en mesos de lagxanw o atalanth yuxh kateidon megas timh aqlhths anhr ou dunamai parerxomai alla lambanw meta
english_head: The soul that drew the twentieth lot chose the life of a lion; it was the soul of Ajax, the son of Telamon, which, because it remembered the adjudication of the arms of Achilles, was unwilling to become a man. The next, the soul of Agamemnon, likewise from hatred of the human race because of its sufferings, substituted the life of an eagle. Drawing one of the middle lots the soul of Atalanta caught sight of the great honors attached to an athlete’s life and could not pass them by but snatched at them.

greek_head: δὲ ταύτην ἰδεῖν τὴν Ἐπειοῦ τοῦ Πανοπέως εἰς τεχνικῆς γυναικὸς ἰοῦσαν φύσιν πόρρω δ' ἐν ὑστάτοις ἰδεῖν τὴν τοῦ
greek_tokens: de outos eidon o epeios o panopeus eis texnikos gunh eimi fus prosw de en ustatos eidon o o gelwtopoiew qersiths piqhkos enduw kata tuxh de o odusseus lagxanw pas ustatos airew eimi mnhmh de o proteros ponos filotimia lwfaw zhtew perieimi xronos polus bios anhr idiwths apragmwn kai mogis euriskw keimai pou kai paramelew
english_head: After her, he said, he saw the soul of Epeius, the son of Panopeus, entering into the nature of an arts and crafts woman. Far off in the rear he saw the soul of the buffoon Thersites clothing itself in the body of an ape. And it fell out that the soul of Odysseus drew the last lot of all and came to make its choice, and, from memory of its former toils having flung away ambition, went about for a long time in quest of the life of an ordinary citizen who minded his own business, and with difficulty found it lying in some corner disregarded by the others,

greek_head: ὑπὸ τῶν ἄλλων καὶ εἰπεῖν ἰδοῦσαν ὅτι τὰ αὐτὰ ἂν ἔπραξεν καὶ πρώτη λαχοῦσα καὶ ἁσμένην ἑλέσθαι καὶ ἐκ τῶν
greek_tokens: upo o allos kai eipon eidon ostis o autos an prassw kai proteros lagxanw kai asmenos airew kai ek o allos dh qhraw wsautws eis anqrwpos eimi kai eis allhlwn o men adikos eis o agrios o de dikaios eis o hmeros metaballw kai passw meicis mignumi epei de oun passw o yuxh o bios airew wsper elaxos en tacis proseimi pros o laxos ekeinos de ekastos os airew daimwn outos fulac sumpempw
english_head: and upon seeing it said that it would have done the same had it drawn the first lot, and chose it gladly. And in like manner, of the other beasts some entered into men and into one another, the unjust into wild creatures, the just transformed to tame, and there was every kind of mixture and combination. But when, to conclude, all the souls had chosen their lives in the order of their lots, they were marshalled and went before Lachesis. And she sent with each,

greek_head: τοῦ βίου καὶ ἀποπληρωτὴν τῶν αἱρεθέντων ὃν πρῶτον μὲν ἄγειν αὐτὴν πρὸς τὴν Κλωθὼ ὑπὸ τὴν ἐκείνης
greek_tokens: o bios kai apoplhrwths o airew os proteros men agw autos pros o klwqw upo o ekeinos xeir te kai epistrofh o o atraktos dinh kurew os lagxanw airew moira outos de efaptw auqis epi o o atropos agw neomai ametastrofos o epiklwqw poiew enteuqen de dh ametastrepti upo o o
english_head: as the guardian of his life and the fulfiller of his choice, the genius that he had chosen, and this divinity led the soul first to Clotho, under her hand and her turning of the spindle to ratify the destiny of his lot and choice; and after contact with her the genius again led the soul to the spinning of Atropos to make the web of its destiny irreversible, and then without a backward look it passed beneath the throne of Necessity.

greek_head: Ἀνάγκης ἰέναι θρόνον καὶ δι' ἐκείνου διεξελθόντα ἐπειδὴ καὶ οἱ ἄλλοι διῆλθον πορεύεσθαι ἅπαντας εἰς τὸ τῆς Λήθης
greek_tokens: anagkh eimi qronon kai dia ekeinos diecerxomai epei kai e allos dierxomai poreuw apas eis o o lhqh pedaw dia kauma te kai pnigos deinos kai gar eimi autos kenos dendron te kai osos gaia fuos skhnaw oun sfazw hdh espera gignomai para o amelhtos potamos e o udwr aggeion oudeis stegw metron men oun tis o udwr pas anagkaion eimi pinw o de fronhsis mh swzw pleos pinw o metron o de aei pinw
english_head: And after it had passed through that, when the others also had passed, they all journeyed to the Plain of Oblivion, through a terrible and stifling heat, for it was bare of trees and all plants, and there they camped at eventide by the River of Forgetfulness, whose waters no vessel can contain. They were all required to drink a measure of the water, and those who were not saved by their good sense drank more than the measure, and each one as he drank forgot all things.

greek_head: πάντων ἐπιλανθάνεσθαι ἐπειδὴ δὲ κοιμηθῆναι καὶ μέσας νύκτας γενέσθαι βροντήν τε καὶ σεισμὸν γενέσθαι καὶ
greek_tokens: pas epilanqanomai epei de koimaw kai mesh nuc gignomai bronth te kai seismos gignomai kai enteuqen ecapinhs allos allh ferw anoos eis o genesis aissw wsper asthr autos de o men udwr kwluw pinw oph mentoi kai opws eis o swma afikneomai ou oida alla ecaifnhs anablepw eidon ewqen eautou keimai epi o purazw kai outws eimi glauc muqos eiswqew kai ou apollumi
english_head: And after they had fallen asleep and it was the middle of the night, there was a sound of thunder and a quaking of the earth, and they were suddenly wafted thence, one this way, one that, upward to their birth like shooting stars. Er himself, he said, was not allowed to drink of the water, yet how and in what way he returned to the body he said he did not know, but suddenly recovering his sight he saw himself at dawn lying on the funeral pyre.—And so, Glaucon, the tale was saved, as the saying is, and was not lost.

greek_head: καὶ ἡμᾶς ἂν σώσειεν ἂν πειθώμεθα αὐτῷ καὶ τὸν τῆς Λήθης ποταμὸν εὖ διαβησόμεθα καὶ τὴν ψυχὴν οὐ μιανθησόμεθα
greek_tokens: kai egw an swzw an peiqw autos kai o o lhqh potamos eu diabainw kai o yuxh ou miainw alla an egw peiqw nomizw aqanatos yuxh kai dunatos pas men kakos anexw pas de agaqos o anoos odaw aei exw kai dikaiosunh meta fronhsis pas tropos epithdeuw ina kai egw autos filos eimi kai o qeaw autos te menw enqade kai epeidan o aqlon
english_head: And it will save us if we believe it, and we shall safely cross the River of Lethe, and keep our soul unspotted from the world. But if we are guided by me we shall believe that the soul is immortal and capable of enduring all extremes of good and evil, and so we shall hold ever to the upward way and pursue righteousness with wisdom always and ever, that we may be dear to ourselves and to the gods both during our sojourn here and when we receive our reward,

greek_head: αὐτῆς κομιζώμεθα ὥσπερ οἱ νικηφόροι περιαγειρόμενοι καὶ ἐνθάδε καὶ ἐν τῇ χιλιέτει πορείᾳ ἣν διεληλύθαμεν εὖ
greek_tokens: autos komizw wsper e nikhforos periageirw kai enqade kai en o xilietei poreia os dierxomai eu prassw
english_head: as the victors in the games go about to gather in theirs. And thus both here and in that journey of a thousand years, whereof I have told you, we shall fare well.
